---
title: Algorithmic Randomness and Complexity
layout: default
noindex: true
---

# Algorithmic Randomness and Complexity

## Introduction

What does it mean to say that an individual mathematical object such as an infinite binary sequence is random? Or to say that one sequence is more random than another? The material in this book arises from questions such as these, and can be thought of as exploring the relationships between three fundamental concepts:

- **Relative computability**, as measured by notions such as Turing reducibility.
- **Information content**, as measured by notions such as Kolmogorov complexity.
- **Randomness** of individual objects, as first successfully defined by Martin-Löf.

### Historical Development

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Von Mises' Approach)</span></p>

The roots of algorithmic randomness go back to the work of **Richard von Mises** in the early 20th century. He argued that a sequence should count as random if all "reasonable" infinite subsequences satisfy the **law of large numbers** (i.e., have the same proportion of 0's as 1's in the limit). Von Mises wrote of "acceptable selection rules" for subsequences. **Wald** later showed that for any countable collection of selection rules, there are sequences that are random in the sense of von Mises, but it was unclear exactly what types of selection rules should be acceptable.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Church Stochasticity)</span></p>

**Church** made the first explicit connection between computability theory and randomness by suggesting that a selection rule be considered acceptable iff it is computable. This definition is now called **Church stochasticity** and can be seen as the birth of the theory of *algorithmic* randomness.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Ville's Theorem)</span></p>

A blow to the von Mises program was dealt by **Ville**, who showed that for any countable collection of selection rules, there is a sequence that is random in the sense of von Mises but has properties that make it clearly nonrandom. In Ville's example, the ratio of 0's to 1's in the first $n$ bits of the sequence is at least 1 for all $n$. This showed that satisfying the law of large numbers alone is insufficient to capture randomness.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Martin-Löf's Definition)</span></p>

**Martin-Löf** noted that the particular statistical tests that had been considered (the law of large numbers, the law of iterated logarithms, etc.) were special cases of a general abstract notion of statistical test based on the notion of an "effectively null" set. He then defined a notion of randomness based on passing *all* such tests (or equivalently, not being in any effectively null set).

Martin-Löf's definition turned out to be not only foundationally well-motivated but mathematically robust and productive. Now known as **1-randomness** or **Martin-Löf randomness**, it is the central notion of randomness in this book. One of the most attractive features is that it can be arrived at from several other approaches:
- The idea that random sequences should be **incompressible**.
- The idea that random sequences should be **unpredictable**.

These approaches lead to equivalent definitions of 1-randomness in terms of, for example, **prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity** and **computably enumerable martingales**.

</div>

### Three Approaches to Randomness

The three major approaches to defining randomness for infinite sequences are:

1. **Typicality** (measure-theoretic): A random sequence should pass all "reasonable" statistical tests. Martin-Löf's approach formalizes "reasonable" via effective null sets.

2. **Incompressibility**: A random sequence should have initial segments that cannot be significantly compressed. This is formalized via Kolmogorov complexity — the length of the shortest description of a string.

3. **Unpredictability**: A random sequence should be one against which no effective betting strategy (martingale) can succeed. This was already present in the original motivation behind von Mises' definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--info" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Info</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Kolmogorov Complexity — Informal Overview)</span></p>

The **Kolmogorov complexity** of a finite string is the length of its shortest description. To formalize this, one uses universal machines, thought of as "optimal description systems". A string $\sigma$ is random iff the Kolmogorov complexity of $\sigma$ is no shorter than the length of $\sigma$ (i.e., $\sigma$ is its own best description).

For infinite sequences, there is a subtlety: no infinite sequence has all initial segments incompressible (Theorem 3.1.4 in the text). This is resolved by introducing **prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity**, which is based on machines whose domains are antichains, and corresponds to the idea that if a string $\tau$ describes a string $\sigma$, then the description should be encoded entirely in the bits of $\tau$, not in its length.

</div>

### Overview of the Book

**Background (Chapters 1–5):**
- **Chapter 1:** Basic notation, conventions, terminology, and a small amount of measure theory.
- **Chapter 2:** A whirlwind tour of computability theory (Turing machines, c.e. sets, etc.).
- **Chapter 3:** Introduction to Kolmogorov complexity — counting theorems, symmetry of information, and the Coding Theorem. Discusses both plain and prefix-free complexity.
- **Chapter 4:** Solovay's results relating plain and prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity, and Gács' separation of two notions of complexity.
- **Chapter 5:** Effective real numbers, in particular left-c.e. reals. A classic example is **Chaitin's $\Omega$**, the measure of the domain of a universal prefix-free machine — the canonical example of a specific 1-random real.

**Notions of Algorithmic Randomness (Chapters 6–8):**
- **Chapter 6:** 1-randomness and $n$-randomness via Martin-Löf tests, martingales, and Kolmogorov complexity. Proves Schnorr's fundamental theorems that these definitions are equivalent. Also covers Ville's Theorem, van Lambalgen's Theorem, effective 0-1 laws, and the Ample Excess Lemma.
- **Chapter 7:** Other notions of randomness — **Schnorr randomness**, **computable randomness**, and **weak $n$-randomness** (introduced by Kurtz). Discusses **Schnorr's critique** that 1-randomness is computably enumerable rather than computable, and hence too strong.
- **Chapter 8:** Interactions between randomness and computability — Kučera-Gács Theorem, Demuth's Theorem, Stephan's dichotomy theorem, and the relationship between $n$-randomness, weak $n$-randomness, and genericity.

**Relative Randomness (Chapters 9–10):**
- **Chapter 9:** Reducibilities as measures of relative randomness on left-c.e. reals. The 1-random left-c.e. reals are exactly those complete for **Solovay reducibility** — exactly those equal to $\Omega$ for some choice of universal prefix-free machine.
- **Chapter 10:** $K$- and $C$-reducibility, initial segment complexity of 1-random sets, **van Lambalgen reducibility**, and **$LR$-reducibility**.

**Lowness and Triviality (Chapters 11–13):**
- **Chapter 11:** $K$-trivial sets — sets whose initial segments have the lowest possible prefix-free complexity. Proves Nies' result that being $K$-trivial, being low for 1-randomness, and being low for $K$ all coincide.
- **Chapter 12:** Lowness and triviality for Schnorr and computable randomness. Nies' result that there are no noncomputable sets that are low for computable randomness.
- **Chapter 13:** Algorithmic dimension — **effective Hausdorff dimension** and **effective packing dimension** characterized via martingales and Kolmogorov complexity. Also covers **Schnorr dimension**.

**Further Results (Chapters 14–16):**
- **Chapter 14:** Strongly jump traceable sets — a proper subclass of the $K$-trivials with deep connections to randomness.
- **Chapter 15:** $\Omega$ as an operator on Cantor space. Studies properties of Omega operators.
- **Chapter 16:** The relationship between Kolmogorov complexity and c.e. sets — Kummer's theorem and Solovay's theorem on enumeration probability.

## Chapter 1: Preliminaries

### 1.1 Notation and Conventions

**Strings.** Unless specified otherwise, a *string* means a finite binary string. We denote:
- The set of finite binary strings by $2^{<\omega}$.
- The set of binary strings of length $n$ by $2^n$.
- The set of binary strings of length $\leqslant n$ by $2^{\leqslant n}$.
- The empty string by $\lambda$.
- The length of a string $\sigma$ by $\lvert\sigma\rvert$.
- The concatenation of strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$ by $\sigma\tau$ or $\sigma {}^\frown \tau$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Length-Lexicographic Ordering)</span></p>

The *length-lexicographic ordering* on $2^{<\omega}$ is defined by: $\sigma <_L \tau$ if either $\lvert\sigma\rvert < \lvert\tau\rvert$, or else both $\lvert\sigma\rvert = \lvert\tau\rvert$ and $\sigma(n) = 0$ for the least $n$ such that $\sigma(n) \neq \tau(n)$.

</div>

**Sets, sequences, and reals.** Unless specified otherwise or clear from context:
- A *set* means a set of natural numbers.
- A *sequence* means an infinite binary sequence.
- A *real* means a real number in $[0, 1]$.

These three classes of objects are essentially the same: we identify a set $A$ with its characteristic function $\chi_A$ (so $A(n) = 1$ iff $n \in A$), with the infinite binary sequence $A(0)A(1)\ldots$, and with the real $0.A(0)A(1)\ldots$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Convention</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1.1.1)</span></p>

Unless mentioned otherwise or clear from context, we assume that all reals are irrational, and hence have unique binary representations.

</div>

We denote the natural numbers by $\mathbb{N}$ or $\omega$ interchangeably, and the **Cantor space** of infinite binary sequences by $2^\omega$.

**Notation.** We write $A =^* B$ to mean $A(n) = B(n)$ for all but finitely many $n$. We write $A \upharpoonright n$ for the restriction of $A$ to its first $n$ bits (the string $A(0)\ldots A(n-1)$). More generally, $A \upharpoonright S$ is the restriction of $A$ to the bits in $S$. We write $\overline{A}$ for the complement $\mathbb{N} \setminus A$.

**Functions.** For functions $f, g, h : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$:

- $f(n) \leqslant g(n) + O(h(n))$: there is a constant $c$ such that $f(n) \leqslant g(n) + c \cdot h(n)$ for all $n$.
- $f(n) = g(n) \pm O(h(n))$: both $f(n) \leqslant g(n) + O(h(n))$ and $g(n) \leqslant f(n) + O(h(n))$.
- $f(n) \leqslant g(n) + o(h(n))$: $\lim_n \frac{f(n) - g(n)}{h(n)} = 0$.
- $f$ and $g$ are *asymptotically equal* ($f \sim g$) if $\lim_n \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 1$.

When we write $\log n$, we mean the base 2 logarithm of $n$, rounded up to the nearest larger integer.

**Lattices.** Let $(P, \leqslant)$ be a partial order. The *join* of $x$ and $y$ is denoted $x \vee y$; the *meet* is denoted $x \wedge y$. If every pair has a join, then $(P, \leqslant)$ is an *uppersemilattice*. If every pair has a meet, it is a *lowersemilattice*. If both, it is a *lattice*. An *ideal* in an uppersemilattice is a subset of $P$ that is closed downward and under joins.

### 1.2 Basics from Measure Theory

We study the Cantor space $2^\omega$ of infinite binary sequences. For $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ we denote by $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$ the set of all extensions of $\sigma$ in $2^\omega$, i.e., $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket = \lbrace \sigma X : X \in 2^\omega \rbrace$. For $A \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, we let $\llbracket A \rrbracket = \bigcup_{\sigma \in A} \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$. We say that $A$ **generates** (or is a *set of generators of*) $\llbracket A \rrbracket$.

The (sub-)basic open sets of Cantor space are $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$ for $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$. In this topology the clopen sets are finite unions of such basic open sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Measure on Cantor Space)</span></p>

The (uniform or Lebesgue) measure of a basic open set $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$ is $\mu(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) = 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$.

A sequence of basic open sets $\lbrace \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket : \sigma \in A \rbrace$ is said to **cover** a set $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ if $C \subseteq \llbracket A \rrbracket$. The **outer measure** of $C$ is

$$\mu^*(C) = \inf\left\lbrace \sum_n 2^{-\lvert\sigma_n\rvert} : \lbrace \llbracket\sigma_n\rrbracket : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace \text{ covers } C \right\rbrace.$$

The **inner measure** of $C$ is $\mu_*(C) = 1 - \mu^*(\overline{C})$, where $\overline{C}$ is the complement $2^\omega \setminus C$. If $C$ is measurable, then $\mu^*(C) = \mu_*(C)$, and we refer to this quantity as the **measure** $\mu(C)$ of $C$.

**Null sets** are those that have measure 0. A set $C$ has measure 0 iff there is a sequence of open covers $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of open sets such that $\lim_n \mu(V_n) = 0$ and $C \subseteq \bigcap_n V_n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1.2.1 — Borel-Cantelli Lemma)</span></p>

Let $\lbrace C_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a sequence of measurable sets such that $\sum_n \mu(C_n) < \infty$. Then $\mu(\lbrace X : \exists^\infty n\, (X \in C_n) \rbrace) = 0$.

*Proof.* Let $D = \lbrace X : \exists^\infty n\, (X \in C_n) \rbrace$. Choose $n_0, n_1, \ldots$ such that $\sum_{j \geqslant n_i} \mu(C_j) \leqslant 2^{-i}$. For each $i$, we have $D \subseteq \bigcup_{j \geqslant n_i} C_j$, so $D$ is a null set.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1.2.2 — Density)</span></p>

A measurable set $A$ has **density** $d$ at $X \in 2^\omega$ if

$$\lim_n 2^n \mu(A \cap \llbracket X \upharpoonright n \rrbracket) = d.$$

Let $D(A) = \lbrace X : A \text{ has density 1 at } X \rbrace$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1.2.3 — Lebesgue Density Theorem)</span></p>

If $A$ is a measurable set then so is $D(A)$. Furthermore, the measure of the symmetric difference of $A$ and $D(A)$ is 0, so $\mu(D(A)) = \mu(A)$.

Thus, if $\mu(A) > 0$ then for any $q < 1$ there is a $\sigma$ such that the relative measure $\frac{\mu(A \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket)}{\mu(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket)}$ is greater than $q$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Tailset)</span></p>

A **tailset** is a set $A \subseteq 2^\omega$ such that for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $X \in 2^\omega$, if $\sigma X \in A$ then $\tau X \in A$ for all $\tau$ with $\lvert\tau\rvert = \lvert\sigma\rvert$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1.2.4 — Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law)</span></p>

If $A$ is a measurable tailset, then either $\mu(A) = 0$ or $\mu(A) = 1$.

*Proof.* Suppose $\mu(A) > 0$. By the Lebesgue Density Theorem, choose $X \in A$ such that $A$ has density 1 at $X$. Since $A$ is a tailset, we know that $2^n \mu(A \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) > 1 - \varepsilon$ for all $\sigma$ of length $n$ (for sufficiently large $n$). Hence $\mu(A) > 1 - \varepsilon$. Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary, $\mu(A) = 1$.

</div>

## Chapter 2: Computability Theory

### 2.1 Computable Functions, Coding, and the Halting Problem

At the heart of our understanding of algorithmic randomness is the notion of an *algorithm*. The tools we use are based on classical computability theory.

Our initial concern is with functions from $A$ into $\mathbb{N}$ where $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, i.e., *partial* functions on $\mathbb{N}$. If $A = \mathbb{N}$ the function is called *total*. The domains and ranges of such functions can be coded as subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ via *coding*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.1.1 — Gödel Number)</span></p>

Let $r \in \mathbb{Q} \setminus \lbrace 0 \rbrace$ and write $r = (-1)^\delta \frac{p}{q}$ with $p, q \in \mathbb{N}$ in lowest terms and $\delta = 0$ or 1. Then define the **Gödel number** of $r$, denoted $\#(r)$, as $2^\delta 3^p 5^q$. Let the Gödel number of 0 be 0.

Similarly, if $\sigma = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ is a finite binary string, then $\#(\sigma) = 2^{a_1+1} 3^{a_2+1} \ldots (p_n)^{a_n+1}$, where $p_n$ denotes the $n$th prime.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Church-Turing Thesis)</span></p>

The celebrated **Church-Turing Thesis** states that *the algorithmic (i.e., intuitively computable) partial functions are exactly those that can be computed by Turing machines on the natural numbers*. We formally adopt the definition of algorithmic, or **computable**, functions as being those that are computable by Turing machines.

</div>

We refer to partial functions computable by Turing machines as **partial computable functions**.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.1.2 — Enumeration Theorem / Universal Turing Machine)</span></p>

There is an algorithmic way of enumerating all the partial computable functions. That is, there is a list $\Phi_0, \Phi_1, \ldots$ of all such functions such that we have an algorithmic procedure for passing from an index $i$ to a Turing machine computing $\Phi_i$, and vice versa. Using such a list, we can define a partial computable function $f(x, y)$ of two variables such that $f(x, y) = \Phi_x(y)$ for all $x, y$. Such a function, and any Turing machine that computes it, are called **universal**.

</div>

We write $\Phi_x(y) \downarrow$ to mean that $\Phi_x$ is defined on $y$ (i.e., the corresponding Turing machine halts on input $y$), and $\Phi_x(y) \uparrow$ to mean that $\Phi_x$ is not defined on $y$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.1.3 — Unsolvability of the Halting Problem)</span></p>

There is no algorithm that, given $x, y$, decides whether $\Phi_x(y) \downarrow$. Indeed, there is no algorithm to decide whether $\Phi_x(x) \downarrow$.

*Proof.* Suppose such an algorithm exists. Then by Proposition 2.1.2, the following function $g$ is (total) computable:

$$g(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Phi_x(x) \uparrow \\ \Phi_x(x) + 1 & \text{if } \Phi_x(x) \downarrow \end{cases}$$

By Proposition 2.1.2, there is a $y$ with $g = \Phi_y$. Since $g$ is total, $g(y) \downarrow$, so $\Phi_y(y) \downarrow$, and hence $g(y) = \Phi_y(y) + 1 = g(y) + 1$, a contradiction.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.1.4)</span></p>

There is no algorithm to decide whether the domain of $\Phi_x$ is empty.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.1.5 — The $s$-$m$-$n$ Theorem)</span></p>

Let $g(x, y)$ be a partial computable function of two variables. Then there is a computable function $s$ of one variable such that, for all $x, y$,

$$\Phi_{s(x)}(y) = g(x, y).$$

</div>

### 2.2 Computable Enumerability and Rice's Theorem

The halting problem can be coded by

$$\emptyset' = \lbrace x : \Phi_x(x) \downarrow \rbrace.$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.2.1 — Computably Enumerable and Computable Sets)</span></p>

A set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is called:

- **computably enumerable** (c.e.) if $A = \text{dom}(\Phi_e)$ for some $e$.
- **computable** if $A$ and $\overline{A} = \mathbb{N} \setminus A$ are both computably enumerable.

A set is *co-c.e.* if its complement is c.e. Thus a set is computable iff it is both c.e. and co-c.e.

</div>

We let $W_e$ denote the $e$th computably enumerable set, i.e., $\text{dom}(\Phi_e)$, and $W_e[s] = \lbrace x \leqslant s : \Phi_e(x)[s] \downarrow \rbrace$.

A family of sets $A_0, A_1, \ldots$ is **uniformly computably enumerable** if $A_n = W_{g(n)}$ for a computable $g$, equivalently if there is a c.e. set $A$ such that $A_n = \lbrace x : \langle n, x \rangle \in A \rbrace$. It is **uniformly computable** if the functions $\chi_{A_0}, \chi_{A_1}, \ldots$ are uniformly computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Simple and Immune Sets)</span></p>

One way to make a set $A$ noncomputable is by ensuring that $A$ is coinfinite and for all $e$, if $W_e$ is infinite then $A \cap W_e \neq \emptyset$. A c.e. set $A$ with these properties is called **simple**. An infinite set that contains no infinite c.e. set is called **immune**. Not all noncomputable c.e. sets are simple: the set $\lbrace 2n : n \in A \rbrace$ is also c.e. and noncomputable, but is not simple.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.2.2)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computably enumerable iff either $A = \emptyset$ or there is a total computable function $f$ from $\mathbb{N}$ onto $A$. (If $A$ is infinite then $f$ can be chosen to be injective.)

</div>

The halting set $\emptyset'$ is c.e. (by Proposition 2.1.3, it is an example of a c.e. set that is not computable). It is also a *complete* c.e. set, in the sense that for any c.e. set $A$, there is an algorithm for computing $A$ using $\emptyset'$.

If $A$ is c.e., then it has a **computable approximation** $\lbrace A_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ of sets such that $A(n) = \lim_s A_s(n)$ for all $n$. We can choose $A_s$ so that $A_0 \subseteq A_1 \subseteq A_2 \subseteq \cdots$.

An **index set** is a set $A$ such that if $x \in A$ and $\Phi_x = \Phi_y$ then $y \in A$. An index set can be thought of as coding a problem about computable functions whose answer does not depend on the particular algorithm used. For example, $\lbrace x : \text{dom}(\Phi_x) = \emptyset \rbrace$ is an index set.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.2.3 — Rice's Theorem)</span></p>

An index set $A$ is computable (and so the problem it codes is decidable) iff $A = \mathbb{N}$ or $A = \emptyset$.

*Proof.* Let $A \notin \lbrace \emptyset, \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ be an index set. Let $e$ be such that $\text{dom}(\Phi_e) = \emptyset$. We may assume w.l.o.g. that $e \in \overline{A}$. Fix $i \in A$. By the $s$-$m$-$n$ theorem, there is a computable $s(x)$ such that for all $y \in \mathbb{N}$:

$$\Phi_{s(x)}(y) = \begin{cases} \Phi_i(y) & \text{if } \Phi_x(x) \downarrow \\ \uparrow & \text{if } \Phi_x(x) \uparrow \end{cases}$$

If $\Phi_x(x) \downarrow$ then $\Phi_{s(x)} = \Phi_i$ and so $s(x) \in A$; if $\Phi_x(x) \uparrow$ then $\Phi_{s(x)} = \Phi_e$ and so $s(x) \notin A$. Thus, if $A$ were computable, $\emptyset'$ would also be computable.

</div>

### 2.3 The Recursion Theorem

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.3.1 — Recursion Theorem, Kleene)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a total computable function. Then there is a number $n$, called a **fixed point** of $f$, such that

$$\Phi_n = \Phi_{f(n)}$$

and hence $W_n = W_{f(n)}$. Furthermore, such an $n$ can be computed from an index for $f$.

*Proof.* Define a total computable function $d$ via the $s$-$m$-$n$ Theorem so that

$$\Phi_{d(e)}(k) = \begin{cases} \Phi_{\Phi_e(e)}(k) & \text{if } \Phi_e(e) \downarrow \\ \uparrow & \text{if } \Phi_e(e) \uparrow \end{cases}$$

Let $i$ be such that $\Phi_i = f \circ d$. Notice that $\Phi_i$ is total. Let $n = d(i)$. Then

$$\Phi_n = \Phi_{d(i)} = \Phi_{\Phi_i(i)} = \Phi_{f \circ d(i)} = \Phi_{f(n)}.$$

</div>

The Recursion Theorem allows us to use an index for a computable function or c.e. set that we are building in a construction as part of that very construction — it is the theoretical underpinning of recursion.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.3.2 — Recursion Theorem with Parameters, Kleene)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a total computable function of two variables. Then there is a total computable function $h$ such that $\Phi_{h(y)} = \Phi_{f(h(y), y)}$, and hence $W_{h(y)} = W_{f(h(y), y)}$, for all $y$. Furthermore, an index for $h$ can be obtained effectively from an index for $f$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.3.3 — Slowdown Lemma)</span></p>

Let $\lbrace U_{e,s} \rbrace_{e,s \in \omega}$ be a computable sequence of finite sets such that $U_{e,s} \subseteq U_{e,s+1}$ for all $e$ and $s$. Let $U_e = \bigcup_s U_{e,s}$. There is a computable function $g$ such that for all $e, s, n$, we have $W_{g(e)} = U_e$ and if $n \notin U_{e,s}$ then $n \notin W_{g(e),s+1}$.

</div>

### 2.4 Reductions

The key concept in Rice's Theorem proof is that of **reduction**: the idea that "if we can do $B$ then this ability also allows us to do $A$". We use this to define partial orderings called *reducibilities* that calibrate problems according to computational difficulty.

#### 2.4.1 Oracle Machines and Turing Reducibility

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Oracle Turing Machine and Turing Reducibility)</span></p>

An **oracle (Turing) machine** is a Turing machine with an extra infinite read-only *oracle tape*, which it can access one bit at a time while performing its computation. If there is an oracle machine $M$ that computes the set $A$ when its oracle tape codes the set $B$, then we say that $A$ is **Turing reducible** to $B$, or **$B$-computable**, or **computable in** $B$, and write $A \leqslant_T B$.

</div>

We write $\Phi^A$ for the function computed by $\Phi$ with oracle $A$. The analog of Proposition 2.1.2 holds for oracle machines: there is a **universal oracle (Turing) machine** $\Phi$ such that $\Phi^A(x, y) = \Phi^A_e(y)$ for all $x, y$ and all oracles $A$.

Turing reducibility is transitive and reflexive, and thus is a preordering on the subsets of $\mathbb{N}$. The equivalence classes of the form $\text{deg}(A) = \lbrace B : B \equiv_T A \rbrace$ are called **Turing degrees** (or *degrees of unsolvability*), often written as **a** for a degree.

The Turing degrees form an uppersemilattice. The **join** operation is $A \oplus B = \lbrace 2n : n \in A \rbrace \cup \lbrace 2n + 1 : n \in B \rbrace$. Clearly $A, B \leqslant_T A \oplus B$, and if $A, B \leqslant_T C$, then $A \oplus B \leqslant_T C$. Kleene and Post showed that the Turing degrees are *not* a lattice.

We let **0** denote the degree of the computable sets. Each degree is countable and has only countably many predecessors, so there are continuum many degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Use of a Computation)</span></p>

The **use** of a converging oracle computation $\Phi^A(n)$ is $x + 1$ for the largest number $x$ such that the value of $A(x)$ is queried during the computation. We denote this use by $\varphi^A(n)$. Conventions on use functions:

1. The use function is strictly increasing where defined: $\varphi^A(m) < \varphi^A(n)$ for all $m < n$.
2. When $A$ is being approximated, $\varphi^A(n)[s] \leqslant \varphi^A(n)[t]$ for all $n$ and $s < t$.
3. $\varphi^A(n)[s] \leqslant s$ for all $n$ and $s$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.1 — Use Principle)</span></p>

Let $\Phi^A(n)$ be a converging oracle computation, and let $B$ be a set such that $B \upharpoonright \varphi^A(n) = A \upharpoonright \varphi^A(n)$. Then $\Phi^B(n) = \Phi^A(n)$.

*Proof.* The sets $A$ and $B$ give the same answers to all questions asked during the relevant computations, so the results must be the same.

</div>

**Relativization.** For a set $A$, the **halting problem relative to $A$** is

$$A' = \lbrace e : \Phi^A_e(e) \downarrow \rbrace.$$

The general process of extending a definition or result to the oracle case is known as **relativization**. For instance, Proposition 2.1.3 can be relativized to show that $A' \nleqslant_T A$ for all $A$.

#### 2.4.2 The Jump Operator and Jump Classes

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.2 — Jump Classes)</span></p>

The function $A \mapsto A'$ is called the *(Turing) jump* of $A$. The *$n$th jump* of $A$, written $A^{(n)}$, is the result of applying the jump operator $n$ times. We write **a'** for $\text{deg}(A')$, and similarly for the $n$th jump notation. We have $\mathbf{0} < \mathbf{0'} < \mathbf{0''} < \cdots$

A set $A$ is **low**$_n$ if $A^{(n)} \equiv_T \emptyset^{(n)}$. The low$_1$ sets are called **low**.

A set $A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$ is **high**$_n$ if $A^{(n)} \equiv_T \emptyset^{(n+1)}$. The high$_1$ sets are called **high**. More generally, an arbitrary set $A$ is called high if $A' \geqslant_T \emptyset''$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.3 — Generalized Low and High)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **generalized low**$_n$ ($\text{GL}_n$) if $A^{(n)} \equiv_T (A \oplus \emptyset')^{(n-1)}$.

A set $A$ is **generalized high**$_n$ ($\text{GH}_n$) if $A^{(n)} \equiv_T (A \oplus \emptyset')^{(n)}$.

Note that if $A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$, then $A$ is $\text{GL}_n$ iff it is low$_n$, and $\text{GH}_n$ iff it is high$_n$.

</div>

#### 2.4.3 Strong Reducibilities

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.4 — Many-One and 1-Reducibility)</span></p>

$A$ is **many-one reducible** ($m$-reducible) to $B$, written $A \leqslant_m B$, if there is a total computable function $f$ such that for all $x$, we have $x \in A$ iff $f(x) \in B$.

If the function $f$ in the definition of m-reducibility is injective, then $A$ is **1-reducible** to $B$, written $A \leqslant_1 B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.5)</span></p>

If $A$ is c.e. then $A \leqslant_1 \emptyset'$.

</div>

Note that $\emptyset'$ is $m$-complete, and even 1-complete. The m-reducibility strictly refines Turing reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.6 — Truth Table Reducibility)</span></p>

A **truth table** on the variables $v_1, v_2, \ldots$ is a (finite) boolean combination $\sigma$ of these variables. We write $A \vDash \sigma$ if $\sigma$ holds with $v_n$ interpreted as $n \in A$. Let $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ be an effective listing of all truth tables.

$A$ is **truth table reducible** to $B$, written $A \leqslant_{tt} B$, if there is a computable function $f$ such that for all $x$,

$$x \in A \iff B \vDash \sigma_{f(x)}.$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.7 — Nerode)</span></p>

A Turing reduction $\Phi$ is a truth table reduction iff $\Phi^X$ is total for all oracles $X$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.8 — Superlow)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **superlow** if $A' \equiv_{tt} \emptyset'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.9 — Weak Truth Table Reducibility)</span></p>

$A$ is **weak truth table reducible** ($wtt$-reducible) to $B$, written $A \leqslant_{wtt} B$, if there are a computable function $f$ and an oracle Turing machine $\Phi$ such that $\Phi^B = A$ and $\varphi^B(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$.

</div>

The reducibilities calibrate sets into degrees of greater and greater fineness, in the order T, wtt, tt, m, 1.

#### 2.4.4 Myhill's Theorem

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.10 — Myhill Isomorphism Theorem)</span></p>

$A \equiv_1 B$ iff there is a computable permutation $h$ of $\mathbb{N}$ such that $h(A) = B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.11 — Productive and Creative Sets)</span></p>

A set $B$ is **productive** if there is a partial computable function $h$ such that if $W_e \subseteq B$ then $h(e) \downarrow \in B \setminus W_e$.

A c.e. set $A$ is **creative** if $\overline{A}$ is productive.

The halting problem is an example of a creative set, since $\overline{\emptyset'}$ is productive via the identity function.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.4.12 — Myhill's Theorem)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a set $A$:

1. $A$ is creative.
2. $A$ is $m$-complete (i.e., $m$-equivalent to $\emptyset'$).
3. $A$ is 1-complete (i.e., 1-equivalent to $\emptyset'$).
4. $A$ is equivalent to $\emptyset'$ up to a computable permutation of $\mathbb{N}$.

</div>

### 2.5 The Arithmetic Hierarchy

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Arithmetic Hierarchy)</span></p>

We define the notions of $\Sigma^0_n$, $\Pi^0_n$, and $\Delta^0_n$ sets as follows. A set $A$ is $\Sigma^0_n$ if there is a computable relation $R(x_1, \ldots, x_n, y)$ such that $y \in A$ iff

$$\underbrace{\exists x_1 \forall x_2 \exists x_3 \forall x_4 \cdots Q_n x_n}_{n \text{ alternating quantifiers}} R(x_1, \ldots, x_n, y).$$

Since the quantifiers alternate, $Q_n$ is $\exists$ if $n$ is odd and $\forall$ if $n$ is even.

The definition of $A$ being $\Pi^0_n$ is the same, except that the leading quantifier is $\forall$ (but there are still $n$ alternating quantifiers in total). It is easy to see that $A$ is $\Pi^0_n$ iff $\overline{A}$ is $\Sigma^0_n$.

Finally, $A$ is $\Delta^0_n$ if it is both $\Sigma^0_n$ and $\Pi^0_n$ (equivalently, if both it and its complement are $\Sigma^0_n$). Note that the $\Delta^0_0$, $\Pi^0_0$, and $\Sigma^0_0$ sets are all exactly the computable sets.

</div>

A set is **arithmetic** if it is in one of the levels of the arithmetic hierarchy.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.5.1 — Kleene)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computably enumerable iff $A$ is $\Sigma^0_1$.

*Proof.* Suppose $A$ is c.e. Then $A = \text{dom}(\Phi_e)$ for some $e$, so $n \in A$ iff $\exists s\, \Phi_e(y)[s] \downarrow$. Thus $A$ is $\Sigma^0_1$. Conversely, if $A$ is $\Sigma^0_1$ then for some computable $R$ we have $n \in A$ iff $\exists x\, R(x, n)$. Define a partial computable function $g$ by letting $g(n) = 1$ at stage $s$ iff $s \geqslant n$ and there is an $x < s$ such that $R(x, n)$ holds. Then $n \in \text{dom}(g)$ iff $n \in A$, so $A$ is c.e.

</div>

### 2.6 The Limit Lemma and Post's Theorem

Saying that a set $A$ is c.e. can be thought of as saying that $A$ has a computable approximation that, for each $n$, starts out by saying that $n \notin A$, and then changes its mind at most once. More precisely, there is a computable binary function $f$ such that for all $n$ we have $A(n) = \lim_s f(n, s)$, with $f(n, 0) = 0$ and $f(n, s + 1) \neq f(n, s)$ for at most one $s$. Generalizing this idea, the limit lemma characterizes the sets computable from the halting problem as those that have computable approximations with *finitely many* mind changes, and hence are "effectively approximable". In other words, the sets computable from $\emptyset'$ are exactly those that have computable approximations, as defined in Section 2.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.6.1 — Limit Lemma, Shoenfield)</span></p>

For a set $A$, we have $A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$ iff there is a computable binary function $g$ such that, for all $n$,

(i) $\lim_s g(n, s)$ exists (i.e., $\lvert\lbrace s : g(n, s) \neq g(n, s + 1)\rbrace\rvert < \infty$), and

(ii) $A(n) = \lim_s g(n, s)$.

*Proof.* ($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $A = \Phi^{\emptyset'}$. Define $g$ by letting $g(n, s) = 0$ if either $\Phi^{\emptyset'}[s] \!\!\upharpoonright$ or $\Phi^{\emptyset'}[s] \!\!\downarrow \neq 1$, and letting $g(n, s) = 1$ otherwise. Fix $n$, and let $s$ be a stage such that $\emptyset'_s \upharpoonright \varphi^{\emptyset'}(n) = \emptyset' \upharpoonright \varphi^{\emptyset'}(n)$ for all $t \geqslant s$. By the use principle (Proposition 2.4.1), $g(n, t) = \Phi^{\emptyset'}(n)[t] = \Phi^{\emptyset'}(n) = A(n)$ for all $t \geqslant s$. Thus $g$ has the required properties.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose such a function $g$ exists. Without loss of generality, we may assume that $g(n, 0) = 0$ for all $n$. To show that $A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$, it is enough to build a c.e. set $B$ such that $A \leqslant_T B$, since by Proposition 2.4.5, every c.e. set is computable in $\emptyset'$. We put $\langle n, k \rangle$ into $B$ whenever we find that

$$\lvert\lbrace s : g(n, s) \neq g(n, s + 1)\rbrace\rvert \geqslant k.$$

Now define a Turing reduction $\Gamma$ as follows. Given an oracle $X$, on input $n$, search for the least $k$ such that $\langle n, k \rangle \notin X$, and if one is found, then output 0 if $k$ is even and 1 if $k$ is odd. Clearly, $\Gamma^B = A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.6.2)</span></p>

For a set $A$, the following are equivalent.

(i) $A \leqslant_{tt} \emptyset'$.

(ii) $A \leqslant_{wtt} \emptyset'$.

(iii) There is a computable binary function $g$ and a computable function $h$ such that, for all $n$,

&nbsp;&nbsp;(a) $\lvert\lbrace s : g(n, s) \neq g(n, s + 1)\rbrace\rvert < h(n)$, and

&nbsp;&nbsp;(b) $A(n) = \lim_s g(n, s)$.

*Proof.* (i) implies (ii) is clear. (ii) implies (iii) follows from the "if" direction of Theorem 2.6.1, together with the remark that if $\Phi$ is a wtt-reduction then we can computably bound the number of times the value of $\Phi^{\emptyset'}[s]$ can change as $s$ increases. (iii) implies (i) follows from the "only if" direction of Theorem 2.6.1, except that we can now make $\Gamma$ into a tt-reduction because we have to check whether $\langle n, k \rangle \notin X$ only for $k < h(n)$.

</div>

Sets with the properties given in Corollary 2.6.2 are called $\omega$-c.e., and will be further discussed in Section 2.7.

The limit lemma relativizes to show that $A \leqslant_T B'$ iff there is a $B$-computable binary function $f$ such that $A(n) = \lim_s f(n, s)$ for all $n$. Combining this fact with induction, we have the following generalization.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.6.3 — Limit Lemma, Strong Form, Shoenfield)</span></p>

Let $k \geqslant 1$. For a set $A$, we have $A \leqslant_T \emptyset^{(k)}$ iff there is a computable function $g$ of $k + 1$ variables such that $A(n) = \lim_{s_1} \lim_{s_2} \ldots \lim_{s_k} g(n, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k)$ for all $n$.

</div>

We now turn to Post's characterization of the levels of the arithmetic hierarchy.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.6.4 — Post's Theorem)</span></p>

Let $n \geqslant 0$.

(i) A set $B$ is $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $B$ is c.e. in some $\Sigma^0_n$ set $\Leftrightarrow$ $B$ is c.e. in some $\Pi^0_n$ set.

(ii) The set $\emptyset^{(n)}$ is $\Sigma^0_n$ $m$-complete.

(iii) A set $B$ is $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ iff $B$ is c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n)}$.

(iv) A set $B$ is $\Delta^0_{n+1}$ iff $B \leqslant_T \emptyset^{(n)}$.

*Proof sketch.* (i) is shown by relativizing the proof that a set is c.e. iff it is $\Sigma^0_1$. (ii) is proved by induction: $\emptyset'$ is $\Sigma^0_1$ $m$-complete, and then one shows that $\emptyset^{(n+1)}$ is c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n)}$, hence $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$. By part (i), for any $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ set $C$, one can define a computable function $f$ such that $n \in C$ iff $f(n) \in \emptyset^{(n+1)}$. (iii) follows directly from (i) and (ii). (iv) follows from (ii) and (iii) by noting that $B$ is $\Delta^0_{n+1}$ iff $B$ and $\overline{B}$ are both $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$, and hence both c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n)}$, which is equivalent to $B \leqslant_T \emptyset^{(n)}$.

</div>

Note in particular that the $\Delta^0_2$ sets are exactly the $\emptyset'$-computable sets, that is, the sets that have computable approximations.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.6.5)</span></p>

(i) $\text{Fin} = \lbrace e : W_e \text{ is finite}\rbrace$ is $\Sigma^0_2$ $m$-complete.

(ii) $\text{Tot} = \lbrace e : \Phi_e \text{ is total}\rbrace$ and $\text{Inf} = \lbrace e : W_e \text{ is infinite}\rbrace$ are both $\Pi^0_2$ $m$-complete.

(iii) $\text{Cof} = \lbrace e : W_e \text{ is cofinite}\rbrace$ is $\Sigma^0_3$ $m$-complete.

*Proof sketch.* For (i), $\emptyset''$ is $\Sigma^0_2$ $m$-complete by Post's Theorem, so it suffices to $m$-reduce $\emptyset''$ to Fin. Using the $s$-$m$-$n$ theorem, one defines a computable function $f$ such that $f(e) \in \text{Fin}$ iff $\Phi^{\emptyset'}_e(e) \!\!\downarrow$ iff $e \in \emptyset''$. Parts (ii) and (iii) are similar but more intricate.

</div>

### 2.7 The Difference Hierarchy

The arithmetic hierarchy gives us one way to extend the concept of computable enumerability. Another way to do so is via the *difference hierarchy*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.7.1 — $n$-Computably Enumerable Sets)</span></p>

Let $n \geqslant 1$. A set $A$ is *$n$-computably enumerable* ($n$-c.e.) if there is a computable binary function $f$ such that for all $x$,

(i) $f(x, 0) = 0$,

(ii) $A(x) = \lim_s f(x, s)$, and

(iii) $\lvert\lbrace s : f(x, s + 1) \neq f(x, s)\rbrace\rvert \leqslant n$.

</div>

Thus the 1-c.e. sets are simply the c.e. sets. The 2-c.e. sets are often called *d.c.e.*, which stands for "difference of c.e.", because of the easily proved fact that $A$ is 2-c.e. iff there are c.e. sets $B$ and $C$ such that $A = B \setminus C$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.7.2 — $\omega$-c.e. Sets)</span></p>

A set $A$ is $\omega$-c.e. if there are a computable binary function $f$ and a computable unary function $g$ such that for all $x$,

(i) $f(x, 0) = 0$,

(ii) $A(x) = \lim_s f(x, s)$, and

(iii) $\lvert\lbrace s : f(x, s + 1) \neq f(x, s)\rbrace\rvert \leqslant g(x)$.

</div>

In Corollary 2.6.2, we saw that the $\omega$-c.e. sets are exactly those that are (w)tt-reducible to $\emptyset'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.7.3 — Arslanov)</span></p>

If $A$ is $\omega$-c.e. then there is a $B \equiv_m A$ and a computable binary function $h$ such that for all $x$,

(i) $h(x, 0) = 0$,

(ii) $B(x) = \lim_s h(x, s)$, and

(iii) $\lvert\lbrace s : h(x, s + 1) \neq h(x, s)\rbrace\rvert \leqslant x$.

*Proof.* Let $g$ be as in Definition 2.7.2. Without loss of generality, we may assume that $g$ is increasing. Let $B = \lbrace g(x) : x \in A \rbrace$. Then $B$ clearly has the desired properties.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.7.4 — $\alpha$-c.e. Sets)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a computable ordinal. A set $A$ is $\alpha$-c.e. relative to a computable system $\mathcal{S}$ of notations for $\alpha$ if there is a partial computable function $f$ such that for all $x$, we have $A(x) = f(x, b)$ for the $\mathcal{S}$-least notation $b$ such that $f(x, b)$ converges.

</div>

It is not hard to check that this definition agrees with the previous ones for $\alpha \leqslant \omega$, independently of the system of notations chosen. As in the c.e. case, we say that a degree is $n$-c.e. if it contains an $n$-c.e. set, and similarly for $\omega$-c.e. and $\alpha$-c.e. degrees.

### 2.8 Primitive Recursive Functions

The class of *primitive recursive* functions is the smallest class of functions satisfying the following properties (where a 0-ary function is just a natural number).

(i) The function $n \mapsto n + 1$ is primitive recursive.

(ii) For each $k$ and $m$, the function $(n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}) \mapsto m$ is primitive recursive.

(iii) For each $k$ and $i < k$, the function $(n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}) \mapsto n_i$ is primitive recursive.

(iv) If $f$ and $g_0, \ldots, g_{k-1}$ are primitive recursive, $f$ is $k$-ary, and the $g_i$ are all $j$-ary, then

$$(n_0, \ldots, n_{j-1}) \mapsto f(g_0(n_0, \ldots, n_{j-1}), \ldots, g_{k-1}(n_0, \ldots, n_{j-1}))$$

is primitive recursive.

(v) If the $k$-ary function $g$ and the $(k+2)$-ary function $h$ are primitive recursive, then so is the function $f$ defined by

$$f(0, n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}) = g(n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1})$$

and

$$f(i+1, n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}) = h(n, f(i, n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}), n_0, \ldots, n_{k-1}).$$

Every primitive recursive function is computable. However, the primitive recursive functions are total and can be effectively listed, so there are computable functions that are not primitive recursive. A famous example is *Ackermann's function*

$$A(m, n) = \begin{cases} n + 1 & \text{if } m = 0 \\\\ A(m - 1, 1) & \text{if } m > 0 \wedge n = 0 \\\\ A(m - 1, A(m, n - 1)) & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

Many natural computable functions are primitive recursive though, and it is sometimes useful to work with an effectively listable class of total computable functions, so we will use primitive recursive functions in a few places below.

### 2.9 A Note on Reductions

There are several ways to describe a reduction procedure. Formally, $A \leqslant_T B$ means that there is an $e$ such that $\Phi^B_e = A$. In practice, though, we never actually build an oracle Turing machine to witness the fact that $A \leqslant_T B$, but avail ourselves of the Church-Turing Thesis to informally describe a reduction $\Gamma$ such that $\Gamma^B = A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Static vs. Dynamic Reductions)</span></p>

One description of a reduction is given in the proof of the $\Leftarrow$ direction of Shoenfield's Limit Lemma (Theorem 2.6.1). This proof gives an example of a *static* definition of a reduction procedure, in that the action of $\Gamma$ is specified by a rule, rather than being defined during a construction. As an example of a *dynamic* definition of a reduction procedure, we reprove the $\Leftarrow$ direction of the limit lemma.

Recall that we are given a computable binary function $g$ such that, for all $n$, $\lim_s g(n, s)$ exists and $A(n) = \lim_s g(n, s)$.

We wish to show that $A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$, by building a c.e. set $B$ and a reduction $\Gamma^B = A$.

We simultaneously construct $B$ and $\Gamma$ in stages. We begin with $B_0 = \emptyset$. At stage $n$, we let $\Gamma^B(n)[n] = g(n, n)$ with use $\gamma^B(n)[n] = \langle n, 0 \rangle + 1$. Furthermore, at stage $s > 0$ we proceed as follows for each $n < s$. If $g(n, s) = g(n, s - 1)$, then we change nothing. Otherwise, we enumerate $\gamma(n, s - 1) - 1$ into $B$, which allows us to redefine $\Gamma^B(n)[s] = g(n, s)$, with use $\gamma^B(n)[s] = \langle n, k \rangle + 1$ for the least $\langle n, k \rangle \notin B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Rules for Building a Reduction $\Delta^C$)</span></p>

More generally, the rules for a reduction $\Delta^C$ to a c.e. set $C$ are as follows, for each input $n$.

1. Initially $\Delta^C(n)[0] \!\!\uparrow$.

2. At some stage $s$ we must define $\Delta^C(n)[s] \!\!\downarrow = i$ for some value $i$, with some use $\delta^C(n)[s]$. By this action, we are promising that $\Delta^C(n) = i$ unless $C \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[s] \neq C_s \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[s]$.

3. The convention now is that $\delta^C(n)[t] = \delta^C(n)[s]$ for $t > s$ unless $C_t \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[s] \neq C_s \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[t]$. Should we find a stage $t > s$ such that $C_t \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[s] \neq C_s \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[t]$, we then again have $\Delta^C(n)[t] \!\!\uparrow$.

4. We now again must have a stage $u \geqslant t$ at which we define $\Delta^C(n)[u] \!\!\downarrow = j$ for some value $j$, with some use $\delta^C(n)[u]$. We then return to step 3, with $u$ in place of $s$.

5. If $\Delta^C$ is to be total, we have to ensure that we stay at step 3 permanently from some point on. That is, there must be a stage $u$ at which we define $\Delta^C(n)[u]$ and $\delta^C(n)[u]$, such that $C_u \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[u] = C \upharpoonright \delta^C(n)[u]$. One way to achieve this is to ensure that, from some point on, whenever we redefine $\delta^C(n)[u]$, we set it to the same value.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Reductions as Continuous Maps)</span></p>

A more formal view of a reduction is as a partial computable map from strings to strings obeying certain continuity conditions. In this view, a reduction $\Gamma^B = A$ is specified by a partial computable function $f : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ such that

1. if $\sigma \prec B$, then $f(\sigma) \prec A$;

2. for all $\sigma \prec \tau$, if both $f(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow$ and $f(\tau) \!\!\downarrow$, then $f(\sigma) \preccurlyeq f(\tau)$; and

3. for all $\tau \prec A$ there is a $\sigma \prec B$ such that $\tau \prec f(\sigma)$.

This last method implies the following interesting observation: A function $f : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ is continuous iff it is computable relative to some oracle.

</div>

### 2.10 The Finite Extension Method

In this section, we introduce one of the main techniques used in classical degree theory. We will refine this technique in the next section to what is known as the finite injury priority method.

The dynamic construction of a reduction from $B$ to $A$ in the limit lemma, discussed in the previous section, has much in common with many proofs in classical computability theory. We perform a construction where some object is built in stages. Typically, we have some overall goal that we break down into smaller subgoals that we argue are all met in the limit. In this case, the goal is to construct the reduction $\Gamma^B = A$. We break this goal into the subgoals of defining $\Gamma^B(n)$ for each $n$, and we accomplish these subgoals by using the information supplied by our "opponent", who is feeding us information about the universe, in this case the values $g(n, s)$.

As an archetype for such proofs, think of Cantor's proof that the collection of all infinite binary sequences is uncountable. One can conceive of this proof as follows. Suppose we could list the infinite binary sequences as $\mathcal{S} = \lbrace S_0, S_1, \ldots \rbrace$, with $S_e = s_{e,0} s_{e,1} \ldots$ It is our goal to construct a binary sequence $U = u_0 u_1 \ldots$ that is not on the list $\mathcal{S}$. We think of the construction as a game against our opponent who must supply us with $\mathcal{S}$. We construct $u$ in stages, at stage $t$ specifying only $u_0 \ldots u_t$, the initial segment of $U$ of length $t + 1$. Our list of *requirements* is the decomposition of the overall goal into subgoals of the form

$$\mathcal{R}_e : U \neq S_e.$$

At stage $e$, we simply ensure that $u_e \neq s_{e,e}$. This action ensures that $U \neq S_e$ for all $e$; in other words, all the requirements are met.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Post's Problem — Question 2.10.1)</span></p>

**Post's Problem.** Does there exist a computably enumerable degree $\mathbf{a}$ with $\mathbf{0} < \mathbf{a} < \mathbf{0}'$?

In 1944, Post observed that all computably enumerable problems known at the time were either computable or of Turing degree $\mathbf{0}'$. He asked the above question.

</div>

Post's Problem was finally given a positive answer by Friedberg and Muchnik, using a new and ingenious method called the priority method. This method was an effectivization of an earlier method discovered by Kleene and Post. The latter is called the finite extension method, and was used to prove the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.10.2 — Kleene and Post)</span></p>

There are degrees $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, both below $\mathbf{0}'$, such that $\mathbf{a} \mid \mathbf{b}$. In other words, there are $\emptyset'$-computable sets that are incomparable under Turing reducibility.

*Proof.* We construct $A = \lim_s A_s$ and $B = \lim_s B_s$ in stages, to meet the following requirements for all $e \in \mathbb{N}$.

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e} : \Phi^A_e \neq B.$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e+1} : \Phi^B_e \neq A.$$

Note that if $A \leqslant_T B$ then there must be some procedure $\Phi_e$ with $\Phi^B_e = A$. Hence, if we meet all our requirements then $A \nleqslant_T B$, and similarly $B \nleqslant_T A$, so that $A$ and $B$ have incomparable Turing degrees. The fact that $A, B \leqslant_T \emptyset'$ will come from the construction and will be observed at the end.

The argument is by finite extensions, in the sense that at each stage $s$ we specify a finite portion $A_s$ of $A$ and a finite portion $B_s$ of $B$. These finite portions $A_s$ and $B_s$ will be specified as binary strings. The key invariant that we need to maintain throughout the construction is that $A_s \preccurlyeq A_u$ and $B_s \preccurlyeq B_u$ for all stages $u \geqslant s$. Thus, after stage $s$ we can only *extend* the portions of $A$ and $B$ that we have specified by stage $s$, which is a hallmark of the finite extension method.

**Construction.**

*Stage 0.* Let $A_0 = B_0 = \lambda$ (the empty string).

*Stage $2e + 1$.* (Attend to $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$.) We will have specified $A_{2e}$ and $B_{2e}$ at stage $2e$. Pick some number $x$, called a *witness*, with $x \geqslant \lvert B_{2e}\rvert$, and ask whether there is a string $\sigma$ properly extending $A_{2e}$ such that $\Phi^\sigma_e(x) \!\!\downarrow$.

If such a $\sigma$ exists, then let $A_{2e+1}$ be the length-lexicographically least such $\sigma$. Let $B_{2e+1}$ be the string of length $x + 1$ extending $B_{2e}$ such that $B_{2e+1}(n) = 0$ for all $n$ with $\lvert B_{2e}\rvert \leqslant n < x$ and $B_{2e+1}(x) = 1 - \Phi^\sigma_e(x)$.

If no such $\sigma$ exists, then let $A_{2e+1} = A_{2e} 0$ and $B_{2e+1} = B_{2e} 0$.

*Stage $2e + 2$.* (Attend to $\mathcal{R}_{2e+1}$.) Define $A_{2e+2}$ and $B_{2e+2}$ by proceeding in the same way as at stage $2e + 1$, but with the roles of $A$ and $B$ reversed.

**End of Construction.**

**Verification.** First note that $A_0 \prec A_1 \prec \cdots$ and $B_0 \prec B_1 \prec \cdots$, so $A$ and $B$ are well-defined.

We show that we meet the requirement $\mathcal{R}_n$ at stage $n + 1$; in fact, we show this for $n = 2e$ (the case where $n$ is odd being completely analogous). At stage $n + 1$, there are two cases to consider. Let $x$ be as defined at that stage.

If there is a $\sigma$ properly extending $A_n$ with $\Phi^\sigma_e(x) \!\!\downarrow$, then our action is to adopt such a $\sigma$ as $A_{n+1}$ and define $B_{n+1}$ so that $\Phi^{A_{n+1}}_e(x) \neq B_{n+1}(x)$. Since $A$ extends $A_{n+1}$ and $\Phi^{A_{n+1}}_e(x) \!\!\downarrow$, it follows that $A$ and $A_{n+1}$ agree on the use of this computation, and hence $\Phi^A_e(x) = \Phi^{A_{n+1}}_e(x)$. Since $B$ extends $B_{n+1}$, we also have $B(x) = B_{n+1}(x)$. Thus $\Phi^A_e(x) \neq B(x)$, and hence $\mathcal{R}_n$ is met.

If there is no $\sigma$ extending $A_n$ with $\Phi^\sigma_e(x) \!\!\downarrow$, then since $A$ is an extension of $A_n$, it must be the case that $\Phi^A(x) \!\!\uparrow$, and hence $\mathcal{R}_n$ is again met.

Finally we argue that $A, B \leqslant_T \emptyset'$. Notice that the construction is in fact fully computable except for the decision as to which case we are in at a given stage. There we must decide whether there is a convergent computation of a particular kind. For instance, at stage $2e + 1$ we must decide whether the following holds:

$$\exists \tau\, \exists s\, [\tau \succ A_{2e} \wedge \Phi^\tau_e(x)[s] \!\!\downarrow].$$

This is a $\Sigma^0_1$ question, uniformly in $x$, and hence can be decided by $\emptyset'$.

</div>

A key ingredient of the proof of Theorem 2.10.2 is the use principle (Proposition 2.4.1). In constructions of this sort, where we build objects to defeat certain oracle computations, a typical requirement will say something like "the reduction $\Gamma$ is not a witness to $A \leqslant_T B$." If we have a converging computation $\Gamma^B(n)[s] \neq A(n)[s]$ and we "preserve the use" of this computation by not changing $B$ after stage $s$ on the use $\gamma^B(n)[s]$ (and similarly preserve $A(n)$), then we will preserve this disagreement. But this use corresponds to only a finite portion of $B$, so we still have all the numbers bigger than it to meet other requirements.

### 2.11 Post's Problem and the Finite Injury Priority Method

A more subtle technique than the finite extension method is the *priority method*. We begin by looking at the simplest incarnation of this elegant technique, the *finite injury priority method*. This method is somewhat like the finite extension method, but with backtracking.

The idea behind it is the following. Suppose we must again satisfy requirements $\mathcal{R}_0, \mathcal{R}_1, \ldots$, but this time we are constrained to some sort of effective construction, so we are not allowed to ask questions of a noncomputable oracle during the construction. As an illustration, let us reconsider Post's Problem (Question 2.10.1). Post's Problem asks us to find a c.e. degree strictly between $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{0}'$. It is clearly enough to construct c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ with incomparable Turing degrees. The Kleene-Post method does allow us to construct sets with incomparable degrees below $\mathbf{0}'$, using a $\emptyset'$ oracle question at each stage, but there is no reason to expect these sets to be computably enumerable. To make $A$ and $B$ c.e., we must have a *computable* (rather than merely $\emptyset'$-computable) construction where elements go into the sets $A$ and $B$ but never leave them.

The key idea, discovered independently by Friedberg and Muchnik, is to pursue multiple strategies for each requirement, in the following sense. We put a *priority ordering* on our requirements, by stating that $\mathcal{R}_j$ has stronger priority than $\mathcal{R}_i$ if $j < i$, and allow $\mathcal{R}_j$ to injure $\mathcal{R}_i$ only if $\mathcal{R}_j$ has stronger priority than $\mathcal{R}_i$. Thus $\mathcal{R}_0$ is never injured. The requirement $\mathcal{R}_1$ may be injured by the action of $\mathcal{R}_0$. However, once this happens $\mathcal{R}_0$ will never act again, so if $\mathcal{R}_1$ is allowed to start over at this point, it will succeed. This process of starting over is called *initialization*. Initializing $\mathcal{R}_1$ means that we restart its action with a new witness, chosen to be larger than any number previously seen in the construction, and hence larger than any number $\mathcal{R}_0$ cares about. This new incarnation of $\mathcal{R}_1$ is guaranteed never to be injured. It should now be clear that, by induction, each requirement will eventually reach a point, following a finite number of initializations, after which it will never be injured and hence will succeed in reaching its goal.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.11.1 — Friedberg, Muchnik)</span></p>

There exist computably enumerable sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A$ and $B$ have incomparable Turing degrees.

*Proof.* We build $A = \bigcup_s A_s$ and $B = \bigcup_s B_s$ in stages to satisfy the same requirements as in the proof of the Kleene-Post Theorem. That is, we make $A$ and $B$ c.e. while meeting the following requirements for all $e \in \mathbb{N}$.

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e} : \Phi^A_e \neq B.$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e+1} : \Phi^B_e \neq A.$$

**The strategy for a single requirement.** We begin by looking at the strategy for a single requirement $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$. We first pick a witness $x$ to *follow* $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$. This *follower* is targeted for $B$, and, of course, we initially keep it out of $B$. We then wait for a stage $s$ such that $\Phi^A_e(x)[s] \!\!\downarrow = 0$. If such a stage does not occur, then either $\Phi^A_e(x) \!\!\uparrow$ or $\Phi^A_e(x) \!\!\downarrow \neq 0$. In either case, since we keep $x$ out of $B$, we have $\Phi^A_e(x) \neq 0 = B(x)$, and hence $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$ is satisfied.

If a stage $s$ as above occurs, then we put $x$ into $B$ and *protect* $A_s$. That is, we try to ensure that any number entering $A$ from now on is greater than any number seen in the construction thus far, and hence in particular greater than $\varphi^A_e(x)[s]$. If we succeed then, by the use principle, $\Phi^A_e(x) = \Phi^A_e(x)[s] = 0 \neq B(x)$, and hence again $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$ is satisfied. We refer to this action of protecting $A_s$ as imposing *restraint* on weaker priority requirements.

Note that when we take this action, we might injure a requirement $\mathcal{R}_{2i+1}$ that is trying to preserve the use of a computation $\Phi^B_i(x')$, since $x$ may be below this use. As explained above, the priority mechanism will ensure that this can happen only if $2i + 1 > 2e$.

**Construction.**

*Stage 0.* Declare that no requirement currently has a follower.

*Stage $s + 1$.* Say that $\mathcal{R}_j$ *requires attention* at this stage if one of the following holds.

(i) $\mathcal{R}_j$ currently has no follower.

(ii) $\mathcal{R}_j$ has a follower $x$ and, for some $e$, either
&nbsp;&nbsp;(a) $j = 2e$ and $\Phi^A_e(x)[s] \!\!\downarrow = 0 = B_s(x)$ or
&nbsp;&nbsp;(b) $j = 2e + 1$ and $\Phi^B_e(x)[s] \!\!\downarrow = 0 = A_s(x)$.

Find the least $j \leqslant s$ with $\mathcal{R}_j$ requiring attention. (If there is none, then proceed to the next stage.) We suppose that $j = 2e$, the odd case being symmetric.

If $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$ has no follower, then let $x$ be a *fresh large* number (that is, one larger than all numbers seen in the construction so far) and appoint $x$ as $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$'s follower.

If $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$ has a follower $x$, then it must be the case that $\Phi^A_e(x)[s] \!\!\downarrow = 0 = B_s(x)$. In this case, enumerate $x$ into $B$ and *initialize* all $\mathcal{R}_k$ with $k > 2e$ by canceling all their followers.

In either case, we say that $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$ *receives attention* at stage $s$.

**End of Construction.**

**Verification.** We prove by induction that, for each $j$,

(i) $\mathcal{R}_j$ receives attention only finitely often, and

(ii) $\mathcal{R}_j$ is met.

Suppose that (i) holds for each $k < j$ in place of $j$. Let $s$ be the least stage such that for all $k < j$, the requirement $\mathcal{R}_k$ does not require attention after stage $s$. By the minimality of $s$, some $\mathcal{R}_k$ with $k < j$ received attention at stage $s$ (or $s = 0$), and hence $\mathcal{R}_j$ does not have a follower at the beginning of stage $s + 1$. Thus, $\mathcal{R}_j$ requires attention at stage $s + 1$, and is appointed a follower $x$. Since $\mathcal{R}_j$ cannot have its follower canceled unless some $\mathcal{R}_k$ with $k < j$ receives attention, $x$ is $\mathcal{R}_j$'s permanent follower.

It is clear by the way followers are chosen that $x$ is never any other requirement's follower, so $x$ will not enter $B$ unless $\mathcal{R}_j$ acts to put it in $B$. So if $\mathcal{R}_j$ never requires attention after stage $s + 1$, then $x \notin B$, and we never have $\Phi^A_e(x)[t] \!\!\downarrow = 0$ for $t > s$, which implies that either $\Phi^A_e(x) \!\!\uparrow$ or $\Phi^A_e(x) \!\!\downarrow \neq 0$. In either case, $\mathcal{R}_j$ is met.

On the other hand, if $\mathcal{R}_j$ requires attention at a stage $t + 1 > s + 1$, then $x \in B$ and $\Phi^A_e(x)[t] \!\!\downarrow = 0$. The only requirements that put numbers into $A$ after stage $t + 1$ are ones weaker than $\mathcal{R}_j$ (i.e., requirements $\mathcal{R}_k$ for $k > j$). Each such strategy is initialized at stage $t + 1$, which means that, when it is later appointed a follower, that follower will be bigger than $\varphi^A_e(x)[t]$. Thus no number less than $\varphi^A_e(x)[t]$ will ever enter $A$ after stage $t + 1$, which implies, by the use principle, that $\Phi^A(x) \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^A_e(x)[t] = 0 \neq B(x)$. So in this case also, $\mathcal{R}_j$ is met. Since $x \in B_{t+2}$ and $x$ is $\mathcal{R}_j$'s permanent follower, $\mathcal{R}_j$ never requires attention after stage $t + 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Bounded Injury)</span></p>

The above proof is an example of the simplest kind of finite injury argument, what is called a *bounded injury* construction. That is, we can put a computable bound *in advance* on the number of times that a given requirement $\mathcal{R}_j$ will be injured. In this case, the bound is $2^j - 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.11.2 — Friedberg)</span></p>

There is a noncomputable c.e. low set.

*Proof.* We construct our set $A$ in stages. To make $A$ noncomputable we need to meet the requirements

$$\mathcal{P}_e : \overline{A} \neq W_e.$$

To make $A$ low we meet the requirements

$$\mathcal{N}_e : (\exists^\infty s\, \Phi^A_e(e)[s] \!\!\downarrow) \Rightarrow \Phi^A_e(e) \!\!\downarrow.$$

To see that such requirements suffice, suppose they are met and define the computable binary function $g$ by letting $g(e, s) = 1$ if $\Phi^A_e(e)[s] \!\!\downarrow$ and $g(e, s) = 0$ otherwise. Then $g(e) = \lim_s g(e, s)$ is well-defined, and by the limit lemma, $A' = \lbrace e : g(e) = 1 \rbrace \leqslant_T \emptyset'$.

The strategy for $\mathcal{P}_e$ is simple. We pick a fresh large follower $x$, and keep it out of $A$. If $x$ enters $W_e$, then we put $x$ into $A$. We meet $\mathcal{N}_e$ by an equally simple conservation strategy. If we see $\Phi^A_e(e)[s] \!\!\downarrow$ then we simply try to ensure that $A \upharpoonright \varphi^A_e(e)[s] = A_s \upharpoonright \varphi^A_e(e)[s]$ by initializing all weaker priority requirements, which forces them to choose fresh large numbers as followers. These numbers will be too big to injure the $\Phi^A_e(e)[s]$ computation after stage $s$. The priority method sorts the actions of the various strategies out. Since $\mathcal{P}_e$ picks a fresh large follower each time it is initialized, it cannot injure any $\mathcal{N}_j$ for $j < e$. It is easy to see that any $\mathcal{N}_e$ can be injured at most $e$ many times, and that each $\mathcal{P}_e$ is met, since it is initialized at most $2^e$ many times.

Actually, the above proof constructs a noncomputable c.e. set that is superlow. (Recall that a set $A$ is superlow if $A' \equiv_{tt} \emptyset'$.)

</div>

### 2.12 Finite Injury Arguments of Unbounded Type

#### 2.12.1 The Sacks Splitting Theorem

There are priority arguments in which the number of injuries to each requirement, while finite, is not bounded by any computable function.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(C.E. Splitting)</span></p>

We write $A = A_0 \sqcup A_1$ to mean that $A = A_0 \cup A_1$ and $A_0 \cap A_1 = \emptyset$. Turing incomparable c.e. sets $A_0$ and $A_1$ such that $A = A_0 \sqcup A_1$ are said to form a *c.e. splitting* of $A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.12.1 — Sacks Splitting Theorem)</span></p>

Every noncomputable c.e. set has a c.e. splitting.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be a noncomputable c.e. set. We build $A_i = \bigcup_s A_{i,s}$ in stages by a priority argument to meet the following requirements for all $e \in \mathbb{N}$ and $i = 0, 1$, while ensuring that $A = A_0 \sqcup A_1$.

$$\mathcal{R}_{e,i} : \Phi^{A_i}_e \neq A.$$

These requirements suffice because if $A_{1-i} \leqslant_T A_i$ then $A \leqslant_T A_i$.

Without loss of generality, we assume that we are given an enumeration of $A$ so that exactly one number enters $A$ at each stage. We must put this number $x \in A_{s+1} \setminus A_s$ into exactly one of $A_0$ or $A_1$, to ensure that $A = A_0 \sqcup A_1$.

To meet $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$, we define the *length of agreement function*

$$l(e, i, s) = \max\lbrace n : \forall k < n\, (\Phi^{A_i}_e(k)[s] = A(k)[s])\rbrace$$

and an associated *use function*

$$u(e, i, s) = \varphi^{A_i}_e(l(e, i, s) - 1)[s],$$

using the convention that use functions are monotone increasing (with respect to the position variable) where defined.

The main idea of the proof is perhaps initially counterintuitive. At each stage $s$, although we want $\Phi^{A_i}_e \neq A$, instead of trying to destroy the agreement between $\Phi^{A_i}_e[s]$ and $A[s]$ represented by $l(e, i, s)$, we try to *preserve* it (a method sometimes called the *Sacks preservation strategy*). The way we implement this preservation is to put numbers entering $A$ after stage $s$ into $A_{1-i}$ and *not* into $A_i$. By the use principle, since this action freezes the $A_i$ side of the computations involved in the definition of $l(e, i, s)$, it ensures that $\Phi^{A_i}_e(k) = \Phi^{A_i}_e(k)[s]$ for all $k < l(e, i, s)$.

Now suppose that $\limsup_s l(e, i, s) = \infty$, so for each $k$ we can find infinitely many stages $s$ at which $k < l(e, i, s)$. For each such stage, $\Phi^{A_i}_e(k) = \Phi^{A_i}_e(k)[s] = A(k)[s]$. Thus $A(k) = A(k)[s]$ for any such $s$. So we can compute $A(k)$ simply by finding such an $s$, which contradicts the noncomputability of $A$. Thus $\limsup_s l(e, i, s) < \infty$, which clearly implies that $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ is met.

In the full construction, of course, we have competing requirements, which we sort out by using priorities. That is, we establish a priority list of our requirements (for instance, saying that $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ is stronger than $\mathcal{R}_{e',i'}$ iff $\langle e, i \rangle < \langle e', i' \rangle$). At stage $s$, for the single element $x_s$ entering $A$ at stage $s$, we find the strongest priority $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ with $\langle e, i \rangle < s$ such that $x_s < u(e, i, s)$ and put $x_s$ into $A_{1-i}$. We say that $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ *acts* at stage $s$. (If there is no such requirement, then we put $x_s$ into $A_0$.)

To verify that this construction works, we argue by induction that each requirement eventually stops acting and is met. Suppose that all requirements stronger than $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ eventually stop acting, say by a stage $s > \langle e, i \rangle$. At any stage $t > s$, if $x_t < u(e, i, t)$, then $x_t$ is put into $A_{1-i}$. The same argument as in the one requirement case now shows that if $\limsup_s l(e, i, s) = \infty$ then $A$ is computable, so $\limsup_s l(e, i, s) < \infty$. Our preservation strategy then ensures that $\limsup_s u(e, i, s) < \infty$. Thus $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ eventually stops acting and is met.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Unbounded Injury)</span></p>

In the above construction, injury to a requirement $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ happens whenever $x_s < u(e, i, s)$ but $x_s$ is nonetheless put into $A_i$, at the behest of a stronger priority requirement. How often $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$ is injured depends on the lengths of agreement attached to stronger priority requirements, and thus cannot be computably bounded.

Note that, for any noncomputable c.e. set $C$, we can easily add requirements of the form $\Phi^{A_i}_e \neq C$ to the above construction, satisfying them in the same way that we did for the $\mathcal{R}_{e,i}$. Thus, as shown by Sacks, in addition to making $A_0 \mid_T A_1$, we can also ensure that $A_i \ngeqslant_T C$ for $i = 0, 1$.

</div>

#### 2.12.2 The Pseudo-Jump Theorem

Another basic construction using the finite injury method was discovered by Jockusch and Shore. It involves what are called *pseudo-jump operators*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.12.2 — Pseudo-Jump Operator, Jockusch and Shore)</span></p>

For an index $e$, let the *pseudo-jump operator* $V_e$ be defined by $V^A_e = A \oplus W^A_e$ for all oracles $A$. We say that $V_e$ is *nontrivial* if $A <_T V^A_e$ for all oracles $A$.

</div>

The jump operator is itself a nontrivial pseudo-jump operator (up to degree). The following pseudo-jump inversion theorem is an important tool in the theory of pseudo-jump operators.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.12.3 — Pseudo-Jump Inversion, Jockusch and Shore)</span></p>

For any nontrivial pseudo-jump operator $V$, there is a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ with $V^A \equiv_T \emptyset'$.

*Proof.* We build $A = \bigcup_s A_s$ in stages. Let $k$ be such that $V^X = X \oplus W^X_k$ for all $X$.

To ensure that $V^A \leqslant_T \emptyset'$, we meet the requirements

$$\mathcal{N}_n : \exists^\infty s\, (n \in W^A_k[s]) \Rightarrow n \in W^A_k,$$

which imply that $W^A_k$ is $\Pi^0_2$. Since $W^A_k$ is necessarily $\Sigma^0_2$ and $V^A$ is the join of $W^A_k$ with a c.e. set, these requirements ensure that $V^A$ is $\Delta^0_2$.

To satisfy $\mathcal{N}_n$, we have a *restraint function* $r(n, s)$, which we define as follows. If $n \in W^A_k[s]$, then $r(n, s)$ is the use of the computation ensuring this fact (that is, $\varphi^A_k(n)[s]$). Otherwise, $r(n, s) = 0$.

Let $\gamma(n, s)$ be the least element of $\mathbb{N}^{[n]}$ greater than or equal to $r(n, s)$ for all $m \leqslant n$. To ensure that $\emptyset' \leqslant_T V^A$, we will make sure that $\gamma(n) = \lim_s \gamma(n, s)$ exists for all $n$.

To make $A$ noncomputable, we meet the requirements
