$$\mathcal{R}_e : \overline{W_e} \neq A,$$

by putting $\gamma(2e, s)$ into $A$ if we have not yet satisfied $\mathcal{R}_e$ and $\gamma(2e, s) \in W_e[s]$. For each $n$ entering $\emptyset'$ at stage $s$, then we put $\gamma(2n + 1, s)$ into $A$.

The full construction proceeds as follows at stage $s$: For each $e$ such that $W_e[s] \cap A[s] = \emptyset$ and $\gamma(2e, s) \in W_e[s]$, put $\gamma(2e, s)$ into $A$. For each $n$ entering $\emptyset'$ at stage $s$, put $\gamma(2n + 1, s)$ into $A$.

We put at most one number into $A$ for the sake of a given $\mathcal{R}$- or $\mathcal{P}$-requirement. By the definition of the $\gamma$ function, for each $n$ there is an $s$ such that, for all $t \geqslant s$, no number less than $r(n, t)$ is put into $A$ at stage $t$. It follows that $\lim_t r(n, t)$ exists, and $\mathcal{N}_n$ is satisfied.

Thus $\gamma(n) = \lim_s \gamma(n, s)$ exists for all $n$, and the construction ensures that if $W_e \cap A = \emptyset$ then $\gamma(2e) \notin A$ iff $\gamma(2e) \notin W_e$, so $\mathcal{R}_e$ is satisfied.

It is also clear that each $\mathcal{P}$-requirement is satisfied by the construction, so we are left with showing that for each $n$, we can $V^A$-computably determine a stage $u$ such that $\gamma(n, s) = \gamma(n, u)$ for all $s \geqslant u$.

</div>

Jockusch and Shore used the pseudo-jump machinery to establish a number of results. One application is a finite injury proof that there is a high incomplete c.e. Turing degree, a result first proved using the infinite injury method, as we will see in Section 2.14.3. The Jockusch-Shore proof of the existence of a high incomplete c.e. degree in fact produces a superhigh degree (where a set $X$ is *superhigh* if $\emptyset'' \leqslant_{tt} X'$), and runs as follows. Relativize the original Friedberg theorem that there is a noncomputable set $W_e$ of low (or even superlow) Turing degree, to obtain an operator $\mathcal{V}_e$ such that for all $Y$ we have that $Y <_T V^Y_e$ and $V^Y_e$ is (super)low over $Y$. Then use Theorem 2.12.3 to obtain a c.e. set $A$ with $V^A_e \equiv_T \emptyset'$. Then $\emptyset'$ is (super)low over $A$ and $A <_T \emptyset'$, and hence $A$ is (super)high and incomplete.

### 2.13 Coding and Permitting

Coding and permitting are two basic methods for controlling the degrees of sets we build.

**Coding** is a way of ensuring that a set $A$ we build has degree at least that of a given set $B$. As the name implies, it consists of encoding the bits of $B$ into $A$ in a recoverable way. One simple way to do this is to build $A$ to equal $B \oplus C$ for some $C$, but in some cases we need to employ a more elaborate coding method.

**Permitting** is a way of ensuring that a set $A$ we build has degree at most that of a given set $B$. Let $B$ be a noncomputable c.e. set, and suppose we are building a c.e. set $A$ to satisfy certain requirements while also ensuring that $A \leqslant_T B$. At a stage $s$ in the construction, we may want to put a number $n$ into $A$. But instead of putting $n$ into $A$ immediately, we wait for a stage $t \geqslant s$ such that $B_{t+1} \upharpoonright n \neq B_t \upharpoonright n$. At that stage, we say that $B$ *permits* us to put $n$ into $A$. If we always wait for permissions of this kind before putting numbers into $A$, then we can compute $A$ from $B$ as follows. Given $n$, look for an $s$ such that $B_s \upharpoonright n = B \upharpoonright n$. Then $n \in A$ iff $n \in A_s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.13.1)</span></p>

Every nonzero c.e. degree contains a simple set.

*Proof.* Let $B$ be a noncomputable c.e. set. We may assume that exactly one element $x_s$ enters $B$ at each stage $s$. We build a c.e. set $A \equiv_T B$ to satisfy the simplicity requirements

$$\mathcal{R}_e : \lvert W_e \rvert = \infty \Rightarrow W_e \cap A \neq \emptyset.$$

At stage $s$, let $a^s_0 < a^s_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $\overline{A_s}$. For each $e < s$, if $W_{e,s} \cap A_s = \emptyset$ and there is an $n \in W_{e,s}$ with $n > 3e$ and $n > x_s$, then put $n$ into $A$. Also, put $a^s_{3x_s}$ into $A$.

The set $A$ is clearly c.e. Each $\mathcal{R}_e$ puts at most one number into $A$, and this number must be greater than $3e$, while for each $x \in B$, the coding of $B$ puts only one number into $A$, and this number is at least $3x$. Thus $A$ is coinfinite.

For each $n$, if $B_s \upharpoonright n + 1 = B \upharpoonright n + 1$, then $n$ cannot enter $A$ after stage $s$, so $n \notin A$ iff $n \notin A_s$. Thus $A \leqslant_T B$.

Given $x$, the set $A$ can compute a stage $s$ such that $a^s_i \in \overline{A}$ for all $i \leqslant 3x$. Then $a^s_{3x} = a^t_{3x}$ for all $t \geqslant s$, so $x \in B$ iff either $x \in B_s$ or $a^s_{3x} \in A$. Thus $B \leqslant_T A$.

</div>

Finally, we need to show that each $\mathcal{R}_e$ is met. Suppose that $W_e$ is infinite but $W_e \cap A = \emptyset$. Then we can computably find $3e < n_0 < n_1 < \cdots$ and $e \leqslant s_0 < s_1 < \cdots$ such that $n_i \in W_{e,s_i}$ for all $i$. None of the $n_i$ are ever permitted by $B$, that is, $n_i \leqslant x_t$ for all $i$ and $t \geqslant s_i$. So $B \upharpoonright n_i = B_{s_i} \upharpoonright n_i$ for all $i$, and hence $B$ is computable, contrary to hypothesis. Thus each requirement is met, and hence $A$ is simple.

### 2.14 The Infinite Injury Priority Method

In this section, we introduce the infinite injury priority method. We begin by discussing the concept of priority trees, then give a few examples of constructions involving such trees.

#### 2.14.1 Priority Trees and Guessing

The Friedberg-Muchnik construction used to prove Theorem 2.11.1 can be viewed another way. Instead of having a single strategy for each requirement, which is restarted every time a stronger priority requirement acts, we can attach multiple versions of each requirement to a *priority tree*, in this case the full binary tree $2^{<\omega}$.

Recall that the relevant requirements are the following.

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e} : \Phi^A_e \neq B.$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e+1} : \Phi^B_e \neq A.$$

Attached to each node $\sigma$ is a "version" $R_\sigma$ of $\mathcal{R}_{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$. We call such a version a *strategy* for $\mathcal{R}_{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, and refer to $\sigma$ itself as a *guess*, for reasons that will soon become clear. Thus there are $2^e$ separate strategies for $\mathcal{R}_e$.

Each strategy $R_\sigma$ has two *outcomes*, 0 and 1. The outcome 1 indicates that $R_\sigma$ appoints a follower but never enumerates this follower into its target set. The outcome 0 indicates that $R_\sigma$ actually enumerates its follower into its target set. We order our guesses lexicographically, and hence if $\sigma$ is to the left of $\tau$, then we think of $\sigma$ as being stronger than $\tau$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(True Outcomes and the True Path)</span></p>

The strategy $R_\lambda$ has a *true outcome* $i$, which is 0 if it eventually enumerates its witness into $B$, and 1 otherwise. Similarly, $R_i$ has a true outcome. The *true path* TP of the construction is the unique path such that $\text{TP}(n)$ is the true outcome of $R_{\text{TP} \upharpoonright n}$. Note that, in the above construction, $\text{TP}(n) = \lim_s \sigma_s(n)$, but with an eye to more complicated tree constructions, we in general define the true path of such a construction as the leftmost path visited infinitely often (that is, the path extending those guesses $\tau$ such that $\tau$ is the leftmost guess of length $\lvert\tau\rvert$ that is visited infinitely often). The idea behind this definition is that the strategies on the true path are the ones that actually succeed in meeting their respective requirements.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Finite vs. Infinite Injury on the Tree)</span></p>

The key difference between finite injury and infinite injury arguments is the following. In an infinite injury argument, the action of a given requirements $\mathcal{R}$ may be infinitary. Obviously, we cannot simply restart weaker priority requirements every time $\mathcal{R}$ acts. Instead, we can have multiple strategies for weaker priority requirements, depending on whether or not $\mathcal{R}$ acts infinitely often. Thus, in a basic setup of this sort, the left outcome of $\mathcal{R}$, representing the guess that $\mathcal{R}$ acts infinitely often, is visited each time $\mathcal{R}$ acts.

In the Friedberg-Muchnik argument, the approximation to the true path moves only to the left as time goes on, since the guessed outcome of a strategy can change from 1 to 0, but never the other way. Thus, the true path is computable in $\emptyset'$. In infinite injury arguments, the approximation to the true path can move both left and right. Assuming that the tree is finitely branching, this possibility means that, in general, the true path is computable only in $\emptyset''$. We have

$$\sigma \prec \text{TP iff } \exists^\infty s\, (\sigma \prec \text{TP}_s) \wedge \exists^{<\infty} s\, (\text{TP}_s <_{\text{lex}} \sigma).$$

</div>

#### 2.14.2 The Minimal Pair Method

Our first example of an infinite injury argument is the construction of a minimal pair of c.e. degrees. A pair of noncomputable degrees $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is a *minimal pair* if the only degree below both $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is $\mathbf{0}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.1 — Lachlan, Yates)</span></p>

There is a minimal pair of c.e. degrees.

*Proof.* We build noncomputable c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ such that every set computable in both $A$ and $B$ is computable. We construct $A$ and $B$ in stages to satisfy the following requirements for each $e$.

$$\mathcal{R}_e : \overline{A} \neq W_e.$$

$$\mathcal{Q}_e : \overline{B} \neq W_e.$$

$$\mathcal{N}_e : \Phi^A_e = \Phi^B_e \text{ total} \Rightarrow \Phi^A_e \text{ computable.}$$

We meet the $\mathcal{R}$- and $\mathcal{Q}$-requirements by a Friedberg-Muchnik type strategy. For instance, to satisfy $\mathcal{R}_e$, we pick a follower $x$, targeted for $A$, and wait until $x$ enters $W_e$. If this event never happens, then $x \in \overline{A} \cap \overline{W_e}$, so $\mathcal{R}_e$ is met. If $x$ does enter $W_e$, then we put $x$ into $A$, thus again meeting $\mathcal{R}_e$.

The $\mathcal{N}$-requirements are trickier to meet. We first discuss how to meet a single $\mathcal{N}_e$ in isolation, and then look at the coherence problems between the various requirements and the solution to these provided by the use of a tree of strategies.

We follow standard conventions, in particular assuming that all uses at stage $s$ are bounded by $s$. As in the proof of the Sacks Splitting Theorem 2.12.1, we have a length of agreement function

$$l(e, s) = \max\lbrace n : \forall k < n\, (\Phi^A_e(k)[s] \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^B_e(k)[s] \!\!\downarrow)\rbrace$$

and an associated use function

$$u(e, s) = \max\lbrace \varphi^A_e(l(e, t) - 1)[t],\, \varphi^B_e(l(e, t) - 1)[t] : t \leqslant s \rbrace,$$

using the convention that use functions are monotone increasing where defined. We now also have a maximum length of agreement function

$$m(e, s) = \max\lbrace l(e, t) : t \leqslant s \rbrace,$$

which can be thought of as a high water mark for the length of agreements seen so far. We say that a stage $s$ is *$e$-expansionary* if $l(e, s) > m(e, s - 1)$, that is, if the current length of agreement surpasses the previous high water mark.

**The key idea for meeting $\mathcal{N}_e$.** Suppose that $n < l(e, s)$, so that $\Phi^A_e(n)[s] \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^B_e(n)[s] \!\!\downarrow$. Now suppose that we allow numbers to enter $A$ at will, but "freeze" the computation $\Phi^B_e(n)[s]$ by not allowing any number to enter $B$ below $\varphi^B_e(n)[s]$ until the next $e$-expansionary stage $t > s$. At this stage $t$, we again have $\Phi^A_e(n)[t] \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^B_e(n)[t] \!\!\downarrow$. But we also have $\Phi^B_e(n)[t] = \Phi^B_e(n)[s]$, by the use principle. Now suppose that we start allowing numbers to enter $B$ at will, but freeze the computation $\Phi^A_e(n)[t]$ by not allowing any number to enter $A$ below $\varphi^A_e(n)[t]$ until the next $e$-expansionary stage $u > t$. Then again $\Phi^A_e(n)[u] \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^B_e(n)[u] \!\!\downarrow$, but $\Phi^A_e(n)[u] = \Phi^A_e(n)[t]$. (Note that we are not saying that these *computations* are the same, only that they have the same *value*.) So if we keep to this strategy, alternately freezing the $A$-side and the $B$-side of our agreeing computations, we ensure that, if $\Phi^A_e = \Phi^B_e$ is total (which implies that there are infinitely many $e$-expansionary stages), then $\Phi^A_e(n) = \Phi^A_e(n)[s]$. Thus, if we follow this strategy for all $n$, we can compute $\Phi^A_e$, and hence $\mathcal{N}_e$ is met.

In summary, the strategy for meeting $\mathcal{N}_e$ is to wait until an $e$-expansionary stage $s$, then impose a restraint $u(e, s)$ on $A$, then wait until the next $e$-expansionary stage $t$, lift the restraint on $A$, and impose a restraint $u(e, t)$ on $B$, and then continue in this way, alternating which set is restrained at each new $e$-expansionary stage.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Coherence of $\mathcal{N}$-Strategies)</span></p>

**Coherence.** When we consider multiple $\mathcal{N}$-requirements, we run into a problem. Consider two requirements $\mathcal{N}_e$ and $\mathcal{N}_i$, with the first having stronger priority. Let $s_0 < s_1 < \cdots$ be the $e$-expansionary stages and $t_0 < t_1 < \cdots$ be the $i$-expansionary stages. During the interval $[s_0, s_1)$, weaker strategies are prevented from putting certain numbers into $A$ by $\mathcal{N}_e$. At stage $s_1$, this restraint is lifted, but if $t_0 \leqslant s_1 < t_1$, then at stage $s_1$ it will be $\mathcal{N}_i$ that prevents weaker strategies from putting certain numbers into $A$. When $\mathcal{N}_i$ drops that restraint at stage $t_1$, we may have a new restraint on $A$ imposed by $\mathcal{N}_e$ (if say $s_2 \leqslant t_1 < s_3$). Thus, although individually $\mathcal{N}_e$ and $\mathcal{N}_i$ each provide infinitely many stages at which they allow weaker strategies to put numbers into $A$, the two strategies *together* may conspire to block weaker strategies from *ever* putting numbers into $A$.

This problem is overcome by having two strategies for $\mathcal{N}_i$. The one guessing that there are only finitely many $e$-expansionary stages has no problems. The one guessing that there are infinitely many $e$-expansionary stages acts only at such stages, and in particular calculates its expansionary stages based only on such stages, which forces its expansionary stages to be nested within the $e$-expansionary stages. Thus the actions of $\mathcal{N}_e$ and $\mathcal{N}_i$ are forced to cohere.

</div>

We now turn to the formal details of the construction. We use the tree $\mathcal{T} = \lbrace \infty, f \rbrace^{<\omega}$. (Of course, this tree is the same as $2^{<\omega}$, but renaming the nodes should help in understanding the construction.) To each $\sigma \in \mathcal{T}$, we assign strategies $N_\sigma$, $R_\sigma$, and $Q_\sigma$ for $\mathcal{N}_{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, $\mathcal{R}_{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, and $\mathcal{Q}_{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, respectively. (It is only the $\mathcal{N}$-requirements that really need to be on the tree, since only they have outcomes we care about, but this assignment is notationally convenient.) Again we use lexicographic ordering, with $\infty$ to the left of $f$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.2 — $\sigma$-Stages and $\sigma$-Expansionary Stages)</span></p>

We define the notions of *$\sigma$-stage*, $m(\sigma, s)$, and *$\sigma$-expansionary stage* by induction on $\lvert\sigma\rvert$.

(i) Every stage is a $\lambda$-stage.

(ii) Suppose that $s$ is a $\tau$-stage. Let $e = \lvert\tau\rvert$. Let

$$m(\tau, s) = \max\lbrace l(e, t) : t < s \text{ is a } \tau\text{-stage}\rbrace.$$

If $l(e, s) > m(\tau, s)$ then we say that $s$ is $\tau$-expansionary and declare $s$ to be a $\tau {}^\frown \infty$-stage. Otherwise, we declare $s$ to be a $\tau {}^\frown f$-stage.

Let $\text{TP}_s$ be the unique string $\sigma$ of length $s$ such that $s$ is a $\sigma$-stage.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.3 — Requiring Attention)</span></p>

We say that $R_\sigma$ *requires attention* at a $\sigma$-stage $s > \lvert\sigma\rvert$ if $W_{\lvert\sigma\rvert, s} \cap A_s = \emptyset$ and one of the following holds.

(i) $R_\sigma$ currently has no follower.

(ii) $R_\sigma$ has a follower $x \in W_{\lvert\sigma\rvert, s}$.

The definition of $Q_\sigma$ requiring attention is analogous.

</div>

**Construction.** At stage $s$, proceed as follows.

*Step 1.* Compute $\text{TP}_s$. Initialize all strategies attached to nodes to the right of $\text{TP}_s$. For the $R$- and $Q$-strategies, this initialization means that their followers are canceled.

*Step 2.* Find the strongest priority $\mathcal{R}$- or $\mathcal{Q}$-requirement that has a strategy requiring attention at stage $s$. Let us suppose that this requirement is $\mathcal{R}_e$ (the $\mathcal{Q}$-requirement case being analogous), and that $R_\sigma$ is the corresponding strategy requiring attention at stage $s$. (Note that $s$ must be a $\sigma$-stage.) We say that $R_\sigma$ *acts* at stage $s$. Initialize all strategies attached to nodes properly extending $\sigma$. If $R_\sigma$ does not currently have a follower, appoint a fresh large follower for $R_\sigma$. Otherwise, enumerate $R_\sigma$'s follower into $A$.

**End of Construction.**

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.4)</span></p>

Each $\mathcal{R}$- and $\mathcal{Q}$-requirement is met.

*Proof.* Consider $\mathcal{R}_e$ (the $\mathcal{Q}$-requirement case being analogous). Let $\sigma = \text{TP} \upharpoonright e$. Assume by induction that the strategies attached to proper prefixes of $\sigma$ act only finitely often, and let $s$ be the least stage such that

1. no strategy attached to a proper prefix of $\sigma$ acts after stage $s$ and

2. $\text{TP}_t$ is not to the left of $\sigma$ for all $t > s$.

By the minimality of $s$, it must be the case that $R_\sigma$ is initialized at stage $s$. Thus, at the next $\sigma$-stage $t > s$, it will be assigned a follower $x$. Since $R_\sigma$ cannot be initialized after stage $t$, this follower is permanent. By the way followers are chosen, $x$ will not be put into $A$ unless $x$ enters $W_e$. If $x$ enters $W_e$ at stage $u > t$, then at the first $\sigma$-stage $v \geqslant u$, the strategy $R_\sigma$ will act, and $x$ will enter $A$. In any case, $R_\sigma$ succeeds in meeting $\mathcal{R}_e$. Note that $R_\sigma$ acts at most twice after stage $s$, and hence the induction can continue.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.5)</span></p>

Each $\mathcal{N}$-requirement is met.

*Proof.* Consider $\mathcal{N}_e$. Let $\sigma = \text{TP} \upharpoonright e$. If $\sigma {}^\frown f \prec \text{TP}$ then there are only finitely many $e$-expansionary stages, so $\Phi_e(A) \neq \Phi_e(B)$, and hence $\mathcal{N}_e$ is met.

So suppose that $\sigma {}^\frown \infty \prec \text{TP}$ and that $\Phi^A_e$ is total. We will show that $\Phi^A_e$ is computable. Let $s$ be the least stage such that

1. no strategy attached to a prefix of $\sigma$ (including $\sigma$ itself) acts after stage $s$ and

2. $\text{TP}_t$ is not to the left of $\sigma$ for all $t > s$.

To compute $\Phi^A_e(n)$, find the least $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$-stage $t_0 > s$ such that $l(e, s) > n$. We claim that $\Phi^A_e(n) = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_0]$.

Let $t_0 < t_1 < \cdots$ be the $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$-stages greater than or equal to $t_0$. Each such stage is $e$-expansionary, so we have $\Phi^A_e(n)[t_i] = \Phi^B_e(n)[t_i]$ for all $i$. We claim that, for each $i$, we have either $\Phi^A_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ or $\Phi^B_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = \Phi^B_e(n)[t_i]$. Assuming this claim, it follows easily that $\Phi^A_e(n)[t_0] = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_1] = \cdots$, which implies that $\Phi^A_e(n) = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_0]$.

To establish the claim, fix $i$. At stage $t_i$, we initialize all strategies to the right of $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$, so any follower of such a strategy appointed after stage $t_i$ must be larger than any number seen in the construction by stage $t_i$, and in particular larger than $\varphi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ and $\varphi^B_e(n)[t_i]$. By the choice of $s$, strategies above or to the left of $\sigma$ do not act after stage $s$. Thus, the only strategies that can put numbers less than $\varphi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ into $A$ or numbers less than $\varphi^B_e(n)[t_i]$ into $B$ between stages $t_i$ and $t_{i+1}$ are the ones associated with extensions of $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$. But such a strategy cannot act except at a $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$-stage, and at each stage at most one strategy gets to act. Thus, at most one strategy associated with an extension of $\sigma {}^\frown \infty$ can act between stages $t_i$ and $t_{i+1}$, and hence only one of $A \upharpoonright \varphi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ and $B \upharpoonright \varphi^B_e(n)[t_i]$ can change between stages $t_i$ and $t_{i+1}$. So either $A \upharpoonright \varphi^A_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = A \upharpoonright \varphi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ or $B \upharpoonright \varphi^B_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = B \upharpoonright \varphi^B_e(n)[t_i]$, which implies that either $\Phi^A_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_i]$ or $\Phi^B_e(n)[t_{i+1}] = \Phi^B_e(n)[t_i]$. As mentioned above, it follows that $\Phi^A_e(n) = \Phi^A_e(n)[t_0]$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.6 — Ambos-Spies, Downey and Welch)</span></p>

There is a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ such that if $C \sqcup D = A$ is a c.e. splitting of $A$, then the degrees of $C$ and $D$ form a minimal pair.

*Proof sketch.* The proof is similar to that of Theorem 2.14.1, using the length of agreement function

$$l(i, j, k, m, s) = \max\lbrace x : \forall y < x\, [\Phi^{W_k}_i(y)[s] \!\!\downarrow = \Phi^{W_m}_j(y)[s] \!\!\downarrow$$

$$\wedge\, \forall z \leqslant \varphi^{W_k}_i(y)[s], \varphi^{W_m}_j(y)[s]\, (W_k[s] \upharpoonright z \sqcup W_m[s] \upharpoonright z = A_s \upharpoonright z)]\rbrace.$$

</div>

#### 2.14.3 High Computably Enumerable Degrees

Another example of an infinite injury argument is the construction of an incomplete high c.e. degree. Recall that a c.e. degree $\mathbf{a}$ is high if $\mathbf{a}' = \mathbf{0}''$. We begin with a few definitions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.7 — Piecewise Trivial)</span></p>

A set $A$ is *piecewise trivial* if for all $e$, the set $A^{[e]}$ is either finite or equal to $\mathbb{N}^{[e]}$. Recall that $A^{[e]}$ denotes the $e$th column $\lbrace \langle e, n \rangle : \langle e, n \rangle \in A \rbrace$ of $A$.

A subset $B$ of $A$ is *thick* if for all $e$ we have $A^{[e]} =^* B^{[e]}$ (i.e., the symmetric difference of $A^{[e]}$ and $B^{[e]}$ is finite).

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.8)</span></p>

(i) There is a piecewise trivial c.e. set $A$ such that $A^{[e]}$ is infinite iff $\Phi_e$ is total.

(ii) If $B$ is a thick subset of such a set $A$, then $B$ is high.

*Proof.* (i) Let $A = \lbrace \langle e, n \rangle : e \in \mathbb{N} \wedge \forall k \leqslant n\, \Phi_e(k) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace$. Then $A$ is c.e., and clearly $A^{[e]}$ is infinite iff $A^{[e]} = \mathbb{N}^{[e]}$ iff $\Phi_e$ is total.

(ii) Define a reduction $\Gamma$ by letting $\Gamma^X(e, s) = 1$ if $\langle e, s \rangle \in X$ and $\Gamma^X(e, s) = 0$ otherwise. If $\Phi_e$ is total then $A^{[e]} = \mathbb{N}^{[e]}$, so $B^{[e]}$ is cofinite, which implies that $\lim_s \Gamma^B(e, s) = 1$. On the other hand, if $\Phi_e$ is not total then $A^{[e]}$ is finite, so $B^{[e]}$ is finite, which implies that $\lim_s \Gamma^B(e, s) = 0$.

Thus, the function $f$ defined by $f(e) = \lim_s \Gamma^B(e, s)$ is total. By the relativized form of the limit lemma, $f \leqslant_T B'$. So $B'$ can decide whether a given $\Phi_e$ is total or not. By Theorem 2.6.5, $\emptyset'' \leqslant_T B'$.

</div>

Thus, to construct an incomplete high c.e. degree, it suffices to prove the following result, which is a weak form of the Thickness Lemma discussed in the next section.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.9 — Shoenfield)</span></p>

Let $C$ be a noncomputable c.e. set, and let $A$ be a piecewise trivial c.e. set. Then there is a c.e. thick subset $B$ of $A$ such that $C \nleqslant_T B$.

*Proof.* We construct $B \subseteq A$ to meet the following requirements for all $e$.

$$\mathcal{R}_e : \lvert A^{[e]} \setminus B^{[e]}\rvert < \infty.$$

$$\mathcal{N}_e : \Phi^B_e \neq C.$$

To meet $\mathcal{R}_e$, we must make sure that almost all of the $e$th column of $A$ gets into $B$. To meet $\mathcal{N}_e$, we use the strategy already employed in the proof of the Sacks Splitting Theorem 2.12.1. That is, we measure the length of agreement

$$l(e, s) = \max\lbrace n : \forall k < n\, (\Phi^B_e(k)[s] = C_s(k))\rbrace,$$

with the idea of preserving $B_s$ on the use $\varphi^B_e(n)[s]$ for all $n < l(e, s)$.

The problem comes from the interaction of this preservation strategy with the strategies for stronger priority $\mathcal{R}$-requirements, since these may be infinitary. It might be the case that we infinitely often try to preserve an agreeing computation $\Phi^B_e(n)[s] = C_s(n)$, only to have some $\langle i, x \rangle$ enter $A$ with $i < e$ and $\langle i, x \rangle < \varphi^B_e(n)[s]$. Since we must put almost every such pair into $B$ for the sake of $\mathcal{R}_i$, our strategy for meeting $\mathcal{N}_e$ might be injured infinitely often.

On the other hand, we do know that $\mathcal{R}_i$ can do only one of two things. Either $A^{[i]}$ is finite, and hence $\mathcal{R}_i$ stops injuring $\mathcal{N}_e$ after some time, or almost all of $A^{[i]}$ is put into $B$. In the latter case, the numbers put into $B$ for the sake of $\mathcal{R}_i$ form a computable set (since $A$ is piecewise trivial). It is easy to adapt the strategy for $\mathcal{N}_e$ to deal with this case. Let $S$ be the computable set of numbers put into $B$ for the sake of $\mathcal{R}_i$. We can then proceed with the Sacks preservation strategy, except that we do not believe a computation $\Phi^B_e(k)[s]$ unless $B_s$ already contains every element of $S \upharpoonright \varphi^B_e(k)[s]$. This modification prevents the action taken for $\mathcal{R}_i$ from ever injuring the strategy for meeting $\mathcal{N}_e$.

Of course, we do not know which of the two possibilities for the action of $\mathcal{R}_i$ actually happens, so, as before, we will have multiple strategies for $\mathcal{N}_e$, representing guesses as to whether or not $A^{[i]}$ is finite.

</div>

#### 2.14.4 The Thickness Lemma

Theorem 2.14.9 is only a weak form of the real Thickness Lemma of Shoenfield. To state the full version, we need a new definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.11 — Piecewise Computable)</span></p>

A set $A$ is *piecewise computable* if $A^{[e]}$ is computable for each $e$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.12 — Thickness Lemma, Shoenfield)</span></p>

Let $C$ be a noncomputable c.e. set, and let $A$ be a piecewise computable c.e. set. Then there is a c.e. thick subset $B$ of $A$ such that $C \nleqslant_T B$.

*Proof sketch.* The proof modifies that of Theorem 2.14.9. In the weak version, we assumed that $A$ was piecewise trivial. Now we have the weaker assumption that $A$ is piecewise computable, that is, every column of $A$ is computable. Thus, for each $e$, there is a c.e. set $W_{g(e)} \subseteq \mathbb{N}^{[e]}$ such that $W_{g(e)}$ is the complement of $A^{[e]}$ in $\mathbb{N}^{[e]}$ (meaning that $\overline{W_{g(e)}} \sqcup A^{[e]} = \mathbb{N}^{[e]}$).

So in order to adjust the definition of $\sigma$-believable computation, for each $\tau \prec \sigma$, we need to know an index $j$ such that $W_j = W_{g(\lvert\tau\rvert)}$. Since we cannot compute such a $j$ from $\lvert\tau\rvert$, we must guess it along the tree of strategies. That is, for each $\tau$, instead of the outcomes $\infty$ and $f$, we now have an outcome $j$ for each $j \in \mathbb{N}$, representing a guess that $W_j = W_{g(\lvert\tau\rvert)}$. The outcome $j$ is taken to be correct at a stage $s$ if $j$ is the least number for which we see the length of agreement between $\mathbb{N}^{[e]} \sqcup W_j$ and $A^{[e]}$ increase at stage $s$.

One important point is that, because the tree of strategies is now infinitely branching, the existence of the true path is no longer automatically guaranteed. However, if $\tau$ is visited infinitely often then $\tau {}^\frown j$ is visited infinitely often for the least $j$ such that $W_j = W_{g(\lvert\tau\rvert)}$, from which it follows that TP is well-defined.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.13 — Strong Thickness Lemma)</span></p>

Let $C$ be such that $\emptyset <_T C \leqslant \emptyset'$ and let $A$ be c.e. There is a c.e. set $B \subseteq A$ such that $B \leqslant_T A$ and for all $e$, if $C \nleqslant_T A^{[<e]}$ then for all $j \leqslant e$ we have $C \neq \Phi^B_j$ and $B^{[j]} =^* A^{[j]}$. It follows that if $C \nleqslant_T A^{[<e]}$ for all $e$ then $C \nleqslant_T B$ and $B$ is a thick subset of $A$. Furthermore, an index for $B$ can be obtained uniformly from indices for $A$ and $C$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.14.14 — Sacks)</span></p>

Let $\mathbf{a}_0 < \mathbf{a}_1 < \cdots$ be an infinite ascending sequence of uniformly c.e. degrees. Then there is an incomplete c.e. degree $\mathbf{b}$ such that $\mathbf{a}_0 < \mathbf{a}_1 < \cdots < \mathbf{b}$. Thus $\mathbf{0}'$ is not a minimal upper bound for any such sequence of degrees.

*Proof.* Let $h$ be a computable function such that $\mathbf{a}_i = \deg(W_{h(i)})$ for all $i$. Let $A = \lbrace \langle i, n \rangle : n \in W_{h(i)} \rbrace$. Let $C = \emptyset'$ and apply the Strong Thickness Lemma to get a c.e. set $B \subseteq A$ such that $C \nleqslant_T B$ and $B^{[i]} =^* A^{[i]}$ (which implies that $\deg(B^{[i]}) = \mathbf{a}_i$) for all $i$. Let $\mathbf{b} = \deg(B)$.

</div>

### 2.15 The Density Theorem

One of the classic applications of the infinite injury method is to extend the Friedberg-Muchnik Theorem to show that not only are there intermediate c.e. degrees, but in fact the c.e. degrees form a dense partial ordering. It is possible to give a short and elegant proof of this result without using priority trees, but it is quite hard to understand how that proof works. Therefore, in this section we give a proof using trees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.15.1 — Sacks Density Theorem)</span></p>

For any c.e. sets $B <_T C$, there are c.e. sets $A_0 \mid_T A_1$ such that $B <_T A_i <_T C$ for $i = 0, 1$.

*Proof.* We will construct $A_0, A_1$ satisfying the following requirements for all $e$.

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e} : \Phi^{A_0}_e \neq A_1.$$

$$\mathcal{R}_{2e+1} : \Phi^{A_1}_e \neq A_0.$$

Additionally, we must meet the global permitting requirement

$$A_0, A_1 \leqslant_T C$$

and the global coding requirement

$$B \leqslant_T A_0, A_1.$$

Together with the incomparability of the $A_i$, these global requirements imply that $B <_T A_i <_T C$ for $i = 0, 1$.

To satisfy the coding requirement, we simply code $B$ into the even bits of each $A_i$. That is, whenever $n$ enters $B$, we put $2n$ into $A_i$, and we ensure that all other numbers entering $A_i$ are odd.

To satisfy the $\mathcal{R}$-requirements, we adopt a Friedberg-Muchnik type strategy, which we later modify to cope with the coding and permitting requirements. Consider the satisfaction of $\mathcal{R}_{2e}$. We pick an odd follower $n$, wait until $\Phi^{A_0}_e(n(i))[s] \!\!\downarrow = 0$, and if that ever happens, put $n(i)$ into $A_1$ and attempt to preserve $\varphi^{A_0}_e(n(i))[s]$. This strategy is not correct as stated, but will serve as a basis for future modifications. The basic problems are that we cannot control $B$ and that we can put numbers into $A_1$ only when permitted to do so by $C$.

In place of a single follower $n$, we will use a potentially infinite collection $\lbrace n(i) : i \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ of followers or *coding markers*. When a follower $n(i)$ is realized and $i$ enters $C$, we put $n(i)$ into $A_1$. The key idea of the proof is Sacks' notion of *delayed permitting*: since $R_\sigma$ is guessing that $(i, u)$ is the true outcome of the $\mathcal{R}_0$ strategy, it believes that any use larger than $n(\sigma, j)$ that the $\mathcal{R}_0$ strategy is trying to preserve will eventually be violated by the coding of $B$ into $A_0$. So once $n(\sigma, j)$ is realized and $C$-permitted, instead of immediately putting $n(\sigma, j)$ into $A_0$, we merely promise to put it there if these uses do go away; that is, if $R_\sigma$ becomes accessible again before $n(\sigma, j)$ is initialized.

This delayed permitting still allows $C$ to compute $A_0$, because $C$ can compute $B$, and hence know whether the relevant uses will ever be violated.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.15.2)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be on the true path, and let $\gamma$ be its true outcome. Then $R_\alpha$ succeeds in meeting $\mathcal{R}_{\lvert\alpha\rvert}$, there are infinitely many $\alpha$-stages where the stage does not end at substage $\lvert\alpha\rvert$, and $R_\alpha$ initializes strategies below $\alpha {}^\frown \gamma$ only finitely often.

*Proof.* Assume by induction that the lemma holds for strategies above $\alpha$. Then, since $\alpha$ can be initialized only when $\alpha_s <_L \alpha$ or when a strategy above it initializes it, there is a stage $s_0$ such that $\alpha$ is not initialized after stage $s_0$. After this stage, no $n(\alpha, i)$ is canceled except for the sake of an $n(\alpha, j)$ with $j < i$, and that all of these have been realized (a concept that will be defined below).

If $\gamma = (i, u)$ then $R_\alpha$ does not initialize strategies below $\alpha {}^\frown \gamma$, and at every stage $s > \lvert\alpha\rvert$ at which $\alpha(s, \lvert\alpha\rvert + 1) = \alpha {}^\frown \gamma$, the stage does not end at substage $\lvert\alpha\rvert$.

If $\gamma = (i, f)$, then the $R_\alpha$-module acts only finitely often.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.15.3)</span></p>

$A_0, A_1 \leqslant_T C$.

*Proof.* We do the $A_0$ case, the $A_1$ case being symmetric.

If $n \in A_0$, then $n$ must be chosen by stage $n$ as $n(\alpha, i)$ for some $\alpha$ and $i$ with $\lvert\alpha\rvert$ even and $i \in C$. If that is the case, let $s$ be the stage at which $i$ enters $C$. Then $n$ will be in $A_0$ iff there is an $\alpha$-stage $t \geqslant s$ at which $n(\alpha, i)$ is still equal to $n$. So we may assume that $s$ is not itself an $\alpha$-stage and $n(\alpha, i)$ is not canceled at stage $s$, since otherwise we would already know whether $n \in A_0$ at stage $s$. Thus $\alpha <_L \alpha_s$. We have two cases.

First suppose that $\beta {}^\frown (k, f) \preccurlyeq \alpha$ for some $k$. Then $\beta {}^\frown (j, o) \preccurlyeq \alpha_s$ for some $j > k$ and $o \in \lbrace f, u \rbrace$. Suppose there is a least $\beta {}^\frown (k, f)$-stage $t > s$. If the construction moves to the left of $\beta {}^\frown (k, f)$ between stages $s$ and $t$ then $\alpha$ is initialized, so $n \notin A_0$. Otherwise, either case 1 or case 3 above holds for $\beta$ at stage $t$, so again $\alpha$ is initialized and $n \notin A_0$. Thus, in this case, we know that $n \notin A_0$ no matter what happens after stage $s$.

Now suppose that $\beta {}^\frown (k, u) \preccurlyeq \alpha$ for some $k$. After stage $s$, there can be an $\alpha$-stage only if there is a $\beta {}^\frown (k, u)$-stage. Now $C$ can use $B$ to figure out whether the use of the relevant computation $\Phi^{A_1}_e(n(\beta, k))$ is $B$-correct. If it is $B$-correct, then $n \notin A_0$, since the only way for there to be a further $\beta {}^\frown (k, u)$-stage is for a strategy above or to the left of $\beta {}^\frown (k, u)$ to enumerate something into $A_i$, which causes $\alpha$ to be initialized. If not, then go to that stage of the construction to check whether $n(\sigma, j)$ has not been initialized in the meantime, which is the only case in which $n(\sigma, j)$ enters $A_0$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.17.7 — Jockusch, Martin)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a set $A$.

1. $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree.
2. For all functions $f$, if $f \leqslant_T A$ then $f \leqslant_\text{tt} A$.
3. For all sets $B$, if $B \leqslant_T A$ then $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$.

*Proof.* To show that (i) implies (ii), suppose that $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree and $f = \Phi^A_e$, and let $g(n)$ be the number of steps taken by the computation $\Phi^A_e(n)$. Then $g \leqslant_T A$, so there is a computable function $h$ that majorizes $g$. Let $\Psi$ be the functional defined as follows. On oracle $X$ and input $n$, run the computation $\Phi^X_e(n)$ for $h(n)$ many steps. If this computation converges, then output its value, and otherwise output 0. Then $\Psi$ is a truth table functional, and $\Psi^A = \Phi^A_e = f$.

Clearly (ii) implies (iii). To show that (iii) implies (i), assume that $A$ does not have hyperimmune-free degree and let $g \leqslant_T A$ be a function not dominated by any computable function. We build a set $B \leqslant_T A$ such that $B \not\leqslant_\text{tt} A$. Let $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ be an effective list of all truth tables. For each $e$ and $n$, proceed as follows. If $\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)[g(n)] \!\!\downarrow$ then let $\langle e, n \rangle \in B$ iff $A \nvDash \sigma_{\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)}$. Note that this definition ensures that $\Phi_e$ does not witness a truth table reduction from $A$ to $B$. If $\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)[g(n)] \!\!\uparrow$ then let $\langle e, n \rangle \in B$. Clearly $B \leqslant_T A$. Assume for a contradiction that $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$. Then there is an $e$ such that $\Phi_e$ witnesses this fact. This $\Phi_e$ is total, so there is a computable function $h$ such that $\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)[h(n)] \!\!\downarrow$ for all $n$. Since $h$ does not dominate $g$, there is an $n$ such that $\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)[g(n)] \!\!\downarrow$. For this $n$, we have $\langle e, n \rangle \in B$ iff $A \nvDash \sigma_{\Phi_e(\langle e, n \rangle)}$, contradicting the choice of $e$.

</div>

The above result cannot be extended to wtt-reducibility. Downey constructed a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ such that for all sets $B$, if $B \leqslant_T A$ then $B \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$. Since $A$ is noncomputable and c.e., it has hyperimmune degree.

### 2.18 Minimal Degrees

The proof of Theorem 2.17.3 uses ideas that go back to a fundamental theorem of Spector.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.1 — Minimal Degree)</span></p>

A degree $\mathbf{a} > \mathbf{0}$ is *minimal* if there is no degree $\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{0} < \mathbf{b} < \mathbf{a}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.2 — Spector)</span></p>

*There is a minimal degree below $\mathbf{0}''$.*

*Proof.* As with Theorem 2.17.3, the proof uses forcing with computable perfect trees. As before, we build a sequence $\lbrace T_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ of computable function trees such that $[T_0] \supseteq [T_1] \supseteq [T_2] \supseteq \cdots$, and take our set $A$ of minimal degree to be any element of $\bigcap_n [T_n]$.

At stage 0, we let $T_0$ be the identity map. The action at stage $2e + 1$ is exactly the same as in the proof of Theorem 2.17.3. That is, we let $i \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ be such that $T_{2e}(i) \neq W_e \upharpoonright \lvert T_{2e}(i) \rvert$ and define $T_{2e+1}$ by letting $T_{2e+1}(\sigma) = T_{2e}(i\sigma)$. As before, this action ensures that $A \neq W_e$.

At stage $2e + 2$, we are given $T_{2e+1}$ and want to meet the requirement

$$\mathcal{R}_e : \Phi^A_e \text{ total} \Rightarrow \Phi^A_e \equiv_T \emptyset \,\lor\, A \leqslant_T \Phi^A_e.$$

The first thing we do is follow the method used in the proof of Theorem 2.17.3 to try to force the nontotality of $\Phi^A_e$. We ask $\emptyset''$ whether there are $n$ and $\sigma$ such that $\Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau)}_e(n) \!\!\uparrow$ for all $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma$. If so, we define $T_{2e+2}(\nu) = T_{2e+1}(\sigma\nu)$. This definition clearly ensures that $\Phi^A_e(n) \!\!\uparrow$, and hence $\mathcal{R}_e$ is met.

If there are no such $n$ and $\sigma$, then we try to ensure that if $\Phi^A_e$ is total then it is computable. The critical question to ask $\emptyset''$ is whether there is a $\sigma$ such that for all $n$ and all $\tau_0, \tau_1 \succcurlyeq \sigma$, if $\Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau_j)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$ for $i = 0, 1$, then $\Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau_0)}_e(n) = \Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau_1)}_e(n)$. If this question has a positive answer, then we define $T_{2e+2}$ by letting $T_{2e+2}(\nu) = T_{2e+1}(\sigma\nu)$. Then, to compute $\Phi^A_e(n)$ (assuming $\Phi^A_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$), we simply need to find a $\mu$ such that $\Phi^{T_{2e+2}(\mu)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$, and we are guaranteed that $\Phi^A_e(n) = \Phi^{T_{2e+2}(\mu)}_e(n)$. Thus, in this case, $\Phi^A_e$ is computable if total, and hence $\mathcal{R}_e$ is met.

If the answer to the above question is negative, then we need to ensure that $A \leqslant_T \Phi^A_e$. The crucial new idea is Spector's notion of an *e-splitting tree*.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.3 — e-Splitting)</span></p>

We say that a function tree $T$ is *e-splitting* if for each $\sigma$, there is an $n$ such that $\Phi^{T(\sigma 0)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow \neq \Phi^{T(\sigma 1)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.4)</span></p>

Let $T$ be $e$-splitting and let $B \in [T]$. If $\Phi^B_e$ is total then $\Phi^B_e \geqslant_T B$.

*Proof.* Suppose we are given $\Phi^B_e$, and that $\Phi^B_e$ is total. Search for an $n_0$ such that $\Phi^{T(0)}_e(n_0) \!\!\downarrow \neq \Phi^{T(1)}_e(n_0) \!\!\downarrow$. Let $i$ be such that $\Phi^B_e(n_0) = \Phi^{T(i)}_e(n_0)$. Then we know that $T(i) \prec B$. Now find $n_1$ such that $\Phi^{T(i0)}_e(n_1) \!\!\downarrow \neq \Phi^{T(i1)}_e(n_1) \!\!\downarrow$. Let $j$ be such that $\Phi^B_e(n_1) = \Phi^{T(ij)}_e(n_1)$. Then $T(ij) \prec B$. Continuing in this way, we can compute more and more of $B$. Thus $B \leqslant_T \Phi^B_e$.

</div>

So to meet $\mathcal{R}_e$, it is enough to ensure that $T_{2e+2}$ is $e$-splitting. But recall that we are now in the case in which for each $\sigma$ there are $\tau_0, \tau_1 \succcurlyeq \sigma$ such that $\Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau_0)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow \neq \Phi^{T_{2e+1}(\tau_1)}_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$. So we can define $T_{2e+2}$ as follows. Let $T_{2e+2}(\lambda) = T_{2e+1}(\lambda)$. Suppose we have defined $T_{2e+2}(\mu)$ to equal $T_{2e+1}(\sigma)$ for some $\sigma$. Search for $\tau_0$ and $\tau_1$ as above and define $T_{2e+2}(\mu i) = T_{2e+1}(\tau_i)$ for $i = 0, 1$. Then $T_{2e+2}$ is computable and $e$-splitting.

As with hyperimmune-free degrees, there are in fact continuum many minimal degrees. A crucial difference between hyperimmune-free degrees and minimal degrees is given by the following result. (We say that a degree $\mathbf{b}$ *bounds* a degree $\mathbf{a}$ if $\mathbf{a} \leqslant \mathbf{b}$.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.5 — Yates, Cooper)</span></p>

*Every nonzero c.e. degree bounds a minimal degree.*

In particular, there are minimal degrees below $\mathbf{0}'$ (which was first shown by Sacks), and hence hyperimmune minimal degrees. The method used to prove Theorem 2.18.5, known as the *full approximation method*, is quite involved and would take us a little too far afield. See Lerman or Odifreddi for more details.

</div>

Minimal degrees form a very interesting class. We finish by quoting two important results about their possible Turing degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.6 — Jockusch and Posner)</span></p>

*All minimal degrees are $\text{GL}_2$. That is, if $\mathbf{a}$ is a minimal degree then $\mathbf{a}'' \leqslant (\mathbf{a} \vee \mathbf{0}')'$.*

</div>

This result improved an earlier one by Cooper, who showed that no minimal degree below $\mathbf{0}'$ can be high. (There are low minimal degrees, as well as minimal degrees below $\mathbf{0}'$ that are not low.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.18.7 — Cooper)</span></p>

*If $\mathbf{b} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$ then there is a minimal degree $\mathbf{a}$ with $\mathbf{a}' = \mathbf{b}$.*

</div>

Thus there is an analog of the Friedberg Completeness Criterion (Theorem 2.16.1) for minimal degrees. It is not possible to prove an analog of the Shoenfield Jump Inversion Theorem since Downey, Lempp, and Shore showed that there are degrees $\text{CEA}(\emptyset')$ and low over $\mathbf{0}'$ that are not jumps of minimal degrees below $\mathbf{0}'$.

### 2.19 $\Pi^0_1$ and $\Sigma^0_1$ Classes

#### 2.19.1 Basics

A *tree* is a subset of $2^{<\omega}$ closed under initial segments. A *path* through a tree $T$ is an infinite sequence $P \in 2^\omega$ such that if $\sigma \prec P$ then $\sigma \in T$. The collection of paths through $T$ is denoted by $[T]$. A subset of $2^\omega$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ *class* if it is equal to $[T]$ for some computable tree $T$.

An equivalent formulation is that $C$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class if there is a computable relation $R$ such that

$$C = \lbrace A \in 2^\omega : \forall n\, R(A \upharpoonright n) \rbrace.$$

It is not hard to show that this definition is equivalent to the one via computable trees.

There is a host of natural examples of important $\Pi^0_1$ classes in several branches of logic. For example, let $A$ and $B$ be disjoint c.e. sets. The collection of *separating sets* $\lbrace X : X \supseteq A \,\wedge\, X \cap B = \emptyset \rbrace$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class. An important special case is an *effectively inseparable* pair, that is, a pair of disjoint c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ for which there is a computable function $f$ such that for all disjoint c.e. $W_e \supseteq A$ and $W_j \supseteq B$, we have $f(e, j) \notin W_e \cup W_j$.

Another example of a $\Pi^0_1$ class is the class of (codes of) extensions of a complete consistent theory. By the proof of Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem, for a theory such as Peano Arithmetic (PA), the set $A$ of sentences provable from PA and the set $B$ of sentences refutable from PA form an effectively inseparable pair.

It is easy to check that the intersection of two $\Pi^0_1$ classes is again a $\Pi^0_1$ class, as is their union. The complement of a $\Pi^0_1$ class is a $\Sigma^0_1$ *class*. Thus $C$ is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class iff there is a computable relation $R$ such that

$$C = \lbrace A : \exists n\, R(A \upharpoonright n) \rbrace.$$

We can think of $\Sigma^0_1$ classes as the analogs for infinite sequences of c.e. sets of finite strings. Indeed, an important fact about $\Sigma^0_1$ classes is that they are exactly the subsets of $2^\omega$ generated by c.e. sets of finite strings, in the following sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.1 — Prefix-Free)</span></p>

A set $A \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ is *prefix-free* if it is an antichain with respect to the natural partial ordering of $2^{<\omega}$; that is, for all $\sigma \in A$ and all $\tau$ properly extending $\sigma$, we have $\tau \notin A$.

</div>

Prefix-free sets will play an important role in this book, for example in the definition of prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity given in Section 3.5. Clearly, a set $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is open iff it is of the form $\llbracket A \rrbracket$ for some prefix-free $A \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.2)</span></p>

A set $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class iff there is a c.e. set $W \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $C = \llbracket W \rrbracket$. This fact remains true if we require $W$ to be prefix-free.

*Proof.* Given a $\Sigma^0_1$ class $C$, let $T$ be a computable tree such that $\overline{C} = [T]$. Let $W$ be the set of minimal elements of $\overline{T}$, that is, strings $\sigma \notin T$ such that every proper substring of $\sigma$ is in $T$. Then $W$ is prefix-free and $C = \llbracket W \rrbracket$.

Conversely, let $W \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ be a c.e. set. Then $\llbracket W \rrbracket = \lbrace A : \exists \langle n, s \rangle\, (A \upharpoonright n \in W_s) \rbrace$, so $\llbracket W \rrbracket$ is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class.

</div>

Thus we can think of $\Sigma^0_1$ classes as effectively open sets, and of $\Pi^0_1$ classes as effectively closed sets. So when we have a $\Sigma^0_1$ class $C$, we assume that we have fixed a c.e. set $W$ such that $C = \llbracket W \rrbracket$ and let $C[s] = \llbracket W[s] \rrbracket$. Similarly, for the $\Pi^0_1$ class $P = \overline{C}$, we let $P[s] = \overline{C}[s]$.

A path $P$ through a tree $T$ is *isolated* if there is a $\sigma \prec P$ such that no other path through $T$ extends $\sigma$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.3)</span></p>

Let $T$ be a computable tree and let $P$ be an isolated path through $T$. Then $P$ is computable.

*Proof.* Let $\sigma \prec P$ be such that no path through $T$ other than $P$ extends $\sigma$. For any $n > \lvert\sigma\rvert$, there is exactly one $\tau \succ \sigma$ such that the portion of $T$ above $\tau$ is infinite (by König's Lemma, which says that if a finitely branching tree is infinite, then it has a path). So to compute $P \upharpoonright n$ for $n > \lvert\sigma\rvert$, we look for an $m \geqslant n$ such that exactly one $\tau \succ \sigma$ of length $n$ has an extension of length $m$ in $T$. Then $P \upharpoonright n = \tau$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.4)</span></p>

Let $C$ be a $\Pi^0_1$ class with only finitely many members. Then every member of $C$ is computable.

*Proof.* Let $T$ be a computable tree such that $C = [T]$. Then $T$ has only finitely many paths, so every path through $T$ is isolated.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.5)</span></p>

Let $C$ be a nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class with no computable members. Then $\lvert C \rvert = 2^{\aleph_0}$.

*Proof.* Let $T$ be a computable tree such that $C = [T]$. Let $S$ be the set of *extendible* elements of $T$, that is, those $\sigma$ such that there is an extension of $\sigma$ in $[T]$. If every $\sigma \in S$ has two incompatible extensions in $S$, then it is easy to show that there are continuum many paths through $T$. Otherwise, there is a $\sigma \in S$ with a unique extension $P \in [T]$. As in the previous proof, $P$ is computable.

</div>

Note also that the question of whether a given computable tree $T$ is finite is an existential one, since it amounts to asking whether there is an $n$ such that no string of length $n$ is in $T$, and hence it can be decided by $\emptyset'$.

The *Cantor-Bendixson derivative* of a $\Pi^0_1$ class $C$ is the class $C'$ obtained from $C$ by removing the isolated points of $C$ (i.e., those elements $X \in C$ such that for some $n$, we have $\llbracket X \upharpoonright n \rrbracket \cap C = \lbrace X \rbrace$). Let $C^{(0)} = C$. For an ordinal $\delta$, let $C^{(\delta + 1)} = (C^{\delta})'$, and for a limit ordinal $\delta$, let $C^{(\delta)} = \bigcap_{\gamma < \delta} C^{(\gamma)}$. The *rank* of $X \in C$ is the least $\delta$ such that $X \in C^{(\delta)} \setminus C^{(\delta+1)}$, if such a $\delta$ exists. Note that, by Proposition 2.19.3, if $X$ has rank 0, then it is computable. If all the elements of $C$ have ranks, then the *rank* of $C$ is the supremum of the ranks of its elements (or, equivalently, the least $\delta$ such that $C^{(\delta + 1)} = \emptyset$).

#### 2.19.2 $\Pi^0_n$ and $\Sigma^0_n$ Classes

There is a hierarchy of classes, akin to the arithmetic hierarchy of sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.6 — $\Pi^0_n$ and $\Sigma^0_n$ Classes)</span></p>

A set $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is a $\Pi^0_n$ *class* if there is a computable relation $R$ such that

$$C = \lbrace A : \forall k_1 \,\exists k_2 \cdots Q k_n\, R(A \upharpoonright k_1, A \upharpoonright k_2, \ldots, A \upharpoonright k_n) \rbrace,$$

where the quantifiers alternate and hence $Q = \forall$ if $n$ is odd and $Q = \exists$ if $n$ is even. The complement of a $\Pi^0_n$ class is a $\Sigma^0_n$ *class*.

</div>

These definitions can be relativized to a given set $X$ (by letting $R$ be $X$-computable) to obtain the notions of $\Pi^{0,X}_n$ class and $\Sigma^{0,X}_n$ class. A $\Pi^0_{n+1}$ class is also a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n)}}_1$ class, since every predicate of the form $\exists k_2 \cdots Q k_n\, R(A \upharpoonright k_1, A \upharpoonright k_2, \ldots, A \upharpoonright k_n)$ with $R$ computable is computable in $\emptyset^{(n)}$. Similarly, every $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ class is also a $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n)}}_1$ class. The converse, however, is not always true, because every $\Pi^{0,\emptyset}_1$ class is closed (indeed, every $\Pi^{0,X}_1$ class is closed for any $X$), but there are $\Pi^0_2$ classes that are not closed.

We say that the $\Sigma^0_1$ classes $C_0, C_1, \ldots$ are *uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$*, and their complements are *uniformly $\Pi^0_1$*, if there are uniformly c.e. sets $V_0, V_1, \ldots \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $C_i = \llbracket V_i \rrbracket$ for all $i$. It is not hard to see that $P_0, P_1, \ldots$ are uniformly $\Pi^0_1$ classes iff there are uniformly computable trees $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ such that $P_i = [T_i]$ for all $i$.

As one might imagine, a $\Delta^0_1$ *class* is one such that both it and its complement are $\Sigma^0_1$. It is not hard to see that $C$ is a $\Delta^0_1$ class iff $C = \llbracket F \rrbracket$ for some finite $F \subset 2^{<\omega}$, so a $\Delta^0_1$ class is just a clopen subset of $2^\omega$. The classes $C_0, C_1, \ldots$ are *uniformly $\Delta^0_1$* if there are uniformly computable finite sets $F_0, F_1, \ldots \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $C_i = \llbracket F_i \rrbracket$ for all $i$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.7)</span></p>

From an index for a $\Sigma^0_1$ class $C$, we can computably find an index for a (prefix-free) c.e. set $W \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $C = \llbracket W \rrbracket$, and vice versa.

</div>

Another way to look at this hierarchy of classes is in terms of effective unions and intersections. In the same way that $\Pi^0_1$ and $\Sigma^0_1$ classes are the effective analogs of closed and open sets, respectively, $\Pi^0_2$ and $\Sigma^0_2$ classes are the effective analogs of $\text{G}_\delta$ and $\text{F}_\sigma$ sets, respectively (where a set is $\text{G}_\delta$ if it is a countable intersection of open sets and $\text{F}_\sigma$ if it is a countable union of closed sets). More precisely, $C$ is a $\Pi^0_2$ class iff there are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ such that $C = \bigcap_n U_n$. Similarly, $C$ is a $\Sigma^0_2$ class iff there are uniformly $\Pi^0_1$ classes $P_0, P_1, \ldots$ such that $C = \bigcup_n P_n$. The same is true for any $n$ in place of 1 and $n + 1$ in place of 2.

#### 2.19.3 Basis Theorems

A *basis* for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes is a collection of sets $\mathcal{C}$ such that every nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class has an element in $\mathcal{C}$. One of the most important classes of results on $\Pi^0_1$ classes is that of basis theorems. A *basis theorem* for $\Pi^0_1$ classes states that every nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class has a member of a certain type. (Henceforth, all $\Pi^0_1$ classes will be taken to be nonempty without further comment.)

For instance, it is not hard to check that the lexicographically least element of a $\Pi^0_1$ class $C$ (i.e., the leftmost path of a computable tree $T$ such that $[T] = C$) has c.e. degree. This fact establishes the Computably Enumerable Basis Theorem of Jockusch and Soare, which states that every nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class has a member of c.e. degree, and is a refinement of the earlier Kreisel Basis Theorem, which states that every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a $\Delta^0_2$ member.

The following is the most famous and widely applicable basis theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.8 — Low Basis Theorem, Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

*Every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a low member.*

*Proof.* Let $C = [T]$ with $T$ a computable tree. We define a sequence $T = T_0 \supseteq T_1 \supseteq \cdots$ of infinite computable trees such that if $P \in \bigcap_e [T_e]$ then $P$ is low.

We begin with $T_0 = 2^{<\omega}$. Suppose that we have defined $T_e$ to be a computable tree. Let

$$U_e = \lbrace \sigma : \Phi^\sigma_e(e)[\lvert\sigma\rvert] \!\!\uparrow \rbrace.$$

Then $U_e$ is a computable tree. If $U_e \cap T_e$ is infinite, then let $T_{e+1} = T_e \cap U_e$. Otherwise, let $T_{e+1} = T_e$. Note that either $\Phi^P_e(e) \!\!\uparrow$ for all $P \in [T_{e+1}]$ or $\Phi^P_e(e) \!\!\downarrow$ for all $P \in [T_{e+1}]$.

Now suppose that $P \in \bigcap_e [T_e]$. We can perform the above construction computably in $\emptyset'$, since $\emptyset'$ can decide the question of whether $U_e \cap T_e$ is finite. Thus $P' \leqslant_T \emptyset'$, since to decide whether $\Phi^P_e(e) \!\!\downarrow$ for any given $e$, and hence $P$ is low.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.9 — Jockusch and Soare, Schaefer)</span></p>

Every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a superlow member.

</div>

The above proof has a consequence that will be useful below. Recall that a set $A$ is superlow if $A' \equiv_\text{tt} \emptyset'$. Marcus Schaefer observed that the proof of the low basis theorem actually produces a superlow set.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.10 — Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

Let $C$ be a $\Pi^0_1$ class and let $X$ be a noncomputable set. Then there is an $A \in C$ such that $X \not\leqslant_T A$ and $A' \leqslant_T X \oplus \emptyset'$.

*Proof.* Let $C = [T]$ with $T$ a computable tree. As in the proof of the low basis theorem, we define a sequence $T = T_0 \supseteq T_1 \supseteq \cdots$ of infinite computable trees.

We begin with $T_0 = 2^{<\omega}$. Suppose that we have defined $T_{2e}$ to be a computable tree. We first proceed as in the proof of the low basis theorem. Let $U_e = \lbrace \sigma : \Phi^\sigma_e(e)[\lvert\sigma\rvert] \!\!\uparrow \rbrace$. If $U_e \cap T_{2e}$ is infinite, then let $T_{2e+1} = T_{2e} \cap U_e$. Otherwise, let $T_{2e+1} = T_{2e}$. As before, $T_{2e+1}$ is a computable tree.

We now handle cone-avoidance as follows. We $X \oplus \emptyset'$-computably search until we find that one of the following holds.

1. There are an $n$ and an extendable $\sigma \in T_{2e+1}$ such that $\Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\downarrow \neq X(n)$.
2. There is an $n$ such that $T_{2e+1} \cap \lbrace \sigma : \Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\uparrow \rbrace$ is infinite.

We claim one of the above must hold. Suppose not, and fix $n$. The fact that 2 does not hold means that we can find a $k$ such that $\Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$ for all $\sigma \in T_{2e+1} \cap 2^k$. The fact that 1 does not hold means that, for each such $\sigma$, if $\Phi^\sigma_e(n) \neq X(n)$, then $T_{2e+1}$ is finite above $\sigma$. Since $X$ is noncomputable, either 1 or 2 must hold. If we find that 1 holds, then let $T_{2e+2} = T_{2e+1} \cap \lbrace \tau : \sigma \preccurlyeq \tau \rbrace$. Otherwise, let $T_{2e+2} = T_{2e+1} \cap \lbrace \sigma : \Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\uparrow \rbrace$. The definition of the trees $T_{2e+2}$ ensures that $X \not\leqslant_T A$.

Now let $A \in \bigcap_e [T_e]$. It is easy to check that we can perform the above construction computably in $X \oplus \emptyset'$, so as in the proof of the low basis theorem, we have $A' \leqslant_T X \oplus \emptyset'$. Furthermore, the definition of the trees $T_{2e+2}$ ensures that $X \not\leqslant_T A$.

</div>

Corollary 2.19.9 and Theorem 2.19.10 cannot be combined. That is, there are a $\Pi^0_1$ class $C$ and a noncomputable set $X$ such that $X$ is computable from every superlow member of $C$. We will see examples in Section 14.4.

Another important basis theorem is provided by the hyperimmune-free degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.11 — Hyperimmune-Free Basis Theorem, Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

*Every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a member of hyperimmune-free degree.*

*Proof.* Let $C$ be a $\Pi^0_1$ class, and let $T$ be a computable tree such that $C = [T]$. We can carry out a construction like that in the proof of Miller and Martin's Theorem 2.17.3 within $T$. We begin with $T_0 = T$. We do not need the odd stages of that construction since we do not need to force noncomputability (as $\mathbf{0}$ is hyperimmune-free). Thus we deal with $\Phi_e$ at stage $e + 1$.

We are given $T_e$ and we want to build $T_{e+1}$ to ensure that either

(i) if $A \in [T_{e+1}]$ then $\Phi^A_e$ is not total, or
(ii) there is a computable function $f$ such that if $A \in [T_{e+1}]$ then $\Phi^A_e(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$.

Let

$$U^n_e = \lbrace \sigma : \Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\uparrow \rbrace.$$

There are two cases. If there is an $n$ such that $U^n_e \cap T_e$ is infinite, then let $T_{e+1} = U^n_e \cap T_e$. In this case, if $A \in [T_{e+1}]$ then $\Phi^A_e(n) \!\!\uparrow$.

Otherwise, let $T_{e+1} = T_e$. In this case, for each $n$ we can compute a number $l(n)$ such that no string $\sigma \in T_e$ of length $l(n)$ is in $U^n_e$. For any such $\sigma$, we have $\Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\downarrow$. Define the computable function $f$ by

$$f(n) = \max\lbrace \Phi^\sigma_e(n) : \sigma \in T_e \,\wedge\, \lvert\sigma\rvert = l(n) \rbrace.$$

Then $A \in [T_{e+1}]$ implies that $\Phi^A_e(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$. So if we let $A \in \bigcap_e [T_e]$, then $A$ is a member of $C$ and has hyperimmune-free degree.

</div>

In the above construction, if $[T]$ has no computable members, then Proposition 2.19.5 implies that each $T_e$ must have size $2^{\aleph_0}$. It is not hard to adjust the construction in this case to obtain continuum many members of $C$ of hyperimmune-free degree. Thus, a $\Pi^0_1$ class with no computable members has continuum many members of hyperimmune-free degree.

The following result is an immediate consequence of the hyperimmune-free basis theorem and Theorem 2.17.2, but it was first explicitly articulated by Kautz, who gave an interesting direct proof. Recall that a set $A$ is computably enumerable in and above a set $X$ ($\text{CEA}(X)$) if $A$ is c.e. in $X$ and $X \leqslant_T A$. A set $A$ is *CEA* if it is $\text{CEA}(X)$ for some $X <_T A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.12 — Jockusch and Soare, Kautz)</span></p>

Every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a member that is not CEA.

</div>

Although the existence of minimal degrees (Theorem 2.18.2) was proved by techniques similar to those used to prove the hyperimmune-free basis theorem, there is no "minimal basis theorem", since there are $\Pi^0_1$ classes with no members of minimal degree. An example is the $\Pi^0_1$ class of all complete extensions of Peano Arithmetic, by Theorem 2.21.9 below. However, using techniques beyond the scope of this book, Groszek and Slaman proved the following elegant result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.13 — Groszek and Slaman)</span></p>

*There is a $\Pi^0_1$ class such that every member either is c.e. and noncomputable, or has minimal degree.*

</div>

We end this section by looking at examples of *nonbasis* theorems.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.14)</span></p>

The intersection of all bases for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes is the collection of computable sets, and hence there is no minimal basis.

*Proof.* If $A$ is a computable set then $\lbrace A \rbrace$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class, so every basis for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes must include $A$. On the other hand, if $B$ is noncomputable then, by Corollary 2.19.4, $\lbrace B \rbrace$ is not a $\Pi^0_1$ class, so every $\Pi^0_1$ class must contain a member other than $B$, and hence the collection of all sets other than $B$ is a basis for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes.

</div>

For our next result, we will need the following definition and lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.15 — Effectively Immune)</span></p>

An infinite set $A$ is *effectively immune* if there is a computable function $f$ such that for all $e$, if $W_e \subseteq A$ then $\lvert W_e \rvert \leqslant f(e)$.

</div>

Post gave the following construction of an effectively immune co-c.e. set $A$. At stage $s$, for each $e < s$, if $W_e[s] \subseteq A_s$ and there is an $x \in W_e[s]$ such that $x > 2e$, then put the least such $x$ into $\overline{A}$. Then $A$ is infinite, and is effectively immune via the function $e \mapsto 2e$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.16 — Martin)</span></p>

If a c.e. set $B$ computes an effectively immune set, then $B$ is Turing complete.

*Proof.* Let $B$ be c.e., and let $A \leqslant_T B$ be effectively immune, as witnessed by the computable function $f$. Let $\Gamma$ be a reduction such that $\Gamma^B = A$.

For each $k$ we build a c.e. set $W_{h(k)}$, where the computable function $h$ is given by the recursion theorem with parameters. Initially, $W_{h(k)}$ is empty. If $k$ enters $\emptyset'$ at stage $t$ then we wait until a stage $s \geqslant t$ at which there is a $q$ such that $\Gamma^B(n)[s] \!\!\downarrow$ for all $n < q$ and there are more than $f(h(k))$ many numbers in $\Gamma^B[s] \upharpoonright q$. We then let $W_{h(k)} = \Gamma^B[s] \upharpoonright q$. This action ensures that $W_{h(k)} \nsubseteq A$. Thus, there is an $n$ such that $\Phi^\sigma_e(n) \!\!\downarrow \neq X(n)$.

So to compute $\emptyset'(k)$ from $B$, we simply look for a stage $s$ at which there is a $q$ such that $\Gamma^B(n)[s] \!\!\downarrow$ for all $n < q$ and there are more than $f(h(k))$ many numbers in $\Gamma^B[s] \upharpoonright q$. If $k$ is not in $\emptyset'$ by stage $s$, it cannot later enter $\emptyset'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.17 — Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

(i) The incomplete c.e. degrees do not form a basis for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes.

(ii) Let $\mathbf{a}$ be an incomplete c.e. degree. Then the degrees less than or equal to $\mathbf{a}$ do not form a basis for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes.

*Proof.* By Lemma 2.19.16, to prove both parts of the theorem, it is enough to find a $\Pi^0_1$ class whose members are all effectively immune. Let $A$ be Post's effectively immune set described after Definition 2.19.15. An infinite subset of an effectively immune set is clearly also effectively immune, so it is enough to find a $\Pi^0_1$ class all of whose members are infinite subsets of $A$.

It is clear from the construction of $A$ that $\lvert \overline{A} \upharpoonright 2e \rvert \leqslant e$ for all $e$. Thus $A \cap [2^k - 1, 2^{k+1} - 2] \neq \emptyset$ for all $k$. Let

$$\mathcal{P} = \lbrace B : B \subseteq A \,\wedge\, \forall k\, (B \cap [2^k - 1, 2^{k+1} - 2] \neq \emptyset) \rbrace.$$

Since $A$ is co-c.e., $\mathcal{P}$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class, and it is nonempty because $A \in \mathcal{P}$. Furthermore, every element of $\mathcal{P}$ is an infinite subset of $A$. As explained in the previous paragraph, these facts suffice to establish the theorem.

</div>

#### 2.19.4 Generalizing the Low Basis Theorem

We would like to extend the low basis theorem above $\mathbf{0}'$. Of course, we cannot hope to prove that for every degree $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$, every $\Pi^0_1$ class has a member whose jump has degree $\mathbf{a}$, since there are $\Pi^0_1$ classes whose members are all computable. We can prove this result, however, if we restrict our attention to *special* $\Pi^0_1$ classes, which are those with no computable members.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.18 — Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

If $C$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class with no computable members and $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$, then there is a $P \in C$ such that $\deg(P') = \deg(P \oplus \emptyset') = \mathbf{a}$.

*Proof.* Let $C = [T]$ with $T$ a computable tree. We $A$-computably define an infinite sequence of computable trees $T_0 \supseteq T_1 \supseteq \cdots$, each with $2^{\aleph_0}$ many paths. We begin with $T_0 = T$.

Given $T_{2e}$, let

$$U_{2e} = \lbrace \sigma \in T_{2e} : \Phi^\sigma_e(e)[\lvert\sigma\rvert] \!\!\uparrow \rbrace.$$

If $U_{2e}$ is finite then let $T_{2e+1} = T_{2e}$. Otherwise, since $U_{2e}$ is a subtree of $T$, it has no computable paths, and hence has $2^{\aleph_0}$ many paths. In this case, let $T_{2e+1} = U_{2e}$.

Now let $\sigma_0, \sigma_1$ be the length-lexicographically least pair of incomparable strings in $T_{2e+1}$ such that for each $i < 2$, the subtree of $T_{2e+1}$ above $\sigma_i$ is infinite (and hence has $2^{\aleph_0}$ many paths). Notice that we can find $\sigma_0, \sigma_1$ using $A$, again because $A \geqslant_T \emptyset'$. Let $T_{2e+2}$ be the subtree of $T_{2e+1}$ above $\sigma_{A(e)}$.

Having defined the $T_e$, let $P \in \bigcap_e [T_e]$. The choice of $T_{2e+1}$ determines whether $\Phi^P_e(e) \!\!\downarrow$, so, since the entire construction is $A$-computable, $P' \leqslant_T A$. On the other hand, we can also perform the construction $(P \oplus \emptyset')$-computably, since all the steps can be done $\emptyset'$-computably, except the choice of which $\sigma_i$ to extend in defining $T_{2e+2}$, which can be done $P$-computably, since the $\sigma_i$ we choose at that stage is the one extended by $P$. Furthermore, that choice determines the $e$th bit of $A$. Thus $A \leqslant_T P \oplus \emptyset'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.19.19 — Jockusch and Soare)</span></p>

For each $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$, the sets with degrees in $\lbrace \mathbf{b} : \mathbf{b}' = \mathbf{a} \rbrace \cup \lbrace \mathbf{0} \rbrace$ form a basis for the $\Pi^0_1$ classes.

</div>

### 2.20 Strong Reducibilities and Post's Program

The concept of hypersimplicity, which we defined in Section 2.17, was introduced by Post as part of an attempt to solve Post's Problem. Although hypersimple sets can be Turing complete, Post showed that no hypersimple set can be tt-complete. Later, Friedberg and Rogers proved that no hypersimple set can be wtt-complete. This result has been extended as follows. A set $A$ is *wtt-cuppable* if there is a c.e. $W <_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$ such that $A \oplus W \geqslant_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(2.20.1 — Downey and Jockusch)</span></p>

*No hypersimple set is wtt-cuppable.*

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is hypersimple, $W$ is c.e., $W \leqslant_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$, and $A \oplus W \geqslant_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. We show that $W \geqslant_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. By the recursion theorem, we may assume that we have a wtt-reduction $\Gamma^{A \oplus W} = B$ with computable use $g$. Without loss of generality, we may assume that $g(n)$ is increasing and even for all $n$ and write $h(n)$ for $\frac{g(n)}{2}$.

We define a strong array $\lbrace F_i \rbrace_{i \in \mathbb{N}}$ as follows, writing $m(i)$ for max $F_i$. Let $F_0 = [0, h(0)]$. Given $F_i$, pick $m(i) + 1$ many fresh large numbers $b^{i+1}_0 < \cdots < b^{i+1}_{m(i)}$ and let $F_{i+1} = (\max F_i, h(b^{i+1}_{m(i)})]$. Since the $F_i$ form a strong array, there are infinitely many $i$ with $F_i \subseteq A$. We can therefore enumerate an increasing computable sequence $i_0 < i_1 < \cdots$ such that $F_{i_k+1} \subseteq A$ for all $k$.

Now for each $k$, we act as follows. Let $b = b^{i_k+1}_{m(i_k)}$. We wait until a stage $s_k$ such that $F_{i_k+1} \subseteq A_{s_k}$. If $k$ enters $\emptyset'$ at stage $t_k \geqslant s_k$, we wait for a stage $s$ such that $\Gamma^{A \oplus W}[s] \upharpoonright (b+1) = B_s \upharpoonright (b+1)$, then put $b^{i_k+1}_0$ into $B$. If later $A$ changes below $h(b)$, we again wait for a stage $s$ such that $\Gamma^{A \oplus W}[s] \upharpoonright (b+1) = B_s \upharpoonright (b+1)$, then put $b^{i_k+1}_j$ into $B$. We continue in this manner, enumerating one more $b^{i_k+1}_j$ into $B$ each time $A$ changes below $h(b)$ and the computation $\Gamma^{A \oplus W} \upharpoonright (b+1)$ recovers.

Thus $W \upharpoonright h(b)$ changes at least once after stage $t_k$. Since $F_{i_k+1} \subseteq A_{s_k}$, we cannot have more than $m(i_k) + 1$ many changes in $A$ below $h(b)$ after stage $s_k$, so we never run out of $b^{i_k+1}_j$'s. Since we change $B \upharpoonright (b+1)$ after the last time $A \upharpoonright h(b)$ changes, $W \upharpoonright h(b)$ must change at that point to ensure that $\Gamma^{A \oplus W} \upharpoonright (b+1) = B \upharpoonright (b+1)$.

So $\emptyset'(k)$ from $W$ is computed as follows: simply look for a stage $s$ at which $W_s \upharpoonright h(b) = W \upharpoonright h(b)$. Then $k \in \emptyset'$ iff $k \in \emptyset'_s$.

</div>
