If we code this length as a binary string $\rho$, then we can give this length in an unambiguous way as $\overline{\rho}$, so $\overline{\rho}\sigma^*_C\tau^*_C$ unambiguously describes $\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle$.

We will use Kolmogorov complexity to study issues related to the relative algorithmic randomness of infinite sequences, but it has also found many other applications. For instance, there is a well-developed theory applying incompressibility to avoid counting in combinatorial arguments. This method is extensively used for lower bound arguments in complexity theory, and is one of the principal reasons for much of the interest in $C$-complexity. We will not dwell on such applications of Kolmogorov complexity here, since they are not directly related to our central theme, but will give a short but interesting example: the construction of an **immune set**, that is, a set with no infinite c.e. subset.

Let $A = \lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma) \geqslant \frac{|\sigma|}{2} \rbrace$. Then $A$ is immune. Indeed, suppose that $A$ has an infinite c.e. subset $B$. Let $h(n)$ be the first string of length greater than $n$ to enter $B$. Then $C(h(n)) \geqslant \frac{|h(n)|}{2} > \frac{n}{2}$, since $h(n) \in A$, but we can generate $h(n)$ given $n$ simply by running the enumeration of $B$ until a string of length greater than $n$ appears, so $C(h(n)) \leqslant C(n) + O(1) \leqslant \log n + O(1)$. For large enough $n$, this is a contradiction.

### 3.2 Conditional complexity

It is often useful to measure the compressibility of a string *given another string*. To do so, we fix a **universal oracle machine** $U$ such that for each oracle Turing machine $M$ there is a coding string $\rho_M$ such that

$$\forall X\, \forall \sigma\, [U^X(\rho_M \sigma) = M^X(\sigma)],$$

where $X$ ranges over all oracles, both finite and infinite. For strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$, the **(plain) Kolmogorov complexity of $\sigma$ given $\tau$** is

$$C(\sigma \mid \tau) = \min\lbrace |\mu| : U^{\overline{\tau}}(\mu) = \sigma \rbrace,$$

where $\overline{\tau}$ is as defined in the proof of Proposition 3.1.5. (The reason we use $\overline{\tau}$ instead of $\tau$ is the following. By the way we have defined the action of oracle machines on finite oracles in Section 2.4.1, for any such machine $M$ and any $\mu$, the set of oracles $\rho$ such that $M^\rho(\mu) \!\!\downarrow$ is prefix-free, as defined in Definition 2.19.1. It is not hard to use this fact to show that $\min\lbrace |\mu| : U^\tau(\mu) = \sigma \rbrace$ does not behave as we would expect conditional complexity to behave. For example, $\min\lbrace |\mu| : U^\sigma(\mu) = \sigma \rbrace$ is not bounded by a constant, but we of course want to have $C(\sigma \mid \sigma) \leqslant O(1)$. With the definition we have given, we do have this property, since it is easy to define an oracle Turing machine $M$ such that $M^{\overline{\sigma}}(\lambda) = \sigma$ for all $\sigma$.)

Notice that this notion of conditional Kolmogorov complexity reduces to unconditional Kolmogorov complexity by letting $\tau = \lambda$, the empty string. That is, $C(\sigma) = C(\sigma \mid \lambda) \pm O(1)$.

In Proposition 3.1.2, we saw that $C(\sigma^*_C) = C(\sigma, C(\sigma)) \pm O(1)$. We can say a little more using conditional complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.2.1)</span></p>

$C(\sigma^*_C \mid \sigma) = C(C(\sigma) \mid \sigma) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

The proof is essentially the same as that of Proposition 3.1.2.

Easy counting arguments give us the following basic result relating the size of a finite set to the complexity of its elements.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.2.2 — Kolmogorov)</span></p>

(i) Let $A \subset 2^{<\omega}$ be finite. Then for each $\tau$ there is a $\sigma \in A$ such that $C(\sigma \mid \tau) \geqslant \log |A|$.

(ii) Let $B \subseteq 2^{<\omega} \times 2^{<\omega}$ be an infinite computably enumerable set such that each set of the form $B_\tau = \lbrace \sigma : \langle \sigma, \tau \rangle \in B \rbrace$ is finite. Then for every $\tau$ and every $\sigma \in B_\tau$, we have $C(\sigma \mid \tau) \leqslant \log |B_\tau| + O(1)$, where the constant term is independent of both $\sigma$ and $\tau$.

</div>

*Proof.* Part (i) is a counting argument, as in the proof of Proposition 3.1.3. For part (ii), given $\tau$, we can describe $\sigma \in B_\tau$ by giving its position in the enumeration of $B_\tau$ derived from a fixed enumeration of $B$. This position is less than or equal to $|B_\tau|$, and hence can be coded by a string of length less than or equal to $\log |B_\tau|$.

Universal oracle machines also allow us to relativize the notion of plain Kolmogorov complexity to any oracle $A$, by letting $U$ be such a machine and defining $C^A(\sigma) = \min\lbrace |\tau| : U^A(\tau) = \sigma \rbrace$. All the basic properties of plain complexity continue to hold in the relativized case, with the same proofs. It is also easy to show that if $B \geqslant_T A$ then $C^B(\sigma) \leqslant C^A(\sigma) + O(1)$.

### 3.3 Symmetry of information

The **(plain) information content** of a string $\tau$ in a string $\sigma$ is defined as

$$I_C(\sigma : \tau) = C(\tau) - C(\tau \mid \sigma).$$

This notion is meant to capture the difference between the intrinsic difficulty of producing $\tau$ given $\sigma$ and that of producing $\tau$ *without* $\sigma$. The following famous result expresses concisely the relationship between $I_C(\sigma : \tau)$ and $I_C(\tau : \sigma)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.3.1 — Symmetry of Information, Levin and Kolmogorov)</span></p>

$$I_C(\sigma : \tau) = I_C(\tau : \sigma) \pm O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)).$$

</div>

Note that it follows immediately from this result that $I_C(\sigma : \tau) = I_C(\tau : \sigma) \pm O(\log n)$, where $n = \max\lbrace |\tau|, |\sigma| \rbrace$.

Theorem 3.3.1 follows easily from the following restated version.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.3.2 — Symmetry of Information, Restated)</span></p>

$$C(\sigma, \tau) = C(\tau \mid \sigma) + C(\sigma) \pm O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)).$$

</div>

To obtain Theorem 3.3.1 from Theorem 3.3.2, we argue as follows. We have

$$C(\sigma, \tau) = C(\tau \mid \sigma) + C(\sigma) \pm O(\log C(\sigma, \tau))$$

and

$$C(\tau, \sigma) = C(\sigma \mid \tau) + C(\tau) \pm O(\log C(\tau, \sigma)).$$

But $C(\sigma, \tau) = C(\tau, \sigma) \pm O(1)$, so

$$I_C(\sigma : \tau) = C(\tau) - C(\tau \mid \sigma) = C(\sigma) - C(\sigma \mid \tau) \pm O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)) = I_C(\tau : \sigma) \pm O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)).$$

*Proof of Theorem 3.3.2.* It is easy to see that

$$C(\sigma, \tau) \leqslant C(\sigma) + C(\tau \mid \sigma) + O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)),$$

since we can describe $\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle$ via a description of $\sigma$, a description of $\tau$ given $\sigma$, and an indication of where to delimit the two descriptions, as in Proposition 3.1.5.

For the other direction, let

$$A = \lbrace (\mu, \nu) : C(\mu, \nu) \leqslant C(\sigma, \tau) \rbrace$$

and

$$A_\mu = \lbrace \nu : (\mu, \nu) \in A \rbrace.$$

The set $A$ is finite, and there is a uniform procedure (over all choices of $\sigma$ and $\tau$) for enumerating it given $C(\sigma, \tau)$. Similarly, the sets $A_\mu$ are finite and there is a uniform procedure (again over all choices of $\sigma$ and $\tau$) for enumerating them given $C(\sigma, \tau)$ and $\mu$. Hence, one can describe $\tau$ given $\sigma$ and $C(\sigma, \tau)$, along with $\tau$'s place in the enumeration order of $A_\sigma$. Thus

$$C(\tau \mid \sigma) \leqslant \log |A_\sigma| + 2 \log C(\sigma, \tau) + O(1).$$

Let $N = 2^{\log |A_\sigma| - 1}$. Since $\log N = \log |A_\sigma| - 1$,

$$C(\tau \mid \sigma) \leqslant \log N + O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)). \tag{3.1}$$

Now consider the set $B = \lbrace \mu : |A_\mu| \geqslant N \rbrace$. We have $\sigma \in B$. Furthermore, there is a uniform procedure (over all choices of $\sigma$ and $\tau$) for enumerating $B$ given $C(\sigma, \tau)$ and $N$, and $|B| \leqslant \frac{|A|}{N} \leqslant \frac{2^{C(\sigma,\tau)}}{N}$. Thus, to describe $\sigma$, we need only give $C(\sigma, \tau)$ and $N$, along with $\sigma$'s place in the enumeration order of $B$. Now, $N$ can be specified with $\log \log |A_\sigma|$ many bits, so

$$C(\sigma) \leqslant \log \frac{2^{C(\sigma,\tau)}}{N} + 2 \log \log |A_\sigma| + 2 \log C(\sigma, \tau) + O(1).$$

But $|A_\sigma| \leqslant 2^{C(\sigma,\tau)}$, so

$$C(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma, \tau) - \log N + O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)). \tag{3.2}$$

Combining (3.1) and (3.2), we have

$$C(\sigma) + C(\tau \mid \sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma, \tau) + O(\log C(\sigma, \tau)),$$

as required.

### 3.4 Information-theoretic characterizations of computability

In this section, we establish some combinatorial facts about the number of strings of a specified complexity, and show that one can use Kolmogorov complexity to provide information-theoretic characterizations of computability. We begin with a result of Loveland. If $A$ is a computable set then we can generate $A \upharpoonright n$ simply by running the computation of $A(i)$ for all $i < n$. Thus $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) = O(1)$, where the constant depends on $A$. Loveland's result is a converse to this fact.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.1 — Loveland)</span></p>

Let $X$ be an infinite computable set. For each $e$, there are only finitely many sets $A$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant e$ for all $n \in X$, and each such $A$ is computable.

</div>

It is interesting to note that Loveland also showed that one cannot weaken the hypothesis of the above theorem to simply say that $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant e$ for infinitely many $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.2 — Chaitin)</span></p>

Let $D : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ be partial computable. There is a constant $c$ such that letting $h(d) = 2^{l(d+c)}$, for each $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$,

$$|\lbrace \tau : D(\tau) = \sigma \wedge |\tau| \leqslant C(\sigma) + d \rbrace| < h(d).$$

That is, the number of $D$-descriptions of $\sigma$ of length at most $C(\sigma) + d$ is $O(2^d)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.3 — Chaitin)</span></p>

There is a computable function $h$ with $h(d) = O(2^d)$ such that, for all $n$ and $d$,

$$|\lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : C(\sigma) \leqslant C(n) + d \rbrace| \leqslant h(d).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.4 — Chaitin)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computable iff $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$. Furthermore, for each $k$ there are only $O(2^d)$ many $A$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + d$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $A$ is computable, then clearly $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$. Now let $e$ be such that $C(n) \leqslant \log n + e$ for all $n$. Fix $d$ and let

$$T = \lbrace \sigma : \forall \tau \preccurlyeq \sigma\, (C(\tau) \leqslant \log |\tau| + d + e) \rbrace.$$

The tree $T$ is c.e. We want to transform it into a computable tree. Each $I_k$ contains some $n$ such that $C(n) \geqslant \log n$. For this $n$, we have $|2^n \cap T| \leqslant h(d + e)$ for all $k$. Let $c \leqslant h(d + e)$ be the largest number such that $m_k = c$ for infinitely many $k$, and let $l$ be such that $m_k \leqslant c$ for all $k > l$. Let $S$ be a computable tree with $[S] = [T]$. Then $S$ has width $c \leqslant h(d + e) = O(2^d)$, so there are only $O(2^d)$ many $A$ in $[S]$, and they are all computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.5 — Chaitin)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computable iff there is an infinite computable set $S$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$ for all $n \in S$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.4.6 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

Let $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$. Then $|\lbrace \tau : C(\sigma, \tau) \leqslant C(\sigma) + d \rbrace| \leqslant O(d^4 2^d)$, where the constant does not depend on $\sigma$.

</div>

### 3.5 Prefix-free machines and complexity

In this section, we introduce the notion of **prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity**. Our main reason for working with this notion is to use it in studying algorithmic randomness of infinite sequences. However, some have argued that prefix-free complexity is *the* correct notion of descriptive complexity even for finite strings.

One argument for the inadequacy of plain Kolmogorov complexity is the following. The intended meaning in saying that $\tau$ is a description of $\sigma$ is that the bits of $\tau$ contain all the information necessary to obtain $\sigma$. But a Turing machine $M$ might produce $\sigma$ by using not only the bits of $\tau$ but also its length. In this way, $\tau$ actually represents $|\tau| + \log |\tau|$ many bits of information. Indeed, this fact was exploited in the proof of Theorem 3.1.4.

Another way to make this argument is that, if $M$ is allowed to use the length of $\tau$ in computing $\sigma$, then there must be some kind of **termination symbol** $T$ (such as a blank space) on $M$'s input tape following the bits of $\tau$. Thus, to output a word in the alphabet $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$, our machine uses an input in the alphabet $\lbrace 0, 1, T \rbrace$, which we may view as cheating. If one accepts this argument then one ought to try to circumvent this shortcoming of plain Kolmogorov complexity. First Levin and later Chaitin suggested using **prefix-free machines** to do so.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Prefix-Free Function and Machine)</span></p>

A **prefix-free function** is one whose domain is prefix-free. Similarly, a **prefix-free (Turing) machine** is one whose domain is prefix-free. It is usual to consider such a machine as being **self-delimiting**, which means that it has a one-way read head that halts when the machine accepts the string described by the bits read so far. The point is that such a machine is forced to accept strings without knowing whether there are any more bits written on the input tape.

</div>

To highlight the role of machines in the definition of Kolmogorov complexity, we will sometimes refer to general Turing machines as **plain machines**.

A partial computable prefix-free function $U$ is **universal** if for each partial computable prefix-free function $f : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$, there is a string $\rho_f$ such that

$$\forall \sigma\, [U(\rho_f \sigma) = f(\sigma)].$$

As in the non-prefix-free case, we call $\rho_f$ the **coding string** and $|\rho_f|$ the **coding constant** of $f$ in $U$, and hence often refer to $U$ as a **universal prefix-free machine**.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.5.1)</span></p>

(i) If $\Phi$ is a prefix-free partial computable function, then there is a prefix-free (self-delimiting) machine $M$ such that $M$ computes $\Phi$.

(ii) There is a universal (self-delimiting) prefix-free machine.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) Let $\Phi$ be partial computable and prefix-free. We build a self-delimiting machine $M$ in stages. Initially, $M$ works on the empty string. At a given point in the action of $M$, let $\sigma$ be the string consisting of the bits read by $M$ so far. The read head does not scan the next symbol unless it sees some extension $\tau$ of $\sigma$ such that $\Phi(\tau) \!\!\downarrow$. At this point, $M$ scans the next symbol $i$ on the read tape. If the string $\sigma' = \sigma i$ remains an initial segment of $\tau$, then $M$ repeats the above process with $\sigma'$ in place of $\sigma$. Otherwise, $M$ goes back to waiting for a string $\tau'$ extending $\sigma'$ upon which $\Phi$ halts. Of course, if the string $\sigma$ consisting of the bits read by $M$ so far is ever such that $\Phi(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow$, then $M$ simply outputs $\Phi(\sigma)$.

(ii) Let $\Psi_e$ be the partial computable function defined by letting $\Psi_e[s] = \Phi_e[s]$ if $\operatorname{dom} \Phi_e[s]$ is prefix-free and $\Psi_e[s] = \Psi_e[s-1]$ otherwise. Then $\lbrace \Psi_e \rbrace_{e \in \omega}$ is an enumeration of all (and only) the prefix-free partial computable functions. We can then define

$$\Psi(1^e 0\sigma) = \Psi_e(\sigma),$$

which is evidently prefix-free and universal. By part (i), there is a self-delimiting machine computing $\Psi$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.5.2 — Universal Prefix-Free Oracle Machine)</span></p>

By the same argument as above, there is a **universal prefix-free oracle machine** $U$, that is, one such that for each prefix-free oracle machine $M$, there is a coding string $\rho_M$ such that for every oracle $X$, we have $\forall \sigma\, [U^X(\rho_M \sigma) = m^X(\sigma)]$ (where $X$ is either a finite or an infinite oracle).

Henceforth in this book, we fix a universal prefix-free oracle machine $\mathcal{U}$. We write $\mathcal{U}(\sigma)$ for $\mathcal{U}^\emptyset(\sigma)$, and thus also think of $\mathcal{U}$ as a universal prefix-free machine in the unrelativized case. All our results will be independent of the choice of $\mathcal{U}$.

</div>

Fix the enumeration $\Psi_0, \Psi_1, \ldots$ of prefix-free partial computable functions given in the proof of Proposition 3.5.1. We will use the following version of the recursion theorem below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.5.3 — Recursion Theorem for Prefix-Free Machines)</span></p>

Let $h : 2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{N} \to 2^{<\omega}$ be a partial computable function such that for each $e$, the function $\lambda\sigma.h(\sigma, e)$ is prefix-free. From an index for $h$, we can compute an index $e$ such that $\Psi_e = \lambda\sigma.h(\sigma, e)$.

</div>

The machine $\mathcal{U}$ is minimal, in the sense that, if $M$ is any prefix-free machine, then $C_\mathcal{U}(\sigma) \leqslant C_M(\sigma) + O(1)$. Thus we can define the **prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity** of a string $\sigma$ to be

$$K(\sigma) = C_\mathcal{U}(\sigma).$$

Similarly, we can define the prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity $K(\sigma \mid \tau)$ of $\sigma$ given $\tau$ and the prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity $K^A(\sigma)$ of $\sigma$ relative to $A$ as we did for plain complexity. As in the case of plain complexity, the choice of universal prefix-free machine does not affect the definition of $K$, up to an additive constant.

When $M$ is a prefix-free machine, we will sometimes write $K_M$ instead of $C_M$.

As we did for plain complexity, we associate a natural number $n$ with its binary representation $\sigma$ and let $K(n) = K(\sigma)$, and similarly for other notation introduced in the case of plain complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.5.4)</span></p>

If $h : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ is computable then $K(h(\sigma)) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$.

</div>

Suppose that $K(\sigma) = n$. Then there is a first string $\tau$ of length $n$ such that the computation of $\mathcal{U}(\tau)$ converges with value $\sigma$. More precisely, there is a least stage $s$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\tau)[s] \!\!\downarrow= \sigma$ for one or more strings $\tau$ of length $n$. We fix the lexicographically least such $\tau$ and denote it by $\sigma^*$.

One of the most important facts about $K$ is that it can be approximated from above. Specifically, let $K_s(\sigma) = \min\lbrace |\tau| : \mathcal{U}(\tau)[s] \!\!\downarrow= \sigma \rbrace$. In other words, $K_s(\sigma)$ is the length of the shortest description of $\sigma$ provided by $\mathcal{U}$ in at most $s$ steps. Of course, there may be no such description. However, it will be technically useful to assume that $K_s(\sigma)$ is always defined. It is easy to show that there is a $c$ such that $K(\sigma) \leqslant 2|\sigma| + c$ for all $\sigma$. Thus, if there is no $\tau$ with $\mathcal{U}(\tau)[s] \!\!\downarrow= \sigma$, then let $K_s(\sigma) = 2|\sigma| + c$. The important properties of this approximation are that

1. The function $(s, \sigma) \mapsto K_s(\sigma)$ is computable,

2. $K_s(\sigma) \geqslant K_{s+1}(\sigma)$ for all $s$ and $\sigma$, and

3. $K(\sigma) = \lim_s K_s(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

On the other hand, we have the following fact (which is also true for plain complexity, with the same proof).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.5.5)</span></p>

There is no partial computable $f$ with unbounded range such that $f(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma)$ whenever $f(\sigma)$ is defined.

</div>

*Proof.* Define a prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. For each $k$, look for a $\sigma_k$ such that $f(\sigma_k) \geqslant 2k$ and let $M(0^k 1) = \sigma_k$. Then $K(\sigma_k) \leqslant C_M(\sigma_k) + O(1) \leqslant k + O(1) < 2k \leqslant f(\sigma_k) \leqslant K(\sigma_k)$ for all sufficiently large $k$, which is a contradiction.

### 3.6 The KC Theorem

A central tool in building prefix-free machines is an effective interpretation of an inequality of Kraft. If $S_0, S_1, \ldots \subset 2^\omega$ are disjoint measurable sets then $\mu(\bigcup_n S_n) = \sum_n \mu(S_n)$, so if $A \subset 2^{<\omega}$ is prefix-free then $\sum_{\sigma \in A} 2^{-|\sigma|} = \mu(\llbracket A \rrbracket) \leqslant 1$. Conversely, suppose that we have a sequence $\lbrace d_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ of natural numbers such that $\sum_i 2^{-d_i} \leqslant 1$. Since we are not arguing effectively, by rearranging the $d_i$ if necessary, we may assume that $d_0 \leqslant d_1 \leqslant d_2 \leqslant \cdots$. Let $\sigma_i$ be the leftmost string of length $d_i$ that does not extend any $\sigma_j$ with $j < i$. An easy induction shows that such a string always exists. Thus we see that, for any sequence $\lbrace d_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ of natural numbers such that $\sum_i 2^{-d_i} \leqslant 1$, there is a prefix-free sequence $\lbrace \sigma_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ of strings such that $|\sigma_i| = d_i$ for all $i$. The KC Theorem is an effectivization of this fact.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.1 — KC Theorem, Levin, Schnorr, Chaitin)</span></p>

Let $(d_i, \tau_i)_{i \in \omega}$ be a computable sequence of pairs (which we call **requests**), with $d_i \in \mathbb{N}$ and $\tau_i \in 2^{<\omega}$, such that $\sum_i 2^{-d_i} \leqslant 1$. Then there is a prefix-free machine $M$ and strings $\sigma_i$ of length $d_i$ such that $M(\sigma_i) = \tau_i$ for all $i$ and $\operatorname{dom} M = \lbrace \sigma_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$. Furthermore, an index for $M$ can be obtained effectively from an index for our sequence of requests.

</div>

The **weight** of a request $(d, \tau)$ is $2^{-|\tau|}$. The **weight** of a computable sequence of requests $(d_i, \tau_i)_{i \in \omega}$ is the sum of the weights of the requests, i.e., $\sum_i 2^{-d_i}$. If this weight is less than or equal to 1, then we say that this sequence is a **KC set**. The beauty of using the KC Theorem to define a prefix-free machine is that we need only build a KC set, issuing requests and ensuring that the weight is kept less than or equal to 1; the prefix-free machine itself is built implicitly for us.

The following is a very useful immediate consequence of the KC Theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.2)</span></p>

Let $(d_i, \tau_i)_{i \in \omega}$ be a KC set. Then $K(\tau_i) \leqslant d_i + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $M$ be as in the KC Theorem. Then $K(\tau_i) \leqslant K_M(\tau_i) + O(1) \leqslant d_i + O(1)$.

If a computable sequence of requests $(d_i, \tau_i)_{i \in \omega}$ has finite weight, then there is a $c$ such that $(d_i + c, \tau_i)_{i \in \omega}$ has weight less than or equal to 1, and hence is a KC set. Thus Corollary 3.6.2 still holds for such a sequence. We will sometimes abuse terminology and refer to such a sequence as a KC set.

A simple application of the KC Theorem is the following. Let $\tau_0, \tau_1, \ldots$ be an effective enumeration of $2^{<\omega}$, with $\tau_0$ being the empty string. Let $d_0 = 2$ and for $i > 0$ let $d_i = |\tau_i| + 2 \log |\tau_i| + 3$. Then

$$\sum_i 2^{-d_i} \leqslant \frac{1}{4} + \sum_{i > 0} \frac{2^{-|\tau_i|}}{3|\tau_i|^2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{n > 0} 2^n \frac{2^{-n}}{n^2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{3}\sum_{n > 0} \frac{1}{n^2} < 1.$$

Thus $\lbrace (d_i, \tau_i) \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ is a KC set, so we have the following upper bound on $K(\tau)$, which we will improve on in Theorem 3.7.4 below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.3)</span></p>

$K(\tau) \leqslant |\tau| + 2 \log |\tau| + O(1)$.

</div>

The following is another useful application of the KC Theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.4)</span></p>

Let $A_0, A_1, \ldots \subset 2^{<\omega}$ be nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and uniformly c.e. Then $K(n) \leqslant -\log \sum_{\sigma \in A_n} 2^{-|\sigma|} + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f(n) = \sum_{\sigma \in A_n} 2^{-|\sigma|}$ and $f(n, s) = \sum_{\sigma \in A_{n,s}} 2^{-|\sigma|}$, where $A_{n,s}$ is the stage $s$ approximation to $A_n$. Note that because the $A_n$ are pairwise disjoint, $\sum_n f(n) \leqslant 1$.

Build a KC set $L$ as follows. For each $n$ and $k$, if there is an $s$ such that $f(n, s) \geqslant 2^{-k}$, then enumerate a request $(k+1, n)$ into $L$. The weight of $L$ is bounded by

$$\sum_n \sum_{k \leqslant -\log f(n)} 2^{-(k+1)} \leqslant \sum_n f(n) \leqslant 1,$$

so $L$ is indeed a KC set. For each $n$ there is an $s$ such that $f(n, s) \geqslant \frac{f(n)}{2}$, so $L$ contains a request $(k+1, n)$ with $k \leqslant -\log f(n) + 1$, which implies, by Corollary 3.6.2, that $K(n) \leqslant -\log f(n) + O(1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.5)</span></p>

Let $C_0, C_1, \ldots$ be nonempty, pairwise disjoint uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes. Then $K(n) \leqslant -\log \mu(C_n) + O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.6.6)</span></p>

Let $B_0, B_1, \ldots \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ be nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and uniformly c.e. Then $K(n) \leqslant -\log \sum_{\tau \in B_n} 2^{-K(\tau)} + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A_n = \lbrace \sigma : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \in B_n \rbrace$. The $A_n$ are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and uniformly c.e. Since $\tau^* \in A_n$ for all $\tau \in B_n$,

$$K(n) \leqslant -\log \sum_{\sigma \in A_n} 2^{-|\sigma|} + O(1) \leqslant -\log \sum_{\tau \in B_n} 2^{-|\tau^*|} + O(1) = -\log \sum_{\tau \in B_n} 2^{-K(\tau)} + O(1).$$

One technique that we will employ several times is to build a KC set and assume, by the recursion theorem, that we have an index for the corresponding prefix-free machine. The legitimacy of this assumption requires a brief comment. It is certainly the case that, when we enumerate a c.e. set of requests $L$, we can use the recursion theorem to assume that we have an index for $L$. If $L$ is a KC set, then the KC Theorem tells us that from this index we can effectively pass to an index for the corresponding prefix-free machine. But if $L$ is not a KC set, then there is no corresponding machine, so we have a problem unless we can ensure that our construction always builds a KC set, regardless of the parameter it is provided. Fortunately, this issue is easily bypassed. Whenever we say that we are building a KC set $L$ using the recursion theorem, we have an additional unstated rule that if we ever wish to add a request to $L$ that would drive its weight above 1, we do not do so, and immediately halt the construction. A construction with this rule always produces a KC set, no matter what the parameter, and in the constructions below, we will always implicitly show that the construction never halts if provided with the correct parameter (i.e., with an index for the machine corresponding to the KC set it builds).

### 3.7 Basic properties of prefix-free complexity

We begin with the following simple but quite fundamental result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.1)</span></p>

$\sum_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}} 2^{-K(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$.

</div>

*Proof.* Since $\mathcal{U}$ is prefix-free, $\sum_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}} 2^{-K(\sigma)} < \sum_{\tau \in \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}} 2^{-|\tau|} \leqslant 1$.

We now show that the upper bound in Proposition 3.6.3 cannot be improved too much.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.2)</span></p>

For any $d$, there are $\sigma$ with $K(\sigma) > |\sigma| + \log |\sigma| + d$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $K(\sigma) \leqslant |\sigma| + \log |\sigma| + d$ for all $\sigma$. Then

$$\sum_\sigma 2^{-K(\sigma)} \geqslant \sum_{|\sigma| > 0} \frac{2^{-|\sigma| - d}}{|\sigma|} = \sum_{n > 0} 2^n \frac{2^{-n-d}}{n} = 2^{-d} \sum_{n > 0} \frac{1}{n} = \infty,$$

contradicting Proposition 3.7.1.

In fact, the argument in the above proof shows the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.3)</span></p>

Let $f : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{N}$ be such that $\sum_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}} 2^{-f(\sigma)} = \infty$. Then $K(\sigma) > |\sigma| + f(|\sigma|)$ for infinitely many $\sigma$.

</div>

The most precise bound on $K(\sigma)$ is given by the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.4 — Chaitin)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) \leqslant |\sigma| + K(|\sigma|) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Consider the prefix-free machine $M$ that, on input $\mu$, searches for strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$ such that $\mu = \tau\sigma$ and $\mathcal{U}(\tau) \!\!\downarrow= |\sigma|$, and, if such a pair is found, outputs $\sigma$. Note that, since $\mathcal{U}$ is prefix-free, there can be at most one such pair $\sigma, \tau$ for any given $\mu$. Furthermore, it is easy to check that $M$ is prefix-free. Thus, letting $\nu_\sigma$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $|\sigma|$, we have

$$K(\sigma) \leqslant C_M(\sigma) + O(1) = |\nu_\sigma| + |\sigma| + O(1) = K(|\sigma|) + |\sigma| + O(1).$$

This result immediately gives us the bound of Proposition 3.6.3, but we can do better by proceeding iteratively. That is, letting $||\sigma||$ be the length of the binary representation of $|\sigma|$, we have $K(\sigma) \leqslant |\sigma| + ||\sigma|| + K(||\sigma||) + O(1) \leqslant |\sigma| + \log |\sigma| + 2 \log \log |\sigma| + O(1)$. Continuing in this way, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.5)</span></p>

For any $k$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, we have $K(\sigma) \leqslant |\sigma| + \log |\sigma| + \log \log |\sigma| + \cdots + (1 + \varepsilon) \log^{(k)} |\sigma| + O(1)$.

</div>

Chaitin showed that the bound given in Theorem 3.7.4 is tight, as part of the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.6 — Counting Theorem, Chaitin)</span></p>

(i) $\max\lbrace K(\sigma) : |\sigma| = n \rbrace = n + K(n) \pm O(1)$.

(ii) $|\lbrace \sigma : |\sigma| = n \wedge K(\sigma) \leqslant n + K(n) - r \rbrace| \leqslant 2^{n-r+O(1)}$, where the constant does not depend on $n$ and $r$.

</div>

Of course, part (i) of the Counting Theorem follows from part (ii). The most elegant way to prove part (ii) was given by Chaitin, and works by way of the minimality of $K$ as an **information content measure**, as we now explain.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.7.7 — Chaitin, after Levin)</span></p>

An **information content measure** (i.c.m.) is a partial function $F : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that

1. $\sum_{F(\sigma)\!\downarrow} 2^{-F(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$ and

2. $\lbrace (\sigma, k) : F(\sigma) \leqslant k \rbrace$ is c.e.

</div>

Notice that $K$ is an information content measure. As with prefix-free machines, we can enumerate the information content measures as $F_0, F_1, \ldots$ in such a way that the corresponding sets given in item 2 of Definition 3.7.7 are uniformly c.e. We can then define a **minimal information content measure**

$$\widehat{K}(\sigma) = \min_{F_k(\sigma)\!\downarrow} \lbrace F_k(\sigma) + k + 1 \rbrace.$$

It is easy to check that $\widehat{K}$ is indeed an information content measure, and that it is minimal in the sense that, for any information content measure $F$, we have $\widehat{K}(\sigma) \leqslant F(\sigma) + O(1)$ for $\sigma \in \operatorname{dom} F$. In particular, $\widehat{K}(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$.

### 3.8 The Kraft–Chaitin version of the Coding Theorem

The Counting Theorem (Theorem 3.7.6) can also be derived from a version of the **Coding Theorem** using the minimal information content measure $\widehat{K}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.8.1 — Coding Theorem, Kraft–Chaitin Version)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) = \widehat{K}(\sigma) + O(1)$.

</div>

This result says that the prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity $K$ is, up to a constant, the same as the minimal information content measure. This equivalence is fundamental: it shows that $K$ is not just one among many measures of descriptive complexity, but is essentially the unique optimal one.

### 3.9 Discrete semimeasures and the Coding Theorem

A **discrete semimeasure** is a function $m : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $\sum_\sigma m(\sigma) \leqslant 1$. A discrete semimeasure is **c.e.** (or **lower semicomputable**) if $\lbrace (\sigma, q) : m(\sigma) > q \rbrace$ is c.e. for $q \in \mathbb{Q}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.9.3)</span></p>

A c.e. discrete semimeasure $m$ is **optimal** (or **universal**) if for every c.e. discrete semimeasure $m'$ we have $m(\sigma) \geqslant O(m'(\sigma))$ (i.e., there is a constant $c$ such that $m(\sigma) \geqslant c \cdot m'(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$).

</div>

Given the universal prefix-free machine $\mathcal{U}$, define $Q(\sigma) = \sum_{\mathcal{U}(\tau) = \sigma} 2^{-|\tau|}$. The function $Q$ is a c.e. discrete semimeasure. In fact, $Q$ is optimal.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.9.4 — Coding Theorem, Levin)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) = -\log m(\sigma) + O(1)$, where $m$ is any optimal c.e. discrete semimeasure.

</div>

One informal interpretation of the Coding Theorem is that if a string has many long descriptions then it also has a short description.

### 3.10 Prefix-free symmetry of information

As with plain complexity, we can explore the notion of symmetry of information for prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity. In the same spirit as for $C$, we may define the **prefix-free information content** of $\tau$ in $\sigma$ as

$$I_K(\sigma : \tau) = K(\tau) - K(\tau \mid \sigma).$$

For the rest of this section, we will drop the subscript $K$ from $I_K$.

First note that the results of Section 3.3 hold for $K$ in place of $C$, since $K$ and $C$ agree up to a log factor. In addition, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.10.1 — Symmetry of Information, Levin and Gács, Chaitin)</span></p>

$I(\langle \sigma, K(\sigma) \rangle : \tau) = I(\langle \tau, K(\tau) \rangle : \sigma) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

Note that given the relationship $K(\rho, K(\rho)) = K(\rho^*) \pm O(1)$, the Symmetry of Information Theorem for prefix-free complexity may be neatly rewritten as

$$I(\sigma^* : \tau) = I(\tau^* : \sigma).$$

As with the $C$ case, the Symmetry of Information Theorem follows from a reformulated version.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.10.2 — Symmetry of Information, Restated)</span></p>

$K(\sigma, \tau) = K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma, K(\sigma)) \pm O(1) = K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

Theorem 3.10.2 gives us Theorem 3.10.1 as follows:

$$K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma, K(\sigma)) = K(\sigma, \tau) \pm O(1) = K(\tau, \sigma) \pm O(1) = K(\tau) + K(\sigma \mid \tau, K(\tau)) \pm O(1),$$

and hence,

$$K(\tau) - K(\tau \mid \sigma, K(\sigma)) = K(\sigma) - K(\sigma \mid \tau, K(\tau)) \pm O(1).$$

*Proof of Theorem 3.10.2.* The second equality is clear, so it is enough to show that $K(\sigma, \tau) = K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) \pm O(1)$.

We first prove that

$$K(\sigma, \tau) \leqslant K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) + O(1).$$

Let $\rho$ be a minimal length description of $\tau$ given $\sigma^*$. Then we can construct a prefix-free machine $M$ that, on input $\sigma^* \rho$, first computes $\sigma$ from $\sigma^*$, then computes $\tau$ from $\sigma^*$ and $\rho$, and finally outputs $\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle$.

We now prove that

$$K(\sigma, \tau) \geqslant K(\sigma) + K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) - O(1).$$

To do this, we prove that

$$K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) \leqslant K(\sigma, \tau) - K(\sigma) + O(1).$$

Let $\nu_0, \nu_1, \ldots$ be a computable ordering of the domain of $\mathcal{U}$ and let $\sigma_s$ and $\tau_s$ be such that $\nu_s = \langle \sigma_s, \tau_s \rangle$. Let $W_\sigma = \lbrace s : \sigma_s = \sigma \rbrace$. Given $n$ and $\sigma$, build a KC set by enumerating requests $(|\nu_s| - n, \tau_s)$ for $s \in W_\sigma$, as long as the weight of these requests does not exceed 1. Call the resulting prefix-free machine $M_{n,\sigma}$.

By the Coding Theorem 3.9.4, there is a constant $c$ such that

$$2^{K(\sigma) - c} \sum_\tau Q(\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle) \leqslant 1$$

for all $\sigma$. Since

$$\sum_{s \in W_\sigma} 2^{-(|\nu_s| - |\sigma^*| + c)} = 2^{K(\sigma) - c} \sum_\tau Q(\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle) \leqslant 1,$$

all relevant requests will be enumerated for the machine $M_{K(\sigma) - c, \sigma}$.

Now define the oracle prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. With $\rho$ on the oracle tape, $M$ first looks for a $\sigma$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\rho) = \sigma$. Then it simulates $M_{K(\sigma) - c, \sigma}$. Since there is an $s \in W_\sigma$ such that $\nu_s = \langle \sigma, \tau \rangle^*$, the KC set defining this machine has as one of its requests $(K(\sigma, \tau) - K(\sigma) + c, \tau)$, so

$$K(\tau \mid \sigma^*) \leqslant K_M(\tau \mid \sigma^*) \leqslant K(\sigma, \tau) - K(\sigma) + O(1).$$

It is sometimes useful to apply symmetry of information to $\sigma\tau$ rather than $\langle \sigma, \tau \rangle$. By Theorem 3.10.2, $K(\sigma, \tau) = K(\sigma, \sigma\tau) \pm O(1) = K(\sigma\tau) + K(\sigma \mid (\sigma\tau)^*) \pm O(1)$, so we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.10.3)</span></p>

$K(\sigma\tau) = K(\sigma, \tau) - K(\sigma \mid (\sigma\tau)^*) \pm O(1) = K(\sigma) + K(\sigma \mid \tau^*) - K(\sigma \mid (\sigma\tau)^*) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

A similar result holds for $C$ in place of $K$.

We finish this section with a useful "mixed" theorem for the complexity of $\sigma\tau$, relating $C$ and $K$ in the spirit of Chapter 4.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.10.4 — Folklore)</span></p>

$C(\sigma\tau) \leqslant K(\sigma) + C(\tau) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\mathcal{V}$ be a universal plain machine. Define a plain machine $M$ as follows. On input $\rho$, look for $\rho_0$ and $\rho_1$ such that $\rho_0 \rho_1 = \rho$ and both $\mathcal{U}(\rho_0) \!\!\downarrow$ and $\mathcal{V}(\rho_1) \!\!\downarrow$. If found, then output $\mathcal{U}(\rho_0)\mathcal{V}(\rho_1)$. Then $C(\sigma\tau) \leqslant C_M(\sigma\tau) + O(1) \leqslant K(\sigma) + C(\tau) + O(1)$.

### 3.11 Initial segment complexity of sets

It follows from Theorem 3.1.4 that there is no set $A$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. Recall that the idea of the proof of Theorem 3.1.4 was to take a string $\sigma\tau$ such that the length of $\tau$ codes $\sigma$, and use $\tau$ alone to describe $\sigma\tau$. This reasoning is refined in the following results of Martin-Löf, the proofs here being drawn from Li and Vitányi.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.11.1 — Martin-Löf, see Li and Vitányi and Staiger)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \infty$. Then for any set $A$ there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant n - f(n)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $g$ be a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} = \infty$ and $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$. Let $G(n) = \sum_{i \leqslant n} 2^{-g(n)}$. Think of $[0, 1]$ as a circle with 0 and 1 identified and let $I_n$ be the interval $[G(n), G(n+1))$ mod 1 on this circle. Let $C_n = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \cap I_n \neq \emptyset \rbrace$, where we think of $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ as a subset of $[0, 1]$. Note that the $C_n$ are uniformly computable and $|C_n| \leqslant 2^n(G(n+1) - G(n))$.

Thinking of $A$ as a real, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $A \in I_n$, since $\lim_n G(n) = \infty$. Thus there are infinitely many $n$ such that $A \upharpoonright n \in C_n$. Given $n$, we can describe any element of $C_n$ by its position in $C_n$, ordered lexicographically. Thus there are infinitely many $n$ such that

$$C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant \log |C_n| + O(1) \leqslant \log(2^n(G(n+1) - G(n))) + O(1) = n - g(n) + O(1).$$

For all such $n$ that are sufficiently large, $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant n - f(n)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.11.2 — Li and Vitányi, after Martin-Löf)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \infty$ and $C(n \mid n - f(n)) = O(1)$. Then for any set $A$ there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - f(n)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.11.3)</span></p>

For any set $A$ there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - \log n$.

</div>

*Proof.* The function $n - \log n \mapsto n$ is computable, so $C(n \mid n - \log n) = O(1)$. Furthermore, $\sum_n 2^{-\log n} = \infty$, so we can apply Theorem 3.11.2.

There has been much work on $C$-complexity oscillations related to the above results. The following is a prefix-free analog of the above results.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.11.4 — after Solovay)</span></p>

If $g$ is computable and $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} = \infty$, then for every set $X$ there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + K(n) - g(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

### 3.12 Computable bounds for prefix-free complexity

Ignoring additive constants, we have seen that for plain complexity, there is a computable upper bound on $C(\sigma)$, namely $|\sigma|$, that is achieved infinitely often. The analogous bound on $K(\sigma)$, however, is $|\sigma| + K(|\sigma|)$, which is not computable, and approximations to this bound, such as $|\sigma| + 2 \log |\sigma|$ or $|\sigma| + \log |\sigma| + 2 \log^{(2)} |\sigma|$, are not achieved infinitely often.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.12.1 — Solovay, see also Gács)</span></p>

There is a computable function $g : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that

(i) $K(\sigma) \leqslant g(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$, and

(ii) $K(\sigma) = g(\sigma)$ for infinitely many $\sigma$.

</div>

*Proof.* It will be notationally convenient to build $g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ so that $K(n) \leqslant g(n)$ for almost all $n$ and $K(n) = g(n)$ for infinitely many $n$.

We can construct a prefix-free machine $M$ such that, for each $\tau$ and $n$, if $s$ is least such that $\mathcal{U}(\tau)[s] \!\!\downarrow= n$, then $M(\tau) \!\!\downarrow= \langle n, s \rangle$. Since $\mathcal{U}$ is universal, there is a constant $c$ such that, for each such $\tau, n, s$, we have $\mathcal{U}(\mu) \!\!\downarrow= \langle n, s \rangle$ for some $\mu$ with $|\mu| \leqslant |\tau| + c$. We can now define a computable function $f$ such that for all $\tau, n, s$, if $s$ is least such that $\mathcal{U}(\tau)[s] \!\!\downarrow= n$, then $\mathcal{U}(\mu)[f(s)] \!\!\downarrow= \langle n, s \rangle$ for some $\mu$ with $|\mu| \leqslant |\tau| + c$. Thus, if $s$ is least such that $K(n) = K_s(n)$, then $K_{f(s)}(\langle n, s \rangle) \leqslant K(n) + c$.

Now define $\widehat{g}$ as follows. Given $x$, find the unique $n$ and $s$ such that $x = \langle n, s \rangle$, and let $\widehat{g}(x) = K_{f(s)}(x)$. Clearly, $K(x) \leqslant \widehat{g}(x)$ for all $x$.

On the other hand, suppose that $x$ is one of the infinitely many integers of the form $\langle n, s \rangle$ where $s$ is least such that $K(n) = K_s(n)$. Then $\widehat{g}(x) = K_{f(s)}(\langle n, s \rangle) \leqslant K(n) + O(1) \leqslant K(x) + O(1)$, the latter inequality coming from the fact that the function $\langle n, s \rangle \mapsto n$ is computable. Thus there is a constant $d \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $K(x) = \widehat{g}(x) - d$ for infinitely many $x$ and $K(x) \leqslant \widehat{g}(x) - d$ for all but finitely many $x$. Define $g(m) = \widehat{g}(m) - d$, then modify finitely many values of $g$ to ensure that $K(m) \leqslant g(m)$ for all $m$.

Note that there is a critical difference between the computable bound $|\sigma| + O(1)$ for $C(\sigma)$ and the computable bound $g(\sigma)$ of Theorem 3.12.1. For the appropriate choice of constant $c$, we know that for each $n$ there is some string $\sigma$ of length $n$ with $C(\sigma) = |\sigma| + c$. For $g$, however, we know only that there are infinitely many $\sigma$ with $K(\sigma) = g(\sigma)$, and apparently only $\emptyset'$ can figure out where these $\sigma$ are. This difference will be important, especially in Chapter 11, where we discuss the class of sets (the **$K$-trivial** sets) that show that Chaitin's Theorem 3.4.4 fails for $K$ in place of $C$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.12.2 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

Let $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be computable. The following are equivalent:

(i) $K(n) \leqslant f(n) + O(1)$.

(ii) $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii) is trivial, as $\sum_n 2^{-K(n)} \leqslant 1$. The other direction follows by the minimality of $K$ as an information content measure, Theorem 3.7.8.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.12.3 — Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

A computable function $f$ is a **Solovay function** if $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$ and $\liminf_n f(n) - K(n) < \infty$ (in other words, there is a $c$ such that $f(n) \leqslant K(n) + c$ for infinitely many $n$).

</div>

As we will see in Theorem 9.4.1, Solovay functions are exactly those computable functions $f$ for which $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$ is a 1-random real, as defined in Chapter 6. They will play a part in several chapters below. We will refer to the Solovay function built in the proof of Theorem 3.12.1 as "Solovay's Solovay function".

For a computable function $t$, the **prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity of $\sigma$ with time bound $t$**, denoted by $K^t(\sigma)$, is $\min\lbrace |\tau| : U(\tau)[t(|\sigma|)] \!\!\downarrow= \sigma \rbrace$, where $U$ is a universal prefix-free function that efficiently simulates every partial computable prefix-free function $f$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.12.4 — Hölzl, Kräling, and Merkle)</span></p>

If $t$ is any superlinear time bound, then the time-bounded version $K^t$ of $K$ is always a Solovay function.

</div>

### 3.13 Universal machines and halting probabilities

In this section, we prove results of Solovay giving estimates of the sizes of some basic sets associated with $\mathcal{U}$. Recall that $\sigma^*$ is the first string $\tau$ of length $K(\sigma)$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\tau) \!\!\downarrow= \sigma$. Recall also that we write $f \sim g$ to mean that $f(n) = O(g(n))$ and $g(n) = O(f(n))$. We slightly abuse notation by writing, for instance, $f(n) \sim 2^n$ to mean that $f \sim n \mapsto 2^n$. We begin with the following fixed-point result for $\mathcal{U}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.1 — Solovay)</span></p>

For each $n$ there are at least $O(2^{n - K(n)})$ many strings $\sigma$ of length $n$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow= \sigma$.

</div>

*Proof.* We define a prefix-free machine $M$ with coding string $\rho$ given by the recursion theorem. On input $\sigma$, the machine $M$ looks for $\tau \in \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}$ and $\mu$ such that $\sigma = \tau\mu$. Then $M$ interprets $\mathcal{U}(\tau)$ as a natural number $n$. If $|\mu| < n - |\rho| - |\tau|$ then $M$ does not halt. Otherwise, $M$ outputs $\rho\tau(\mu \upharpoonright (n - |\rho| - |\tau|))$.

Given $n$, let $S_n = \lbrace \rho n^* \nu : |\nu| = n - |\rho| - K(n) \rbrace$. If $\rho n^* \nu \in S_n$ then $\mathcal{U}(\rho n^* \nu) = M(n^* \nu) = \rho n^* \nu$, and $|S_n| = 2^{n - |\rho| - K(n)} = O(2^{n - K(n)})$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.2 — Solovay)</span></p>

Let

(i) $p(n) = |\lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace|$,

(ii) $P(n) = |\lbrace \sigma \in 2^{\leqslant n} : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace|$,

(iii) $p'(n) = |\lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) = n \rbrace|$, and

(iv) $d(n) = |\lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) \leqslant n \rbrace|$.

Then $p(n) \sim P(n) \sim d(n) \sim 2^{n - K(n)}$. Furthermore, there is a number $k$ such that $\sum_{n \leqslant j \leqslant n+k} p'(j) \sim 2^{n - K(n)}$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A_n = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace$. The $A_n$ are pairwise disjoint, uniformly c.e. subsets of the prefix-free set $\operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}$. By Proposition 3.6.4,

$$K(n) \leqslant -\log \sum_{\sigma \in A_n} 2^{-|\sigma|} + O(1) = -\log(p(n) 2^{-n}) + O(1),$$

so $p(n) \leqslant O(2^{n - K(n)})$. By Lemma 3.13.1, in fact $p(n) = O(2^{n - K(n)})$.

Since $p(n) \leqslant P(n)$, we have the lower bound $P(n) \geqslant O(2^{n - K(n)})$. For the upper bound, first notice that there is a $c$ such that

$$P(n) = \sum_{j \leqslant n} p(n-j) \leqslant \sum_{j \leqslant n} c \cdot 2^{n-j-K(n-j)}.$$

Now, $K(n) \leqslant K(n-j) + K(j) + O(1)$, so $-K(n-j) \leqslant -K(n) + 2 \log j + O(1)$. Thus there is a $c'$ such that

$$P(n) \leqslant \sum_{j \leqslant n} c' 2^{n-j-K(n)+2\log j} = c' 2^{n-K(n)} \sum_{j \leqslant n} j^2 2^{-j} = O(2^{n-K(n)}),$$

as $\sum_j j^2 2^{-j}$ converges. So $P(n) = O(2^{n-K(n)})$.

Clearly, $d(n) \leqslant P(n) = O(2^{n - K(n)})$, and it follows from Lemma 3.13.1 that $d(n) \geqslant O(2^{n - K(n)})$, so $d(n) = O(2^{n - K(n)})$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.3 — Solovay)</span></p>

There is a $c$ such that if $m \geqslant c$, then for each $\sigma$ there is a $\tau \in 2^{K(\sigma) + m}$ with $\mathcal{U}(\tau) = \sigma$.

</div>

Say that $\tau$ is a **$d$-minimal program** if $\mathcal{U}(\tau) = \sigma$ and $|\tau| \leqslant K(\sigma) + d$, and that $\tau$ is a $d$-minimal program if it is a $d$-minimal program for some $\sigma$. There is a $d$ such that the number of $d$-minimal programs of length $n$ is $O(2^{n - K(n)})$.

We now turn to further results of Solovay concerning the sets $\mathcal{D}_n = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^{\leqslant n} : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace$. We have $P(n) = |\mathcal{D}_n|$ as in Theorem 3.13.2. Since the $\mathcal{D}_n$ are uniformly c.e., we can uniformly compute $\mathcal{D}_n$ given $n$ and $P(n)$. Thus

$$K(\mathcal{D}_n) \leqslant K(n, P(n)) + O(1) \leqslant K(n) + K(P(n) \mid n^*) + O(1).$$

By Theorem 3.13.2, there is a $c$ such that $P(n) \leqslant 2^{n - K(n) + c}$, so if we know $n - K(n)$ then we can describe $P(n)$ with a string of length $n - K(n) + c$. Furthermore, $K(n - K(n) \mid n^*) = O(1)$, so

$$K(P(n) \mid n^*) \leqslant n - K(n) + K(n - K(n) \mid n^*) + O(1) = n - K(n) + O(1).$$

Thus $K(\mathcal{D}_n) \leqslant K(n) + n - K(n) + O(1) = n + O(1)$. We now show that this estimate is sharp.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.4 — Solovay)</span></p>

$K(\mathcal{D}_n) = n \pm O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.5 — Solovay)</span></p>

$K((\mathcal{D}_n)^* \mid \mathcal{D}_n) = O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We can determine $(\mathcal{D}_n)^*$ given $K(\mathcal{D}_n)$ and $n$, so $K((\mathcal{D}_n)^* \mid \mathcal{D}_n) \leqslant K(K(\mathcal{D}_n) \mid \mathcal{D}_n) + O(1)$. But $K(\mathcal{D}_n) = n \pm O(1)$, so $K(K(\mathcal{D}_n) \mid \mathcal{D}_n) = K(n \mid \mathcal{D}_n) = O(1)$, since $n$ is the length of the longest string in $\mathcal{D}_n$, and hence we can determine $n$ given $\mathcal{D}_n$.

The sets $\mathcal{D}_n$ are closely related to Chaitin's famous number $\Omega$, whose properties we will further explore in Section 6.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.6 — Chaitin)</span></p>

The **halting probability** $\Omega$ of $\mathcal{U}$ is $\mu(\llbracket \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U} \rrbracket) = \sum_{\sigma \in \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}} 2^{-|\sigma|}$.

</div>

Of course, the value of $\Omega$ depends on the choice of $\mathcal{U}$, much as $\emptyset'$ depends on the choice of enumeration of the partial computable functions. We have seen in Myhill's Theorem 2.4.12 that all versions of $\emptyset'$ are the same up to computable permutations of $\mathbb{N}$. In Section 9.2 we will see that, as with $\emptyset'$, all versions of $\Omega$ are quite closely related, and it does make sense to ignore the machine-dependence of $\Omega$ in most circumstances. Let $\Omega_s = \sum_{\sigma \in \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}[s]} 2^{-|\sigma|}$. Note that $\Omega = \lim_s \Omega_s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.13.7 — Solovay)</span></p>

(i) $K(\mathcal{D}_n \mid \Omega \upharpoonright n) = O(1)$. (Indeed, $\mathcal{D}_n$ can be computed from $\Omega \upharpoonright n$.)

(ii) $K(\Omega \upharpoonright n \mid \mathcal{D}_{n + K(n)}) = O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) It is easy to see that $\Omega$ is not computable (since otherwise we would be able to compute the domain of $\mathcal{U}$). So in particular $\Omega \notin \mathbb{Q}$, which implies that, for each $n$, we must have $\Omega_s \upharpoonright n = \Omega \upharpoonright n$ for some $s$. Given $\Omega \upharpoonright n$, look for such an $s$. Then $\sigma \in \mathcal{D}_n$ iff $\sigma \in \operatorname{dom} \mathcal{U}[s]$.

(ii) Given $n$, let $D = \mathcal{D}_{n + K(n)}$. We may assume that $n$ is large enough so that $D \neq \emptyset$. Using $D$, we can find the least $j$ such that $j + K(j) = n + K(n)$. We have $|n - j| = |K(n) - K(j)| \leqslant K(|n - j|) + O(1) \leqslant 2 \log |n - j| + O(1)$, so $|n - j| + O(1)$. Thus $K(n \mid D) = O(1)$.

### 3.14 The conditional complexity of $\sigma^*$ given $\sigma$

In this section, we investigate how difficult it is to produce $\sigma^*$ given $\sigma$. This question is equivalent to asking how difficult it is to produce $K(\sigma)$ given $\sigma$, as shown by the following lemma, which has the same proof as the analogous Proposition 3.2.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.14.1)</span></p>

$K(\sigma^* \mid \sigma) = K(K(\sigma) \mid \sigma) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

It follows from this result that $K(\sigma^* \mid \sigma) \leqslant \log |\sigma| + O(\log^{(2)} |\sigma|)$. The following result shows that this bound is not too far from optimal.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.14.2 — Gács, Solovay)</span></p>

For all sufficiently large $n$, there is a $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that

$$K(\sigma^* \mid \sigma) \geqslant \log n - \log^{(2)} n - 3.$$

</div>

*Proof.* Assume for a contradiction that $K(\sigma^* \mid \sigma) < k(n)$ for all $\sigma \in 2^n$, where $k(n) = \log n - \log^{(2)} n - 3$.

We say that $\tau \in 2^{< k(n)}$ is **active for** $\sigma$ if there is a $\mu$ such that $\mathcal{U}^\sigma(\tau) = \mu$ and $\mathcal{U}(\mu) = \sigma$. Note that for each $\sigma \in 2^n$, there is a $\tau$ that is active for $\sigma$ such that $\mathcal{U}^\sigma(\tau) = \sigma^*$.

Since the active strings all have length less than $k(n)$, the number of strings that are active for a given $\sigma$ is in $[1, 2^{k(n)})$. Let

$$A_i = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \text{there are exactly } 2^{k(n)} - i \text{ many strings active for } \sigma \rbrace,$$

and let $S_i = \bigcup_{j < i} A_j$. By Corollary 3.14.4 (a combinatorial lemma), there is a least $i$ such that $|A_i| > n^{4i}$, and for this $i$ we have $|S_i| = O(n^{4(i-1)})$.

Since $|A_i| > n^{4i}$, there must be a $\sigma \in A_i$ such that $K(\sigma) \geqslant 4i \log n$. Now suppose that we know the parameters $i$, $|S_i|$, and $n$. Then we can wait until a stage $s$ such that $S_{i,s} = S_i$, and hence compute $A_i$. Every string entering $A_{i+1}$ that is not in $S_i$ is in $A_i$. But since $|A_i| > n^{4i}$, we know that $K(\sigma) \geqslant 4i \log n$. We have just argued that we can compute $\sigma$ given $i$, $|S_i|$, and $n$. Thus $K(\sigma) \leqslant K(i) + K(|S_i|) + K(n) + O(1)$.

Now, $K(n) \leqslant \log n + O(\log^{(2)} n)$, so

$$K(i) \leqslant k(n) + O(\log k(n)) = \log n - \log^{(2)} n - 3 + O(\log(\log n - \log^{(2)} n - 3)) \leqslant \log n + O(\log^{(2)} n).$$

Finally, $|S_i| = O(n^{4(i-1)})$, so

$$K(|S_i|) \leqslant \log(|S_i|) + O(\log^{(2)} |S_i|) \leqslant 4(i-1) \log n + O(\log^{(2)} n).$$

Thus $K(i) + K(|S_i|) + K(n) = (4i - 2) \log n + O(\log^{(2)} n)$, and hence $4i \log n \nleqslant K(i) + K(|S_i|) + K(n) + O(1)$ for sufficiently large $n$, as desired.

The same proof also gives the analogous result for plain complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.14.5 — Gács, Solovay)</span></p>

For all sufficiently large $n$, there is a $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $C(C(\sigma) \mid \sigma) \geqslant \log n - \log^{(2)} n - 3$.

</div>

Note that this lower bound is close to the obvious upper bound $C(C(\sigma) \mid \sigma) \leqslant C(C(\sigma)) + O(1) \leqslant \log |\sigma| + O(1)$.

Chaitin conjectured the following, which implies the above and was also conjectured correct by Solovay.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Conjecture 3.14.6)</span></p>

There is an infinite sequence $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ such that

(i) $|\sigma_n| = n$,

(ii) $K(\sigma_n) \sim n$, and

(iii) $K(\sigma_n^* \mid \sigma_n) \sim \log n$.

Solovay pointed out that these $\sigma_n$ might well satisfy $K(\sigma_n) \leqslant \frac{n}{\log^{(2)} n}$, say.

</div>

### 3.15 Monotone and process complexity

The original machine characterization of randomness was due to Levin, and involved what are called **monotone machines**, and a resulting notion of **monotone complexity**. A related notion is the **process complexity** of Schnorr, which uses a different kind of monotone machine.

Here, we are viewing Cantor space as a continuous sample space, and thinking of a sequence as a limit of computable sequences, rather than a limit of strings.

Levin considered machines with possibly infinite outputs. On a given input $\sigma$, such a machine $M$ either halts with a finite output or computes forever, producing a potentially infinite sequence. (So, given a fixed input, if at some stage $M$ outputs some $\tau$, and then at a later stage it outputs $\widehat{\tau}$, then $\tau \preccurlyeq \widehat{\tau}$.) We write $M(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow$ if $M$ has either of these behaviors after reading exactly $\sigma$ from its input tape.

Following Levin, we call a machine **monotone** if its action is continuous, that is, for all $\sigma \prec \tau$, if $M(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow$ and $M(\tau) \!\!\downarrow$ then $M(\sigma) \preccurlyeq M(\tau)$. Another way of thinking of such machines is to define them as a computably enumerable $W$ collection of pairs of strings $(\sigma, \tau)$ such that for all pairs $(\sigma, \tau)$ and $(\widehat{\sigma}, \widehat{\tau})$ in $W$, if $\sigma \preccurlyeq \widehat{\sigma}$, then $\tau$ is compatible with $\widehat{\tau}$. Note that (discrete) prefix-free machines are monotone; their definition simply asks that for each $\sigma$ there be at most one $\tau$ with $(\sigma, \tau) \in W$. Discrete monotone machines are also known as **process machines**.

We fix a universal monotone machine $\mathcal{M}$ and a universal discrete monotone machine $\mathcal{D}$. Using these machines we can define two varieties of monotone Kolmogorov complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.1 — Levin, Schnorr)</span></p>

(i) The **monotone complexity** of $\sigma$ is $Km(\sigma) = \min\lbrace |\tau| : \sigma \preccurlyeq \mathcal{M}(\tau) \!\!\downarrow \rbrace$.

(ii) The **process complexity** of $\sigma$ is $Km_D(\sigma) = \min\lbrace |\tau| : \mathcal{D}(\tau) \!\!\downarrow= \sigma \rbrace$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Process Complexity — Schnorr's Motivation)</span></p>

The latter notion was introduced by Schnorr and further developed by him, where process complexity was used to obtain a characterization of 1-randomness. Schnorr called it process complexity because he regarded information as being given with a direction. As he put it, "he who wants to understand a book will not read it backwards, since the comments or facts which are given in its first part will help him to understand subsequent chapters (this means they help him to find regularities in the rest of the book). Hence anyone who tries to detect regularities in a process (for example an infinite sequence or an extremely long finite sequence) proceeds in the direction of the process."

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.2 — Levin, Solomonoff)</span></p>

A **strict process machine** is a partial computable function $M : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ such that if $\tau \in \operatorname{dom}(M)$ and $\tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$, then $\tau' \in \operatorname{dom}(M)$ and $M(\tau') \preccurlyeq M(\tau)$.

Let $U$ be a universal strict process machine. The **strict process complexity** $Km_S(\sigma)$ of $\sigma$ is $C_U(\sigma)$.

</div>

The distinction between a strict process machine and a process machine is almost completely unimportant in this book. Until we reach that point, all theorems we state hold for both measures, and we will stick to Schnorr's definition.

Clearly, $Km(\sigma) \leqslant Km_D(\sigma) + O(1) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$ and $C(\sigma) \leqslant Km_D(\sigma) + O(1)$. Furthermore, the identity machine is monotone, so $Km_D(\sigma) \leqslant |\sigma| + O(1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.3)</span></p>

$Km(0^n 1) = Km_D(0^n 1) \pm O(1) = K(1^n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We have observed that $Km(\sigma) \leqslant Km_D(\sigma) + O(1) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$ for all $\sigma$, and clearly $K(0^n 1) = K(1^n) \pm O(1)$, so it is enough to show that $K(1^n) \leqslant Km(0^n 1) + O(1)$. The minimal-length $\mathcal{M}$-descriptions of $0^n 1$ over all $n$ form a prefix-free set, so if we let $L = \lbrace (k + 1, 1^n) : \exists \tau \in 2^k\, (0^n 1 \preccurlyeq \mathcal{M}(\tau)) \rbrace$, then $L$ is c.e. and

$$\sum_{(k+1, 1^n) \in L} 2^{-(k+1)} \leqslant \sum_n \sum_{k \geqslant Km(0^n 1)} 2^{-(k+1)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-Km(0^n 1)} \leqslant 1,$$

so $L$ is a KC set. Furthermore, $(Km(0^n 1) + 1, 1^n) \in L$ for all $n$, so $K(1^n) \leqslant Km(0^n 1) + O(1)$.

In some ways, the theory of monotone complexity is smoother than those of plain and prefix-free complexity. For example, we have the following simpler version of Chaitin's Theorem 3.4.4.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.5)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computable iff $Km(A \upharpoonright n) = O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $A$ is computable then there is a monotone machine that on input $\lambda$ outputs $A$, so $Km(A \upharpoonright n) = O(1)$. Conversely, if $Km(A \upharpoonright n) = O(1)$ then there are computable sets $B_0, \ldots, B_k$ such that for each $n$ there is an $i \leqslant k$ for which $A \upharpoonright n \prec B_i$. But that clearly implies that $A = B_i$ for some $i \leqslant k$, so $A$ is computable.

For process complexity, we have the following analogous result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.6)</span></p>

A set $A$ is computable iff $Km_D(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant Km_D(1^n) + O(1)$ for all $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $M$ be a machine such that for each $\sigma$ we have $M(\sigma) \!\!\downarrow= 1^n$ where $n$ is the position of $\sigma$ in the length-lexicographic ordering of $2^{<\omega}$. Then $M$ is a discrete monotone machine, so $Km_D(1^n) \leqslant \log n + O(1)$. Since monotone machines are Turing machines, at $C$-random $n$ we have $Km_D(1^n) = \log n \pm O(1)$. Now we simply follow the proof of Theorem 3.4.4.

We have $Km(1^n) = O(1)$ and $Km(0^n) = O(1)$ for all $n$, but for each $c$ we can find an $n$ such that $Km(0^n 1^n) > c$, so $Km$ is not subadditive. However, it is possible to recover a mild form of subadditivity by mixing complexities.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.7)</span></p>

$Km(\sigma\tau) \leqslant K(\sigma) + Km(\tau) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $N$ be a machine defined as follows. On input $\rho$, the machine $N$ looks for $\mu$ and $\nu$ such that $\rho = \mu\nu$ and $\mathcal{U}(\mu) \!\!\downarrow$. Then $N$ computes $\mathcal{U}(\mu)\mathcal{M}(\nu)$. (In other words, $N$ writes $\mathcal{U}(\mu)$ on its output tape, then proceeds to emulate $\mathcal{M}$ on input $\nu$.) The machine $N$ is monotone. If $\mu$ is a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\sigma$ and $\nu$ is a minimal-length $\mathcal{M}$-program such that $\tau \preccurlyeq \mathcal{M}(\nu) \!\!\downarrow$, then $\sigma\tau \preccurlyeq N(\mu\nu) \!\!\downarrow$, so $Km(\sigma\tau) \leqslant |\mu\nu| + O(1) = K(\sigma) + Km(\tau) + O(1)$.

Process complexity is also not subadditive. The usual argument for plain complexity uses complexity oscillations applied to a long random string. This argument is not available for process complexity, but it is nevertheless possible to show that subadditivity fails.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.8 — Day)</span></p>

For any $d$ there are $\sigma$ and $\tau$ such that $Km_D(\sigma\tau) > Km_D(\sigma) + Km_D(\tau) + d$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.9 — Day)</span></p>

If $A \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ is prefix-free then

$$\sum_{\sigma \in A} 2^{-Km_D(\sigma)} \leqslant 1.$$

</div>

*Proof.* Let $B = \lbrace \tau_\sigma : \sigma \in A \rbrace$, where $\tau_\sigma$ is a minimal description of $\sigma$ with respect to the fixed universal discrete monotone machine $\mathcal{D}$. If $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are distinct elements of $B$, then $\mathcal{D}(\tau_1)$ and $\mathcal{D}(\tau_2)$ are incompatible, so $B$ is also prefix-free. Thus $\sum_{\sigma \in A} 2^{-Km_D(\sigma)} = \sum_{\tau \in B} 2^{-|\tau|} = \mu(\llbracket B \rrbracket) \leqslant 1$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.15.4 — Uspensky and Shen)</span></p>

For any $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, we have $K(\sigma) \leqslant Km(\sigma) + \log |\sigma| + \log \log(|\sigma|) + \cdots + (1 + \varepsilon) \log^{(k)} (|\sigma|)$.

</div>

Since $C(\sigma) \leqslant Km_D(\sigma) + O(1) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$, some results come for free. For instance, the estimate in Lemma 4.2.1 below shows that for any $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, we have $K(\sigma) \leqslant Km_D(\sigma) + \log |\sigma| + \log \log(|\sigma|) + \cdots + (1 + \varepsilon) \log^{(k)} (|\sigma|)$. Actually, the strengthening of this result to $Km$ is also true.

### 3.16 Continuous semimeasures and $KM$-complexity

In Section 3.9, we defined the notion of a discrete semimeasure. This notion is not compatible with the usual measure on Cantor space. Its continuous analog is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.16.1)</span></p>

A **continuous semimeasure** is a function $\delta : \lbrace \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket : \sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \rbrace \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that

(i) $\delta(\llbracket \lambda \rrbracket) \leqslant 1$ and

(ii) $\delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) \geqslant \delta(\llbracket \sigma 0 \rrbracket) + \delta(\llbracket \sigma 1 \rrbracket)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

To simplify notation, we often think of $\delta$ as a function from $2^{<\omega}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ and write $\delta(\sigma)$ for $\delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)$.

There is a big difference between continuous semimeasures and the discrete semimeasures of Section 3.9. For example, if we let $\delta(\llbracket 0^k \rrbracket) = 1$ for all $k$ and $\delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = 0$ for all other $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, then $\delta$ is a continuous semimeasure, but $\sum_\sigma \delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \infty$.

Continuous semimeasures are closely connected to the important notion of supermartingale, which we discuss in Chapter 6.

A continuous semimeasure $\delta$ is **computably enumerable** if it is approximable from below; that is, there are uniformly computable functions $\delta_s : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that for all $s$ and $\sigma$ we have $\delta_{s+1}(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) \geqslant \delta_s(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)$ and $\lim_s \delta_s(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)$.

A c.e. continuous semimeasure $\delta$ is **optimal** if for every c.e. continuous semimeasure $\delta'$, we have $\delta \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \geqslant O(\delta'(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket))$. Levin showed that there is an optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure. Given this fact, we can associate a version of Kolmogorov complexity to a continuous semimeasure.

Fix an optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure $\delta$ and let $KM(\sigma) = -\log \delta(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)$. This notion is called the **a priori entropy** of $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$; we will also refer to it as the **$KM$-complexity** of $\sigma$.

Along the lines of the Coding Theorem 3.9.4, but for continuous semimeasures, we can show that $KM$ corresponds to the probability that a string is output by a given universal monotone machine. The following result is due to Levin.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(3.16.2 — Levin)</span></p>

(i) If $L$ is a monotone machine, then $M_L$ is a c.e. continuous semimeasure, where $M_L : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ is defined by $M_L(\sigma) = \mu(\llbracket \lbrace \tau : \exists \sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma\, ((\tau, \sigma') \in L) \rbrace \rrbracket)$.

(ii) If $m$ is a c.e. continuous semimeasure, then there exists a monotone machine $L$ such that $M_L = m$.

(iii) If $\mathcal{M}$ is a universal monotone machine, then $M_\mathcal{M}$ is an optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure, so $KM(\sigma) = -\log(M_\mathcal{M}(\sigma)) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

It was a long-standing open question whether $KM$ and $Km$ are the same, that is, whether a natural analog of the Coding Theorem 3.9.4 holds for continuous semimeasures. In one direction, we saw in Theorem 3.16.2 that $KM(\sigma) = -\log(M_\mathcal{M}(\sigma)) \pm O(1)$. But $M_\mathcal{M}(\sigma) = \mu(\llbracket \lbrace \tau : \exists \sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma\, ((\tau, \sigma') \in \mathcal{M}) \rbrace \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-Km(\sigma)}$. Thus $KM(\sigma) \leqslant Km(\sigma) + O(1)$. In Section 4.5, we will see results by Gács and Day showing that the converse does not hold, although proving that fact is remarkably difficult.
