## Chapter 4: Relating Complexities

In this chapter, we look at some of the fundamental relationships between flavors of Kolmogorov complexity. The first four sections explore relationships between plain and prefix-free complexity, while the last deals with the relationship between $Km$ and $KM$. Throughout this chapter, in addition to our fixed universal prefix-free machine $\mathcal{U}$, we fix a universal Turing machine $\mathcal{V}$ and define $C$ using this machine.

### 4.1 Levin's Theorem relating $C$ and $K$

Our first results relating $K$ and $C$ are due to Levin. They appeared in his dissertation; the first published proofs were in Gács.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.1.1 — Levin)</span></p>

(i) $C(\sigma) = \min\lbrace n : K(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant n \rbrace \pm O(1)$.

(ii) $C(\sigma) = K(\sigma \mid C(\sigma)) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $m_\sigma = \min\lbrace n : K(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant n \rbrace$.

Part (i). Consider a prefix-free oracle machine $M$, with coding constant $c$ given by the recursion theorem, such that $M^n(\tau) = \mu$ iff $\lvert\tau\rvert = n - c$ and $\mathcal{V}(\tau) = \mu$. Given $\sigma$, let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$. On input $\tau$ and oracle $C(\sigma) + c$, this machine will output $\sigma$. Thus $K(\sigma \mid C(\sigma) + c) \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + c = C(\sigma) + c$, and hence $C(\sigma) \geqslant m_\sigma - O(1)$.

Now consider a plain machine $N$ that, on input $\tau$, searches for $\mu \preccurlyeq \tau$ such that $\mathcal{U}^{\lvert\tau\rvert}(\mu) \!\!\downarrow$, and if such a $\mu$ is found, outputs $\mathcal{U}^{\lvert\tau\rvert}(\mu)$. Note that, by the prefix-freeness of $\mathcal{U}$, such a $\mu$, if it exists, is unique. Suppose that $K(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant n$, and let $\mu$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\sigma$ given $n$. Note that $\lvert\mu\rvert \leqslant n$. Let $\tau = \mu 0^{n - \lvert\mu\rvert}$. Then $N(\tau) = \mathcal{U}^n(\mu) = \sigma$, so $C(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + O(1) = n + O(1)$. Thus $C(\sigma) \leqslant m_\sigma + O(1)$.

Part (ii). We have $K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma - 1) \geqslant m_\sigma - 1$, so $K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma) \geqslant K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma - 1) - O(1) \geqslant m_\sigma - O(1)$. But also $K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma) \leqslant m_\sigma$, so in fact $K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma) = m_\sigma \pm O(1)$. Now part (i) gives us

$$K(\sigma \mid C(\sigma)) = K(\sigma \mid m_\sigma) \pm O(1) = m_\sigma \pm O(1) = C(\sigma) \pm O(1).$$

### 4.2 Solovay's Theorems relating $C$ and $K$

In this section and the next, we look at the beautiful unpublished material of Solovay relating $C$ and $K$. The positive results involve simulations of Turing machines by prefix-free machines and vice versa. The negative ones involve the construction of an infinite sequence of strings whose plain and prefix-free complexities behave very differently in the limit. These results also have a bearing on the relationship between $C$-randomness and strong $K$-randomness (as defined in Sections 3.1 and 3.8, respectively).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.1 — Solovay)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) + \cdots + C^{(n)}(\sigma) + O(C^{(n+1)}(\sigma))$ for any $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* This follows from Lemma 4.2.3 below by an easy induction, but we give a direct proof here.

Recall the following idea from Proposition 3.1.5 and Lemma 3.15.4. For a string $\sigma = a_0 a_1 \ldots a_m$, let $\overline{\sigma} = a_0 a_0 a_1 a_1 \ldots a_m a_m 01$. That is, $\overline{\sigma}$ is the result of doubling each bit of $\sigma$ and adding $01$ to the end. The key fact about this operation is that if $\sigma \neq \tau$ then $\overline{\sigma}$ and $\overline{\tau}$ are incompatible.

Thus there is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that $M(\overline{\tau}) = \mathcal{V}(\tau)$ for each $\tau$. Taking $\tau$ to be a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$, this fact shows that $K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\overline{\tau}\rvert + O(1) = O(C(\sigma))$, thus proving the lemma for $n = 0$.

But we can also build a prefix-free machine $M$ so that for each $\tau$ we have $M(\overline{\tau'}\tau) = \mathcal{V}(\tau)$, where $\tau'$ is a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$. On input $\mu$, the machine $M$ simply looks for a splitting $\mu = \mu_1 \mu_2$ such that $\mu_1 = \overline{\nu}$ for some $\nu$, then computes $\mathcal{V}(\nu)$, and if that halts and equals $\lvert\mu_2\rvert$, computes $\mathcal{V}(\mu_2)$ and outputs the result if any. Taking $\tau$ to be a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$, this construction shows that $K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\overline{\tau'}\tau\rvert = C(\sigma) + O(C^{(2)}(\sigma))$, thus proving the lemma for $n = 1$.

It should now be clear how to iterate this process to prove the lemma in general.

The more precise relationships between $C$ and $K$ are as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.2 — Solovay)</span></p>

$$K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma)). \tag{4.1}$$

*and*

$$C(\sigma) = K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma)). \tag{4.2}$$

</div>

*Proof.* It is useful to recall what the $O$-notation means here. For example, (4.1) means that there is a $d$ such that

$$C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) - dC^{(3)}(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) + dC^{(3)}(\sigma)$$

for all $\sigma$. We have already seen that the second inequality holds for some $d$. The difficulty comes in proving the first inequality.

One might expect (4.1) and (4.2) to be part of an infinite sequence of approximations involving increasing numbers of iterations of $C$ and $K$, respectively, as in Proposition 4.2.1. In the next section we will see that, remarkably, this is not the case.

As Solovay observed, (4.1) and (4.2) are in fact equivalent. Indeed, we will also prove that

$$K^{(2)}(\sigma) - C^{(2)}(\sigma) = O(K^{(3)}(\sigma)) \tag{4.3}$$

and

$$K^{(3)}(\sigma) \text{ is asymptotically equal to } C^{(3)}(\sigma). \tag{4.4}$$

Granted these two facts, (4.1) and (4.2) are clearly equivalent. The proof of the above equations proceeds in two stages. First we prove that

$$K(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) + O(1), \tag{4.5}$$

which is not too difficult. Then we prove the more difficult inequality

$$C(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) + O(1). \tag{4.6}$$

Note that (4.5) and (4.6) are close to (4.2), the problem being that (4.5) has the term $KC$ rather than $K^{(2)}$. It turns out that we can use the estimate on $K - C$ to get one on $K^{(2)} - KC$ to establish (4.2). After doing so, it will remain to prove (4.3) and (4.4) to get (4.1).

We begin by establishing (4.5).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.3)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Define a prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. On input $\mu$, first attempt to simulate $\mathcal{U}$ by searching for $\mu_1 \preccurlyeq \mu$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\mu_1) \!\!\downarrow$. If such a string is found then let $\mu_2$ be such that $\mu = \mu_1 \mu_2$ and check whether $\lvert\mu_2\rvert = \mathcal{U}(\mu_1)$. If so, output $\mathcal{V}(\mu_2)$ (if this value is defined).

Notice that $M$ is prefix-free, since, firstly, $\mathcal{U}$ is prefix-free, and, secondly, if $M$ halts on $\mu$ then $\mu = \mu_1 \mu_2$ with $\mathcal{U}(\mu_1) \!\!\downarrow$ and $\lvert\mu\rvert = \lvert\mu_1\rvert + \lvert\mathcal{U}(\mu_1)\rvert$, which means that all extensions of $\mu_1$ on which $M$ halts have the same length, and hence are pairwise incompatible.

Given $\sigma$, let $\tau_2$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$ and let $\tau_1$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $C(\sigma) = \lvert\tau_2\rvert$. Then $M(\tau_1 \tau_2) = \mathcal{V}(\tau_2) = \sigma$, and hence $K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\tau_2\rvert + \lvert\tau_1\rvert + O(1) = C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) + O(1)$.

We now establish (4.6). Fix a computable enumeration of $\text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}$ and let $L_n$ be the list of strings $\tau \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}$ such that $\lvert\tau\rvert = n$, ordered according to this enumeration. We will show that there is a $c$ such that $\lvert L_{K(\sigma)}\rvert \leqslant 2^{K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c}$. We will then argue as follows (in the proof of Lemma 4.2.6). Given $\sigma$, let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\sigma$. Let $\mu_1$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $K^{(2)}(\sigma)$ and let $\mu_2$ be a string of length $K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c$ encoding the position of $\tau$ on the list $L_{K(\sigma)}$. The strings $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ together allow us to compute both $K(\sigma) = \mathcal{U}(\mu_1) + \lvert\mu_2\rvert - c$ and the position of $\tau$ on the list $L_{K(\sigma)}$, and hence allow us to compute $\sigma$. Since $\mathcal{U}$ is prefix-free, the string $\mu_1 \mu_2$ is enough to allow us to compute $\sigma$, whence $C(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\mu_1 \mu_2\rvert + O(1) = K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) + O(1)$.

We now give the details of this argument.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.4)</span></p>

$\lvert L_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{n - K(n) + O(1)}$.

</div>

*Proof.* We wish to apply the KC Theorem 3.6.1 to build a prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. We enumerate the $L_n$ simultaneously. Whenever we first see that $\lvert L_n \rvert \geqslant 2^k$, we enumerate a request $(n - k + 1, n)$. We claim that these requests form a KC set. Assume this claim for now, and let $k_n$ be the largest $k$ such that $\lvert L_n \rvert \geqslant 2^k$. Then $K(n) \leqslant n - k_n + O(1)$, so $2^{K(n)} \leqslant 2^{n - k_n + O(1)}$. Thus $\lvert L_n \rvert < 2^{k_n + 1} \leqslant 2^{n - K(n) + O(1)}$.

So we are left with establishing the claim. Defining $k_n$ as above, this task amounts to showing that

$$\sum_n \sum_{i \leqslant k_n} 2^{-(n-i+1)} \leqslant 1.$$

To show that the above inequality holds, first note that

$$\sum_{i \leqslant k_n} 2^{-(n-i+1)} = \sum_{j=n-k_n+1}^{n+1} 2^{-j} \leqslant \sum_{j \geqslant n-k_n+1} 2^{-j} = 2^{k_n - n}.$$

Since $2^{k_n} \leqslant \lvert L_n \rvert$, we now have $\sum_{i \leqslant k_n} 2^{-(n-i+1)} \leqslant 2^{-n} \lvert L_n \rvert$, and hence

$$\sum_n \sum_{i \leqslant k_n} 2^{-(n-i+1)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-n} \lvert L_n \rvert = \sum_n \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \sigma \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U} \wedge \lvert\sigma\rvert = n \rbrace = \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \sigma \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U} \rbrace \leqslant 1.$$

This fact establishes the claim and completes the proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.5)</span></p>

$\lvert L_{K(\sigma)} \rvert \leqslant 2^{K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + O(1)}$.

</div>

We now establish (4.6).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.6)</span></p>

$C(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $c$ be such that $\lvert L_{K(\sigma)} \rvert \leqslant 2^{K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c}$ for all $\sigma$. We define a machine $M$ as follows.

On input $\mu$, first attempt to simulate $\mathcal{U}$ by searching for $\mu_1 \preccurlyeq \mu$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\mu_1) \!\!\downarrow$. If such a string is found then let $\mu_2$ be such that $\mu = \mu_1 \mu_2$. Let $n = \lvert\mu_2\rvert + \mathcal{U}(\mu_1) - c$ and interpret $\mu_2$ as a number $j$ in the interval $[1, 2^{\lvert\mu_2\rvert}]$ in the natural way. Enumerate $L_n$ until its $j$th element $\tau$ appears, if ever. If such an element $\tau$ is found, compute $\mathcal{U}(\tau)$ and output the result (if this value is defined).

Given $\sigma$, let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\sigma$ and let $\nu_2$ be a string of length $K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c$ encoding the position $j$ of $\tau$ on the list $L_{K(\sigma)}$. Let $\nu_1$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $K^{(2)}(\sigma)$. If we run $M$ on input $\nu_1 \nu_2$ then $M$ will set $n = \lvert\nu_2\rvert + \mathcal{U}(\nu_1) - c = K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c + K^{(2)}(\sigma) - c = K(\sigma)$ and will proceed to search the list $L_n$ for its $j$th element, namely $\tau$. It will then output $\mathcal{U}(\tau) = \sigma$.

Since $\lvert\nu_1 \nu_2\rvert = K^{(3)}(\sigma) + K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + c$, the result follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.7)</span></p>

$C(\sigma) = K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $D(\sigma) = K(\sigma) - C(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma)$. We need to show that $\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert = O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

By Lemma 4.2.6, $D(\sigma) \geqslant -K^{(3)}(\sigma) - O(1)$.

By Lemma 4.2.3, $D(\sigma) \leqslant K(C(\sigma)) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + O(1)$. By the facts about the relationship between $K$-complexity and addition and subtraction of integers mentioned above,

$$\lvert K(C(\sigma)) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) \rvert \leqslant K(\lvert C(\sigma) - K(\sigma) \rvert) + O(1) \leqslant K(\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) + O(1).$$

Thus $D(\sigma) \leqslant K(\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) + O(1)$.

Putting the two previous paragraphs together, we see that

$$\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert \leqslant K^{(3)}(\sigma) + K(\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert) + O(1).$$

For $k$ ranging over the natural numbers, $K(k) = o(k)$, so

$$\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert - o(\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert) \leqslant K^{(3)}(\sigma),$$

whence $\lvert D(\sigma) \rvert = O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.8)</span></p>

$\lvert K^{(2)}(\sigma) - C^{(2)}(\sigma) \rvert = O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* By Lemma 4.2.7, $C(\sigma)$ and $K(\sigma)$ differ by $O(K^{(2)}(\sigma))$, and hence their $K$-complexities differ by $O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$. That is,

$$K(C(\sigma)) = K^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma)). \tag{4.7}$$

Likewise, (4.7) shows that $K^{(2)}(C(\sigma))$ and $K^{(3)}(\sigma)$ differ by $o(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$, whence

$$K^{(2)}(C(\sigma)) = O(K^{(3)}(\sigma)). \tag{4.8}$$

Replacing $\sigma$ by $C(\sigma)$ in (4.2), we have

$$C^{(2)}(\sigma) = K(C(\sigma)) - K^{(2)}(C(\sigma)) \pm O(K^{(3)}(C(\sigma))).$$

By (4.8), this equation gives us

$$C^{(2)}(\sigma) = K(C(\sigma)) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma)). \tag{4.9}$$

Combining this equation with (4.7) establishes the lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.9)</span></p>

$K^{(3)}(\sigma)$ is asymptotically equal to $C^{(3)}(\sigma)$.

</div>

*Proof.* By Lemma 4.2.8, $K^{(2)}(\sigma)$ and $C^{(2)}(\sigma)$ differ by $O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$, so $\lvert K^{(3)}(\sigma) - K(C^{(2)}(\sigma)) \rvert = o(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$, and hence $K^{(3)}$ and $KC^{(2)}$ are asymptotically equal. But $K$ and $C$ are asymptotically equal, so $KC^{(2)}$ and $C^{(3)}$ are asymptotically equal.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.10)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* From Lemma 4.2.7 we have $K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + K^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$. Using Corollary 4.2.8 we then have $K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma))$. By Corollary 4.2.9, it follows that $K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

This result completes the proof of Theorem 4.2.2.

The following corollary of the previous results will be useful below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.11 — Solovay)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Combine Corollaries 4.2.9 and 4.2.10 with (4.9).

The results above all have relativized forms. For instance, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.12 — Solovay)</span></p>

$$K(\sigma \mid \tau) = C(\sigma \mid \tau) + C^{(2)}(\sigma \mid \tau) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma \mid \tau)),$$

$$C(\sigma \mid \tau) = K(\sigma \mid \tau) - K^{(2)}(\sigma \mid \tau) \pm O(K^{(3)}(\sigma \mid \tau)),$$

*and*

$$C(\sigma \mid \tau) \leqslant K(\sigma \mid \tau) + O(1) \leqslant C(\sigma \mid \tau) + K(C(\sigma \mid \tau)) + O(1).$$

</div>

Another point worth noticing is that the upper bound on the size of $L_{K(\sigma)}$ given in Corollary 4.2.5 is strict, as shown by the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.2.13 — Solovay)</span></p>

$\lvert L_{K(\sigma)} \rvert \geqslant 2^{K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + O(1)}$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose otherwise. Define the prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. By the recursion theorem, we may assume we know the coding constant $c$ of $M$. On input $\mu$, search for a splitting $\mu = \mu_1 \mu_2$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\mu_1) \!\!\downarrow$ and $\lvert\mu_2\rvert = \mathcal{U}(\mu_1) - \lvert\mu_1\rvert - (c + 1)$. If such a splitting is found then let $k$ be the element of $[1, 2^{\lvert\mu_2\rvert}]$ coded by $\mu_2$, and search for the $k$th element enumerated into $L_{\mathcal{U}(\mu_1)}$. If such an element $\tau$ is found, compute $\mathcal{U}(\tau)$ and output the result, if any.

By assumption, there is a $\sigma$ such that $\lvert L_{K(\sigma)} \rvert \leqslant 2^{K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) - (c+1)}$. Let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\sigma$ and let $\mu_1$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $K(\sigma)$. Let $\mu_2$ be a string of length $K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) - (c + 1)$ coding the position of $\tau$ in $L_{K(\sigma)}$. Then $M(\mu_1 \mu_2) \!\!\downarrow= \sigma$. But $\lvert\mu_1 \mu_2\rvert = K^{(2)}(\sigma) + K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) - (c + 1) = K(\sigma) - (c + 1)$. But $c$ is the coding constant of $M$, so $K(\sigma) \leqslant K(\sigma) - 1$, which is a contradiction.

### 4.3 Strong $K$-randomness, $C$-randomness, and limitations on the results of the previous section

In this section, we will present Solovay's theorem that, roughly speaking, every strongly $K$-random string is $C$-random, but the converse is not true. One intuitive explanation for this difference between the two notions of randomness is the following. Suppose we know a string $\sigma$. If $\sigma$ is $C$-random then also knowing $C(\sigma)$ gives us no additional information, but if $\sigma$ is strongly $K$-random then also knowing $K(\sigma)$ gives us $K(\lvert\sigma\rvert)$. Thus there is more information in the prefix-free complexity of a strongly $K$-random string than in the plain complexity of a $C$-random string. Indeed, this extra amount of information is exploited in the proof of Theorem 4.3.7 below, which has as an immediate corollary that not every $C$-random string is strongly $K$-random.

The existence of $C$-random but not strongly $K$-random strings will also allow us to show that the main results of the previous section cannot be improved. For instance, in the previous section we established the identity (4.1): $K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\sigma))$. At first glance, one would expect this identity to be part of an infinite sequence of identities with decreasing $O$-terms (as in Proposition 4.2.1). However, the methods of this section will show that the identity $K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) + C^{(3)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(4)}(\sigma))$ is *not* true, and hence (4.1) is as sharp as possible.

#### 4.3.1 Positive results

The following proposition combines some information about plain and prefix-free complexity from Chapter 3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.1)</span></p>

There are constants $c_C$ and $c_K$ such that

(i) $C(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + c_C$,

(ii) $\lvert\lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : C(\sigma) \leqslant n + c_C - j \rbrace\rvert = O(2^{n-j})$,

(iii) $K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + c_K$, and

(iv) $\lvert\lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : K(\sigma) \leqslant n + K(n) + c_K - j \rbrace\rvert = O(2^{n-j})$.

</div>

Let $m_C(\sigma) = \lvert\sigma\rvert + c_C - C(\sigma)$ and $m_K(\sigma) = \lvert\sigma\rvert + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + c_K - K(\sigma)$. In light of Proposition 4.3.1, we see that these values measure how far the complexity of $\sigma$ falls from its potential maximum. For either version of randomness, the random strings are those strings $\sigma$ for which the corresponding $m(\sigma)$ is small.

Recall that, for fixed constants $c$ and $d$, we say that $\sigma$ is $C$-random if $C(\sigma) \geqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - c$ and strongly $K$-random if $K(\sigma) \geqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) - d$. As we will see, the following theorem implies that every strongly $K$-random string is $C$-random for some constant.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.2 — Solovay)</span></p>

$m_K(\sigma) \geqslant m_C(\sigma) - O(\log m_C(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Since $C(\sigma) = \lvert\sigma\rvert - m_C(\sigma) + O(1)$,

$$K(C(\sigma)) = K(\lvert\sigma\rvert - m_C(\sigma) + O(1)) \leqslant K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + K(m_C(\sigma) + O(1)) \leqslant K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + O(\log m_C(\sigma)).$$

By Lemma 4.2.3, $K(\sigma) \leqslant C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) + O(1)$. Consequently,

$$K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - m_C(\sigma) + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + O(\log m_C(\sigma)).$$

Rearranging this inequality, we get

$$\lvert\sigma\rvert + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) - K(\sigma) \geqslant m_C(\sigma) - O(\log m_C(\sigma)).$$

Since the left-hand side of this inequality is $m_K(\sigma)$ (up to the constant $c_K$), it follows that $m_K(\sigma) \geqslant m_C(\sigma) - O(\log m_C(\sigma))$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.3 — Solovay)</span></p>

Every strongly $K$-random string is $C$-random for some constant.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\sigma$ is strongly $K$-random then $m_K(\sigma) \leqslant c$ for some fixed $c$ (independent of $\sigma$). By Theorem 4.3.2, $m_C(\sigma) - d \log m_C(\sigma) \leqslant c$ for some fixed $d$, which clearly implies that $m_C(\sigma) \leqslant c'$ for some fixed $c'$.

#### 4.3.2 Counterexamples

We now turn to counterexamples. We will present Solovay's construction of an infinite sequence of strings whose plain complexities behave "as differently as possible" from their prefix-free complexities, and examine the consequences of the existence of such sequences.

We begin with a few lemmas that will be useful below. The first says that if $\tau$ is $C$-random given $n$, then there are many $\mu$'s of length $n$ such that $\tau\mu$ is $C$-random (though for a different constant).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.4 — Solovay)</span></p>

For each $c$ there is a $d$ such that, for all $n$ and $\tau$, if

$$C(\tau \mid n) \geqslant \lvert\tau\rvert - c \tag{4.10}$$

then

$$\lvert\lbrace \mu \in 2^n : C(\tau\mu) \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + n - d \rbrace\rvert \leqslant 2^{n-c}. \tag{4.11}$$

</div>

*Proof.* Fix $c$. We build a machine that, for each $d$, $\tau$, and $n$ not satisfying (4.11), provides a short $C$-program for $\tau$ given $n$. The length of this program will depend on $d$, and we will show that, for large enough $d$, this program is shorter than $\lvert\tau\rvert - c$, so that any $\tau$ and $n$ not satisfying (4.11) also fail to satisfy (4.10).

For each $d$, $m$, and $n$, let $S_{d,m,n}$ be a list of all $\sigma \in 2^m$ such that $\lvert\lbrace \mu \in 2^n : C(\sigma\mu) \leqslant m + n - d \rbrace\rvert > 2^{n-c}$. Notice that the $S_{d,m,n}$ are uniformly c.e. Furthermore, there are at most $2^{m+n-d+1}$ many pairs $(\sigma, \mu)$ such that $\lvert\sigma\rvert = m$ and $C(\sigma\mu) \leqslant m + n - d$, whence $\lvert S_{d,m,n} \rvert \leqslant 2^{m+c-d+1}$.

Define the (plain) machine $M$ as follows. On input $(\sigma, n)$, search for $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ such that $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}$ and $\sigma = \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \sigma_3$. If such $\sigma_i$ exist, then let $d = \mathcal{U}(\sigma_1)$ and $m = \mathcal{U}(\sigma_2) + \lvert\sigma_3\rvert$. Interpret $\sigma_3$ as a number $j$ in the interval $[1, 2^{\lvert\sigma_3\rvert}]$ and output the $j$th element of $S_{d,m,n}$, if any.

Consider $d$, $n$, and $\tau$ not satisfying (4.11), and let $m = \lvert\tau\rvert$. Then $\tau \in S_{d,m,n}$. Let $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ be strings of minimal length such that $\mathcal{U}(\sigma_1) = d$ and $\mathcal{U}(\sigma_2) = d - c + 1$ and $\sigma_3$ has length $m + c - d + 1$ and codes the position of $\tau$ in $S_{d,m,n}$. Then $M(\sigma_1 \sigma_2 \sigma_3, n) = \tau$, so

$$C(\tau \mid n) \leqslant K(d) + K(d - c + 1) + m + c - d + 1 + O(1) \leqslant m - d + O(\log d) = \lvert\tau\rvert - d + O(\log d),$$

where the $O$ constant does not depend on $\tau$ or $n$. Choosing $d$ large enough, we see that if $n$ and $\tau$ do not satisfy (4.11) then $C(\tau \mid n) \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert - c$, so $n$ and $\tau$ do not satisfy (4.10).

Combining Lemma 4.3.4 with part (iv) of Lemma 4.3.1, we have the following corollary, which says that if $\tau$ is $C$-random given $n$, then, since there are many $\mu$'s of length $n$ such that $\tau\mu$ is $C$-random, there is such a $\mu$ that is strongly $K$-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.5 — Solovay)</span></p>

For each $c$ there is a $d$ such that, for all $n$ and $\tau$, if $C(\tau \mid n) \geqslant \lvert\tau\rvert - c$ then there is a $\mu \in 2^n$ with $C(\tau\mu) \geqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + n - d$ and $K(\mu) \geqslant n + K(n) - d$.

</div>

The next lemma says that, given any $n$, we can find an $m$ such that the prefix-free complexity of the strongly $K$-random strings of length $m$ is close to $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.6 — Solovay)</span></p>

There is a $c$ such that for each $n$ there is an $m$ with $\lvert m + K(m) - n \rvert \leqslant c$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $d$ is large enough then $\lvert K(m + d) - K(m) \rvert \leqslant K(d) + O(1) < d$. Let $f(m) = m + K(m)$. Then $f(m + d) - f(m) = m + d + K(m + d) - m - K(m) = d + K(m + d) - K(m)$, so $0 < f(m + d) - f(m) < 2d$. Let $c = 2d$ and, given $n$, choose $m$ such that $\lvert f(m) - n \rvert$ is minimal. Then $\lvert f(m) - n \rvert \leqslant c$, since otherwise one of $f(m + d)$ or $f(m - d)$ would be closer to $n$ than $f(m)$.

We are now ready to prove the main theorem of this section.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.7 — Solovay)</span></p>

There is an infinite sequence of strings $\nu_i$ such that

(i) $\lim_i \lvert\nu_i\rvert = \infty$,

(ii) $C(\nu_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert \pm O(1)$, and

(iii) $\lim_i \frac{m_K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1$.

</div>

Before proceeding with the proof, we note the following consequence. By item (i) in Theorem 4.3.7, each $\nu_i$ is $C$-random. By item (iii), $\nu_i$ is not strongly $K$-random for large enough $i$. Hence the converse to Corollary 4.3.3 does not hold.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.8 — Solovay)</span></p>

There is a $c$ such that, for all $d$, there are infinitely many strings that are $C$-random with constant $c$ but not strongly $K$-random with constant $d$.

</div>

*Proof of Theorem 4.3.7.* The idea of the proof is to find $\tau_i$ and $n_i$ such that $\tau_i$ is hard to describe given $n_i$, but easy given $K(n_i)$. Then Corollary 4.3.5 implies that there is a $\mu_i$ of length $n_i$ that is random enough so that from $K(\mu_i)$ we can compute $K(n_i)$ (so $\tau_i$ is easy to describe given $K(\mu_i)$), but at the same time $\tau_i \mu_i$ is $C$-random. Letting $\nu_i = \tau_i \mu_i$, we have that $K(\nu_i)$ is not much larger than $K(\mu_i)$, but $C(\nu_i)$ is as large as possible. By carefully choosing $\tau_i$ and $n_i$ so that $\nu_i$ is sufficiently longer than $\mu_i$, we will be able to satisfy the requirements of the theorem.

We now proceed with the details of the proof. Recall that $\sigma^*$ is the first to halt among the minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-programs for $\sigma$. By Theorem 3.14.2, we can select $n_0, n_1, \ldots$ such that $\log n_i = 2^{2^i}$ and $K(n_i^* \mid n_i) \geqslant 2^i - O(i)$. By Proposition 3.14.1,

$$K(n_i^* \mid n_i) = K(K(n_i) \mid n_i) \pm O(1) \leqslant K^{(2)}(n_i) + O(1) \leqslant \log 2^{2^i} + O(\log^{(2)} 2^{2^i}) = 2^i + O(i),$$

so $K(n_i^* \mid n_i) = 2^i \pm O(i)$. Thus $K^{(2)}(n_i^* \mid n_i) = O(i)$ (and hence, of course, $K^{(3)}(n_i^* \mid n_i) = o(i)$), so by Corollary 4.2.12,

$$C(n_i^* \mid n_i) = \lvert\tau_i\rvert \pm O(1).$$

Let $\tau_i$ be the first to halt among the minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-programs for $n_i^*$ given $n_i$. (That is, $\tau_i$ is $(n_i^*)_c^*$, where the second star is defined with respect to $\mathcal{V}^{n_i}$.) Then $\lvert\tau_i\rvert = C(n_i^* \mid n_i) = 2^i \pm O(i)$. Also, by the minimality of $\tau_i$, $C(\tau_i \mid n_i) = \lvert\tau_i\rvert \pm O(1)$. (That is, $\lvert\tau_i\rvert = C(\tau_i \mid n_i) \leqslant C(n_i^* \mid n_i) + O(1) \leqslant \lvert\tau_i\rvert + O(1)$.) Thus it follows that $\tau_i$ is hard to describe given $n_i$, but as we will see below, $\tau_i$ is relatively easy to describe given $n_i^*$, and hence given $n_i$ and $K(n_i)$.

By Corollary 4.3.5, there is a $\mu_i \in 2^{n_i}$ such that $C(\tau_i \mu_i) = \lvert\tau_i\rvert + n_i \pm O(1)$ and $K(\mu_i) = n_i + K(n_i) \pm O(1)$. Let $\nu_i = \tau_i \mu_i$. Then $\lim_i \lvert\nu_i\rvert = \infty$, and each $\nu_i$ is $C$-random, since $C(\nu_i) = \lvert\tau_i\rvert + n_i \pm O(1) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert \pm O(1)$. So we are left with showing that $\lim_i \frac{m_K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1$.

We begin by computing $K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert)$. First,

$$K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) = K(\lvert\tau_i\rvert + n_i \pm O(1)) = K(n_i) \pm O(K(\lvert\tau_i\rvert)).$$

Now,

$$K(\lvert\tau_i\rvert) = K(2^i \pm O(i)) = K(2^i) \pm O(\log i),$$

since $K(2^i) = K(i) \pm O(1) = O(\log i)$. Therefore, $K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) = K(n_i) \pm O(\log i)$.

We now need an upper bound on $K(\nu_i)$. If we have a $\mathcal{U}$-program $\sigma$ for $\mu_i$ and a $\mathcal{U}$-program $\sigma'$ for $\tau_i$ given $\sigma$, then we can easily obtain a $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\nu_i = \tau_i \mu_i$ of length roughly $\lvert\sigma\sigma'\rvert$. Thus,

$$K(\nu_i) \leqslant K(\mu_i) + K(\tau_i \mid \mu_i^*) + O(1) = n_i + K(n_i) + K(\tau_i \mid \mu_i^*) \pm O(1).$$

So we will obtain our bound on $K(\nu_i)$ by bounding $K(\tau_i \mid \mu_i^*)$.

Now, $n_i = \lvert\mu_i\rvert$, so $K(n_i \mid \mu_i^*) = O(1)$. Since $\lvert\mu_i^*\rvert = K(\mu_i) = n_i + K(n_i) \pm O(1)$, $K(n_i) \pm O(1)$, we have $K(K(n_i) \mid \mu_i^*) = O(1)$. By Proposition 3.14.1, $K(n_i^* \mid n_i, K(n_i)) = O(1)$. Thus,

$$K(n_i^* \mid \mu_i^*) \leqslant K(\tau_i \mid n_i^*) + K(n_i^* \mid \mu_i^*) + O(1) = K(\tau_i \mid n_i^*) + O(1).$$

Recall that $\tau_i$ is the first to halt among the minimal-length $\mathcal{V}$-programs for $n_i^*$ given $n_i$. By the relativized version of Proposition 3.2.1,

$$K(\tau_i \mid n_i^*) \leqslant K(\lvert\tau_i\rvert \mid n_i^*) + O(1) = O(\log \lvert\tau_i\rvert) = O(i),$$

so $K(\tau_i \mid \mu_i^*) = O(i)$.

Putting the last three paragraphs together, we have

$$K(\nu_i) \leqslant n_i + K(n_i) + O(i).$$

Together with the computation of $K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert)$ above, this bound implies that

$$m_K(\nu_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert + K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K(\nu_i) \geqslant 2^i + n_i + K(n_i) - O(i) - [n_i + K(n_i) + O(i)] = 2^i - O(i) = \log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert - O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert).$$

On the other hand, it is easy to get an upper bound on $m_K(\nu_i)$ using the results of Section 4.2. Recall that $C(\nu_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert \pm O(1)$. Also, by Corollary 4.2.11, $K(\nu_i) = C(\nu_i) + K(C(\nu_i)) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\nu_i))$, so

$$m_K(\nu_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert + K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K(\nu_i) = C(\nu_i) + K(C(\nu_i)) \pm O(1) - [C(\nu_i) + K(C(\nu_i)) \pm O(C^{(3)}(\nu_i))] = O(C^{(3)}(\nu_i)) = O(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert).$$

Thus $m_K(\nu_i) = O(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$, but we are not quite done because of the multiplicative constant that might be hidden in the $O$-term. The following method of finishing the proof was suggested by Joe Miller.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.9)</span></p>

$K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) \leqslant K^{(2)}(\nu_i) + K(m_K(\nu_i)) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Assume that $c_i = K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K^{(2)}(\nu_i) - K(m_K(\nu_i))$ is positive, since otherwise we are done. From minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-programs for $K(\nu_i)$, for $m_K(\nu_i)$, and for $c_i$, we can compute $K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert)$, and hence can compute $\lvert\nu_i\rvert = m_K(\nu_i) + K(\nu_i) - K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert)$. Thus,

$$K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) \leqslant K^{(2)}(\nu_i) + K(m_K(\nu_i)) + K(c_i) + O(1) = K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - c_i + K(c_i) + O(1).$$

So $c_i \leqslant K(c_i) + O(1)$, whence $c_i = O(1)$.

To finish the proof of the theorem given this lemma, we have the following inequality.

$$m_K(\nu_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert + K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K(\nu_i) \leqslant K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K^{(2)}(\nu_i) + K^{(3)}(\nu_i) + O(1) \quad \text{by Lemma 4.2.6}$$

$$\leqslant K(m_K(\nu_i)) + K^{(3)}(\nu_i) + O(1) \quad \text{by Lemma 4.3.9}$$

$$\leqslant K(m_K(\nu_i)) + \log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert + o(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert).$$

By the crude bound $m_K(\nu_i) = O(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$ established above, we have $K(m_K(\nu_i)) = o(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$, so $m_K(\nu_i) \leqslant \log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert + o(\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$, whence

$$\limsup_i \frac{m_K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} \leqslant 1.$$

Together with (4.12), this inequality implies that $\lim_i \frac{m_K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1$, completing the proof of the theorem.

The following theorem, whose proof is based on Theorem 4.3.7, has as a corollary that the identities (4.1) and (4.2) cannot be improved, and the inequality (4.5) cannot be reversed.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.10 — Solovay)</span></p>

There are infinite sequences of strings $\nu_i$, $\tau_i$, and $\mu_i$ such that

(i) $\lim_i \lvert\nu_i\rvert = \infty$,

(ii) $C(\tau_i) = C(\nu_i) + O(1)$,

(iii) $\lim_i \frac{K(\tau_i) - K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1$,

(iv) $K(\mu_i) = K(\nu_i) + O(1)$, and

(v) $\lim_i \frac{C(\nu_i) - C(\mu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.3.11 — Solovay)</span></p>

None of the following relations hold in general.

$$K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + C^{(2)}(\sigma) + C^{(3)}(\sigma) \pm O(C^{(4)}(\sigma)).$$

$$C(\sigma) = K(\sigma) - K^{(2)}(\sigma) + K^{(3)}(\sigma) \pm O(K^{(4)}(\sigma)).$$

$$K(\sigma) = C(\sigma) + K(C(\sigma)) \pm O(1).$$

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\nu_i$, $\tau_i$, and $\mu_i$ be as in Theorem 4.3.10.

If the first equation were true then, since $C(\nu_i) = C(\tau_i) \pm O(1)$, we would have $K(\nu_i) - K(\tau_i) = O(C^{(4)}(\nu_i)) = O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$. But then $\lim_i \frac{K(\tau_i) - K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 0$, contradicting Theorem 4.3.10.

The argument for the second equation is the same, with $\mu_i$ in place of $\tau_i$ and $K$ and $C$ interchanged.

Similarly, if the third equation were true then we would have $K(\nu_i) - K(\tau_i) = O(1)$, which would lead to the same contradiction as before.

### 4.4 Muchnik's Theorem on $C$ and $K$

In [288], Muchnik proved the following interesting result on the relationship between prefix-free complexity and plain complexity. The proof we give, which is based on Solovay's results discussed above, is due to Miller.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.4.1 — Muchnik)</span></p>

For each $d$ there are strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$ such that $K(\sigma) > K(\tau) + d$ and $C(\tau) > C(\sigma) + d$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace \nu_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ be the sequence constructed in Theorem 4.3.7. For each $i$, take $\rho_i$ to be a strongly $K$-random string of length $\lvert\nu_i\rvert - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}$. (Here we slightly abuse notation by writing $\frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}$ to mean $\lfloor \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2} \rfloor$.) First note that

$$K(\rho_i) - K(\nu_i) = \lvert\rho_i\rvert + K(\lvert\rho_i\rvert) - K(\nu_i) \pm O(1) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2} + K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}) - K(\nu_i) \pm O(1)$$

$$= \lvert\nu_i\rvert - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2} + K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) - K(\nu_i) \pm O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert) = m_K(\nu_i) - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2} \pm O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert).$$

Therefore,

$$\lim_i \frac{K(\rho_i) - K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = \lim_i \frac{m_K(\nu_i) - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}.$$

By Corollary 4.3.3, $\rho_i$ is $C$-random, so $C(\nu_i) - C(\rho_i) = \lvert\nu_i\rvert - \lvert\rho_i\rvert \pm O(1) = \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2} \pm O(1)$, and hence

$$\lim_i \frac{C(\nu_i) - C(\rho_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = \frac{1}{2}.$$

So for any $d$ we can take $i$ large enough so that $K(\rho_i) - K(\nu_i) \geqslant d$ and $C(\nu_i) - C(\rho_i) \geqslant d$.

Miller observed that the estimates used in both proofs can be improved. It is not hard to show that $K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert - \frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}) = K(\lvert\nu_i\rvert) \pm O(1)$, and from this fact, that $K(\tau_i) = K(\rho_i) \pm O(1)$ and $C(\tau_i) = C(\rho_i) \pm O(1)$. The weaker estimates above were used because they are sufficient for our purposes and require no explanation.

Miller's methods allow an additional improvement to the previous result, namely that we can choose $\sigma$ and $\tau$ to have the same length.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.4.2 — Miller)</span></p>

For each $d$ there are strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$ of the same length such that $K(\sigma) \geqslant K(\tau) + d$ and $C(\tau) \geqslant C(\sigma) + d$.

</div>

*Proof.* We extend the proof above. For each $i$, define $\tau_i = \rho_i 0^{\frac{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}{2}}$. Then $\lvert\tau_i\rvert = \lvert\nu_i\rvert$. Furthermore, $K(\tau_i) = K(\rho_i) \pm O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$ and $C(\tau_i) = C(\rho_i) \pm O(\log^{(3)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert)$. Thus

$$\lim_i \frac{K(\tau_i) - K(\nu_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert} = \frac{1}{2} = \lim_i \frac{C(\nu_i) - C(\tau_i)}{\log^{(2)} \lvert\nu_i\rvert}.$$

As above, for any $d$ we can take $i$ large enough so that $K(\tau_i) - K(\nu_i) \geqslant d$ and $C(\nu_i) - C(\tau_i) \geqslant d$.

### 4.5 Monotone complexity and $KM$-complexity

As mentioned in Section 3.16, it was a long-standing open question whether $KM$ and $Km$ are the same. We saw in that section that $KM(\sigma) \leqslant Km(\sigma) + O(1)$. Levin conjectured that in fact $Km(\sigma) = KM(\sigma) \pm O(1)$. This conjecture was finally refuted by Gács.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.1 — Gács)</span></p>

There exists a function $f$ with $\lim_n f(n) = \infty$ such that $Km(\sigma) - KM(\sigma) \geqslant f(\lvert\sigma\rvert)$ for infinitely many $\sigma$. Indeed, we may choose $f$ to be the inverse of Ackermann's function.

</div>

Although we will not discuss reducibilities arising from measures of complexity until later, the following corollary to Gács' Theorem is worth noting here. A natural question given Gács' Theorem is whether the corresponding reducibilities are different. That is, let $A \leqslant_{KM} B$ if $KM(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant KM(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$, and similarly for $Km$ ($Km$-reducibility is also known as **monotone reducibility**). Then is $\leqslant_{KM}$ different from $\leqslant_{Km}$? Miller observed that this question is easily settled using Gács' Theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.2 — Miller)</span></p>

The reducibilities $\leqslant_{KM}$ and $\leqslant_{Km}$ are different.

</div>

*Proof.* We define $A = \bigcup_s \alpha_s$ in stages, with each $\alpha_s$ a finite string. Let $\alpha_0$ be such that $Km(\alpha_0) - KM(\alpha_0) > 1$. Now suppose we have defined $\alpha_s$. For a string $\tau$ of length at least $\lvert\alpha_s\rvert + 1$, let $\widehat{\tau}$ be obtained by replacing the initial segment of $\tau$ of length $\lvert\alpha_s\rvert$ by $\alpha_s$. Then it is easy to check that $Km(\widehat{\tau}) = Km(\tau) \pm O(1)$ and $KM(\widehat{\tau}) = KM(\tau) \pm O(1)$, where the constants depend only on $\alpha_s$. So we can choose a $\tau$ such that $Km(\widehat{\tau}) - KM(\widehat{\tau}) > s + 1$ and let $\alpha_{s+1} = \widehat{\tau}$.

Returning to Theorem 4.5.1, we see that, while Gács proved that $KM$ and $Km$ do not agree within an additive constant, it seemed possible that the difference might be very slight. As we will discuss in this section, Adam Day established a strong extension of Gács' Theorem with a proof significantly extending and clarifying the original.

How different can these two complexities be? The following theorem gives an upper bound on their difference.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.3 — Gács, Uspensky and Shen)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) \leqslant KM(\sigma) + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + O(1)$. Thus, for any $k \geqslant 1$ and $\varepsilon > 0$,

$$Km(\sigma) \leqslant KM(\sigma) + \log \lvert\sigma\rvert + \log \log \lvert\sigma\rvert + \cdots + (1 + \varepsilon) \log^{(k)} \lvert\sigma\rvert + O(1).$$

</div>

*Proof.* For each $n$, we have $\sum_{\sigma \in 2^n} 2^{-KM(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$, by the definition of $KM$. Thus

$$\sum_\sigma 2^{-KM(\sigma) - K(\lvert\sigma\rvert)} = \sum_n 2^{-K(n)} \sum_{\sigma \in 2^n} 2^{-KM(\sigma)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-K(n)} < 1.$$

So $\lbrace (k+1, \sigma) : \sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \wedge k \geqslant KM(\sigma) + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) \rbrace$ is a KC set.

Given the upper bound provided by Theorem 4.5.3, the following result, which brings Gács' inverse Ackermann function up to two logs, is not far from optimal. (Indeed, since the upper bound in Theorem 4.5.3 holds for $K$ as well as $Km$, it would be surprising if it were tight, as that would imply that, in a sense, a prefix-free machine is just as good at approximating $KM$ as a monotone machine.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.4 — Day)</span></p>

Let $c < \frac{1}{2}$. Then there exist infinitely many $\sigma$ such that $Km(\sigma) > KM(\sigma) + c \log \log \lvert\sigma\rvert$.

</div>

We follow Day's presentation of his complex proof. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a fixed universal monotone machine. We think of this machine as given by axioms of the form $(\sigma, \tau)$, and write $(\sigma, \tau) \in \mathcal{M}$ to mean that on input $\sigma$, the machine $\mathcal{M}$ writes at least $\tau$ on its output tape. We write $\sigma \approx \tau$ to mean that $\sigma \preccurlyeq \tau$ or $\tau \preccurlyeq \sigma$. In this case, we say that $\sigma$ and $\tau$ are **comparable**, and otherwise that they are **incomparable**.

To show that the above theorem holds, we will develop a proof that monotonic descriptional complexity and monotone algorithmic probability do not agree to within an additive constant. The improvement in the lower bound follows from the construction used in the proof. This proof builds on the original work of Gács but is more intricate. A main point of difference is that the algorithm at the heart of the new proof works on sets of strings as opposed to individual strings.

From now on we will refer to a c.e. continuous semimeasure as just a c.e. semimeasure. We will also drop the word monotonic and just talk about descriptional complexity and algorithmic probability. We know that $M_\mathcal{M}$ (as defined in Theorem 3.16.2) majorizes all c.e. semimeasures. So, if it were true that $Km(\sigma) = KM(\sigma) + O(1)$, then for any c.e. semimeasure $m$ that we could construct, there would be some constant $c$ such that $Km(\sigma) \leqslant -\log(m(\sigma)) + c$.

The general approach of the proof will be to build a c.e. semimeasure for which this is false, which means constructing a c.e. semimeasure $m$ with the property that for all $c$ there is a $\sigma$ such that $Km(\sigma) > -\log(m(\sigma)) + c$.

Of course, we want to do more, and prove Theorem 4.5.4. This result will come out of the construction used in the proof. For now we will focus just on showing that $Km$ and $KM$ are different. We will leave the analysis of the size of the difference until later.

Another way to look at this problem is to consider whether for any $n$, we can construct a semimeasure $m$ such that $m(\lambda) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ and there exists a $\sigma$ such that $Km(\sigma) > -\log(m(\sigma))$. Viewing the problem this way helps simplify the proof a little, because we can build a c.e. semimeasure $a(\sigma)$ such that for all $i$,

1. $a(0^i 1) \leqslant 2^{-2i-1}$,
2. $a(0^i) = \sum_j a(0^{i+j} 1)$, and
3. there exists a $\sigma$ such that $Km(0^i 1\sigma) > -\log(a(0^i 1\sigma))$.

Then we can *scale* the semimeasure $a$ to construct a new semimeasure $m$ with the desired properties.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.5)</span></p>

If a semimeasure $a$ with the above properties exists, then Theorem 4.5.1 holds.

</div>

*Proof.* From $a$ we can define a c.e. semimeasure $m$ by letting $m(0^i 1\sigma) = 2^i a(0^i 1\sigma)$ and $m(0^i) = \sum_j m(0^{i+j} 1)$. Then $m(\sigma) \geqslant m(\sigma 0) + m(\sigma 1)$ for all $\sigma$, and

$$m(\lambda) = \sum_i m(0^i 1) = \sum_i 2^i a(0^i 1) \leqslant \sum_i 2^i 2^{-2i-1} \leqslant 1,$$

so $m$ is a c.e. semimeasure. As $M_\mathcal{M}$ majorizes all c.e. semimeasures, there is some $d$ such that $M_\mathcal{M}(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{-d} m(\sigma)$. Thus $KM(\sigma) \leqslant -\log(m(\sigma)) + d$. Now given any $c$, let $i \geqslant c + d$. By our hypothesis on $a$, there is some $\sigma$ such that

$$Km(0^i 1\sigma) > -\log(a(0^i 1\sigma)) = -\log(2^{-i} m(0^i 1\sigma))$$

$$= -\log(m(0^i 1\sigma)) + i \geqslant -\log(m(0^i 1\sigma)) + c + d \geqslant KM(0^i 1\sigma) + c.$$

Since we want $a$ to be c.e., we can construct it in stages depending on some computable enumeration of the universal monotone machine $\mathcal{M}$. We will start with $a(\sigma, 0) = 0$ for all $\sigma$. At each stage $t+1$, we have the option of choosing a single string $\rho$ and a positive integer $x$ such that $a(\lambda, t) + 2^{-x} \leqslant 1$, and defining $a(\sigma, t+1)$ as follows:

$$a(\sigma, t+1) = \begin{cases} a(\sigma, t) + 2^{-x} & \text{if } \sigma \preccurlyeq \rho \\\\ a(\sigma, t) & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

We will write simply $a(\rho, t+1) = a(\rho, t) + 2^{-x}$ to refer to this process. If we select $\rho$ at stage $t+1$ and $a(\rho, t) = 0$, we will simplify the notation further and write $a(\rho, t+1) = 2^{-x}$. If no such $\rho$ is chosen for a stage $t+1$ then this simply means that $a(\sigma, t+1) = a(\sigma, t)$ for all $\sigma$.

Our objective is to construct a c.e. semimeasure $a$ that meets the following requirements for all $i$:

$$R_i : a(0^i 1) \leqslant 2^{-2i-1} \text{ and there is a } \sigma \text{ s.t. } Km(0^i 1\sigma) > -\log(a(0^i 1\sigma))$$

For most of this proof we will focus on meeting a single requirement $R_i$ for some arbitrary $i$. Once we can achieve a single requirement, it will be relatively easy to meet all requirements.

A natural way to view this construction is as a game between us constructing the semimeasure and an opponent constructing the universal monotone machine. For example, to meet the requirement that there is some $1\sigma$ such that $Km(1\sigma) > -\log(a(1\sigma))$, we could choose a string $\sigma$ and set $a(1\sigma, 1) = 2^{-r}$. Then we could wait and see whether a pair $(\tau, 1\sigma')$ is enumerated into $\mathcal{M}$ where $\lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant r$ and $\sigma \preccurlyeq \sigma'$. If such a pair is not enumerated into $\mathcal{M}$ then the requirement is met because $Km(1\sigma) > r = -\log(a(1\sigma))$. If such a pair is enumerated into $\mathcal{M}$ then we can make another change to $a$ at some future stage.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.6)</span></p>

We choose a string $\sigma$ and set $a(\sigma, 1) = 2^{-4}$. Now the opponent must respond by ensuring that $Km(\sigma) \leqslant 4$. To do so, the opponent must enumerate some pair $(\tau, \sigma')$ into $\mathcal{M}$, where $\lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant 4$ and $\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma$. Let us suppose that the opponent enumerates $(0000, \sigma)$ into $\mathcal{M}$. Then either it is possible for the opponent to add $(000, \sigma)$ into $\mathcal{M}$ or not. If not, we can set $a(\sigma, 2) = a(\sigma, 1) + 2^{-4} = 2^{-3}$. Now, as it is not possible for the opponent to add $(000, \sigma)$ to $\mathcal{M}$, the opponent must find a new string, say $001$, and add $(001, \sigma)$ to $\mathcal{M}$. However, in this case, the opponent has used both $0000$ and $001$ to describe $\sigma$, a waste of resources. Alternatively, assume that the opponent can enumerate $(000, \sigma)$ into $\mathcal{M}$. We can think of the opponent as holding $000$ as a description in reserve for $\sigma$. Now we make this reservation useless, by

1. never again adding measure to a string comparable with $\sigma$, and
2. using only units of measure of size at least $2^{-3}$ from now on.

As we are using increments of measure of size $2^{-3}$ or more, the opponent must always respond with strings of length at most $3$. However, the opponent cannot use $000$ because $000$ can be used only to describe a string comparable with $\sigma$. Hence, if we never increase the measure on a string comparable with $\sigma$, the opponent can never use $000$. In this case, $0001$ is a gap in the domain of $\mathcal{M}$, as it cannot be used to respond to any of our increments in measure, which again is a waste of the opponent's resources.

</div>

A proof of Theorem 4.5.1 can be developed by generalizing the approach of this example. A first step toward this generalization is the following. We take a string $\sigma$. We have two integer parameters $r, g$ with $0 < r < g$. We want to increase the measure on $\sigma$ in increments of $2^{-g}$. Each time we increase the measure we want the opponent to respond. To do so, we do not increase the measure on $\sigma$ directly but on some extension of $\sigma$. Let $\Xi = \lbrace \sigma\tau : \tau \in 2^{g-r} \rbrace$. We will take some $\xi \in \Xi$ and increase the measure on $\xi$ instead of on $\sigma$. A first attempt at the algorithm we need is as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Algorithm</span><span class="math-callout__name">(1)</span></p>

Let $t$ be the current stage in the enumeration of $\mathcal{M}$. Choose some $\xi \in \Xi$ that has not been used before (so that $a(\xi, t) = 0$). If there is some description of a string $\sigma'$ with $\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma$ in the domain of $\mathcal{M}_t$ that can be shortened to a description of length $r$, then terminate the algorithm. Otherwise, increase the measure on $\xi$ by $2^{-g}$, and wait until a stage $t'$ at which the opponent describes $\xi$ with a string of length at most $g$. If we have not used all the elements of $\Xi$, then let $t = t'$ and repeat. If we have used all the elements of $\Xi$, then the measure on $\sigma$ must be $\lvert\Xi\rvert 2^{-g} = 2^{-r}$, so wait until the opponent uses a string of length $r$ to describe $\sigma$.

</div>

If we apply this algorithm, then the opponent must either use a string of length $r$ to describe $\sigma$, or have some string that describes a string comparable with $\sigma$ that can be shortened to a string of length $r$. The crucial fact is that, at some point, we increased the measure on $\xi$ by just $2^{-g}$, and the opponent had to respond by finding a *new* string of length $r$. The string is new in the sense that it is not just some initial segment of a string already used.

As it stands, this algorithm can make only a small gap between $Km$ and $-\log(a)$. The hard part is amplifying this gap. Amplification can be done by using the algorithm recursively. In the algorithm above, we act by setting $a(\xi, t) = 2^{-g}$ for some $\xi$ at some stage $t$. However, rather than just directly increasing the measure on $\xi$, we could instead run the algorithm again on some *extension* of $\xi$. This action would have the effect of increasing the measure on $\xi$ while forcing the opponent to waste even more resources. However, to implement this strategy, a number of issues need to be dealt with:

1. We want to make sure our algorithm increases the measure enough. Algorithm 1 will increase the measure on $\sigma$ somewhere between $2^{-g}$ and $2^{-r}$, which is too great a range.
2. We need to ensure that any gaps the algorithm makes in the domain of $\mathcal{M}$ are never used again. The algorithm above does not address this.
3. Each time we use an extension of a string, we go deeper into the tree of binary strings. We would like to minimize the depth that we go to in order to get the best possible lower bound on the difference between $Km$ and $KM$.

#### Key Definitions for the Proof

At any stage $t$, the approximation $\mathcal{M}_t$ tells us the options the opponent has left. In particular, $\mathcal{M}_t$ tells us what the opponent can and cannot use certain strings for. We need a more exact notion for the idea of the opponent shortening an existing description. In his original proof, Gács came up with the notion of a string $\tau$ being *reserved* as a description for $\sigma$. The idea is not just that the opponent can use $\tau$ to describe $\sigma$, but additionally that the opponent cannot use $\tau$ to describe a string incomparable with $\sigma$. For $\tau$ to be reserved for $\sigma$, the opponent must have already used a string comparable with $\tau$ to describe an extension of $\sigma$.

There are really two ideas in this definition that are worth separating out and making more explicit. First we will say that a string $\tau$ is **fragmented** by $\sigma$ if some string comparable to $\tau$ describes an extension of $\sigma$, which means the opponent cannot use $\tau$ to describe a string incomparable with $\sigma$ (as otherwise $\mathcal{M}$ would not be a monotone machine). Second, a string $\tau$ is **$\mathcal{M}$-incompatible** with $\sigma$ if some string comparable with $\tau$ describes a string incomparable with $\sigma$. This means that $\tau$ cannot be used to describe $\sigma$. Hence another way of saying a string $\tau$ is reserved for $\sigma$ is that $\tau$ is fragmented by $\sigma$ but $\tau$ is not $\mathcal{M}$-incompatible with $\sigma$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.7)</span></p>

(i) The strings of length $r$ **fragmented** by $\sigma$ at stage $t$ are those in the set

$$F_r(\sigma, t) = \lbrace \tau \in 2^r : \exists(\tau', \sigma') \in \mathcal{M}_t\ (\tau' \approx \tau \wedge \sigma \preccurlyeq \sigma') \rbrace.$$

(ii) The strings of length $r$ **$\mathcal{M}$-incompatible** with $\sigma$ at stage $t$ are those in the set

$$I_r(\sigma, t) = \lbrace \tau \in 2^r : \exists(\tau', \sigma') \in \mathcal{M}_t\ (\tau' \approx \tau \wedge \sigma \mid \sigma') \rbrace.$$

(iii) The strings of length $r$ **reserved** for $\sigma$ at stage $t$ are those in the set

$$R_r(\sigma, t) = F_r(\sigma, t) \setminus I_r(\sigma, t).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.8)</span></p>

Suppose that

$$\mathcal{M}_t = \lbrace (00, 11), (000, 1), (000, 11), (001, 11), (100, 01), (110, 011), (111, 1) \rbrace.$$

If we consider the strings of length $2$, working through the above definitions establishes that $F_2(01, t) = \lbrace 01, 10, 11 \rbrace$, that $I_2(01, t) = \lbrace 00, 11 \rbrace$, and that $R_2(01, t) = \lbrace 01, 10 \rbrace$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.9)</span></p>

If $\Sigma$ is a set of finite strings, then

(i) $F_r(\Sigma, t) = \bigcup_{\sigma \in \Sigma} F_r(\sigma, t)$ and

(ii) $I_r(\Sigma, t) = \bigcap_{\sigma \in \Sigma} I_r(\sigma, t)$.

</div>

We will explain later why we take the intersection in the definition of $I_r(\Sigma, t)$. The following lemmas show how the sets $F_r(\sigma, t)$, $I_r(\sigma, t)$, $F_r(\Sigma, t)$, and $I_r(\Sigma, t)$ change as the parameters that define them vary.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.10)</span></p>

(i) If $r_0 \leqslant r_1$ then $[\![F_{r_0}(\sigma, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![F_{r_1}(\sigma, t)]\!]$ and $[\![I_{r_0}(\sigma, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![I_{r_1}(\sigma, t)]\!]$.

(ii) If $\sigma_0 \preccurlyeq \sigma_1$ then $[\![F_r(\sigma_0, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![F_r(\sigma_1, t)]\!]$ and $[\![I_r(\sigma_0, t)]\!] \subseteq [\![I_r(\sigma_1, t)]\!]$.

(iii) If $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ then $[\![F_r(\sigma, t_0)]\!] \subseteq [\![F_r(\sigma, t_1)]\!]$ and $[\![I_r(\sigma, t_0)]\!] \subseteq [\![I_r(\sigma, t_1)]\!]$.

</div>

Here $[\![S]\!]$ denotes the set of infinite extensions of elements of $S$. In summary, $[\![F_r(\sigma, t)]\!]$ and $[\![I_r(\sigma, t)]\!]$ are both increasing in $t$ and decreasing in $r$. In the length of $\sigma$, we have that $[\![F_r(\sigma, t)]\!]$ is decreasing and $[\![I_r(\sigma, t)]\!]$ is increasing.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.11)</span></p>

If $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1 \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, then $\Sigma_0 \preccurlyeq \Sigma_1$ if for all $\sigma_1 \in \Sigma_1$, there exists a $\sigma_0 \in \Sigma_0$ such that $\sigma_0 \preccurlyeq \sigma_1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.12)</span></p>

(i) If $r_0 \leqslant r_1$ then $[\![F_{r_0}(\Sigma, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![F_{r_1}(\Sigma, t)]\!]$ and $[\![I_{r_0}(\Sigma, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![I_{r_1}(\Sigma, t)]\!]$.

(ii) If $\Sigma_0 \preccurlyeq \Sigma_1$ then $[\![F_r(\Sigma_0, t)]\!] \supseteq [\![F_r(\Sigma_1, t)]\!]$ and $[\![I_r(\Sigma_0, t)]\!] \subseteq [\![I_r(\Sigma_1, t)]\!]$.

(iii) If $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ then $[\![F_r(\Sigma, t_0)]\!] \subseteq [\![F_r(\Sigma, t_1)]\!]$ and $[\![I_r(\Sigma, t_0)]\!] \subseteq [\![I_r(\Sigma, t_1)]\!]$.

</div>

#### The Strategy and Gain

We are going to describe an algorithm that can be used to prove Theorems 4.5.1 and 4.5.4. The algorithm will be allocated a certain amount of measure to spend on the construction of the c.e. semimeasure $a$. It will also be given a set of strings $\Sigma$, and it will be able to increase the measure only on extensions of these strings. Ultimately we want the algorithm to establish some difference between $Km(\rho)$ and $-\log(a(\rho))$ for some string $\rho$ that extends some element of $\Sigma$. We will argue that if this does not happen, then some contradiction must ensue. The basic idea is to make the opponent run out of short descriptions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.13)</span></p>

If $\Sigma \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, and $r, r', t_0, t_1 \in \mathbb{N}$ are such that $r \leqslant r'$ and $t_0 \leqslant t_1$, then

$$G^{r'}_r(\Sigma; t_0, t_1) = [\![F_r(\Sigma, t_1)]\!] \setminus [\![I_{r'}(\Sigma, t_0)]\!].$$

</div>

The value $G^{r'}_r(\Sigma; t_0, t_1)$ can be thought of as the gain the algorithm obtains using the strings in $\Sigma$ between stages $t_0$ and $t_1$, and using units of measure between $r$ and $r'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.14 — $(r, b, \Sigma)$-strategy)</span></p>

If $r, b \in \mathbb{N}$, and $\Sigma$ is a finite prefix-free subset of $2^{<\omega}$, then an $(r, b, \Sigma)$**-strategy** is an algorithm that, if implemented at stage $t_0$, ensures that one of the following holds.

(i) (a) There is a $\sigma \in \Sigma$ such that for some $\sigma'$ extending $\sigma$, we have $Km(\sigma') > -\log(a(\sigma'))$, and (b) for all stages $t$ and all $\sigma \in \Sigma$, we have $a(\sigma, t) < \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r}$; or

(ii) at some stage $t_1$, for some $r' \geqslant r$ computable from $(r, b)$,

- (a) for all $\sigma \in \Sigma$, $2^{-r} \leqslant a(\sigma, t_1) < \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r}$,
- (b) $\mu(G^{r'}_r(\Sigma; t_0, t_1)) \geqslant \left(1 + \frac{b}{2}\right) a(\Sigma, t_1)$, and
- (c) for all $\widehat{\Sigma} \subseteq \Sigma$, we have $\mu(G^{r'}_r(\widehat{\Sigma}; t_0, t_1)) \geqslant \left(1 + \frac{b}{2}\right) a(\Sigma, t_1) - 2^{-r} D(b) \lvert\Sigma \setminus \widehat{\Sigma}\rvert$,

where $D(b) = 5 \left(\frac{5}{4}\right)^b - 4$ and $a(\Sigma, t) = \sum_{\sigma \in \Sigma} a(\sigma, t)$.

</div>

The input parameters for the strategy are $r$, $b$, and $\Sigma$. The set $\Sigma$ is a finite prefix-free set of binary strings. The $r$ parameter determines the measure that the algorithm should spend on every string in $\Sigma$. The $b$ parameter determines the amount of gain that the algorithm should make.

The definition gives two possible outcomes to a strategy. Outcome (i) is the preferred outcome. If outcome (i) occurs, then we have established a difference between $Km$ and $KM$. However, if we do not achieve this outcome, then outcome (ii) is at least a step in the right direction. Provided $b > 0$, outcome (ii) ensures that some difference between $\mu(G^{r'}_r(\Sigma; t_0, t_1))$ and $a(\Sigma, t_1)$ is created. Condition (c) ensures that no individual string in $\Sigma$ contributes too much to the overall gain made. The function $D(b)$ is chosen because it has the properties that $D(0) = 1$ and $D(b+1) = \frac{5}{4} D(b) + 1$.

#### The Base Strategy

The idea behind the basic $(r, 0, \Sigma)$-strategy is simple. For every $\sigma \in \Sigma$ we set $a(\sigma) = 2^{-r}$. The opponent is then forced to respond by setting $Km(\sigma) \leqslant r$. To do this, the opponent must find some string $\tau$ of length at most $r$ and enumerate $(\tau, \sigma)$ into $\mathcal{M}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.15)</span></p>

Let $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $\Sigma$ be a finite prefix-free subset of $2^{<\omega}$ such that $\lvert\Sigma\rvert \leqslant 2^r$. If $t_0$ is the current stage in the construction of $a$ and $a(\Sigma, t_0) = 0$, then we can implement an $(r, 0, \Sigma)$-strategy with $r' = r$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\Sigma = \lbrace \sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_n \rbrace$. The desired strategy can be implemented as follows. First, let $a(\sigma_i, t_0 + i) = 2^{-r}$ for all $i$ such that $1 \leqslant i \leqslant n$. (We know that $a(\sigma_i, t_0 + i - 1) = 0$ because $\Sigma$ is prefix-free.) This definition ensures that $a(\Sigma, t_0 + n) = \lvert\Sigma\rvert 2^{-r}$. The second step is to wait until a stage $t_1$, when the opponent responds by setting $Km_{t_1}(\sigma) \leqslant r$ for all $\sigma \in \Sigma$. If this response never happens, then for some $\sigma \in \Sigma$, we have $Km(\sigma) > r = -\log(2^{-r}) = -\log(a(\sigma))$, so outcome (i) occurs.

If at some stage $t_1$, we have $Km_{t_1}(\sigma) \leqslant r$ for all $\sigma \in \Sigma$, then for each $i$ such that $1 \leqslant i \leqslant n$, the opponent must have enumerated some $(\tau_i, \sigma'_i)$ into $\mathcal{M}_{t_1}$, where $s_i = \lvert\tau_i\rvert \leqslant r$ and $\sigma'_i \succcurlyeq \sigma_i$. In this case we will show that outcome (ii) occurs.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.16)</span></p>

If $\sigma_0 \mid \sigma_1$ then $F_p(\sigma_0, t) \subseteq I_p(\sigma_1, t)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.17)</span></p>

If $r_0 \leqslant r_1 \leqslant \cdots \leqslant r_n$ and $t_0 \leqslant t_1 \leqslant \cdots \leqslant t_n$ are sequences of natural numbers, and $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \ldots, \Sigma_{n-1}$ are pairwise disjoint prefix-free subsets of $2^{<\omega}$ such that $\Sigma = \Sigma_0 \cup \Sigma_1 \cup \cdots \cup \Sigma_{n-1}$ is also prefix-free, then

$$\mu(G^{r_n}_{r_0}(\Sigma; t_0, t_n)) \geqslant \sum_{i < n} \mu(G^{r_{n-1-i}}_{r_{n-i}}(\Sigma_i; t_i, t_{i+1})).$$

</div>

#### Building $(r, b+1, \Sigma)$-strategies

We have seen that we can use strategies sequentially without losing any of their individual gains. However, we need to do better: we need to be able to combine strategies in such a way as to *increase* our gain. To do so, we will make use of reservations.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.18)</span></p>

Consider the following implementation of three strategies in sequence and assume that they all have outcome (ii). Take some $r_0 \in \mathbb{N}$. Fix a $b \in \mathbb{N}$ and assume we have an $(r, b, \Sigma)$ strategy for any appropriate $r$ and $\Sigma$. Let $r_1$ be the $r'$ computable from $(r_0, b)$, as in Definition 4.5.14. Similarly, let $r_2$ be the $r'$ computable from $(r_1, b)$, and let $r_3$ be the $r'$ computable from $(r_2, b)$. Take any string $\sigma$ and let $\Sigma_2 = \lbrace \sigma 00 \rbrace$, let $\Sigma_1 = \lbrace \sigma 01\tau : \tau \in 2^{r_1 - r_0} \rbrace$, let $\Sigma_0 = \lbrace \sigma 1\tau : \tau \in 2^{r_2 - r_0} \rbrace$, and let $\Sigma = \Sigma_0 \cup \Sigma_1 \cup \Sigma_2$.

Starting at stage $t_0$, we first implement an $(r_2, b, \Sigma_0)$-strategy. When this strategy finishes at stage $t_1$, we implement an $(r_1, b, \Sigma_1)$-strategy. Finally, when this strategy finishes at stage $t_2$, we implement an $(r_0, b, \Sigma_2)$-strategy. Let $t_3$ be the stage at which this final strategy finishes. By Proposition 4.5.17,

$$\mu(G^{r_3}_{r_0}(\Sigma; t_0, t_3)) \geqslant \sum_{i \leqslant 2} \mu(G^{r_{3-i}}_{r_{3-1-i}}(\Sigma_i; t_i, t_{i+1})) \geqslant \sum_{i \leqslant 2} \left(1 + \frac{b}{2}\right) a(\Sigma_i, t_{i+1}) = \left(1 + \frac{b}{2}\right) a(\Sigma, t_3).$$

Now $a(\sigma) \geqslant a(\Sigma) \geqslant \lvert\Sigma_0\rvert 2^{-r_2} + \lvert\Sigma_1\rvert 2^{-r_1} + \lvert\Sigma_2\rvert 2^{-r_0} = 3 \cdot 2^{-r_0}$. This approach has allowed us to use strategies to increase the measure on $\sigma$ in three increments of $2^{-r_0}$ without losing any of the gain made by the three strategies individually.

</div>

The main algorithm improves Algorithm 1 by splitting into two phases. In the first, the **advantage phase**, the objective will be to force the opponent to reserve a number of strings of length $p$. In the second, the **spend phase**, we make sure that we have placed enough measure on each string so that the strategies can be used recursively. The use of these two phases is another new feature of this proof.

#### The Main Algorithm

We are given $r, b \in \mathbb{N}$ and a prefix-free set of strings $\Sigma$ such that $\lvert\Sigma\rvert \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r} \leqslant 1$. We let $t_0$ be the stage that the algorithm starts and we require that $a(\Sigma, t_0) = 0$. To implement an $(r, b+1, \Sigma)$-strategy, we first determine all the values $n, p, q, e, r_i, r^*_j, r'$, as shown in Figure 4.2. In the algorithm, $\Upsilon'$ represents those elements of $\Upsilon$ whose measure we will increase in the current pass through the advantage phase of the algorithm.

**Advantage Phase.** Let $t$ be the current stage. Let $\Upsilon = \lbrace \sigma 0\tau : \sigma \in \Sigma \wedge \tau \in 2^{p-r} \rbrace$. Let $\Xi = \lbrace v\rho : v \in \Upsilon \wedge \rho \in 2^e \rbrace$. Let $\Upsilon' = \lbrace v \in \Upsilon : R_p(v, t) = \emptyset \rbrace$. If $\lvert\Upsilon'\rvert < \frac{1}{4}\lvert\Upsilon\rvert$, then move to the spend phase. For each $v \in \Upsilon'$, choose a $\xi_v \in \Xi$ such that $\xi_v \succcurlyeq v$ and $a(\xi_v, t) = 0$. Let $\Xi' = \lbrace \xi_v : v \in \Upsilon' \rbrace$. Choose the least $i < n-1$ that has not been used previously. Let $\Psi_i = \lbrace \xi\tau : \xi \in \Xi' \wedge \tau \in 2^{r^*_{n-i-1} - q} \rbrace$ and implement an $(r^*_{n-i-1}, b, \Psi_i)$-strategy. If this strategy finishes at stage $t'$, then wait until stage $t''$ such that $Km_{t''}(v) \leqslant p$ for all $v \in \Upsilon$ such that $a(v, t') \geqslant 2^{-p}$. Repeat the advantage phase.

**Spend Phase.** Let $t$ be the current stage. Let $\Sigma' = \lbrace \sigma \in \Sigma : a(\sigma, t) < 2^{-r} \rbrace$. If $\Sigma' = \emptyset$ then terminate the algorithm. Otherwise choose the least $i < 8$ such that $i$ has not been used previously in the spend phase. Let $\Phi_i = \lbrace \sigma 10^1 1\tau : \sigma \in \Sigma' \wedge \tau \in 2^{r_{7-i} - r - 3} \rbrace$ and run a $(r_{7-i}, b, \Phi_i)$-strategy. Repeat the spend phase.

#### Verification

We now verify the correctness of this algorithm. The algorithm starts at stage $t_0$. We will define the following parameters assuming that the algorithm terminates at some stage $t_1$. Let $n_a$ be the number of times the advantage phase is run. Let $n_s$ be the number of times the spend phase is run.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.19)</span></p>

If $t$, $p$, and $\sigma$ are such that $Km_{t}(\sigma) \leqslant p$, then $R_p(\sigma, t) \neq \emptyset$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $Km_{t}(\sigma) \leqslant p$, then there exists $(\tau, \sigma') \in \mathcal{M}_t$ such that $s = \lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant p$ and $\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma$. Consider $\tau' = \tau 0^{p-s}$. As $\lvert\tau'\rvert = p$ and $\tau \preccurlyeq \tau'$, and $\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma$, we have $\tau' \in F_p(\sigma, t)$. If $\tau' \in I_p(\sigma, t)$ then there would exist $\tau'' \approx \tau'$ and $\rho \mid \sigma$ such that $(\tau'', \rho) \in \mathcal{M}_t$. However, then $\tau'' \approx \tau$ and $\rho \mid \sigma'$, so this fact would contradict the definition of a monotone machine. Thus $\tau' \notin I_p(\sigma, t)$, and so $\tau' \in R_p(\sigma, t)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.20)</span></p>

The advantage phase of the algorithm never runs out of choices for $\xi_v$ or $i$, and the spend phase never runs out of choices for $i$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.21)</span></p>

For all $\sigma \in \Sigma$, if the algorithm terminates at stage $t_1$, then $2^{-r} \leqslant a(\sigma, t_1) < \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.22)</span></p>

If the algorithm does not terminate then outcome (i) is achieved.

</div>

*Proof.* The number of times any phase is repeated is bounded, so if the algorithm does not terminate, then this fact must be caused by waiting for a response from the opponent or by waiting for another strategy to finish. Using the basic strategy as an inductive base case, this situation can occur only if for some $\sigma \in \Sigma$, there is a $\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma$ with $Km(\sigma') > -\log(a(\sigma'))$. Hence (i)(a) holds. Now (i)(b) holds by Proposition 4.5.21, because if the algorithm does not exceed the measure upper bound when it terminates, then it will not exceed the upper bound if it takes no action from some point onwards.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.23)</span></p>

If the algorithm terminates then

$$\mu(G^{r'}_r(\widehat{\Sigma}\lbrace 1 \rbrace; t_{\mathrm{mid}}, t_1)) \geqslant (1 + \tfrac{b}{2}) a(\Sigma\lbrace 1 \rbrace, t_1) - a(\Sigma\lbrace 1 \rbrace \setminus \widehat{\Sigma}\lbrace 1 \rbrace, t_1) D(b).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.27)</span></p>

If the algorithm terminates and $\widehat{\Sigma} \subseteq \Sigma$, then

$$\mu(G^{r'}_p(\widehat{\Sigma}\lbrace 0 \rbrace; t_0, t_{\mathrm{mid}})) \geqslant \tfrac{5}{8} \lvert\Sigma\rvert 2^{-r} - \lvert\Sigma \setminus \widehat{\Sigma}\rvert 2^{-r} + (1 + \tfrac{b}{2}) a(\Sigma\lbrace 0 \rbrace, t_1) - a(\Sigma\lbrace 0 \rbrace \setminus \widehat{\Sigma}\lbrace 0 \rbrace, t_{\mathrm{mid}}) D(b).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.29)</span></p>

If the algorithm terminates then

$$\mu(G^{r'}_r(\widehat{\Sigma}; t_0, t_1)) \geqslant (1 + \tfrac{b+1}{2}) a(\Sigma, t_1) - 2^{-r} D(b+1) \lvert\Sigma \setminus \widehat{\Sigma}\rvert.$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.30)</span></p>

If the algorithm terminates then outcome (ii) occurs.

</div>

*Proof.* By Proposition 4.5.21, condition (ii)(a) holds. By Proposition 4.5.29, condition (ii)(c) holds. Thus condition (ii)(b) holds by taking $\widehat{\Sigma}$ to be $\Sigma$.

Hence we have established the existence of an $(r, b+1, \Sigma)$-strategy. By induction we can assert the existence of an $(r, b+1, \Sigma)$-strategy for any $r, b \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $\lvert\Sigma\rvert \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r} < 1$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
