  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.31)</span></p>

If $r \geqslant 1$ and $b 2^{-r-1} \geqslant 1$, then for any $\sigma$ such that $a(\sigma, t_0) = 0$, an $(r, b, \lbrace \sigma \rbrace)$-strategy achieves outcome (i).

</div>

*Proof.* Because $\lvert\lbrace \sigma \rbrace\rvert \frac{5}{4} 2^{-r} < 1$, we have sufficient measure to implement such a strategy. However, if the algorithm terminates at some stage $t_1$, then

$$\mu(G^{r'}_r(\lbrace \sigma \rbrace; t_0, t_1)) \geqslant (1 + \tfrac{b}{2}) 2^{-r} = 2^{-r} + b 2^{-r-1} > 1,$$

which is impossible. Thus outcome (i) must occur.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(4.5.32)</span></p>

There is a c.e. semimeasure $a$ such that for all $i$, we have $a(0^i 1) \leqslant 2^{-2i-1}$ and there exists a $\sigma_i$ such that $Km(0^i 1\sigma_i) > -\log(a(0^i 1\sigma_i))$.

</div>

*Proof.* The semimeasure $a$ can be constructed by running a $(2i + 2, 2^{2i+3}, \lbrace 0^i 1 \rbrace)$-strategy for each $i$. Then for all $i$, we have $a(0^i 1) < \frac{5}{4} 2^{-2i-2} < 2^{-2i-1}$. Also, by Proposition 4.5.31, because $2^{2i+3} 2^{-2i-2-1} = 1$, the strategy achieves outcome (i), and hence there is some $\sigma_i$ such that $Km(0^i 1\sigma_i) > -\log(a(0^i 1\sigma_i))$.

Now Theorem 4.5.1 holds by Propositions 4.5.5 and 4.5.32.

#### The Lower Bound on $Km - KM$

To determine a lower bound on the difference between $Km$ and $KM$, we need to bound the maximum string length used by an $(r, b, \Sigma)$-strategy. We will do so by determining an upper bound for the number of bits appended to a string in $\Sigma$ by the strategy. If $b = 0$, then the strategy does not use any extensions of strings in $\Sigma$, so the number of extra bits appended is $0$. If $b > 0$, an upper bound on the number of bits needed to extend a string in $\Sigma$ to a string in $\Psi_0$ is $1 + r^*_{n-1} - r = 1 + S(b)$ many bits (the extra $1$ corresponds to the use of a $0$ or $1$ to distinguish between the advantage and spend phases). The function $S(b)$ can be defined inductively as follows: $S(0) = 0$ and $S(b+1) = (8 + 2^{b+8})S(b) + b + 9$.

Now an upper bound on the maximum string length used can be obtained by taking the maximum length $l$ of any string in $\Sigma$ and adding the upper bound for the number of bits added by an $(r, i, \Sigma)$ strategy for all $i$ with $1 \leqslant i \leqslant b$. Hence, we have that the maximum possible string length is

$$l + \sum_{i=1}^{b} (1 + S(i) - S(i-1)) = l + b + S(b) - S(0) = l + b + S(b).$$

We will show that for $b$ sufficiently large, $S(b) < 2^{(b^2)}$.

*Proof of Theorem 4.5.4.* We need to generalize Proposition 4.5.5 a little. Fix $n$. We have $\sum_i 2^{-\frac{i}{n}} < \infty$, so let $k_n$ be such that $2^{k_n}$ is larger than this sum. Now we can construct a semimeasure $a$ such that $a(0^i 1) \leqslant 2^{-(1+\frac{1}{n})i - k_n}$ for all $i$. We construct $a$ by running a $(j(n+1) + k_n + 1, 2^{j(n+1)+k_n+2}, \lbrace 0^{jn} 1 \rbrace)$-strategy for each $j$. We do not exceed the bound on the measure for $a$ because

$$m(\lambda) = \sum_i m(0^i 1) = \sum_i 2^i a(0^i 1) \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-\frac{i}{n} - k_n} = 2^{-k_n} \sum_i 2^{-\frac{i}{n}} \leqslant 1.$$

Because $b 2^{-r-1} = 2^{j(n+1)+k_n+2} 2^{-j(n+1)-k_n-1-1} = 1$, each strategy achieves outcome (i). Thus, for each $j$ there is a $\sigma_j \succcurlyeq 0^{jn} 1$ such that $Km(\sigma_j) > -\log a(\sigma_j)$. We can apply the same scaling as before to $a$ to construct a semimeasure $m$; i.e., for all $\sigma$, we let $m(0^i \sigma) = 2^i a(0^i \sigma)$. Again, we have

$$m(\lambda) = \sum_i m(0^i 1) = \sum_i 2^i a(0^i 1) \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-\frac{i}{n} - k_n} \leqslant 1.$$

We now have that for each $j$ there is a $\sigma_j \succcurlyeq 0^{jn} 1$ such that $Km(\sigma_j) > -\log m(\sigma_j) + jn$. By the discussion immediately preceding this proof, the maximum length of a string used by the strategy corresponding to $j$ is at most $\lvert 0^{jn} 1\rvert + S(b) + b$, where in this case $b = 2^{j(n+1)+k_n+2}$, and if $j$ is large enough then this number is less than $2^{2^{j(n+2)}}$.

Hence, for infinitely many $j$, we can take $\sigma_j$ so that $\lvert\sigma_j\rvert < 2^{2^{j(n+2)}}$, that is, $\log \log \lvert\sigma_j\rvert < 2j(n+2)$. As $M_\mathcal{M}$ majorizes all c.e. continuous semimeasures, there must be some $d_n$ such that $Km(\sigma_j) - KM(\sigma_j) > jn - d_n$. Thus we have

$$Km(\sigma_j) - KM(\sigma_j) > jn \frac{2j(n+2)}{2j(n+2)} - d_n > \frac{n}{2(n+2)} \log \log \lvert\sigma_j\rvert - d_n.$$

For any $c < \frac{1}{2}$, there is an $n$ such that $\frac{n}{2(n+2)} > c + \varepsilon$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$. Now, for sufficiently large $j$ it must be the case that $\varepsilon \log \log \lvert\sigma_j\rvert > d_n$. Hence, for such $j$,

$$Km(\sigma_j) - KM(\sigma_j) > (c + \varepsilon) \log \log \lvert\sigma_j\rvert - d_n > c \log \log \lvert\sigma_j\rvert.$$

## Chapter 5: Effective Reals

In this chapter we study the left computably enumerable reals, and discuss some other classes of effectively approximable reals. Left computably enumerable reals occupy a similar place in the study of relative randomness to that of computably enumerable sets in the study of relative computational complexity. Most of the material in this chapter used in the rest of the book is contained in the first two sections.

Recall that all the reals we consider are in $[0, 1]$, and are identified with elements of $2^\omega$ and with subsets of $\mathbb{N}$. Recall also our convention that all reals will be irrational and hence have unique binary expansions.

### 5.1 Computable and Left-c.e. Reals

Let $\alpha$ be a real. By $L(\alpha)$ we mean the *left cut* of $\alpha$, that is, $\lbrace q \in \mathbb{Q} : q < \alpha \rbrace$. It is well known that such cuts can be used to define the reals from the rationals, as can Cauchy sequences. As we will see, both of these approaches can be effectivized.

Recall that we identify a real $\alpha$ with the set of natural numbers $A$ such that $\alpha = 0.A$, that is, such that the $n$th bit of the binary expansion of $\alpha$ is 1 iff $n \in A$. Thus, it is natural to define $\alpha$ to be a *computable real* if $A$ is a computable set. Equivalently, $\alpha$ is computable if there is an algorithm that, on input $n$, returns the $n$th bit of $\alpha$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.1)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is computable iff $L(\alpha)$ is computable.

*Proof.* If $L(\alpha)$ is computable then we can compute $\alpha \upharpoonright n$, since it is the lexicographically largest string $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $0.\sigma \in L(\alpha)$. (By our convention that $\alpha$ is irrational, $\alpha \neq 0.\sigma$.)

If $\alpha$ is computable, then the following is an algorithm for computing $L(\alpha)$. Let $q_n = 0.(A \upharpoonright n + 1)$ (i.e., $q_n$ is the rational whose binary expansion is given by the first $n + 1$ bits of $A$). Given $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, wait until either $q \leqslant q_n$ for some $n$, in which case $q \in L(\alpha)$, or $q - q_n \geqslant 2^{-n}$ for some $n$, in which case $q \notin L(\alpha)$, since $\alpha - q_n < 2^{-n}$. It is clear that one of the two cases must occur.

</div>

Relativizing this result, we see that a real $\alpha$ has the same degree as $L(\alpha)$. Soare observed that (by the above proof) $\alpha \leqslant_{tt} L(\alpha)$ for all $\alpha$, but he also showed that there is a c.e. set $A$ such that for $\alpha = 0.A$, we have $L(\alpha) \nleqslant_{tt} \alpha$.

Turning to the Cauchy sequence approach, a natural effectivization is to consider those reals $\alpha$ that are limits of *computable* sequences of rationals. As we will see, though, this class is much larger than that of the computable reals, even if we insist that the sequences of rationals be monotonic. The reason is that, to fully effectivize the notion of Cauchy sequence, we should require not only that the sequence be computable, but also that it have a computable *rate of convergence*. The following result is implicit in Turing.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.2 — Turing)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. The real $\alpha$ is computable.
2. There is a computable sequence of rationals $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ such that $\lvert\alpha - q_n\rvert < 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.
3. There is a computable sequence of rationals $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ and a computable function $f$ such that $\lvert\alpha - q_{f(n)}\rvert < 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii): If $\alpha = 0.A$ for a computable set $A$ then let $q_n = 0.(A \upharpoonright n + 1)$. Then $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ and $\lvert\alpha - q_n\rvert < 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii): Obvious.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (i): Let $r_n = q_{f(n)}$. By our convention that $\alpha$ is irrational, for each $k$ there must be an $n$ such that the first $k+1$ bits of the binary expansions of $r_n - 2^{-n}$ and $r_n + 2^{-n}$ agree, since any convergent sequence of rationals not having this property must converge to a rational. Given $k$, search for an $n$ with the above property and define $A(k)$ to be the $k$th bit of $r_n$. Since $\alpha \in (r_n - 2^{-n}, r_n + 2^{-n})$, the $k$th bit of $\alpha$ must be the same as that of the binary expansion of $r_n$, whence $\alpha = 0.A$.

</div>

Of course, we can relativize the definition of computable real to any given oracle. Relativizing it to $\emptyset'$, we get the notion of a $\Delta^0_2$ *real*. These are exactly the reals that can be approximated by a computable sequence of rationals (with a possibly noneffective rate of convergence).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.3 — Demuth, Ho)</span></p>

A real is $\Delta^0_2$ iff there is a computable sequence of rationals converging to it.

*Proof.* If $A$ is a $\Delta^0_2$ set, then fixing a computable sequence $A_n$ converging to $A$, let $q_n = 0.(A_n \upharpoonright n)$. Then $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to 0.A$.

If $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ is a computable sequence of rationals, the $q \in L(\alpha)$ iff $\exists n \forall m > n\; (q < q_m)$ iff $\forall n \exists m > n\; (q < q_m)$, so $L(\alpha)$ is both $\Sigma^0_2$ and $\Pi^0_2$, and hence is $\Delta^0_2$.

</div>

It is well known that the computable reals (and hence, by relativization, the $\Delta^0_2$ reals) form a real closed field. The methods used to prove this fact are along the lines of the ones used in the proof of Theorem 5.4.7 below.

Having defined computable reals, it is natural to look for a definition of computably enumerable reals. The following is the natural definition based on the Dedekind cut approach.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.4)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is **left computably enumerable** (*left-c.e.*) if $L(\alpha)$ is c.e.

</div>

Left-c.e. reals are often referred to simply as "c.e. reals", but we wish to avoid any confusion between c.e. *reals* and c.e. *sets*. Left-c.e. reals have also been called *recursively enumerable*, *left computable*, *left semicomputable*, and *lower semicomputable*. Of course, we also have *right-c.e. reals*, which are the reals $\alpha$ such that $L(\alpha)$ is co-c.e. Equivalently, $\alpha$ is right-c.e. if $1 - \alpha$ is left-c.e.

The following is the most useful characterization of the left-c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.5 — Soare)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. The real $\alpha$ is left-c.e.
2. There is a computable sequence of rationals $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$.
3. There is a computable sequence of rationals $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ such that $\lbrace q_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$ is computable.
4. There is a computable sequence of positive rationals $r_0, r_1, \ldots$ such that $\alpha = \sum_n r_n$.

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (iii): Suppose $\alpha$ is left-c.e. For $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, let $\#(q)$ be the Gödel number of $q$, as in Definition 2.1.1. Let $q_0 \in L(\alpha)$, wait for a stage $s$ such that for $q = \max L(\alpha)[s]$, we have $q_n < q$ and $\#(q_n) < \#(q)$. Such a stage must exist because $L(\alpha)$ has no largest element. Let $q_{n+1} = q$. It is easy to see that $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$. Since the $q_i$ are increasing in Gödel number, $\lbrace q_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$ is computable.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (ii): Obvious.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iv): Let $r_0 = q_0$ and $r_{n+1} = q_{n+1} - q_n$.

(iv) $\Rightarrow$ (i): $L(\alpha) = \lbrace q \in \mathbb{Q} : \exists k\; (q < \sum_{n < k} r_n) \rbrace$, and hence is c.e.

</div>

It is easy to see that, in both (ii) and (iii) above, we can replace the strictly increasing sequence $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ by a nondecreasing sequence $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$.

As with c.e. sets, whenever we mention a left-c.e. real $\alpha$, we assume we have a fixed approximation to $\alpha$ in mind, that is, a sequence of rationals $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots$ converging to $\alpha$.

If $\alpha$ is computable, then *every* nondecreasing approximation of $\alpha$ has computable range.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.6 — Folklore)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ is computable and $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$ is a computable sequence then $\lbrace q_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$ is computable.

*Proof.* Given a rational $q$, if $q \notin L(\alpha)$ then $q \notin \lbrace q_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$. Otherwise, there must be an $n$ such that $q \leqslant q_n$. Then $q \in \lbrace q_i : i \in \omega \rbrace$ iff $q = q_i$ for some $i \leqslant n$.

</div>

It is important to note that a left-c.e. real is *not* one of the form $0.A$ for a c.e. set $A$. Reals of the latter form are called *strongly c.e.* It is easy to check (and will follow immediately from Theorem 5.1.7) that every strongly c.e. real is left-c.e., but as will see in Corollary 5.1.9, the converse is not true.

It is possible to characterize the sets $A$ such that $0.A$ is left-c.e. A set $A$ is *almost c.e.* if there is a computable approximation $\lbrace A_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ to $A$ such that if $A_s(n) = 1$ and $A_{s+1}(n) = 0$, then there is an $m < n$ with $A_s(m) = 0$ and $A_{s+1}(m) = 1$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.7 — Calude, Hertling, Khoussainov, and Wang)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is left-c.e. iff $\alpha = 0.A$ for an almost c.e. set $A$.

*Proof.* For strings $\tau$ and $\sigma$, it is easy to see that $0.\sigma \leqslant 0.\tau$ iff for every $n < \lvert\sigma\rvert$, if $\sigma(n) = 1$ and $\tau(n) = 0$, then there is an $m < n$ such that $\sigma(m) = 0$ and $\tau(m) = 1$ (where we take $\tau(k) = 0$ if $k \geqslant \lvert\tau\rvert$). So if $\alpha$ is left-c.e. then let $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ be a computable sequence of rationals and let $A_s$ be such that $0.A_s = q_s$ for all $s$. Then $A = \lim_s A_s$ is almost c.e. and $\alpha = 0.A$. Conversely, if $A$ is almost c.e. then, letting $q_s = 0.(A_s \upharpoonright s)$, we have $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to 0.A$.

</div>

Almost c.e. sets do share one important property with c.e. sets: If $A$ is almost c.e., then $A_{s+1} \neq A_s \Rightarrow \forall t > s\; (A_t \neq A_s)$. That is, once the approximation to an initial segment of $A$ has changed, it can never return to its old value.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.8)</span></p>

There is an almost c.e. set that is not c.e.

*Proof.* We build an approximation to an almost c.e. set $A$. We begin with $A_0(2e) = 0$ and $A_0(2e + 1) = 1$ for all $e$. At stage $s$, if $2e + 1$ enters $W_e$ then we let $A_{s+1}(2e + 1) = 0$ and $A_{s+1}(2e) = A_s(2e)$. (Otherwise, $A_{s+1}(2e) = A_s(2e)$ and $A_{s+1}(2e + 1) = A_s(2e + 1)$.) Clearly, $A$ is almost c.e. and $A \neq W_e$ for all $e$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.9 — Soare)</span></p>

There is a left-c.e. real that is not strongly c.e.

</div>

It is easy to adapt the above proof to show that there is an almost c.e. set that is not $n$-c.e. for any $n$. On the other hand, every almost c.e. set $A$ is $\omega$-c.e., since the approximation to $A \upharpoonright k$ can change at most $2^k$ many times.

Of course, not every real of the form $0.A$ for an $\omega$-c.e. set is left-c.e. For example, if $\alpha$ is right-c.e. and not computable, then it cannot be left-c.e. The following is a less obvious example.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.10 — Ambos-Spies, Weihrauch, and Zheng)</span></p>

There is a d.c.e. set $D$ such that $0.D$ is neither left-c.e. nor right-c.e.

*Proof.* Let $A \mid_T B$ be c.e. sets. Let $D = \overline{A} \oplus B$. Assume for a contradiction that $0.D$ is left-c.e. (the right-c.e. case being analogous). Then $D$ is almost c.e. We claim that we can compute $A$ using $B$. Suppose we know $A \upharpoonright n$. Let $s$ be such that $D_s \upharpoonright 2n = D \upharpoonright 2n$. We can find such an $s$ using $A \upharpoonright n$ and $B$. Now let $t \geqslant s$ be such that either $n \in A_t$ or $D_t(2n) = 1$, which must exist by the definition of $D$. If $n \in A_t$ then we know that $n \in A$. If $D_t(2n) = 1$, then we must have $D(2n) = 1$, as the approximation to $D \upharpoonright 2n$ changes. So in this case we know that $n \notin A$. Thus $A \leqslant_T B$, which is a contradiction.

</div>

Four important examples of left-c.e. reals that figure prominently in the study of algorithmic randomness are the halting probabilities of prefix-free machines, the weights of c.e. prefix-free sets (where the *weight* of a prefix-free set of strings $S$ is $\mu(\llbracket S \rrbracket) = \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$), the measures of $\Sigma^0_1$ classes, and the least members of $\Pi^0_1$ classes. Indeed, all of these examples exactly characterize the left-c.e. reals. The following theorem assembles the work of several researchers in the area.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.11)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. The real $\alpha$ is left-c.e.
2. There is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that $\alpha = \mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket)$.
3. There is a c.e. prefix-free set $A \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\alpha = \sum_{\sigma \in A} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$.
4. There is a computable prefix-free set $B \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\alpha = \sum_{\sigma \in B} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$.
5. There is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class $C$ such that $\alpha = \mu(C)$.
6. There is a $\Pi^0_1$ class $P$ such that $\alpha$ is the least element of $P$.
7. There is a computable tree $T$ such that $\alpha$ is the leftmost path of $T$.

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii): Let $\alpha$ be left-c.e. Then there is a computable sequence of positive rationals whose sum is $\alpha$. Each rational is a sum of finitely many rationals of the form $2^{-n}$, so there is computable sequence $n_0, n_1, \ldots$ such that $\sum_i 2^{-n_i} = \alpha$. Then $\lbrace (n_i, \lambda) \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ is a KC set, so by the KC Theorem there is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that $\mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket) = \alpha$.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii): Let $A = \text{dom}\; M$.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (iv): Let $B = \lbrace \tau : \exists \sigma \preccurlyeq \tau\; (\sigma \in A_{\lvert\tau\rvert}) \rbrace$.

(iv) $\Rightarrow$ (v): Let $C = \llbracket B \rrbracket$.

(v) $\Rightarrow$ (vi): Let $R$ be a c.e. prefix-free set such that $C = \llbracket R \rrbracket$, and let $P$ be the class of reals $\beta$ such that $\beta \geqslant \mu(C)$. It is easy to check that $\beta \in P$ iff $0.(\beta \upharpoonright n + 1) + 2^{-n} \geqslant \sum_{\sigma \in R_n} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ for all $n$, so $P$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class.

(vi) $\Rightarrow$ (vii): Let $T$ be such that $P = [T]$.

(vii) $\Rightarrow$ (i): Let $\sigma_s$ be the leftmost node at level $s$ of $T$ and let $q_s = 0.\sigma_s$. It is easy to check that $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$.

</div>

So we see that left-c.e. reals correspond to (the measures of) domains of prefix-free machines, much as c.e. sets correspond to domains of Turing machines. This fact helps explain the importance of left-c.e. reals to the theory of algorithmic randomness.

One easy but useful fact about the class of left-c.e. reals is that it is closed under addition. Recall that we always work modulo 1, so by $\alpha + \beta$ we mean the fractional part of $\alpha + \beta$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.1.12)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are left-c.e. reals, then so are $\alpha + \beta$ and $\alpha\beta$.

</div>

### 5.2 Real-Valued Functions

In this section we consider effective functions from a countable domain $D$ (such as $\mathbb{N}$, $2^{<\omega}$, or $\mathbb{Q}$) to $\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of reals is *uniformly computable* if the corresponding sequence of left cuts is uniformly computable, and *uniformly left-c.e.* if the corresponding sequence of left cuts is uniformly c.e.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.2.1)</span></p>

Let $f : D \to \mathbb{R}$.

1. The function $f$ is *computable* if its values are uniformly computable reals.
2. The function $f$ is *computably enumerable* or $\Sigma^0_1$ if its values are uniformly left-c.e. reals.

</div>

Both of these definitions can be relativized in the obvious way. It is easy to check that $f$ is c.e. iff there are uniformly computable functions $f_s : D \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that for all $s$ and $x \in D$, we have $f_{s+1}(x) \geqslant f_s(x)$ and $\lim_s f_s(x) = f(x)$, or, in other words, $f$ can be approximated from below. Another characterization is that $f$ is c.e. iff the set $\lbrace (x, q) \in D \times \mathbb{Q} : q < f(x) \rbrace$ is c.e., and $f$ is computable iff this set is computable.

It is important to note that when we define a computable function $f : D \to \mathbb{Q}$, we always mean one for which there is an algorithm to determine $f(x)$ given $x$, *not* a computable function $f : D \to \mathbb{R}$ that happens to take values in $\mathbb{Q}$.

We also define a function $f : D \to \mathbb{R}$ to be *co-c.e.* if its values are uniformly right-c.e. reals (i.e., their left cuts are uniformly co-c.e.). Note that a function is co-c.e. iff it can be approximated from above.

### 5.3 Representing Left-c.e. Reals

As we have seen, two ways to represent a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ are by a computable nondecreasing sequence of rationals with limit $\alpha$ and by the measure of a $\Sigma^0_1$ class. In this section, we explore computability-theoretic aspects of these two ways of approximating left-c.e. reals.

#### 5.3.1 Degrees of Representations

A *representation* of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is the range of a computable sequence of rationals $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$. Representations were first analyzed by Calude, Coles, Hertling, and Khoussainov. By the relativized version of Proposition 5.1.6, every representation of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is computable in $\alpha$, and by Theorem 5.1.5, $\alpha$ has a computable representation. The following result gives some more information about the possible degrees of representations of $\alpha$. Recall that a c.e. splitting of a c.e. set $A$ is a pair of disjoint c.e. sets $B$ and $C$ such that $A = B \cup C$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.1 — Calude, Coles, Hertling, and Khoussainov)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real. Every representation of $\alpha$ is half of a c.e. splitting of $L(\alpha)$.

*Proof.* Let $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$ be a computable sequence of rationals, and let $A$ be its range. Clearly, $A$ is an infinite c.e. subset of $L(\alpha)$. We claim that so is $L(\alpha) \setminus A$. By our convention that $\alpha$ is irrational, for each $n$ there is an $m > n$ such that $q_m > q_n$. Thus $q \in L(\alpha) \setminus A$ iff $q \in L(\alpha)$ and there is an $n$ such that $q < q_n$ and $q \neq q_m$ for $m < n$. Thus $L(\alpha) \setminus A$ is c.e.

</div>

If $A$ is half of a c.e. splitting of a c.e. set $B$, then $A \leqslant_{wtt} B$, because to tell whether $n \in A$, we first check whether $n \in B$, and if so then we enumerate $A$ and $B \setminus A$ until $n$ appears in one of these sets. Thus we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.2 — Soare)</span></p>

If $A$ is a representation of $\alpha$ then $A \leqslant_{wtt} L(\alpha)$.

</div>

It is not difficult to show, using a diagonalization argument, that the converse to Proposition 5.3.1 is not true. However, we do have the following partial converse.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.3 — Calude, Coles, Hertling, and Khoussainov)</span></p>

Let $A$ be a representation of $\alpha$. If $B \subseteq A$ is infinite, then $B$ represents $\alpha$ iff $B$ is half of a c.e. splitting of $A$.

*Proof.* The "only if" direction has the same proof as Proposition 5.3.1. For the other direction, let $q_0 \leqslant q_1 \leqslant \cdots \to \alpha$ be a computable sequence of rationals with range $A$. Since $B$ and $A \setminus B$ are both c.e., given $n$ we can compute whether $q_n \in B$, and hence effectively produce an infinite subsequence of the $q_n$ with range $B$.

</div>

Calude, Coles, Hertling, and Khoussainov also showed that every left-c.e. real $\alpha$ has a representation of the same degree as $\alpha$. Their result follows immediately from the following exact characterization of the $m$-degrees of representations of a left-c.e. real.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.4 — Downey)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real. For every c.e. splitting $B \sqcup C$ of $L(\alpha)$, there is a representation $A$ of $\alpha$ such that $A \equiv_m B$. Thus, the $m$-degrees of halves of c.e. splittings of $L(\alpha)$ and the $m$-degrees of representations of $\alpha$ coincide.

</div>

This characterization, together with known results on c.e. splittings and wtt-degrees, yields several facts about representations, including many of the results in the original paper. For instance, by Sacks' Splitting Theorem 2.12.1, every noncomputable left-c.e. real $\alpha$ has representations in infinitely many degrees (because Theorem 2.12.1 implies that we can split $L(\alpha)$ into Turing incomparable $A_0$ and $A_1$; but then we can also split $A_0$ into Turing incomparable $B_0$ and $B_1$ and have a representation of $\alpha$ of the same degree as $B_0$, and continue in this manner to obtain representations in infinitely many degrees).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.5)</span></p>

1. There is a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ with representations of every c.e. $m$-degree.
2. There is a left-c.e. real $\beta$ such that the set of Turing degrees of representations of $\beta$ forms an atomless boolean algebra that is nowhere dense in the c.e. degrees.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.6)</span></p>

If a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ has representations in each degree below that of $\alpha$, then $\alpha$ is either complete or $\text{low}_2$.

</div>

#### 5.3.2 Presentations of Left-c.e. Reals

Another natural way to represent left-c.e. reals is via measures.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.7 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

A *presentation* of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is a prefix-free c.e. set $A \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\alpha = \mu(\llbracket A \rrbracket) = \sum_{\sigma \in A} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$.

</div>

We have seen that every left-c.e. real $\alpha$ has a representation of degree $\alpha$. The same is not true of presentations. Downey and LaForte showed that there is a noncomputable left-c.e. real all of whose presentations are computable. We will obtain this result as a corollary to Theorem 5.3.13 below. Downey and LaForte also showed that such "only computably presentable" reals can be high, but that, as we will see in Theorem 5.3.16 below, they cannot be of promptly simple degree. Using a $0'''$ argument, Wu constructed a c.e. set such that any $A$-computable, noncomputable left-c.e. real has a noncomputable presentation.

Strong reducibilities often play a large role in effective mathematics, since naturally occurring reductions tend to be stronger than Turing reductions. In this case, the relevant reducibility seems to be weak truth table reducibility. The following result is easy to prove.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.8 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real and let $A$ be a presentation of $\alpha$. Then $A \leqslant_{wtt} \alpha$ with use function the identity.

</div>

What is interesting is that there is a sort of converse to this result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.9 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A$ is a presentation of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ and $C \leqslant_{wtt} A$ is c.e., then there is a presentation $B$ of $\alpha$ with $B \equiv_{wtt} C$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.10 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

There exist a strongly c.e. real $\alpha$ and a presentation $B$ of $\alpha$ such that $B \nleqslant_{tt} \alpha$.

</div>

#### 5.3.3 Presentations and Ideals

A class $I \subseteq 2^\omega$ is a *wtt-ideal* if it is closed under join and downward closed under wtt-reducibility. For a left-c.e. real $\alpha$, let $\mathcal{I}(\alpha)$ be the collection of all c.e. sets $C$ such that there is a presentation $A$ of $\alpha$ with $C \equiv_{wtt} A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.11 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ is a left-c.e. real then $\mathcal{I}(\alpha)$ is a $\Sigma^0_3$ wtt-ideal.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.12 — Downey and Terwijn)</span></p>

$\mathcal{I}(\alpha)$ is either $\lbrace W_e : e \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ or $\Sigma^0_3$-complete.

*Proof sketch.* Let $\alpha = 0.A$ be a left-c.e. real. By Proposition 5.3.8 and Theorem 5.3.9, $\mathcal{I}(\alpha) = \lbrace W_e : e \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ iff $A$ is wtt-complete. Now suppose that $A$ is not wtt-complete. We can show that $\mathcal{I}(\alpha)$ is $\Sigma^0_3$-complete using methods of Rogers and Kallibekov.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.13 — Downey and Terwijn)</span></p>

Let $\mathcal{I}$ be a $\Sigma^0_3$ wtt-ideal of c.e. sets. There is a noncomputable left-c.e. real $\alpha$ such that $\mathcal{I}(\alpha) = \mathcal{I}$.

</div>

The computable sets form a $\Sigma^0_3$ wtt-ideal, so we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.14 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

There is a noncomputable left-c.e. real all of whose presentations are computable.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.15 — Yates)</span></p>

Let $I \in \Sigma^0_3$ and let $\mathcal{C} = \lbrace W_i : i \in I \rbrace$ contain all the finite sets. There are uniformly c.e. $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ such that $\mathcal{C} = \lbrace V_e : e \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$.

</div>

#### 5.3.4 Promptly Simple Sets and Presentations of Left-c.e. Reals

Recall that a coinfinite c.e. set $A$ is promptly simple if there are a computable function $f$ and an enumeration $\lbrace A_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ of $A$ such that for every infinite c.e. set $W$, there are an $n$ and an $s$ with $n \in W_e[s] \setminus W_e[s-1] \cap A_{f(s)}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.3.16 — Downey and LaForte)</span></p>

If a left-c.e. real computes a promptly simple set then it has a noncomputable presentation.

</div>

### 5.4 Left-d.c.e. Reals

#### 5.4.1 Basics

There are other natural classes of effective reals between the left-c.e. reals and the $\Delta^0_2$ reals. Several researchers have investigated hierarchies in this region.

The following is a particularly interesting example of such a class of reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.1)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is *left-d.c.e.* if there exist left-c.e. reals $\beta$ and $\gamma$ such that $\alpha = \beta - \gamma$.

</div>

This terminology is a bit misleading, since left-d.c.e. reals are the differences of left-c.e. reals, not the ones whose left cuts are d.c.e. sets. However, if $A$ is a d.c.e. set, then $0.A$ is a left-d.c.e. real, so by Theorem 5.1.10, there are left-d.c.e. reals that are neither left-c.e. nor right-c.e. The following is an alternate characterization of the left-d.c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.2 — Ambos-Spies, Weihrauch, and Zheng)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is left-d.c.e. iff there is a computable sequence of rationals $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ such that $\sum_n \lvert q_{n+1} - q_n\rvert < \infty$.

*Proof.* If such a sequence exists, then let $\beta = q_0 + \sum \lbrace q_{n+1} - q_n : q_{n+1} - q_n \geqslant 0 \rbrace$ and $\gamma = \sum \lbrace q_n - q_{n+1} : q_{n+1} - q_n < 0 \rbrace$. Since $\sum_n \lvert q_{n+1} - q_n\rvert < \infty$, both $\beta$ and $\gamma$ are finite, and they are both left-c.e. Clearly, $\alpha = \beta - \gamma$.

For the converse, let $\beta$ and $\gamma$ be left-c.e. and let $\alpha = \beta - \gamma$. Let $r_0 < r_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ and $s_0 < s_1 < \cdots \to \gamma$ be computable sequences of rationals. Let $q_n = r_n - s_n$. Then $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$, and

$$\sum_n \lvert q_{n+1} - q_n\rvert = \sum_n \lvert r_{n+1} - s_{n+1} - (r_n - s_n)\rvert \leqslant \sum_n (r_{n+1} - r_n) + \sum_n (s_{n+1} - s_n) = \beta - r_0 + \gamma - s_0 < \infty.$$

</div>

It is easy to see that if $A$ is an $n$-c.e. set for some $n$, then $0.A$ is a left-d.c.e. real. We can push this fact one level further using Theorem 5.4.2. We begin with a lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.3 — Downey, Wu, and Zheng)</span></p>

Let $A$ be $\omega$-c.e. Let $f$ and $g$ be computable functions such that $A(n) = \lim_s f(n, s)$ and $\lvert\lbrace s : f(n, s+1) \neq f(n, s) \rbrace\rvert < g(n)$ for all $n$. If $\sum_n g(n) 2^{-n} < \infty$, then $0.A$ is left-d.c.e.

*Proof.* We may assume that exactly one $n_s$ enters or leaves $A$ at each stage $s$. Then $\lbrace 0.A_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ is a computable sequence of rationals converging to $0.A$, and

$$\sum_s \lvert 0.A_s - 0.A_{s+1}\rvert = \sum_s 2^{-n_s} = \sum_n g(n) 2^{-n} < \infty.$$

By Theorem 5.4.2, $0.A$ is left-d.c.e.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.4 — Downey, Wu, and Zheng)</span></p>

Every $\omega$-c.e. degree contains a left-d.c.e. real.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be $\omega$-c.e. By Proposition 2.7.3, there are an $\omega$-c.e. set $B \equiv_T A$ and a computable function $f$ satisfying conditions (i)-(iii) in that proposition. Since $\sum_n n 2^{-n} < \infty$, it follows from Lemma 5.4.3 that $0.B$ is a left-d.c.e. real.

</div>

The converse to this result does not hold. Indeed, Zheng showed that there is a left-d.c.e. real whose degree is not $\omega$-c.e.

We have seen in Theorem 5.1.3 that every $\Delta^0_2$ real is the limit of a computable sequence of rationals. It might seem reasonable to conjecture that perhaps *every* $\Delta^0_2$ degree contains a left-d.c.e. real. However, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.5 — Downey, Wu, and Zheng)</span></p>

There are $\Delta^0_2$ degrees containing no left-d.c.e. reals.

</div>

#### 5.4.2 The Field of Left-d.c.e. Reals

One of the reasons to consider left-d.c.e. reals is that they form the smallest field containing the left-c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.6 — Ambos-Spies, Weihrauch, and Zheng)</span></p>

The left-d.c.e. reals form a field.

*Proof.* Rearranging terms shows closure under addition, subtraction, and multiplication (e.g., $(\alpha - \beta)(\gamma - \delta) = (\alpha\gamma + \beta\delta) - (\beta\gamma + \alpha\delta)$), and we have already mentioned that the class of left-c.e. reals is closed under addition and multiplication). We show closure under division.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta \neq 0$ be left-d.c.e. reals. Let $q_0, q_1, \ldots \to \alpha$ and $r_0, r_1, \ldots \to \beta$ be computable sequences of rationals such that $\sum_n \lvert q_{n+1} - q_n\rvert < \infty$ and $\sum_n \lvert r_{n+1} - r_n\rvert < \infty$. We may assume that the $r_n$ are bounded away from 0. Let $M$ be larger than both the above sums, and larger than $\lvert q_n\rvert$ and $\frac{1}{\lvert r_n\rvert}$ for all $n$. Then $s_0, s_1, \ldots = \frac{q_0}{r_0}, \frac{q_1}{r_1}, \ldots \to \frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ is a computable sequence of rationals, and showing that $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ is left-d.c.e., it is enough to show that $\sum_n \lvert s_{n+1} - s_n\rvert < \infty$. But this sum is equal to

$$\sum_n \left\lvert \frac{q_{n+1}}{r_{n+1}} - \frac{q_n}{r_n} \right\rvert \leqslant \sum_n \left\lvert \frac{q_{n+1} - q_n}{r_{n+1}} \right\rvert + \sum_n \left\lvert \frac{q_n}{r_n r_{n+1}} (r_n - r_{n+1}) \right\rvert < M \sum_n \lvert q_{n+1} - q_n\rvert + M^3 \sum_n \lvert r_n - r_{n+1}\rvert < M^2 + M^4.$$

</div>

Ng and Raichev have shown that this field behaves well analytically. Recall that a field $F$ is *real closed* if every polynomial of odd degree with coefficients in $F$ has a root in $F$, and for each $x \in F$, there is a $y \in F$ such that $x = y^2$ or $x = -y^2$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.7 — Ng, Raichev)</span></p>

The field of left-d.c.e. reals is real closed.

</div>

The proof uses the following proposition, which ensures that analytic functions preserve the left-d.c.e. property of their roots.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.8)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a real function that is analytic at some $u_0 \in \mathbb{R}_2$ (where $\mathbb{R}_2$ denotes the left-d.c.e. reals). Suppose its Taylor series converges in some open interval $E$ centered at $u_0$, and that there are uniformly computable sequences of rationals $a_{0,n}, a_{1,n}, \ldots \to f^{(n)}(u_0)$ such that $\sup_n \sum_k \lvert a_{k+1,n} - a_{k,n}\rvert < \infty$. Then every root of $f(x)$ in $E$ is also in $\mathbb{R}_2$.

</div>

The theorem follows from this proposition: any polynomial $f(x) \in \mathbb{R}_2[x]$ satisfies the hypotheses of the proposition (since $f^{(m)}(0) = 0$ for all sufficiently large $m$), so every such odd-degree polynomial has a root in $\mathbb{R}_2$, and $\sqrt{\gamma} \in \mathbb{R}_2$ for any nonnegative $\gamma \in \mathbb{R}_2$.

The proof that roots of analytic functions in $E$ lie in $\mathbb{R}_2$ proceeds by Newton's method. Fix a simple root $r$ of $f$ in $E$. Define $f_k \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$ by $f_k(x) = a_{k,0} + a_{k,1}(x - u_0) + \cdots + a_{k,k} \frac{(x - u_0)^k}{k!}$. One shows that:

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.9)</span></p>

There are rationals $\alpha, \beta, M, m, m'$ such that $[\alpha, \beta]$ is an interval in $E$ containing $r$ and for all $x \in [\alpha, \beta]$, we have $\lvert f''(x)\rvert < M$ and $0 < m < f'(x) < m'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.10)</span></p>

Let $x_0, x_1, \ldots$ be a sequence of points in a closed bounded interval $I \subseteq E$. Then $\sum_k \lvert f_{k+1}(x_k) - f_k(x_k)\rvert < \infty$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.11)</span></p>

$\lim_k f_k = f$ (and, similarly, $\lim_k f'_k = f'$ and $\lim_k f''_k = f''$) uniformly on $[\alpha, \beta]$.

</div>

Using these lemmas, one defines sequences of approximate roots $r_s$ of $f_s$ (for each $s$) and shows that $\lim_s r_s = r$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.12)</span></p>

There is an $s_0$ such that for all $s \geqslant s_0$, $[\alpha, \beta]$ contains a simple root $r_s$ of $f_s$, $\lvert f''_s(x)\rvert < M$, and $0 < m < \lvert f'_s(x)\rvert < m'$ for all $x \in [\alpha, \beta]$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.13)</span></p>

$\lim_s r_s = r$.

</div>

Let $K = \frac{M}{2m}$ and $\eta < \min\lbrace \frac{1}{2K}, \frac{\eta}{2} \rbrace$. For all $s \geqslant s_0$, we also want $\lvert r_s - r\rvert < \frac{\eta}{2}$. Let $y_0 \in \mathbb{Q}$ be such that $\lvert y_0 - r\rvert < \frac{\eta}{2}$. For each $s$, define a computable sequence of rationals by $x_{s,0} = y_0$ and $x_{s,n+1} = x_{s,n} - \frac{f_s(x_{s,n})}{f'_s(x_{s,n})}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.14 — Newton's Method of Locating Roots)</span></p>

For each $s$, if $x' \in [\alpha, \beta]$ and $x'' = x' - \frac{f_s(x')}{f'_s(x')}$, then $\lvert x'' - r_s\rvert \leqslant K\lvert x' - r_s\rvert^2$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.15)</span></p>

For all $s$ and $n$, we have $\lvert x_{s,n+1} - x_{s,n}\rvert \leqslant ((K\eta)^{n+1} + (K\eta)^n)\lvert y_0 - r_s\rvert$.

</div>

One then constructs a computable sequence $y_0, y_1, \ldots$ converging to $r$ by carefully interleaving Newton iterations at different levels.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.17)</span></p>

$\lim_n y_n = r$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(5.4.18)</span></p>

$r \in \mathbb{R}_2$.

*Proof.* It remains to show that $\sum_i \lvert y_{i+1} - y_i\rvert < \infty$. By Lemma 5.4.15 and the construction of the $y_i$, we have

$$\sum_i \lvert y_{i+1} - y_i\rvert \leqslant \sum_i ((K\eta)^{i+1} + (K\eta)^i) 2\delta + \sum_s \frac{1}{m} \lvert f_{s+1}(r'_{s+1}) - f_s(r'_{s+1})\rvert + \sum_s \frac{1}{2^s} < \infty.$$

</div>

This establishes Proposition 5.4.8, and hence proves the theorem.

Similar methods can also be used to show that the computable reals form a real closed field (see for instance [318]), and hence, by relativization, that so do the $\Delta^0_2$ reals, a fact first explicitly noted and proved directly by Raichev.

## Chapter 6: Martin-Löf Randomness

In this chapter, we will introduce three cornerstone approaches to the definition of algorithmic randomness for infinite sequences.

1. The *computational paradigm*: Random sequences are those whose initial segments are all hard to describe, or, equivalently, hard to compress. This approach is probably the easiest one to understand in terms of the previous sections.

2. The *measure-theoretic paradigm*: Random sequences are those with no "effectively rare" properties. If the class of sequences satisfying a given property is an effectively null set, then a random sequence should not have this property. This approach is the same as the *stochastic paradigm*: a random sequence should pass all effective statistical tests.

3. The *unpredictability paradigm*: This approach stems from the most intuitive conception of randomness, namely that one should not be able to predict the next bit of a random sequence, even if one knows all preceding bits, in the same way that a coin toss is unpredictable even given the results of previous coin tosses.

We will see that all three approaches can be used to define the same notion of randomness, which is called Martin-Löf randomness or 1-randomness. This notion was the first successful attempt to capture the idea of a random infinite sequence, and is still the best known and most studied of the various definitions proposed to date.

### 6.1 The Computational Paradigm

In a way, the definition of algorithmic randomness is more satisfying for infinite sequences than it is for strings. One of the main reasons for this phenomenon, as we will see, is that algorithmic randomness of sequences is absolute, in that no matter which universal machine one chooses, the class of random sequences remains the same.

A natural first attempt at defining a notion of randomness for a set $A$ would be to say that $A$ is random if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. Unfortunately, *no* set satisfies this condition. This is a fundamental observation of Martin-Löf that we have already seen in Theorem 3.1.4. As we will see in Section 6.7, it *is* possible to give a satisfactory definition of randomness for infinite sequences using plain complexity, but this is a recent development. We can vindicate the intuition leading to the incorrect attempt at a definition of randomness for infinite sequences via plain complexity by using prefix-free complexity instead.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.1.1 — Levin, Chaitin)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **1-random** if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

We will call a degree (Turing or otherwise) 1-random if it contains a 1-random set.

</div>

The reason for the term "1-random" will become clear once we introduce the notion of $n$-randomness for arbitrary $n$ in Section 6.8. Notice that, in the nomenclature of Chapter 3, a sequence is 1-random iff all its initial segments are weakly $K$-random. One cannot replace weak $K$-randomness by strong $K$-randomness, as we saw in Theorem 3.11.1.

Recall Chaitin's halting probability $\Omega$, which we introduced in Definition 3.13.6:

$$\Omega = \mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\;\mathcal{U}\rrbracket) = \sum_{\sigma \in \text{dom}\;\mathcal{U}} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}.$$

As we will see, $\Omega$ is 1-random. It is in fact the best known example of a 1-random real. As we discussed following Definition 3.13.6, while the value of $\Omega$ depends on the choice of $\mathcal{U}$, we will see in Section 9.2 that all versions of $\Omega$ are quite closely related, and it makes sense to ignore the machine-dependence of $\Omega$ in most circumstances.

Recall also that $\Omega_s = \sum_{\mathcal{U}(\sigma)[s] \downarrow} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$. The rationals $\Omega_s$ approximate $\Omega$ from below, so $\Omega$ is a left-c.e. real. One way to look at $\Omega$ is as a highly compressed version of $\emptyset'$, as the following results show.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.1.2 — Calude and Nies)</span></p>

$\Omega \equiv_{wtt} \emptyset'$.

*Proof.* Since $\Omega$ is a left-c.e. real, it is computable in $\emptyset'$. For the other direction, let $M$ be the prefix-free machine defined by letting $M(0^e 1) \downarrow = \lambda$ whenever $\Phi_e(e) \downarrow$. Since $\mathcal{U}$ is universal, there is a $\rho$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\rho 0^e 1) \downarrow$ iff $e \in \emptyset'$. To determine whether $e \in \emptyset'$ given $\Omega$, wait for a stage $s$ such that $\Omega - \Omega_s < 2^{-(\lvert\rho\rvert + e + 1)}$. If $\mathcal{U}(\rho 0^e 1)[s] \downarrow$ then $e \in \emptyset'$. Otherwise, $\mathcal{U}(\rho 0^e 1) \uparrow$, so $e \notin \emptyset'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.1.3 — Chaitin)</span></p>

$\Omega$ is 1-random.

*Proof.* By Proposition 6.1.2, $\Omega$ is not rational, so for each $n$ there is an $s$ with $\Omega_s \upharpoonright n = \Omega \upharpoonright n$. We build a prefix-free machine $M$. By the recursion theorem, we may assume we know its coding constant $c$ in $\mathcal{U}$. Whenever at a stage $s$ we have $\mathcal{U}(\sigma)[s] = \Omega_s \upharpoonright n$ for some $\tau$ such that $\lvert\tau\rvert < n - c$ (which means that $K(\Omega_s \upharpoonright n) < n - c$), we choose a string $\mu \notin \text{rng}\;\mathcal{U}[s]$ and declare $M(\tau) = \mu$. Since $M$ is coded in $\mathcal{U}$ with coding constant $c$, there must be a $\nu$ such that $\lvert\nu\rvert \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + c < n$ and $\mathcal{U}(\nu) = \mu$. Since $\mu \notin \text{rng}\;\mathcal{U}[s]$, it follows that $\nu \notin \text{dom}\;\mathcal{U}[s]$, so $\Omega - \Omega_s \geqslant 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert} > 2^{-n}$, and hence $\Omega \upharpoonright n \neq \Omega_s \upharpoonright n$. This procedure ensures that if $\lvert\tau\rvert < n - c$ then $\mathcal{U}(\tau) \neq \Omega \upharpoonright n$, whence $K(\Omega \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - c$ for all $n$.

</div>

It is important to note that $\Omega$ is a somewhat misleading example of 1-randomness, as it is rather computationally powerful. We will see in Section 9.2 that all 1-random left-c.e. reals behave like $\Omega$ (indeed they are all versions of $\Omega$); in particular, they are all Turing (and even weak truth table) complete. We will also see, in Section 8.3, that 1-random sets can be arbitrarily powerful, in the sense that for every set $A$ there is a 1-random set $R \geqslant_T A$. However, heuristically speaking, randomness should be antithetical to computational power, inasmuch as classes of sets with a certain given amount of computational power (such as computing $\emptyset'$, or being a PA degree) tend to have measure 0. We will see below that as we move from 1-randomness to more powerful notions of algorithmic randomness, we do indeed tend to lose computational power.

Chaitin also defined another left-c.e. real, $\widehat{\Omega} = \sum_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}} 2^{-K(\sigma)}$. It is not hard to modify the above proof to show that $\widehat{\Omega}$ is 1-random. Another example of a 1-random left-c.e. real is the output probability $Q(\sigma)$ from Definition 3.9.3. The easiest way to see that $Q(\sigma)$ is 1-random for any $\sigma$ is using the methods of Chapter 9.

Thus we have concrete examples of 1-random sets. As we will see in the following section, almost every set is 1-random (as we would expect). It was an interesting question of Solovay whether $\liminf_s K(\Omega \upharpoonright n) - n = \infty$. This question was eventually solved in the affirmative by Chaitin. More recently, Miller and Yu gave a powerful characterization of randomness that has this result as a corollary. Namely, they proved that $A$ is 1-random iff $\sum_{n \in \mathbb{N}} 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} < \infty$. We will prove this result in Theorem 6.6.1 below.

The following result is also worth noting.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.1.4 — Fortnow, Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

If there is an infinite computable set $S$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + O(1)$ for all $n \in S$, then $A$ is 1-random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is not 1-random and let $S = \lbrace s_0 < s_1 < \cdots \rbrace$ be computable. If $\sigma = \tau\rho$ and $\lvert\sigma\rvert = s_{\lvert\tau\rvert}$, then we can give a prefix-free description of $\sigma$ by giving a prefix-free description of $\tau$ and then giving $\rho$ itself, so $K(\sigma) \leqslant K(\tau) + \lvert\rho\rvert + O(1)$. So if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - d$, then $K(A \upharpoonright s_n) \leqslant n - d + (s_n - n) + O(1) = s_n - d + O(1)$. Since for each $d$ there is an $n$ with $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - d$, the same is true if we restrict ourselves to $n \in S$.

</div>

### 6.2 The Measure-Theoretic Paradigm

The main idea behind the measure-theoretic, or stochastic, paradigm is that a random sequence should have no rare properties. In a remarkable early paper, von Mises attempted to formalize this notion. He pointed out that if we flip a fair coin, then we should surely expect to see as many heads as tails in the limit. Thus a random binary sequence $a_0 a_1 \ldots$ should obey the *law of large numbers* by having the property that $\lim_n \frac{\sum_{i < n} a_i}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$. But of course this condition is not enough, because highly nonrandom sequences such as $010101\ldots$ satisfy it. We clearly need the law of large numbers to hold for *subsequences* as well. Thus von Mises proposed calling a binary sequence $a_0 a_1 \ldots$ random if for every *selection function* $f$ with $f(0) < f(1) < \cdots$, we have $\lim_n \frac{\sum_{i < n} a_{f(i)}}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

This definition is not quite precise, though, because of the undefined concept of a selection function. Clearly we cannot allow $f$ to be just any increasing function, in the modern set-theoretic sense, since if a sequence $a_0 a_1 \ldots$ contains infinitely many 0's, then there is an increasing function $f$ such that $a_{f(i)} = 0$ for all $i$. On the other hand, Wald showed that for any *countable* collection of selection functions, there is a sequence that is random in the sense of von Mises. Church proposed restricting $f$ to computable increasing functions; we now call sequences that satisfy the law of large numbers for all computable selection functions *computably stochastic*, or *Church stochastic*. We will study this notion, as well as the related one of *von Mises-Wald-Church stochasticity*, where partial computable selection functions are allowed, in Section 7.4.1.

The problem with this promising notion was pointed out by Ville, who showed that for any countable collection of selection functions (thus in particular Church's), there is a sequence $a_0 a_1 \ldots$ that is random in the sense of von Mises but has $\sum_{i < n} a(i) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$ for all $n$. Such a sequence is clearly not random. After all, if we were to flip a supposedly fair coin many times and never at any point observed an excess of tails over heads, we would surely begin to suspect that something was amiss. We will prove Ville's Theorem in Section 6.5.

Ville suggested adding a further statistical law, the law of iterated logarithms, to von Mises' definition. However, one might well ask how we can be sure that adding this law would be enough. We could add more and more statistical laws to our collection of desiderata for random sequences, but there is no reason to believe we would ever be done, and we certainly do not want a definition of randomness that changes with time, if we can avoid it. Martin-Löf's fundamental idea was to define an abstract notion of a performable statistical test for randomness, and require that a random sequence pass *all* such tests. He did so by effectivizing the notion of a set of measure 0.

To motivate Martin-Löf's definition via a simple example, consider the class $C$ of all sequences $A$ such that $A(2^k) = 0$ for all $k$. Clearly, such sequences are not random. If we are presented with a sequence $A$ that might be in $C$, we can perform a test for membership of $A$ in $C$ to within a confidence level of $2^{-n}$ by considering all $k < n$. If for all such $k$ we have $A(2^k) = 0$, then we have some reason to believe that $A \in C$. (Of course, if not, then we can be certain that $A \notin C$.) We might be wrong about our belief that $A \in C$, but the set of all $A$ that our test indicates are in $C$ has measure $2^{-n}$, so we can have greater and greater confidence that $A \in C$ as we perform finer and finer levels of this test.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.1 — Martin-Löf)</span></p>

1. A **Martin-Löf test** is a sequence $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes such that $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.

2. A class $C \subset 2^\omega$ is **Martin-Löf null** if there is a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $C \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

3. A set $A \in 2^\omega$ is **Martin-Löf random** if $\lbrace A \rbrace$ is not Martin-Löf null.

</div>

In the above definition, we did not require that $U_0 \supseteq U_1 \supseteq \cdots$, but we could have: Given a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, let $V_n = \bigcup_{m > n} U_m$. Then $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test, $V_0 \supseteq V_1 \supseteq \cdots$, and $\bigcap_n U_n = \bigcap_n V_n$.

We could also have defined a Martin-Löf test to be a sequence $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes for which there is a computable function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that $\lim_n f(n) = 0$ and $\mu(U_n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$: Given such a test, let $V_n = U_m$ for the least $m$ such that $f(m) \leqslant 2^{-n}$. Then $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test and $\bigcap_n U_n = \bigcap_n V_n$.

We can now restate the above example: The class $C$ of all $A$ such that $A(2^k) = 0$ for all $k$ is Martin-Löf null, and hence no such set is Martin-Löf random. Much more interestingly, the class of sets that do not satisfy the law of large numbers is Martin-Löf null, and similarly, for any computable selection function $f$, the class of sets that do not satisfy the law of large numbers for the subsequence of bits picked out by $f$ is also Martin-Löf null. Thus, if $A$ is Martin-Löf random then it is Church stochastic. The converse is not true, however, because we can build a Martin-Löf test containing Ville's sequence mentioned above.

Martin-Löf randomness is equivalent to 1-randomness, which helps justify the notion of 1-randomness as a reasonable notion of algorithmic randomness. To show this fact, we begin with a lemma. We will use only its first part here, but the second part will be useful below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.2)</span></p>

Let $M$ be a prefix-free machine, let $k \in \mathbb{N}$, and let $S = \lbrace \sigma : K_M(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - k \rbrace$. Then $\mu(\llbracket S \rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-k} \mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket)$ via a procedure that is computable in $\mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket)$ and uniform in $k$.

*Proof.* For each $\sigma \in S$ there is a string $\tau_\sigma$ such that $\lvert\tau_\sigma\rvert \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - k$ and $M(\tau_\sigma) \downarrow = \sigma$. Thus

$$\mu(\llbracket S \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-(\lvert\tau_\sigma\rvert + k)} = 2^{-k} \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-\lvert\tau_\sigma\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k} \sum_{\tau \in \text{dom}\; M} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} = 2^{-k} \mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket).$$

To compute $\mu(\llbracket S \rrbracket)$ to within $2^{-c}$ given $\mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket)$, find a finite set $F \subseteq \text{dom}\; M$ such that $\mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; M\rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket F \rrbracket) < 2^{-c+k}$. Let $N_0$ be the restriction of $M$ to $F$ and $N_1$ be the restriction of $M$ to $\text{dom}\; M \setminus F$. Let $T_i = \lbrace \sigma : K_{N_i}(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - k \rbrace$. Note that $T_0$ is finite, so we can compute its measure. Now $\mu(\llbracket\text{dom}\; N_1\rrbracket) < 2^{-c+k}$, so $\mu(\llbracket T_1 \rrbracket) < 2^{-c}$. Since $S = T_0 \cup T_1$, we have $\mu(\llbracket S \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket T_0 \rrbracket) \leqslant \mu(\llbracket T_1 \rrbracket) < 2^{-c}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.3 — Schnorr)</span></p>

A set is Martin-Löf random iff it is 1-random.

*Proof.* ($\Rightarrow$) Let $U_k = \lbrace X : \exists n\; K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - k \rbrace$. The $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$, and by Lemma 6.2.2, $\mu(U_k) \leqslant 2^{-k}$, so $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. If $A$ is Martin-Löf random then $A \notin \bigcap_k U_k$, so there is a $k$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > n - k$ for all $n$, and thus $A$ is 1-random.

($\Leftarrow$) Let $A$ be a set that is not Martin-Löf random. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Martin-Löf test such that $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. Let $R_0, R_1, \ldots$ be a uniformly c.e. sequence of prefix-free sets such that $U_n = \llbracket R_n \rrbracket$ for all $n$. We have

$$\sum_{n \geqslant 2} \sum_{\sigma \in R_{n^2}} 2^{-(\lvert\sigma\rvert - n)} = \sum_{n \geqslant 2} 2^n \mu(U_{n^2}) \leqslant \sum_{n \geqslant 2} 2^{-n^2 + n} < \sum_{m \geqslant 2} 2^{-m} < 1.$$

So by the minimality of $K$ among information content measures (Theorem 3.7.8), there is a constant $c$ such that if $\sigma \in R_{n^2}$ for some $n \geqslant 2$ then $K(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - n + c$. Since $A \in U_{n^2}$ for all $n$, for each $n$ there is a $k$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright k) \leqslant k - n + c$, and hence $A$ is not 1-random.

</div>

The $\Leftarrow$ direction of the above proof is taken from Chaitin. It is also possible to prove this direction of the theorem using the KC Theorem; see for instance [118].

One quite important property of Martin-Löf's definition is the existence of a *universal* Martin-Löf test, in the following sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.4 — Martin-Löf)</span></p>

A Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is **universal** if $\bigcup_n U_n$ contains every Martin-Löf null set, in other words, for any Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, we have $\bigcap_n V_n \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

</div>

In other words, there *is* a single statistical test leading to a good definition of randomness for individual sequences. The Martin-Löf test built in the first part of the proof of Theorem 6.2.3 is universal. We give another construction of such a test in the following proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.5 — Martin-Löf)</span></p>

There exists a universal Martin-Löf test.

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace R^0_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, \lbrace R^1_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, \ldots$ be an effective listing of all uniformly c.e. subsets of $2^{<\omega}$. Let $S^i_n$ be the result of enumerating $R^i_n$ and stopping the enumeration if the measure of $\llbracket R^i_n \rrbracket$ ever threatens to exceed $2^{-n}$. Then $\lbrace \llbracket S^0_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, \lbrace \llbracket S^1_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, \ldots$ is an effective listing of all Martin-Löf tests. Let $U_n = \bigcup_i \llbracket S^i_{n+i+1} \rrbracket$.

The $U_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ and

$$\mu(U_n) = \sum_i \mu(\llbracket S^i_{n+i+1} \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-(n+i+1)} = 2^{-n},$$

so $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. For any Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, there is an $i$ such that $V_n = \llbracket S^i_n \rrbracket$ for all $n$. Then $V_{n+i+1} \in U_n$ for all $n$, so $\bigcap_n V_n \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

</div>

Note that if $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a universal Martin-Löf test then a set $A$ is 1-random iff $A \notin \bigcap_n U_n$. In particular, each complement $\overline{U_n}$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class consisting entirely of 1-random sets, a fact that will often be quite useful. (For instance, it implies that there are low 1-random sets, by the low basis theorem.) That there is no $\Pi^0_1$ class consisting exactly of the 1-random sets follows from the following result, since the only $\Pi^0_1$ class of measure 1 is $2^\omega$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.6 — Martin-Löf)</span></p>

The class of 1-random sets has measure 1.

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a universal Martin-Löf test. The class of 1-random sets is the complement of $\bigcap_n U_n$, and $\mu(\bigcap_n U_n) = 0$.

</div>

Solovay gave the following alternate definition of 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.7 — Solovay)</span></p>

A **Solovay test** is a sequence $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes such that $\sum_n \mu(S_n) < \infty$. A set $A$ is **Solovay random** if for every such test, $A$ is in only finitely many $S_n$.

Equivalently, $A$ is Solovay random if for all computable sequences $\lbrace I_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of intervals with rational endpoints such that $\sum_n \lvert I_n\rvert < \infty$, the set $A$ is in only finitely many $I_n$. Thus, we also refer to a sequence of intervals as a Solovay test.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.2.8 — Solovay)</span></p>

A set is Solovay random iff it is 1-random.

*Proof.* A Martin-Löf test is a Solovay test, so if a set is Solovay random then it is Martin-Löf random. Now suppose that $A$ is Martin-Löf random, and let $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Solovay test. There is an $m$ such that $\sum_{n > m} \mu(S_n) < 1$. Let $U_k = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : \exists^{\geqslant 2^k} n > m\; (\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket \in S_n) \rbrace \rrbracket$. The $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$, and it is not hard to see that $\mu(U_k) \leqslant 2^{-k} \sum_{n > m} \mu(S_n) < 2^{-k}$. Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test, and hence there is a $k$ such that $A \notin U_k$. So $A$ is in at most $2^k + m$ many $S_n$. Since $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ was an arbitrary Solovay test, $A$ is Solovay random.

</div>

### 6.3 The Unpredictability Paradigm

#### 6.3.1 Martingales and Supermartingales

It seems reasonable to say that most people would intuitively identify randomness with unpredictability. In a random process such as repeatedly tossing a fair coin, knowledge of the first $n$ tosses should be of no help for predicting the result of the $(n+1)$st toss. Similarly, if we are betting on the successive bits of a random sequence, we should not expect to be able to make much money, no matter what betting strategy we apply. We can formalize the notion of a betting strategy as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.1 — Levy)</span></p>

A function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ is a **martingale** if for all $\sigma$,

$$d(\sigma) = \frac{d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1)}{2}.$$

It is a **supermartingale** if for all $\sigma$,

$$d(\sigma) \geqslant \frac{d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1)}{2}.$$

A (super)martingale $d$ **succeeds** on a set $A$ if $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$. The collection of all sets on which $d$ succeeds is called the **success set** of $d$, and is denoted by $S[d]$.

</div>

The idea is that a martingale $d(\sigma)$ represents the capital that we have after betting on the bits of $\sigma$ while following a particular betting strategy ($d(\lambda)$ being our starting capital). The martingale condition $d(\sigma) = \frac{d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1)}{2}$ is a fairness condition, ensuring that the expected value of our capital after a bet is equal to our capital before the bet. (In the case of supermartingales, we are allowed to discard part of our capital, such as by buying drinks or tipping the dealer.)

Ville proved that the success sets of (super)martingales correspond precisely to the sets of measure 0. (This fact can be proved in the same way as Theorem 6.3.4 below, ignoring the effectivity of the notions involved in that result.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.2)</span></p>

1. If $d$ is a (super)martingale and $r \in \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$, then $rd$ is a (super)martingale.
2. If $d_0, d_1, \ldots$ are (super)martingales such that $\sum_n d_n(\lambda) < \infty$, then $\sum_n d_n$ is a (super)martingale.

</div>

The following result is known as Kolmogorov's Inequality, but is due to Ville.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.3 — Kolmogorov's Inequality, Ville)</span></p>

Let $d$ be a (super)martingale.

1. For any string $\sigma$ and any prefix-free set $S$ of extensions of $\sigma$, we have $\sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} d(\tau) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma)$.

2. Let $R_k = \lbrace \sigma : d(\sigma) \geqslant k \rbrace$. Then $\mu(\llbracket R_k \rrbracket) \leqslant \frac{d(\lambda)}{k}$.

*Proof.* To prove (i), it is enough to consider finite $S$. We proceed by induction. For the $n = 1$ case, if $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma$ then $2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} d(\tau) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma)$ follows easily from the definition of a (super)martingale.

Suppose (i) holds for sets of at most $n$ elements, and let $S$ have $n + 1$ many $\sim_d$-equivalence classes. Let $\nu$ be the longest string such that every element of $S$ extends $\nu$. For $i < 2$, let $S_i = \lbrace \tau \in S : \nu i \preccurlyeq \tau \rbrace$. Then for $i < 2$ we have $\lvert S_i\rvert \leqslant n$, so by the inductive hypothesis, $\sum_{\tau \in S_i} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} d(\tau) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\nu i\rvert} d(\nu i)$. Thus

$$\sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} d(\tau) \leqslant 2^{-(\lvert\nu\rvert + 1)}(d(\nu 0) + d(\nu 1)) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert} d(\nu) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma),$$

the last inequality following by the $n = 1$ case above.

To prove (ii), let $P \subseteq R_k$ be a prefix-free set such that $\llbracket P \rrbracket = \llbracket R_k \rrbracket$. By part (i) with $\sigma = \lambda$,

$$\mu(\llbracket P \rrbracket) = \sum_{\tau \in P} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\tau \in P} \frac{d(\tau)}{k} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} \leqslant \frac{d(\lambda)}{k}.$$

</div>

To define a notion of randomness using martingales, we have to restrict the class of martingales we consider. Schnorr did so by effectivizing the notion of martingale. We say that a (super)martingale $d$ is *computably enumerable* (or $\Sigma^0_1$) if it is a c.e. function in the sense of Definition 5.2.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.4 — Schnorr)</span></p>

A set is 1-random iff no c.e. (super)martingale succeeds on it.

*Proof.* We show that Martin-Löf tests and (super)martingales are essentially the same, thus effectivizing Ville's work mentioned above.

Let $d$ be a c.e. (super)martingale. Let $U_n = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : d(\sigma) \geqslant 2^n \rbrace \rrbracket$. The $U_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes, and $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ by Kolmogorov's Inequality. So $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. Moreover, $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$ iff $A \in S[d]$.

Conversely, let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Martin-Löf test. Let $R_0, R_1, \ldots$ be a uniformly c.e. sequence of prefix-free generators for this test. For each $n$, define $d_n$ as follows. Whenever a string $\sigma$ enters $R_n$, add 1 to the value of $d_n(\tau)$ for every $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma$ and add $2^{k - \lvert\sigma\rvert}$ to $d_n(\sigma \upharpoonright k)$ for each $k < \lvert\sigma\rvert$. It is easy to check that the $d_n$ are uniformly c.e. martingales and $d_n(\lambda) \leqslant 2^{-n}$. Thus $d = \sum_n d_n$ is a c.e. martingale. Moreover, $A \in S[d]$ iff $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$.

</div>

By applying the second part of the above proof to a universal Martin-Löf test, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.5 — Schnorr)</span></p>

There is a universal c.e. martingale, that is, a c.e. martingale $d$ such that for any c.e. martingale $f$, we have $S[f] \subseteq S[d]$.

</div>

As we have seen, not only is there a universal prefix-free machine, but in fact prefix-free complexity is minimal among information content measures. Given a martingale $d$, if $f$ is a constant multiple of $d$ then $S[f] = S[d]$, and in fact $d$ and $f$ behave essentially the same on all sequences. Thus martingales are in a sense really specified only up to constant multiples. This observation leads to the following definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.6)</span></p>

A c.e. (super)martingale $d$ is **optimal** if for each (super)martingale $f$ there is a constant $c$ such that $c d(\sigma) \geqslant f(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

We can strengthen Corollary 6.3.5 for supermartingales as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
