  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.7 — Schnorr, essentially Levin)</span></p>

There is an optimal c.e. supermartingale.

*Proof.* We can construct a computable enumeration $d_0, d_1, \ldots$ of all c.e. supermartingales up to multiplication by a constant as follows. First notice that we can list all c.e. functions $d$ such that $d(\sigma) \leqslant 2^{\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ for all $\sigma$. Then we can modify each such function $d$ by not allowing the approximation to $d(\sigma i)$ to increase while that increase threatens to violate the condition $d(\sigma) \geqslant \frac{d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1)}{2}$. Now the supermartingale $d(\sigma) = \sum_n 2^{-n} d_n(\sigma)$ is optimal.

</div>

We will see in Section 6.3.3 that this result does not hold for martingales in place of supermartingales.

We finish this section by noting that the use of limsup's in the definition of success for (super)martingales is not essential, as witnessed by the following result, sometimes known as the "savings trick". The name comes from the idea of waiting until we win, say, two dollars, then saving one dollar and betting the second dollar until we have three dollars, at which point we save two dollars and bet one, and so on. This idea can be turned into a formal construction, but we also give a different proof that is easier in the case of c.e. martingales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.8 — Folklore)</span></p>

Let $d$ be a (super)martingale. From $d$ we can effectively define a (super)martingale $\widehat{d}$ such that $S[\widehat{d}] = S[d]$ and for all $A$ we have $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$ iff $\lim_n \widehat{d}(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$. If $d$ is computable, we can make $\widehat{d}$ also be computable, and if $d$ is c.e., we can make $\widehat{d}$ also be c.e.

*Proof.* We first give a direct construction of $\widehat{d}$. We may assume that $d(\sigma) > 0$ for all $\sigma$. We define $\widehat{d}$ using an auxiliary "savings function" $s : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$, whose value at any string will be less than $\widehat{d}$. Let $\widehat{d}(\lambda) = d(\lambda)$ and $s(\lambda) = 0$. Given $\widehat{d}(\sigma)$ and $s(\sigma)$, for $i = 0, 1$, let $\widehat{d}(\sigma i) = s(\sigma) + (\widehat{d}(\sigma) - s(\sigma)) \frac{d(\sigma i)}{d(\sigma)}$. Intuitively, we bet $s(\sigma)$ much of our capital evenly, and copy $d$'s bet on the rest. Now, for each $i = 0, 1$, approximate $\widehat{d}(\sigma i) - s(\sigma)$ to within $\frac{1}{4}$, and if this approximation $r$ is greater than $\frac{3}{4}$, then let $s(\sigma i) = s(\sigma) + r - \frac{1}{4}$ (which ensures that $0 < \widehat{d}(\sigma i) - s(\sigma i) < 1$). Otherwise, let $s(\sigma i) = s(\sigma)$.

It is now easy to check that $\widehat{d}$ is a $d$-computable (super)martingale, and that $A \in S[d]$ iff $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$ iff $\lim_n s(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$ iff $\lim_n \widehat{d}(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$ iff $A \in S[\widehat{d}]$.

Note that the $\widehat{d}$ built in our first construction has the property that for all $\sigma$ and $\tau$, we have $\widehat{d}(\sigma\tau) \geqslant \widehat{d}(\sigma) - 2$, since the unsaved part of our capital never reaches 2. We will refer to this feature of $\widehat{d}$ as the **savings property**.

</div>

#### 6.3.2 Supermartingales and Continuous Semimeasures

Recall the notions of a continuous semimeasure and of the complexity measure $KM$ from Section 3.16. As discussed in that section, Levin directly constructed an optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure, but there is a natural correspondence between continuous semimeasures and supermartingales, so his result can be interpreted as a result about supermartingales.

More precisely, let $d$ be a supermartingale. Then $\delta(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) = 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma)$ is a continuous semimeasure, and this process can be reversed. Thus Schnorr's optimal c.e. supermartingale is equivalent to Levin's optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure $\delta$. The quantity $\delta(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket)$ is sometimes called the *a priori probability* of $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$.

In particular, we see that, since $KM(\sigma)$ is defined as $-\log \delta(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket)$ for a fixed optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure $\delta$, we have $KM(\sigma) = -\log d(\sigma) + \lvert\sigma\rvert$, where $d$ is the optimal c.e. supermartingale corresponding to $\delta$. Another way of obtaining $KM$ is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.9 — Uspensky)</span></p>

The a priori entropy $KM$ is (up to an additive constant) the smallest function $f$ that is approximable from above and such that $\sum_{\sigma \in M} 2^{-f(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$ for any prefix-free set $M$.

*Proof.* Clearly $KM$ is approximable from above. Let $d$ be an optimal c.e. supermartingale with $d(\lambda) = 1$. If $M$ is prefix-free then $\sum_{\sigma \in M} 2^{-KM(\sigma)} = \sum_{\sigma \in M} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma) \leqslant 1$ by Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3).

Now let $\delta$ be our fixed optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure. If $f$ satisfies the conditions of the proposition, then let $m(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket)$ be the maximum of $\sum_{\tau \in M} 2^{-f(\sigma\tau)}$ over all prefix-free sets $M$. Note that $m(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-f(\sigma)}$. Then $m$ is a c.e. continuous semimeasure, so $\delta(\sigma) \geqslant O(m(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket))$, and hence $KM(\sigma) = -\log \delta(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \leqslant -\log m(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) + O(1) \leqslant f(\sigma) + O(1)$.

</div>

One easy consequence of the supermartingale characterization of $KM$ is that $A$ is 1-random iff $KM(A \upharpoonright n) = n \pm O(1)$. The same is true for the related notions of monotone complexity and process complexity, which we introduced in Section 3.15.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.10 — Levin, Schnorr)</span></p>

A set $A$ is 1-random iff $Km(A \upharpoonright n) = Km_D(A \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1) = n \pm O(1)$.

*Proof.* If $Km(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ then $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$, so $A$ is 1-random. We now prove the converse. Since $Km(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant Km_D(A \upharpoonright n)$, it is enough to assume that $A$ is 1-random and show that $Km(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

Write $\mathcal{M}(\sigma)[s] = \tau$ to mean that at stage $s$, the machine $\mathcal{M}$ has read $\sigma$ and output $\tau$ so far. Let $U_k = \llbracket \lbrace \rho : \exists s\; \exists \sigma\; (\lvert\sigma\rvert \leqslant \lvert\rho\rvert - k \wedge \rho \preccurlyeq \mathcal{M}(\sigma)[s]) \rbrace \rrbracket$. Note that if $Km(\rho) \leqslant \lvert\rho\rvert - k$, then $\rho \in U_k$. The $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes. Let $R$ be a prefix-free set of generators for $U_k$. For each $\rho \in R$, let $\sigma_\rho$ be such that $\lvert\sigma_\rho\rvert \leqslant \lvert\rho\rvert - k$ and $\rho \preccurlyeq \mathcal{M}(\sigma_\rho)[s]$ for some $s$. By monotonicity, the set of $\sigma_\rho$ for $\rho \in R$ is prefix-free, so

$$\mu(U_k) = \sum_{\rho \in R} 2^{-\lvert\rho\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\rho \in R} 2^{-(\lvert\sigma_\rho\rvert + k)} = 2^{-k} \sum_{\rho \in R} 2^{-\lvert\sigma_\rho\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}.$$

Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test, so if $A$ is 1-random then there is a $k$ such that $A \notin U_k$, which implies that $Km(A \upharpoonright n) > n - k$ for all $n$.

</div>

One consequence of the above result is that all 1-random sets have the same initial segment complexity with respect to both monotone complexity and process complexity. Thus, if we are interested in relating initial segment complexity to levels of randomness, these complexities, like $KM$, are too coarse.

#### 6.3.3 Martingales and Optimality

In this section we show that Theorem 6.3.7 does not hold for martingales; that is, there is no optimal c.e. martingale. We begin by showing that the particular proof of Theorem 6.3.7 cannot work, as there is no computable enumeration of all c.e. martingales. (This result follows from Theorem 6.3.12 below, but is more easily proved directly.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.11 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is no computable enumeration of all c.e. martingales.

*Proof.* Suppose that $d_0, d_1, \ldots$ is a computable enumeration of all c.e. martingales. (We do not require that all approximations to each $d_i$ appear on the list, just that for every c.e. martingale $d$ there is an $i$ such that $d = d_i$.) From this enumeration we can produce an enumeration $f_0, f_1, \ldots$ of all c.e. martingales that are not the constant zero function as follows. Let $S = \lbrace i : \exists s\; d_i(\lambda)[s] > 0 \rbrace$. Since $S$ is c.e., we can enumerate its elements $s_0, s_1, \ldots$, and let $f_i = d_{s_i}$. Note that for each $i$ and $n$, since $f_i$ is a martingale (rather than just a supermartingale), there must be a $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $f_i(\sigma) > 0$.

We now derive a contradiction by defining a strictly positive c.e. martingale $d$ not equal to any $f_i$. In fact, $d$ will be a computable rational-valued martingale. For a string $\sigma \neq \lambda$, let $\sigma^- = \sigma \upharpoonright (\lvert\sigma\rvert - 1)$, that is, the string obtained by removing the last bit of $\sigma$, and let $\sigma^c = \sigma^-(1 - \sigma(\lvert\sigma\rvert))$, that is, the string obtained by flipping the last bit of $\sigma$.

Let $s$ be such that $f_0(\lambda)[s] > 0$ and let $d(\lambda) = \frac{f_0(\lambda)[s]}{2}$. Having defined $d$ on strings of length $n$, search for an $s$ and a $\sigma \in 2^{n+1}$ such that $f_{n+1}(\sigma)[s] > \frac{f_{n+1}(\sigma)[s]}{2}$. Let $d(\sigma) = \min(d(\sigma^-), \frac{f_{n+1}(\sigma)[s]}{2})$, let $d(\sigma^c) = 2d(\sigma^-) - d(\sigma)$, and for all other $\tau \in 2^{n+1}$ other than $\sigma$, let $d(\tau) = d(\tau^-)$.

It is straightforward to check that $d$ is a strictly positive c.e. martingale, but for each $n$ there is a $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $d(\sigma) < f_n(\sigma)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.12 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is no optimal c.e. martingale.

*Proof.* Let $d$ be a c.e. martingale. We build a c.e. martingale $f$ such that for each $c$ there is a $\sigma$ with $f(\sigma) \geqslant cd(\sigma)$, which implies that $d$ is not optimal. It is enough to build uniformly c.e. martingales $f_0, f_1, \ldots$ so that for each $n$ we have $f_n(\lambda) = 1$ and $f_n(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{2n} d(\sigma)$ for some $\sigma$, since we can then take $f = \sum_n 2^{-n} f_n$.

Given $n$, we begin by letting $f_n(\lambda) = 1$. Let $\sigma_0 = \lambda$. As long as $d(\lambda)[s] < 2^{-2n}$, we define $f_n(0^{s+1}) = 2^{s+1}$ and $f_n(\tau) = 0$ for all other strings of length $s + 1$. If we find an $s_0$ such that $d(\lambda)[s_0] \geqslant 2^{-2n}$, then we wait until a stage $t_0$ such that $\sum_{\tau \in 2^{s_0+1}} d(\tau)[t_0] \geqslant 2^{s_0 + 1 - 2n}$, which must occur because $d$ is a martingale. Let $i < 2$ be such that $d(0^{s_0} i)[t_0] \leqslant d(0^{s_0}(1-i))[t_0]$, and let $\sigma_1 = 0^{s_0} i$. Let $f_n(\sigma_1) = 2^{\lvert\sigma_1\rvert + s_1 + 1}$, and let $f_n(\tau) = 0$ for all other $\tau \in 2^{\lvert\sigma_1\rvert + s_1 + 1}$.

We now repeat the above procedure with $\sigma_1$ in place of $\sigma_0$, and so on. Each time we define $\sigma_i$, it is because $d(\sigma_{i-1}) \geqslant 2^{-2n} f_n(\sigma_{i-1})$. The latter implies that $d(\lambda) \geqslant (m + \frac{1}{2}) 2^{-2n}$, which cannot be the case for all $i$. So there is an $i$ such that $\sigma_i$ is never defined, which means that $d(\sigma_{i-1}) < 2^{-2n} f_n(\sigma_{i-1})$.

</div>

#### 6.3.4 Martingale Processes

As mentioned above, our concept of martingale is a simple special case of the original notion from probability theory, which we now discuss. A *$\sigma$-algebra* on $2^\omega$ is a collection of subsets of $2^\omega$ closed under complements and countable unions. For any collection $\mathcal{C}$ of subsets of $2^\omega$, there is a smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing $\mathcal{C}$, called the $\sigma$-algebra *generated* by $\mathcal{C}$. The $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}$ generated by the collection of basic clopen sets of $2^\omega$ is called the *Borel $\sigma$-algebra* on $2^\omega$. A *random variable* on $2^\omega$ is a function $X : 2^\omega \to \mathbb{R}$ that is $\mathcal{B}$-measurable. The *expectation* $E[X]$ of $X$ is its integral over $2^\omega$ with respect to $\mu$.

A function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}$ can be thought of as a sequence of random variables $X^d_0, X^d_1, \ldots$, where $X^d_n(\alpha) = d(\alpha \upharpoonright n)$. It is then easy to check that $d$ is a martingale iff it is nonnegative and for each $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$,

$$E[X^d_{\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1} \mid \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket] = d(\sigma).$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.13)</span></p>

A sequence of random variables $X_0, X_1, \ldots$ is a **martingale** if for every $n$ we have $E[X_n] < \infty$ and for every $r_0, \ldots, r_n \in \mathbb{R}$,

$$E[X_{n+1} \mid \lbrace \alpha : X_0(\alpha) = r_0, \ldots, X_n(\alpha) = r_n \rbrace] = r_n.$$

</div>

In other words, a martingale is a sequence of random variables such that the expectation of each random variable conditional on the observed values of the previous ones is equal to the observed value of the immediately preceding one.

Hitchcock and Lutz defined a function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}$ to be a *martingale process* if the corresponding random variables $X^d_0, X^d_1, \ldots$ form a martingale. It is easy to see that condition (6.1) implies condition (6.2), so every martingale is a martingale process.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.14 — Hitchcock and Lutz)</span></p>

Let $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ be a function. We write $\sigma \sim_d \tau$ if $\lvert\sigma\rvert = \lvert\tau\rvert$ and $d(\sigma \upharpoonright n) = d(\tau \upharpoonright n)$ for all $n \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert$. We write $s_d(\sigma)$ for the size of the $\sim_d$-equivalence class of $\sigma$. A function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ is a **martingale process** if for all $\sigma$,

$$s_d(\sigma) d(\sigma) = \sum_{\tau \sim_d \sigma} \frac{d(\tau 0) + d(\tau 1)}{2}.$$

</div>

As Hitchcock and Lutz remarked, in the fairness condition for martingales given in Definition 6.3.1, the average is taken over all sequences with the same *bit history*, while in the fairness condition for martingale processes, the average is taken over all sequences with the same *capital history*.

As with martingales, we say that a martingale process $d$ *succeeds* on a set $A$ if $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$, and the collection of all sets on which $d$ succeeds is denoted by $S[d]$. In addition to having its source in a probability-theoretic notion of martingale, the notion of martingale process is interesting to us because it provides a computable, rather than computably enumerable, characterization of 1-randomness. (See the discussion of "Schnorr's critique" in the introduction to Chapter 7.) Putting together results of Hitchcock and Lutz and Merkle, Mihailović, and Slaman, we now show that a set is 1-random iff no computable martingale process succeeds on it. We begin with an analog of Lemma 6.3.3, adapted from Hitchcock and Lutz.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.15)</span></p>

Let $d$ be a martingale process.

1. Let $B$ be a set of $\sim_d$-equivalent strings, and let $S$ be a prefix-free set of extensions of elements of $B$ such that $S$ is closed under $\sim_d$. Let $\sigma \in B$. Then

    $$\sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} d(\tau) \leqslant s_d(\sigma) 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma),$$

    where, as above, $s_d(\sigma)$ is the size of the $\sim_d$-equivalence class of $\sigma$.

2. Let $R_k = \lbrace \sigma : d(\sigma) \geqslant k \rbrace$. Then $\mu(\llbracket R_k \rrbracket) \leqslant \frac{d(\lambda)}{k}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.16 — Hitchcock and Lutz)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then no computable martingale process succeeds on $A$.

*Proof.* The proof is basically the same as that of the corresponding direction of Theorem 6.3.4: Let $d$ be a computable martingale process. Let $U_n = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : d(\sigma) > 2^n \rbrace \rrbracket$. The $U_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes, and $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ by Lemma 6.3.15. So $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. Moreover, $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$ iff $A \in S[d]$. So if $A$ is 1-random then $A \notin S[d]$.

</div>

We now turn to the converse of this result, beginning with an auxiliary lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.17 — Merkle, Mihailović, and Slaman)</span></p>

Let $C \subset 2^{<\omega}$ be a computable prefix-free set such that $\mu(\llbracket C \rrbracket) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$. We can effectively define a computable martingale process $d$ such that $d(\lambda) = 1$ and $d(\sigma) = 2$ for all $\sigma \in C$.

*Proof.* Let $D$ be the set of all extensions of elements of $C$. For $\sigma \in D$, let $d(\sigma) = 2$. For $\sigma \notin D$, let $b = \lvert 2^{\lvert\sigma\rvert} \setminus D\rvert$, let $c = \lvert C \cap 2^{\lvert\sigma\rvert}\rvert$, and let $d(\sigma) = 1 - \frac{c}{b}$, which must be nonnegative by our assumption that $\mu(\llbracket C \rrbracket) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$. It is easy to check by induction that $d$ is a martingale process.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.18 — Merkle, Mihailović, and Slaman)</span></p>

There is a computable martingale process that succeeds on every non-1-random set.

*Proof.* Let $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ be a universal Martin-Löf test. Let $V_\sigma = U_{\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1} \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$. Let $D_\sigma \subset 2^{<\omega}$ be uniformly computable prefix-free sets such that $V_\sigma = \llbracket\sigma D_\sigma\rrbracket$. Since $\mu(U_{\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1}) \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert - 1}$, we have $\mu(\llbracket D_\sigma \rrbracket) < \frac{1}{2}$, so there are uniformly computable martingale processes $\widetilde{d}_\sigma$ such that $\widetilde{d}_\sigma(\lambda) = 1$ and $\widetilde{d}_\sigma(\tau) = 2$ for all $\tau \in D_\sigma$. Let $d_\sigma$ be the martingale process defined by letting $d_\sigma(\sigma\rho) = \widetilde{d}_\sigma(\rho)$ and $d_\sigma(\nu) = 1$ if $\sigma \not\preccurlyeq \nu$.

We now use the $d_\sigma$ to define a computable martingale process $d$. It is easiest to describe the action of $d$ along a particular sequence $A$. At first $d$ copies $d_\lambda$. If there is an $n_0$ such that $d_\lambda(A \upharpoonright n_0) = 2$ (and hence $d(A \upharpoonright n_0) = 2$), then $d$ begins to copy $2 d_{A \upharpoonright n_0}$. If there is an $n_1$ such that $d_{A \upharpoonright n_0}(A \upharpoonright n_1) = 2$ (and hence $d(A \upharpoonright n_0) = 4$), then $d$ begins to copy $4 d_{A \upharpoonright n_1}$. In general, with $n_k$ having been defined, $d$ copies $2^{k+1} d_{A \upharpoonright n_k}$ until, if ever, there is an $n_{k+1}$ such that $d_{A \upharpoonright n_k}(A \upharpoonright n_{k+1}) = 2$ (and hence $d(A \upharpoonright n_0) = 2^{k+2}$). Then $d$ begins to copy $2^{k+2} d_{A \upharpoonright n_{k+1}}$.

For each $\tau \in 2^{<\omega}$, let $\sigma_\tau$ be such that $d(\tau)$ is defined as copying $d_{\sigma_\tau}$. It is easy to see by induction on the length of strings that $\sigma \mapsto d_\sigma$ is a computable function, and that if $\tau \sim_d \tau'$ then $\sigma_\tau = \sigma_{\tau'}$. Since the $d_\sigma$ are themselves computable martingale processes, we see that $d$ is a computable martingale process. Clearly, if $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$, then $d$ succeeds on $A$.

</div>

As a final note on computable martingale processes, we mention the following useful result. For a proof, see Hitchcock and Lutz.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.3.19 — Hitchcock and Lutz)</span></p>

For every computable martingale process $f$ and every $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a martingale process $d' : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}^{\geqslant 0}$ that is computable as a function from strings to rationals, so that $\lvert d(\sigma) - d'(\sigma)\rvert < \varepsilon$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

### 6.4 Relativizing Randomness

Like most computability-theoretic notions, 1-randomness can be relativized in a straightforward way. Given a set $X$, we say that $A$ is **1-random relative to $X$** if $K^X(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. We can also define a Martin-Löf test relative to $X$ in the same way as we defined an unrelativized Martin-Löf test, but with classes that are $\Sigma^0_1$ relative to $X$. The same proof as above shows that $A$ is 1-random relative to $X$ iff it passes every Martin-Löf test relative to $X$ iff no $X$-c.e. martingale succeeds on $X$.

Most other notions in the theory of algorithmic randomness can be similarly relativized, and we often do so below without further comment. There are a few situations, however, in which relativization is more problematic. See Chapter 15 for an example.

It is useful to note that the construction of a universal Martin-Löf test above can be easily adapted to produce a *universal oracle Martin-Löf test*, that is, a test $U^X_0, U^X_1, \ldots$ that is a universal Martin-Löf test relative to $X$ for all $X$.

### 6.5 Ville's Theorem

A **selection function**, or **selection rule**, is a partial function $f : 2^{<\omega} \to \lbrace \text{yes}, \text{no} \rbrace$. We think of $f$ as deciding whether or not to select the $(k+1)$st bit of a sequence based on the observed values of the first $k$ bits. For a sequence $\alpha$ and a selection function $f$, let $s_f(\alpha, n)$ be the $n$th number $k$ such that $f(\alpha \upharpoonright k) = \text{yes}$, if such a number exists. If $s_f(\alpha, n-1)$ is defined, let $S_f(\alpha, n) = \sum_{i < n} \alpha(s_f(\alpha, i))$. In other words, $S_f(\alpha, n)$ is the number of 1's among the first $n$ many bits of $\alpha$ selected by $f$. If $f$ is the function sending every string to yes, then we write $S(\alpha, n)$ for $S_f(\alpha, n)$. (So $S(\alpha, n)$ is the number of 1's among the first $n$ bits of $\alpha$.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.5.1 — Ville's Theorem)</span></p>

Let $E$ be any countable collection of selection functions. Then there is a sequence $\alpha = \alpha_0 \alpha_1 \ldots$ such that the following hold.

1. $\lim_n \frac{S(\alpha, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.
2. For every $f \in E$ that selects infinitely many bits of $\alpha$, we have $\lim_n \frac{S_f(\alpha, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.
3. For all $n$, we have $\frac{S(\alpha, n)}{n} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$.

</div>

Before proving the full theorem, we prove a finite version. Strictly speaking, the following result is not needed for the proof of Ville's Theorem we give, but it is helpful to the comprehension of the ideas of the proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.5.2 — Finite version of Ville's Theorem)</span></p>

Let $E$ be any finite collection of selection functions. Then there is a sequence $\alpha$ satisfying the conclusion of Theorem 6.5.1.

*Proof.* Without loss of generality, we assume that the function sending every string to yes is in $E$, and hence we need to meet only conditions (ii) and (iii) of Theorem 6.5.1. We build $\alpha$ in stages.

Given $\alpha \upharpoonright n$, let $C(n) = \lbrace f \in E : f(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = \text{yes} \rbrace$ and $\alpha_n = \lvert \lbrace j < n : C(j) = C(n) \rbrace \rvert \bmod 2$. That is, we let $\alpha_n$ be 1 iff the subset $C(n)$ of $E$ consisting of those functions that select the $n$th bit of $\alpha$ appears an even number of times among the $C(j)$ with $j \leqslant n$. Evidently, $\alpha$ satisfies (iii), since each 1 appearing in $\alpha$ is preceded by an occurrence of 0 that can be uniquely chosen to match it.

Now suppose that $\lvert \lbrace n : f(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = \text{yes} \rbrace \rvert = \infty$ and let $n_0 < n_1 < \cdots$ be a list of all $n$ such that $f(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = \text{yes}$. For any $C \in E$, if we take the (possibly finite) sequence $n_{i_0} < n_{i_1} < \cdots$ of all $n_i$ such that $C(n_i) = C$, we have $C(n_{i_j}) = 0$ for $j$ even and $C(n_{i_j}) = 1$ for $j$ odd, so for all $n$, the number of 1's among the first $n$ many bits of $\alpha$ selected by $f$ differs from the number of 0's among these bits by at most 1 for each $C \in E$. In other words, $\lvert \frac{n}{2} - S_f(\alpha, n) \rvert \leqslant 2^{\lvert E \rvert}$. Thus $\lim_n \frac{S_f(\alpha, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

</div>

The method above clearly fails when we try to use it in the case that $E$ is infinite. The problem is that (for example) each subset of $E$ might occur only once among the $C(n)$, making $\alpha = 0^\omega$. The proof of Ville's Theorem in the general case uses the construction of a *finite* subset $C(n)$ to determine $q_n$, together with a combinatorial trick to decide which of the $f \in E$ are allowed to be in $C(n)$. Roughly, $f_k$ is allowed to occur with $f_{k+m+1}$ only if it has occurred sufficiently often by itself, or with some of $f_1, \ldots, f_{k+m}$, as made precise below.

The key to the proof of Theorem 6.5.1 is the following lemma. Let $\mathcal{A}$ denote the class of infinite sequences of subsets of $\mathbb{N}$. For $A \in \mathcal{A}$, let $I^A_\ell = \lbrace n : \ell \in A(n) \rbrace$. For $\alpha \in 2^\omega$ and $I = \lbrace n_0 < n_1 < \cdots \rbrace \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, recall that $\alpha \upharpoonright I = \alpha(n_0)\alpha(n_1) \ldots$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.5.3)</span></p>

To each $A \in \mathcal{A}$ we can assign a sequence $\alpha$ such that the value of $\alpha(n)$ depends only on $A(0), \ldots, A(n)$; for any $\ell$ such that $I^A_\ell$ is infinite, $\lim_n \frac{S(\alpha \upharpoonright I^A_\ell, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$; and $\frac{S(\alpha, n)}{n} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$ for all $n$.

</div>

*Proof of Theorem 6.5.1 from Lemma 6.5.3.* Let $f_0, f_1, \ldots$ list the elements of $E$. We may assume that $f_0$ is the function sending every string to yes, so we need to meet only conditions (ii) and (iii) of Theorem 6.5.1. Let $A(n) = \lbrace i : f_i(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = \text{yes} \rbrace$ and let $\alpha(n)$ be as in the lemma. Then condition (iii) is satisfied by the lemma, as is condition (ii), since for any $f_i$ such that $f_i(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = \text{yes}$ for infinitely many $n$,

$$\lim_n \frac{S_{f_i}(\alpha, n)}{n} = \lim_n \frac{S(\alpha \upharpoonright I^A_i, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}.$$

*Proof of Lemma 6.5.3.* We first build a map from $\mathcal{A}$ into itself, denoting the image of $A$ by $A^\ast$. Each coordinate of $A^\ast$ will be a nonempty finite subset of the corresponding coordinate of $A$. In building $A^\ast$, we also define an auxiliary family of numbers $I(n)$.

Suppose that we have already defined $A^\ast(m)$ and $I(m)$ for all $m < n$ so that $A^\ast(m) = \lbrace j \in A(m) : j \leqslant I(m) \rbrace$. Let $I(n)$ be the least $i$ such that there is a $j \in A(n)$ with $\lvert \lbrace m < n : j \in A^\ast(m) \wedge I(m) = i \rbrace \rvert \leqslant 3^i$ and let

$$A^\ast(n) = \lbrace j \in A(n) : j \leqslant I(n) \rbrace.$$

The construction of $A^\ast(n)$ depends only on $\lbrace A(i) : i \leqslant n \rbrace$. Furthermore, for each $i$, we have $I(n) = i$ for at most $i \cdot 3^i$ many $n$.

From $A^\ast$ we get $\alpha$ by letting

$$\alpha(n) = \lvert \lbrace j < n : A^\ast(j) = A^\ast(n) \rbrace \rvert \bmod 2.$$

Clearly, $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ depends only on $A(0), \ldots, A(n)$. Furthermore, as with the finite construction, we can pair each $i$ such that $\alpha(i) = 1$ with a $j < i$ such that $\alpha(j) = 0$, so $\frac{S(\alpha, n)}{n} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$ for all $n$. To verify the remaining property in the lemma, fix an $\ell$ that occurs in infinitely many $A(n)$. We need to show that $\lim_n \frac{S(\alpha \upharpoonright I^A_\ell, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

Let $n_0 < n_1 < \cdots$ be a listing of $I^A_\ell$. Since $I(n) = \ell$ for at most finitely many $n$, it follows that $\ell \in A^\ast(n_m)$ for cofinitely many $m$. For each $i \geqslant \ell$, there are at most $3^i$ many occurrences of $i$ in $I(n_0), I(n_1), \ldots$ prior to the first occurrence of $i+1$ in that list.

Let $k$ be least such that for the least $m$ with $I(n_m) = k$, we have $\ell < I(n_i)$ for all $i \geqslant m$. Let

$$\gamma = A^\ast(n_m) A^\ast(n_{m+1}) \ldots$$

For $j \geqslant k$, let

$$\sigma(j) = A^\ast(n_i) A^\ast(n_{i+1}) A^\ast(n_{i+2}) \ldots A^\ast(n_{i+r}),$$

where $i$ is least such that $I(n_i) = j$ and $r$ is least such that $I(n_{i+r+1}) = j+1$. Note that $\gamma = \sigma(k)\sigma(k+1) \ldots$. Also, $A^\ast(i)$ is part of $\gamma$ for cofinitely many $i$ such that $\ell \in A(i)$.

For $i \geqslant k$, each of the sets appearing in $\sigma(i)$ is a subset of $\lbrace 0, \ldots, i \rbrace$, so there are at most $2^i$ many such sets. Therefore, since there are at least $3^i$ many occurrences of $i$ in $I(n_0), I(n_1), \ldots$ prior to the first occurrence of $i+1$, we see that $\gamma$ has the form $\sigma(k)\sigma(k+1)\sigma(k+2) \ldots$, where for all $m \geqslant 0$, the block $\sigma(k+m)$ has length at least $3^{k+m}$ and contains at most $2^{k+m}$ many distinct sets.

Let $m$ be such that $\gamma(0) = A^\ast(n_m)$, and let $\beta = \alpha \upharpoonright (I^A_\ell \cap [n_m, \infty))$. Since $\beta$ is a tail of $\alpha \upharpoonright I^A_\ell$, it is enough to show that $\lim_n \frac{S(\beta, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

Let $j$ be sufficiently large so that there is an $m(j)$ such that $\gamma(j)$ is within $\sigma(k + m(j) + 1)$. Let $N_0(j)$ be the number of 0's in $\beta \upharpoonright j$ and $N_1(j)$ the number of 1's in $\beta \upharpoonright j$. Since the block $\sigma(k + m(j))$ has at least $3^{k+m(j)}$ many coordinates, $N_0(j) + N_1(j) \geqslant 3^{k+m(j)}$.

Let $p$ be the length of the initial segment of $\alpha \upharpoonright I^A_\ell$ missing from $\beta$. Then $N_1(j) \leqslant N_0(j) + p$, whence

$$N_1(j) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}(N_0(j) + N_1(j) + p).$$

There are at most $2^{k+i}$ distinct sets in $\sigma(k+i)$, and this number bounds the number of unmatched 0's in that block. Therefore,

$$N_0(j) \leqslant N_1(j) + \sum_{i \leqslant m(j)+1} 2^{k+i} \leqslant N_1(j) + 2^{k+m(j)+2}.$$

Thus,

$$N_1(j) \geqslant \frac{1}{2}(N_0(j) + N_1(j) - 2^{k+m(j)+2}).$$

We finish by evaluating $R(j) = \frac{N_1(j)}{N_0(j) + N_1(j)}$. Clearly, if $\lim_j R(j) = \frac{1}{2}$ then $\lim_j \frac{S(\beta, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$. The upper bound gives

$$R(j) \leqslant \frac{N_0(j) + N_1(j) + p}{2(N_0(j) + N_1(j))},$$

which has limit $\frac{1}{2}$. The lower bound gives

$$R(j) \geqslant \frac{N_0(j) + N_1(j) - 2^{k+m(j)+2}}{2(N_0(j) + N_1(j))} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{2^{k+m(j)+2}}{N_0(j) + N_1(j)},$$

which converges to $\frac{1}{2}$, as $N_0(j) + N_1(j) \geqslant 3^{k+m(j)}$.

Lieb, Osherson, and Weinstein made the following observations about their proof. Let $h$ be the function sending every string to yes. Choose a selection function $f_\ell$ that selects infinitely many bits of $\alpha$, and define the **fluctuation about the mean** to be

$$\delta_\ell(n) = S_{f_\ell}(\alpha, n) - \frac{1}{2}.$$

By the reasoning above we have seen that $\delta_\ell$ is bounded by an $\ell$-dependent constant. This property mimics the behavior of $h$, whose fluctuation is never positive. Furthermore, using the reasoning above, there is a number $C_\ell \geqslant 0$ such that $\delta_\ell(n) \geqslant -C_\ell n^{\frac{\log 2}{\log 3}}$. The 3 occurs because of the use of $3^i$ in the proof, and an arbitrary number $r$ could have been used instead of 3, yielding the conclusion that for each $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a constant $C_\ell(\varepsilon) \geqslant 0$ such that for every $n$,

$$\delta_\ell(n) \geqslant -C_\ell(\varepsilon) n^\varepsilon.$$

### 6.6 The Ample Excess Lemma

While the definition of a set $A$ being 1-random requires only that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$, there is a "gap phenomenon" that ensures that, in fact, the initial segment complexity of $A$ must be quite a bit higher than this lower bound.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.1 — Ample Excess Lemma, Miller and Yu)</span></p>

A set $A$ is 1-random iff $\sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} < \infty$.

*Proof.* If $A$ is not 1-random then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) < n$, so $\sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} = \infty$.

For the other direction, note that for any $m$,

$$\sum_{\sigma \in 2^m} \sum_{n \leqslant m} 2^{n - K(\sigma \upharpoonright n)} = \sum_{\sigma \in 2^m} \sum_{\tau \prec \sigma} 2^{\lvert\tau\rvert - K(\tau)} = \sum_{\tau \in 2^{\leqslant m}} 2^{m - \lvert\tau\rvert} 2^{\lvert\tau\rvert - K(\tau)} = 2^m \sum_{\tau \in 2^{\leqslant m}} 2^{-K(\tau)} < 2^m.$$

Therefore, for each $c$ there are at most $2^{m-c}$ strings $\sigma \in 2^m$ for which $\sum_{n \leqslant m} 2^{n - K(\sigma \upharpoonright n)} \geqslant 2^c$. Thus $\mu(\lbrace A : \sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} \geqslant 2^c \rbrace) \leqslant 2^{-c}$, and hence $U_c = \lbrace A : \sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} \geqslant 2^c \rbrace$ has measure at most $2^{-c}$. Furthermore, the $U_c$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$, and hence form a Martin-Löf test. If $A$ is 1-random then there is a $c$ such that $A \notin U_c$. Then $\sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} < 2^c$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.2 — Chaitin)</span></p>

A set $A$ is 1-random iff $\lim_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - n = \infty$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.3 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Let $A$ be 1-random and let $f$ be a function such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \infty$. Then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > n + f(n) - O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.4 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then $K^A(n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n + O(1)$.

*Proof.* Let $d$ be such that $\sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} < d$. Let

$$S = \lbrace (c + d, n) : \exists s\; (K_s(A \upharpoonright n) - n < c) \rbrace.$$

The set $S$ is c.e. relative to $A$ and $\sum_{(c+d,n) \in S} 2^{-(c+d)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n) - d} < 1$. Furthermore, for each $n$, we have $(K(A \upharpoonright n) - n + d + 1, n) \in S$. Thus, by the relativized form of the KC Theorem, $K^A(n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n + O(1)$.

</div>

Using Corollaries 3.11.3 and 6.6.2, we can prove the result mentioned in Section 3.8 that there are many strings that are weakly $K$-random yet highly non-$C$-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.5)</span></p>

There are infinitely many strings $\sigma$ such that $K(\sigma) \geqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert$ but $C(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - \log \lvert\sigma\rvert$.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be 1-random. By Corollary 3.11.3, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - \log n$. By Corollary 6.6.2, for all but finitely many such $n$, we have $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.6.6 — Merkle)</span></p>

If $A = \sigma_0 \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \ldots$ and $K(\sigma_i) < \lvert\sigma_i\rvert$ for all $i$, then $A$ is not 1-random.

*Proof.* Let $M$ be the prefix-free machine that, on input $\tau$, searches for $n$ and a partition $\tau = \rho \nu_0 \ldots \nu_{n-1}$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\rho) \!\downarrow = n$ and $\mathcal{U}(\nu_i) \!\downarrow$ for all $i < n$, and if these are found, outputs $\mathcal{U}(\nu_0) \ldots \mathcal{U}(\nu_{n-1})$. Then for each $n$ we have $M(n^\ast \sigma_0^\ast \ldots \sigma_{n-1}^\ast) = \sigma_0 \ldots \sigma_{n-1}$, so

$$K(\sigma_0 \ldots \sigma_{n-1}) \leqslant \lvert n^\ast \sigma_0^\ast \ldots \sigma_{n-1}^\ast \rvert + O(1) = K(n) + \sum_{i < n} K(\sigma_i) \leqslant 2\log n + \lvert\sigma_0 \ldots \sigma_{n-1}\rvert - n + O(1).$$

Thus for each $k$ there is an $m$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright m) \leqslant m - k$, and hence $A$ is not 1-random.

</div>

### 6.7 Plain Complexity and Randomness

Though the characterizations of 1-random sets in terms of initial segment complexities such as monotone and prefix-free complexity are satisfying, they raise the natural question, which remained open for nearly forty years, of whether there is a plain complexity characterization of 1-randomness. Miller and Yu have given such a characterization, which we now present, along with characterizations that mix plain and prefix-free complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.7.1 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Define a computable function $G : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ by

$$G(n) = \begin{cases} K_{s+1}(t) & \text{if } n = 2^{\langle s,t \rangle} \text{ and } K_{s+1}(t) \neq K_s(t) \\ n & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

The function $G$ is a variation on Solovay's Solovay function from the proof of Theorem 3.12.1. Note that

$$\sum_n 2^{-G(n)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-n} + \sum_t \sum_{m \geqslant K(t)} 2^{-m} = 2 + 2\sum_t 2^{-K(t)} < \infty.$$

Furthermore, if $n = 2^{\langle s,t \rangle}$ for the least $s$ such that $K_{s+1}(t) = K(t)$, then $G(n) = K(t)$, so we can think of $G$ as an infinitely often correct computable simulation of $K$ that grows fairly fast (cf. Theorem 3.12.1).

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.7.2 — Miller and Yu, Gács)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is 1-random.
2. $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$.
3. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$.
4. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$ for every computable function $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$.
5. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - G(n) - O(1)$.

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii): Let $U_k = \lbrace A \mid \exists n\; C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) < n - K(n) - k \rbrace$. Then $A \in U_k$ iff $\exists n \exists s\; C_s(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) + K_s(n) < n - k$, so the $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$. Furthermore,

$$\mu(U_k) \leqslant \sum_n \mu(\lbrace A : C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) < n - K(n) - k \rbrace) \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-n} 2^{n - K(n) - k} = 2^{-k} \sum_n 2^{-K(n)} \leqslant 2^{-k}.$$

Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. If $A$ is 1-random, then there is a $k$ such that $A \notin U_k$, whence $\forall n\; C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \geqslant n - K(n) - k$.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii): Immediate.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (iv): Let $g$ be a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$. By the minimality of $K$ among information content measures (Theorem 3.7.8), we have $K(n) \leqslant g(n) + O(1)$, so if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$ then $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$.

(iv) $\Rightarrow$ (v): Immediate since $G$ is computable and $\sum_n 2^{-G(n)} < \infty$.

(v) $\Rightarrow$ (i): Assume that $A$ is not 1-random. We show that (v) does not hold. By Theorem 3.7.6, there is a $c$ such that for all $t$ and $k$,

$$\lvert \lbrace \sigma \in 2^t \mid K(\sigma) \leqslant t - k \rbrace \rvert \leqslant 2^{t - K(t) - k + c}.$$

We construct a (non-prefix-free) machine $M$ that gives relatively short descriptions of $A \upharpoonright n$ when $n = 2^{\langle s,t \rangle}$ and $s$ is least such that $K_{s+1}(t) = K(t)$. For $s, t \in \omega$, let $n = 2^{\langle s,t \rangle}$. To $\langle s, t \rangle$ we devote the $M$-programs with lengths from $\frac{n}{2} + c + 1$ to $n + c$. Note that distinct pairs do not compete for elements in the domain of $M$.

Fix $k \in \omega$ and let $m = n - K_{s+1}(t) - k + c$. Note that $m \leqslant n + c$. If $m \geqslant \frac{n}{2} + c + 1$, then for each $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $K(\sigma \upharpoonright t) \leqslant t - k$, we try to pick a $\tau \in 2^m$ and define $M(\tau) = \sigma$. Different $k$ do not compete for programs, but it is still possible that there are not enough strings of length $m$ for all such $\sigma$, so we assign programs until we run out.

If $K_{s+1}(t) = K(t)$, then $G(n) = K(t)$ and $n, k, m$ are as above, then as long as $m \geqslant \frac{n}{2} + c + 1$, for every $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $K(\sigma \upharpoonright t) \leqslant t - k$, we have $C_M(\sigma) \leqslant m$.

The point of the definition of $M$ is that if $K_{s+1}(t) = K(t)$ and $n, k, m$ are as above, then as long as $m \geqslant \frac{n}{2} + c + 1$, for every $\sigma \in 2^n$ such that $K(\sigma \upharpoonright t) \leqslant t - k$, we have $C_M(\sigma) \leqslant m$.

Fix $k$. Since $A$ is not 1-random, there is a $t$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright t) \leqslant t - k$. We may assume that $t$ is large enough so that $K(t) \leqslant 2^{t-1} - k - 1$. Let $s$ be least such that $K_{s+1}(t) = K(t)$, let $n = 2^{\langle s,t \rangle}$, and let $m = n - K(t) - k + c$. Then $m \geqslant n - 2^{t-1} + k + 1 - k + c \geqslant \frac{n}{2} + c + 1$, because $n \geqslant 2^t$. Thus

$$C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C_M(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1) \leqslant m + O(1) = n - G(n) - k + c + O(1) \leqslant n - G(n) - k + O(1),$$

where the constant is independent of $n$ and $k$. Because $k$ is arbitrary, (v) does not hold.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.7.3 — Bienvenu, Merkle, and Nies)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a co-c.e. function $g$.

1. $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$ and $\liminf_n g(n) - K(n) < \infty$.
2. A set $A$ is 1-random iff $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$.

</div>

### 6.8 $n$-randomness

There appear to be three natural ways to extend Martin-Löf's definition to obtain a notion of $n$-randomness for $n > 1$. One is simply to replace the $\Sigma^0_1$ classes in his definition by $\Sigma^0_n$ classes. Another is to notice the importance of the fact that the components of a Martin-Löf test are open, and hence replace them by *open* $\Sigma^0_n$ classes. The third is to view $\Sigma^0_1$ classes as generated by c.e. subsets of $2^{<\omega}$, and hence replace them by classes generated by $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ classes, hence equating $n$-randomness with 1-randomness relative to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. Fortunately, while these approaches may not yield the same tests, they do yield the same notion of randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.1 — Kurtz)</span></p>

For each $n > 0$, the set $C_n = \lbrace (i, q) : q \in \mathbb{Q} \wedge \mu(S^n_i) > q \rbrace$ is $\Sigma^0_n$. Thus, the measures $\mu(S^n_i)$ are uniformly $\emptyset^{(n)}$-computable.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.2)</span></p>

Let $f = \Phi^{\emptyset'}_e$ be a total function. Then $\bigcup_i S^1_{f(i)}$ is a $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset'}_1$ class, and an index for this class can be obtained effectively from $e$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.3 — Kurtz, Kautz)</span></p>

1. From an index of a $\Sigma^0_n$ class $S$ and $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, we can compute an index of a $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ class $U \supseteq S$ such that $\mu(U) - \mu(S) < q$.
2. From an index of a $\Pi^0_n$ class $P$ and $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, we can compute an index of a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ class $V \subseteq S$ such that $\mu(S) - \mu(V) < q$.
3. From an index of a $\Sigma^0_n$ class $S$ and $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, we can $\emptyset^{(n)}$-compute an index of a closed $\Pi^0_{n-1}$ class $V \subseteq S$ such that $\mu(S) - \mu(V) < q$. Moreover, if $\mu(P)$ is $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computable then an index of $V$ can be found $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computably.
4. From an index of a $\Pi^0_n$ class $P$ and $q \in \mathbb{Q}$, we can $\emptyset^{(n)}$-compute an index of an open $\Sigma^0_{n-1}$ class $U \supseteq P$ such that $\mu(U) - \mu(P) < q$. Moreover, if $\mu(P)$ is $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computable then an index of $U$ can be found $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computably.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.4 — Kurtz)</span></p>

1. A $\Sigma^0_n$ *(Martin-Löf) test* is a sequence $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^0_n$ classes such that $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.
2. A set $A$ is **$n$-random** (or $\Sigma^0_n$-random) if for every $\Sigma^0_n$ test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_n U_n$.
3. We can similarly define $\Pi^0_n$ tests, $\Delta^0_n$ tests, and so on, and associated notions of randomness.
4. A set $A$ is **arithmetically random** if it is $n$-random for all $n$.

</div>

Notice that a $\Sigma^0_1$ test is a Martin-Löf test. As usual, we can relativize the notion of $n$-randomness to any oracle $X$ by using $\Sigma^{0,X}_n$ classes in place of $\Sigma^0_n$ classes. An *open $\Sigma^0_n$ test* is a $\Sigma^0_n$ test whose components are open.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.5 — Kautz)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. The set $A$ is $n$-random.
2. For every open $\Sigma^0_n$ test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_n U_n$.
3. The set $A$ is 1-random relative to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$.

*Proof.* Obviously (i) implies (ii), and (ii) implies (iii) because every $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ class is an open $\Sigma^0_n$ class. So it is enough to show that $\neg$(i) implies $\neg$(iii). Suppose there is a $\Sigma^0_n$ test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. By Theorem 6.8.3, for each $n$, we can effectively obtain a $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ class $V_n \supseteq U_{n+1}$ such that $\mu(V_n) \leqslant \mu(U_{n+1}) + 2^{-(n+1)} \leqslant 2^{-n}$. Then $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test relative to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$, and $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$.

</div>

It is typically most useful to think of $n$-randomness as 1-randomness relative to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$, as we can then apply the relativized form of results obtained for 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.8.6 — Kautz, after Schnorr)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is $n$-random.
2. $K^{\emptyset^{(n-1)}}(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.
3. No $\Sigma^0_n$ (super)martingale succeeds on $A$.

</div>

We can define a relativized version of $\Omega$ by letting $\Omega^X$ be the halting probability of $\mathcal{U}^X$, a universal oracle monotone machine. We can then define $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(n-1)}}$ is a somewhat natural example of an $n$-random real. Becher and Grigorieff gave a number of examples of $n$-random reals based on output probabilities of (unrelativized) universal monotone machines.

### 6.9 Van Lambalgen's Theorem

In this section, we introduce a fundamental tool in the study of $n$-randomness. Suppose we have a random sequence. Intuitively, we would expect that if we pick out two subsequences that do not share elements, then these subsequences should be highly independent. Conversely, if we put together two sequences that are random relative to each other, we would expect the result to remain random. Van Lambalgen's Theorem confirms these intuitions for $n$-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.1 — van Lambalgen)</span></p>

If $A \oplus B$ is $n$-random then $A$ and $B$ are relatively $n$-random.

*Proof.* We do the $n = 1$ case. The general case follows by relativizing the proof to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. Suppose that $A \oplus B$ is 1-random. We show that $B$ is 1-random relative to $A$, the other direction being symmetric.

Suppose that $B$ is not 1-random relative to $A$. Let $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ be a universal oracle Martin-Löf test. Then $B \in \bigcap_i U^A_i$. Let $V_i = \lbrace \llbracket \sigma \oplus \tau \rrbracket : \tau \in U^{2i}_{2i} \wedge \lvert\sigma\rvert = i \rbrace$. The $V_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes because $\mu(U^X_{2i}) \leqslant 2^{-2i}$ for all $X$. But $A \oplus B \in V_i$, since there are infinitely many initial segments $\sigma \prec A$ and $\tau \prec B$ with $\sigma \oplus \tau$ in some $U_{2i}$. So $A \oplus B$ is not 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.2 — van Lambalgen)</span></p>

If $A$ is $n$-random and $B$ is $n$-random relative to $A$, then $A \oplus B$ is $n$-random.

*Proof.* This proof is due to Nies. Suppose that $A \oplus B$ is not $n$-random. We show that either $A$ is not $n$-random or $B$ is not $n$-random relative to $A$. Let $V_0 \supseteq V_1 \supseteq \cdots$ be uniformly $\Sigma^0_n$ open sets such that $A \oplus B \in \bigcap_i V_i$ and $\mu(V_i) \leqslant 2^{-2i}$, which exist by Corollary 6.8.5. We write $\llbracket \sigma \oplus \tau \rrbracket$ for the class of sets $X = X_0 \oplus X_1$ such that $\sigma \prec X_0$ and $\tau \prec X_1$.

Let $S_i$ be the open set generated by $\lbrace \sigma : \mu(V_i \cap \llbracket \sigma \oplus \lambda \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-i - \lvert\sigma\rvert} \rbrace$. Clearly, $S_{i+1} \subseteq S_i$, and the $S_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_n$ open sets. Let $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ be a prefix-free set of generators for $S_i$. Since the sets $V_i \cap \llbracket \sigma_j \oplus \lambda \rrbracket$ are pairwise disjoint and $\mu(V_i) \leqslant 2^{-2i}$, we have $\sum_j 2^{-i - \lvert\sigma_j\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-2i}$, and hence $\mu(S_i) \leqslant \sum_j 2^{-\lvert\sigma_j\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-i}$. Thus, if $A \in \bigcap_i S_i$ then $A$ is not $n$-random.

Otherwise, there is a $j$ such that $A \notin S_i$ for all $i > j$. For each such $i$, let $R^k_i$ be the open set generated by $\lbrace \tau \in 2^k : \llbracket (A \upharpoonright k) \oplus \tau \rrbracket \subseteq V_i \rbrace$. Then $\mu(R^k_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$, since $A \notin S_i$. Moreover, since $V_i$ is open, $R^k_i \subseteq R^{k+1}_i$. Let $R_i = \bigcup_k R^k_i$. The $R_i$ are open and uniformly $\Sigma^0_n$ relative to $A$, and $\mu(R_i) = \sup_k \mu(R^k_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$. Furthermore, $B \in R_i$ for each $i > j$, so $B$ is not $n$-random relative to $A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.3 — van Lambalgen)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A \oplus B$ is $n$-random.
2. $A$ is $n$-random and $B$ is $n$-random relative to $A$.
3. $B$ is $n$-random and $A$ is $n$-random relative to $B$.
4. $A$ and $B$ are relatively $n$-random.

In particular, if $A$ and $B$ are both $n$-random, then $A$ is $n$-random relative to $B$ iff $B$ is $n$-random relative to $A$.

</div>

The last part of van Lambalgen's Theorem may seem counterintuitive, as it says that if $A$ is $n$-random, then there is no way to build a set $B$ that is $n$-random relative to $A$ without at the same time making $A$ be $n$-random relative to $B$. It implies for instance that if $A$ is $\Delta^0_2$ and 1-random, then $A$ is 1-random relative to $B$, and also that every $n$-random set is $n$-random relative to measure 1 many sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.4)</span></p>

If $A \oplus B$ is 1-random, then $A \mid_T B$, so $A, B <_T A \oplus B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.5 — Kurtz)</span></p>

No minimal degree is 1-random.

</div>

Given an infinite and coinfinite set $X$, let $x_0 < x_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $X$, let $y_0 < y_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $\overline{X}$, and define $A \oplus_X B$ to be $\lbrace x_n : n \in A \rbrace \cup \lbrace y_n : n \in B \rbrace$. The proof of van Lambalgen's Theorem clearly works for $A \oplus_X B$ in place of $A \oplus B$ for any computable $X$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.9.6)</span></p>

If $A$ is $n$-random then so is the $i$th column $A^{[i]}$ of $A$.

</div>

### 6.10 Effective 0-1 Laws

In this section, we prove effective versions of measure-theoretic 0-1 laws such as Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law (Theorem 1.2.4).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.10.1 — Kučera)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a $\Pi^0_1$ class of positive measure. If $A$ is 1-random then some translate of $A$ lies in $P$. That is, there are $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $B \in P$ such that $A = \sigma B$.

*Proof.* Suppose that for every $\sigma$ and $B$, if $A = \sigma B$ then $B \notin P$. Let $W \subset 2^{<\omega}$ be a prefix-free c.e. set such that $\overline{P} = \llbracket W \rrbracket$. Let $S_0 = W$ and $S_{n+1} = \lbrace \sigma\tau : \sigma \in S_n \wedge \tau \in W \rbrace$. Let $U_n = \llbracket S_n \rrbracket$. The $U_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$. Furthermore, $\mu(U_0) < 1$ and $\mu(U_{n+1}) = \mu(U_n)\mu(U_0) = \mu(U_0)^{n+2}$. Thus we can pick a Martin-Löf test $U_{n_0}, U_{n_1}, \ldots$. Since we are assuming that if $A = \sigma B$ then $B \in \overline{P}$, we have $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. Thus $A$ is not 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.10.2 — Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n > 0$ and let $P$ be a $\Pi^0_n$ class of positive measure. If $A$ is $n$-random then there are $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $B \in P$ such that $A = \sigma B$. Thus, $P$ contains a member of each $n$-random degree.

*Proof.* By Theorem 6.8.3, there is a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ class $V \subseteq P$ of positive measure. By the relativized form of Lemma 6.10.1, there are $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $B \in V \subseteq P$ such that $A = \sigma B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.10.3 — Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n > 0$ and let $C$ be a $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ or $\Pi^0_{n+1}$ class that is degree invariant, or even just closed under translations. Then $C$ contains either all $n$-random sets or no $n$-random sets.

*Proof.* It is enough to prove the result for a $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ class $C$, since the result for a $\Pi^0_{n+1}$ class then follows by taking its complement. Let $A$ be $n$-random. If $C$ has measure 1, then it is a union of $\Pi^0_n$ classes, at least one of which has positive measure. By Theorem 6.10.2, this class must contain a translation of $A$, and hence $C$ must contain $A$. If $C$ has measure 0, then it is a union of $\Pi^0_n$ classes of measure 0. For any such class $P$, using Theorem 6.8.3, we can $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computably find $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ classes $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ such that $\mu(U_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$ and $P \subseteq \bigcap_i U_i$. This is a Martin-Löf test relative to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. Thus $A \notin \bigcap_i U_i$, and hence $A \notin P$. So $A \notin C$.

</div>

### 6.11 Infinitely Often Maximally Complex Sets

One might naturally wonder whether there is a plain complexity characterization of 1-randomness simpler than the ones in Theorem 6.7.2. We have seen that we cannot have $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for *all* $n$, so the following weakening of this condition is a natural candidate.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.1)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **infinitely often $C$-random** if there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

</div>

Martin-Löf noted that being infinitely often $C$-random implies being 1-random. An easy proof of this fact was found by Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn: By Proposition 3.10.4, we know that $C(\sigma\tau) \leqslant K(\sigma) + C(\tau) + \lvert\tau\rvert + O(1)$. If $A$ is not 1-random, then for each $d$ there is an $n$ with $K(A \upharpoonright n) < n - d$. If $m > n$ then, by the above, $C(A \upharpoonright m) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) + (m - n) < m - d$. Since $d$ is arbitrary, $A$ is not infinitely often $C$-random.

As we will see, the notion of being infinitely often $C$-random turns out to be too strong to capture 1-randomness. In fact, it is a plain complexity characterization of 2-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.2 — after Martin-Löf, see Li and Vitányi)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. The set $A$ is infinitely often $C$-random.
2. There are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

*Proof.* Obviously (ii) implies (i). Now suppose that there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. We have

$$C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) + 2\log(\lvert n - C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n)\rvert) + O(1),$$

since the $2\log(\lvert n - C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n)\rvert)$ term allows room for a description of $n$ given $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n)$. So there are infinitely many $n$ such that

$$n - C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant 2\log(\lvert n - C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n)\rvert) + O(1).$$

For such $n$, we have $n - C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant O(1)$.

</div>

To show that being infinitely often $C$-random is equivalent to being 2-random, Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn began with the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.3 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

If a set is infinitely often $C$-random then it is 2-random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is not 2-random. Then there are $n_0, n_1, \ldots$ such that $K^{\emptyset'}(A \upharpoonright n_i) < n_i - i$ for all $i$. Let $\sigma_i$ be a minimal length $\mathcal{U}^{\emptyset'}$-program for $A \upharpoonright n_i$. Let $s_i > n_i$ be such that $\mathcal{U}^{\emptyset'}(\sigma_i)[s_i] \!\downarrow$ with $\emptyset'$-correct use. (That is, $\upharpoonright u = \emptyset' \upharpoonright u$.)

Let $M$ be a Turing machine defined as follows. On input $\tau$, first $M$ searches for $\sigma$ and $\mu$ such that $\sigma\mu = \tau$ and $\mathcal{U}^{\emptyset'}(\sigma)[\lvert\tau\rvert] \!\downarrow$. If there is such a $\sigma$ (which must then be unique), let $\nu = \mathcal{U}^{\emptyset'}(\sigma)[\lvert\tau\rvert]$. Then $M$ outputs $\nu\mu$.

Fix $i$ and let $t \geqslant s_i$. Let $\mu = A(n_i) \ldots A(t-1)$. Then $M(\sigma_i\mu) \!\downarrow = (A \upharpoonright n_i)\mu = A \upharpoonright t$. So $C(A \upharpoonright t) \leqslant \lvert\sigma_i\mu\rvert < t - i + O(1)$. Since for each $i$ this fact holds for all sufficiently large $t$, we conclude that $A$ is not infinitely often $C$-random.

</div>

Miller proved the converse to Theorem 6.11.3, thus establishing the equivalence between being infinitely often $C$-random and being 2-random. Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn obtained the same result independently, with a somewhat simpler proof that we now present.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.4 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

A **compression function** is a one-to-one function $F : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\lvert F(\sigma)\rvert \leqslant C(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.5 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

There is a low compression function.

*Proof.* Let $\mathcal{V}$ be the universal Turing machine relative to which we define $C$. Let $P$ be the $\Pi^0_1$ class of graphs of partial functions extending $\mathcal{V}$. Applying the low basis theorem, let $A$ be a low element of $P$ and let $f$ be the partial function determined by $A$. Let $F(\sigma)$ be the length-lexicographically least $\tau$ such that $f(\tau) = \sigma$. Note that such a $\tau$ always exists because $\mathcal{V}$ extends $f$. Furthermore, $F \leqslant_T A$, so $F$ is low, and $F(\sigma)$ is at least as short as the shortest $\mathcal{V}$-program for $\sigma$, so $\lvert F(\sigma)\rvert \leqslant C(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.6 — Miller; Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

A set is 2-random iff it is infinitely often $C$-random.

*Proof.* By Theorem 6.11.3, it is enough to assume that $A$ is not infinitely often $C$-random and show that $A$ is not 2-random. Let $F$ be a low compression function. By the definition of compression function, $\lim_n n - \lvert F(A \upharpoonright n)\rvert = \infty$. Let $P_{i,m} = \lbrace X : \forall n \geqslant m\; (\lvert F(X \upharpoonright n)\rvert < n - i) \rbrace$, and let $U_i = \bigcup_m P_{i,m}$. The $U_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_2$ relative to $F$. Furthermore,

$$\mu(P_{i,m}) \leqslant \mu(\lbrace X : \lvert F(X \upharpoonright m)\rvert < m - i \rbrace) = 2^{-m}\lvert\lbrace \sigma \in 2^m : \lvert F(\sigma)\rvert < m - i \rbrace\rvert < 2^{-m} 2^{m-i} = 2^{-i}.$$

Since $P_{i,0} \subseteq P_{i,1} \subseteq \cdots$, it follows that $\mu(U_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$. Thus $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ is a $\Sigma^0_2$ test relative to $F$, and hence is not 1-random relative to $F$. But $F$ is low, so $A$ is not 1-random relative to $\emptyset'$, and hence is not 2-random.

</div>

By the comment following the proof of Theorem 6.11.3, we also have the following result, which can be a useful tool in studying 2-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.7 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

For every sufficiently fast growing computable function $g$, a set $A$ is 2-random iff there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C^g(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. By an appropriate choice of universal Turing machine, there is a $c$ such that we can take "sufficiently fast growing" to mean $g(n) \geqslant n^2 + c$.

</div>

Turning to prefix-free complexity, we have the following analog of Definition 6.11.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.8)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **infinitely often strongly $K$-random** if there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + K(n) - O(1)$.

</div>

By Corollary 4.3.3, if a set is infinitely often strongly $K$-random then it is infinitely often $C$-random, and hence 2-random. The following result is a partial converse.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.11.9 — Yu, Ding, and Downey, after Solovay)</span></p>

If a set is 3-random then it is infinitely often strongly $K$-random.

*Proof.* Let $P_{i,m} = \lbrace A : \forall n \geqslant m\; (K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + K(n) - i) \rbrace$ and let $U_i = \bigcup_i P_{i,m}$. Since $\emptyset'$ can compute $K$, the $U_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_3$ classes. Furthermore, by Theorem 3.7.6,

$$\mu(P_{i,m}) \leqslant 2^{-m}\lvert\lbrace \sigma \in 2^m : K(\sigma) \leqslant m + K(m) - i \rbrace\rvert = O(2^{-i}).$$

Since $P_{i,0} \subseteq P_{i,1} \subseteq \cdots$, it follows that $\mu(U_i) = O(2^{-i})$. Thus there is a $c$ such that $U_c, U_{c+1}, \ldots$ is a $\Sigma^0_3$ test. If $A$ is not infinitely often strongly $K$-random, then $A \in \bigcap_{i \geqslant c} U_i$, so $A$ is not 3-random.

</div>

It had been a very interesting open question whether being infinitely often strongly $K$-random is equivalent to being 2-random, to being 3-random, or to neither. This problem was solved by Miller, who showed that a set is 2-random iff it is infinitely often strongly $K$-random. It follows that a set is infinitely often $C$-random iff it is infinitely often strongly $K$-random. We will delay the proof because it relies on notions of lowness not introduced until later in the book.

It is natural to ask whether there are unrelativized complexity characterizations (using either plain or prefix-free complexity) of 3-randomness, or even of $n$-randomness for larger $n$. Such characterizations can be obtained using the results in Section 10.2.4 below.

### 6.12 Randomness for Other Measures and Degree-Invariance

#### 6.12.1 Generalizing Randomness to Other Measures

We have restricted our development so far to the context of the uniform measure $\mu$, but it is straightforward to adapt our definitions and many results to any other probability measure $\nu$ on Cantor space. When doing so, however, we need to take into account the complexity of $\nu$. In this context, it is best to think of probability measures as generated by premeasures.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.12.1)</span></p>

A (probability) **premeasure** is a function $\rho : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $\rho(\lambda) = 1$ and $\rho(\sigma 0) + \rho(\sigma 1) = \rho(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

For a premeasure $\rho$, let the function $\mu^\ast_\rho : 2^\omega \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ be defined by letting $\mu^\ast_\rho(A)$ be the infimum of $\sum_{\sigma \in U} \rho(\sigma)$ over all $U$ such that $A \subseteq \llbracket U \rrbracket$. Then $\mu^\ast_\rho$ is an outer measure. Let $\mu_\rho$ be the corresponding measure. We have $\mu_\rho(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \rho(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$. For instance, if $\rho(\sigma) = 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ for all $\sigma$, then $\mu_\rho = \mu$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.12.2)</span></p>

The **rational representation** of a premeasure $\rho$ is the set $\lbrace (\sigma, r, q) : \rho(\sigma) \in (r, q) \rbrace \subset 2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$. We identify $\rho$ with this representation, which allows us to talk about the degree of $\rho$ and relativize concepts to $\rho$.

A measure $\nu$ on $2^\omega$ is **computable** if $\nu = \mu_\rho$ for some computable $\rho$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(6.12.3)</span></p>

Let $\rho$ be a premeasure.

1. A **Martin-Löf $\mu_\rho$-test** is a sequence $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^{0,\rho}_1$ classes such that $\mu_\rho(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for all $n$.
2. A class $C \subset 2^\omega$ is **Martin-Löf $\mu_\rho$-null** if there is a $\mu_\rho$-Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $C \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.
3. A set $A \in 2^\omega$ is **Martin-Löf $\mu_\rho$-random**, or **1-$\mu_\rho$-random**, if $\lbrace A \rbrace$ is not Martin-Löf $\mu_\rho$-null.

</div>

When we are given a measure $\nu$, we assume we have a particular premeasure $\rho$ such that $\nu = \mu_\rho$, and hence write simply "1-$\nu$-random". This notation makes particular sense when $\nu$ is computable, in which case we always assume that the corresponding $\rho$ is computable, making the classes in the definition of Martin-Löf $\nu$-test actual $\Sigma^0_1$ classes.

Clearly, randomness is not invariant under change of measure. For example, let $\nu$ be defined by letting $\nu(\llbracket \sigma 0 \rrbracket) = \frac{\nu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)}{3}$ and $\nu(\llbracket \sigma 1 \rrbracket) = \frac{2\nu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)}{3}$. Then no 1-random set is 1-$\nu$-random. But in this book we will often be more interested in degrees than in individual sets, which leads to the natural question of whether the 1-random and 1-$\nu$-random *degrees* are the same.

Even among computable measures, some that will lead to different 1-random degrees than $\mu$. For example, we can simply concentrate all our measure on a computable set, for instance defining $\nu(\llbracket 0^n \rrbracket) = 1$ for all $n$ and $\nu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = 0$ for all other $\sigma$. Then $0^\omega$ is a computable 1-$\nu$-random set. To isolate this kind of example, define a measure $\nu$ to be **atomic** if $\nu(\lbrace A \rbrace) > 0$ for some $A \in 2^\omega$. We call such an $A$ an **atom** of $\nu$. A nonatomic measure is called a **continuous** measure.

As it turns out, computability and continuity are enough to ensure invariance. In the following subsection, we will prove that if $\nu$ is computable and continuous, then a degree is 1-$\nu$-random iff it is 1-random.

#### 6.12.2 Computable Measures and Representing Reals

In this subsection, we will show that if $\nu$ is computable and continuous, then a degree is 1-$\nu$-random iff it is 1-random. This result was first proved by Levin, but also appears in Kautz' dissertation, whose account we follow. The key intuition we will exploit is that the identification of $2^\omega$ with $[0, 1]$ actually depends on the measure. That is, which subsets of $2^\omega$ we should identify with what intervals depends on the choice of measure.

In the usual representation, if $A \in 2^\omega$ begins with 10, say, then the 1 corresponds to the fact that the real $\alpha$ identified with $A$ is in $[\frac{1}{2}, 1]$ and the 0 to the fact that it is in $[\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{4}]$. That is, looking at the first $n$ bits of $A$ corresponds to knowing $\alpha$ to within $2^{-n}$, by computing an interval of length $2^{-n}$ within which $\alpha$ must lie. Now suppose that $\nu$ is the measure mentioned above corresponding to the distribution where 1's are twice as likely as 0's (that is, the measure defined by $\nu(\llbracket \sigma 0 \rrbracket) = \frac{\nu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)}{3}$ and $\nu(\llbracket \sigma 1 \rrbracket) = \frac{2\nu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)}{3}$). Then the first bit of $A$ being 1 indicates that the real identified with $A$ is in $[\frac{1}{3}, 1]$, while the second bit being 0 indicates that it is in $[\frac{1}{3}, \frac{5}{9}]$. Thus we have the following definition, where from now on we fix a computable probability measure $\nu$ on $2^\omega$ and a corresponding computable premeasure $\rho$.

## Chapter 7: Other Notions of Algorithmic Randomness

In [348, 349], Schnorr analyzed his characterization of 1-randomness in terms of c.e. martingales (Theorem 6.3.4). He argued that this result demonstrates a failure of 1-randomness to capture the intuition behind Martin-Löf's original definition, because effective randomness should be concerned with defeating *computable* betting strategies rather than computably enumerable ones, as the latter are fundamentally asymmetric, in the same way that a c.e. set is semi-decidable rather than decidable. This chapter begins by looking at two natural notions of effective randomness introduced by Schnorr with this critique in mind. It then discusses several further notions, including weak $n$-randomness (introduced by Kurtz), and nonmonotonic randomness.

### 7.1 Computable Randomness and Schnorr Randomness

#### 7.1.1 Basics

As mentioned in Section 6.3.1, c.e. martingales are not the most obvious effectivization of the notion of martingale. It is natural to investigate, as Schnorr did, what happens if we define randomness with respect to *computable* martingales, which are those that are computable functions in the sense of Definition 5.2.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.1 — Computable Randomness, Schnorr)</span></p>

A set is **computably random** if no computable martingale succeeds on it.

</div>

In this definition, we can take our martingales to be rational-valued. (Recall that when we say that a rational-valued function $f$ is computable, we mean that there is an algorithm to determine $f(n)$ given $n$, not just that $f$ is a computable function in the sense of Definition 5.2.1 that happens to take values in $\mathbb{Q}$.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.2 — Schnorr)</span></p>

For each computable martingale $d$ there is a computable martingale $f : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $S[f] = S[d]$. In fact, $d(\sigma) < f(\sigma) < d(\sigma) + 1$ for all $\sigma$.

*Proof.* Let $f(\lambda)$ be a rational strictly between $d(\lambda)$ and $d(\lambda) + 1$. Assume that we have defined $f(\sigma)$ to be a rational strictly between $d(\sigma)$ and $d(\sigma) + 1$. It is easy to check that we can find rationals $r_0$ and $r_1$ such that $f(\sigma) = \frac{r_0 + r_1}{2}$ and for each $i < 2$, we have $d(\sigma i) < r_i < d(\sigma i) + 1$. Let $f(\sigma i) = r_i$. Now $f$ is a computable rational-valued martingale, and clearly $S[f] = S[d]$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.3 — Folklore)</span></p>

$A$ is computably random iff $\lim_n d(A \upharpoonright n)$ exists for all computable martingales $d$.

*Proof.* If $\lim_n d(A \upharpoonright n)$ exists for all computable martingales $d$ then clearly $A$ is computably random. For the nontrivial direction, suppose that $A$ is computably random but $d$ is a computable martingale such that $\lim_n d(A \upharpoonright n)$ fails to exist.
