Since $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) < \infty$, there are rationals $r < s$ such that there are infinitely many $n$ with $d(A \upharpoonright n) < r$ and infinitely many $n$ with $d(A \upharpoonright n) > s$. We define a new computable martingale $\widehat{d}$. We begin with $\widehat{d}(\lambda) = 1$. Along a sequence $B$, we bet as follows. We bet evenly until (if ever) we find an $n_0$ such that $d(B \upharpoonright n_0) < r$, then follow $d$'s bets until (if ever) we find an $n_1$ such that $d(B \upharpoonright n_1) > s$, then bet evenly until we find an $n_2$ such that $d(B \upharpoonright n_2) < r$, and so on. If $B = A$ then we will find such $n_0, n_1, \ldots$. Between $A \upharpoonright n_{2i}$ and $A \upharpoonright n_{2i+1}$, we increase our capital by a factor greater than $\frac{s}{r}$, while between $A \upharpoonright n_{2i+1}$ and $A \upharpoonright n_{2i+2}$, we do not change our capital. Thus $\widehat{d}$ succeeds on $A$, contradicting the computable randomness of $A$.

</div>

According to Schnorr, however, computable randomness is not quite effective enough, because given a set that is not computably random, we do have a computable strategy for making arbitrarily much money betting on its bits, but we may not be able to know ahead of time how long it will take us to increase our capital to a given amount. In a way, this situation is similar to having a computable approximation to a real without necessarily having a computable modulus of convergence, which we know is a characterization of the real being $\Delta^0_2$, rather than computable. Thus we have the following weaker notion of randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.4 — Schnorr Randomness)</span></p>

(i) An **order** is an unbounded nondecreasing function from $\mathbb{N}$ to $\mathbb{N}$. For a (super)martingale $d$ and an order $h$, let $S_h[d] = \lbrace X : \limsup_n \frac{d(X \upharpoonright n)}{h(n)} = \infty \rbrace$. We say that this (super)martingale $h$-**succeeds** on the elements of $S_h[d]$, and call $S_h[d]$ the $h$-**success set** of $d$.

(ii) A set $A$ is **Schnorr random** if $A \notin S_h[d]$ for every computable martingale $d$ and every computable order $h$.

</div>

Note that if $\limsup_n \frac{d(X \upharpoonright n)}{g(n)} > 0$ for an order $g$, then $X \in S_h[d]$ for any sufficiently slowly growing order $h$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.5 — Schnorr Tests and Schnorr Nullity)</span></p>

(i) A Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a **Schnorr test** if the measures $\mu(U_n)$ are uniformly computable.

(ii) A class $C \subset 2^\omega$ is **Schnorr null** if there is a Schnorr test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $C \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

</div>

It is sometimes convenient to take an alternate definition of a Schnorr test as a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $\mu(U_n) = 2^{-n}$ for all $n$. As we now show, these definitions yield the same null sets, so we use them interchangeably.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.6 — Schnorr)</span></p>

Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr test. There is a Schnorr test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $\mu(V_n) = 2^{-n}$ and $\bigcap_n U_n \subseteq \bigcap_n V_n$.

*Proof.* For each $n$, proceed as follows. Let $r_0 < r_1 < \cdots < \mu(U_n)$ be a computable sequence of rationals such that $\mu(U_n) - r_i \leqslant 2^{-i}$. At stage $s$, first ensure that $V_n[s] \supseteq U_n[s]$. If there is an $i$ such that $\mu(V_n[s]) < 2^{-n} - 2^{-i}$ and $\mu(U_n[s]) \geqslant r_i$, then add basic open sets to $V_n[s]$ to ensure that its measure is equal to $2^{-n} - 2^{-i}$. It is easy to check that this action ensures that $V_n \supseteq U_n$ and $\mu(V_n) = 2^{-n}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.7 — Schnorr)</span></p>

A class $C \subset 2^\omega$ is Schnorr null iff there are a computable martingale $d$ and a computable order $h$ such that $C \subseteq S_h[d]$. Thus, a set $A$ is Schnorr random iff $\lbrace A \rbrace$ is not Schnorr null.

*Proof.* ($\Leftarrow$) Let $d$ be a computable martingale and $h$ a computable order. We build a Schnorr null set containing $S_h[d]$. By Proposition 7.1.2, we may assume that $d$ is rational-valued. We may also assume that $d(\lambda) \in (0, 1)$.

Let $U_i = \bigcup \lbrace \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket : d(\sigma) > h(\lvert\sigma\rvert) > 2^i \rbrace$. The $U_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes, and $\mu(U_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$ by Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3). To compute $\mu(U_i)$ to within $2^{-c}$, let $l$ be such that $h(l) > 2^c$. For each $n \in (m, l)$, find a finite set $F_n \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\llbracket F_n \rrbracket \subseteq S_n$ and $\mu(S_n \setminus \llbracket F_n \rrbracket) < 2^{-(c+n+1)}$. Let $V_k = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : \exists^{\geqslant 2^k} n > m\; (\sigma \in F_n) \rbrace \rrbracket$. Then $V_k \subseteq U_k$, and

$$\mu(U_k) - \mu(V_k) \leqslant \sum_{n > l} \mu(S_n) + \sum_{m < n < l} \mu(S_n \setminus \llbracket F_n \rrbracket) < 2^{-c}.$$

Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Schnorr test, and clearly $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$.

($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$. We build a computable martingale $d$ and a computable order $h$. [The proof proceeds by showing how to construct a computable martingale from a Schnorr test that succeeds on $A$ with a computable order.]

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.8 — Franklin and Stephan, after Schnorr)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is not Schnorr random.

(ii) There is a computable martingale $d$ and a computable function $f$ such that $\exists^\infty n\; (d(A \upharpoonright f(n)) \geqslant n)$.

(iii) There is a computable martingale $d'$ with the savings property and a computable function $f'$ such that $\exists^\infty n\; (d'(A \upharpoonright f'(n)) \geqslant n)$.

</div>

Many notions connected with 1-randomness can be adapted to Schnorr randomness if the right way to "increase the effectivity" is found. For example, the following is a characterization of Schnorr randomness analogous to Solovay's Definition 6.2.7.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.9 — Total Solovay Test, Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

A **total Solovay test** is a sequence $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes such that $\sum_n \mu(S_n)$ is finite and computable. A set $A$ **passes** this test if it is in only finitely many of the $S_n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.10 — Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

A set is Schnorr random iff it passes all total Solovay tests.

*Proof.* A Schnorr test is a total Solovay test, so if a set passes all total Solovay tests then it is Schnorr random. Now let $A$ be Schnorr random and let $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a total Solovay test. The measures $\mu(S_n)$ are uniformly computable, since they are uniformly left-c.e. and their sum is finite and computable. There is an $m$ such that $\sum_{n > m} \mu(S_n) < 1$. Let

$$U_k = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : \exists^{\geqslant 2^k} n > m\; (\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket \in S_n) \rbrace \rrbracket.$$

The $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$, and it is not hard to see that $\mu(U_k) \leqslant 2^{-k} \sum_{n > m} \mu(S_n) < 2^{-k}$. To compute $\mu(U_k)$ to within $2^{-c}$, let $l$ be such that $\sum_{n \geqslant l} \mu(S_n) < 2^{-(c+1)}$. Then $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Schnorr test, and $A$ is in at most $2^k + m$ many $S_n$.

</div>

#### 7.1.2 Limitations

Despite Schnorr's critique, 1-randomness has remained the paradigmatic notion of algorithmic randomness, and has received considerably more attention than Schnorr randomness. One reason may simply be that Martin-Löf's definition came first, and is perfectly adequate for many results. Another important reason, however, is that the mathematical theory of Schnorr randomness is not as well behaved as that of 1-randomness.

For example, the existence of universal Martin-Löf tests (and corresponding universal objects such as universal c.e. martingales and prefix-free complexity) is a powerful tool in the study of 1-randomness that is not available in the case of Schnorr randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.11 — Schnorr)</span></p>

There is no universal Schnorr test.

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace U_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr test. Define a computable set $A \notin U_1$ as follows. Suppose we have defined $A \upharpoonright n$ so that $2^n \mu(U_1 \cap \llbracket A \upharpoonright n \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_{1 \leqslant k \leqslant n} 2^{-k}$. Then there is an $i < 2$ such that $2^{n+1} \mu(U_1 \cap \llbracket (A \upharpoonright n) i \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_{1 \leqslant k \leqslant n+1} 2^{-k}$, so by approximating $\mu(U_1)$ to within $2^{-(n+1)}$, we can determine an $i < 2$ such that $2^{n+1} \mu(U_1 \cap \llbracket (A \upharpoonright n) i \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_{1 \leqslant k \leqslant n+1} 2^{-k}$. Let $A(n) = i$. The set $A$ is computable, and $\llbracket A \upharpoonright n \rrbracket \nsubseteq U_1$ for all $n$, so since $U_1$ is open, $A \notin U_1$. Thus there is a computable set not contained in $\bigcap_i U_i$, and hence $\lbrace U_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ is not universal.

</div>

In a similar vein, the proof of Theorem 6.3.11 also establishes the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.12 — Schnorr)</span></p>

There is no effective enumeration of all computable (super)martingales, nor is there an effective enumeration of all computable rational-valued (super)martingales.

</div>

Another basic tool in the study of 1-randomness that is not available for either Schnorr or computable randomness is van Lambalgen's Theorem. As observed by several people, the more difficult direction of van Lambalgen's Theorem, namely Theorem 6.9.2, does hold for both Schnorr and computable randomness, with essentially the same proof. Furthermore, if $A \oplus B$ is either Schnorr or computably random, then so are $A$ and $B$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.13)</span></p>

(i) If $A$ is Schnorr random and $B$ is Schnorr random relative to $A$, then $A \oplus B$ is Schnorr random.

(ii) If $A \oplus B$ is Schnorr random then so are $A$ and $B$.

(iii) Items (i) and (ii) also hold for computable randomness.

</div>

The analog of Theorem 6.9.1 fails, however. That is, there are sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A \oplus B$ is Schnorr random but $A$ and $B$ are not relatively Schnorr random, and the same is true for computable randomness.

#### 7.1.3 Computable Measure Machines

One of the problems with the mathematical theory of Schnorr randomness had been the lack of a machine characterization analogous to the Kolmogorov complexity definition of 1-randomness. Whether such a characterization could be given was a long-standing open question, which was solved by Downey and Griffiths, who gave a characterization in terms of a new class of machines.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.14 — Computable Measure Machine, Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

A **computable measure machine** is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}\, M \rrbracket)$ is computable.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.15 — Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

A set is Schnorr random iff $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for all computable measure machines $M$.

*Proof.* ($\Rightarrow$) Suppose that $M$ is a computable measure machine and $\limsup_n n - K_M(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$. Let $U_k = \lbrace A : \exists n\, K_M(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - k \rbrace$. The $U_k$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes, and by Lemma 6.2.2, $\mu(U_k) \leqslant 2^{-k}$ and the measures $\mu(U_k)$ are uniformly computable. Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Schnorr test. Since $A \in \bigcap_k U_k$, it follows that $A$ is not Schnorr random.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose that $A$ is not Schnorr random, so there is a Kurtz null test $\lbrace \llbracket S_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $A \in \bigcap_n \llbracket S_n \rrbracket$, where $\mu(\llbracket S_n \rrbracket) = 2^{-n}$ and the $S_n$ are uniformly computable, finite, and prefix-free. By the KC Theorem there is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that for each $n$ and $\sigma \in S_{2n+2}$, there is a $\tau$ of length $\lvert\sigma\rvert - (n+1)$ such that $M(\tau) = \sigma$. Furthermore, $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}\, M \rrbracket)$ is computable. For each $k$ there is an $n$ such that $A \upharpoonright n \in S_k$, whence $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) = n - (i+1) < n - i$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.16)</span></p>

For all computable measure machines $M_0$ and $M_1$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $K_N(\sigma\tau) \leqslant K_{M_0}(\sigma) + K_{M_1}(\tau)$ for all $\sigma$ and $\tau$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.17 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is a computable measure machine $M$ such that $K_M(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + 2\log\lvert\sigma\rvert + O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.18)</span></p>

For each computable measure machine $M$ there is a prefix-free machine $N$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}\, N \rrbracket) = 1$ and $K_N(\sigma) \leqslant K_M(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

</div>

No universal prefix-free machine can be a computable measure machine, since the measure of such a machine is a version of $\Omega$, and hence 1-random. However, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.19)</span></p>

For each computable measure machine $M$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $\text{rng}\, N = 2^{<\omega}$ and

$$K_N(\sigma) \leqslant \min\lbrace \lvert\sigma\rvert + 2\log\lvert\sigma\rvert,\; K_M(\sigma) \rbrace + O(1).$$

Furthermore, $N$ can be chosen such that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}\, N \rrbracket) = 1$.

</div>

#### 7.1.4 Computable Randomness and Tests

In this section we present a test characterization of computable randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.20 — Computably Graded Test, Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

A **computably graded test** is a pair consisting of a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ and a computable function $f : 2^{<\omega} \times \omega \to \mathbb{R}$ such that, for any $n \in \omega$, any $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, and any finite prefix-free set of strings $\lbrace \sigma_i \rbrace_{i \leqslant I}$ with $\bigcup_{i=0}^{I} \llbracket\sigma_i\rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$, the following conditions are satisfied:

(i) $\mu(V_n \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \leqslant f(\sigma, n)$,

(ii) $\sum_{i=0}^{I} f(\sigma_i, n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$, and

(iii) $\sum_{i=0}^{I} f(\sigma_i, n) \leqslant f(\sigma, n)$.

A set passes the test $(\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, f)$ if it passes $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.21 — Computable Rational Probability Distribution, Merkle, Mihailović, and Slaman)</span></p>

(i) A **computable rational probability distribution** (or measure) is a computable function $\nu : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that $\nu(\lambda) = 1$ and $\nu(\sigma) = \nu(\sigma 0) + \nu(\sigma 1)$.

(ii) A **bounded Martin-Löf test** is a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ for which there exists a computable rational probability distribution $\nu$ such that $\mu(V_n \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-n} \nu(\sigma)$ for all $n$ and $\sigma$.

</div>

It is not difficult to check that $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a bounded Martin-Löf test iff there is an $f$ such that $(\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, f)$ is a computably graded test. We will show that computably graded tests characterize computable randomness. We first need a lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.22 — Schnorr)</span></p>

From a co-c.e. martingale $c : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}$ we can effectively find a computable martingale $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that $d(\sigma) \geqslant c(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$.

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace c_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ be an effective decreasing approximation to $c$. That is, the $c_s$ are uniformly computable functions from $2^{<\omega}$ to $\mathbb{Q}$, and for all $\sigma$ and $s$ we have $c_{s+1}(\sigma) < c_s(\sigma)$ and $c(\sigma) = \lim_s c_s(\sigma)$. We inductively define a computable martingale $d$ such that $d(\sigma) > c(\sigma) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ for all $\sigma$.

Let $d(\lambda) = c_0(\lambda) + 1$. Now assume that $d(\sigma)$ has been defined so that $d(\sigma) > c(\sigma) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$. Let $n$ be such that $2d(\sigma) > c_n(\sigma 0) + c_n(\sigma 1) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert+1}$. Let $d(\sigma 1) = c_n(\sigma 1) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ and $d(\sigma 0) = 2d(\sigma) - d(\sigma 1)$. By definition $d(\sigma 1) > c(\sigma 1) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, and $d(\sigma 0)$ is defined explicitly to satisfy the martingale property. Furthermore,

$$d(\sigma 0) = 2d(\sigma) - d(\sigma 1) = 2d(\sigma) - c_n(\sigma 1) - 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} > c_n(\sigma 0) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} > c(\sigma 0) + 2^{-\lvert\sigma 0\rvert}.$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.23 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte; implicit in Merkle, Mihailović, and Slaman)</span></p>

(i) From a computable martingale $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ we can effectively define a computably graded test $(\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, f)$ such that if $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$ then $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$.

(ii) From a computably graded test $(\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}, f)$ we can effectively define a computable martingale $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that if $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$ then $\limsup_n d(A \upharpoonright n) = \infty$.

Therefore, a set is computably random iff it passes all computably graded tests iff it passes all bounded Martin-Löf tests.

</div>

#### 7.1.5 Bounded Machines and Computable Randomness

It is also possible to obtain a machine characterization for computable randomness. If $M$ is a prefix-free machine, let

$$S^n_M = \lbrace \sigma : K_M(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - n \rbrace.$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.24 — Bounded Machine, Mihailović)</span></p>

A **bounded machine** is a prefix-free machine such that there is a computable rational probability distribution $\nu$ with $\mu(\llbracket S^n_M \rrbracket \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-n} \nu(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$ and $n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.25 — Mihailović)</span></p>

A set $X$ is computably random iff $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for every bounded machine $M$.

</div>

#### 7.1.6 Process Complexity and Computable Randomness

In Section 6.3.2, we saw that process complexity can be used to characterize 1-randomness. In this section, we show that a variation on process complexity can be used to give an analogous characterization of computable randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.26 — Quick Process Machine, Levin and Day)</span></p>

A process machine $P$ is a **quick process machine** if it is total and there is a computable order $h$ such that $\lvert P(\tau) \rvert \geqslant h(\lvert\tau\rvert)$ for all $\tau$.

</div>

For a quick process machine $P$, any $P$-description of a string $\sigma$ must be among the strings $\tau$ such that $h(\lvert\tau\rvert) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert$, so the complexity $C_P(\sigma)$ of $\sigma$ relative to $P$ can be determined by computing the values of $P(\tau)$ for all such $\tau$ (which are defined, since $P$ is total). Thus $C_P$ is computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.27 — Quick Process Random, Day)</span></p>

$A$ is **quick process random** if $C_P(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for all quick process machines $P$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.1.28 — Day)</span></p>

A set is quick process random iff it is computably random.

</div>

Clearly, 1-randomness implies computable randomness, which in turn implies Schnorr randomness. We will show in Section 8.11.2 that neither of these implications can be reversed.

### 7.2 Weak $n$-Randomness

#### 7.2.1 Basics

Kurtz introduced a notion of randomness based on the idea of being contained in effective sets of measure 1 rather than avoiding effective sets of measure 0.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.1 — Weak 1-Randomness, Kurtz)</span></p>

A set is **weakly 1-random**, or **Kurtz random**, if it is contained in every $\Sigma^0_1$ class of measure 1.

</div>

We will later see that the relativized notion of weak $(n+1)$-randomness is actually a strong form of $n$-randomness. There is an equivalent formulation of this notion in terms of tests, established by Wang but implicit in some of the proofs in Kurtz.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.2 — Kurtz Null Test, Wang)</span></p>

A **Kurtz null test** is a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ for which there is a computable function $f : \omega \to (2^{<\omega})^{<\omega}$ such that $V_n = \llbracket f(n) \rrbracket$ for all $n$.

Another way to state this definition is that there are finite, uniformly computable $S_0, S_1, \ldots \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $V_n = \llbracket S_n \rrbracket$. Note that if $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Kurtz null test, then both $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ and $\lbrace \overline{V_n} \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ are uniformly $\Delta^0_0$ classes.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.3 — Wang, after Kurtz)</span></p>

A set is weakly 1-random iff it passes all Kurtz null tests.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is not weakly 1-random and let $U$ be a $\Sigma^0_1$ class of measure 1 such that $A \notin U$. To define $V_n$, let $s$ be least such that $\mu(U_s) > 1 - 2^{-n}$. Let $V_n = \overline{U_s}$. It is easy to check that $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Kurtz null test, and that $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$.

Conversely, suppose there is a Kurtz null test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$. Then $U = \bigcup_n \overline{V_n}$. Then $U$ is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class of measure 1, and $A \notin U$, so $A$ is not weakly 1-random.

</div>

A Kurtz null test is clearly a Schnorr test, so we see that every Schnorr random set is weakly 1-random.

Of course, we can generalize Kurtz randomness to higher levels. Thus a set is **weakly $n$-random**, or **Kurtz $n$-random**, if it is a member of all $\Sigma^0_n$ classes of measure 1. Here the distinction between classes and open $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-1)}}_1$ classes of measure 1 is important.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.4 — Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n \geqslant 2$.

(i) From the index of a $\Sigma^0_n$ class $C$ we can computably obtain the index of a $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-2)}}_2$ class $\widehat{C} \subseteq C$ such that $\mu(\widehat{C}) = \mu(C)$.

(ii) From the index of a $\Pi^0_n$-class $V$ we can uniformly and computably obtain the index of a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n-2)}}_2$ class $\widehat{V} \supseteq V$ such that $\mu(\widehat{V}) = \mu(V)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.5)</span></p>

Let $n \geqslant 2$. A set is weakly $n$-random iff it is in every $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-2)}}_2$ class of measure 1.

</div>

We can also have a "one jump" characterization of weak $n$-randomness via the analog of a Kurtz null test. We will refer to the tests in part (iii) of the following theorem as $\Sigma^0_n$ **Kurtz null tests**.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.6 — Kautz, Wang, after Kurtz)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for $n \geqslant 2$.

(i) $A$ is weakly $n$-random.

(ii) For every $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computable sequence of $\Sigma^0_{n-1}$ classes $\lbrace S_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ with $\mu(S_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_i S_i$.

(iii) For every $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computable sequence of $\Sigma^{0,\emptyset^{(n-2)}}_1$ classes $\lbrace S_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ with $\mu(S_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_i S_i$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.7 — Kurtz)</span></p>

Every $n$-random set is weakly $n$-random and every weakly $(n+1)$-random set is $n$-random.

*Proof.* An $n$-random set passes every $\Sigma^0_n$ Kurtz null test, and hence is weakly $n$-random. If $A$ is not $n$-random then there is a $\Sigma^0_n$ Martin-Löf test $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ such that $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. Let $V = \bigcap_n \overline{U_n}$. Then $V$ is a $\Sigma^0_{n+1}$ class of measure 1 not containing $A$, so $A$ is not weakly $(n+1)$-random.

</div>

We will see in Section 8.13 that neither of the implications in Theorem 7.2.7 can be reversed.

In some ways, weak 2-randomness is the weakest level of randomness at which "typical" random behavior happens. A good example of this phenomenon is the following result, which says that the behavior of weak 2-random sets is very unlike that of computationally powerful 1-random sets such as $\Omega$. It will be generalized below in Theorem 8.13.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.8 — Downey, Nies, Weber, and Yu)</span></p>

Every weakly 2-random degree forms a minimal pair with $\mathbf{0'}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.9 — Martin, see Solovay)</span></p>

There is no $\Delta^0_2$ weakly 2-random set.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.10 — Downey, Nies, Weber, and Yu)</span></p>

There is no universal generalized Martin-Löf test.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.11 — Hirschfeldt and Miller)</span></p>

For any $\Sigma^0_3$ class $S$ of measure 0, there is a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ such that $A \leqslant_T X$ for all 1-random $X \in S$.

</div>

We can combine Theorem 7.2.8 with the following result to obtain a characterization of the weakly 2-random sets within the 1-random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.12 — Downey, Nies, Weber, and Yu)</span></p>

For a 1-random set $A$, the following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is weakly 2-random.

(ii) The degree of $A$ forms a minimal pair with $\mathbf{0'}$.

(iii) $A$ does not compute any noncomputable c.e. set.

</div>

#### 7.2.2 Characterizations of Weak 1-Randomness

There is also a martingale characterization of weak 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.13 — Wang)</span></p>

$A$ is not weakly 1-random iff there is a computable martingale $d$ and a computable order $h$ such that $d(A \upharpoonright n) > h(n)$ for all $n$.

</div>

Downey, Griffiths, and Reid gave a machine characterization of weak 1-randomness, along the lines of the one for Schnorr randomness in Theorem 7.1.15. That is, it uses initial segment complexity but measured with respect to a restricted class of machines.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.14 — Computably Layered Machine, Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

A **computably layered machine** is a prefix-free machine $M$ for which there is a computable function $f : \omega \to (2^{<\omega})^{<\omega}$ such that

(i) $\bigcup_i f(i) = \text{dom}\, M$.

(ii) If $\sigma \in f(i+1)$, then there is a $\tau \in f(i)$ such that $M(\tau) \preccurlyeq M(\sigma)$.

(iii) If $\sigma \in f(i)$, then $\lvert M(\sigma) \rvert = \lvert\sigma\rvert + i + 1$.

The idea of a computably layered machine is that each layer $f(i)$ of the domain provides a layer of the range, and the elements of the range become more refined as $i$ increases.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.15 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

$A$ is weakly 1-random iff for all computably layered machines $M$, we have $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

</div>

In Theorem 7.1.15, we saw that Schnorr randomness can be characterized in terms of computable measure machines. It is an indication of the close relationship between Schnorr randomness and weak 1-randomness that this class of machines can also be used to characterize weak 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.16 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

$A$ is not weakly 1-random iff there is a computable measure machine $M$ and a computable function $f$ such that for all $n$, we have $K_M(A \upharpoonright f(n)) < f(n) - n$.

</div>

We finish this section with a Solovay-type characterization of weak 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.17 — Kurtz-Solovay Test, Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

A **Kurtz-Solovay test** is a pair $(f, V)$ consisting of a computable function $f$ and a computable collection $V = \lbrace V_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ of finite, uniformly computable sets of strings such that $\sum_n \mu(\llbracket V_n \rrbracket)$ is finite and computable. We say that $A$ **fails** a Kurtz-Solovay test if for all $n$, the set $A$ is in at least $n$ many of $\llbracket V_0 \rrbracket, \llbracket V_1 \rrbracket, \ldots, \llbracket V_{f(n)} \rrbracket$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.18 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

$A$ is weakly 1-random iff $A$ does not fail any Kurtz-Solovay test.

</div>

#### 7.2.3 Schnorr Randomness via Kurtz Null Tests

Schnorr randomness can also be characterized in terms of Kurtz null tests.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.19 — Kurtz Array, Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

A **Kurtz array** is a uniform family of Kurtz null tests $\lbrace V^n_j \rbrace_{n,j \in \omega}$ such that $\mu(V^n_j) \leqslant 2^{-j+n+1}$ for all $n$ and $j$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.20 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

A set $A$ is Schnorr random iff for all Kurtz arrays $\lbrace V^n_j \rbrace_{n,j \in \omega}$, there is an $n$ such that $A \notin V^n_j$ for all $j$.

</div>

Nicholas Rupprecht noticed that the characterization above allows for another elegant characterization in terms of a variation on Kurtz null tests.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.21 — Finite Total Solovay Test, Rupprecht)</span></p>

A **finite total Solovay test** is a sequence $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of uniformly computable clopen sets (i.e., $S_n = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : \sigma \in D_{g(n)} \rbrace \rrbracket$ where $g$ is computable) such that $\sum_n \mu(S_n)$ is finite and computable. A set $A$ **passes** this test if it is in only finitely many of the $S_n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.22 — Rupprecht)</span></p>

A set is Schnorr random iff it passes all finite total Solovay tests.

</div>

#### 7.2.4 Weakly 1-Random Left-c.e. Reals

There are no weakly 1-random c.e. sets. In fact, we have the following, where a set is **bi-immune** if neither it nor its complement contains an infinite c.e. set.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.23 — Jockusch, see Kurtz)</span></p>

If $A$ is weakly 1-random then $A$ is bi-immune.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.24 — Jockusch, see Kurtz)</span></p>

There are continuum many degrees that contain no weakly 1-random sets.

</div>

While no c.e. set can be weakly 1-random, the same is not true of c.e. *degrees*. Of course we know that the complete c.e. degree is 1-random, and hence weakly 1-random, but in fact, as shown by Kurtz, every nonzero c.e. degree contains a weakly 1-random left-c.e. real. The following is an extension of that result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.25 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

Every nonzero c.e. degree contains a weakly 1-random left-c.e. real.

</div>

#### 7.2.5 Solovay Genericity and Randomness

Solovay used forcing with closed sets of positive measure in his construction of a model of set theory in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable. Kautz made it explicit by giving a miniaturization (i.e., an effectivization) of Solovay's notion that yields a characterization of weak $n$-randomness in terms of a forcing relation, in the same way that Cohen forcing is miniaturized to yield the notion of $n$-genericity.

Let $\mathbb{P}_n$ denote the partial ordering of $\Pi^0_n$ classes of positive measure under inclusion. We work in the language of arithmetic with an additional set constant $X$ and a membership symbol $\in$. For a sentence $\varphi$ in this language and a set $A$, we write $A \vDash \varphi$ to mean that $\varphi$ holds when $X$ is interpreted as $A$ and all other symbols are interpreted in the usual way.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.26 — Solovay $n$-Genericity, Kautz)</span></p>

(i) For $T \in \mathbb{P}_n$, we write $T \Vdash \varphi$ if $A \vDash \varphi$ for all $A \in T$.

(ii) For a set $A$, we write $A \Vdash \varphi$ if there is a $T \in \mathbb{P}_n$ such that $A \in T$ and $T \Vdash \varphi$.

(iii) A set $A$ is **Solovay $n$-generic** if for every $\Sigma^0_n$ formula $\varphi$, either $A \Vdash \varphi$ or $A \Vdash \neg\varphi$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.27 — Kautz)</span></p>

(i) *(Monotonicity)* If $T \Vdash \varphi$ then for all $\widehat{T} \subset T$ in $\mathbb{P}_n$, we have $\widehat{T} \Vdash \varphi$.

(ii) *(Consistency)* It is not the case that $T \Vdash \varphi$ and $T \Vdash \neg\varphi$.

(iii) *(Quasi-completeness)* For every $\Sigma^0_n$ sentence $\varphi$ and each $T \in \mathbb{P}_n$, there is an $S \subseteq T$ in $\mathbb{P}_n$ such that either $S \Vdash \varphi$ or $S \Vdash \neg\varphi$.

(iv) *(Forcing=Truth)* $A$ is Solovay $n$-generic iff for each $\Sigma^0_n$ or $\Pi^0_n$ sentence $\varphi$, we have $A \Vdash \varphi$ iff $A \vDash \varphi$.

</div>

Kautz also showed that Solovay $n$-genericity exactly characterizes weak $n$-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.2.28 — Kautz)</span></p>

A set is Solovay $n$-generic iff it is weakly $n$-random.

</div>

### 7.3 Decidable Machines

Bienvenu and Merkle introduced a class of machines that can be used to classify most of the randomness notions we have met so far, in particular, weak 1-randomness, Schnorr randomness, and 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.1 — Decidable Machine, Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

A machine $M$ is **decidable** if $\text{dom}(M)$ is computable.

</div>

Any computable measure machine is decidable, as is any bounded machine. As remarked following the proof of Theorem 2.19.2, any $\Sigma^0_1$ class can be generated by a computable set of strings, so Martin-Löf tests can be thought of as determined by uniformly computable sets of strings. Then if we follow the KC Theorem version of the proof of Schnorr's Theorem 6.2.3, which converts tests to machines, it is not hard to show the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.2 — Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

A set $X$ is 1-random iff $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for all decidable prefix-free machines $M$. Moreover, there is a fixed decidable prefix-free machine $N$ such that $X$ is 1-random iff $K_N(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

</div>

Note that $K_M$ for a decidable machine $M$ is a particular example of a computable function $f$ with $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} \leqslant 1$, so the above result can be restated as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.3)</span></p>

A set $X$ is 1-random iff for all computable functions $f$ with $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, we have $f(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$.

</div>

For Schnorr randomness, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.4 — Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

A set $X$ is Schnorr random iff for all decidable prefix-free machines $M$ and all computable orders $g$, we have $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$.

*Proof.* Suppose that $X$ is not Schnorr random. Then by Definition 7.1.4, there is a computable martingale $d$ and a computable order $h$ such that $d(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant h(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. Let $g$ be a computable order with $g(n) = o(h(n))$. We may assume that $d(\lambda) \leqslant 1$. Let $A_k$ be the set of strings $\sigma$ such that $d(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{k+1} g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)$ but $d(\tau) < 2^{k+1} g(\lvert\tau\rvert)$ for all $\tau \prec \sigma$. Then $X \in \bigcap_k \llbracket A_k \rrbracket$. By Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3),

$$2^{k+1} \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert))} = \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + k + 1 + \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert))} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} d(\sigma) \leqslant 1.$$

Thus $\sum_k \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert))} \leqslant 1$. Therefore, we can apply the KC Theorem to get a prefix-free machine $M$ corresponding to the requests $(\lvert\sigma\rvert - \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)), \sigma)$ for all $k$ and $\sigma \in A_k$.

Since $d(\lambda) \leqslant 1$, for all $\sigma$ we have that $2^{\lvert\sigma\rvert} \geqslant d(\sigma)$, so if $\sigma \in A_k$ then $\lvert\sigma\rvert - \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)) \geqslant k$. Thus, to check whether $\tau \in \text{dom}\, M$, it suffices to consider $A_k$ for $k \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert$. For each such $k$, there are only finitely many $\sigma$ with $\lvert\sigma\rvert - \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)) = \lvert\tau\rvert$. Once we have made requests for all such $\sigma$, we know $\tau$ can no longer be added to the domain of $M$.

Hence $M$ is a decidable machine, and since $K_M(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - \log(g(\lvert\sigma\rvert))$ for all $\sigma \in A_k$, there are infinitely many $k$ such that $K_M(X \upharpoonright k) \leqslant k - \log(g(k))$. Taking a computable order $\widehat{g}$ with $\widehat{g}(n) = o(\log g(n))$, for each $c$ there is an $n$ such that $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) < n - \widehat{g}(n) - c$.

For the converse, suppose we have a computable order $g$ and a prefix-free decidable machine $M$ such that for each $k$ we have $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) < n - g(n) - k$ for infinitely many $n$. For each $k$ let $A_k$ be the set of $\sigma$ such that $K_M(\sigma) < \lvert\sigma\rvert - g(\lvert\sigma\rvert) - k$ but $K_M(\tau) \geqslant \lvert\tau\rvert - g(\lvert\tau\rvert) - k$ for all $\tau \prec \sigma$. The $A_k$ form a uniformly computable family of prefix-free subsets of $2^{<\omega}$ such that, for all $k$,

$$\sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-K_M(\sigma) - k} \leqslant 2^{-k}.$$

We can then define a martingale $d$ as follows. Let $d^p_\sigma$ be the martingale that doubles its capital along $\sigma$ except for the first $p$ many bits. Let

$$d(\tau) = \sum_k \sum_{\sigma \in A_k} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)} d_\sigma^{\frac{g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)}{2}}(\tau).$$

But $2^{\frac{g(\lvert\sigma\rvert)}{2}}$ is a computable order, and there are infinitely many prefixes of $X$ in $\bigcup_k A_k$, so $X$ is not Schnorr random by Definition 7.1.4.

</div>

For weak 1-randomness, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.5 — Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $X$ is not weakly 1-random.

(ii) There exists a decidable prefix-free machine $M$ and a computable order $f$ such that $C_M(X \upharpoonright f(n)) \leqslant f(n) - n$ for all $n$.

(iii) There exists a decidable (plain) machine $M$ and a computable order $f$ such that $C_M(X \upharpoonright f(n)) \leqslant f(n) - n$ for all $n$.

</div>

Using similar methods, Bienvenu and Merkle also obtained the following characterization of weak 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.3.6 — Bienvenu and Merkle)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $X$ is not weakly 1-random.

(ii) There exists a decidable prefix-free machine $M$ and a computable order $h$ such that $C_M(x \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - h(n)$ for all $n$.

(iii) There exists a decidable (plain) machine $M$ and a computable order $h$ such that $C_M(x \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - h(n)$ for all $n$.

</div>

### 7.4 Selection Revisited

#### 7.4.1 Stochasticity

In this section we return to the roots of the subject, re-examining von Mises' idea of selection. As we have seen in Section 6.5, a selection function is a partial function $f : 2^{<\omega} \to \lbrace \text{yes}, \text{no} \rbrace$. Recall that we think of $f$ as a rule that, given some bits of a sequence, selects the next bit to bet on.

Recall also the following notation from Section 6.5. For a sequence $A$ and a selection function $f$, let $s_f(A, n)$ be the $n$th number $k$ such that $f(A \upharpoonright k) = \text{yes}$, if such a number exists. If $s_f(A, n-1)$ is defined, let $S_f(A, n) = \sum_{i < n} A(s_f(A, i))$. In other words, $S_f(A, n)$ is the number of 1's among the first $n$ bits of $A$ selected by $f$. We call $s_0(A, n), s_1(A, n), \ldots$ the **sequence of places of $A$ selected by $f$**.

Given a collection $\mathcal{C}$ of selection functions, we say that $A$ is **stochastic for $\mathcal{C}$** if for any $f \in \mathcal{C}$, either the sequence of places of $A$ selected by $f$ is finite, or $\lim_n \frac{S_f(A, n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.1)</span></p>

A sequence is **von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic** if it is stochastic for all partial computable selection functions. A sequence is **Church stochastic** if it is stochastic for all computable selection functions.

</div>

There is a natural way to turn a selection function $f$ into a (super)martingale $d$. For each $k$ and $i = 0, 1$, define the martingale $d^i_k$ as follows. Let $d^i_k(\lambda) = 1$. Given $d^i_k(\sigma)$, if $f(\sigma) = \text{no}$, then let $d^i_k(\sigma j) = d^i_k(\sigma)$ for $j = 0, 1$. If $f(\sigma) = \text{yes}$, then let $d^i_k(\sigma i) = (1 + 2^{-k}) d^i_k(\sigma)$ and $d^i_k(\sigma(1-i)) = (1 - 2^{-k}) d^i_k(\sigma)$. If $f(\sigma) \uparrow$, then let $d^i_k(\sigma j) = 0$ for $j = 0, 1$. In other words, $d^i_k$ waits for $f$ to select a place, then bets a part of its capital that depends on $k$ that the next bit will be $i$.

Let $d(\sigma) = \sum_k 2^{-k}(d^0_k(\sigma) + d^1_k(\sigma))$. Then $d$ is clearly a martingale, and it is also easy to see that if $f$ is total computable then $d$ is a computable martingale, while if $f$ is partial computable then $d$ is a c.e. supermartingale.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.2 — Schnorr, Shen, Wang)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then $A$ is von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic. If $A$ is computably random then $A$ is Church stochastic.

</div>

So we see that computably random sets satisfy the law of large numbers with respect to computable selection functions. We will show in Corollary 7.4.7 that computable randomness does not imply von Mises-Wald-Church stochasticity. Later, at the end of Section 8.4, we will show that Schnorr randomness does not imply Church stochasticity, a result due to Wang.

The converse to Theorem 7.4.2 fails to hold. Indeed, for any countable collection $E$ of selection functions, being stochastic for $E$ is not enough to imply even weak 1-randomness. To see that this is the case, fix $E$ and let $\alpha$ be as in Ville's Theorem 6.5.1. That is, $\alpha$ is stochastic for $E$, but $\frac{S(\alpha, n)}{n} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$ for all $n$. Then $\alpha$ is not weakly 1-random, because it is contained in the $\Pi^0_1$ class $\lbrace X : \forall n\; (\frac{S(X, n)}{n} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}) \rbrace$, which has measure 0.

#### 7.4.2 Partial Computable Martingales and a Threshold Phenomenon for Stochasticity

In defining a martingale $d$ based on a partial computable selection function $f$ as in the previous subsection, instead of having $d(\sigma j) = 0$ if $f(\sigma) \uparrow$, we can let $d(\sigma j)$ be undefined in that case. We can also apply the proof of Proposition 7.1.2 to make $d$ into a *partial computable martingale*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.3)</span></p>

A **partial computable (super)martingale** is a partial computable function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that, if $d(\sigma i)$ is defined, then so are $d(\sigma)$ and $d(\sigma(1-i))$, and $d$, where defined, satisfies the (super)martingale property.

A set is **partial computably random** if no partial computable martingale succeeds on it.

</div>

This strengthening of the notion of computable randomness was first considered by Ambos-Spies and Wang. By the comments above, if a set is partial computably random then it is von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic. It is also clear that 1-random sets are partial computably random, since a partial computable martingale $d$ can be turned into a c.e. martingale $\widetilde{d}$ by defining $\widetilde{d}(\sigma)$ to be 0 while $d(\sigma)$ is undefined and then redefining $\widetilde{d}(\sigma)$ to be $d(\sigma)$.

Using this notion, we can show that there are von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic sets whose initial segment complexities are quite far from those of 1-random sets. The following theorem follows a line of work including Daley and Li and Vitányi.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.4 — Merkle, Lathrop and Lutz, Muchnik)</span></p>

(i) There is a computably random $A$ such that for all computable orders $f$, we have $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant f(n)$ for almost all $n$.

(ii) There is a partial computably random $B$ such that for all computable orders $f$, we have $C(B \upharpoonright n \mid n) \leqslant f(n) \log n$ for almost all $n$.

</div>

Theorem 7.4.4 can be viewed as the culmination of a long sequence of results on stochastic and random sequences. Daley looked at replacing some results on randomness due to Loveland by results on stochasticity. Lathrop and Lutz introduced what they called *ultracompressible* sets, defined as those sets $X$ such that for every computable order $g$, we have $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + g(n)$ for almost all $n$. They proved the following result, which follows from Theorem 7.4.4 (i) by taking, say, $f(n) = \log g(n)$, since $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(C(X \upharpoonright n \mid n))$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.5 — Lathrop and Lutz)</span></p>

There are computably random ultracompressible sets.

</div>

Similarly, Theorem 7.4.4 (ii) shows that there are partial computably random sets $X$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant O((\log n)^2)$, say, thus establishing a strong failure of the converse to the first part of Theorem 7.4.2.

Merkle also showed that being stochastic does have *some* consequences for the initial segment complexity of a set. The following result generalizes the fact that no c.e. set can be von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic (which is obvious because every infinite c.e. set contains an infinite computable subset). Together with Theorem 7.4.4 (ii), this result establishes a kind of threshold phenomenon in the initial segment complexity behavior of von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic sequences.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.6 — Merkle)</span></p>

If there is a $c$ such that $C(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant c \log n$ for almost all $n$, then $X$ is not von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic.

</div>

By Theorem 7.4.4, there is a computably random $X$ such that $C(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant 2 \log n + O(1)$, so we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.7 — Ambos-Spies)</span></p>

There is a computably random set that is not von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic.

</div>

#### 7.4.3 A Martingale Characterization of Stochasticity

Ambos-Spies, Mayordomo, Wang, and Zheng have shown that stochasticity can be viewed as a notion of randomness for a restricted kind of martingale. These authors stated the following for a notion of time-bounded stochasticity, but their proof works for Church stochasticity as well.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.8 — Simple Martingale, Ambos-Spies, Mayordomo, Wang, and Zheng)</span></p>

(i) A martingale $d$ is **simple** if there is a number $q \in \mathbb{Q} \cap (0, 1)$ such that for all $\sigma$ and $i = 0, 1$,

$$d(\sigma i) \in \lbrace d(\sigma),\; (1+q) d(\sigma),\; (1-q) d(\sigma) \rbrace.$$

(ii) A martingale $d$ is **almost simple** if there is a finite set $\lbrace q_0, \ldots, q_m \rbrace \subset \mathbb{Q} \cap (0, 1)$ such that for all $\sigma$ and $i = 0, 1$, there is a $k \leqslant m$ such that

$$d(\sigma i) \in \lbrace d(\sigma),\; (1+q_k) d(\sigma),\; (1-q_k) d(\sigma) \rbrace.$$

We say that a set is **(almost) simply random** if no (almost) simple computable martingale succeeds on it. Actually, almost simple randomness and simple randomness coincide.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.9 — Ambos-Spies, Mayordomo, Wang, and Zheng)</span></p>

If $d$ is an almost simple martingale then there are finitely many simple martingales $d_0, \ldots, d_m$ such that $S[d] \subseteq \bigcup_{k \leqslant m} S[d_k]$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.10)</span></p>

A set is almost simply random iff it is simply random.

</div>

We can now state the following characterizations of stochasticity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.4.11 — Ambos-Spies, Mayordomo, Wang, and Zheng)</span></p>

(i) A set is Church stochastic iff it is simply random.

(ii) A set is von Mises-Wald-Church stochastic iff no partial computable (almost) simple martingale succeeds on it.

</div>

### 7.5 Nonmonotonic Randomness

#### 7.5.1 Nonmonotonic Betting Strategies

In this section we consider the effect of allowing betting strategies that can bet on the bits of a sequence out of order. This concept was introduced by Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky. We use the notation of Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.1)</span></p>

A **finite assignment** (f.a.) is a sequence $x = (r_0, a_0), \ldots, (r_n, a_n) \in (\mathbb{N} \times \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace)^{<\omega}$ such that the $r_i$ are pairwise distinct. We call the set of $r_i$ the **(selection) domain** and denote it by $\text{dom}(x)$.

A **scan rule** is a partial function $s$ from the set of finite assignments to $\mathbb{N}$ such that for all finite assignments $x$, we have $s(x) \notin \text{dom}(x)$.

</div>

The idea is that the $r_i$ are the places selected by our nonmonotonic betting strategy, and the scan rule is the function that determines the next place to bet on based on the observed values of the previously selected places, i.e., the $a_i$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.2 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

A **stake function** is a partial function from the collection of finite assignments to $[0, 2]$.

A **nonmonotonic betting strategy** is a pair consisting of a scan rule and a stake function.

</div>

We can now define the analog of a martingale for a nonmonotonic betting strategy $(s, q)$. This is called a **capital function**, $d$. The idea is that given a set $X$, we begin with $d(\lambda)$, and given a finite assignment $x$, the strategy picks $s(x)$ as the next place to bet on. If $q(x) < 1$, it is betting that $X(s(x)) = 1$, and staking $1 - q(x)$ much of its capital on that bet; if $q(x) > 1$, it is betting that $X(s(x)) = 0$, and staking $q(x) - 1$ much of its capital on that bet; and if $q(x) = 1$, it is betting evenly. Then, as in the case of a martingale, if $X(s(x)) = 0$, the current capital is multiplied by $q(x)$, and otherwise it is multiplied by $2 - q(x)$.

We can consider nonmonotonic betting strategies as a game played on sequences. Let $b = (s, q)$ be a nonmonotonic betting strategy. We define the partial functional $p$ by $p^X(0) = \lambda$ and

$$p^X(n+1) = p^X(n) {}^\frown \big(s(p^X(n)),\; q(p^X(n))\big).$$

We have $p^X(n+1) \uparrow$ if $s(p^X(n))$ is undefined. Using this functional we can formally define the payoff of our strategy as

$$c^X(n+1) = \begin{cases} q \cdot p^X(n) & \text{if } X(p^X(n)) = 0 \\ (2-q) \cdot p^X(n) & \text{if } X(p^X(n)) = 1. \end{cases}$$

Finally, we can define the **payoff function** of $b$, which is our notion of a nonmonotonic martingale, as

$$d^X_b(n) = d^X_b(\lambda) \prod_{i=1}^{n} c^X(i).$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.3 — Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky)</span></p>

A nonmonotonic betting strategy $b$ **succeeds** on $X$ if

$$\limsup_{n \to \infty} d^X_b(n) = \infty.$$

We say that $X$ is **nonmonotonically random** (or **Kolmogorov-Loveland random**) if no partial computable nonmonotonic betting strategy succeeds on it.

We say that $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is **Kolmogorov-Loveland null** if there is a partial computable nonmonotonic betting strategy succeeding on all $X \in C$.

</div>

The use of partial computable nonmonotonic strategies in Definition 7.5.3 is not essential.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.4 — Merkle)</span></p>

A sequence $X$ is nonmonotonically random iff no total computable nonmonotonic betting strategy succeeds on it.

</div>

The fundamental properties of nonmonotonic randomness were first established by Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky. Many of these were improved in the later paper of Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan, which, by and large, used techniques that were elaborations of those of Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.5 — Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then $A$ is nonmonotonically random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is not nonmonotonically random and that $b$ is a partial computable betting strategy that succeeds on $A$. We define a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ as follows. We put $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$ into $V_n$ if there is a $j$ such that $d^n_b(j) \geqslant 2^n$. (Note that this notation implies that the first $j$ places selected by $b$ are all less than $\lvert\sigma\rvert$.) It is straightforward to check that Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3) holds for nonmonotonic martingales as well, and that this fact implies that $\lbrace V_n : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ is a Martin-Löf test. Since clearly $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$, we see that $A$ is not 1-random.

</div>

At the time of writing, it remains a fundamental open question whether this theorem can be reversed.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Open Question</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.6 — Muchnik, Semenov, and Uspensky)</span></p>

Is every nonmonotonically random sequence 1-random?

</div>

Thus we know that 1-randomness implies nonmonotonic randomness, which in turn clearly implies (partial) computable randomness, since monotonic betting strategies are a special case of nonmonotonic ones. The same proof as above shows that if we looked at computably enumerable nonmonotonic betting strategies, we would have a notion equivalent to 1-randomness.

We can separate nonmonotonic randomness from computable (or even partial computable) randomness by combining the remark following Theorem 7.4.5 with the following result, which shows that nonmonotonic randomness is quite close to 1-randomness in a certain sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.7 — Muchnik)</span></p>

(i) Let $h$ be a computable order such that $K(A \upharpoonright h(n)) \leqslant h(n) - n$ for all $n$. Then $A$ is not nonmonotonically random.

(ii) Indeed, there are two partial computable nonmonotonic betting strategies such that any sequence satisfying the hypothesis of (i) is covered by one of them.

(iii) Moreover, these strategies can be converted into total computable ones.

</div>

One key problem when dealing with nonmonotonic randomness is the lack of universal tests. The level to which the lack of universality hurts us can be witnessed by the following simple result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.8 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

No partial computable nonmonotonic betting strategy succeeds on all computably enumerable sets.

</div>

Strangely enough, it turns out that *two* nonmonotonic betting strategies are enough to succeed on all c.e. sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.9 — Muchnik; Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

There exist computable nonmonotonic betting strategies $b_0$ and $b_1$ such that for each c.e. set $W$, at least one of $b_0$ or $b_1$ succeeds on $W$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.10 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

The class of Kolmogorov-Loveland null sets is not closed under finite unions.

</div>

#### 7.5.2 Van Lambalgen's Theorem Revisited

We have seen that looking at splittings yields significant insight into randomness, as witnessed by van Lambalgen's Theorem (see Section 6.9). For an infinite and coinfinite set $Z$, let $z_0 < z_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $Z$, and recall that $A_0 \oplus_Z A_1 = \lbrace z_n : n \in A_0 \rbrace \cup \lbrace y_n : n \in A_1 \rbrace$, where $y_0 < y_1 < \cdots$ are the elements of $\overline{Z}$. That is, we use $Z$ as a guide as to how to merge $A_0$ and $A_1$. Clearly, the proof of van Lambalgen's Theorem shows that if $Z$ is computable then $A_0 \oplus_Z A_1$ is 1-random iff $A_i$ is 1-random relative to $A_{1-i}$ for $i = 0, 1$. Here is an analog of van Lambalgen's Theorem for nonmonotonic randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.11 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

Suppose that $A = A_0 \oplus_Z A_1$ for a computable $Z$. Then $A$ is nonmonotonically random iff $A_i$ is nonmonotonically random relative to $A_{1-i}$ for $i = 0, 1$.

</div>

An important corollary of this result is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.5.12 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

If $Z$ is computable and $A_0 \oplus_Z A_1$ is nonmonotonically random, then at least one of $A_0$ or $A_1$ is 1-random.

</div>

### 7.6 Demuth Randomness

In this section we introduce a notion of randomness due to Demuth, whose underlying thesis was that reasonable functions should behave well on random points.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.6.1 — Demuth)</span></p>

A sequence of $\Sigma^0_1$ classes $\lbrace \llbracket W_{g(e)} \rrbracket \rbrace_{e \in \omega}$ is called a **Demuth test** if

(i) $\mu(\llbracket W_{g(e)} \rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-e}$ and

(ii) $g$ is an $\omega$-c.e. function; that is, there are a computable function $f$ and a computable approximation $g(e) = \lim_s g(e, s)$ such that $\lvert\lbrace s : g(e, s+1) \neq g(e, s) \rbrace\rvert < f(e)$ for all $e$.

We say that a set $A$ **passes** this Demuth test if it passes it in the Solovay test sense: $A \notin \llbracket W_{g(e)} \rrbracket$ for almost all $e$. A set is **Demuth random** if it passes all Demuth tests.

</div>

The idea here is that while the levels of the test are all c.e. objects, the function picking the indices of the levels is not computable but only approximable. If the function $g$ used to define the test members is only $\Delta^0_2$ then we get another notion of randomness, called **limit randomness**, studied by Kučera and Nies. Limit randomness is implied by Schnorr randomness relative to $\emptyset'$ (a concept further studied by Barmpalias, Miller, and Nies), and hence by 2-randomness. Kučera and Nies also studied weak Demuth randomness, where a set is **weakly Demuth random** if it passes all Demuth tests $\lbrace \llbracket W_{g(e)} \rrbracket \rbrace_{e \in \omega}$ such that $\llbracket W_{g(0)} \rrbracket \supseteq \llbracket W_{g(1)} \rrbracket \supseteq \cdots$.

Every Martin-Löf test is a Demuth test, and every Demuth test is a Martin-Löf test relative to $\emptyset'$, so we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.6.2)</span></p>

2-randomness implies Demuth randomness, which in turn implies 1-randomness.

</div>

Notice that if a set is $\omega$-c.e. then it cannot be Demuth random, and hence $\Omega$ is not Demuth random. (Consider the finite Demuth test given by $V_e = \llbracket \Omega \upharpoonright e + 1 \rrbracket$ for $e \in \omega$.) Thus Demuth randomness is stronger than 1-randomness. Demuth constructed a $\Delta^0_2$ Demuth random set, showing that 2-randomness is stronger than Demuth randomness. One method to establish this result is to construct a test that serves a purpose similar to that of a universal Martin-Löf test. (There is no universal Demuth test.)

This is done by enumerating all pairs $\lbrace (\varphi_e(\cdot, \cdot), \psi_e) : e \in \omega \rbrace$, where $\varphi_e$ and $\psi_e$ are partial computable functions, and where we allow $\varphi_e(x, s)[t] \downarrow$ only if certain conditions are satisfied (ensuring that we enumerate all partial Demuth tests and then can assemble the tails as usual to make a test universal for Demuth randomness). This test is of the form $\lbrace \llbracket W_{h(e)} \rrbracket \rbrace_{e \in \omega}$ with $h$ a $\Delta^0_2$ function (in fact an $(\omega + 1)$-c.e. function). The leftmost set passing this test will be Demuth random and $\Delta^0_2$. Thus we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.6.3 — Demuth)</span></p>

There exists a $\Delta^0_2$ Demuth random set.

</div>

We will show in Theorem 8.14.2 that all Demuth random sets are $\text{GL}_1$. It is also possible to show that no Demuth random set is of hyperimmune-free degree. (Indeed, Miller and Nies (see [306]), proved that if a set is $\text{GL}_1$ and DNC then it is hyperimmune. Hence no 1-random set that is $\text{GL}_1$ can be hyperimmune-free. But there are hyperimmune free weakly 2-random sets, as we see in the next chapter.) This result shows that weak 2-randomness and Demuth randomness are incomparable notions.

### 7.7 Difference Randomness

Following the proof of Theorem 6.1.3, we discussed the class of 1-random sets that do not compute $\emptyset'$. Results such as Theorems 8.8.4 and 11.7.4 below will show that the sets in this class behave much more like "truly random" sets than computationally powerful 1-random sets such as $\Omega$. In this section, we present a test set characterization of this class due to Franklin and Ng, which attests to its naturality as a notion of randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.7.1 — D.c.e. Test, Franklin and Ng)</span></p>

A **d.c.e. test** is a sequence $\lbrace U_n \setminus V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that the $U_n$ and $V_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes and $\mu(U_n \setminus V_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for all $n$. A set $A$ **passes** this test if $A \notin \bigcap_n U_n \setminus V_n$.

A set is **difference random** if it passes all d.c.e. tests.

</div>

This definition can be generalized in a natural way to define $n$-c.e. tests and $n$-c.e. randomness for all $n > 1$ (where 2-c.e. randomness is what we have called difference randomness). However, Franklin and Ng showed that this hierarchy of randomness notions collapses; that is, for every $n > 1$, a set is $n$-c.e. random iff it is difference random.

Difference randomness can also be characterized in terms of a special kind of Demuth test.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.7.2 — Strict Demuth Test, Franklin and Ng)</span></p>

A Demuth test $\lbrace \llbracket W_{g(n)} \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is **strict** if $g$ has an $\omega$-c.e. approximation $g(n, s)$ such that if $g(n, s+1) \neq g(n, s)$ then $\llbracket W_{g(n), s+1} \rrbracket \cap \llbracket W_{g(n), t} \rrbracket = \emptyset$ for all $t \leqslant s$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.7.3 — Franklin and Ng)</span></p>

A set $A$ is difference random iff for every strict Demuth test $\lbrace \llbracket W_{g(n)} \rrbracket \rbrace_{e \in \omega}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_n \llbracket W_{g(n)} \rrbracket$.

</div>

Using this characterization, we can show that the difference random sets are exactly the "computationally weak" 1-random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.7.4 — Franklin and Ng)</span></p>

A set is difference random iff it is 1-random and does not compute $\emptyset'$.

</div>

Thus every difference random set is 1-random, and this implication is strict. Lemma 7.7.3 shows that every Demuth random set is difference random. As we will see in Theorem 8.13.1, no weakly 2-random set can compute $\emptyset'$, so weak 2-randomness also implies difference randomness. As mentioned in the previous section, Demuth randomness and weak 2-randomness are incomparable notions, so both of these implications are strict.

### 7.8 Finite Randomness

A **finite Martin-Löf test** is a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace \llbracket V_e \rrbracket : e \in \omega \rbrace$ where $\lvert V_e \rvert < \infty$ for all $e$; that is, each $\llbracket V_e \rrbracket$ is clopen. A set is **finitely random** if it passes all finite Martin-Löf tests. We say that a finite Martin-Löf test is a **computably finite Martin-Löf test** if, additionally, there is a computable function $f$ such that $\lvert V_e \rvert < f(e)$. A set is **computably finitely random** if it passes all computably finite Martin-Löf tests. Interestingly, for $\Delta^0_2$ sets finite randomness and 1-randomness coincide.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.8.1)</span></p>

If $A$ is finitely random and $\Delta^0_2$ then $A$ is 1-random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is $\Delta^0_2$ and not 1-random. Let $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ be a universal Martin-Löf test. Let $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ be the test defined by letting $V_i[s] = U_i[t]$ for the largest $t \leqslant s$ such that $A_s \in U_i[t]$, if there is one, and $t = s$ otherwise. Then $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ is a finite test, and its intersection contains $A$.

</div>

Beyond the $\Delta^0_2$ sets the notions are quite different. One could argue that notions of finite randomness are not really randomness notions (because they are not stochastic), but it might in any case be interesting to see what insight they give into computability.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.8.2 — Brodhead, Downey, and Ng)</span></p>

A computably enumerable degree contains a computably finitely random set iff it is not totally $\omega$-c.e.

</div>

### 7.9 Injective and Permutation Randomness

Two variations on nonmonotonic randomness were suggested by Miller and Nies as possible methods of attacking Question 7.5.6. Both use the idea of restricting betting strategies to *oblivious* scan rules; that is, no matter what the input sequence $A$ is, the places to be scanned are determined exactly by the previous *places* we have bet on, independently of the observed values of $A$ at these places, so that the scan rule maps $\mathbb{N}^{<\omega}$ to $\mathbb{N}$. Thus, there is an $f$ such that if we have bet on positions $n_0, \ldots, n_k$, then we will bet on position $f(\langle n_0, \ldots, n_k \rangle)$, where of course this value cannot be in $\lbrace n_0, \ldots, n_k \rbrace$.
