A sequence is **injectively random** if it is nonmonotonically random for all computable betting strategies based on such scan rules. It is **permutation random** if it is nonmonotonically random for all computable betting strategies based on such scan rules with the additional requirement that the strategy must bet on all bits eventually. (That is, the scan rule is also surjective.)

Clearly, every nonmonotonically random set is injectively random, every injectively random set is permutation random, and every permutation random set is computably random. The following is what is known in this area.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(7.9.1 — Kastermans and Lempp)</span></p>

There is an injectively random set that is not 1-random.

</div>

There seem to be a number of other potentially interesting randomness notions intermediate between computable randomness and 1-randomness. We mention two that can be motivated by thinking about the coding of tests into supermartingales in the proof of Schnorr's Theorem 6.3.4 that a set is 1-random iff no c.e. supermartingale succeeds on it. There, we start with a uniformly c.e. sequence $R_0, R_1, \ldots$ of prefix-free generators for a Martin-Löf test. We build a c.e. supermartingale $d$ that bets evenly on $\sigma 0$ and $\sigma 1$ until it finds that, say, $\sigma 0 \in R_n$, at which point it starts to favor $\sigma 0$, to an extent determined by $n$. If later $d$ finds that $\sigma 1 \in R_m$, then what it does is determined by the relationship between $m$ and $n$. If $m < n$ then $d$ still favors $\sigma 0$, though to a lesser extent than before. If $m = n$ then $d$ again bets evenly on $\sigma 0$ and $\sigma 1$. If $m > n$ then $d$ switches allegiance and favors $\sigma 1$.

The computable enumerability of $d$ is essential in the above. A computable supermartingale (which we have seen we may assume is rational-valued without loss of generality) has to decide which side to favor, if any, immediately. Hitchcock has asked whether an intermediate notion, where we allow our supermartingale to be c.e. but do not allow it to switch allegiances in the way described above, is still powerful enough to capture 1-randomness. A *Kastergale* is a pair consisting of a partial computable function $g : 2^{<\omega} \to \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ and a c.e. supermartingale $d$ such that $g(\sigma) \downarrow = i$ iff $\exists s\; (d_s(\sigma i) > d_s(\sigma(1-i)))$ iff $d(\sigma i) > d(\sigma(1-i))$. A set is **Kastermans random** if no Kastergale succeeds on it. A *Hitchgale* is the same as a Kastergale, except that in addition the proportion $\frac{d_s(\tau j)}{d_s(\tau)}$ (where we regard $\frac{0}{0}$ as being 0) is a $\Sigma^0_1$ function, so that if we ever decide to bet some percentage of our capital on $\tau j$, then we are committed to betting at least that percentage from that point on, even if our total capital on $\tau$ increases later. A set is **Hitchcock random** if no Hitchgale succeeds on it. It is open whether these notions differ from each other or from 1-randomness.

## Chapter 8: Algorithmic Randomness and Turing Reducibility

In this chapter, we look at the distribution of 1-random and $n$-random degrees among the Turing (and other) degrees. Among the major results discussed are the Kučera-Gács Theorem 8.3.2 that every set is computable from a 1-random set; Theorem 8.8.8, due to Barmpalias, Lewis, and Ng, that every PA degree is the join of two 1-random degrees; and Stillwell's Theorem 8.15.1 that the "almost all" theory of degrees is decidable. The proof of Theorem 8.12.1, due to de Leeuw, Moore, Shannon, and Shapiro, that if a set is c.e. relative to positive measure many sets, then it is c.e., has as a corollary the fundamental result (first explicitly formulated by Sacks) that if a set is computable relative to positive measure many sets, then it is computable. Its proof introduces a basic technique called the majority vote technique.

### 8.1 $\Pi^0_1$ Classes of 1-Random Sets

For each $n \geqslant 1$, relativizing $\Omega$ to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$ shows that there are $n$-random sets computable in $\emptyset^{(n)}$. We can get a stronger result using $\Pi^0_1$ classes. For each constant $c$, the class $\lbrace A : \forall n\, K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - c \rbrace$ is clearly a $\Pi^0_1$ class, so basic facts about $\Pi^0_1$ classes give us several interesting results about the 1-random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.1.1)</span></p>

The collection of 1-random sets is a $\Sigma^0_2$ class.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.1.2)</span></p>

There exist low 1-random sets.

*Proof.* Apply the Low Basis Theorem 2.19.8.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.1.3)</span></p>

There exist 1-random sets of hyperimmune-free degree.

*Proof.* Apply the Hyperimmune-Free Basis Theorem 2.19.11.

</div>

Proposition 8.1.2 is obviously particular to 1-randomness, since no 2-random set can be $\Delta^0_2$, let alone low. In Theorem 8.21.2, we will see that this is also the case for Proposition 8.1.3, since every 2-random set has hyperimmune degree. On the other hand, the above results obviously relativize, so, for instance, for all $n \geqslant 1$, there exist $n$-random sets with $\emptyset^{(n)}$-computable jumps.

### 8.2 Computably Enumerable Degrees

Kučera completely answered the question of which c.e. degrees contain 1-random sets, by showing that the only such degree is the complete one.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.2.1 — Kučera)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random, $B$ is a c.e. set, and $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$, then $B \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset'$. In particular, if $A$ is 1-random and has c.e. degree, then $A \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset'$.

*Proof.* Kučera's original proof used Arslanov's Completeness Criterion (Theorem 2.22.3). We give a new direct proof.

Suppose that $A$ is 1-random, $B$ is c.e., and $\Psi^B = A$. Let $c$ be such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > n - c$ for all $n$. We will enumerate a KC set, with constant $d$ given by the recursion theorem. Let $k = c + d + 1$. We want to ensure that if $B \upharpoonright \psi^B(n+k)[s] = B_s \upharpoonright \psi^B(n+k)[s]$, then $\emptyset'_s(n) = \emptyset'(n)$, which clearly ensures that $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} B$. We may assume that our approximations are sufficiently sped-up so that $\Psi^B(n+k)[s] \!\downarrow$ for all $s$ and all $n \leqslant s$.

If $n$ enters $\emptyset'$ at some stage $s \geqslant n$, we wish to change $B$ below $\psi^B(n+k)[s]$. We do so by forcing $A$ to change below $n + k$. That is, we enumerate a KC request $(n + 1, A_s \upharpoonright n + k)$. This request ensures that $K(A_s \upharpoonright n + k) \leqslant n + k - c$, and hence that $A \upharpoonright n + k \neq A_s \upharpoonright n + k$. Thus $B \upharpoonright \psi^B(n+k)[s] \neq B_s \upharpoonright \psi^B(n+k)[s]$, as required. (Note that we enumerate at most one request, of weight $2^{-(n+1)}$, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and hence our requests indeed form a KC set.)

</div>

In the above proof, if $\Psi$ is a wtt-reduction, then so is the reduction from $B$ to $\emptyset'$ that we build. Hence, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.2.2)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random, $B$ is a c.e. set, and $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} B$, then $B \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. In particular, if $A$ is 1-random and has c.e. wtt-degree, then $A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$.

</div>

### 8.3 The Kučera-Gács Theorem

In this section we present a basic result, proved independently by Kučera and Gács, about the distribution of 1-random sets and the relationship between 1-randomness and Turing reducibility. Specifically, we show that every set is computable from some 1-random set. We begin with an auxiliary result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.3.1 — Space Lemma, Merkle and Mihailović)</span></p>

Given a rational $\delta > 1$ and integer $k > 0$, we can compute a length $l(\delta, k)$ such that, for any martingale $d$ and any $\sigma$,

$$\lvert \lbrace \tau \in 2^{l(\delta,k)} : d(\sigma\tau) \leqslant \delta d(\sigma) \rbrace \rvert \geqslant k.$$

*Proof.* By Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3), for any $l$ and $\sigma$, the average of $d(\sigma\tau)$ over strings $\tau$ of length $l$ is $d(\sigma)$. Thus

$$\frac{\lvert \lbrace \tau \in 2^l : d(\sigma\tau) > \delta d(\sigma) \rbrace \rvert}{2^l} < \frac{1}{\delta}.$$

Let $l(\delta, k) = \lceil \log \frac{k}{1-\delta^{-1}} \rceil$, which is well-defined since $\delta > 1$. Then

$$\lvert \lbrace \tau \in 2^{l(\delta,k)} : d(\sigma\tau) \leqslant \delta d(\sigma) \rbrace \rvert > 2^{l(\delta,k)} - \frac{2^{l(\delta,k)}}{\delta} = 2^{l(\delta,k)}(1 - \delta^{-1}) \geqslant k.$$

</div>

One should think of the Space Lemma as saying that, for any $\sigma$ and any martingale $d$, there are at least $k$ many extensions $\tau$ of $\sigma$ of length $\lvert\sigma\rvert + l(\delta, k)$ such that $d$ cannot increase its capital at $\sigma$ by more than a factor of $\delta$ while betting along $\tau$.

We wish to show that a given set $X$ can be coded into a 1-random set $R$. Clearly, unless $X$ itself is 1-random, there is no hope of doing such a coding if the reduction allows for the recovery of $X$ as an identifiable subsequence of $R$. The key idea in the proof below, which we take from Merkle and Mihailović, is to use the intervals provided by the Space Lemma to do the coding.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.3.2 — Kučera-Gács Theorem)</span></p>

Every set is wtt-reducible to a 1-random set.

*Proof.* Let $d$ be a universal c.e. martingale. We assume that we have applied the savings trick of Proposition 6.3.8 to $d$ so that for any $R$, if $\liminf_n d(R \upharpoonright n) < \infty$ then $R$ is 1-random.

Let $r_0 > r_1 > \cdots$ be a collection of positive rationals such that, letting $\beta_i = \prod_{j \leqslant i} r_j$, the sequence $\lbrace \beta_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ converges to some value $\beta$. Let $l_s = l(r_s, 2)$ be as in the Space Lemma, which means that for any $\sigma$ there are at least two strings $\tau$ of length $l_s$ with $d(\sigma\tau) \leqslant r_s d(\sigma)$. Partition $\mathbb{N}$ into consecutive intervals $\lbrace I_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ with $\lvert I_s \rvert = l_s$.

Fix a set $X$. We construct a 1-random set $R$ that wtt-computes $X$. At stage $s$, we specify $R$ on the elements of $I_s$. We denote the part of $R$ specified before stage $s$ by $\sigma_s$. (That is, $\sigma_s = R \upharpoonright \sum_{i < s} l_i$.) If $s > 0$, then we assume by induction that $d(\sigma_s) \leqslant \beta_{s-1}$. Since $\beta_s = r_s \beta_{s-1}$ (when $s > 0$) and $l_s = l(r_s, 2)$, there are at least two $s$-admissible strings. Let $\tau_0$ and $\tau_1$ be the lexicographically least and greatest among such strings, respectively. Let $\sigma_{s+1} = \sigma_s \tau_i$, where $i = X(s)$.

Now $\liminf_n d(R \upharpoonright n) \leqslant \beta$, so $R$ is 1-random. We now show how to compute $X(s)$ from $\sigma_{s+1} = R \upharpoonright \sum_{i \leqslant s} l_i$. We know that $\sigma_{s+1}$ is either the leftmost or the rightmost $s$-admissible extension of $\sigma_s$, and being $s$-admissible is clearly a co-c.e. property, so we wait until either all extensions of $\sigma$ to the left of $\sigma_{s+1}$ are seen to be not $s$-admissible, or all extensions of $\sigma$ to the right of $\sigma_{s+1}$ are seen to be not $s$-admissible. In the first case, $s \notin X$, while in the second case, $s \in X$.

</div>

We will refer to the kind of coding used in the above proof as *Gács coding*, as it is an adaptation by Merkle and Mihailović of the original method used by Gács.

### 8.4 A "No Gap" Theorem for 1-Randomness

The Kučera-Gács Theorem allows us to examine more deeply the characterization of 1-randomness by Miller and Yu, Theorem 6.7.2. Recall that this theorem states that a set is 1-random iff any of the following equivalent conditions holds:

1. $C(A \upharpoonright n \mid n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$.
2. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$.
3. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$ for every computable function $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$.
4. $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - G(n) - O(1)$, where $G(n)$ is a particular Solovay function.

Using the Kučera-Gács Theorem, Bienvenu and Downey showed that this is not a coincidence, as *all* functions that can take the place of $G$ in Theorem 6.7.2 are Solovay functions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.4.1 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

Let $g$ be a function such that $A$ is 1-random iff $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - g(n) - O(1)$. Then $g$ is a Solovay function.

</div>

To prove this result, we show that in both characterizations of 1-randomness, $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ and $C(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - O(1)$, the lower bound on the complexity is very sharp; that is, there is no "gap phenomenon".

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.4.2 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

(i) There is no function $h$ that tends to infinity such that if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - h(n) - O(1)$ then $A$ is 1-random. In fact, there is no function $h$ that tends to infinity such that if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - h(n) - O(1)$ then $A$ is Church stochastic.

(ii) There is no function $h$ such that if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - h(n) - O(1)$ then $A$ is 1-random (or even Church stochastic).

*Proof.* Since we can replace $h$ by the function $n \mapsto \min\lbrace h(k) : k \geqslant n \rbrace$, we may assume that $h$ is nondecreasing. We build a single $A$ that establishes all parts of the theorem simultaneously. Let $h^{-1}(n)$ be the least $m$ such that $h(m) = n$.

If $h$ is computable, then we can simply take a 1-random $X$ and obtain $A$ by inserting zeroes into $X$ at a sufficiently sparse set of positions, say $h^{-1}(0), h^{-1}(1), \ldots$. However, we are working with an arbitrary order $h$, which may grow slower than any computable order, so this direct construction may not work. To overcome this problem, we invoke the Kučera-Gács Theorem and choose our 1-random set $X$ so that $h \leqslant_\text{T} X$. Intuitively, the sequence $A$ resulting from inserting zeroes into $X$ at positions $h^{-1}(0), h^{-1}(1), \ldots$ should not be 1-random, or even Church stochastic, since the part of $A$ consisting of the original bits of $X$ should be able to compute the places at which zeroes were inserted. However, the insertion of zeroes may destroy the Turing reduction $\Phi$ from $X$ to $h^{-1}$. In other words, looking at $A$, we may not be able to distinguish the bits of $X$ from the inserted zeroes.

We solve this problem by delaying the insertion of the zeroes to "give $\Phi$ enough time" to compute the positions of the inserted zeroes. We still have $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - h(n) - O(1)$. And similarly, since $X$ is 1-random, by Theorem 6.7.2 we have $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) - h(n) - O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.4.3 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

There is no function $g$ that tends to infinity such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + g(n) - O(1)$ for every 1-random $A$.

Similarly, there is no function $g$ that tends to infinity such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K(n) + h(n) - O(1)$ for every 1-random $A$.

</div>

The consequences of Theorem 8.4.2 go beyond its applications to Solovay functions. For example, it can be used to show that Schnorr randomness does not imply Church stochasticity, as mentioned in Section 7.4.1. By Theorem 7.3.4, if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - h(n) - O(1)$ for some computable order $h$, then $A$ is Schnorr random. On the other hand, Theorem 8.4.2 says that this condition is not sufficient for $A$ to be Church stochastic.

### 8.5 Kučera Coding

One of the most basic questions we might ask about the distribution of 1-random sets is which Turing degrees contain such sets. Kučera gave the following partial answer, which was later extended by Kautz to $n$-random sets using the jump operator, as we will see in Theorem 8.14.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.5.1 — Kučera)</span></p>

If $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$ then $\mathbf{a}$ contains a 1-random set.

*Proof.* Let $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$ and let $X \in \mathbf{a}$. Let $R$ be as in the proof of Theorem 8.3.2. Then $X \leqslant_\text{T} R$. Furthermore, we can compute $R$ if we know $X$ and can tell which sets are $s$-admissible for each $s$. The latter question can be answered by $\emptyset'$. Since $X \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, it follows that $R \leqslant_\text{T} X$. Thus $R$ is a 1-random set of degree $\mathbf{a}$.

</div>

We now give a different proof of Theorem 8.5.1, which is of course also an alternative proof of the Kučera-Gács Theorem 8.3.2, along the lines of the original proof of Kučera. This version is due to Reimann (see also Reimann and Slaman). Let $S_0, S_1, \ldots$ be an effective list of the prefix-free c.e. subsets of $2^{<\omega}$, and let $S_{k,s}$ be the stage $s$ approximation to $S_k$. We begin with Kučera's useful construction of a universal Martin-Löf test.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Construction</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Kučera's Universal Martin-Löf Test)</span></p>

Fix $n \in \mathbb{N}$. For each $e > n$, enumerate all elements of $S_{\Phi_e(e)}$ (we take $S_{\Phi_e(e)}$ to be empty if $\Phi_e(e) \!\uparrow$) into a set $R_n$ as long as $\sum_{\sigma \in S_{\Phi_e(e),s}} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} < 2^{-e}$. Let $U_n = \llbracket R_n \rrbracket$.

The $R_n$ are uniformly c.e. and $\sum_{\sigma \in R_n} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{e > n} 2^{-e} = 2^{-n}$. Thus $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ is a Martin-Löf test. To see that it is universal, let $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Martin-Löf test. Let $e$ be such that $V_i = \llbracket S_{\Phi_e(i)} \rrbracket$ for all $i$. Every computable function possesses infinitely many indices, so for each $n$ there is an $i > n$ such that $\Phi_e = \Phi_{\Phi_e(i)}$. For such an $i$, we have $\llbracket S_{\Phi_e(i)} \rrbracket = \llbracket S_{\Phi_{\Phi_e(i)}} \rrbracket = V_i$, which means that every element of $\llbracket S_{\Phi_e(i)} \rrbracket$ is enumerated into $R_n$. So $\bigcap_m V_m \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

</div>

We next need a lemma similar in spirit to the Space Lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.5.2)</span></p>

Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be Kučera's universal Martin-Löf test, and let $P_n$ be the complement of $U_n$. Let $C$ be a $\Pi^0_1$ class. Then there exists a computable function $\gamma : 2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}^+$ such that for any $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $n \in \mathbb{N}$,

$$P_n \cap C \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket \neq \emptyset \implies \mu(C \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) \geqslant \gamma(\sigma, n).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Alternate Proof of Theorem 8.5.1 — Kučera Coding)</span></p>

Let $B \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$. We need to show that there is a 1-random set $A$ such that $A \equiv_\text{T} B$. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be Kučera's universal Martin-Löf test. We describe how to code $B$ into an element $A$ of $\overline{U_0}$. Let $T$ be a computable tree such that $[T] = \overline{U_0}$ and let $E$ be the set of extendible nodes of $T$ (i.e., those nodes extended by paths of $T$). Note that $E$ is co-c.e., and hence $\emptyset'$-computable.

Let $\gamma$ be as in Lemma 8.5.2 with $C = \overline{U_0}$, and let $b(\sigma) = \lceil -\log \gamma(\sigma, 0) \rceil$. Then we know that, for any $\sigma$, if $\overline{U_0} \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket \neq \emptyset$ then $\sigma$ has at least two extensions of length $b(\sigma) + 1$ in $E$, as pointed out in the previous paragraph.

Suppose that, at stage $n$, we have defined $A \upharpoonright m_n$ for some $m_n$. Then $m_{n+1} = b(A \upharpoonright m_n) + 1$. Now, to compute $B(n)$ using $A$, let $\sigma = A \upharpoonright m_n$ and let $\tau = A \upharpoonright m_{n+1}$. We know that $\tau$ is either the leftmost or the rightmost extension of $\sigma$ of length $b(\sigma) + 1$ in $E$, as pointed out in the previous paragraph. Let $A \upharpoonright b(\sigma) = \sigma \tau_i$, where $i = B(n)$.

Since $A \in [T] = \overline{U_0}$, we know that $A$ is 1-random. Since we are assuming that $B \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, we have $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$. For the other direction, first note that the function $n \mapsto m_n$ is computable in $A$. To see that this is the case, assume that we have already computed $m_n$. Then $m_{n+1} = b(A \upharpoonright m_n) + 1$.

Since $A \equiv_\text{T} B$.

</div>

The Kučera-Gács Theorem goes against the intuition that random sets should be highly disordered, and hence computationally weak. However, note that this result is quite specific to 1-randomness. For example, if $A$ is 2-random then it is 1-random relative to $\emptyset'$, and hence relative to $\Omega$, so by van Lambalgen's Theorem, $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $A$, and hence $\Omega \nleqslant_\text{T} A$. Thus, no 2-random set can compute $\emptyset'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.5.3 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Let $A$ be 1-random and $B$ be $n$-random. If $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$ then $A$ is $n$-random.

*Proof.* The $n = 1$ case is trivial, so assume that $n > 1$. Let $X \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n-1)}$ be 1-random. Since $B$ is $n$-random, it is 1-random relative to $X$. By van Lambalgen's Theorem, $X$ is 1-random relative to $B$, and hence relative to $X$, and hence $A$ is $n$-random.

</div>

### 8.6 Demuth's Theorem

By Theorem 8.2.1, the only 1-random c.e. degree is $\mathbf{0}'$, so the class of 1-random degrees is not closed downwards, even among the nonzero degrees. The following result shows that there is a sense in which it *is* closed downwards with respect to truth table reducibility. The proof we give is due to Kautz, and uses the results and notation of Section 6.12.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.6.1 — Demuth's Theorem)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random and $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$ is not computable, then there is a 1-random $C \equiv_\text{T} B$.

*Proof.* Let $e$ be such that $\Phi^A_e = B$ and $\Phi^X_e$ is total for all oracles $X$. Let

$$\rho(\sigma) = \mu\left(\bigcup \lbrace \llbracket\tau\rrbracket : \forall n < \lvert\sigma\rvert\, (\Phi^\tau_e(n) \!\downarrow\!= \sigma(n)) \rbrace\right)$$

and let $\nu = \mu_\rho$. We claim that $B$ is 1-$\nu$-random. Suppose otherwise, and let $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Martin-Löf $\nu$-test such that $B \in \bigcap_n V_n$. Let $U_n = \lbrace X : \Phi^X_e \in V_n \rbrace$. It is easy to check that the $U_n$ are uniformly $\Sigma^0_1$ classes and $\mu(U_n) = \nu(V_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$, so the $U_n$ form a Martin-Löf test. But $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$, contradicting the hypothesis that $A$ is 1-random. Thus $B$ is 1-$\nu$-random.

Let $C \in \bigcap_n (B \upharpoonright s)_\nu$. Then $B = \text{seq}_\nu C$, since $B$ is not computable, and hence neither is $C$. By Theorem 6.12.9, $C \equiv_\text{T} B$ and $C$ is 1-random.

</div>

Too recently for inclusion in this book, Bienvenu and Porter [private communication] gave an example of a set $B \leqslant_\text{tt} \Omega$ such that there is no 1-random $C \equiv_\text{wtt} B$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.6.2 — Kautz)</span></p>

There is a 1-random degree $\mathbf{a}$ such that every noncomputable $\mathbf{b} \leqslant \mathbf{a}$ is 1-random.

*Proof.* By Proposition 8.1.3, there is a 1-random set $A$ of hyperimmune-free degree. By Proposition 2.17.7, if $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ then $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$, so the corollary follows from Demuth's Theorem.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.6.3)</span></p>

If $A$ is an incomplete left-c.e. real then $A \nleqslant_\text{tt} \Omega$.

*Proof.* By Theorem 8.2.1, no incomplete c.e. degree is 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.6.4 — Lachlan)</span></p>

There is a c.e. set that is wtt-complete but not tt-complete.

*Proof.* The previous corollary, combined with Proposition 6.1.2, shows that $\Omega$ is wtt-complete but not tt-complete, so it is enough to define a c.e. set $A \equiv_\text{tt} \Omega$. An example of such a set is the set of dyadic rationals less than $\Omega$.

</div>

### 8.7 Randomness Relative to Other Measures

In this section, we revisit the material of Section 6.12. It is natural to ask, given a set $X$, whether there is a (possibly noncomputable) measure $\nu$ such that $X$ is 1-$\nu$-random. (This question can be thought of as asking whether $X$ has *some* randomness content that can be extracted by an appropriate choice of $\nu$.) Of course, every set is 1-$\nu$-random if it is an atom of $\nu$. If $X$ is computable then $\lbrace X \rbrace$ is a $\Sigma^0_1$ class, so being an atom is the only way that $X$ can be 1-$\nu$-random. The following result shows that if $X$ is noncomputable, then there is always a less trivial way to make $X$ be 1-$\nu$-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.7.1 — Reimann and Slaman)</span></p>

If $A$ is noncomputable then there is a $\nu$ that is 1-$\nu$-random and $A$ is not an atom of $\nu$.

</div>

The proof of this result uses the relativized form of the following basis theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.7.2 — Reimann and Slaman; Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

For every 1-random set $X$ and every nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class $P$, there is an $X$-left-c.e. real $A \in P$ such that $X$ is 1-random relative to $A$.

</div>

A rather more subtle question is which sets are 1-$\nu$-random for some *continuous* measure $\nu$. Reimann and Slaman showed that a variation on the halting problem is *never continuously random*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.7.3 — Kjos-Hanssen and Montalbán)</span></p>

If $P$ is a countable $\Pi^0_1$ class and $\nu$ is continuous, then no member of $P$ is 1-$\nu$-random.

*Proof.* Since $\nu$ is continuous and $P$ is countable, $\nu(P) = 0$, so there is a $\nu$-computable function $t$ such that $\nu(P_{t(n)}) \leqslant 2^{-n}$. Thus $\lbrace P_{t(n)} \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf $\nu$-test covering $P$.

</div>

For any computable ordinal $\alpha$, there is a set $X$ of degree $\mathbf{0}^\alpha$ that is a member of some countable $\Pi^0_1$ class, so there is no bound below $\Delta^1_1$ on the complexity of the collection NCR of never continuously random sets. Reimann and Slaman have further explored this class and, more generally, the classes $\text{NCR}_n$ of sets that are never $n$-$\nu$-random for a continuous $\nu$, obtaining some remarkable results. They showed that $\text{NCR} \subset \Delta^1_1$ and, using very deep methods such as Borel Determinacy, that every class $\text{NCR}_n$ is countable. They also showed that, quite surprisingly, there is a precise sense in which metamathematical methods such as Borel Determinacy are *necessary* to prove this result.

### 8.8 Randomness and PA Degrees

In light of earlier results in this chapter, it is natural to ask whether the collection of 1-random degrees is closed upwards. In this section we give a negative answer to this question by examining the relationship between randomness and the PA degrees introduced in Section 2.21, a connection first explored by Kučera.

As we saw in Section 2.21, one of the characterizations of the PA degrees (which we can take as a definition) is that $\mathbf{a}$ is PA iff each partial computable $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$-valued function has an $\mathbf{a}$-computable total extension. As we saw in Section 2.22, this fact implies that if $\mathbf{a}$ is PA then it computes a diagonally noncomputable (DNC) function, that is, a total function $g$ such that $g(n) \neq \Phi_n(n)$ for all $n$. The same is true of 1-random degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.1 — Kučera)</span></p>

Every 1-random set computes a DNC function.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be 1-random. Let $f(n)$ be the position of $A \upharpoonright n$ in some effective listing of finite binary strings. Since $A$ is 1-random, $K(f(n)) = K(A \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1) \geqslant n - O(1)$. On the other hand, if $\Phi_n(n) \!\downarrow$, then $K(\Phi_n(n)) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$, so there are only finitely many $n$ such that $f(n) = \Phi_n(n)$. By altering $f$ at these finitely many places, we obtain an $A$-computable DNC function.

</div>

This result has the following interesting consequences, the first of which also has a direct proof using a version of the recursion theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.2 — Kučera)</span></p>

If $\mathbf{a}$ is 1-random and c.e. then $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{0}'$.

*Proof.* Apply Arslanov's Completeness Criterion, Theorem 2.22.3 (together with Theorem 2.22.1).

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.3 — Kučera)</span></p>

If $A$ and $B$ are 1-random and $A, B \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, then the degrees of $A$ and $B$ do not form a minimal pair.

*Proof.* By Theorem 8.8.1, each of $A$ and $B$ computes a DNC function, and hence computes a fixed-point free function (by Theorem 2.22.1). By Theorem 2.22.7, the degrees of $A$ and $B$ do not form a minimal pair.

</div>

Corollary 8.8.3 also follows from Theorem 7.2.11, since if $A, B \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, then $\lbrace A, B \rbrace$ is a $\Sigma^0_3$ class (in fact, a $\Pi^0_2$ class).

Let $A$ and $B$ be relatively 1-random $\Delta^0_2$ sets. Heuristically, $A$ and $B$ should have very little common information, and thus we might expect their degrees to form a minimal pair. Corollary 8.8.3 shows that this is not the case, however. Still, our intuition is not far off. We will see in Corollary 8.12.4 that if $A$ and $B$ are relatively weakly 2-random, then their degrees form a minimal pair. Furthermore, we will see in Corollary 11.7.3 that even for relatively 1-random $A$ and $B$, any set computable in both $A$ and $B$ must be quite close to being computable, in a sense that we will discuss in Chapter 11.

The exact relationship between PA degrees and 1-random degrees was clarified by the following result of Stephan, which also yields Kučera's aforementioned results as corollaries.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.4 — Stephan)</span></p>

If a degree $\mathbf{a}$ is both PA and 1-random then $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}'$.

*Proof.* Let $A \not\geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ have PA degree. We first construct a partial computable function $f$ such that for each $e$, the class of all sets $B$ such that $\Phi^B_e$ is a total extension of $f$ has small measure. Then we use $f$ to show that $A$ is not 1-random, using the fact that, since $A$ has PA degree, $A$ can compute a total extension of $f$.

We proceed as follows for all $e$ simultaneously. Let $I_e = [2^{e+2}, 2^{e+3} - 1]$. Within $I_e$, define $f$ as follows. At stage $s$, let $a_s$ be the least element of $I_e$ such that $f(a_s)$ has not yet been defined. For $i = 0, 1$, let $P_{e,s,i}$ be the class of all $B$ such that $\Phi^B_e(n)[s] \!\downarrow$ for all $n \in I_e$. Let $d_{e,s,i} = \mu(P_{e,s,i})$. If $d_{e,s,0} + d_{e,s,1} > 2^{-(e+1)}$, then choose $i < 2$ such that $d_{e,s,i} \leqslant d_{e,s,1-i}$ and let $f(a_s) = i$. Otherwise, do nothing at this stage.

Since $A$ is PA, it can compute a total $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$-valued extension $g$ of $f$. Let $e_0 < e_1 < \cdots$ be such that $\Phi^A_{e_i} = g$ for all $i$. Let $S = \lbrace P_{e(s),r(s),0} \cup P_{e(s),r(s),1} : s \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$. Then $S$ is a Solovay test. Let $h(i)$ be the least $s$ such that all computations $\Phi^A_{e_i}(n)$ for $n \in I_{e_i}$ have settled by stage $s$. Then $h \leqslant_\text{T} A$. There must be infinitely many $s$ such that $h(k_s) < s$, we also have $h(k_s) < r(s)$, and hence $A \in P_{e(s),r(s),0} \cup P_{e(s),r(s),1}$, and $d_{e(s),s,0} + d_{e(s),s,1} \leqslant 2^{-(e(s)+1)} \leqslant 1$, so $S$ is a Solovay test, and hence $A$ is not 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.5 — Kučera)</span></p>

There are PA degrees that are not 1-random.

*Proof.* Apply Theorem 8.8.4 to a low PA degree.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.6 — Kučera)</span></p>

The collection of 1-random degrees is not closed upwards.

</div>

As Stephan put it, Theorem 8.8.4 "says that there are two types of Martin-Löf random sets: the first type are the computationally powerful sets which permit to solve the halting problem [$\emptyset'$]; the second type of random sets are computationally weak in the sense that they are not [PA]. Every set not belonging to one of these two types is not Martin-Löf random." We have already alluded to this dichotomy between the 1-random sets that compute $\emptyset'$ and the ones that do not, and have seen in Section 7.7 that the latter can be characterized as the difference random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.7 — Stephan)</span></p>

Let $\mathbf{a} \not\geqslant \mathbf{0}'$. Then there is a degree $\mathbf{b} \geqslant \mathbf{a}$ that is not 1-random.

*Proof.* Let $f(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $\emptyset'_s \upharpoonright n = \emptyset' \upharpoonright n$. Then a set computes $\emptyset'$ iff it computes a function that majorizes $f$. By the relativized form of the hyperimmune-free basis theorem, there is a PA degree $\mathbf{b} \geqslant \mathbf{a}$ that is hyperimmune-free relative to $\mathbf{a}$. Since $\mathbf{a} \not\geqslant \mathbf{0}'$, there is no $\mathbf{a}$-computable function that majorizes $f$. Since every $\mathbf{b}$-computable function is majorized by some $\mathbf{a}$-computable function, there is no $\mathbf{b}$-computable function that majorizes $f$. Thus $\mathbf{b} \not\geqslant \mathbf{0}'$. Since $\mathbf{b}$ is a PA degree, it cannot be 1-random.

</div>

We conclude this section with a further interesting connection between PA and 1-random degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.8 — Barmpalias, Lewis, and Ng)</span></p>

Every PA degree is the join of two 1-random degrees.

</div>

Thus, an incomplete PA degree is an example of a non-1-random degree that is the join of two 1-random degrees.

Note that Theorem 8.8.8 does not hold for 1-genericity, or even weak 1-genericity, in place of 1-randomness, since there are hyperimmune-free PA degrees, but by Theorem 2.24.12, every weakly 1-generic set has hyperimmune degree.

While the 1-random degrees are not closed upwards, one might wonder about weaker notions of randomness such as weak 1-randomness. The following corollary was obtained by direct methods, before it was noticed that it could be obtained as an application of Theorems 8.8.4 and 8.8.8, together with Theorem 8.11.11 below, which states that on the hyperimmune-free degrees, 1-randomness and weak 1-randomness are equivalent.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.9 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

The weakly 1-random degrees are not closed upwards. Indeed, there is a degree that is not weakly 1-random but is above a 1-random degree.

*Proof.* Let $\mathbf{a}$ be hyperimmune-free and PA. By Theorem 8.8.4, $\mathbf{a}$ is not 1-random. By Theorem 8.11.11, $\mathbf{a}$ is not weakly 1-random. But by Theorem 8.8.8, $\mathbf{a}$ is the join of two 1-random degrees.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.10 — Indifferent Sets)</span></p>

For a set $I$, we write $R =_I \widehat{R}$ to mean that $R(i) = \widehat{R}(i)$ for all $i \notin I$.

A set $I$ is **indifferent** for a 1-random set $R$ if $\widehat{R}$ is 1-random for all $\widehat{R} =_I R$.

</div>

Figueira, Miller, and Nies showed that every 1-random set has an infinite indifferent set. Their proof uses a computability-theoretic notion due to Trakhtenbrot. A set $A$ is **autoreducible** if there is a Turing functional $\Phi$ such that $\Phi^{A \setminus \lbrace n \rbrace}(n) = A(n)$ for all $n$. That is, membership of $n$ in $A$ can be determined from $A$ without querying $A$ about $n$ itself.

Figueira, Miller, and Nies observed that no 1-random set is autoreducible. Indeed, if $A$ is autoreducible then it is easy to define a partial computable nonmonotonic betting strategy that succeeds on $A$, so $A$ is not 1-random, by Theorem 7.5.5.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.8.11 — Figueira, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

If $P$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class and $A \in P$ is not autoreducible, then there is an infinite $I \leqslant_\text{T} A'$ that is indifferent for $A$ in $P$. Thus, since every 1-random set is contained in a $\Pi^0_1$ class all of whose elements are 1-random, every 1-random set has an infinite indifferent set.

</div>

### 8.9 Mass Problems

A **mass problem** is a subset of $\omega^\omega$. We view the elements of such a class $P$ as solutions to some problem, which we identify with $P$. There are two natural notions of reducing one problem to another in this context.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.1 — Muchnik, Medvedev)</span></p>

Let $P$ and $Q$ be mass problems.

(i) $P$ is **weakly reducible** (or **Muchnik reducible**) to $Q$, denoted $P \leqslant_\text{w} Q$, if for each $f \in Q$, there is a $g \leqslant_\text{T} f$ in $P$.

(ii) $P$ is **strongly reducible** (or **Medvedev reducible**) to $Q$, denoted $P \leqslant_\text{s} Q$, if there is a functional $\Psi$ such that $\Psi^f \in P$ for every $f \in Q$.

</div>

In other words, $P$ is weakly reducible to $Q$ if from each solution to $Q$ we can effectively obtain a solution to $P$, and $P$ is strongly reducible to $Q$ if there is a single effective procedure for converting solutions to $Q$ into solutions to $P$.

Note that if $X, Y \in 2^\omega$ then $\lbrace X \rbrace \leqslant_\text{w} \lbrace Y \rbrace$ iff $\lbrace X \rbrace \leqslant_\text{s} \lbrace Y \rbrace$ iff $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y$, so these notions can be seen as extensions of Turing reducibility.

We can take equivalence classes of mass problems under these reducibilities to arrive at the notions of weak and strong degrees of mass problems. The weak and strong degrees both form lattices, with bottom element the degree of $\omega^\omega$ (which consists of exactly those mass problems that have computable elements), and top element $\emptyset$.

Let $R$ be the mass problem consisting of all 1-random sets. While $R$ is not itself a $\Pi^0_1$ class, it is weakly equivalent to any nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class $P$ all of whose elements are 1-random, such as the complement of one of the levels of a universal Martin-Löf test. The weak degree of $R$ can be characterized as the largest weak degree of $\Pi^0_1$ classes of positive measure.

Let $\mathcal{P}_\text{w}$ be the structure of weak degrees of $\Pi^0_1$ classes, with $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$ its bottom and top degrees, respectively. We establish some further properties of the weak degree $\mathbf{r_1}$ of $R$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.2 — Simpson)</span></p>

Let $P, Q \subseteq 2^\omega$ be nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ classes such that $\mu(P) > 0$ and $\deg_\text{w}(Q) < 1$. Then $\deg_\text{w}(P) \wedge \deg_\text{w}(Q) < 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.3 — Simpson)</span></p>

Let $P, Q \subseteq 2^\omega$ be nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ classes such that $P \leqslant_\text{w} Q$. Then there is a nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class $\widehat{Q} \subseteq Q$ such that $P \leqslant_\text{s} \widehat{Q}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.4 — Simpson)</span></p>

(i) $0 < \mathbf{r_1} < 1$.

(ii) If $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{P}_\text{w}$ is such that $\mathbf{q} < 1$, then $\mathbf{r_1} \wedge \mathbf{q} < 1$. (That is, $\mathbf{r_1}$ does not join to $\mathbf{1}$.)

(iii) If $\mathbf{q_0}, \mathbf{q_1} \in \mathcal{P}_\text{w}$ and $\mathbf{r_1} \geqslant \mathbf{q_0} \vee \mathbf{q_1}$, then $\mathbf{r_1} \geqslant \mathbf{q_0}$ or $\mathbf{r_1} \geqslant \mathbf{q_1}$. (That is, $\mathbf{r_1}$ is meet-irreducible.)

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.5 — Simpson)</span></p>

Let $P \subseteq 2^\omega$ be a nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class and let $S \subseteq \omega^\omega$ be a $\Sigma^0_3$ class. Then there is a nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class $Q \subseteq 2^\omega$ such that $Q \equiv_\text{w} P \cup S$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.9.6 — Simpson)</span></p>

The degree $\mathbf{r}^*_2$ is the largest weak degree of a $\Pi^0_1$ subset of $2^\omega$ whose upward closure in the Turing degrees has positive measure.

</div>

### 8.10 DNC Degrees and Subsets of Random Sets

In this section, we prove a strong extension of Kučera's Theorem 8.8.1 that every 1-random set computes a DNC function, by characterizing the DNC degrees in terms of 1-random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.10.1 — Kučera)</span></p>

If $P$ is a $\Pi^0_1$ class of positive measure, then there is a nonempty $\Pi^0_1$ class $Q \subseteq P$ and a $c$ such that for all $e$,

$$Q \cap P_e \neq \emptyset \implies \mu(Q \cap P_e) \geqslant 2^{-e-c}.$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.10.2 — Kjos-Hanssen; Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ computes a DNC function.

(ii) $A$ computes an infinite subset of a 1-random set. (In other words, there is a 1-random set that is not weakly 1-random relative to $A$.)

(iii) There is a $\Pi^{0,A}_1$ class of measure 0 containing a 1-random set.

</div>

The connection between DNC degrees and 1-randomness can be clarified as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.10.3 — Ambos-Spies, Kjos-Hanssen, Lempp, and Slaman; Kumabe and Lewis)</span></p>

There is a DNC degree that does not bound any 1-random degree.

</div>

Kumabe and Lewis showed that there is a minimal DNC degree, which, by Corollary 6.9.5, cannot be 1-random. Ambos-Spies, Kjos-Hanssen, Lempp, and Slaman showed that there is an ideal $I$ in the Turing degrees containing a DNC degree but no 1-random degree, and used this result to derive a separation between two principles in the context of reverse mathematics.

By Theorems 8.10.2 and 8.10.3, there is a subset of a 1-random set that does not compute any 1-random sets.

### 8.11 High Degrees and Separating Notions of Randomness

#### 8.11.1 High Degrees, Schnorr Randomness, and Computable Randomness

We have seen in Corollary 8.8.2 that all 1-random sets of c.e. degree (and in particular all 1-random left-c.e. reals) are of Turing degree $\mathbf{0}'$. This result fails for computably random (and hence for Schnorr random) sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.1 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Every high c.e. degree is computably random.

</div>

We will prove a stronger version of this result in Theorem 8.11.6 below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.2 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

If a nonhigh set is Schnorr random then it is 1-random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is nonhigh and not 1-random. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a universal Martin-Löf test. Let $f(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $A \in U_n[s]$. Then $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$, so by Theorem 2.23.7, there is a computable function $g$ such that $g(n) \geqslant f(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. Let $V_n = U_n[g(n)]$. Then $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a total Solovay test and $A \in V_n$ for infinitely many $n$, so $A$ is not Schnorr random by Theorem 7.1.10.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.3 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Every Schnorr random set of c.e. degree is high.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.4 — Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

Every Schnorr random left-c.e. real is high.

*Proof of Corollary 8.11.4.* Let $\alpha$ be a Schnorr random left-c.e. real. Let $T = \lbrace \langle i, j \rangle : \lvert W_i \rvert > j \rbrace$. Note that $T$ is c.e. We build a functional $\Gamma$ such that for each $i$, we have $\Gamma^\alpha(i, k) = T(\langle i, k \rangle)$ for almost all $k$. Then it is easy to see that $\alpha'$ can decide which $W_i$ are infinite, and hence $\alpha$ is high.

For each $i$, we also build a collection $Q_i$ of open intervals with rational endpoints forming a total Solovay test.

At each stage $s$, proceed as follows. For each $\langle i, k \rangle < s$ such that $\Gamma^{\alpha_s}(i, k)$ is currently undefined, let $\Gamma^{\alpha_s}(i, k) = T_s(\langle i, k \rangle)$ with use $k$. For each $\langle i, k \rangle$ entering $T$ at stage $s$, add $(\alpha_s, \alpha_s + 2^{-k})$ to $Q_i$.

Each $Q_i$ is either finite or contains an interval of length $2^{-k}$ for each $k$. In either case, $Q_i$ is a total Solovay test. Since $\alpha$ can be in only finitely many intervals in $Q_i$, for almost all $k$, if $\langle i, k \rangle$ enters $T$ at stage $s$, then $\alpha \upharpoonright k \neq \alpha_s \upharpoonright k$, whence $\Gamma^\alpha(i, k) = T(\langle i, k \rangle)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.5 — Extension of the Space Lemma)</span></p>

Given a rational $\delta > 1$ and integer $k > 0$, we can compute a length $l(\delta, k)$ such that, for any martingale $d$ and any $\sigma$,

$$\lvert \lbrace \tau \in 2^{l(\delta,k)} : \forall \rho \preccurlyeq \tau\, (d(\sigma\rho) \leqslant \delta d(\sigma)) \rbrace \rvert \geqslant k.$$

</div>

#### 8.11.2 Separating Notions of Randomness

In Section 7.1.1, we mentioned that 1-randomness, computable randomness, and Schnorr randomness are all distinct, even for left-c.e. reals. Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn established a definitive result on separating these notions of randomness, by showing that the degrees within which they can be separated are exactly the high degrees. The easy direction of this result is Theorem 8.11.2. Remarkably, the converse to this theorem also holds.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.6 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is high.

(ii) There is a $B \equiv_\text{T} A$ that is computably random but not 1-random.

(iii) There is a $C \equiv_\text{T} A$ that is Schnorr random but not computably random.

Furthermore, the same equivalences hold if $A$, $B$, and $C$ are restricted to left-c.e. reals.

</div>

For separating weak 1-randomness from stronger randomness notions, the dividing line is hyperimmunity. We will show that every hyperimmune degree contains a weakly 1-random set that is not Schnorr random, but if a set of hyperimmune-free degree is weakly 1-random, then it is in fact weakly 2-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.7 — Kurtz)</span></p>

Every weakly 1-generic set is weakly 1-random.

*Proof.* Let $G$ be weakly 1-generic. Let $S$ be a $\Sigma^0_1$ class of measure 1. Let $V$ be a c.e. set of strings generating $S$. Since $S$ has measure 1, $V$ is dense, so $G$ meets $V$, and hence must be a member of $S$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.8 — Kurtz)</span></p>

Every hyperimmune degree is weakly 1-random.

</div>

Neither Theorem 8.11.7 nor Corollary 8.11.8 can be reversed, as we have seen in Proposition 8.1.3 that there are 1-random sets of hyperimmune-free degree, and by Theorem 2.24.19, the weakly 1-generic degrees coincide with the hyperimmune degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.9)</span></p>

No weakly 1-generic set is Schnorr random.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be weakly 1-generic. Let $S_n = \lbrace \sigma 0^n : \sigma \in 2^n \rbrace$ and $S = \bigcup_n S_n$. Then $S$ is computable and dense, so $A$ meets $S$ infinitely often. In other words, $A \in \llbracket S_n \rrbracket$ for infinitely many $n$. But $\sum_n \mu(\llbracket S_n \rrbracket) = \sum_n 2^{-n} = 2$, so $\lbrace \llbracket S_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a total Solovay test, and $A$ is not Schnorr random by Theorem 7.1.10.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.10)</span></p>

Every hyperimmune degree contains a weakly 1-random set that is not Schnorr random.

</div>

However, the following result shows that outside of the hyperimmune degrees, many of our notions of randomness coincide.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.11 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

If $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree then $A$ is weakly 1-random iff $A$ is 1-random.

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree and is not 1-random. Let $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a nested Martin-Löf test such that $A \in \bigcap_n V_n$. Using $A$, we can compute a stage $g(n)$ such that $A \in V_n$. Since $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree, there is a computable function $f$ so that $f(n) \geqslant g(n)$ for all $n$. Let $U_n = V_n[f(n)]$. Then $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Kurtz null test such that $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$, so $A$ is not weakly 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.12 — Yu)</span></p>

If $A$ has hyperimmune-free degree then $A$ is weakly 1-random iff $A$ is weakly 2-random.

</div>

This result improves an earlier direct proof by Miller [personal communication] that there is a weakly 2-random hyperimmune-free degree. It has the following interesting corollary, which extends Corollary 8.6.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.13 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

There is a degree $\mathbf{a}$ such that every nonzero degree $\mathbf{b} \leqslant \mathbf{a}$ is weakly 2-random.

*Proof.* By Proposition 8.1.3, there is a 1-random set $A$ of hyperimmune-free degree. Let $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ be noncomputable. By Proposition 2.17.7, $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$, so by Demuth's Theorem 8.6.1, there is a 1-random $C \equiv_\text{T} B$. By Theorem 8.11.12, $C$ is weakly 2-random.

</div>

As in the 1-randomness case, it is not known whether $\mathbf{a}$ can be made hyperimmune.

#### 8.11.3 When van Lambalgen's Theorem Fails

We are now in a position to prove the result, mentioned in Section 7.1.2, that van Lambalgen's Theorem fails for Schnorr and computable randomness. This result was originally proved by Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan, with the following extension obtained by Yu.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.14 — Yu)</span></p>

Let $A \oplus B$ be Schnorr random and computable in an incomplete c.e. set. Then $A$ is not Schnorr random relative to $B$ and $B$ is not Schnorr random relative to $A$. The same is true for computable randomness.

*Proof.* By Theorem 7.1.13, $A$ and $B$ are Schnorr random. By Theorem 8.2.1, $A$, $B$, and $A \oplus B$ are not 1-random. By Theorem 8.11.2, they are high. Thus $(A \oplus B)' \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset'' <_\text{T} \emptyset''' \equiv_\text{T} B''$. By Theorem 8.11.2 relativized to $B$, we conclude that $A$ is not Schnorr random relative to $B$, since otherwise it would be 1-random relative to $B$, and hence 1-random. The symmetric argument shows that $B$ is not Schnorr random relative to $A$.

This result holds also for computable randomness because if $A \oplus B$ is computably random then it is Schnorr random, and if $A$ is not Schnorr random relative to $B$ then $A$ is not computably random relative to $B$.

</div>

By Theorem 8.11.1 (and the existence of an incomplete c.e. set, shown in Section 2.14.3), there is a computably random (and hence Schnorr random) set computable in an incomplete c.e. set, so we see that van Lambalgen's Theorem fails for Schnorr and computable randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.11.15 — Merkle, Miller, Nies, Reimann, and Stephan)</span></p>

There are sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A \oplus B$ is Schnorr random but $A$ and $B$ are not relatively Schnorr random. The same is true for computable randomness.

</div>

Barmpalias, Downey, and Ng have shown that this result holds also for weak 2-randomness.

### 8.12 Measure Theory and Turing Reducibility

The first applications of measure theory to computability theory were due to Spector, who showed that almost all pairs of hyperdegrees are incomparable, and, even earlier, to de Leeuw, Moore, Shannon, and Shapiro. Their result, which we now discuss, was not well known in the computability theory community until recently, and has as an immediate corollary another fundamental theorem first explicitly stated by Sacks. It says that if a set is c.e. relative to positive measure many oracles, then it is in fact c.e. Its proof uses what is known as the *majority vote technique*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.1 — de Leeuw, Moore, Shannon, and Shapiro)</span></p>

If $\mu(\lbrace X : A \text{ is c.e. in } X \rbrace) > 0$ then $A$ is c.e.

*Proof.* If $\lbrace X : A \text{ is c.e. in } X \rbrace$ has positive measure then there is an $e$ such that $S = \lbrace X : W^X_e = A \rbrace$ has positive measure. By the Lebesgue Density Theorem 1.2.3, there is an $X$ such that $\lim_n 2^n \mu(S \cap \llbracket X \upharpoonright n \rrbracket) > \frac{1}{2}$ and let $\sigma = X \upharpoonright n$. We can enumerate $A$ by "taking a vote" among the sets extending $\sigma$. More precisely, $n \in A$ iff $2^n \mu(\lbrace Y : \sigma \prec Y \wedge n \in W^Y_e \rbrace) > \frac{1}{2}$, and the set of $n$ for which this inequality holds is clearly c.e.

</div>

Theorem 8.12.1 has a very interesting corollary, which was not explicitly stated in the original paper, but later independently formulated by Sacks. For a set $A$, let $A^{\leqslant_\text{T}} = \lbrace B : A \leqslant_\text{T} B \rbrace$. It is natural to ask whether there is a noncomputable set $A$ such that $A^{\leqslant_\text{T}}$ has positive measure. Such a set would have a good claim to being "almost computable".

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.2 — Sacks)</span></p>

If $\mu(A^{\leqslant_\text{T}}) > 0$ then $A$ is computable.

*Proof.* If $\mu(A^{\leqslant_\text{T}}) > 0$ then there is an $i$ such that $\lbrace B : \Phi^B_i = A \rbrace$ has positive measure. It is now easy to show that there are $j$ and $k$ such that $\lbrace B : W^B_j = A \rbrace$ and $\lbrace B : W^B_k = \overline{A} \rbrace$ both have positive measure. Thus $A$ and $\overline{A}$ are both c.e.

</div>

It is not hard to adapt the proof of Theorem 8.12.1 to show that if $\mu(\lbrace B : A \text{ is } \Delta^{0,B}_2 \rbrace) > 0$ then $A$ is $\Delta^0_2$. From this result it follows that if $\mathbf{d} > \mathbf{0}'$, then $\mu(\lbrace B : \deg(B') \geqslant \mathbf{d} \rbrace) = 0$. In Section 8.14 we will see that, in fact, almost all sets are $\text{GL}_1$.

The following is a useful consequence of Corollary 8.12.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.3)</span></p>

If $A >_\text{T} \emptyset$ and $X$ is weakly 2-random relative to $A$, then $A \nleqslant_\text{T} X$.

*Proof.* Suppose that $\emptyset <_\text{T} A \leqslant_\text{T} X$. Let $e$ be such that $\Phi^X_e = A$, and let $S = \lbrace B : \Phi^B_e = A \rbrace$. Then $S$ is a $\Pi^{0,A}_2$ class, and by Corollary 8.12.2, it is null. Since $X \in S$, it follows that $X$ is not weakly 2-random relative to $A$.

</div>

We will see in Section 11.7 that this result does not hold for 1-randomness in place of weak 2-randomness, although it does come close to holding in that case. The sets $A$ that can be computed by a set that is 1-random relative to $A$ are called *bases for 1-randomness*, and turn out to form a class of "randomness-theoretically weak" sets with several natural characterizations. Also known as the *$K$-trivial sets*, they will be the focus of Chapter 11.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.4 — Kautz)</span></p>

If two sets are relatively weakly 2-random, then their degrees form a minimal pair.

*Proof.* Let $B$ and $C$ be relatively weakly 2-random, and let $A \leqslant_\text{T} B, C$. Since $B$ is weakly 2-random relative to $C$, it is weakly 2-random relative to $A$. By Corollary 8.12.3, $A$ is computable.

</div>

As we have seen in Corollary 8.8.3, Corollary 8.12.4 does not hold for 1-randomness, and indeed we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.5)</span></p>

The class of sets of the form $A \oplus B$ such that $A$ and $B$ form a minimal pair has measure 1, and includes all weakly 2-random sets but not all 1-random sets.

</div>

Note that, by Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law, Theorem 1.2.4, any reasonable degree-theoretic property like being the join of a minimal pair will hold of either almost all or almost no sets. As in the previous corollary, for many properties $P$ that hold of almost all sets, careful analysis has revealed the least $n$ such that $P$ holds of all $n$-random sets.

Corollary 8.12.2 has the following generalization, whose proof is a straightforward modification of the majority vote proof of Theorem 8.12.1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.6 — Stillwell)</span></p>

If $\mu(\lbrace X : A \leqslant_\text{T} B \oplus X \rbrace) > 0$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$.

</div>

An immediate corollary to this result (which we will improve in Theorem 8.15.1) is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.12.7 — Stillwell)</span></p>

Let $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ be degrees. Then $(\mathbf{a} \vee \mathbf{b}) \wedge (\mathbf{a} \vee \mathbf{c})$ is defined and equal to $\mathbf{a}$ for almost all $\mathbf{c}$.

*Proof.* Let $A \in \mathbf{a}$ and $B \in \mathbf{b}$. For any set $D$, Theorem 8.12.6 implies that $\mu(\lbrace C : D \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus C \wedge D \nleqslant_\text{T} A \rbrace) = 0$. Since there are only countably many $D \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus B$,

$$\mu(\lbrace C : \exists D \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus B\, (D \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus C \wedge D \nleqslant_\text{T} A) \rbrace) = 0.$$

So for almost all $C$, every $D$ that is computable in both $A \oplus B$ and $A \oplus C$ is also computable in $A$. Since $A$ is computable in $A \oplus B$ and $A \oplus C$, the corollary follows.

</div>

### 8.13 $n$-Randomness and Weak $n$-Randomness

In Theorem 7.2.7, we saw that every $n$-random set is weakly $n$-random, and every weakly $(n+1)$-random set is $n$-random. In this section, we show that neither implication can be reversed, even at the level of degrees.

An important special case of the following result was given in Theorem 7.2.8.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.13.1 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

If $A$ is weakly $(n+1)$-random and $\emptyset <_\text{T} B \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n)}$, then $A \vert_\text{T} B$.

*Proof.* Clearly $A \nleqslant_\text{T} B$, since otherwise $\lbrace A \rbrace$ would be a $\Pi^0_{n+1}$ class of measure 0, and hence cannot contain a weakly $(n+1)$-random set. Then $A$ is weakly $n$-random iff for every $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-computable sequence of $\Sigma^0_1$ classes $\lbrace U_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ such that $\mu(U_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$, we have $A \notin \bigcap_i U_i$. Let $P_e = \lbrace X : \Phi^X_e \text{ is total} \rbrace$, let $Q_e = \lbrace X : \forall k\, (\Phi^X_e(k) \!\downarrow\!= B(k)) \rbrace$, and let $R_e = P_e \cap Q_e$. Then $P_e$ is a $\Pi^0_2$ class, and $Q_e$ is a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n)}}_{1}$ class. Since $n \geqslant 1$, $R_e$ is a $\Pi^{0,\emptyset^{(n)}}_{1}$ class of measure 0, and hence cannot contain a weakly $(n+1)$-random set. The theorem now follows, since any set computing $B$ must be in $R_e$ for some $e$.

</div>

It follows immediately from the above theorem that no weakly $(n+1)$-random set can be $\emptyset^{(n)}$-computable, or even compute a noncomputable $\emptyset^{(n)}$-computable set. Since there are $\emptyset^{(n)}$-computable $n$-random sets, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.13.2 — Kurtz)</span></p>

For every $n \geqslant 1$, there is an $n$-random set that is not of weakly $(n+1)$-random degree, and indeed cannot be computed by any set of weakly $(n+1)$-random degree.

</div>

Kurtz showed that there is a weakly 1-random set that is not of 1-random degree. Kautz extended this result (at the set level) to show that if $n \geqslant 1$, then there is a weakly $n$-random set that is not $n$-random (though this result was stated without proof earlier by Gaifman and Snir). We give a simpler one, which works at the level of degrees as well, using the following relativized form of Theorem 7.2.25. Note that this result does not follow immediately by relativizing the proof of Theorem 7.2.25, because weak $(n+1)$-randomness is not the same as weak 1-randomness relative to $\emptyset^{(n)}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.13.3 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

If $X >_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n-1)}$ is c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$ then there is a weakly $n$-random set $A$ such that $A \oplus \emptyset^{(n-1)} \equiv_\text{T} X$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.13.4 — Kurtz, Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n \geqslant 1$. There is a weakly $n$-random set that is not of $n$-random degree.

*Proof.* Let $X$ be c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$ and such that $\emptyset^{(n-1)} <_\text{T} X <_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n)}$. By the theorem, there is a weakly $n$-random set $S$ such that $S \oplus \emptyset^{(n-1)} \equiv_\text{T} X$. By the relativized form of Corollary 8.8.2, if $Y$ is $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$-c.e. and there is an $n$-random set $R$ with $R \oplus \emptyset^{(n-1)} \equiv_\text{T} Y$, then $Y \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n)}$, so there is no $n$-random set Turing equivalent to $S$.

</div>

Barmpalias, Downey, and Ng have improved Theorem 8.13.3 in the $n = 2$ case as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.13.5 — Barmpalias, Downey, and Ng)</span></p>

If the degree of $X$ is hyperimmune relative to $\emptyset'$ then there is a weakly 2-random set $A$ such that $A \oplus \emptyset' \equiv_\text{T} A' \equiv_\text{T} X$.

</div>

There is no known exact characterization of the degrees that are jumps of weak 2-random sets.

### 8.14 Every 2-Random Set is $\text{GL}_1$

The fact that all 1-random left-c.e. reals are complete might lead to the false impression that randomness is a highness notion. In general, however, the reality is quite the opposite. We already saw results in this direction in Section 8.8, and will see further results showing that randomness is in fact antithetical to computational power, and in many ways, sufficiently random sets begin to resemble highly nonrandom (e.g., computable) ones. Thus randomness is in a sense a lowness notion. In this section, we prove a result that lends evidence to this claim. Recall that a set $X$ is generalized low ($\text{GL}_1$) if $X' \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset' \oplus X$. The following result generalizes Corollary 8.12.2. We will see in Corollary 8.14.4 that the $n + 1$ in its statement is optimal.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.14.1 — Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n > 0$. The class $\lbrace X : X^{(n)} \equiv_\text{T} X \oplus \emptyset^{(n)} \rbrace$ includes every $(n+1)$-random set, and hence has measure 1. In particular, every 2-random set is $\text{GL}_1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.14.2 — Demuth)</span></p>

All Demuth random sets are $\text{GL}_1$.

</div>

Using Theorem 8.14.1, Kautz generalized Theorem 8.5.1 as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.14.3 — Kautz)</span></p>

If $\mathbf{a} \geqslant \mathbf{0}^{(n)}$ then there is an $n$-random set $A$ with $A^{(n-1)} \in \mathbf{a}$.

*Proof.* The proof of Theorem 8.5.1 relativizes. Recall that in that proof we constructed a perfect tree $T \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ such that $[T] \subseteq \overline{U_0}$, where $U_0$ is the first element of Kučera's universal Martin-Löf test. To ensure the effectiveness of the coding of sets into paths of $T$ used in the proof, we used Lemma 8.5.2, which allows us to compute effective lower bounds for the measures of nonempty sets of the form $\overline{U_0} \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$. We can relative this construction to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. We can construct $T$ computably in $\emptyset^{(n)}$, now letting $U_0$ be the first element of Kučera's universal Martin-Löf test relativized to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. The necessary lower bounds given by the relativized form of Lemma 8.5.2 are now computable in $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$. We thus get an $n$-random set $A$ such that $A \oplus \emptyset^{(n-1)} \in \mathbf{a}$. By Theorem 8.14.1, $A^{(n-1)} \equiv_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset^{(n-1)}$, so $A^{(n-1)} \in \mathbf{a}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.14.4 — Kautz)</span></p>

Let $n > 0$. The class $\lbrace X : X^{(n)} \equiv_\text{T} X \oplus \emptyset^{(n)} \rbrace$ does not contain every $n$-random set.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be $n$-random and such that $A^{(n-1)} \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n)}$. Then $A^{(n)} \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n+1)}$ while $A \oplus \emptyset^{(n)} \leqslant_\text{T} A^{(n-1)} \oplus \emptyset^{(n)} \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n)}$.

</div>

By relativizing Theorem 8.14.1 and Corollary 8.14.4, we get the following useful result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.14.5)</span></p>

The class $\lbrace X : (X \oplus A)^{(n)} \equiv_\text{T} X \oplus A^{(n)} \rbrace$ includes every set that is $(n+1)$-random relative to $A$, and hence has measure 1, but does not include every set that is $n$-random relative to $A$.

</div>

### 8.15 Stillwell's Theorem

It is well known that the theory of the Turing degrees is undecidable, as shown by Lachlan, and in fact computably isomorphic to the theory of second order arithmetic, as shown by Simpson. On the other hand, in this section we use material from previous sections to prove Stillwell's Theorem that the "almost all" theory of the Turing degrees is decidable. Here the quantifier $\forall$ is interpreted to mean "for measure 1 many". By Fubini's Theorem (see Section 1.2), $A \subset (2^\omega)^n$ has measure 1 iff almost all sections of $A$ with first coordinate fixed have measure 1. This fact allows us to deal with nested quantifiers.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.15.1 — Stillwell)</span></p>

The "almost all" theory of the Turing degrees is decidable.

*Proof.* Here variables $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}, \ldots$ range over degrees. Our terms are built from these variables, $\mathbf{0}$, the jump operator $'$, $\vee$ (join), and $\wedge$ (meet). An atomic formula is one of the form $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ for terms $t_0, t_1$, and formulas in general are built from atomic ones using the connectives $\&$ (used here for "and" to avoid confusion with the meet symbol) and $\neg$, and the quantifier $\forall$, interpreted to mean "for measure 1 many".

Corollary 8.12.7 allows us to compute the meet of any two terms of the form $\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_k \vee \mathbf{0}^{(m)}$ and $\mathbf{b}_0 \vee \mathbf{b}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{b}_{k'} \vee \mathbf{0}^{(n)}$ as $\mathbf{c}_0 \vee \mathbf{c}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{c}_l \vee \mathbf{0}^{(\min\lbrace m, n \rbrace)}$, where the $\mathbf{c}_i$ are the variables common to both terms. For example, $(\mathbf{a}_1 \vee \mathbf{a}_3 \vee \mathbf{0}^{(4)}) \wedge (\mathbf{a}_1 \vee \mathbf{a}_5 \vee \mathbf{a}_7 \vee \mathbf{0}^{(6)}) = \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \mathbf{0}^{(4)}$. Note that we also have $\mathbf{a} \wedge \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$.

Now we can give a normal form for terms. By Theorem 8.14.5, $\mathbf{a}^{(n)} = \mathbf{a} \vee \mathbf{0}^{(n)}$ for almost all $\mathbf{a}$. We can use this fact to compute the jump of any term of the form $\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l)}$. That is, as $(\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l)})' = \mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l+1)}$, it follows that

$$(\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l)})' = \mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l+1)}.$$

Hence, using the rule for $\wedge$, the jump rule, and the join rule $(\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l)}) \vee (\mathbf{b}_0 \vee \mathbf{b}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{b}_n \vee \mathbf{0}^{(k)}) = (\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{b}_0 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{b}_n \vee \mathbf{0}^{(\max\lbrace l, k \rbrace)})$, we can reduce any term $t$ to one of the form

$$\mathbf{a}_0 \vee \mathbf{a}_1 \vee \cdots \vee \mathbf{a}_m \vee \mathbf{0}^{(l)}.$$

The proof is completed by giving the decision procedure. We show that every formula with free variables is *effectively 0-1-valued*. That is, it is satisfied by a set of instances of measure 0 or 1, and we can effectively determine which is the case. We proceed by structural induction.

To see that $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ is effectively 0-1-valued, first put the terms $t_i$ in normal form, then calculate $t_0 \wedge t_1$. If $t_0 \wedge t_1 = t_0$ then $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ for almost all instances. Otherwise, we must have $t_0 \wedge t_1 < t_0$, either because $t_0$ contains a variable not in $t_1$, or because the $\mathbf{0}^{(l)}$ term in $t_0$ is too big.
