In either case, $t_0 \leqslant t_1$ is false for almost all instances.

If $\psi_0, \psi_1$ are effectively 0-1-valued then clearly so are $\psi_0 \& \psi_1$ and $\neg\psi_i$. Fubini's Theorem shows that if $\psi$ is effectively 0-1-valued, then so is $\forall a_i \psi(a_0, \ldots, a_n)$, thus completing the proof.

</div>

### 8.16 DNC Degrees and Autocomplexity

Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan extended Theorem 8.8.1 to characterize the sets that compute diagonally noncomputable functions in terms of Kolmogorov complexity. Kanovich formulated the following definition of complexity and autocomplexity for computably enumerable sets, and stated the result that complex c.e. sets are wtt-complete and autocomplex sets are Turing complete.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.1 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan; Kanovich)</span></p>

(i) $A$ is *$h$-complex* if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant h(n)$ for all $n$.

(ii) $A$ is *(order) complex* if there is a computable order $h$ such that $A$ is $h$-complex.

(iii) $A$ is *autocomplex* if there is an $A$-computable order $h$ such that $A$ is $h$-complex.

</div>

Notice that we could have used $K$ in place of $C$ in the definition above, since the two complexities differ by only a log factor. Notice too that every 1-random set is complex.

We now establish some interesting characterizations of autocomplexity and complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.2 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is autocomplex.

(ii) There is an $A$-computable $h$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright h(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$.

(iii) There is an $A$-computable $f$ such that $C(f(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$.

(iv) For every $(A)$-computable (order) $h$, there is an $A$-computable $g$ such that $C(g(n)) \geqslant h(n)$ for all $n$.

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii). Let $g$ be an $A$-computable order such that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant g(n)$ for all $n$. Then we get (ii) by taking $h(n) = \min\lbrace k : g(k) \geqslant n \rbrace$.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii). Let $\widehat{f}(n)$ be the encoding of $A \upharpoonright h(n)$ as a natural number. Then $C(\widehat{f}(n)) \geqslant n - O(1)$, so we can take $f(n) = \widehat{f}(n+k)$ for a suitable $k$.

(iii) $\Leftrightarrow$ (iv). Clearly (iv) implies (iii). Now let $h$ be $A$-computable and let $f$ be as in (iii). Let $g(n) = f(h(n))$. Then $C(g(n)) = C(f(h(n))) \geqslant h(n)$.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (i). Let $f$ be as in (iii). Let $\Phi$ be such that $\Phi^A = f$ and let $g$ be the use function of that computation, which by the usual assumptions on the use is an $A$-computable order. Then for any $m \geqslant g(n)$, the value of $f(n)$ can be computed from $n$ and $A \upharpoonright m$, so $n \leqslant C(f(n)) \leqslant C(A \upharpoonright m) + 2 \log n + O(1)$. Hence, for almost all $n$ and all $m \geqslant g(n)$, we have $C(A \upharpoonright m) \geqslant \frac{n}{2}$. Therefore, a finite variation of the $A$-computable order $n \mapsto \frac{1}{2}\max\lbrace m : g(m) \leqslant n \rbrace$ witnesses the autocomplexity of $A$.

</div>

Similar methods allow us to characterize the complex sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.3 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is complex.

(ii) There is a computable function $h$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright h(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$.

(iii) $A$ tt-computes a function $f$ such that $C(f(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$.

(iv) $A$ wtt-computes a function $f$ such that $C(f(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$.

</div>

The following result can be viewed as the ultimate generalization of Kučera's Theorem 8.8.1 that 1-randoms have DNC degree.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.4 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

A set is autocomplex iff it is of DNC degree.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be autocomplex and let $f$ be as in Lemma 8.16.2 (iii). Then for almost all $n$,

$$C(\Phi_n(n)) \leqslant C(n) + O(1) \leqslant \log n + O(1) < n \leqslant C(f(n)),$$

so a finite variation of $f$ is DNC.

Now suppose that $A$ is not autocomplex. We show that no $\Phi_i^A$ is DNC. Fix $i$. Let $U$ be the universal machine used to define $C$. For each $\sigma$ let $e(\sigma)$ be such that $\Phi_{e(\sigma)}(n)$ is computed as follows. If $U(\sigma) = \tau$ for some $\tau$, then compute $\Phi_i^\tau(n)$ and output the result if this computation halts.

Let $h(n)$ be the maximum of the uses of all computations $\Phi_i^A(e(\sigma))$ with $\lvert\sigma\rvert < n$. Then $h \leqslant_\text{T} A$, so by Lemma 8.16.2, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright h(n)) < n$. For any such $n$, let $\sigma_n \in 2^{<n}$ be such that $U(\sigma_n) = A \upharpoonright h(n)$. Then

$$\Phi_{e(\sigma_n)}(e(\sigma_n)) = \Phi_i^{A \upharpoonright h(n)}(e(\sigma_n)) = \Phi_i^A(e(\sigma_n)),$$

so $\Phi_i^A$ is not DNC.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.5 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is complex.

(ii) $A$ tt-computes a DNC function.

(iii) $A$ wtt-computes a DNC function.

In particular, the sets that wtt-compute DNC functions and those that wtt-compute computably bounded DNC functions coincide.

</div>

By Theorems 8.10.3 and 8.16.4, there is an autocomplex set that does not compute any 1-random set. Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan showed that the proof of Theorem 8.10.3 can be used to extend this result as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.6 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

There is a complex set that does not compute any 1-random set.

</div>

Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan also showed that a set is complex iff it is not wtt-reducible to any hyperimmune set. Miller defined a set $A$ to be *hyperavoidable* if there is a computable order $h$ such that if $\Phi_e(n) \downarrow$ for all $n < e$ then $A \upharpoonright h(e) \neq \Phi_e \upharpoonright h(e)$. Miller showed that these sets are exactly the ones that are not wtt-reducible to a hyperimmune set, so it turns out that a set is hyperavoidable iff it is complex.

Initial segment complexity and Turing complexity are strongly related in the presence of effective enumerations. For instance, in Theorem 9.14.6, we will show that if a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is 1-random and $B$ is a c.e. set, then there is a wtt-reduction $\Gamma$ with identity use such that $\Gamma^\alpha = B$. In the present context, we have the following result, which follows from the lemmas above and Arslanov's Completeness Criterion (Theorem 2.22.3; see also the comment following the proof of that theorem and Theorem 2.22.1, which says that computing DNC functions is equivalent to computing fixed-point free functions).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.7 — Kanovich)</span></p>

Let $A$ be a c.e. set.

(i) $A$ is complex iff $A$ is wtt-complete.

(ii) $A$ is autocomplex iff $A$ is Turing complete.

</div>

We finish this section with a result that will be important when we consider lowness for weak 1-randomness in Section 12.4. A set $A$ is *weakly computably traceable* if there is a computable $h$ such that for all $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$, there is a computable $g$ with $\lvert D_{g(n)} \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ for all $n$ and $f(n) \in D_{g(n)}$ for infinitely many $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.16.8 — Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) The degree of $A$ is DNC or high.

(ii) $A$ is autocomplex or of high degree.

(iii) There is a $g \leqslant_\text{T} A$ such that either $g$ dominates all computable functions or for every partial computable $h$, we have $g(n) \neq h(n)$ for almost all $n$.

(iv) There is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ such that for every computable $h$, we have $f(n) \neq h(n)$ for almost all $n$.

(v) $A$ is not weakly computably traceable.

Furthermore, if the degree of $B$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC, then for each $g \leqslant_\text{T} B$, there are computable $h$ and $\widehat{h}$ such that

$$\forall n\; \exists m \in [n, \widehat{h}(n)] \; (h(m) = g(m)).$$

*Proof.* (i) $\Leftrightarrow$ (ii) by Theorem 8.16.4.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (v). Suppose that (v) fails but $A$ is either autocomplex or high. Let $h$ be a computable function witnessing the fact that $A$ is weakly computably traceable.

If $A$ is autocomplex, by Lemma 8.16.2, there is an $A$-computable function $p$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright p(n)) > \log n + h(n)$ for all $n$. Let $g$ be a computable function such that $\lbrace D_{g(n)} \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ weakly traces $p(n)$ and $\lvert D_{g(n)} \rvert < h(n)$ for all $n$. We can describe $p(n)$ by giving $n$ and the program for $g$, together with $\log h(n)$ many bits to say which of the elements of $D_{g(n)}$ is $p(n)$. Thus for almost all $n$, we have $C(p(n)) \leqslant \log n + O(\log h(n)) < \log n + h(n)$, contradicting the choice of $p$.

If $A$ is high, then let $p \leqslant_\text{T} A$ dominate all computable functions. Let $g$ be a computable function such that $\lbrace D_{g(n)} \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ weakly traces $p(n)$. Then the function $n \mapsto \max D_{g(n)} + 1$ is computable, so there are infinitely many $n$ such that $p > \max D_{g(n)}$, which is a contradiction.

(v) $\Rightarrow$ (iv). If (iv) fails then given any $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$, there is a computable function $h$ with $f(n) = h(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. Then (v) fails by letting $g$ be such that $D_{g(n)} = \lbrace h(n) \rbrace$.

(iv) $\Rightarrow$ (iii). If $A$ is high then, by Theorem 8.16.4, there is an $A$-computable function that dominates all computable functions. So suppose that $A$ is not high. Let $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ be such that for every computable $h$, we have $f(n) \neq h(n)$ for almost all $n$.

Suppose $\varphi$ is a partial computable function such that $f(n) = \varphi(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. Let $p \leqslant_\text{T} A$ be such that for each $k$ there is an $n \geqslant k$ with $\varphi(n)[p(k)] \downarrow = f(n)$. Since $A$ is not high, there is a computable function $q$ such that $q(k) > p(k)$ for infinitely many $k$. We may assume that $q$ is nondecreasing. Let $h(n) = \varphi(n)[q(n)]$ if this value is defined, and $h(n) = 0$ otherwise. Then $h$ is total and computable. If $q(k) \geqslant p(k)$ then there is an $n \geqslant k$ such that $\varphi(n)[q(n)] = \varphi(n)[p(k)] = f(n)$, whence $h(n) = f(n)$. Thus there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f(n) = h(n)$, contradicting the choice of $f$.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (i). By Theorem 2.23.7, if $g$ dominates all computable functions then $A$ is high. Otherwise, let $h(e) = \Phi_e(e)$. Then $g(e) \neq h(e)$ for almost all $e$, so a finite variation of $g$ is DNC.

Finally, we verify the last part of the theorem. Let $B$ be a set whose degree is hyperimmune-free and not DNC, and let $g \leqslant_\text{T} B$. Then $B$ is not high, so there is a computable $h$ such that $h(n) = g(n)$ infinitely often. Let $f(n)$ be the least $m \geqslant n$ such that $h(m) = g(m)$. Then $f \leqslant_\text{T} B$. Since $B$ has hyperimmune-free degree, there is a computable function $\widehat{h}$ such that $f(n) \leqslant \widehat{h}(n)$ for all $n$. Then $\forall n\; \exists m \in [n, \widehat{h}(n)] \; (h(m) = g(m))$.

</div>

### 8.17 Randomness and $n$-Fixed-Point Freeness

Many results in this book demonstrate that randomness is antithetical to computational strength, and is in a sense a lowness property. Nevertheless, as the results of this section will show, $n$-random sets do have some computational power.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.17.1 — Jockusch, Lerman, Soare, and Solovay)</span></p>

We define a relation $A \sim_n B$ as follows.

(i) $A \sim_0 B$ if $A = B$.

(ii) $A \sim_1 B$ if $A =^* B$.

(iii) If $n \geqslant 2$ then $A \sim_n B$ if $A^{(n-2)} \equiv_\text{T} B^{(n-2)}$.

A total function $f$ is *$n$-fixed-point free* ($n$-FPF) if $W_{f(x)} \not\sim_n W_x$ for all $x$.

</div>

Note that the 0-FPF functions are just the usual fixed-point free functions. In Theorem 8.8.1 we saw that every 1-random set computes a DNC function, and hence a fixed-point free function. We now extend this result as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.17.2 — Kučera)</span></p>

Every $(n+1)$-random set computes an $n$-FPF function.

*Proof.* We begin with the case $n = 1$, proving that each 2-random set computes a 1-FPF function. We then indicate how to modify that proof to obtain the general case.

Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be Kučera's universal Martin-Löf test, as constructed in Section 8.5. We need only the first element of this test, whose definition we now recall. Let $S_0, S_1, \ldots$ be an effective list of the prefix-free c.e. subsets of $2^{<\omega}$. For each $e > 0$, enumerate all elements of $S_{\Phi_e(e)}$ (where we take $S_{\Phi_e(e)}$ to be empty if $\Phi_e(e) \uparrow$) into a set $R$ as long as $\sum_{\sigma \in S_{\Phi_e(e)}, x} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} < 2^{-e}$. (That is, if this sum ever gets to $2^{-e}$, stop enumerating the elements of $S_{\Phi_e(e)}$ into $R$ before it does.) Let $U_0 = \llbracket R \rrbracket$.

This construction relativizes to a given set $X$ in the obvious way, yielding a class $U_0^X$. Let $D^X$ be the complement of $U_0^X$. Note that $D^X$ has positive measure.

Now let $A$ be 2-random. Since we are interested in $A$ only up to degree, we may assume by Theorem 6.10.2 that $A \in D^{\emptyset'}$. Let $h$ be a computable function such that $W_{h(e)}^{\emptyset'} = \lbrace i : W_e^{[i]}$ is finite$\rbrace$. (Recall that $W_e^{[i]}$ is the $i$th column of $W_e$.) For each $e$ and $j$, define the $\Sigma_1^{0,\emptyset'}$ class $V_j^e$ as follows. If $\lvert W_{h(e)}^{\emptyset'} \rvert < j + 1$ then let $V_j^e = \emptyset$. Otherwise, let $F_j$ be the smallest $j+1$ many elements of $W_{h(e)}^{\emptyset'}$ and let $V_j^e = \bigcup\lbrace \llbracket\tau\rrbracket : \forall i \in F_j \; (\tau(i) = 1) \rbrace$. The $V_j^e$ are uniformly $\Sigma_1^{0,\emptyset'}$ classes, and $\mu(V_j^e) < 2^{-j}$. Using the recursion theorem with parameters, let $g$ be a computable function such that $S_{\Phi_{g(e)}(g(e))} = V_{g(e)}^e$ for all $e$. We may assume that $g(e) > 0$ for all $e$.

By the construction of $U_0^{\emptyset'}$, we have $V_{g(e)}^e \cap D^{\emptyset'} = \emptyset$ for all $e$. Let $\sigma_e$ be the shortest initial segment of $A$ containing $g(e) + 1$ many elements. Then, for each $e$, either $\lvert W_{h(e)}^{\emptyset'} \rvert < g(e) + 1$ or there must be an $i < \lvert\sigma_e\rvert$ such that $W_{h(e)}^{\emptyset'}(i) \neq \sigma_e(i)$, since otherwise we would have $\llbracket\sigma_e\rrbracket \subseteq V_{g(e)}^e$, contradicting our assumption that $A \in D^{\emptyset'}$.

We are ready to define our 1-FPF function $f$. Let $f$ be an $A$-computable function such that

$$W_{f(e)} = \lbrace \langle i, n \rangle : i < \lvert\sigma_e\rvert \wedge \sigma_e(i) = 0 \wedge n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace.$$

We may assume that $A(0) = 0$, so that $W_{f(e)}^{\emptyset^{(n-2)}} \geqslant_\text{T} B^{[0]} \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n-2)}$. We claim that $W_e^{(n-2)} \not\equiv_\text{T} W_{f(e)}^{\emptyset^{(n-2)}}$ for all $e$.

The argument for the case $n \geqslant 2$ proceeds similarly, replacing $\emptyset'$ by $\emptyset^{(n)}$ and working with $(n+1)$-random sets in $D^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$.

</div>

### 8.18 Jump Inversion

In this section, we show that the jump operator can be used to study the distribution of 1-random ($\Delta_2^0$) degrees. We do this by proving a basis theorem for $\Pi_1^0$ classes of positive measure. It is of course not the case that a nontrivial $\Pi_1^0$ class must have members with all possible jumps, since we could be dealing with a countable $\Pi_1^0$ class, which might even have only one noncomputable member. However, as we recall from Theorem 2.19.18, if $P$ is a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class with no computable members and $X \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, then there is an $A \in P$ such that $A' \equiv_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset' \equiv_\text{T} X$.

Applying this result to a $\Pi_1^0$ class of 1-random sets, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.18.1)</span></p>

If $X \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ then there is a 1-random $A$ such that $A' \equiv_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset' \equiv_\text{T} X$.

</div>

The situation for $\Delta_2^0$ 1-randoms is less clear, because there is no extension of Theorem 2.19.18 where we additionally have $A \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$. In fact, Cenzer showed that there are $\Pi_1^0$ classes with no computable members such that every member is $\text{GL}_1$, and hence every $\Delta_2^0$ member is low.

To prove jump inversion for $\Delta_2^0$ 1-randoms, we replace Theorem 2.19.18 with a similar basis theorem for *fat* $\Pi_1^0$ classes.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.18.2 — Kučera; Downey and Miller)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a $\Pi_1^0$ class of positive measure, and let $X \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ be $\Sigma_2^0$. Then there is a $\Delta_2^0$ set $A \in P$ such that $A' \equiv_\text{T} X$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.18.3 — Kučera; Downey and Miller)</span></p>

For every $\Sigma_2^0$ set $X \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, there is a 1-random $\Delta_2^0$ set $A$ such that $A' \equiv_\text{T} X$.

</div>

The proof of Theorem 8.18.2 can be viewed as a finite injury construction relative to $\emptyset'$. In that sense, it is similar to Sacks' construction in [342] of a minimal degree below $\emptyset'$. We require two additional ideas. The first is the method of forcing with $\Pi_1^0$ classes, which was introduced by Jockusch and Soare to prove the low basis theorem. This method is used to ensure that $A' \leqslant_\text{T} X$. The second is Kučera's Lemma 8.10.1, which allows us to computably bound the positions of branchings as in Kučera coding.

*Proof of Theorem 8.18.2.* Let $P$ be a $\Pi_1^0$ class with positive measure and let $Q$ and $c$ be as in Lemma 8.10.1. That is, $Q \subseteq P$ is a $\Pi_1^0$ class, and for an effective listing $P_0, P_1, \ldots$ of $\Pi_1^0$ classes, if $Q \cap P_e \neq \emptyset$, then $\mu(Q \cap P_e) \geqslant 2^{-(e+c)}$. Note that $\mu(Q) > 0$ since $P_e = Q$ for some $e$.

We build an $A \in Q$ with $V^A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. We construct $A$ by initial segments using a $\emptyset'$ oracle. At each stage $s$ of the construction, we will define a string $\sigma_s \in 2^{<\omega}$ and a string $\tau_s \in 2^s$, which is intended to approximate $A' \upharpoonright s$. We will tentatively restrict $A$ to the class $F_{\sigma_s}$ in order to force its jump. It is important to note that this restriction may be injured at a later stage by the enumeration of an $e < s$ into $X$.

Next we define a computable function $f$ that grows fast enough to ensure that it eventually bounds the branchings between elements of $F_\sigma$ for every $\sigma$. We will use $f$ to code elements of $X$ into $A$ (or more precisely, into $A'$). Let $h : 2^{<\omega} \times 2^{<\omega} \to \omega$ be a computable function such that $P_{h(\tau,\sigma)} = \llbracket\tau\rrbracket \cap F_\sigma$ for all $\tau$ and $\sigma$. Let $f(0) = 0$ and let

$$f(s+1) = 1 + \max\lbrace h(\tau,\sigma) + c : \tau \in 2^{f(s)} \wedge \sigma \in 2^s \rbrace.$$

The construction proceeds in stages, with stages using a $\emptyset'$ oracle and the computation being computable relative to $\emptyset'$.

</div>

### 8.19 Pseudo-Jump Inversion

In the same way that the construction of a low c.e. set was generalized by Jockusch and Shore (as we have seen in Section 2.12.2), Nies and Kučera independently realized that Theorem 8.18.2 can be extended to a similar pseudo-jump theorem. Recall that an operator of the form $V^Y = Y \oplus W^Y$ for a c.e. set $W$ with $Y <_\text{T} V^Y$ for all oracles $Y$ is called a nontrivial pseudo-jump operator. The following is a randomness version of Theorem 2.12.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.19.1 — Nies; Kučera)</span></p>

For any nontrivial pseudo-jump operator $V$ there is a 1-random set $B$ with $V^B \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. Indeed, if $P$ is any $\Pi_1^0$ class of positive measure, then there is an $A \in P$ such that $V^A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$.

</div>

Nies' proof is a full approximation argument, and hence an effective construction, but we will use Kučera's oracle construction, as it is simpler.

*Proof of Theorem 8.19.1.* The proof resembles that of the Friedberg Completeness Criterion, Theorem 2.16.1, combined with Kučera-Gács coding. Let $P$ be a $\Pi_1^0$ class of positive measure, and let $Q$ and $c$ be as in Lemma 8.10.1. That is, $Q \subseteq P$ is a $\Pi_1^0$ class, and for an effective listing $P_0, P_1, \ldots$ of $\Pi_1^0$ classes, if $Q \cap P_e \neq \emptyset$, then $\mu(Q \cap P_e) \geqslant 2^{-(e+c)}$. Let $k > -\log \mu(Q)$.

We build an $A \in Q$ with $V^A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$. As in the proof of the Friedberg Completeness Criterion, at even stages we force the (pseudo-)jump and at odd ones we code.

At stage 0 we consider the $\Pi_1^0$ class $M_0 = \lbrace Y : 0 \notin V^Y \rbrace$. Let $e$ be such that $P_e = Q_0$ and let $m_0 = 2^{e+c} + 1$. Then $\mu(Q_0) > 2^{-m_0}$, so there is a split in $Q_0$ by level $m_0$. That is, there are at least two strings of length $m_0$ that can be extended to elements of $Q_0$. As with Kučera-Gács coding, if $0 \in \emptyset'$ then let $A_1$ be the rightmost such string, and otherwise let $A_1$ be the leftmost such string. Let $Q_1 = Q_0 \cap \llbracket A_1 \rrbracket$.

The construction now works inductively in the obvious way. At stage $2n$ we look at $M_n = \lbrace Y : n \notin V^Y \rbrace$. If $M_n \cap Q_{n-1} = \emptyset$, we let $Q_n = Q_{n-1} = A_{2n-1}$. Otherwise, we let $Q_n = Q_{n-1} \cap M_0$. In either case we let $A_{2n} = A_{2n-1}$. At stage $2n + 1$, we let $e$ be such that $P_e = Q_{2n}$ and let $m_n = 2^{e+c+1}$. As before, there are at least two strings of length $m_n$ that can be extended to elements of $Q_{2n}$, and with Kučera-Gács coding, if $0 \in \emptyset'$ then let $A_{2n+1}$ be the rightmost such string, and otherwise we let $A_{2n+1}$ be the leftmost such string. Finally, we let $Q_{2n+1} = Q_{2n} \cap \llbracket A_{2n+1} \rrbracket$.

It is easy to see that the questions we have to ask of $\emptyset'$ at each stage can be computably bounded in advance, so this construction produces an $A$ with $V^A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$.

Other extensions along similar lines can be found in Cenzer, LaForte, and Wu. For instance, using somewhat similar techniques, they showed the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.19.2 — Cenzer, LaForte, and Wu)</span></p>

Let $V$ be a nontrivial pseudo-jump operator, let $P$ be a $\Pi_1^0$ class of positive measure, and let $C \geqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$. Then there is an $A \in P$ such that $V^A \equiv_\text{T} C$.

</div>

It is interesting to note that, in contrast to Theorem 8.19.1, there are nontrivial pseudo-jump operators $V$ for which there is no c.e. set $A$ with $V^A \equiv_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$, as was recently proved by Ng and Wu.

### 8.20 Randomness and Genericity

Martin-Löf's original definition of 1-randomness arises from the classical interpretation of randomness as a notion of being *typical in terms of measure*. We can also look at sets that are typical in terms of category. These are the (Cohen) generic sets, which are members of every (suitably definable) dense open class. In the effective setting, we have the $n$-generic and weakly $n$-generic sets introduced in Section 2.24.

In this section, we look at how these two notions of being typical relate to each other. The answer, perhaps surprisingly, is that genericity and randomness are more or less orthogonal, especially when we look at higher levels of the arithmetic hierarchy of classes.

#### 8.20.1 Similarities Between Randomness and Genericity

Before concentrating on the differences between randomness and genericity, we note that some results we have discussed above are special cases of general results on forcing, and hence have analogs for genericity. Van Lambalgen's Theorem is an example.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.1 — Yu)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for any $n \geqslant 1$.

(i) $A \oplus B$ is $n$-generic.

(ii) $A$ is $n$-generic and $B$ is $n$-generic relative to $A$.

(iii) $B$ is $n$-generic and $A$ is $n$-generic relative to $B$.

(iv) $A$ and $B$ are relatively $n$-generic (i.e., $A$ is $n$-generic relative to $B$ and $B$ is $n$-generic relative to $A$).

</div>

Another example of a theorem about randomness that has an analog for genericity is Theorem 8.5.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.2 — Csima, Downey, Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Miller)</span></p>

Let $B$ be 1-generic relative to $X$ and let $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$ be 2-generic. Then $A$ is 1-generic relative to $X$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.3 — Csima, Downey, Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Miller)</span></p>

If $B$ is $n$-generic and $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$ is 2-generic, then $A$ is $n$-generic.

*Proof.* Let $X = \emptyset^{(n-1)}$ in Theorem 8.20.2.

</div>

It is natural to ask whether the full analog of Theorem 8.5.3 holds for genericity, that is, whether a 1-generic set below an $n$-generic set is automatically $n$-generic. The following result, whose proof can be found in Csima, Downey, Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Miller, gives a negative answer to this question.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.4 — Csima, Downey, Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Miller)</span></p>

Every 1-generic set computes a 1-generic set that is not weakly 2-generic.

</div>

#### 8.20.2 $n$-Genericity Versus $n$-Randomness

We now discuss the interplay between $n$-genericity and $n$-randomness. In general, genericity and randomness are antithetical. We already saw in Proposition 8.11.9 that 1-genericity is enough to ensure that a set is not even Schnorr random. For 1-randomness, we have the following stronger result. (As we will see in Theorem 8.11.7, for weak 1-randomness, the situation is quite different.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.5 — Demuth and Kučera)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-generic and $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ then $B$ is not 1-random.

*Proof.* By Theorem 2.24.5, the degree of $B$ is not DNC, and hence by Theorem 8.8.1, $B$ is not 1-random.

</div>

Since $\Omega \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset'$ and there are $\Delta_2^0$ 1-generics, this theorem works only in one direction.

Kurtz showed that the upward closure of the weakly 2-generic degrees has measure 0. (We will see in Theorem 8.21.3 that this result cannot be extended to 1-genericity.) To prove this result, we need the following lemma, whose proof is fairly typical (no pun intended) of measure-theoretic arguments in computability theory.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.6 — Kurtz)</span></p>

There is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ such that for almost every $A$, the function $f$ dominates every $A$-computable function.

*Proof.* It is enough to build uniformly $\emptyset'$-computable functions $f_{n,k}$ such that for each $n$ and $k$, for measure $1 - 2^{-(n+k+1)}$ many sets $A$, if $\Phi_k^A$ is total then $f_{n,k}$ majorizes $\Phi_k^A$. We can then let $f(m) = \max_{k,n \leqslant m} f_{k,n}(m)$ and it is easy to check that $f$ is as required, since it dominates every $f_{n,k}$.

We define $f_{n,k}(m)$ as follows. $S = \lbrace A : \Phi_k^A(m) \downarrow \rbrace$. It is easy to see that $\mu(S)$ is a left-c.e. real, so using $\emptyset'$, we can find a rational $q$ such that $q < \mu(S)$ and $\mu(S) - q \leqslant 2^{-(n+k+m+2)}$. We can then find a prefix-free finite set $F$ of strings such that $\sigma \in F \Rightarrow \Phi_k^{\sigma}(m) \downarrow$ and $\mu(\llbracket F \rrbracket) > q$. Let $f_{n,k}(m) = \max\lbrace \Phi_k^{\sigma}(m) : \sigma \in F \rbrace$.

This definition ensures that, for each $m$, the class of all $A$ such that $\Phi_k^A(m) \downarrow$ but not dominated by $f_{n,k}(m)$ has measure less than $2^{-(n+k+m+2)}$. Thus the class of all $A$ such that $\Phi_k^A$ is total but not majorized by $f_{n,k}$ is less than $\sum_m 2^{-(n+k+m+2)} = 2^{-(n+k+1)}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.20.7 — Kurtz)</span></p>

The upward closure of the weakly 2-generic degrees has measure 0.

*Proof.* Let $f$ be as in Lemma 8.20.6. Recall that the principal function $p_G$ of an infinite set $G = \lbrace a_0 < a_1 < \cdots \rbrace$ is defined by $p_G(n) = a_n$, and that for a string $\sigma$, we let $p_\sigma(k)$ be the position of the $k$th 1 in $\sigma$, or undefined if there is no such position. Let $S_n$ be the set of strings such that for at least $n$ many $k$, we have $p_\sigma(k) > f(k)$. Each $S_n$ is a $\Delta_2^0$ dense set of strings, so if $G$ is weakly 2-generic, then it meets each $S_n$, which implies that $p_G$ is not dominated by $f$. So if a set computes a weakly 2-generic set, then it must be in the class of measure 0 of sets computing functions not dominated by $f$.

</div>

### 8.21 Properties of Almost All Degrees

Randomness is, of course, deeply connected with "almost everywhere" behavior, since any property that holds of almost all sets holds of all sufficiently random sets. In this section, we prove several results about computability theoretic properties that hold of almost all degrees. We begin with Martin's Theorem 8.21.1 that almost all degrees are hyperimmune. We then proceed to three theorems due to Kurtz, with related proofs of increasing complexity: Theorem 8.21.3 that almost every degree bounds a 1-generic degree, Theorem 8.21.8 that almost every degree is CEA, and Theorem 8.21.16 that for almost every degree $\mathbf{a}$, the 1-generic degrees are downwards dense below $\mathbf{a}$. We will also comment on the levels of effective randomness needed to ensure that a particular set has each of these properties.

Of course, Theorem 8.21.16 implies Theorem 8.21.3 (as does Theorem 8.21.8, as we will see in Theorem 8.21.15), which in turn implies Theorem 8.21.1, since every 1-generic degree is hyperimmune, but presenting the simpler proofs of Theorems 8.21.1 and 8.21.3 will help in explaining Kurtz' method of risking measure, which is an elaboration of the method used in the proof of Martin's Theorem. (Similar ideas had even earlier been used by Paris, in proving Corollary 8.21.23 below.)

#### 8.21.1 Hyperimmunity

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.1 — Martin)</span></p>

Almost every degree is hyperimmune.

*Proof.* By Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law, Theorem 1.2.4, it suffices to prove that there are positive measure many sets of hyperimmune degree. To do so, we construct a functional $\Xi$ so that for positive measure many $A$, we have that $\Xi^A$ is total and not majorized by any computable function. We will meet the following requirements for positive measure many sets $A$.

$$R_e : \Phi_e \text{ total} \Rightarrow \Xi^A \text{ is not majorized by } \Phi_e.$$

The most naive attempt to meet $R_e$ is to choose a witness $n_e$ and define $\Xi^A(n_e) = \Phi_e(n_e) + 1$ for all $A$. The problem with this approach, of course, is that $\Phi_e(n_e)$ may not be defined, and we need to make $\Xi^A$ be total for positive measure many sets $A$. Thus we begin with a small class of $A$'s, choose a witness $n_e^0$, and define $\Xi^A(n_e^0) = \Phi_e(n_e^0) + 1$ for all $A$ in our class. If $\Phi_e(n_e^0) \uparrow$, then $\Xi^A(n_e^0) \uparrow$ for all such $A$, but $R_e$ is automatically met, because $\Phi_e$ is not total. If $\Phi_e(n_e^0) \downarrow$, at which point we define $\Xi^A(n_e^0) = \Phi_e(n_e^0) + 1$ for all such $A$, and then move to a new class of sets, using a new witness $n_e^1$. This process can continue until we either cover all sets or have a final class $C$ of sets for which we have appointed a harmful witness $n_e^k$ such that $\Phi_e(n_e^k) \uparrow$, whence $\Xi^A(n_e^k) \uparrow$ for all $A \in C$. By ensuring that the measure of $C$ is at most $2^{-(e+2)}$, we can restrict the total effect of harmful witnesses in the construction to a class of measure at most $\frac{1}{2}$.

The $R_e$ work independently. Let $\nu_i^0, \ldots, \nu_i^{2^i - 1}$ list $2^i$ in lexicographic order. Then $R_e$ begins by working only above $\nu_{e+2}^0$, appointing a witness $n_e^0$ for all extensions of $\nu_{e+2}^0$. We ask that $\Xi^A(n_e^0) \uparrow$ for all $A$ extending $\nu_{e+2}^0$, unless we see that $\Phi_e(n_e^0) \downarrow$, at which point we define $\Xi^A(n_e^0) = \Phi_e(n_e^0) + 1$ for all such $A$. We then begin working above $\nu_{e+2}^1$, appointing a fresh large witness $n_e^1$. Again we ask that $\Xi^A(n_e^1) \uparrow$ for all $A$ extending $\nu_{e+2}^1$ unless we see that $\Phi_e(n_e^1) \downarrow$, at which point we define $\Xi^A(n_e^1) = \Phi_e(n_e^1) + 1$ for all such $A$, and begin working above $\nu_{e+2}^2$, and so on. At each stage $s$, if no $R_e$ has asked that $\Xi^A(s) \uparrow$ or already defined $\Xi^A(s)$, then we define $\Xi^A(s) = 0$.

Since each $R_e$ can get stuck on at most one cone, and that cone has measure $2^{-(e+2)}$, we see that $\Xi^A$ fails to be total on a class of measure at most $\sum_e 2^{-(e+2)} = \frac{1}{2}$, and for all other $A$, each $R_e$ is met.

</div>

Kautz noted the following feature of the above proof. The class of all $A$ such that $\Xi^A$ is total is a $\Pi_2^0$ class of measure $\frac{1}{2}$, contains elements of all 2-random degrees. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.10.2, for construction, for every $A$ in this class, $\Xi^A$ is not majorized by any computable function, so we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.2 — Kautz)</span></p>

If $\mathbf{a}$ is 2-random, then $\mathbf{a}$ is hyperimmune. Thus, the class of sets of hyperimmune degree includes every 2-random set but not every 1-random set.

</div>

We have already seen, in Theorem 8.11.12, that this result cannot be extended from 2-randomness to weak 2-randomness. Note that this fact shows that Theorem 6.10.2 cannot be extended from $n$-randomness to weak $n$-randomness.

Kurtz showed that there is no single total computable operator $\Psi$ such that for almost every $A$, the function $\Psi^A$ is not majorized by any computable function.

Theorem 8.21.2 can also be obtained by the following elegant argument of Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn. By Theorem 6.11.7, there is a computable $g$ and a $d$ such that a set $A$ is 2-random iff $C^g(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. Fix a 2-random set $A$ and let $d$ be such that $C^g(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - d$ for all $n$. Let $f(k)$ be the least $n$ such that there are $p_0, \ldots, p_{k-1} \leqslant n$ with $C^g(A \upharpoonright p_i) \geqslant p_i - d$ for $i < k$. Then $f$ is $A$-computable. We claim that $f$ is not majorized by any computable function. Assume for a contradiction that $h$ is computable and majorizes $f$. Define the $\Pi_1^0$ class

$$P = \lbrace Z : \forall k\; \exists p_1, \ldots, p_k \leqslant h(k)\; (C^g(Z \upharpoonright p_i) \geqslant p_i - d) \rbrace.$$

Then $P \neq \emptyset$, and every member of $P$ is 2-random. Since $P$ must have a $\Delta_2^0$ member and there are no $\Delta_2^0$ sets that are 2-random, we have a contradiction.

#### 8.21.2 Bounding 1-Generics

The following result follows from Theorem 8.21.8 below, but discussing its proof will help us to understand the more difficult proofs of Theorems 8.21.8 and 8.21.16. It should be contrasted with Theorem 8.20.7.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.3 — Kurtz)</span></p>

Almost every set computes a 1-generic set.

*Proof Sketch.* By Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law, Theorem 1.2.4, it suffices to prove that there are positive measure many sets computing 1-generic sets. We build a functional $\Xi$ so that for positive measure many sets $A$, we have that $\Xi^A$ is total and the following requirements are met.

$$R_e : \Xi^A \text{ meets or avoids } V_e.$$

Let $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ be an effective enumeration of the c.e. sets of strings. We build $\Xi$ so that for positive measure many $A$, we have that $\Xi^A$ is total and the following requirements are met.

It will convenient to think of $\Xi$ as a partial computable function from strings to strings, but we write $\Xi^\sigma$ instead of $\Xi(\sigma)$. Recall from Section 2.9 that the main rule we need to observe is that for $\sigma \prec \tau$, if $\Xi^\sigma \downarrow$ and $\Xi^\tau \downarrow$, then $\Xi^\sigma \preccurlyeq \Xi^\tau$. Note that it is not necessary that if $\Xi^\tau \downarrow$ then $\Xi^\sigma \downarrow$ for all $\sigma \prec \tau$.

</div>

Kautz analyzed the proof of Theorem 8.21.3 and showed the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.4 — Kautz)</span></p>

Every 2-random set computes a 1-generic set.

</div>

In Theorem 8.21.8, we will show that every 2-random set is CEA, and in Theorem 8.21.15, that every CEA set computes a 1-generic, from which Theorem 8.21.4 will follow. We will also give a direct proof at the end of Section 8.21.3.

By Theorem 8.11.12, there are weakly 2-random sets of hyperimmune-free degree, and by Theorem 2.24.12, none of these can compute a 1-generic. Thus Theorem 8.21.4 cannot be extended from 2-randomness to weak 2-randomness.

Kurtz pointed out the following corollary to the above results.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.5 — Kurtz)</span></p>

For almost all degrees $\mathbf{a}$, and indeed for any 2-random degree $\mathbf{a}$, the degrees below $\mathbf{a}$ are not densely ordered.

*Proof.* Let $\mathbf{a}$ bound a 1-generic degree $\mathbf{b}$. By Theorem 2.24.9, $\mathbf{b}$ is c.e. in some strictly lower degree. The corollary now follows by applying the relativized form of Theorem 2.18.5 that every nonzero c.e. degree bounds a minimal degree.

</div>

Although almost every degree bounds a 1-generic degree, almost no degrees are bounded by a 1-generic degree.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.6 — Kurtz)</span></p>

Almost no set is computable in a 1-generic set.

*Proof.* Suppose otherwise. Then there must be a $\Phi$ and a $k$ for which the set of $A$ such that $\Phi^G = A$ for some 1-generic $G$ has measure greater than $2^{-k}$. We obtain a contradiction by building a c.e. $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\mu(\lbrace A : A = \Phi^B$ for some $B$ meeting $S \rbrace) \leqslant 2^{-k}$, and if $G$ is 1-generic, then either $G$ meets $S$ or $\Phi^G$ is not total.

Let $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ be an effective listing of $2^{<\omega}$. Let $f(i)$ be the least $n$ such that $\sigma_n \succcurlyeq \sigma$ and $\Phi^{\tau_n}$ is defined on at least $k + i + 1$ many inputs, if such an $n$ exists, and let $f(i)$ be undefined otherwise. Let $S = \lbrace \sigma_{f(i)} : f(i) \downarrow \rbrace$.

Clearly, $S$ is c.e. If $f(i) \downarrow$, then the class of all $A$ such that $A = \Phi^B$ for some $B \succ \sigma_{f(i)}$ has measure at most $2^{-(k+i+1)}$, so $\mu(\lbrace A : A = \Phi^B$ for some $B$ meeting $S \rbrace) \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-(k+i+1)} = 2^{-k}$. Now let $G$ be 1-generic. If $G$ does not meet $S$, which implies that $f(i) \uparrow$, and hence $\Phi^G$ can be defined on at most $k + i$ many inputs, so $\Phi^G$ is not total.

</div>

There is a local characterization of the sets computable in 1-generic ones. A c.e. $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ is a $\Sigma_1^0$-*cover* of a set $A$ if for each $\sigma \prec A$ there is a $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma$ in $S$. This $\Sigma_1^0$-cover is *proper* if no element of $S$ is an initial segment of $A$. It is *tight* if it is proper and for each $\Sigma_1^0$-cover $\widehat{S}$ of $A$, there are $\sigma \in S$ and $\tau \in \widehat{S}$ such that $\sigma \preccurlyeq \tau$. One might think of a tight cover as a "simple set for covers". The following result completely classifies the sets that are computable in 1-generic ones.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.7 — Chong and Downey)</span></p>

A set $A$ has no tight $\Sigma_1^0$-cover iff there is a 1-generic $G \geqslant_\text{T} A$.

</div>

The proof in Chong and Downey shows that there is a single procedure $\Phi$ such that if $A$ is computable in a 1-generic set, then there is a 1-generic $G \leqslant_\text{T} A''$ such that $A = \Phi^G$. Notice that, by Theorems 8.21.6 and 8.21.7, almost every set has a tight $\Sigma_1^0$-cover.

#### 8.21.3 Every 2-Random Set is CEA

In Theorem 2.24.9 we saw that every 1-generic set $A$ is CEA, that is, computably enumerable relative to some $X <_\text{T} A$. In this section we prove the remarkable result of Kurtz that being CEA is typical behavior for sets also in the measure-theoretic sense. That is, almost every set, and in fact every 2-random set, is CEA. Kurtz showed that the particular $X$ used in the proof of Theorem 2.24.9 will not work for this measure-theoretic version, because it is almost always $A$-computable, so a different and more complex approach will be needed. The proof resembles that of Theorem 8.21.3 (that almost every set computes a 1-generic set). The main ideas of the proof we present are due to Kurtz, but we will give the details as carefully presented by Kautz. Kautz' version allows us to show that 2-randomness suffices to ensure that a set is CEA.

The following result should also be compared with Theorem 15.4.1 below, which states that every 2-random real $\alpha$ is equal to $\Omega^B$ for some $B$ (and hence is left-c.e. relative to $B$). As we will see in Chapter 11, however, this $B$ cannot be $\alpha$-computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.8 — Kurtz; Kautz)</span></p>

Every 2-random set is CEA.

</div>

*Proof.* We construct a functional $\Xi$ so that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Xi^A \text{ total} \wedge \Xi^A \not\leqslant_\text{T} A \rbrace) \geqslant \frac{1}{4}$ and $A$ is c.e. in $\Xi^A$ whenever $\Xi^A$ is total. By Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law, Theorem 1.2.4, the existence of such a functional is enough to conclude that almost every set is CEA. At the end of the proof we will argue that 2-randomness suffices.

To make $A$ c.e. in $\Xi^A$ we will ask that $n \in A$ iff $\langle n, m \rangle \in \Xi^A$ for some $m$. We say that a string $\xi$ is *acceptable* for a string $\sigma$ if $\xi(\langle n, m \rangle) = 1 \Rightarrow \sigma(n) = 1$. We will always require that $\Xi^\sigma[s]$ be acceptable. We will also meet requirements of the form

$$R_e : A \neq \Phi_e^{\Xi^A}.$$

In the proof of Theorem 8.21.3, there are blue$_e$ strings, each of which is the base of a cone within which we are attempting to meet $R_e$. Above each such string, there is a tree of strings whose leaves are yellow$_e$, except for one red$_e$ leaf above which we are testing the c.e. set of strings $V_e$ in an attempt to turn the red$_e$ node green$_e$. The method of this construction is in some ways similar, but more complex.

As before, to attempt to meet $R_e$ in the cone above a blue$_e$ string $\nu$, we give the color red$_e$ to $\nu 1^{e+2}$, and give the other length $e + 2$ extensions of $\nu$ the color yellow$_e$. However, the role of the red$_e$ node will be different in this construction, and there are now new players, the purple$_e$ strings. These represent cones $R_e$ forbids us to build within during the construction, because we seem to be failing to satisfy $R_e$ within these cones. We cannot afford to allow the measure of such cones to become too large, so the role of the red$_e$ strings is, as Kurtz put it, to act as "safety valves" for the pressure exerted by the purple$_e$ strings. If the measure of cones corresponding to purple$_e$ strings becomes too large, the "safety valve" pops, and a backup strategy allows us to succeed.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.9)</span></p>

At a stage $s$, we say that a string $\theta \in 2^{\leqslant s}$ *threatens* a requirement $R_e$ if there are $\nu \prec \tau \preccurlyeq \theta$ such that $\nu$ is blue$_e$ and $\tau$ is yellow$_e$, and a $\xi \in 2^{\leqslant s}$ that is acceptable for $\theta$, such that

(i) $\Xi^\tau[s] \preccurlyeq \xi$,

(ii) $\Phi_e^\xi(k)[s] = \tau(k) = 0$ for some $k$ such that $\lvert\nu\rvert < k \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert$, and

(iii) no initial segment of $\theta$ has color purple$_j$ for $j \leqslant e$.

</div>

In the construction, we will give any string that threatens $R_e$ the color purple$_e$. If $\theta$ is purple$_e$, then its image under $\Xi$ could be an initial segment of a set $B$ such that $\Phi_e^B = A$ for some $A$ extending $\theta$. We hope to avoid having $\Xi^A = B$ for such $A$ and $B$, so we stop building $\Xi$ above purple$_e$ nodes.

If $A \succ \tau$ for a yellow$_e$ string $\tau$ and no initial segment of $A$ is purple$_j$ for $j \leqslant e$, then we cannot have $A = \Phi_e^{\Xi^A}$, and hence $A$ satisfies $R_e$. To see that this is the case, suppose otherwise. Then $\Phi_e^{\Xi^A}(k) = \nu(k) = 0$ for some $k$ with $\lvert\nu\rvert < k \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert$, since $\tau$ is not $\nu 1^{e+2}$. But then some initial segment of $A$ extending $\tau$ will eventually threaten $R_e$, and hence colored purple$_e$.

Thus, if there are not too many purple$_e$ strings, then $R_e$ is met on a class of sufficiently large measure. Our main concern is what to do when we do have too many purple$_e$ strings.

For each purple$_e$ string $\theta$, let $\theta'$ be the unique string of length $\lvert\theta\rvert$ extending the red$_e$ string extending $\nu$ with $\theta(m) = \theta'(m)$ for all $m \geqslant \lvert\rho\rvert$. Note that item (ii) above implies that there is a $k$ such that $\Phi_e^\xi(k) = \tau(k) = 0 \neq 1 = \rho(k)$, since $\rho = \nu 1^{e+2}$. Thus, if we choose to define $\Xi^A$ on sets $A \succ \rho$ to emulate $\Xi^\xi$, then we cannot have $\Phi_e^{\Xi^A} = A$ for such $A$. If the density of purple$_e$ strings above $\nu$ grows too big, then using this fact we will be able to satisfy $R_e$ above these $\theta'$, giving them color green$_e$.

More precisely, when the density of purple$_e$ strings above the blue$_e$ string $\nu$ exceeds $2^{-(e+3)}$, there must be some yellow$_e$ string $\tau$ such that the density of purple$_e$ strings above $\tau$ also exceeds $2^{-(e+3)}$. Let $\theta_0, \ldots, \theta_n$ be the purple$_e$ strings above such a $\tau$. For each $\theta_i$, let $\xi_i$ be a string witnessing the threat to $R_e$ as in Definition 8.21.9. Then

(i) $\xi_i$ is acceptable for $\theta_i$,

(ii) $\Xi^\tau \preccurlyeq \xi_i$, and

(iii) $\Phi_e^{\xi_i}(k) = \tau(k) = 0 = \theta_i(k)$ for some $k$ with $\rho(k) = 1$.

The construction will be such that $\Xi^{\rho}[s] = \Xi^\tau[s]$.

Now we act as follows. First, any string extending $\nu$ loses its color, including $\nu$ itself. (We are considering $R_e$ in isolation here. In the presence of other requirements, strings colored purple, for $i < e$ do not lose their colors, to respect the priority order of requirements.) Then we give each $\theta_i'$ color green$_e$. Finally, for each $i \leqslant n$, we define $\Xi^{\theta_i'} = \xi_i$, which ensures that $\Phi_e^{\Xi^{\theta_i'}}(k) \neq \theta_i'(k)$ for some $k$ with $\lvert\nu\rvert < k \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert$, justifying the use of the color green$_e$ for the strings $\theta_i'$. Note that each time we are forced to pop our safety valve in this way, we are guaranteed to satisfy $R_e$ on a set of relative measure at least $2^{-(e+5)}$ above $\nu$. We will be able to show that this measure is sufficient for our purposes. Notice also that we are now able to replicate this construction on strings extending those that have lost their color.

**Construction.**

At each stage $s$, we let $D_s$ be the set of strings $\sigma$ on which we have defined $\Xi^\sigma[s]$. The leaves of $D_s$ are called the *active strings* at stage $s + 1$.

*Stage* 0. Assign $\lambda$ the color blue$_0$, assign 00, 01, and 10 the color yellow$_0$, and assign 11 the color red$_0$.

*Stage* $s + 1$. This stage consists of four substages.

*Substage* 1. For each $e \leqslant s$ and each $\sigma \in D_s$, if $\sigma$ threatens $R_e$ then color $\sigma$ purple$_e$.

*Substage* 2. For each $e \leqslant s$ and each blue$_e$ string $\nu$, if the density of purple$_e$ strings extending $\nu$ is at least $2^{-(e+3)}$, then proceed as follows. (We say that $R_e$ *acts for* $\nu$.)

Let $\rho$ be the red$_e$ string extending $\nu$, and $\tau$ a yellow$_e$ string extending $\nu$ with the highest density of purple$_e$ strings extending it. Let $\theta_0, \ldots, \theta_n$ be the purple$_e$ strings extending $\tau$, and let $\xi_0, \ldots, \xi_n$ be corresponding witnesses for the $\theta_i$'s threat to $R_e$. For each $i \leqslant n$, let $\theta_i'$ be the string of length $\lvert\theta_i\rvert$ extending $\rho$ with $\theta_i(m) = \theta_i'(m)$ for all $m \geqslant \lvert\rho\rvert$.

Declare that every $\sigma \succcurlyeq \nu$ loses its color unless it is colored purple$_j$ for some $j < e$. There are now two cases.

*Case 1.* $\rho$ has an extension with color purple$_j$ for some $j < e$. In this case, do nothing.

*Case 2.* Otherwise. Then for each $i \leqslant n$, let $\Xi^{\theta_i'} = \xi_i$. Give each $\theta_i'$ color green$_e$.

*Substage* 3. For each active string $\sigma$ that is not red$_e$ for any $e$ and has no substring that is purple$_e$ for any $e$, proceed as follows. Let $e$ be least such that $\sigma$ has no substring colored green$_e$ or yellow$_e$. Let $\xi$ be the lexicographically least string such that $\sigma(n) = 1$ iff $\xi(\langle n, m \rangle) = 1$ for some $m$. (Note that $\xi$ is acceptable for $\sigma$.) Let $\Xi^{\sigma 0} = \Xi^{\sigma 1} = \xi$, and give $\sigma 0$ and $\sigma 1$ color blue$_e$.

*Substage* 4. For each $e$ and each active blue$_e$ string $\nu$, let $\Xi^{\nu\sigma} = \Xi^\nu$ for all $\sigma \in 2^{e+2}$. Give $\nu 1^{e+2}$ color red$_e$. Give the other length $e + 2$ extensions of $\nu$ color yellow$_e$.

**End of Construction.**

**Verification.** We will need the following color classes.

$$B_e = \lbrace A : \exists \sigma \prec A \; (\sigma \text{ has final color yellow}_e \text{ or green}_e) \rbrace.$$

$$S_e = \lbrace A : \exists \sigma \prec A \; (\sigma \text{ has final color red}_e) \rbrace.$$

$$P_e = \lbrace A : \exists \sigma \prec A \; (\sigma \text{ has final color purple}_e) \rbrace.$$

Let $S = \bigcup_e S_e$ and $P = \bigcup_e P_e$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.10)</span></p>

Let $A \in \bigcap_e B_e$. Then $\Xi^A$ is total, $A$ is c.e. in $\Xi^A$, and $A \not\leqslant_\text{T} \Xi^A$.

*Proof.* Substages 3 and 4 of the construction ensure that $\Xi^A$ is total and that $n \in A$ iff $\langle n, m \rangle \in \Xi^A$ for some $m$, whence $A$ is c.e. in $\Xi^A$. Thus we are left with showing that $A \not\leqslant_\text{T} \Xi^A$. Assume for a contradiction that there is a least $e$ such that $A = \Phi_e^{\Xi^A}$. Let $\sigma \prec A$ have final color yellow$_e$ or green$_e$.

*Case 1.* $\sigma$ has final color green$_e$. Then, by construction, at some stage we define $\Xi^\sigma = \xi$ for some $\xi$ with $\Phi_e^\xi(k) = 0 \neq 1 = \sigma(k)$ for some $k$, which is a contradiction.

*Case 2.* $\sigma$ has final color yellow$_e$. Let $\rho$ be the associated red$_e$ string. There is a $k$ such that $\rho(k) = 1 \neq 0 = \sigma(k)$. By our assumption, $\sigma(k) = \Phi_e^{\Xi^A}(k)$. Let $\xi$ be an initial segment of $\Xi^A$ such that $\Phi_e^\xi(k) = 0$. Then $\xi$ is acceptable for $\sigma$, and thus $\sigma$ threatens $R_e$ via $\xi$ at all sufficiently large stages. Thus $\sigma$ is colored purple$_e$ unless some initial segment of it is colored purple$_j$ for some $j \leqslant e$ at some point in the construction. The string $\tau$ can never later lose its yellow$_e$ color, for if it did, then $\sigma$ would also lose its yellow$_e$ color. But then the construction above $\tau$ is eventually permanently halted, contradicting the assumption that $A \in \bigcap_e B_e$.

</div>

We will now show through a sequence of lemmas that $\mu(\bigcap_e B_e) \geqslant \frac{1}{4}$. Let $B_{-1} = 2^\omega$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.11)</span></p>

$\mu(B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S \cup P)) = 0$.

*Proof.* We do the case $e > 0$. The $e = 0$ case is similar, using $\sigma = \lambda$.

Assume for a contradiction that $\mu(B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S \cup P)) > 0$. The class $B_{e-1}$ is a disjoint union of clopen sets $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$ where $\sigma$ has final color yellow$_{e-1}$ or green$_{e-1}$. For some such $\sigma$, we have $\mu(\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket \setminus (B_e \cup S \cup P)) > 0$. Let $s_0$ be the stage at which $\sigma$ receives its final color.

By the Lebesgue Density Theorem 1.2.3, there is a $\widehat{\sigma} \succcurlyeq \sigma$ such that $\mu(\llbracket\widehat{\sigma}\rrbracket \setminus (B_e \cup S \cup P)) > 2^{-\lvert\widehat{\sigma}\rvert}(1 - 2^{-(2e+5)})$. In particular, $\llbracket\widehat{\sigma}\rrbracket$ is not contained in $B_e \cup S \cup P$, so no substring of $\widehat{\sigma}$ has final color red$_i$ or purple$_i$ for any $i$, and no substring of $\widehat{\sigma}$ has final color yellow$_e$, green$_e$, red$_e$, or purple$_e$, which implies that no substring of $\widehat{\sigma}$ has final color blue$_e$. We may assume that $\widehat{\sigma}$ is sufficiently long so that some substring $\tau$ of $\widehat{\sigma}$ is active at stage $s_0$.

First suppose that $\tau$ has a purple$_i$ substring $\gamma$ for some $i$ at stage $s_0$. We know that $\gamma$ will eventually lose its color. Suppose this loss of color comes from some $R_j$ with $j < e$ acting for a blue$_j$ node $\nu$. Then it is easy to check from the construction that $\nu \prec \sigma$, so $R_j$'s action also causes $\sigma$ to lose its color, which is a contradiction. Thus $\gamma$'s loss of color must come from some $R_j$ with $j \geqslant e$ acting for a blue$_j$ node $\nu \preccurlyeq \tau$. Now it is easy to check from the construction that some predecessor of $\tau$ must have color blue$_e$ at some stage $s \geqslant s_0$.

Now suppose that $\tau$ has no purple$_i$ substrings for any $i$ at stage $s_0$. It cannot be the case that $\tau$ is colored red$_j$ for some $j < e$, then it has a blue$_e$ substring. Otherwise, it becomes blue$_e$ at stage $s_0$.

Thus, in either case, there is some stage $s \geqslant s_0$ at which $\widehat{\sigma}$ has a blue$_e$ substring. It is then easy to check from the construction that there must be a stage $s' > s$ at which some initial segment of $A$ has color yellow$_e$. This color cannot be permanent, since $A \notin B_e$, so there must be a stage $s'' > s'$ at which again some initial segment of $A$ receives color yellow$_e$, and so on. Thus $A \in Y_e$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.12)</span></p>

$B_{e+1} \subseteq B_e$.

*Proof.* Let $A \in B_{e+1}$, and let $\sigma \prec A$ have final color green$_{e+1}$ or yellow$_{e+1}$, with $\nu \prec \sigma$ the associated blue$_{e+1}$ string. When $\nu$ received its blue$_{e+1}$ color, there must have been some string $\tau \prec \nu$ with color green$_e$ or yellow$_e$, as otherwise $\nu$ would have been colored blue$_e$. Any action removing this color from $\tau$ would necessarily remove the color from $\sigma$ as well, and hence $\tau$'s color is final.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.13)</span></p>

$\mu(S \cup P) + \mu(\bigcap_e B_e) = 1$.

*Proof.* We again let $B_{-1} = 2^\omega$. Since

$$(B_{e-1} \setminus B_e) \cap \overline{S \cup P} = B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S \cup P),$$

it follows from Lemma 8.21.11 that $\mu((B_{e-1} \setminus B_e) \cap \overline{S \cup P}) = 0$, and hence

$$\mu\left(\bigcup_e (B_{e-1} \setminus B_e) \cap \overline{S \cup P}\right) = 0.$$

By Lemma 8.21.12, $\bigcup_e (B_{e-1} \setminus B_e) \cup \bigcap_e B_e = 2^\omega$, and it is easy to see from the construction that $(S \cup P) \cap (\bigcap_e B_e) = \emptyset$, so $S \cup P \subseteq \bigcup_e (B_{e-1} \setminus B_e)$, and hence $\mu(S \cup P) = \mu(\bigcup_e (B_{e-1} \setminus B_e))$.

Thus

$$\mu(S \cup P) + \mu\left(\bigcap_e B_e\right) = \mu\left(\bigcup_e (B_{e-1} \setminus B_e)\right) + \mu\left(\bigcap_e B_e\right) = 1.$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.14)</span></p>

$\mu(S) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$ and $\mu(P) \leqslant \frac{1}{4}$.

*Proof.* By construction, the set of strings $\nu$ with final color blue$_e$ is prefix-free. For any such $\nu$, there is a single red$_e$ node $\tau$ above $\nu$, and $\mu(\llbracket\tau\rrbracket) = 2^{-(e+2)}\mu(\llbracket\nu\rrbracket)$. Thus $\mu(S_e) \leqslant 2^{-(e+2)}$, and hence $\mu(S) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$. Similarly, the density of purple$_e$ strings in $\llbracket\nu\rrbracket$ for any blue$_e$ string $\nu$ is bounded by $2^{-(e+3)}$, and hence $\mu(P_e) \leqslant 2^{-(e+3)}$, whence $\mu(P) \leqslant \frac{1}{4}$.

</div>

Putting together the previous lemmas, we have $\mu(\bigcap_e B_e) \geqslant \frac{1}{4}$, which together with Lemma 8.21.10 and Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law, shows that almost every set is CEA.

The class $\lbrace A : \Xi^A \text{ total} \rbrace$ is a $\Pi_2^0$ class of positive measure, and hence, by Theorem 6.10.2, for every 2-random set $X$, there are $A$ and $\sigma$ such that $X = \sigma A$ and $\Xi^A$ is total. If $A \in S \cup P$ then $\Xi^A$ is not total, and if $A \in \bigcap_e B_e$ then $\Xi^A <_\text{T} A$, so by Lemma 8.21.13, $\mu(\lbrace A : \Xi^A \text{ total} \wedge A \leqslant_\text{T} \Xi^A \rbrace) = 0$. This class is the union of the $\Pi_2^0$ classes $\lbrace A : \Xi^A \text{ total} \wedge A = \Phi_e^{\Xi^A} \rbrace$, so it cannot contain any 2-random sets. Thus for every 2-random set $X$, there are $A$ and $\sigma$ with $X = \sigma A$ such that $\Xi^A$ is total (which implies that $A$ is c.e. in $\Xi^A$) and $\Xi^A <_\text{T} A$, so that $A$ is CEA, and hence so is $X$.

The following result shows that Theorem 8.21.3 follows from Theorem 8.21.8. We give a proof due to Shore.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.15 — Folklore)</span></p>

If a set is CEA then it computes a 1-generic set.

*Proof.* Let $A$ be c.e. in $B <_\text{T} A$. We construct a 1-generic set $G \leqslant_\text{T} A$ via a finite extension argument. Let $A_0, A_1, \ldots$ be a $B$-c.e. approximation to $A$. Let $c(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $A_s \upharpoonright n = A \upharpoonright n$. Let $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ be an effective enumeration of the c.e. sets of strings. Let $G_0 = \lambda$. At stage $s$, if there is a least $e \leqslant s$ such that we have never acted for $e$ and there is a $\sigma \in V_e[c(s+1)]$ with $\sigma \succcurlyeq G_s$, then let $G_{s+1} = \sigma$. (In this case, we say we have acted for $e$.) Otherwise, let $G_{s+1} = G_s$.

It is easy to check that $\lim_s \lvert G_s \rvert = \infty$. Let $G = \lim_s G_s$. Assume for a contradiction that $G$ is not 1-generic. Show that $c \leqslant_\text{T} B$, which is impossible, as it implies that $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$. Let $e$ be such that $G$ neither meets nor avoids $V_e$. Let $s_0$ be a stage after which we never act for a number less than $e$. Let $s \geqslant s_0$ and assume we know $G_s$. Then we can find a stage $t$ such that some extension of $G_s$ occurs in $V_e[t]$. (Such an extension must exist, since $G$ does not avoid $V_e$.) We must have $t > c(s+1)$, since otherwise we would have acted for $e$ at stage $s$. Thus we can compute $c(s+1)$ using $B$ (as the least $u$ such that $A_u \upharpoonright n = A_t \upharpoonright n$), from which we can compute $G_{s+1}$. Proceeding by induction, we can compute $c$ from $B$.

</div>

As mentioned following Theorem 8.21.4, there are weakly 2-random sets that do not compute any 1-generic sets. Thus there are weakly 2-random sets that are not CEA.

Theorems 8.21.8 and 8.21.15 together give us Kautz' Theorem 8.21.4 that every 2-random set computes a 1-generic set. The following is a direct proof, applying the analysis in the proof of Theorem 8.21.8 to the similar (but simpler) construction in the proof of Theorem 8.21.3.

*Proof of Theorem 8.21.4.* Let $\Xi$ be the functional built in the proof of Theorem 8.21.3. Then $\lbrace A : \Xi^A \text{ total} \rbrace$ is a $\Pi_2^0$ class of positive measure, and hence, by Theorem 6.10.2, contains elements of all 2-random degrees. Thus it is enough to show that if $\Xi^A$ is total and $A$ is 2-random, then $\Xi^A$ is 1-generic.

For the construction in the proof of Theorem 8.21.3, let

$$B_e = \lbrace A : \exists \sigma \prec A \; (\sigma \text{ has final color yellow}_e \text{ or green}_e) \rbrace,$$

$$S_e = \lbrace A : \exists \sigma \prec A \; (\sigma \text{ has final color red}_e) \rbrace,$$

and $S = \bigcup_e S_e$. (Recall that in this construction there are no purple strings.) Arguing as in the proof of Theorem 8.21.8, we can show that $\mu(S) + \mu(\bigcap_e B_e) = 1$.

Let $Y_e$ be the class of all $A$ such that, infinitely often, some initial segment of $A$ receives color yellow$_e$. Then $Y_e$ is a $\Pi_2^0$ class, and $Y_e \cap \bigcap_e B_e = \emptyset$, so $\mu(Y_e) = 0$. Thus if $A$ is 2-random, $A \notin Y_e$ for all $e$. We will show that then $A \in \bigcap_e B_e$, which implies by construction that if $\Xi^A$ is total then it is 1-generic, thus proving the theorem.

So we are left with showing that if $A \notin \bigcap_e B_e$ then $A \in Y_e$ for some $e$. Let $e$ be least such that $A \notin B_e$. We do the argument for the case $e > 0$; the case $e = 0$ is similar.

Let $s$ be such that for each $i < e$, some initial segment of $A$ has received final color yellow$_i$ or green$_i$ by stage $s$. Let $\sigma \prec A$ have final color yellow$_{e-1}$ or green$_{e-1}$. Suppose that $\tau \prec A$ has some color$_e$ at a stage $t > s$. Recall that if $\tau$ later loses its color through the action of some $R_i$, then $\tau$ must have a blue$_i$ substring $\nu$, and in this case every successor of $\nu$ loses its color. Thus $\tau$ can lose its color only through the action of $R_e$, since for $i > e$ no substring of $\tau$ can be blue$_i$, while for $i < e$, any blue$_i$ substring of $\tau$ is also a substring of $\sigma$, whose color is permanent.

Now assume for a contradiction that the color $\tau$ has at stage $t$ is red$_e$. Since $\Xi^A$ is total, this color cannot be permanent, so at some point it is removed through the action of $R_e$, which means that $\tau$ then receives color green$_e$, which must be permanent, since $R_e$ never acts to remove this color, and by the argument in the previous paragraph, neither does any other $R_i$. But we are assuming that $A \notin B_e$, so we have a contradiction.

Thus no initial segment of $A$ receives color red$_e$ after stage $s$. It is then easy to check from the construction that there must be a stage $s' > s$ at which some initial segment of $A$ has color yellow$_e$. This color cannot be permanent, since $A \notin B_e$, so there must be a stage $s'' > s'$ at which again some initial segment of $A$ receives color yellow$_e$, and so on. Thus $A \in Y_e$.

#### 8.21.4 Where 1-Generic Degrees are Downward Dense

A class $C$ of degrees is *downward dense* below a degree $\mathbf{a}$ if for all nonzero $\mathbf{b} \leqslant \mathbf{a}$ there is a $\mathbf{c} \leqslant \mathbf{b}$ in $C$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.16 — Kurtz)</span></p>

For almost all degrees $\mathbf{a}$, the class of 1-generic degrees is downward dense below $\mathbf{a}$.

*Proof.* The method of this proof is based on that of Theorem 8.21.3, but is considerably more complicated. Assume for a contradiction that the class of degrees below which the class of 1-generic degrees is not downward dense has positive measure. Then there is an $e$ such that the class $C$ of $A$ such that $\Phi_e^A$ is total, noncomputable, and does not compute a 1-generic set is positive. By the Lebesgue Density Theorem 1.2.3, there is a $\sigma$ such that $\mu(C \cap \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket) > \frac{7}{8} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$. Let $\Psi^A = \Phi_e^{\sigma A}$. Then the class of all $A$ such that $\Psi^A$ is total, noncomputable, and does not compute a 1-generic set is greater than $\frac{7}{8}$. Our contradiction will be obtained by constructing a functional $\Phi$ such that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Phi^{\Psi^A} \text{ total and 1-generic} \rbrace) > \frac{1}{8}$. To do so, we need to ensure that for measure $\frac{1}{8}$ many sets $A$, we have $\Phi^{\Psi^A}$ is total and the following requirements are met, where $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ is an effective enumeration of the c.e. sets of strings.

$$R_e : \Phi^{\Psi^A} \text{ meets or avoids } V_e.$$

</div>

Let

$$p^*(\sigma) = \mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ is total, noncomputable, and extends } \sigma \rbrace).$$

We will use the method of Theorem 8.21.3, but with $p^*(\sigma)$ replacing $2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ as the measure of $\llbracket\sigma\rrbracket$. Again we will have blue$_e$, red$_e$, yellow$_e$, and green$_e$ strings, but we will need much more subtlety in the way that these colors are assigned. There will also be an additional color, gray$_e$, whose role will be discussed later.

Unfortunately, we cannot actually compute $p^*$. If we could, then we could proceed very much as in the proof of Theorem 8.21.3. Working above a blue$_e$ string $\nu$ for the sake of $R_e$, we would search for a maximal finite set $E_\nu$ of pairwise incompatible extensions of $\nu$ such that for some $\rho \in E_\nu$,

$$p^*(\nu) 2^{-(e+3)} \leqslant p^*(\rho) \leqslant p^*(\nu) 2^{-(e+2)}.$$

We would then give $\rho$ color red$_e$ and the other members of $E_\nu$ color yellow$_e$, and proceed much as before. Such a construction would give us a $\Phi$ such that

$$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and } \Phi^{\Psi^A} \text{ not 1-generic} \rbrace) \leqslant \frac{1}{2},$$

which would yield our contradiction, since then

$$\mu(\lbrace A : \Phi^{\Psi^A} \text{ total and 1-generic} \rbrace) \geqslant \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{2} > \frac{1}{8}.$$

Of course, we cannot compute the actual value of $p^*$, so we will be forced to use an approximation $p$ to $p^*$ defined as follows. Let $F(\sigma)$ be the longest initial segment of $\Psi^\sigma \| \lvert\sigma\rvert \|$. Let

$$p_s(\sigma) = \mu(\llbracket\lbrace \tau \in 2^s : \sigma \preccurlyeq F(\tau) \rbrace\rrbracket)$$

and let $p(\sigma) = \lim_s p_s(\sigma)$. Note that $p(\sigma) = \mu(\lbrace A : \sigma \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \rbrace)$ (where by $\sigma \preccurlyeq \Psi^A$ we mean that $\Psi^A(n) \models \sigma(n)$ for all $n < \lvert\sigma\rvert$). At a stage $s$ of the construction, we will use $p_s$ in place of $p^*$. The new ideas in this construction will allow us to overcome the difficulties that this use of approximations causes.

One obvious issue is that $p_s$ converges to $p$ and not to $p^*$. Let

$$Y = \lbrace \sigma : \mu(\lbrace A : \sigma \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \wedge \Psi^A \text{ is nontotal or computable} \rbrace) > \tfrac{p(\sigma)}{2} \rbrace.$$

We will later show that the class of sets $A$ that meet $Y$ has measure less than $\frac{1}{4}$. The key additional color gray$_e$ comes in. The gray$_e$ strings will act as "traffic lights". The red$_e$ and yellow$_e$ strings will occur as extensions of gray$_e$ strings, while the blue$_e$ strings control the placement of the gray$_e$ strings. In this construction, it will be possible for one blue$_e$ string to extend another. With this fact in mind, we say that a blue$_e$ string $\nu'$ *belongs* to a blue$_e$ string $\nu$ if $\nu$ is the longest blue$_e$ proper substring of $\nu'$. A green$_e$ or gray$_e$ string $\gamma$ belongs to $\nu$ if $\nu$ is the longest (not necessarily proper) blue$_e$ substring of $\gamma$. We will ensure that if $\nu$ is a blue$_e$ string, then either no string properly extending $\nu$ has a color, or else the green$_e$, blue$_e$, and gray$_e$ strings that belong to $\nu$ form a finite maximal set of incomparable extensions of $\nu$. When we compute the density of green$_e$ and red$_e$ strings that extend a blue$_e$ string $\nu$, we will use only those that belong to $\nu$.

The construction involves a new mechanism called a *wipe-out*. If $\nu$ is a blue$_e$ string at a stage $s$ such that $p_s(\nu) > 0$, we begin by giving $\nu$ itself the color gray$_e$ (without removing the color blue$_e$ from $\nu$). Suppose that at a stage $t > s$ we appoint a red$_e$ string belonging to $\nu$, and that at a later stage $u$, the density of red$_e$ and green$_e$ strings belonging to $\nu$ becomes unacceptably small. That is, for the red$_e$ and green$_e$ strings $\delta_0, \ldots, \delta_n$ belonging to $\nu$,

$$\frac{\sum_{j \leqslant n} p_u(\delta_j)}{p_u(\nu)} \leqslant 2^{-(e+6)}.$$

We then remove all colors from all strings properly extending $\nu$, and remove the color gray$_e$ from $\nu$. We refer to this process as a *wipe-out* above $\nu$. Let $\alpha_0, \ldots, \alpha_m$ be the active strings extending $\nu$ at stage $u$. We do nothing above $\nu$ unless there is a stage $v > u$ such that $\sum_{j \leqslant m} p_v(\alpha_j) > \frac{p_v(\nu)}{2}$. The key observation is that, unless $\nu \in Y$, such a $v$ must exist. In this case, we give each $\alpha_j$ the color gray$_e$, and for each $j \leqslant m$ we begin to seek red$_e$ extensions of $\alpha_j$.

If at some stage $w > v$, the density of red$_e$ and green$_e$ strings belonging to $\nu$ is again unacceptably small, then we again perform a wipe-out above $\nu$, and try to place gray$_e$ strings above $\nu$ as before. Note that wipe-outs can occur at most finitely often, since each wipe-out requires the approximation to $p(\nu)$ to at least double. After the final wipe-out, we can meet $R_e$ above $\nu$ without any interference.

**Construction.**

*Stage* 0. The only active string is $\lambda$. Give $\lambda$ the color blue$_0$ and define $\Phi^\lambda = \lambda$.

*Stage* $s + 1$. At this stage, we work for each $e \leqslant s + 1$. The stage is divided into five substages.

*Substage* 1. (Red action) For each red$_e$ string $\rho$ for which there is a $\nu \in V_e[s]$ with $\Phi^\rho \preccurlyeq \nu$, proceed as follows. Let $\Phi^{\rho i} = \nu$ for $i = 0, 1$. Remove the color red$_e$ from $\rho$ and give it the color green$_e$. For the unique gray$_e$ string $\gamma \preccurlyeq \rho$, remove the color gray$_e$ from $\gamma$, and remove the color of all yellow$_e$ strings extending $\gamma$.

*Substage* 2. (Gray placement, or wipe-out recovery) For each blue$_e$ and nongray$_e$ string $\nu$ such that no string properly extending $\nu$ has a color, proceed as follows. Let $\alpha_0, \ldots, \alpha_n$ be the active strings that extend $\nu$. If $\sum_{j \leqslant n} p_s(\alpha_j) > \frac{p_s(\nu)}{2}$ then give each $\alpha_i$ the colors gray$_e$ and red$_e$.

*Substage* 3. (Red push-out) For each red$_e$ string $\rho$, proceed as follows. Let $\gamma$ be the unique gray$_e$ substring of $\rho$. If $\frac{p_{s+1}(\rho i)}{p_{s+1}(\gamma)} < 2^{-(e+4)}$ for $i = 0, 1$ then do nothing. Otherwise, let $i$ be least such that $\frac{p_{s+1}(\rho i)}{p_{s+1}(\gamma)} \geqslant 2^{-(e+4)}$. Remove the red$_e$ color from $\rho$. Give the red$_e$ color to $\rho i$ and the color yellow$_e$ to $\rho(1-i)$. Let $\Phi^{\rho j} = \Phi^\rho$ for $j = 0, 1$.

*Substage* 4. (Wipe-outs) For each blue$_e$ string $\nu$, proceed as follows. Let $\delta_0, \ldots, \delta_n$ be the red$_e$ and green$_e$ strings belonging to $\nu$. If $\frac{\sum_{j \leqslant n} p_{s+1}(\delta_j)}{p_{s+1}(\nu)} > 2^{-(e+6)}$ then do nothing. Otherwise, remove all colors from all proper extensions of $\nu$; if $\nu$ has color gray$_e$ then remove this color.

*Substage* 5. (Blue placement) For each nonactive string $\sigma$, proceed as follows. If there is an $e$ such that $\sigma$ has a blue$_e$ and nongray$_e$ substring $\nu$ none of whose proper extensions has a color (so that we are still attempting wipe-out recovery above $\nu$), then do nothing. Otherwise, let $e$ be least such that $\sigma$ extends neither a green$_e$ nor a yellow$_e$ string, and give $\sigma$ the color blue$_e$.

**End of Construction.**

**Verification.** We will need the following classes.

$$B_e = \lbrace X : X \text{ has an initial segment with final color green}_e \text{ or yellow}_e \rbrace.$$

$$S_e = \lbrace X : X \text{ has an initial segment with final color red}_e \rbrace.$$

Let $Y$ be as above.

Let $X \in \bigcap_e B_e$. We claim that $\Phi^X$ is total and 1-generic. To establish this claim, fix $e$. Since $X \in B_e$ there is a string $\sigma \prec X$ with final color green$_e$ or yellow$_e$. In the green$_e$ case, there is a $\tau \preccurlyeq \Phi^X$ in $V_e$ by the construction. In the yellow$_e$ case, there cannot be an extension of $\Phi^\sigma$ in $V_e$, since otherwise $R_e$ would eventually act to remove the color yellow$_e$ from $\sigma$. In either case, $\Phi^X$ meets or avoids $V_e$. Since for each $n$ there is an $e$ such that $V_e = 2^{\geqslant n}$, we see that $\Phi^X$ is also total.

Thus it is enough to show that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } \bigcap_e B_e \rbrace) > \frac{1}{8}$. Every set is in one of the following classes, where we let $B_{-1} = 2^\omega$.

(i) $\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } \bigcap_e B_e \rbrace$,

(ii) $\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace$,

(iii) $\bigcup_e \lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } S_e \setminus \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace$,

(iv) $\bigcup_e \lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace$,

(v) $\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ not total} \rbrace$.

The last class has measure less than $\frac{1}{8}$, so it is enough to show that the sum of the measures of the classes in items 2–4 is at most $\frac{3}{4}$. We will do so by showing that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \in \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) < \frac{1}{4}$, that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } S_e \setminus \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) \leqslant 2^{-(e+2)}$, and that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) = 0$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.17)</span></p>
