$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \in \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) < \frac{1}{4}$.

*Proof.* Let $T$ be the set of elements of $Y$ with no proper substring in $T$. Then

$$\frac{1}{4} \geqslant 2\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ is nontotal or computable} \rbrace) \geqslant 2\sum_{\sigma \in T} \mu(\lbrace A : \sigma \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \text{ and } \Psi^A \text{ is computable or nontotal} \rbrace) > \sum_{\sigma \in T} p(\sigma) = \sum_{\sigma \in T} \mu(\lbrace A : \sigma \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \rbrace) = \mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \in \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.18)</span></p>

$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ is total and in } S_e \setminus \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) \leqslant 2^{-(e+2)}$.

*Proof.* Let $R$ be the set of strings with final color red$_e$ and $G$ be the set of strings with final color gray$_e$. Note that the elements of $G$ are pairwise incompatible. Each element of $R$ has a substring in $G$, and each element of $G$ has at most one extension in $R$.

Let $\rho \in R$ and $\gamma \in G$ be such that $\gamma \preccurlyeq \rho$. If $p(\rho) > 2^{-(e+2)} p(\gamma)$ then $p(\rho 0) + p(\rho 1) < \frac{p(\rho)}{2}$, since otherwise the color red$_e$ would be passed from $\rho$ to one of the $\rho i$ at some stage. Thus in this case $\rho \in Y$. So

$$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ is total and in } S_e \setminus \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) \leqslant \sum \lbrace p(\rho) : \rho \in R \setminus Y \rbrace \leqslant \sum \lbrace 2^{-(e+2)} p(\gamma) : \gamma \in G \rbrace \leqslant 2^{-(e+2)}.$$

</div>

We finish by showing that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) = 0$. The intuitive reason that this fact is true is that if $\Psi^A$ is total and in $B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket)$, then we make infinitely many attempts to satisfy $R_e$ for $A$, and as we will see, the probability that we do so is 0. We begin with some auxiliary lemmas.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.19)</span></p>

$B_e \subseteq B_{e-1}$.

*Proof.* The proof is the same as that of Lemma 8.21.12.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.20)</span></p>

$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ is computable and not in } \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace) = 0$.

*Proof.* Assume not. Then there is a computable set $C \notin \llbracket Y \rrbracket$ such that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A = C \rbrace) > 0$. Since $\lbrace A : \Psi^A = C \rbrace = \bigcap_n \lbrace A : C \upharpoonright n \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \rbrace$, for all sufficiently large $n$ we have

$$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A = C \rbrace) > \frac{\mu(\lbrace A : C \upharpoonright n \preccurlyeq \Psi^A \rbrace)}{2} = \frac{p(C \upharpoonright n)}{2},$$

whence $C \upharpoonright n \in Y$, contradicting the assumption that $C \notin \llbracket Y \rrbracket$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.21)</span></p>

Suppose $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) > 0$. Then there is a $\sigma$ such that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } \llbracket\sigma\rrbracket_2 \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) > \delta 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ for any $\delta < 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.22)</span></p>

$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_{e-1} \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) = 0$.

*Proof.* Assume for a contradiction that the lemma fails. Let $\sigma$ be as in the previous lemma for $\delta = 1 - 2^{-(e+7)}$. If $e > 0$ then, since $B_{e-1}$ is generated by the strings with final color green$_e$ or yellow$_e$, we may assume that $\sigma$ extends such a string.

If a string has final color gray$_e$ then every set extending it is either in $B_e \cup S_e$ or is the limit of a sequence of red$_e$ strings, in which case it is computable. Thus, by Lemma 8.21.20, $\sigma$ cannot extend such a string. It follows that we appoint a maximal set $I$ of pairwise incompatible extensions of $\sigma$ and give each of them the color blue$_e$. There is a $\nu \in I$ such that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } \llbracket\nu\rrbracket \setminus (B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket) \rbrace) > (1 - 2^{-(e+7)}) 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert}$.

After $\nu$ has wiped-out for the last time at a stage $s_0$, we appoint a maximal set of pairwise incompatible gray$_e$ strings $\gamma_0, \ldots, \gamma_m \succcurlyeq \nu$. Let $\delta_i^s$ be the red$_e$ or green$_e$ string extending $\gamma_i$ at stage $s \geqslant s_0$, or $\delta_i^s = \gamma_i$ if there is no such string. Let $C_s = \llbracket\lbrace \delta_i^s : i \leqslant m \rbrace\rrbracket$ and let $C = \bigcap_s C_s$. Let $X \in C$. If there is an $i$ such that the $\delta_i^s$ come to a finite limit $\delta_i \prec X$ then $\delta_i$ is either green$_e$ or red$_e$, and hence $X \in B_e \cup S_e$. Otherwise, $X$ is the limit of $\delta_i^s$ for some $i$, and hence $X$ is computable.

$\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } C \rbrace) \leqslant \mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } B_e \cup S_e \cup \llbracket Y \rrbracket \rbrace)$.

Therefore, to obtain a contradiction, it is enough to show that $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } C \rbrace) \geqslant 2^{-(e+7)} 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert}$. Assume not. Then, since $C_{s_0} \supseteq C_{s_0+1} \supseteq \cdots$, for all sufficiently large $s$, we have $\mu(\lbrace A : \Psi^A \text{ total and in } C_s \rbrace) < 2^{-(e+7)} 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert}$. It is then easy to see that for all sufficiently large $s$ we have $\sum_{i \leqslant m} p_s(\delta_i^s) < 2^{-(e+7)} 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert} < 2^{-(e+6)} p_s(\nu)$, the last inequality holding because $p(\nu) > 2^{-\lvert\nu\rvert + 1}$. Therefore, $\nu$ must wipe-out again after stage $s_0$, contradicting the choice of $s_0$.

</div>

As argued above, this lemma completes the proof of the theorem.

The original proof of the following consequence of the above result was, to the authors' knowledge, the first use of the method of risking measure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.23 — Paris)</span></p>

The upward closure of the class of minimal degrees has measure 0.

</div>

Jockusch showed that the initial segment of degrees below a 1-generic degree is never a lattice, so we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(8.21.24 — Kurtz)</span></p>

The upward closure of the class of degrees whose initial segments form lattices has measure 0.

</div>

## Chapter 9: Measures of Relative Randomness

Measures of relative complexity such as Turing reducibility, wtt-reducibility, and so on have proven to be central tools in computability theory. The resulting degree structures have been widely studied, and there has been considerable interplay between structural results and insights informing our understanding of the concept of computability. In this chapter, we examine reducibilities that can act as measures of relative complexity, and hence perform a similar role in the theory of algorithmic randomness.

We use the term "reducibility" loosely, to mean any transitive and reflexive preorder on $2^\omega$. For example, given the association between randomness and high prefix-free complexity of initial segments, it is natural to consider *$K$-reducibility*, where $A \leqslant_K B$ if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$, and think of this preorder as a reducibility measuring relative randomness, although there is no corresponding concept of reduction in this case.

### 9.1 Solovay Reducibility

As we will see below, probably the easiest way to define Solovay reducibility on left-c.e. reals is to say that $\alpha$ is Solovay reducible to $\beta$ if there are a constant $c$ and a left-c.e. real $\gamma$ such that $c\beta = \alpha + \gamma$. We begin, however, with Solovay's original definition, which is not restricted to left-c.e. reals (although Solovay reducibility is badly behaved outside the left-c.e. reals, and hence its applications to date have been restricted to the left-c.e. reals).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.1 — Solovay Reducibility)</span></p>

A real $\alpha$ is **Solovay reducible**, or **S-reducible**, to a real $\beta$ (written $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$) if there are a constant $c$ and a partial computable function $f : \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that if $q \in \mathbb{Q}$ and $q < \beta$, then $f(q) \!\downarrow < \alpha$ and $\alpha - f(q) < c(\beta - q)$.

</div>

One way to look at this definition is that a sequence of rationals converging to $\beta$ from below can be used to generate one that converges to $\alpha$ from below at the same rate or faster. This idea is made precise for left-c.e. reals in the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.2 — Calude, Coles, Hertling, and Khoussainov)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals, and let $q_0 < q_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ and $r_0 < r_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ be computable sequences of rationals. Then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ iff there are a constant $c$ and a computable function $g$ such that $\alpha - q_{g(n)} < c(\beta - r_n)$ for all $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$, and let $c$ and $f$ be as in Definition 9.1.1. Given $n$, we have $f(r_n) \!\downarrow < \alpha$, so there is an $m$ such that $f(r_n) < q_m$. Let $g(n) = m$. Then $\alpha - q_{g(n)} < \alpha - f(r_n) < c(\beta - r_n)$.

Conversely, suppose there are $c$ and $g$ as above. Given $s \in \mathbb{Q}$, search for an $n$ such that $s < r_n$, and if such an $n$ is found, then let $f(s) = q_{g(n)}$. If $s < \beta$ then $n$ exists, and $\alpha - f(s) < c(\beta - r_n) < c(\beta - s)$. $\square$

The connection between Solovay reducibility and relative randomness comes from the fact that Solovay reducibility implies $K$-reducibility (as well as *$C$-reducibility*, where $A \leqslant_C B$ if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$). To prove this fact, we begin with a lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.3 — Solovay)</span></p>

For each $k$ there is a constant $c_k$ such that for all $n$ and all $\sigma, \tau \in 2^n$, if $\lvert 0.\sigma - 0.\tau \rvert < 2^{k-n}$, then $C(\sigma) = C(\tau) \pm c_k$ and $K(\sigma) = K(\tau) \pm c_k$.

</div>

*Proof.* Fix $k$. Given $\sigma$ of some length $n$, we can compute the set $S$ of $\tau \in 2^n$ such that $\lvert 0.\sigma - 0.\tau \rvert < 2^{k-n}$. The size of $S$ depends only on $k$, and we can describe any $\tau \in S$ by giving $\sigma$ and $\tau$'s position in the lexicographic ordering of $S$. Thus $C(\tau) \leqslant C(\sigma) + O(1)$ and $K(\tau) \leqslant K(\sigma) + O(1)$, where the constants depend only on $k$. The lemma follows by symmetry. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.4 — Solovay)</span></p>

If $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ then $C(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(\beta \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$ and $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\beta \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $c$ and $f$ be as in Definition 9.1.1. Let $k$ be such that $c + 2 < 2^k$. Let $\beta_n = 0.(\beta \upharpoonright n)$ and $\alpha_n = 0.(\alpha \upharpoonright n)$. Since $\beta_n$ is rational and $\beta - \beta_n \leqslant 2^{-n}$, we have $\alpha - f(\beta_n) < c 2^{-n}$. Let $\tau_n \in 2^n$ be such that $\lvert 0.\tau_n - f(\beta_n) \rvert < 2^{-n}$. Then

$$\lvert \alpha_n - 0.\tau_n \rvert \leqslant \lvert \alpha - \alpha_n \rvert + \lvert \alpha - f(\beta_n) \rvert + \lvert 0.\tau_n - f(\beta_n) \rvert < (c+2) 2^{-n} < 2^{k-n}.$$

Thus, by Lemma 9.1.3, $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\tau_n) + O(1)$. But $\tau_n$ can be obtained computably from $\beta \upharpoonright n$, so $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\beta \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. The proof for plain complexity is the same. $\square$

One useful property of Solovay reducibility is that it also implies Turing reducibility (which we will see later is not the case for $K$-reducibility).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.5)</span></p>

For any reals $\alpha$ and $\beta$, if $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{T} \beta$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $c$ and $f$ be as in Definition 9.1.1. For each $n$, we can $\beta$-computably find a rational $q_n < \beta$ such that $\beta - q_n < 2^{-n}$. Then $g(q_0), g(q_1), \ldots$ is a $\beta$-computable sequence of rationals converging to $\alpha$ at a computably bounded rate. Thus $\alpha \leqslant_\text{T} \beta$. $\square$

On the other hand, S-reducibility does not imply wtt-reducibility. Recall that a real is strongly c.e. if it is a c.e. set when thought of as a subset of $\mathbb{N}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

There exist left-c.e. reals $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ such that $\alpha \nleqslant_\text{wtt} \beta$. Moreover, $\beta$ can be chosen to be strongly c.e.

</div>

We finish the section on Solovay reducibility by giving another characterization on left-c.e. reals, namely that $\alpha$ is Solovay reducible to $\beta$ iff there are a constant $c$ and a left-c.e. real $\gamma$ such that $c\beta = \alpha + \gamma$. We begin with a useful lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.7 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals and let $r_0 < r_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ be a computable sequence of rationals. Then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ iff there is a computable sequence of rationals $p_0 < p_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ such that for some constant $d$ we have $p_s - p_{s-1} < d(r_s - r_{s-1})$ for all $s$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.1.8 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals. The following are equivalent.

1. $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$.
2. For each computable sequence of nonnegative rationals $b_0, b_1, \ldots$ such that $\beta = \sum_n b_n$, there are a constant $d$ and a computable sequence of rationals $\varepsilon_0, \varepsilon_1, \ldots \in [0, d]$ such that $\alpha = \sum_n \varepsilon_n b_n$.
3. There are a constant $d$ and a left-c.e. real $\gamma$ such that $d\beta = \alpha + \gamma$.

</div>

### 9.2 The Kučera-Slaman Theorem

Solovay introduced Solovay reducibility to define a class of reals with many of the properties of $\Omega$, but with a machine-independent definition. First he noted that $\Omega$ is *Solovay complete*, in the sense that $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \Omega$ for all left-c.e. reals $\alpha$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.2.1 — Solovay)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ is a left-c.e. real then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \Omega$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real. Let $M$ be a prefix-free machine such that $\alpha = \mu(\llbracket \operatorname{dom} M \rrbracket)$, and let $\tau$ be such that $\mathcal{U}(\tau\sigma) = M(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$. If we can approximate $\Omega$ to within $2^{-(\lvert\tau\rvert + n)}$, then we can approximate $\alpha$ to within $2^{-n}$, so $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \Omega$. $\square$

Furthermore, for any (not necessarily left-c.e.) real $\alpha$, if $\Omega \leqslant_\text{S} \alpha$ then, by Theorem 9.1.4, $\alpha$ is 1-random. Solovay proved that the Solovay-complete left-c.e. reals (which he called *$\Omega$-like*) possess many of the properties of $\Omega$. He remarked: "It seems strange that we will be able to prove so much about the behavior of $[K(\Omega \upharpoonright n)]$ when, a priori, the definition of $\Omega$ is thoroughly model dependent. What our discussion has shown is that our results hold for a class of reals (that include the value of the universal measures ...) and that the function $[K(\Omega \upharpoonright n)]$ is model independent to within $O(1)$."

Several left-c.e. reals that arise naturally in the theory of algorithmic randomness are $\Omega$-like. For example, Kučera and Slaman showed that if $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a universal Martin-Löf test, then $\mu(U_n)$ is $\Omega$-like for all $n > 0$. Another example are the values $Q(\sigma)$ from Definition 3.9.3.

The following two results establish that using $\Omega$-like reals in place of $\Omega$ makes no difference in the same way that using any set of the same m-degree as the halting problem gives a version of the halting problem, by Myhill's Theorem. Thus, it turns out that Solovay's observations are not so strange after all.

The first result establishes that any $\Omega$-like real is a halting probability.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.2.2 — Calude, Hertling, Khoussainov, and Wang)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ is $\Omega$-like then there is a universal prefix-free machine $\widehat{\mathcal{U}}$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \operatorname{dom} \widehat{\mathcal{U}} \rrbracket) = \alpha$.

</div>

The complete characterization of the 1-random left-c.e. reals in terms of Solovay reducibility is given by the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.2.3 — Kučera-Slaman Theorem)</span></p>

If $\alpha$ is 1-random and left-c.e. then it is $\Omega$-like.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\beta$ be a left-c.e. real. We need to show that $\beta \leqslant_\text{S} \alpha$. Define a Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ in stages. At stage $s$ act as follows. If $\alpha_s \in U_n[s]$ then do nothing. Otherwise let $t$ be the last stage at which we put anything into $U_n$ (or $t = 0$ if there is no such stage), and put the interval $(\alpha_s, \alpha_s + 2^{-n}(\beta_s - \beta_t))$ into $U_n$. We have $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}\beta$, so $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test. Thus there is an $n$ such that $\alpha \notin U_n$. Let $s_0 = 0$ and let $s_1, s_2, \ldots$ be the stages at which we put something into $U_n$. Then for $i > 0$ we have $\alpha_{s_{i+1}} - \alpha_{s_i} > 2^{-n}(\beta_{s_i} - \beta_{s_{i-1}})$, so $\beta \leqslant_\text{S} \alpha$. $\square$

Thus we see that for left-c.e. reals $\alpha$, the following are equivalent.

1. $\alpha$ is 1-random.
2. $\beta \leqslant_\text{S} \alpha$ for all left-c.e. reals $\beta$.
3. $\Omega \leqslant_\text{S} \alpha$.
4. $K(\beta \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$ for all left-c.e. reals $\beta$.
5. $K(\Omega \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.
6. $K(\Omega \upharpoonright n) = K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1)$.
7. $\alpha$ is the halting probability of some universal machine, that is, a version of $\Omega$.

Notice in particular that if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are both 1-random left-c.e. reals, then $\alpha \equiv_\text{S} \beta$, and hence $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) = K(\beta \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1)$. Since the 1-randomness of $X$ requires only that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + O(1)$, but $K(X \upharpoonright n)$ can be as large as $n + K(n) \pm O(1)$, the prefix-free complexity of the initial segments of a 1-random set can oscillate quite a bit. However, this oscillation is the same for all left-c.e. reals. That is, remarkably, all 1-random left-c.e. reals have high complexity (well above $n$) and relatively low complexity (close to $n$) at the same initial segments.

It is interesting to note that all 1-random left-d.c.e. reals are either left-c.e. or right-c.e.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.2.4 — Rettinger)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a 1-random left-d.c.e. real. Then either $\alpha$ or $1 - \alpha$ is a left-c.e. real.

</div>

### 9.3 Presentations of Left-c.e. Reals and Complexity

Recall from Chapter 5 that a presentation of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is a prefix-free c.e. set $W \subset 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\alpha = \sum_{\sigma \in W} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$. In Corollary 5.3.14 we saw that there are noncomputable left-c.e. reals $\alpha$ such that any presentation of $\alpha$ is computable. By Theorems 9.2.2 and 9.2.3, such reals cannot be 1-random, since the domain of a universal prefix-free machine is not computable. However, Stephan and Wu showed that they must have rather high initial segment complexity, and in particular must be weakly 1-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.3.1 — Stephan and Wu)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real for which there is a computable function $f$ such that $K(\alpha \upharpoonright f(n)) < f(n) - n$ for all $n$. Then $\alpha$ has a noncomputable presentation.

</div>

In particular, by Theorem 7.2.16, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.3.2 — Stephan and Wu)</span></p>

If every presentation of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is computable then $\alpha$ is either computable or weakly 1-random but not 1-random.

</div>

### 9.4 Solovay Functions and 1-randomness

Recall from Section 3.12 that Solovay proved the existence of a Solovay function, that is, a computable function $f$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, whence $K(n) \leqslant f(n) + O(1)$, and $\liminf_n f(n) - K(n) < \infty$. It turns out that, among the computable functions $f$ such that sum $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$ is finite, the Solovay functions are precisely those for which this sum is a 1-random real. This proof exploits the fact that the Kučera-Slaman Theorem provides a method for characterizing Solovay functions in terms of $\Omega$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.4.1 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

Let $f$ be a computable function. The following are equivalent.

1. $f$ is a Solovay function.
2. $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$ is a 1-random real.

</div>

The following easy corollary, which says that there are *nondecreasing* computable upper bounds for $K$, is not at all obvious without the previous result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.4.2 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

There exist nondecreasing Solovay functions.

</div>

*Proof.* It suffices to take a computable sequence $\lbrace r_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of rational numbers such that every $r_n$ is a negative power of 2, the $r_n$ are nonincreasing, and $\sum_n r_n$ is a 1-random real. (It is easy to see that such sequences exist.) Then let $f(n) = -\log(r_n)$ for all $n$. The function $f$ is computable, nondecreasing, and, by Theorem 9.4.1, is a Solovay function. $\square$

### 9.5 Solovay Degrees of Left-c.e. Reals

As with any reducibility, we can take equivalence classes of reals under S-reducibility, which we call **Solovay degrees** or **S-degrees**, and are partially ordered in the obvious way. One way to express the main result of Section 9.2 is that there is a largest S-degree of left-c.e. reals, which consists exactly of the 1-random left-c.e. reals, or, equivalently, the versions of $\Omega$. We have seen that S-reducibility implies Turing reducibility, and it is easy to show that if $\sigma$ is computable then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ for any left-c.e. real $\beta$. Thus there is also a least S-degree of left-c.e. reals, which consists exactly of the computable reals. As we will see, between these two extremes there is a rich structure.

It was observed by Solovay and others, such as Calude, Hertling, Khoussainov, and Wang, that the Solovay degrees of left-c.e. reals form an uppersemilattice, with the join operation induced by addition (or multiplication). Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies showed that this uppersemilattice is **distributive**, where an uppersemilattice is distributive if $c \leqslant a \vee b \Rightarrow \exists c_0 \leqslant a\; \exists c_1 \leqslant b\; (c = c_0 \vee c_1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.1 — Solovay; Calude, Hertling, Khoussainov, and Wang; Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

The Solovay degrees of left-c.e. reals form a distributive uppersemilattice, with the join operation induced by addition.

</div>

On the other hand, the S-degrees of left-c.e. reals do not form a lattice.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.2 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

There exist c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ such that the Solovay degrees of $A$ and $B$ have no infimum in the Solovay degrees of left-c.e. reals.

</div>

Some results about the structure of the S-degrees of left-c.e. reals come for free from the fact that S-reducibility implies Turing reducibility. For instance, there are minimal pairs of S-degrees of left-c.e. reals because there are minimal pairs of c.e. Turing degrees. Other results require work, however. The question that led both authors into the study of algorithmic randomness was whether the partial order of S-degrees of left-c.e. reals is dense. This question led to the following results.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\gamma <_\text{S} \alpha <_\text{S} \Omega$ be left-c.e. reals. There are left-c.e. reals $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ such that $\gamma <_\text{S} \beta_i <_\text{S} \alpha$ for $i = 0, 1$ and $\beta_0 + \beta_1 = \alpha$.

</div>

In other words, in the S-degrees of left-c.e. reals, every incomplete degree splits over every lesser degree.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\gamma <_\text{S} \Omega$ be a left-c.e. real. There is a left-c.e. real $\beta$ such that $\gamma <_\text{S} \beta <_\text{S} \Omega$.

</div>

These two theorems together establish the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

The partial order of Solovay degrees of left-c.e. reals is dense.

</div>

The hypothesis that $\alpha <_\text{S} \Omega$ in the statement of Theorem 9.5.3 is necessary. In fact, the S-degree of $\Omega$ does not split at all in the S-degrees of left-c.e. reals, which can be seen as a structural reflection of the qualitative difference between 1-random and non-1-random left-c.e. reals. This fact will follow from a stronger result that shows that, despite the upwards density of the S-degrees of left-c.e. reals, there is a sense in which the S-degree of $\Omega$ is very much above all other S-degrees of left-c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.8 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals and let $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ and $\beta_0 < \beta_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ be computable sequences of rationals. Let $f$ be an increasing computable function and let $k > 0$. If $\beta$ is 1-random and there are infinitely many $s$ such that $k(\alpha - \alpha_s) > \beta - \beta_{f(s)}$, then $\alpha$ is 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.9 — Demuth)</span></p>

If $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ are left-c.e. reals such that $\alpha_0 + \alpha_1$ is 1-random then at least one of $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ is 1-random.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.10 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

A left-c.e. real $\gamma$ is 1-random if and only if it cannot be written as $\alpha + \beta$ for left-c.e. reals $\alpha, \beta <_\text{S} \gamma$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.11 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\mathbf{d}$ be a Solovay degree of left-c.e. reals. The following are equivalent:

1. $\mathbf{d}$ is incomplete.
2. $\mathbf{d}$ splits.
3. $\mathbf{d}$ splits over any lesser Solovay degree.

</div>

We finish this section by noting that the structure of S-degrees of left-c.e. reals is not too simple.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.5.12 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

The first-order theory of the uppersemilattice of Solovay degrees of left-c.e. reals is undecidable.

</div>

### 9.6 cl-reducibility and rK-reducibility

Despite the success of S-reducibility in characterizing the 1-random left-c.e. reals, there are reasons to search for other measures of relative randomness. For one thing, S-reducibility is quite strong, and is rather uniform in a way that $K$-reducibility, for instance, is not. (That is, if $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ then $\alpha$ is quite closely tied to $\beta$ in a computability-theoretic sense, while there is no reason to suppose that, whatever we mean by "$A$ is no more random than $B$", this notion should imply that $A$ is so closely tied to $B$.) Furthermore, S-reducibility is quite badly behaved outside the left-c.e. reals, as exemplified by the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.1)</span></p>

There is a right-c.e. real $\beta$ that is not S-above any left-c.e. real (including the computable reals).

</div>

Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte introduced another measure of relative complexity, now often called *computable Lipschitz* or **cl-reducibility**. This reduction was originally called *sw-reducibility* by Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.2 — cl-reducibility)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **cl-reducible** to a set $B$ (written $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$) if there is a functional $\Gamma$ such that $\Gamma^B = A$ and $\gamma^B(n) \leqslant n + O(1)$.

If the constant is zero, then we will call this an **ibT-reduction**, for *identity bounded Turing* reduction, and write $A \leqslant_\text{ibT} B$.

</div>

If $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$ then we can describe $A \upharpoonright n$ using $B \upharpoonright n$ and $O(1)$ many more bits (to represent the additional bits of $B$ below $\gamma^B(n)$). Thus $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$ and $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. In other words, like S-reducibility, cl-reducibility implies both $K$-reducibility and $C$-reducibility. Obviously, cl-reducibility also implies Turing (and even wtt-) reducibility.

We would like a measure of relative randomness and cl-reducibility while being less uniform and more closely tied to initial segment complexity. The following is a natural candidate, introduced by Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.3 — rK-reducibility)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **relative $K$-reducible** or **rK-reducible** to a set $B$ (written $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$) if $K(A \upharpoonright n \mid B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant O(1)$.

</div>

Since $C(\sigma \mid \tau) \leqslant K(\sigma \mid \tau) + O(1)$ and $K(\sigma \mid \tau) \leqslant 2C(\sigma \mid \tau) + O(1)$, it would not change this definition to use plain complexity in place of prefix-free complexity. If $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$ then the initial segments of $A$ are easy to describe given the corresponding initial segments of $B$, and thus it makes sense to think of $A$ as no more random than $B$. Clearly, rK-reducibility implies $K$-reducibility and $C$-reducibility.

We now give alternate characterizations of rK-reducibility that demonstrate how it can be seen as a less uniform version of both S-reducibility and cl-reducibility. Item (ii) below can be seen as a form of "computation with advice".

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$.
2. There are a partial computable function $f : 2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{N} \to 2^{<\omega}$ and a constant $k$ such that for each $n$ there is a $j \leqslant k$ for which $f(B \upharpoonright n, j) \!\downarrow = A \upharpoonright n$.
3. There are a partial computable function $g : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{Q}$ and a constant $c$ such that for each $n$ there is a $\tau \in 2^{n+c}$ with $\lvert 0.B - 0.\tau \rvert \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for which $g(\tau) \!\downarrow$ and $\lvert 0.A - g(\tau) \rvert \leqslant 2^{-n}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{rK} \beta$.

</div>

For left-c.e. reals, we have another characterization of rK-reducibility in terms of "settling times". It roughly says that if $\alpha \leqslant_\text{rK} \beta$ then there are approximations to $\alpha$ and $\beta$ such that $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ cannot change much after the stage at which $\beta \upharpoonright n$ settles to its true value. For a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ and a fixed computable approximation $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$, let the **mind-change function** $m(\alpha, n, s, t)$ be the cardinality of $\lbrace u \in [s, t] : \alpha_u \upharpoonright n \neq \alpha_{u+1} \upharpoonright n \rbrace$, and let $m(\alpha, n, s) = \lim_t m(\alpha, n, s, t)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.7 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals. The following are equivalent.

1. $\alpha \leqslant_\text{rK} \beta$.
2. There are a constant $k$ and computable approximations $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ and $\beta_0 < \beta_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ such that for all $n$ and $t > s$, if $\beta_t \upharpoonright n = \beta_s \upharpoonright n$ then $m(\alpha, n, s, t) \leqslant k$.
3. There are a constant $k$ and computable approximations $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ and $\beta_0 < \beta_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ such that for all $n$ and $s$, if $\beta \upharpoonright n = \beta_s \upharpoonright n$ then $m(\alpha, n, s) \leqslant k$.

</div>

Despite its nonuniform nature, rK-reducibility implies Turing reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.8 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} B$.

</div>

Since any computable set is rK-reducible to any other set, the above theorem shows that the computable sets form the least rK-degree. By the Kučera-Slaman Theorem, together with the fact that rK-reducibility implies $K$-reducibility and is implied by S-reducibility, the largest rK-degree of left-c.e. reals is the same as the largest S-degree of left-c.e. reals, namely the 1-random left-c.e. reals. Furthermore, like the case of S-reducibility but unlike that of cl-reducibility, the rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals are an uppersemilattice, with join induced by addition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.9 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

The rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals form an uppersemilattice with least degree that of the computable sets, highest degree that of the 1-random left-c.e. reals, and join induced by addition.

</div>

Notice that the only use of the fact that we were working with left-c.e. reals was in showing that $\alpha, \beta \leqslant_\text{rK} \alpha + \beta$. That is of course not necessarily the case in general, since, for example, $\Omega + (1 - \Omega) \nleqslant_\text{rK} 1$. It follows from the proof of Theorem 9.5.2 that the rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals are not a lattice.

As we will see in Section 9.8, the analogs of Theorem 9.5.3 and Corollary 9.5.5 hold for rK-reducibility. Together with Corollary 9.5.9, they give the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.10 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

The partial order of rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals is dense. Furthermore, in the rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals, every incomplete degree splits over every lesser degree, while the complete degree does not split at all.

</div>

Raichev observed that rK-lower cones are well-behaved, in the following sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.6.11 — Raichev)</span></p>

For any $A$, the reals rK-reducible to $A$ form a real closed field.

</div>

Thus rK-reducibility shares many of the nice structural properties of S-reducibility on the left-c.e. reals, while still being a reasonable measure of relative randomness on all sets. There are still some basic open questions about rK-reducibility, however, such as whether the semilattice of rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals is distributive, or whether every set is rK-reducible to a 1-random set.

### 9.7 $K$-reducibility and $C$-reducibility

Recall that $A$ is $K$-reducible to $B$ (written $A \leqslant_K B$) if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$, and $A$ is $C$-reducible to $B$ (written $A \leqslant_C B$) if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. These reducibilities are the most basic measures of relative randomness based on the Kolmogorov complexity approach. If $A$ is 1-random and $A \leqslant_K B$, then $B$ is clearly 1-random. The same holds if $A \leqslant_C B$ by Theorem 6.7.2.

We have seen in Theorem 3.4.4 that a set $A$ is computable iff $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$. The following theorem extends that result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.7.1 — Stephan)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals. If $\alpha \leqslant_C \beta$ then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{T} \beta$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\beta \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset'$, then there is nothing to prove, so we assume that $\beta <_\text{T} \emptyset'$. Let $f(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $n \in \emptyset'_s$, if there is such an $s$, and $f(n) \!\uparrow$ otherwise. Let $g(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $\beta \upharpoonright n = \beta_s \upharpoonright n$. Then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f(n) \!\downarrow > g(n)$, since otherwise we could compute $\emptyset'$ using $g$ and hence using $\beta$. Thus we can find a $\beta$-computable infinite set $S \subseteq \emptyset'$ such that $f(n) > g(n)$ for all $n \in S$.

For $n \in S$, we can determine $\beta \upharpoonright n$ by computing $f(n)$, since then $\beta \upharpoonright n = \beta_{f(n)} \upharpoonright n$. Thus for all such $n$ we have $C(\beta \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$, and hence $C(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$. Since $S$ is $\beta$-computable, the relativized form of Chaitin's Theorem 3.4.5 implies that $\alpha \leqslant_\text{T} \beta$. $\square$

For left-c.e. reals, it follows from the Kučera-Slaman Theorem that the largest $K$-degree and the largest $C$-degree both consist exactly of the 1-random left-c.e. reals. Furthermore, the $K$-degrees and $C$-degrees of left-c.e. reals have basic structural properties similar to those of the S-degrees and rK-degrees of left-c.e. reals.

Sets with the property that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ (including all the computable sets) are called *$K$-trivial* because they form the lowest $K$-degree, and are of great interest in the theory of algorithmic randomness, as we will see in Chapter 11.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.7.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals. Then $C((\alpha + \beta) \upharpoonright n) = \max(C(\alpha \upharpoonright n), C(\beta \upharpoonright n)) \pm O(1)$ and $K((\alpha + \beta) \upharpoonright n) = \max(K(\alpha \upharpoonright n), K(\beta \upharpoonright n)) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We do the proof for prefix-free complexity; the same works for plain complexity. Let $\gamma = \alpha + \beta$. Let $\alpha_0 < \alpha_1 < \cdots \to \alpha$ and $\beta_0 < \beta_1 < \cdots \to \beta$ be computable sequences of rationals and let $\gamma_s = \alpha_s + \beta_s$.

If we know $\gamma \upharpoonright n$ then we can find an $s$ such that $\gamma_s \upharpoonright n = \gamma \upharpoonright n$. The approximation to $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ can change at most once after stage $s$, since if there were two such changes, we would have $\gamma - \gamma_s \geqslant \alpha - \alpha_s > 2^{-n}$, and hence $\gamma_s \upharpoonright n \neq \gamma \upharpoonright n$. Thus we can describe $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ given $\gamma \upharpoonright n$ and one more bit of information, whence $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\gamma \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. Similarly, $K(\beta \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\gamma \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

For the other direction, fix $n$. Let $s$ be least such that $\alpha_s \upharpoonright n = \alpha \upharpoonright n$ and let $t$ be least such that $\beta_t \upharpoonright n = \beta \upharpoonright n$. Suppose that $s \geqslant t$. Then given $\alpha \upharpoonright n$, we can compute $s$, which also gives us $\beta \upharpoonright n = \beta_s \upharpoonright n$. From this information plus a couple of bits to represent possible carries from further bits of $\alpha$ and $\beta$, we obtain $\gamma \upharpoonright n$. Thus $K(\gamma \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. Similarly, if $t \geqslant s$ then $K(\gamma \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(\beta \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.7.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

The $K$-degrees and $C$-degrees of left-c.e. reals both form uppersemilattices with join given by addition.

</div>

We will see in Section 9.8 that the analogs of Theorem 9.5.3 and Corollary 9.5.5 hold for $K$-reducibility and $C$-reducibility. Together with Corollary 9.5.9, they give the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.7.4 — after Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

The partial orders of $K$-degrees and $C$-degrees of left-c.e. reals are dense. Furthermore, in both the $K$-degrees and $C$-degrees of left-c.e. reals, every incomplete degree splits over every lesser degree, while the complete degree does not split at all.

</div>

As mentioned above, the fact that S-reducibility, cl-reducibility, and rK-reducibility imply Turing reducibility allows us to transfer certain results, such as the existence of minimal pairs, from the Turing case. This tool is not available for $K$-reducibility. We will see in Theorem 9.15.8 that minimal pairs of $K$-degrees do exist, but it is not known whether there are minimal pairs of $K$-degrees of left-c.e. (or even $\Delta^0_2$) reals.

### 9.8 Density and Splittings

In this section, we give a unified proof of the density and splitting theorems for various reducibilities considered above (Corollary 9.5.5 and Theorems 9.5.3, 9.6.10, and 9.7.4). Indeed, we will show that analogous results hold for all reducibilities with a few basic properties given in the following definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.8.1)</span></p>

Let $C_0, C_1, \ldots$ be an effective list of all left-c.e. reals. A reducibility $\text{r}$ on the left-c.e. reals is $\Sigma^0_3$ if there is a total computable function $\Phi$ such that for all $a, b$, we have $C_a \leqslant_\text{r} C_b$ iff $\exists k\; \forall m\; \exists n\; \Phi(a, b, k, m, n)$.

The reducibility $\text{r}$ is **standard** if $\text{r}$ is $\Sigma^0_3$, every computable real is r-reducible to any given left-c.e. real, addition is a join in the r-degrees of left-c.e. reals, and for any left-c.e. real $\alpha$ and any rational $q > 0$, we have $\alpha \equiv_\text{r} q\alpha$.

</div>

Standard reducibilities include Solovay reducibility, rK-reducibility, $K$-reducibility, and $C$-reducibility (but not cl-reducibility, as addition is not a join in the cl-degrees of left-c.e. reals). We begin with the splitting theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.8.2 — after Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\text{r}$ be a standard reducibility on the left-c.e. reals. Let $\gamma <_\text{r} \alpha <_\text{S} \Omega$ be left-c.e. reals. There are left-c.e. reals $\beta^0$ and $\beta^1$ such that $\gamma <_\text{r} \beta^i \leqslant_\text{r} \alpha$ for $i = 0, 1$ and $\beta^0 + \beta^1 = \alpha$.

</div>

We now prove the upwards density theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.8.3 — after Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\text{r}$ be a standard reducibility on the left-c.e. reals. Let $\gamma <_\text{r} \Omega$ be a left-c.e. real. There is a left-c.e. real $\beta$ such that $\gamma <_\text{r} \beta <_\text{r} \Omega$.

</div>

Combining Theorems 9.8.2 and 9.8.3, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.8.4 — after Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\text{r}$ be a standard reducibility on the left-c.e. reals that is at least as strong as Solovay reducibility. Then the r-degrees of left-c.e. reals are dense.

</div>

### 9.9 Monotone Degrees and Density

One notable reducibility missing from the list above is monotone reducibility, introduced in Section 4.5. Certainly this is a $\Sigma^0_3$ reducibility for which the degree of $\Omega$ is the top degree among left-c.e. reals and the computable sets form the bottom degree. However, it is not known whether addition induces a join on this degree structure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Open Question</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.9.1)</span></p>

Does addition induce a join on the monotone degrees of left-c.e. reals?

</div>

We conjecture that the answer is no. In spite of this lack of knowledge, there is still a downward density theorem for monotone reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.9.2 — Calhoun)</span></p>

The monotone degrees of left-c.e. reals are downward dense, meaning that if $\mathbf{b}$ is a nonzero $Km$-degree of left-c.e. reals then there is a $Km$-degree of left-c.e. reals $\mathbf{a}$ with $\mathbf{b} > \mathbf{a} > \mathbf{0}$.

</div>

The proof uses the following useful lemma, which says that we can use simple permitting in this case.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.9.3 — Calhoun)</span></p>

Suppose that $A = \lim_s A_s$ and $B = \lim_s B_s$ are monotonic approximations to left-c.e. reals $A$ and $B$ and $f$ is a computable function such that $B_s \upharpoonright n = B \upharpoonright n \Rightarrow A_{f(s)} \upharpoonright n = A \upharpoonright n$ for all $s$ and $n$. Then $A \leqslant_{Km} B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Open Question</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.9.4)</span></p>

Are the $Km$-degrees of left-c.e. reals dense?

</div>

### 9.10 Further Relationships between S-, cl-, and rK-reducibilities

It follows from Theorem 9.1.6 that S-reducibility does not imply cl-reducibility on the left-c.e. reals. The following result shows that S-reducibility and cl-reducibility are in fact incomparable on the left-c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

There exist left-c.e. reals $\alpha \leqslant_\text{cl} \beta$ such that $\alpha \nleqslant_\text{S} \beta$. Moreover, $\alpha$ can be chosen to be strongly c.e.

</div>

On the other hand, S-reducibility and cl-reducibility do coincide on c.e. *sets*, as the following results show.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real and $\beta$ be a strongly c.e. real. If $\alpha \leqslant_\text{cl} \beta$ then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a strongly c.e. real and $\beta$ be a left-c.e. real. If $\alpha \leqslant_\text{S} \beta$ then $\alpha \leqslant_\text{cl} \beta$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

For c.e. sets $A$ and $B$, we have $A \leqslant_\text{S} B$ iff $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$.

</div>

Thus cl-reducibility can be a particularly useful tool in studying c.e. sets. For example, we have seen that there is a largest S-degree of left-c.e. reals, but we now show that there is no largest S-degree of c.e. *sets*.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $A$ be a c.e. set. There is a c.e. set that is not cl-below $A$, and hence is not S-below $A$.

</div>

Since both S-reducibility and cl-reducibility imply rK-reducibility, it follows from Theorems 9.1.6 and 9.10.1 that rK-reducibility does not imply either of them on left-c.e. reals. We now show that this is the case even for c.e. sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

There exist c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$ but $A \nleqslant_\text{cl} B$.

</div>

One further difference between cl-reducibility and S-reducibility is that if $\alpha$ is a noncomputable left-c.e. real, then there is a noncomputable c.e. set cl-below $\alpha$, while there may not be any noncomputable c.e. set S-below $\alpha$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.7 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real. Then there is a c.e. set $B$ such that $B \leqslant_\text{cl} \alpha$ and $\alpha \leqslant_\text{rK} B$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.10.8 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is a noncomputable left-c.e. real $\alpha$ such that all c.e. sets S-below $\alpha$ are computable.

</div>

### 9.11 A Minimal rK-degree

In this section, we prove the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.11.1 — Raichev and Stephan)</span></p>

There is a minimal rK-degree.

</div>

This result is interesting because rK is one of the few measures of relative randomness for which we know whether or not there is a minimal degree. The proof below uses the fact that rK is reasonably well-behaved on very sparse sets. Merkle and Stephan have exploited this idea of using sparse sets to establish results about measures of relative randomness quite fruitfully, as we will see in Section 9.15.

*Proof of Theorem 9.11.1.* We build a $\Pi^0_1$ class $[T]$ for a computable tree $T$ with no computable paths. The tree $T$ will have the property that for all total functionals $\Phi$, for all $X \in [T]$, there is a string $\sigma \prec X$ such that one of the following holds:

1. $\Phi^X$ and $\Phi^Y$ are compatible for all $Y \in [T]$ extending $\sigma$ or
2. $\Phi^Z$ and $\Phi^Y$ are incompatible for all $Z \neq Y$ extending $\sigma$ in $[T]$.

We make the set $S$ of splitting nodes of $[T]$ computably sparse. That is, for all computable functions $g$,

$$\forall^\infty \sigma \in S\; \forall \tau \in S\; (\sigma \prec \tau \to g(\lvert\sigma\rvert) < \lvert\tau\rvert).$$

Now let $A$ be a path on $T$ of hyperimmune-free degree, obtained using the Hyperimmune-Free Basis Theorem 2.19.11. We claim that $A$ has minimal rK-degree. Suppose that $\emptyset \neq_\text{T} B \leqslant_\text{rK} A$. Then $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ also and hence, as $A$ is hyperimmune free, $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$. Let $\Phi^A = B$ be the witnessing truth table reduction with computable use $\varphi$.

We show that $A \leqslant_\text{rK} B$. Since $B$ is not computable, (ii) must hold for $\Phi$. Let $\sigma$ be the string mentioned in (ii). We need the following lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.11.2)</span></p>

For almost all $n$ and almost all stages $t$, the tree $T_t$ has at most two extensions of $\sigma$ of length $n$ with extensions in $T_t$ that can map to $B \upharpoonright n$ under $\Phi$.

</div>

To complete the proof, given $B \upharpoonright n$, run through computable approximations of $T_s$ until a sufficiently large stage $t$ is found where $T_t$ has at most two extensions of $\sigma$ that can map to $B \upharpoonright n$ under $\Phi$. The stage $t$ exists by Lemma 9.11.2. We can find these extensions effectively from $B \upharpoonright n$ as $\Phi$ is a tt-reduction. The output of the two strings being found, one will be $A \upharpoonright n$. This is an rK-reduction. $\square$

Raichev and Stephan established various facts about minimal rK-degrees. For example, they showed that there are $2^{\aleph_0}$ many of them, as the hyperimmune-free basis theorem shows that every $\Pi^0_1$ class with no computable members has $2^{\aleph_0}$ many hyperimmune-free members. Additionally, they showed that minimal rK-degrees have fairly low initial segment complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.11.3 — Raichev and Stephan)</span></p>

If $A$ has minimal rK-degree, then for any computable order $g$, and for $Q$ either $C$ or $K$,

$$Q(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant Q(n) + g(n) + O(1).$$

</div>

This result shows that if $A$ has minimal rK-degree then it is far from random. By the Kučera-Gács Theorem 8.3.2, we know that there is a 1-random $R$ with $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} R$. Now choose an $h$ growing much faster than the use of the reduction $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} R$. Then $A_h \leqslant_\text{S} R$, where $A_h$ is as in the above proof, and hence we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.11.4 — Raichev and Stephan)</span></p>

Every real of minimal rK-degree is rK-reducible to a 1-random real.

</div>

Raichev and Stephan also showed that there are 1-random reals with no minimal rK-degrees below them.

### 9.12 Initial Segment Complexity and Completeness for Left-c.e. Reals

Although $K$-reducibility is far from implying cl-reducibility, even on left-c.e. reals, if the prefix-free complexity of the initial segments of a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ is much greater than that of the corresponding initial segments of a left-c.e. real $\beta$, then we do have $\beta <_\text{cl} \alpha$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.12.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be left-c.e. reals such that $\lim_n K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) - K(\beta \upharpoonright n) = \infty$. Then $\beta <_\text{cl} \alpha$.

</div>

Stephan has shown that this result has quite limited applicability, however. Its hypothesis implies that $\lim_n K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) - K(n) = \infty$, which implies in turn that $\alpha$ is wtt-complete.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.12.2 — Stephan)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real such that $\lim_n K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) - K(n) = \infty$. Then $\alpha$ is wtt-complete, and indeed $\alpha \geqslant_\text{cl} \emptyset'$.

</div>

*Proof.* We give a new proof of this result. Let $n_0, n_1, \ldots$ be an enumeration without repetitions of $\emptyset'$. Let $L = \lbrace (K_t(n_s), \alpha_s \upharpoonright n_s) : t \geqslant s \rbrace$. Then the weight of $L$ is bounded by $\sum_s \sum_{\mathcal{U}(\sigma) = n_s} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} < 1$, so $L$ is a KC-set. Thus $K(\alpha_s \upharpoonright n_s) \leqslant K(n_s) + O(1)$. Hence, for all but finitely many $s$, we have $\alpha \upharpoonright n_s \neq \alpha_s \upharpoonright n_s$. So, for all but finitely many $n$, we can compute $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ using $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ by searching for a stage $s$ such that $\alpha \upharpoonright n = \alpha_s \upharpoonright n$, and noting that $n \in \emptyset'$ iff $n = n_t$ for some $t \leqslant s$. $\square$

Of course, we can use any c.e. set in place of $\emptyset'$ in the above proof, so we have the following result, whose second part follows by Theorem 9.10.5.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.12.3)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real. Then either $\alpha \geqslant_\text{cl} B$ for all c.e. sets $B$ or there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$.

Thus, if $A$ is a c.e. set then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

Stephan also noted that the analog of Theorem 9.12.2 holds for plain complexity. He attributes this result to folklore.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.12.4)</span></p>

Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real such that $\lim_n C(\alpha \upharpoonright n) - C(n) = \infty$. Then $\alpha$ is wtt-complete, and indeed $\alpha \geqslant_\text{cl} \emptyset'$.

</div>

### 9.13 cl-reducibility and the Kučera-Gács Theorem

Recall that the Kučera-Gács Theorem 8.3.2 states that every set is wtt-reducible to a 1-random set. In this section, we show that this wtt-reduction cannot in general be improved to a cl-reduction. In other words, the use of the reduction in the Kučera-Gács Theorem cannot be made to be $n + O(1)$. Indeed, there is a $\emptyset'$-computable set that is not cl-reducible to any complex set. (Recall that a set $A$ is complex if there is a computable order $h$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > h(n)$ for all $n$.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.13.1 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

Let $\Gamma$ be a cl-reduction with use bounded by $x + c$, let $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, and let $f$ be a computable order. There exists a $\tau \succ \sigma$ such that for every $\mu \in 2^{\lvert\tau\rvert + c}$, if $\Gamma^\mu = \tau$ then $K(\mu) < f(\lvert\mu\rvert)$. Furthermore, $\tau$ can be found $\emptyset'$-computably.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.13.2 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

There is an $A \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$ that is not cl-reducible to any complex set.

</div>

### 9.14 Further Properties of cl-reducibility

This section could be called "When reducibilities go bad". We will see that in spite of a number of nice features mentioned in the previous section, cl-reducibility also has some undesirable properties.

#### 9.14.1 cl-reducibility and Joins

By Theorem 9.5.2, and the note following it, the cl-degrees of left-c.e. reals do not form a lowersemilattice. That is a common feature of many reducibilities. However, the cl-degrees of left-c.e. reals also do not form an uppersemilattice; that is, there is no join operation for them. This result was first proved directly by Downey, Hirschfeldt, and LaForte, but follows from the proof of the following result of Yu and Ding.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.1 — Yu and Ding)</span></p>

There is no cl-complete left-c.e. real.

</div>

Actually, they proved something even stronger:

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.2 — Yu and Ding)</span></p>

There are two left-c.e. reals $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ such that there is no left-c.e. real $\beta$ with $\alpha_i \leqslant_\text{cl} \beta$ for $i = 0, 1$.

</div>

The main idea of the construction is that if $\beta$ is a left-c.e. real that cl-computes both $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$, then alternatingly adding little bits to $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ (drip-feeding them, as it were) is sufficient to drive $\beta$ to be too large. Intuitively, a real $\beta$ computing another real $\gamma$ with use every $x$ means that as soon as $\gamma$ changes at position $x$, the real $\beta$ must change at a position less than or equal to $x$. That is, if $\gamma$ can be computed with oracle $\beta$ and use $x$, then $\beta$ is not less than $\gamma$. So if there were a largest c.e. cl-degree $\beta$, we could select two reals $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ and change them alternatingly to drive $\beta$ to be very large.

Furthermore, we can reserve parts of the reals for this purpose, to work independently for each requirement. Suppose that $\Gamma_0$ and $\Gamma_1$ are cl-reductions and that $\beta$ is a left-c.e. real. For all such triples, we need to meet the requirement

$$R_{\Gamma_0, \Gamma_1, \beta} : \text{ Either } \Gamma_0^\beta \neq \alpha_0 \text{ or } \Gamma_1^\beta \neq \alpha_1.$$

For simplicity, we take the reductions $\Gamma_i$ to be ibT-reductions. To meet requirement $R = R_{\Gamma_0, \Gamma_1, \beta}$, we describe a family of modules, each indexed by an interval of natural numbers. Let $a \leqslant b$. A stage $s$ is *expansionary* for the $[a, b)$-module (for $R$) if for both $i = 0, 1$ we have $\Gamma_i^{\beta_s}[s] \supset \alpha_{i,s} \upharpoonright b$. The instructions for the module are as follows.

Repeat the following $2^{b-a} - 1$ many times:

1. At the next expansionary stage, add $2^{-b}$ to $\alpha_0$.
2. At the next expansionary stage, add $2^{-b}$ to $\alpha_1$.

At the end, wait for the next expansionary stage and then return.

To meet the requirement $R$, we run the $[k, k + 2^k)$-module for $R$ for some $k$. This module cannot return, so it must get stuck waiting for some expansionary stage, and hence $R$ is met. It is easy to see that assuming that we start with $\alpha_i \upharpoonright [a, b) = 0^{b-a}$, the $[a, b)$-module for any requirement makes changes only in $\alpha_i \upharpoonright [a, b)$ (for both $i = 0, 1$) and so if for distinct requirements we run modules on disjoint intervals, then there is no interaction between the requirements and so we can meet them all.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.3)</span></p>

Suppose that $\alpha_{0,t_0} \upharpoonright [a, b) = \alpha_{1,t_0} \upharpoonright [a, b) = 0^{b-a}$. Suppose that at stage $t_0$, an $[a, b)$-module for $R$ begins and returns at a stage $t_1$. Then

$$\beta_{t_1} \upharpoonright a - \beta_{t_0} \upharpoonright a \geqslant b - a.$$

</div>

*Proof.* By induction on $b - a$. If $b = a$ there is nothing to prove. Assume the lemma holds when $b - a = n$, and let $a < b$ be such that $b - a = n + 1$. The key observation is that the $[a, b)$-module consists of three parts:

1. Running the $[a + 1, b)$-module.
2. Running one iteration of adding $2^{-b}$ to $\alpha_0$ and then $\alpha_1$ (and waiting for expansionary stages).
3. Running the $[a + 1, b)$-module again.

Note that both times we run the $[a + 1, b)$-module, we start with $\alpha_i \upharpoonright [a + 1, b) = 0^n$. Let $s_0$ be the stage at which the first $[a + 1, b)$-module returns, and $s_1$ be the stage at which the second one begins. By induction,

$$\beta_{s_0} \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_{t_0} \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant n$$

and

$$\beta_{t_1} \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_{s_1} \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant n.$$

Between $s_0$ and $s_1$ we have a change in $\alpha_0(a)$, which forces a change in $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$ by the next expansionary stage, and then a change in $\alpha_1(n)$, which forces another change in $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$. Each of these changes adds at least 1 to $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$. Thus, in total,

$$\beta_{t_1} \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_{t_0} \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant 2(n + 1),$$

which implies that

$$\beta_{t_1} \upharpoonright a - \beta_{t_0} \upharpoonright a \geqslant n + 1,$$

as required. This concludes the verification: the $[k, k + 2^k)$-module can never return because that would force $\beta \upharpoonright k \geqslant 2^k$, which is impossible.

The Kučera-Slaman Theorem 9.2.3 says that all 1-random left-c.e. reals are the same in terms of their complexity oscillations, and have sequences of rationals converging to them at essentially the same rates; and that from any of them, we can obtain any given left-c.e. real via a rather strong reduction. One consequence of Theorem 9.14.2, however, is that, in general, there is no efficient algorithm (in terms of the number of bits used) to obtain the *bits* of a left-c.e. real from the bits of a 1-random left-c.e. real.

#### 9.14.2 Array Noncomputability and Joins

By adding multiple permitting to the argument above, Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg were able to classify the degrees within which constructions such as the one in the proof of Theorem 9.14.2 are possible.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.4 — Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a degree **d**.

(i) There are left-c.e. reals $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ in **d** that do not have a common upper bound in the ibT-degrees (or cl-degrees) of left-c.e. reals.

(ii) **d** is c.e. and array noncomputable.

</div>

*Proof.* We begin by showing that we can perform the construction in the previous subsection within an array noncomputable c.e. degree. Recall that a c.e. degree **d** is array noncomputable iff for each (or some) very strong array (i.e., partition $F_0, F_1, \ldots$ of $\mathbb{N}$ of increasing size), there is a c.e. set $D \in \mathbf{d}$ such that for all c.e. sets $W$ there are infinitely many $n$ with $W \upharpoonright F_n = D \upharpoonright F_n$. Later, we will calculate the desired size of $F_n$ for this proof.

Again, a module for requirement $R$ will operate on some interval $[a, b)$; the notion of an expansionary stage for a module for $R$ on an interval $[a, b)$ is defined as before. To such a module, we will assign an $n$ such that $\lvert F_n \rvert > 2(2^{b-a} - 1)$. The new module uses permissions from $F_n$.

The new module $M_R(a, 1, \varepsilon)$ is:

> Wait for an expansionary stage, then request permission. When permission is received, add $2^{-a-1}$ to $\alpha$; wait for another expansionary stage, and return $\emptyset$.

For $n > 1$, the new module $M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ is:

> Let $b$ be the least $b > a$ such that $2^{-b} < \frac{\varepsilon}{2}$. Let $\varepsilon' = \frac{\varepsilon}{2(b+n-a)}$.
>
> 1. For $k = a + 1, a + 2, \ldots, b + n$, call $M_R(k, n - 1, \varepsilon')$, and add the returned set to $U$.
> 2. Add the current version of $\beta \upharpoonright b$ to $U$, and wait for a stage at which $\beta \upharpoonright b$ changes.
> 3. Wait for an expansionary stage, then request permission. When permission is received, add $2^{-b-n-1}$ to $\alpha$. Wait for another expansionary stage, and return $U$.

For every $R$ and $n$, we run $M_R(a, 2^{a+1}, 2^{-n})$ on the $n$th interval $[a, c)$ assigned for $R$. We also let $\alpha(c_n) = 1$ iff $n \in D$, where $c_n$ is the $n$th element of $C$. That is the construction.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.5)</span></p>

$\alpha_i \leqslant_\text{ibT} \beta$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $s$ is a stage such that $\beta_s \upharpoonright n = \beta \upharpoonright n$ then we never add $2^{-n}$ to $\beta$ after stage $s$. This fact implies that $f_{i,t}(n) = f_{i,s}(n)$ for all $t > s$. Thus $f_{i,s}(n) = \alpha_i \upharpoonright n$.

We now prove the converse: if $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ are left-c.e. reals with array computable (Turing) degrees, then $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ have a common upper bound in the cl-degrees. The idea is that we can approximate $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$ with the number of mind changes (i.e., the function taking $n$ to $\lvert\lbrace s : \alpha_{i,s+1} \upharpoonright n \neq \alpha_{i,s} \upharpoonright n \rbrace\rvert$) bounded by a very slow growing function. This fact follows from the characterization of array computability as uniform total $\omega$-c.e.-ness in Lemma 2.23.6: A c.e. degree **d** is array computable iff for every computable order $h$, every $f \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{d}$ has an $h$-c.e. approximation. We can then use this slow bound on the number of mind changes to build a left-c.e. real $\beta$ that changes somewhere on its first $n$ digits whenever either $\alpha_0$ or $\alpha_1$ does so.

Again we think of these reals as elements of the interval $[0, 1]$. A request that $\beta \upharpoonright n$ change is met by adding $2^{-n}$ to $\beta$. If the number of requests for a $\beta \upharpoonright n$ change does not exceed a bound $g(n)$ such that

$$\sum_{n \geqslant 1} g(n) 2^{-n} < 1, \tag{9.2}$$

then we can construct a left-c.e. real that changes appropriately whenever we ask it to. In our case, a request to change $\beta \upharpoonright n$ is made whenever we see a new value for $\alpha_0 \upharpoonright n$ or $\alpha_1 \upharpoonright n$, so if the number of such new values is at most $\frac{g(n)}{2}$, then the plan will work. Thus, all we need to do is fix some computable order $g$ that grows sufficiently slowly so that (9.2) holds, and approximate the functions $n \mapsto \alpha_i \upharpoonright n$ in a $\frac{g(n)}{2}$-c.e. way, which is possible, as mentioned above.

There is not much to add to give a formal construction. Fix a computable order $g$ that satisfies (9.2). For $i = 0, 1$, fix computable binary functions $f_i$ such that (writing $f_{i,s}(n)$ for $f_i(s, n)$), for all $n$, the value $f_{i,s}(n)$ is a binary string of length $n$,

$$\lvert\lbrace s : f_{i,s+1}(n) \neq f_{i,s}(n) \rbrace\rvert \leqslant \frac{g(n)}{2},$$

and

$$\lim_s f_{i,s}(n) = \alpha_i \upharpoonright n.$$

We define a left-c.e. real $\beta$: start with $\beta_0 = 0$ and let

$$\beta_{s+1} = \beta_s + \sum \lbrace 2^{-n} : f_{i,s+1}(n) \neq f_{i,s}(n) \rbrace + \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \sigma \in U_s \wedge \sigma \prec \beta_s \rbrace.$$

The fact that $g$ satisfies (9.2) and the restriction on the number of mind-changes for the $f_i$ imply that $\beta = \lim_s \beta_s < 1$, so $\beta$ is well-defined.

#### 9.14.3 Left-c.e. Reals cl-reducible to Versions of $\Omega$

Although there is no maximal c.e. cl-degree, we can say a little about the cl-degrees of 1-random left-c.e. reals.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.6 — Downey and Hirschfeldt)</span></p>

If $A$ is a c.e. set and $\alpha$ is a 1-random left-c.e. real, then $A \leqslant_\text{ibT} \alpha$.

</div>
