*Proof.* Given $A$ and $\alpha$ as above, we must construct $\Gamma^\alpha = A$ with use $\gamma(x) = x$. Since $\alpha$ is 1-random, there is a $c$ such that $K(\alpha \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - c$ for all $n$. We enumerate a KC set $L$ and assume by the recursion theorem that we know an $e$ such that if $(m, \sigma) \in L$ then $K(\sigma) \leqslant m + e$.

Initially, we define $\Gamma^{\alpha_s}(n) = 0$ for all $n$, and maintain this definition unless $n$ enters $A_{s+1} \setminus A_s$. As usual, at such a stage, we would like to change $\Gamma^\alpha(n)$ from 0 to 1. To do this, we need $\alpha \upharpoonright n \neq \alpha_s \upharpoonright n$. Should we see a stage $t \geqslant s$ with $\alpha_t \upharpoonright n \neq \alpha_s \upharpoonright n$ then we can simply declare that $\Gamma^{\alpha_u}(n) = 1$ for all $u \geqslant t$. For $n > e + c + 2$, we can force such a $t$ to exist. We simply enumerate a request $(n - c - e - 1, \alpha_s \upharpoonright n)$ into $L$, ensuring that $K(\alpha_s \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - c - 1$, so that $\alpha \upharpoonright n \neq \alpha_s \upharpoonright n$. Note that $L$ is indeed a KC set because we make at most one request for each $n > e + c + 2$.

One consequence of this result is Proposition 6.1.2, that $\Omega$ is wtt-complete.

Barmpalias and Lewis proved that not every left-c.e. real is cl-reducible to a 1-random left-c.e. real. (This result should be compared with Theorem 9.13.2.) As with Theorem 9.14.2, this result was also strengthened by Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg, to classify the degrees within which it holds.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.7 — Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a c.e. degree **d**.

(i) There is a left-c.e. real $\alpha \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{d}$ not cl-reducible to any 1-random left-c.e. real.

(ii) **d** is array noncomputable.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that **d** is array noncomputable. As in the previous subsection, we begin with the construction of a left-c.e. real not ibT-reducible to a 1-random left-c.e. real, and later add multiple permitting.

We build a left-c.e. real $\alpha$ to meet the requirements

$$R_{\Gamma, \beta} : \text{ If } \Gamma^\beta = \alpha \text{ then } \beta \text{ is not 1-random.}$$

Here $\Gamma$ ranges over all ibT-functionals and $\beta$ ranges over all left-c.e. reals. To meet $R_{\Gamma, \beta}$, we run infinitely many modules, where the $n$th module enumerates a finite set of strings $U_n$ with $\mu(\llbracket U_n \rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-n}$, such that if $\Gamma^\beta = \alpha$ then $\beta \in \llbracket U_n \rrbracket$. Thus, together, these modules enumerate a Martin-Löf test covering $\beta$, whence $R_{\Gamma, \beta}$ is satisfied.

The modules are built by recursion from smaller and smaller building blocks. The module $M_R(a, 1, \varepsilon)$ is the following.

> Wait for an expansionary stage, then add $2^{-a-1}$ to $\alpha$. Wait for another expansionary stage, and return $\emptyset$.

For $n > 1$, the module $M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ is the following, where $U$ is the set it enumerates.

> Let $b$ be the least $b > a$ such that $2^{-b} < \frac{\varepsilon}{2}$. Let $\varepsilon' = \frac{\varepsilon}{2(b + n - a)}$.
>
> 1. For $k = a + 1, a + 2, \ldots, b + n$, call $M_R(k, n - 1, \varepsilon')$, and add the returned set to $U$.
> 2. Add the current version of $\beta \upharpoonright b$ to $U$, and wait for a stage at which $\beta \upharpoonright b$ changes.
> 3. Wait for an expansionary stage, then add $2^{-b-n-1}$ to $\alpha$. Wait for another expansionary stage, and return $U$.

We verify some properties of these modules, which will lead to the full construction.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.8)</span></p>

A module $M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ does not change $\alpha \upharpoonright a$. Indeed, there is a computable function $c(a, n, \varepsilon)$ such that for all $R$, $a$, $n$, and $\varepsilon$, if $c = c(a, n, \varepsilon)$, and a module $M = M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ starts at stage $s$ with $\alpha_s \upharpoonright [a, c) = 0^{c-a}$, then throughout its run, $M$ changes only $\alpha \upharpoonright [a, c)$, and if it returns at a stage $t$, then $\alpha_t \upharpoonright [a, c) = 10^{c-a-1}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.9)</span></p>

The measure of the set of strings enumerated by a module $M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ is at most $\varepsilon$.

</div>

*Proof.* By a straightforward induction on $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.10)</span></p>

Suppose that a module $M_R(a, n, \varepsilon)$ starts running at stage $s$ (with $\alpha_s \upharpoonright [a, c) = 0^{c-a}$, where $c$ is as above), and returns at stage $t$. Then $\beta_t \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_s \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant n$.

</div>

*Proof.* By induction on $n$. The base case $n = 1$ is easy: if $M_R(a, 1, \varepsilon)$ starts running at stage $s$ and $\alpha_s(a) = 0$, then the module changes $\alpha_s(a)$ to 1, and by the next expansionary stage we get a change in $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$, which implies that $\beta_t \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_s \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant 1$.

Now assume that the lemma is proved for some $n \geqslant 1$. Suppose we start running $M_R(a, n + 1, \varepsilon)$ at stage $s_a$ with $\alpha_{s_0} \upharpoonright [a, c) = 0^{c-a}$ (where $c = c(a, n + 1, \varepsilon)$). Calculate the associated $b$ and $\varepsilon'$. For $k = a + 1, \ldots, b + n + 1$, let $s_k$ be the stage at which the recursive call of $M_R(k, n, \varepsilon')$ returns. By induction, we have, for all such $k$,

$$\beta_{s_k} \upharpoonright k + 1 - \beta_{s_{k-1}} \upharpoonright k + 1 \geqslant n.$$

So at the end of step (2) of the module $M_R(a, n + 1, \varepsilon)$, say at a stage $t_0$, we have $\beta_{t_0} > \beta^*$, so $d_{a+1}(\beta_{s_a}, \beta_{t_0}) \geqslant n$; in other words, $\beta_{t_0} \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_{s_a} \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant n$. At step 3 of the module, we change $\alpha(a)$, and by the next expansionary stage, we have a change in $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$, which adds at least 1 to $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1$. So if the module returns at stage $t_1$, then

$$\beta_{t_1} \upharpoonright a + 1 - \beta_{s_a} \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant n + 1,$$

as required.

The construction is now clear: we partition $\mathbb{N}$ into disjoint intervals, all of the form $[a, c(a, 2^{a+1}, 2^{-n}))$. A requirement $R = R_{\Gamma, \beta}$ will be assigned infinitely many such intervals and run $M_R(a, 2^{a+1}, 2^{-n})$ on the $n$th interval assigned to it. By Lemma 9.14.10, none of these modules can return, because we cannot have $\beta \upharpoonright a + 1 \geqslant 2^{a+1}$. If the hypothesis $\Gamma^\beta = \alpha$ of $R$ holds, then the $R$-modules cannot ever be stuck waiting for expansionary stages. For such $R$, for every $n$ such that $W_R \upharpoonright F_n = D \upharpoonright F_n$, every request for permission by the $n$th module for $R$ gets stuck waiting for $\beta$ to leave the set of intervals $U$ that it enumerates. In other words, if the $n$th module for $R$ enumerates $U_n$, and $\Gamma^\beta = \alpha$, then $\beta \in U_n$, as required. If this is the case for all $n$, then $\beta$ is not 1-random.

Now we turn to the other direction of the theorem: If $\alpha$ has array computable Turing degree, then there is some 1-random left-c.e. real $\beta \geqslant_\text{ibT} \alpha$.

Again the idea is to use an approximation to $\alpha$ which does not change too much, and build $\beta$ by requesting that it change by at least $2^{-n}$ whenever $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ changes. On top of this, we need to ensure that $\beta$ is 1-random. To accomplish this task, let $U$ be the second element of a universal Martin-Löf test. If we ensure that $\beta \notin U$ then we will have ensured that $\beta$ is 1-random.

We identify $U$ with a prefix-free set of generators for $U$. Then $\sum_{\sigma \in U} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$. To ensure that $\beta$ is not in $U$, whenever we discover that there is some $\sigma \in U$ that is an initial segment of the current version of $\beta$, we request that this initial segment change by adding $2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ to $\beta$. If the approximation to $\alpha$ is sufficiently tight so that the total amount added to $\beta$ on behalf of following $\alpha$ is less than $\frac{1}{2}$, this strategy will succeed.

So fix a computable order $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-n} g(n) < \frac{1}{2}$ (we allow the value 0 for $g$ for finitely many inputs; we assume that if $g(n) = 0$ then $\alpha \upharpoonright n$ is simply given to us as a parameter in the construction).

Get a computable approximation $f_s(n)$ for the function $n \mapsto \alpha \upharpoonright n$ such that for all $n$, we have $\lvert\lbrace s : f_{s+1}(n) \neq f_s(n) \rbrace\rvert \leqslant g(n)$. The left-c.e. real $\beta$ is defined by recursion. We start with $\beta_0 = 0$ and let

$$\beta_{s+1} = \beta_s + \sum \lbrace 2^{-n} : f_{s+1}(n) \neq f_s(n) \rbrace + \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \sigma \in U_s \wedge \sigma \prec \beta_s \rbrace.$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.11)</span></p>

$\beta < 1$ (and so is well-defined as an element of $2^\omega$).

</div>

*Proof.* There are two kinds of contributions to $\beta$: following $\alpha$ and avoiding $U$. Now, $g$ satisfies $\sum_n 2^{-n} g(n) < \frac{1}{2}$ and for all $n$ we have $\lvert\lbrace s : f_{s+1}(n) \neq f_s(n) \rbrace\rvert \leqslant g(n)$, so the total added to $\beta$ by the clause $\sum \lbrace 2^{-n} : f_{s+1}(n) \neq f_s(n) \rbrace$ is less than $\frac{1}{2}$. Because $\beta$ is a left-c.e. real, for every $\sigma \in U$, there is at most one stage $s$ at which we have $\sigma \in U_s$ and $\sigma \prec \beta_s$, so the contribution of the clause $\sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \sigma \in U_s \wedge \sigma \prec \beta_s \rbrace$ is at most $\sum_{\sigma \in U} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.12)</span></p>

$\beta \notin U$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\beta \in U$ then there is some $\sigma \in U$ such that $\sigma \prec \beta$. Then at a late enough stage $s$ we have $\sigma \in U_s$ and $\sigma \prec \beta_s$, so at stage $s$ we add $2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ to $\beta$ and force $\sigma \not\prec \beta$ for ever.

Finally, the first clause in the definition of $\beta$ shows that $\beta \geqslant_\text{ibT} \alpha$, as in Lemma 9.14.5.

#### 9.14.4 cl-degrees of Versions of $\Omega$

We have seen that the Kučera-Slaman Theorem fails for cl-reducibility. One possibility for rescuing some of the content of that result in the cl case is that at least different versions of $\Omega$ might have the same cl-degree. As we will see in this subsection, this possibility also fails.

We begin with the following definition and a result about its connection to 1-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.13 — Fenner and Schaefer)</span></p>

A set $A$ is *$k$-immune* if there is no computable sequence $F_0, F_1, \ldots$ of pairwise disjoint sets, each of size at most $k$, such that $F_n \cap A \neq \emptyset$ for all $n$.

</div>

Note that every set is 0-immune, that the 1-immune sets are exactly the immune sets, and that hyperimmune sets are $k$-immune for all $k$. Fenner and Schaefer showed that the classes of $k$-immune sets form a proper hierarchy. We now show that for 1-random sets, the situation is different.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.14)</span></p>

Suppose that $\lvert B \rvert = m$, and let $\mathcal{B}$ be a collection of $n$ pairwise disjoint subsets of $B$, each of size $k$. Then there are exactly $2^{m - kn}(2^k - 1)^n$ subsets of $B$ that have nonempty intersection with every set in $\mathcal{B}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.15 — Folklore)</span></p>

Every 1-random (or even weakly 1-random) set is $k$-immune for all $k$, but no 1-random set is hyperimmune.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $F_0, F_1, \ldots$ be a computable collection of pairwise disjoint sets, each of size at most $k$. Let $\mathcal{Q}$ be the class of sets that have nonempty intersection with every $F_n$. Then $\mathcal{Q}$ is a $\Pi_1^0$ class. Let $I_n = [0, \max \bigcup_{i < n} F_i]$. By Lemma 9.14.14, for each $n$, the number of subsets of $I_n$ that intersect every $F_i$ for $i < n$ is bounded above by

$$2^{\lvert I_n \rvert - kn}(2^k - 1)^n 2^{-\lvert I_n \rvert} = 2^{-kn}(2^k - 1)^n = (1 - 2^{-k})^n,$$

which goes to 0 as $n$ increases, since $1 - 2^{-k} < 1$. Thus $\mathcal{Q}$ is a null $\Pi_1^0$ class. So if $A$ is 1-random then $A \notin \mathcal{Q}$, and hence $F_0, F_1, \ldots$ witnesses the $k$-immunity of $A$.

Now let $G_0, G_1, \ldots$ be a computable sequence of pairwise disjoint sets such that $\lvert G_n \rvert = 2^n$. Let $V_n$ be the class of sets $A$ such that $G_n \cap A = \emptyset$. Then $\mu(V_n) = 2^{-n}$. So if we let $U_n = \bigcup_{m > n} V_n$ then $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ is a Martin-Löf test, and $\bigcap_n U_n$ is the collection of sets $A$ for which there are infinitely many $n$ with $G_n \cap A = \emptyset$, and so contains every hyperimmune set.

We are now ready to show that not all 1-random left-c.e. reals have the same cl-degree.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.14.16 — Stephan)</span></p>

There exist 1-random left-c.e. reals of different cl-degrees.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\alpha$ be a 1-random left-c.e. real. Thinking of $\alpha$ as a subset of $\mathbb{N}$, we have that $\mathbb{N} \setminus \alpha$ is also 1-random, so by Theorem 9.14.15, there is a computable sequence $t_0 < t_1 < \cdots$ such that for each $n$, at least one bit of $\alpha \upharpoonright (t_n, t_{n+1}]$ is a 0, and the size of $(t_n, t_{n+1}]$ is increasing in $n$.

Let $\beta$ be the real such that $\beta(x) = 1$ iff $x = t_n$ for some $n > 0$ (in other words, $\beta = \sum_{n > 0} 2^{-t_n - 1}$). Since $\beta$ is computable, $\alpha + \beta$ is left-c.e. and 1-random.

We claim that $\alpha$ does not cl-compute $\alpha + \beta$. Suppose that it does. Then there is a $c$ and a partial computable function $\psi$ such that for all $n$,

$$\psi(\alpha \upharpoonright (n + c)) = (\alpha + \beta)(n).$$

For all $m$, the block $\alpha \upharpoonright (t_m, t_{m+1}]$ is not a string of 1's, so adding $2^{-t_m - 1}$ to $\alpha$ does not create a carry before the $t_m$th bit of $\alpha$. Thus, for every $n > 0$,

$$\alpha \upharpoonright (t_n + 1) + \beta \upharpoonright (t_n + 1) = (\alpha + \beta) \upharpoonright (t_n + 1). \tag{9.5}$$

By Theorem 9.14.15, $\alpha$ is not $c$-immune, so there are infinitely many $n$ such that $\alpha \upharpoonright [t_n - c, t_n) = 1^c$ and $t_{n-1} < t_n - c$. By (9.5), for each such $n$,

$$\psi(\alpha \upharpoonright t_n) = (\alpha + \beta)(t_n - c) = 1 - \alpha(t_n).$$

We can now define a c.e. supermartingale $M$ that succeeds on $\alpha$, which is a contradiction: Begin with $M(\lambda) = 1$. If there is an $n$ such that $\lvert\sigma\rvert = t_n$ and $\sigma \upharpoonright [t_n - c, t_n) = 1^c$, where $t_{n-1} < t_n - c$, then wait for $\psi(\sigma)$ to converge. If that never happens then $M(\sigma i) = 0$ for $i = 0, 1$. Otherwise, let $i = 1 - \psi(\sigma)$ and let $M(\sigma i) = 2M(\sigma)$ and $M(\sigma(1-i)) = 0$. For all other $\sigma$, let $M(\sigma i) = M(\sigma)$ for $i = 0, 1$.

### 9.15 $K$-degrees, $C$-degrees, and Turing Degrees

Merkle and Stephan have given simple and short but clever arguments about the structure of the $C$- and $K$-degrees, and their relationships with the Turing degrees, by using sparse sets. We will see other relationships between these measures of relative randomness and Turing degrees for *random* sets in Chapter 10.

We begin with the following result, which we state in relativized form for future reference, and which will be important in Chapter 11.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.1 — Merkle and Stephan, after Chaitin)</span></p>

If $K^X(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K^X(n) + O(1)$ for all $n$ in an infinite set $S$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} S \oplus X'$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let

$$T = \lbrace \sigma : \forall \tau \preccurlyeq \sigma\, (\lvert\tau\rvert \in S \Rightarrow K^X(\tau) \leqslant K^X(\lvert\tau\rvert) + c) \rbrace.$$

Then $T$ is an $(S \oplus X')$-computable tree, $A \in [T]$, and, by the relativized form of the Counting Theorem 3.7.6, $T$ has bounded width. Thus $A$ is an isolated path of $T$, and hence is $(S \oplus X')$-computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.2 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

Let $Y \subseteq \lbrace 2^n : n \in \omega \rbrace$ and $X \leqslant_K Y$. Then $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y \oplus \emptyset'$. Hence, if $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} Y$ then $X \leqslant_K Y$ implies $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y$.

</div>

*Proof.* The idea is to code $Y$ by a certain sparse set of numbers. Let

$$g(n) = 2^n + \sum_{k < n} 2^k Y(2^{k+2}).$$

Then $g(n) \in [2^n, 2^{(n+1)} - 1]$ and we can compute $Y \upharpoonright 2^{(n+1)}$ from $g(n)$. Thus $K(Y \upharpoonright g(n)) \leqslant K(g(n)) + O(1)$, so $K(X \upharpoonright g(n)) \leqslant K(g(n)) + O(1)$. By Lemma 9.15.1, $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y \oplus \emptyset'$.

A similar proof establishes the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.3 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

If $Y \subseteq \lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in \omega \rbrace$ and $X \leqslant_C Y$ then $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.4 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

There is a minimal pair of $C$-degrees of computably enumerable sets.

</div>

*Proof.* Choose $A, B \subseteq \lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in \omega \rbrace$ whose Turing degrees form a minimal pair and apply Theorem 9.15.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.5 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

A $C$-degree contains at most one Turing degree.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A \equiv_C B$. Let $\widehat{A} = \lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in A \rbrace$ and $\widehat{B} = \lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in B \rbrace$. Then $\widehat{A} \equiv_C \widehat{B}$, so by Theorem 9.15.3, $\widehat{A} \equiv_\text{T} \widehat{B}$, and hence $A \equiv_\text{T} B$.

Not all $C$-degrees can contain entire Turing degrees. Recall that $A$ is complex if there is a computable order $h$ with $C(A \upharpoonright h(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$ (or, equivalently, a computable order $g$ with $K(A \upharpoonright g(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.6 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

If $A$ is complex then neither the $C$-degree nor the $K$-degree of $A$ contains an entire Turing degree.

</div>

*Proof.* We do the proof for $C$, the $K$ case being essentially the same. Fix $h$ as above. Let $B \equiv_C A$. Let $D = \lbrace h(4n) : n \in B \rbrace$. Then $D \equiv_\text{T} B$, but $D \not\equiv_C A$, since $C(A \upharpoonright h(4n)) \geqslant 4n$, while $C(D \upharpoonright h(4n)) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1) \leqslant n + O(1)$.

For the $K$-degrees, we can apply similar methods to get minimal pairs.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.7 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

Suppose that $Y$ is not $K$-trivial and $Y \leqslant_K X$. Then there is a function $f \leqslant_\text{T} Y \oplus \emptyset'$ such that, for each $n$, the set $X$ has at least one element between $n$ and $f(n)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.8 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

Suppose that $A$ and $B$ are $\emptyset'$-hyperimmune sets, forming a Turing minimal pair relative to $\emptyset'$. Then the $K$-degrees of $A$ and $B$ form a minimal pair. Hence there are minimal pairs of $K$-degrees of $\Sigma_2^0$ sets.

</div>

*Proof.* Without loss of generality we may assume that $A, B \subseteq \lbrace 2^n : n \in \omega \rbrace$. Let $Y \leqslant_K A, B$. By Theorem 9.15.2, $Y \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset', B \oplus \emptyset'$. Thus $Y \leqslant_\text{T} \emptyset'$, as $A$ and $B$ form a Turing minimal pair above $\emptyset'$. But by Lemma 9.15.7, if $Y$ is not $K$-trivial, then $A$ and $B$ are not hyperimmune relative to $\emptyset'$, and the result follows.

Using similar techniques, Merkle and Stephan have constructed nontrivial branching degrees in the $C$- and $K$-degrees, where a degree is *branching* if it is the infimum of two incomparable degrees.

Theorem 9.15.8 improves a basic result of Csima and Montalbán, who were the first to construct a minimal pair of $K$-degrees. Their method is interesting and informative in its own right, and will be dealt with in Section 11.12.

We finish the section with the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.15.9 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

There is a minimal $C$-degree.

</div>

*Proof.* We use perfect trees whose branches are subsets of $\lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in \omega \rbrace$. Let $T_0$ be the computable tree of branches of this form. Having defined $T_e$, let $T_{e+1}$ be a perfect computable subtree of $T_e$ so that one of the following holds:

1. $\Phi_e^A$ is partial for all branches $A$ of $T_{e+1}$; or
2. $\Phi_e^A$ is computable for all branches $A$ of $T_{e+1}$; or
3. $\Phi_e^A$ is total for all branches $A$ of $T_{e+1}$ and for any two distinct branches $A$ and $B$ of $T_{e+1}$, if $\sigma$ is their longest common initial segment, then there is an $x$ such that $\Phi_e^A(x) \neq \Phi_e^B(x)$, and any branch $C$ of $T_{e+1}$ that extends $\sigma$ coincides with either $A$ or $B$ up to the max of the uses of $\Phi_e^A(x)$ and $\Phi_e^B(x)$.

The usual arguments show that it is always possible to construct $T_{e+1}$, and that if $A \in \bigcap_e T_e$ then $A$ is noncomputable and has minimal Turing degree. Let $B \leqslant_C A$ be noncomputable. Since $A \subset \lbrace 2^{2^n} : n \in \omega \rbrace$, by Theorem 9.15.3, $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$. Let $e$ be such that $\Phi_e^A = B$. Then case 3 above must hold of $T_{e+1}$. Given $n$, suppose that $C$ and $D$ are branches of $T_{e+1}$ such that $\Phi_e^C(m) = B(m)$ and $\Phi_e^D(m) = B(m)$ whenever the relevant uses are less than $n$, but $C \upharpoonright n \neq A \upharpoonright n$ and $D \upharpoonright n \neq A \upharpoonright n$. Let $\sigma$ be the longest common substring of $C$ and $D$. By the choice of $C$, $D$ also extends $\sigma$, and $A \upharpoonright n$ is one of at most two strings of length $n$ on $T_{e+1}$ compatible with $B \upharpoonright n$, whence $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

Using more intricate methods, it is possible to prove that every c.e. $C$-degree bounds a minimal $C$-degree and a minimal rK-degree. It is unknown whether there is a minimal $K$-degree.

### 9.16 The Structure of the Monotone Degrees

In this section we discuss results of Calhoun on the structure of monotone degrees. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be our fixed universal monotone machine. We begin with a technical lemma about the behavior of $Km$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.1 — Calhoun)</span></p>

Let $s(\sigma, \tau)$ be the shortest initial segment of $\sigma$ that is incomparable with $\tau$ if $\sigma \mid \tau$, and let $s(\sigma, \tau) = \lambda$ otherwise.

(i) $K(s(\sigma, \tau)) \leqslant Km(\sigma) + Km(\tau) + 2 \log Km(\sigma) + 2 \log Km(\tau) + O(1)$.

(ii) $K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) \leqslant 3 \max \lbrace Km(\sigma 0), Km(\sigma 1) \rbrace + O(1)$.

(iii) $Km(\tau) \leqslant Km(\sigma \tau) + K(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + O(1)$.

(iv) For every $\sigma$ there is a $c$ such that $Km(\tau) \leqslant Km(\sigma \tau) + c$ for all $\tau$.

(v) For each computable set $B$ there is a $c$ such that $Km(\sigma {}^\frown (B \upharpoonright n)) \leqslant K(\sigma) + c$ for all $\sigma$ and $n$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.2 — Calhoun)</span></p>

If $A$ is neither 1-random nor computable, then there is a $B$ such that $A \mid_{Km} B$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $X$ be 1-random. We build $B$ in stages by a finite extension argument.

For even $s$, let $B_{s+1} = B_s 0^k$ for some $k$ such that $Km(B_s 0^k) < Km(A \upharpoonright (\lvert B_s \rvert + k)) - s$. Such a $k$ exists because $Km(A \upharpoonright n)$ is unbounded, while $Km(B_s 0^\omega)$ is computable.

For odd $s$, let $B_{s+1} = B_s {}^\frown (X \upharpoonright k)$ for some $k$ such that $Km(B_s {}^\frown (X \upharpoonright k)) > Km(A \upharpoonright (\lvert B_s \rvert + k)) + s$. Such a $k$ exists because $B_s X$ is 1-random, while $A$ is not 1-random, and hence $Km(B_s {}^\frown (X \upharpoonright n)) = n \pm O(1)$ is bounded away from $n$.

The even stages ensure that $Km(A \upharpoonright n) \not\leqslant Km(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$ and the odd stages ensure that $Km(B \upharpoonright n) \not\leqslant Km(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

It is straightforward to extend the proof of Theorem 9.16.2 to construct a perfect tree such that any two paths have incomparable $Km$-degrees, yielding the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.3 — Calhoun)</span></p>

There is an antichain of $Km$-degrees of size $2^{\aleph_0}$.

</div>

Since $A \leqslant_{Km_D} B$ implies $A \leqslant_{Km} B$, the two previous theorems hold also of the discrete monotone degrees.

Calhoun proved some further structural results about the monotone degrees, such as the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.4 — Calhoun)</span></p>

There is a minimal pair of $Km$-degrees.

</div>

*Proof.* We construct $A$ and $B$ in stages. Let $A_0 = B_0 = \lambda$ and $n_0 = 0$.

At an even stage $s$, proceed as follows. Let $c_1$ be the constant from Lemma 9.16.1 (ii), and let $c_2$ be the constant from Lemma 9.16.1(v) applied to $0^\omega$. Let $A_s^*$ be an extension of $A_s$ with $Km(A_s^*) > Km(A_s)$. A string $\sigma$ is *terminal* if there is no proper extension $\tau$ of $\sigma$ with $Km(\tau) = Km(\sigma)$. Let $n$ be large enough so that the following hold:

(i) $n \geqslant \lvert A_s^* \rvert$.

(ii) There is no terminal $\sigma$ with $\lvert\sigma\rvert > n$ and $Km(\sigma) < Km(A_s^*) + c_2 + s$.

(iii) For all $m \geqslant n$, we have $K(m) \geqslant 3(K(A_s^*) + c_1 + c_2 + s)$, where $c_1$ is the constant from Lemma 9.16.1 (ii).

Let $B_{s+1} = B_s {}^\frown 0^{n - \lvert B_s \rvert}$ and $A_{s+1} = A_s^* {}^\frown 0^{n - \lvert A_s^* \rvert}$, and let $n_{s+1} = n$.

At an odd stage we do the same, but switching the roles of $A$ and $B$.

To see that $A$ and $B$ form a minimal pair, suppose $X \leqslant_{Km} A, B$ is not computable. We say $s$ is an *increment stage* if $Km(X \upharpoonright n_{s+1}) > Km(X \upharpoonright n_s)$. There must be infinitely many increment stages, so suppose there are infinitely many even increment stages, the odd case being symmetric. For each even increment stage $s$, let $x$ be such that $n_s < x \leqslant n_{s+1}$ and $Km(X \upharpoonright x - 1) < Km(X \upharpoonright x)$. As $x > n_s$, if $X \upharpoonright x - 1$ is terminal, then $Km(X \upharpoonright x - 1) \geqslant K(A_s^*) + c_2 + s$ by condition (ii). If $X \upharpoonright x - 1$ is not terminal, then

$$Km(X \upharpoonright x) = \max \lbrace Km((X \upharpoonright x - 1) {}^\frown 0), Km((X \upharpoonright x - 1) {}^\frown 1) \rbrace \geqslant \frac{K(x)}{3} - c_1,$$

by Lemma 9.16.1 (ii). By condition (iii), $K(x) \geqslant 3(K(A_s^*) + c_1 + c_2 + s)$. Thus, in either case, $Km(X \upharpoonright x) \geqslant K(A_s^*) + c_2 + s$. By Lemma 9.16.1(v),

$$Km(A \upharpoonright n_{s+1}) = Km(A_s^* {}^\frown 0^{x - \lvert A_s^* \rvert}) \leqslant K(A_s^*) + c_2.$$

Since $s$ is unbounded and $X \leqslant_{Km} A$, we have a contradiction.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.5 — Calhoun)</span></p>

If $f$ is strictly increasing and computable, then $Km((A \otimes f) \upharpoonright n) = Km(A \upharpoonright f^{-1}[n]) \pm O(1)$. Hence, if $Km(A \upharpoonright n) = Km(B \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1)$ then $Km((A \otimes f) \upharpoonright n) = Km((B \otimes f) \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

Here $A \otimes f(n)$ is $A(f^{-1}(n))$ if $n$ is in the range of $f$, and 0 otherwise. Similarly, $\sigma \otimes f$ is $\sigma(f^{-1}(n))$ if $n$ is in the range of $f$ and $f^{-1}(n) < \lvert\sigma\rvert$, and is 0 for all other $n < f(\lvert\sigma\rvert)$. Also $f^{-1}[n] = \max \lbrace k : f(k) \leqslant n \rbrace$.

Calhoun also showed how to construct many uncountable $Km$-degrees. For a set $A$ and a strictly increasing function $f$, let $A \otimes f(n)$ be $A(f^{-1}(n))$ if $n$ is in the range of $f$, and 0 otherwise.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.16.6 — Calhoun)</span></p>

There is an order-preserving embedding from the rationals into the monotone degrees such that each degree in the image of the embedding has cardinality $2^{\aleph_0}$.

</div>

*Proof.* For any rational number $r \in (0, 1)$, let $f_r(n) = \lfloor \frac{n}{r} \rfloor$. Let $A$ be 1-random. By Theorem 9.16.5, $Km(A \otimes f_r \upharpoonright n) = Km(A \upharpoonright f_r^{-1}[n]) \pm O(1) = f_r^{-1}[n] \pm O(1) = rn \pm O(1)$. Then the map $r \mapsto \text{deg}_{KM}(A \otimes f_r)$ induces the desired embedding: If $r < s$ the $\lim_n sn - rn = \infty$, so $A \otimes f_r <_{Km} A \otimes f_s$. Furthermore, if $A \neq B$ are 1-random, then $A \otimes f_r \equiv_{Km} B \otimes f_r$ but $A \otimes f_r \neq B \otimes f_r$. Since there are continuum many 1-randoms, each degree in our map has size continuum.

### 9.17 Schnorr Reducibility

One can define measures of relative randomness tailored to notions other than 1-randomness. We give an example in this section. Given the machine characterization of Schnorr randomness from Section 7.1.3, we can define a reducibility analogous to $K$-reducibility for Schnorr randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.17.1 — Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

A set $A$ is *Schnorr reducible* to a set $B$ (written $A \leqslant_\text{Sch} B$) if for each computable measure machine $M$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $K_N(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_M(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

</div>

Clearly, if $A$ is Schnorr random and $A \leqslant_\text{Sch} B$, then $B$ is Schnorr random. We prove two more facts about this reducibility. The first shows that it is not implied by $K$-reducibility, or even by cl-reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.17.2 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There are c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$ but $A \not\leqslant_\text{Sch} B$.

</div>

*Proof.* By Proposition 7.1.19, we can restrict our attention to machines $M$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom } M \rrbracket) = 1$. Let $M_0, M_1, \ldots$ be an effective list of all prefix-free machines. We build a computable measure machine $M$ and c.e. sets $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$ to satisfy the requirements

$$\mathcal{R}_e : \mu(\llbracket \text{dom } M_e \rrbracket) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \exists n \, (K_M(B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_{M_e}(A \upharpoonright n) - e).$$

We build $M$ indirectly, by enumerating a KC set $L$.

For each $\mathcal{R}_e$ we set aside a block $[n_e, n_e + 3e + 1]$, with $n_{e+1} > n_e + 3e + 1$. We will never allow $n_e + j$ for $0 < j \leqslant 3e + 1$ to enter $B$, but we may possibly put $n_e$ into $B$. Thus there are $2^{e+1}$ many possible values for $B \upharpoonright (n_e + 3e + 2)$. For each $e$, we enumerate a request $(2e + 2, \tau)$ for each of these possible values $\tau$. These are all the requests ever put into $L$, so the weight of $L$ is equal to $\sum_e 2^{e+1} 2^{-(2e+2)} = \sum_e 2^{-(e+1)} = 1$. Thus there is a computable measure machine $M$ such that $K_M(B \upharpoonright (n_e + 3e + 2)) = 2e + 2$ for all $e$. So it is enough to ensure that if $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom } M_e \rrbracket) = 1$ then $K_{M_e}(A \upharpoonright (n_e + 3e + 2)) \geqslant 3e + 2$, regardless of the other values of $A$.

There are fewer than $2^{3e+2}$ many $M_e$-programs of length less than $3e + 2$, so there must be a string $\tau \in 2^{3e+2}$ such that if $A(n_e) \ldots A(n_e + 3e + 1) = \tau$, then $K_{M_e}(A \upharpoonright (n_e + 3e + 2)) \geqslant 3e + 2$, regardless of the other values of $A$. So for each $e$ we wait for a stage $s$ such that $1 - \mu(\llbracket \text{dom } M_e[s] \rrbracket) < 2^{-3e+1}$. At that stage, we know $M_e(\mu)$ for all $M_e$-programs $\mu$ of length less than $3e + 2$, and hence can determine a $\tau$ as above. We put numbers into $A$ to ensure that $A(n_e) \ldots A(n_e + 3e + 1) = \tau$, and put $n_e$ into $B$. The latter action ensures that $A \leqslant_\text{cl} B$.

It turns out that tt-reducibility is related to Schnorr reducibility much as wtt-reducibility is to $K$-reducibility. This is not a surprising fact, since the essential difference between a tt-reduction and a wtt-reduction is that the former has a computable domain, which is exactly what distinguishes a computable measure machine from an ordinary prefix-free machine. To obtain a version of tt-reducibility that implies Schnorr reducibility, we need to restrict the use in the same way that we did to obtain cl-reducibility from wtt-reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.17.3 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

A set $A$ is *strongly truth table reducible* to a set $B$ (written $A \leqslant_\text{stt} B$) if $A \leqslant_\text{tt} B$ via a truth table reduction $\Gamma$ with use $\gamma(n) \leqslant n + O(1)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(9.17.4 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A \leqslant_\text{stt} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{Sch} B$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A \leqslant_\text{stt} B$ via a reduction $\Gamma$ with use $\gamma(n) \leqslant n + c$. Let $\gamma_0, \ldots, \gamma_{2^c - 1}$ be the strings of length $c$. Let $M$ be a computable measure machine. Build a KC set $L$ as follows. For every $\sigma$ and $\tau$ such that $M(\sigma) \!\downarrow\! = \tau$ and every $j < 2^c$, enumerate a request $(\lvert\sigma\rvert + c, \Gamma^{\tau \gamma_j} \upharpoonright \lvert\tau\rvert)$. (Since $\Gamma$ is a tt-reduction, $\Gamma^{\tau \gamma_j} \upharpoonright \lvert\tau\rvert$ is always defined.) For each $\sigma \in \text{dom } M$, we add $2^c 2^{-(\lvert\sigma\rvert + c)} = 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$ to the weight of $L$, so this weight is equal to $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom } M \rrbracket)$. Thus $L$ is a KC set with computable weight, and hence there is a corresponding computable measure machine $N$ given by the KC Theorem. If $M(\sigma) \!\downarrow\! = B \upharpoonright n$ then there is a string $\mu$ of length $\lvert\sigma\rvert + c$ such that $N(\mu) \!\downarrow\! = \Gamma^{B \upharpoonright (n+c)} \upharpoonright n = A \upharpoonright n$, so $K_N(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_M(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$.

## Chapter 10: Complexity and Relative Randomness for 1-Random Sets

In this chapter, we examine basic questions about the function $K$, as well as related questions about the orderings $\leqslant_C$ and $\leqslant_K$, and other natural measures of relative complexity. We also introduce some other natural measures of relative randomness. These measures, which include *low for 1-randomness reducibility* $\leqslant_\text{LR}$ and *van Lambalgen reducibility* $\leqslant_\text{vL}$, are at first sight not directly related to initial segment complexity, but can be used to naturally relate initial segment complexity to Turing reducibility and other measures of relative computational complexity. Most of the material in this chapter concerns relative initial segment complexity and relative randomness within the class of 1-random sets, where some of the stronger reducibilities considered in the previous chapter do not seem to give us much information.

### 10.1 Uncountable Lower Cones in $K$-reducibility and $C$-reducibility

One property that typical computability-theoretic reducibilities share is that, for any given set $A$, there are only countably many sets reducible to $A$. This *countable predecessor property* does not hold of $\leqslant_K$ and $\leqslant_C$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.1.1 — Yu, Ding, and Downey)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then there are $2^{\aleph_0}$ many sets $K$-reducible to $A$, and indeed there are sets of every m-degree $K$-reducible to $A$.

</div>

*Proof.* For a set $B$, let $\widehat{B} = \lbrace 2^m : m \in B \rbrace$. Then $K(\widehat{B} \upharpoonright \log n) \leqslant O(\log \log n) \leqslant O(\log n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$, so $\widehat{B}$ is $K$-reducible to $A$. But the map $B \mapsto \widehat{B}$ is 1-1 and m-degree preserving (except when $B = \mathbb{N}$), so the result follows.

Essentially the same proof shows the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.1.2 — Yu, Ding, and Downey)</span></p>

If $A$ is 1-random then there are $2^{\aleph_0}$ many sets $C$-reducible to $A$, and indeed there are sets of every m-degree $C$-reducible to $A$.

</div>

Perhaps strangely, it was rather difficult to construct an uncountable $K$-degree. One consequence of this result is that it is not obvious that there are continuum many $K$-degrees, since the usual argument (every degree is countable but there are continuum many sets) does not apply. However, while the cardinality of sets $K$-reducible to a given set $A$ might be large, the *measure* of the class of sets $K$-reducible to $A$ is always 0, as shown by Yu, Ding, and Downey and proved below as Theorem 10.3.13. This result is enough to conclude that there are uncountably many $K$-degrees, and in fact enough to prove the result of Yu and Ding that there are continuum many $K$-degrees, as we will discuss in Section 10.3.3.

Another route to this result is through Theorem 10.5.12 below that 1-random $K$-degrees are countable. Since there are continuum many 1-random sets, this result implies that there are continuum many $K$-degrees of 1-random sets.

### 10.2 The $K$-complexity of $\Omega$ and Other 1-random Sets

#### 10.2.1 $K(A \upharpoonright n)$ versus $K(n)$ for 1-random Sets $A$

We have seen a number of results, particularly those of Miller and Yu in Section 6.6, establishing relatively precise bounds on the behavior of $K(A \upharpoonright n)$ for a 1-random set $A$. We now present results of Solovay on the relationship between $K(n)$ and $K(A \upharpoonright n)$ for 1-random sets $A$.

Recall Corollary 6.6.3, which states that if $A$ is 1-random and $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \infty$, then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > n + f(n) - O(1)$. As mentioned in Section 6.6, Solovay had earlier proved this result for computable $f$. Note that, since $K(A \upharpoonright m) \leqslant m + K(m) + O(1)$ for all $m$, the $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) > n + f(n) - O(1)$ must be fairly complex, in the sense that $K(n) \geqslant f(n) - O(1)$ for all such $n$. Solovay showed that, for computable $f$, there must also be such complex $n$ for which the complexity of $A \upharpoonright n$ is not too high.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.1 — Solovay)</span></p>

Let $A$ be 1-random. Let $f$ be a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \infty$, and let $h$ be a computable order. Then there exist infinitely many $n$ such that $K(n) \geqslant f(n) - O(1)$ and $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + h(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\widehat{f}(n) = \min \lbrace f(n), 2 \log n \rbrace$. Since $\sum_{f(n) \geqslant 2 \log n} 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, we have $\sum_n 2^{-\widehat{f}(n)} = \infty$. It is enough to prove the theorem with $\widehat{f}$ in place of $f$, since then the fact that $K(n) < 2 \log n$ for almost all $n$ implies that $\widehat{f}(n) = f(n)$ for almost all of the infinitely many $n$ mentioned in the theorem.

We claim there is a computable function $g \geqslant \widehat{f}$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} = \infty$ and $K(n) \leqslant g(n) + h(n) + O(1)$. Assuming the claim for now, we argue as follows. Since $g \geqslant \widehat{f}$, it is enough to prove the theorem for $g$ in place of $f$. By Theorem 3.11.4, $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + K(n) - g(n) + O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$. For each such $n$,

$$K(n) \geqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n + g(n) - O(1) \geqslant g(n) - O(1),$$

since $A$ is 1-random, and

$$K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + K(n) - g(n) + O(1) \leqslant n + h(n) + O(1).$$

The construction of $g$ proceeds by defining it in stages so that for each $n$, we have $g(n)$ equal to either $\widehat{f}(n)$ or $2 \log n$. We define $g(n)$ for $n < m_i$ for some $m_i$. Let $m_{i+1}$ be least such that

$$1 \leqslant \sum_{\substack{m_i \leqslant n < m_{i+1} \\ h(n) \geqslant i}} 2^{-\widehat{f}(n)} \leqslant 2,$$

which exists because $\sum_n 2^{-\widehat{f}(n)} = \infty$ and $\lim_n h(n) = \infty$. For $n \in [m_i, m_{i+1})$, let $g(n) = 2 \log n$ if $h(n) < i$ and $g(n) = \widehat{f}(n)$ otherwise. Then

$$\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} \geqslant \sum_i \sum_{\substack{m_i \leqslant n < m_{i+1} \\ h(n) \geqslant i}} 2^{-\widehat{f}(n)} \geqslant \sum_i 1 = \infty$$

and

$$\sum_n 2^{-g(n) - h(n)} \leqslant \sum_i \left( \sum_{\substack{m_i \leqslant n < m_{i+1} \\ h(n) \geqslant i}} 2^{-\widehat{f}(n) - h(n)} + \sum_{\substack{m_i \leqslant n < m_{i+1} \\ h(n) < i}} 2^{-2 \log n - h(n)} \right) \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-i} + \sum_n 2^{-2 \log n} < \infty.$$

#### 10.2.2 The Rate of Convergence of $\Omega$ and the $\alpha$ Function

The following definition provides a kind of "monotone approximation" to $K$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.2 — Chaitin)</span></p>

Let $\alpha(n) = \min \lbrace K(m) : m \geqslant n \rbrace$.

</div>

Note that if $n > m$ then $\alpha(n) \geqslant \alpha(m)$. We can similarly define $\alpha^X$ for any set $X$ using $K^X$. The function $\alpha$ grows slower than any computable order.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.3 — Chaitin)</span></p>

If $g$ is a computable order then $\alpha(n) < g(n)$ for almost all $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $h(k)$ be the least $n$ such that $g(n) > k$. Then $K(h(k)) \leqslant K(k) + O(1) \leqslant O(\log k)$. Since $h(g(n)) > n$ for all $n$, we have $\alpha(n) \leqslant K(h(g(n))) \leqslant O(\log g(n))$, which means that $\alpha(n) < g(n)$ for all sufficiently large $n$.

We now consider the rate of convergence of $\Omega$ as a series, in the version defined as $\sum_n 2^{-K(n)}$. Let

$$s(n) = -\log \sum_{j \geqslant n} 2^{-K(j)}.$$

Notice that $s(n)$ goes to infinity with $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.4 — Solovay)</span></p>

$\alpha(n) - O(\log \alpha(n)) \leqslant s(n) \leqslant \alpha(n)$.

</div>

*Proof.* By definition, there is an $m \geqslant n$ such that $K(m) = \alpha(n)$, so $\sum_{j \geqslant n} 2^{-K(j)} \geqslant 2^{-K(m)} = 2^{-\alpha(n)}$. Taking logs, we see that $s(n) \leqslant \alpha(n)$.

For the other inequality, we will show how to compute from $\Omega \upharpoonright n$ an integer $t_n$ so that

(i) $K(t_n \mid \Omega \upharpoonright n) = O(1)$ and

(ii) $s(t_n) > n$.

Assuming we have such integers, let $k = s(n)$. Then $s(t_k) > s(n)$, so $t_k > n$. Together with (i), this fact implies that

$$\alpha(n) \leqslant K(t_k) \leqslant K(\Omega \upharpoonright k) + O(1) \leqslant k + K(k) + O(1) = s(n) + O(\log s(n)),$$

giving the desired inequality.

We finish by defining integers $t_n$ satisfying (i) and (ii). Our result uses Theorem 3.13.2, which we now recall. Let

$$p(n) = \lvert (\lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\downarrow\! \rbrace) \rvert.$$

Then Theorem 3.13.2 states that $p(n) \sim 2^{n - K(n)}$.

As usual, let $\Omega_s = \sum_{\mathcal{U}(\sigma)[s]} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert}$, where we may assume that at each stage $s$ exactly one new string $\sigma_s$ enters the domain of $\mathcal{U}$. Given $\Omega \upharpoonright n$, compute the least $k_n$ such that $\Omega_{k_n} \upharpoonright n = \Omega \upharpoonright n$. Let $t_n'$ be the least integer greater than $\lvert\sigma_i\rvert$ for all $i \leqslant k_n$. Then there is a $c$ such that

$$2^{-n} > \Omega - \Omega \upharpoonright n \geqslant \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} : \lvert\sigma\rvert \geqslant t_n' \wedge \mathcal{U}(\sigma) \!\downarrow\! \rbrace = \sum_{j \geqslant t_n'} 2^{-j} p(j) \geqslant c \sum_{j \geqslant t_n'} 2^{-j} 2^{j - K(j)} = c \cdot 2^{-s(t_n')}.$$

Taking logs, we see that $s(t_n') \geqslant n + O(1)$. Thus, for a suitable choice of $k$, if we let $t_n = t_{n+k}'$ then $s(t_n) > n$. Furthermore,

$$K(t_n \mid \Omega \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(t_n \mid \Omega \upharpoonright n + k) + K(\Omega \upharpoonright n + k \mid \Omega \upharpoonright n).$$

The first term is $O(1)$ by the explicit description of $t_n'$ from $\Omega \upharpoonright n$. The second is evidently $O(1)$ since $k$ is fixed independently of $n$.

#### 10.2.3 Comparing Initial Segment Complexities of 1-random Sets

The first person to study (implicitly) the $K$-degrees of 1-random sets was Solovay. He used the relativized version of the $\alpha$ function of the previous subsection to show that no 2-random set is $K$-reducible to $\Omega$. Of course, we already know that this is the case. Indeed, Theorem 15.6.5 (which we discussed in Section 6.11) implies that if $A$ is 2-random and $A \leqslant_K B$, then $B$ is 2-random. (We will extend this result to $n$-randomness in Corollary 10.3.11.) However, Solovay's methods are of independent interest, so we give his original proof here. We begin with the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.5 — Solovay)</span></p>

There are infinitely many $m$ such that $K(\Omega \upharpoonright m) \leqslant k + \alpha^{\emptyset'}(m) + O(\log \alpha^{\emptyset'}(m))$.

</div>

*Proof.* We work with $\alpha^\Omega$, which is equal to $\alpha^{\emptyset'}$ up to an additive constant. Let $\mathcal{U}$ be our fixed universal prefix-free oracle machine, and adopt the convention that if $\mathcal{U}^X(\tau) \!\downarrow\! = \rho$, then the use of this computation is at least $\lvert\rho\rvert$. Let $M$ be the prefix-free machine defined as follows. The machine $M$ tries to write its input as $\sigma \tau \nu$ such that $\mathcal{U}(\sigma) = \lvert\tau\rvert$ and $\mathcal{U}^\nu(\tau) \!\downarrow\!$ with use exactly $\lvert\nu\rvert$. If that is possible then $M$ outputs $\nu$.

Let $p_n$ be the least $i$ such that $\alpha^\Omega(i) \geqslant n$. It is easy to see that $K^\Omega(p_n) \leqslant n + O(1)$. Let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}^\Omega$-program for $p_n$. Let $\sigma$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\tau$. Let $m_n$ be least such that $\mathcal{U}^{\Omega \upharpoonright m_n}(\tau) \!\downarrow\!$.

Then $M(\sigma \tau (\Omega \upharpoonright m_n)) \!\downarrow\! = \Omega \upharpoonright m_n$. We have $\lvert\tau\rvert = n + O(1)$, whence $\lvert\sigma\rvert \leqslant O(\log n)$. Furthermore, $m_n \geqslant p_n$, so $\alpha^\Omega(m_n) \geqslant n$. Thus

$$K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + \lvert\tau\rvert + \lvert\Omega \upharpoonright m_n\rvert + O(1) \leqslant m_n + n + O(\log n) \leqslant m_n + \alpha^\Omega(m_n) + O(\log \alpha^\Omega(m_n)).$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.6 — Solovay)</span></p>

$K^{\emptyset'}(n) \leqslant K(n) - \alpha(n) + O(\log \alpha(n))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $s(n) = -\log \sum_{j \geqslant n} 2^{-K(j)}$. By Theorem 10.2.4, there is a $c$ such that $s(n) \geqslant \alpha(n) - c \log \alpha(n)$. Let $m_k$ be the least $n$ such that $\alpha(n) = k$. (The sums below are taken over only those $k$ such that $m_k$ is defined.) Let

$$S = \lbrace (K(n) - \alpha(n) + (c + 2) \log \alpha(n), n) : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace.$$

Then $S$ is $\emptyset'$-computable. Furthermore,

$$\sum_n 2^{-K(n) + \alpha(n) - (c+2) \log \alpha(n)} = \sum_k \sum_{\alpha(n) = k} 2^{-K(n) + k - (c+2) \log k} = \sum_k 2^{k - (c+2) \log k} 2^{-s(m_k)} \leqslant \sum_k 2^{-2 \log k} < \infty.$$

Thus, by the KC Theorem relativized to $\emptyset'$, we have $K^{\emptyset'}(n) \leqslant K(n) - \alpha(n) + (c + 2) \log \alpha(n) + O(1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.7 — Solovay)</span></p>

If $X$ is 2-random then $K(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + \alpha(n) - O(\log \alpha(n))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $p_n$ be the position of $X \upharpoonright n$ in the length-lexicographic listing of $2^{<\omega}$. Note that $p_n \geqslant n$. If $X$ is 2-random then $K^{\emptyset'}(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. By Lemma 10.2.6, we have

$$K(X \upharpoonright n) = K(p_n) \geqslant K^{\emptyset'}(p_n) + \alpha(p_n) - O(\log \alpha(p_n)) \geqslant n + \alpha(n) - O(\log \alpha(n)),$$

the last inequality following by the monotonicity of $\alpha$.

We will return to the connections between 2-randomness and the $\alpha$ and $s$ functions at the end of Section 10.4.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.8 — Solovay)</span></p>

If $X$ is 2-random then $X \not\leqslant_K \Omega$.

</div>

There is no obvious way to extend Solovay's methods beyond 2-randomness. However, we will introduce methods below that allow us to show in Corollary 10.3.11 that indeed $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}} \!\mid_K\! \Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ (and, in fact, the $K$-degrees of $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}}$ and $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ have no upper bound) for all $m \neq n$.

We finish this subsection with the following result, which shows that $K(\Omega \upharpoonright n)$ is sometimes very close to $n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.9 — Solovay)</span></p>

There is an infinite sequence of pairs of numbers $m_n^0, m_n^1$ such that for $i = 0, 1$,

(i) $\alpha^{\emptyset'}(m_n^i) = n \pm O(1)$,

(ii) $K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n^i) \leqslant m_n^i + n + O(1)$,

(iii) $K(m_n^0) = \alpha(m_n^0) \pm O(1)$, and

(iv) $K(m_n^1) = \log m_n^1 + K(\log m_n^1) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* As in the proof of Theorem 10.2.5, we work with $\alpha^\Omega$ in place of $\alpha^{\emptyset'}$. We begin by building $m_0, m_1, \ldots$ satisfying (i) and (ii). That is, $\alpha^{\emptyset'}(m_n) = n \pm O(1)$ and $K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n) \leqslant m_n + n + O(1)$.

At a first pass, we could use the proof of Theorem 10.2.5. Let $p_n$ and $m_n$ be as in that proof. Then $\alpha^\Omega(m_n) \geqslant n$. But $K^\Omega(p_n) \leqslant n + O(1)$ and $m_n$ can be obtained $\Omega$-computably from $p_n$, so in fact $\alpha^\Omega(m_n) = n \pm O(1)$, as required. Furthermore, as shown in the proof of Theorem 10.2.5, $K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n) \leqslant m_n + n + O(\log n)$.

To remove the $O(\log n)$ term, we modify the machine $M$ from the proof of Theorem 10.2.5 to a new prefix-free machine $N$. The idea is to interlace the oracle and input information to eliminate the need to provide the length of the input as additional information. Thus $N$ attempts to write its input as $x_0 y_0 x_1 y_1 \ldots x_n y_n \sigma$ (where the $x_i$ are bits and $\sigma$ is a string) so that letting $\tau = x_0 \ldots x_n$ and $\nu = y_0 \ldots y_n \sigma$, we have $\mathcal{U}^\nu(\tau) \!\downarrow\!$. It is easy to check that $N$ is prefix-free and that, with $m_n$ as above and by a similar argument as before, $K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n) \leqslant m_n + n + O(1)$.

Now we obtain $m_n^0$ and $m_n^1$ from $m_n$. Let $d$ be such that for every $m$ there is an $m \in [n, 4n]$ with $K(m) \geqslant \log m + K(\log m) - d$. Let $m_n^0$ be the least number greater than or equal to $m_n$ such that $K(m_n^0) = \alpha(m_n^0) \pm O(1)$ and let $m_n^1$ be the least number greater than or equal to $m_n$ such that $K(m_n^1) \geqslant \log m_n^1 + K(\log m_n^1) - d$. Then (iii) and (iv) are satisfied.

By monotonicity, $\alpha^{\emptyset'}(m_n^i) \geqslant \alpha^{\emptyset'}(m_n) = n \pm O(1)$. But the $m_n^i$ can be obtained $\emptyset'$-computably from $m_n$, so

$$\alpha^{\emptyset'}(m_n^i) \leqslant K^{\emptyset'}(m_n^i) \leqslant K^{\emptyset'}(m_n) + O(1) \leqslant n + O(1).$$

Thus (i) is satisfied.

Finally, it is easy to check that each $m_n^i$ is computable from $\Omega \upharpoonright m_n$, so we can describe $\Omega \upharpoonright m_n^i$ in a prefix-free way by giving a prefix-free description of $\Omega \upharpoonright m_n$, and, since that description provides us with $m_n$ and $m_n^i$, a plain description of $\Omega \upharpoonright [m_n, m_n^i)$. Thus

$$K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n^i) \leqslant K(\Omega \upharpoonright m_n) + C(\Omega \upharpoonright [m_n, m_n^i)) + O(1) \leqslant m_n + n + m_n^i - m_n + O(1) = m_n^i + n + O(1),$$

so (ii) is satisfied.

#### 10.2.4 Limit Complexities and Relativized Complexities

There are echoes of Solovay's work in more recent results of Bienvenu, Muchnik, Shen, and Vereshchagin.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.10 — Vereshchagin)</span></p>

$C^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) = \limsup_n C(\sigma \mid n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\mathcal{V}$ be our fixed universal plain machine. Let $M(\tau, n) = \mathcal{V}^{\emptyset'}(\tau)[n]$ (where both the oracle and the universal machine are being approximated). If $n$ is large enough and $\tau$ is a minimal-length $\mathcal{V}^{\emptyset'}$-program for $\sigma$, then $M(\tau, n) = \sigma$, whence $C(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant \lvert\tau\rvert + O(1) = C^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) + O(1)$. Thus $\limsup_n C(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant C^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) + O(1)$.

For the other direction, let $k = \limsup_n C(\sigma \mid n) + 1$. Let $V_n = \lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma \mid n) < k \rbrace$. We have $\lvert V_n \rvert < 2^k$ for all $n$. Let $B = \lbrace \tau : \exists m \forall n \geqslant m\, (\lvert V_n \cup \lbrace \tau \rbrace \rvert < 2^k) \rbrace$. Then $B$ is $\emptyset'$-c.e. (uniformly in $k$), $\sigma \in B$, and $\lvert B \rvert < 2^k$. Since we can describe $\sigma$ from $\emptyset'$ by giving its position in the enumeration of $B$ as a string of length $k$, we have $C^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) \leqslant k + O(1) = \limsup_n C(\sigma \mid n) + O(1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.11 — Bienvenu, Muchnik, Shen, and Vereshchagin)</span></p>

$K^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) = \limsup_n K(\sigma \mid n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* The argument that $\limsup_n K(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant K^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) + O(1)$ is the same as in the previous proof, with $\mathcal{U}$ in place of $\mathcal{V}$, since then $M$ is prefix-free for any fixed $n$.

Let $(\sigma_0, m_0, k_0), (\sigma_1, m_1, k_1), \ldots$ be an effective enumeration of $2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}$. Let $K_0 = K$. Given $K_s$, let $J$ be the result of altering $K_s$ so that $J(\sigma_s \mid n) = \min \lbrace k_s, K(\sigma_s \mid n) \rbrace$ for all $n \geqslant m_s$, leaving all other values of $K_s$ unchanged. If $J$ is an information content measure, in the sense of Definition 3.7.7, then let $K_{s+1} = J$ and say that $k$ is good for $\sigma_s$. Otherwise let $K_{s+1} = K_s$.

Let $I(\sigma)$ be the least $k$ that is good for $\sigma$. Checking whether a given $J$ as above is an i.c.m. can be done $\emptyset'$-computably, so $I(\sigma)$ is $\emptyset'$-approximable from above. For each $s$, let $I_s(\sigma)$ be the least $k$ that has been verified to be good for $\sigma$ by stage $s$ (or $\infty$ if there has been none). It is then easy to verify by induction that $\sum_\sigma 2^{-I_s(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$ for all $s$, so that $\sum_\sigma 2^{-I(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$. Thus $I$ is an i.c.m. relative to $\emptyset'$, and hence $K^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) \leqslant I(\sigma) + O(1)$.

Let $k = \limsup_n K(\sigma \mid n)$, let $m$ be such that $K(\sigma \mid n) \leqslant k$ for all $n \geqslant m$, and let $s$ be such that $(\sigma_s, m_s, k_s) = (\sigma, m, k)$. Then the $J$ defined at stage $s$ of the above construction equals $K_s$, whence $k$ is good for $\sigma$. Thus $I(\sigma) \leqslant k$, and hence $K^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) \leqslant k + O(1) = \limsup_n K(\sigma \mid n) + O(1)$.

It is straightforward to translate the above proof into the language of discrete semimeasures. The *limit frequency* of a partial function $f$ is the function $q_f(k) = \lim\inf_n \frac{\lvert\lbrace i < n : f(i) = k \rbrace\rvert}{n}$. Using the translation of the construction above, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.12 — Muchnik)</span></p>

Every $\emptyset'$-c.e. discrete semimeasure is equal to $q_f$ for some total computable function $f$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.13 — Bienvenu, Muchnik, Shen, and Vereshchagin)</span></p>

For every partial computable function $f$, the function $q_f$ is bounded above by a $\emptyset'$-c.e. discrete semimeasure.

</div>

Using almost the same proof we can show the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.2.14 — Bienvenu, Muchnik, Shen, and Vereshchagin)</span></p>

$KM^{\emptyset'}(\sigma) = \limsup_n KM(\sigma \mid n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

Bienvenu, Muchnik, Shen, and Vereshchagin remarked that it is unknown whether the analogous result holds for monotone complexity $Km$.

### 10.3 Van Lambalgen Reducibility

In this section we introduce a measure of relative randomness that is interesting not only for its own sake, but also as a tool for the analysis of $K$- and $C$-reducibility and their interactions with Turing reducibility. The following definition is based on van Lambalgen's Theorem, which recall states that $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random iff $X$ is 1-random and $Y$ is 1-random relative to $X$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.1 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

We say that $X$ is *van Lambalgen reducible* to $Y$, or simply *vL-reducible* to $X$, and write $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$, if for all $Z$, if $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random, then $Y \oplus Z$ is 1-random.

</div>

As usual, we call the equivalence classes induced by this reducibility the van Lambalgen degrees (vL-degrees).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.2 — Nies)</span></p>

We say that $Y$ is *low for 1-randomness reducible* to $X$, or simply *LR-reducible* to $X$, and write $Y \leqslant_\text{LR} X$, if for all $Z$, if $Z$ is 1-random relative to $X$ then $Z$ is 1-random relative to $Y$.

</div>

By van Lambalgen's Theorem, if $X$ and $Y$ are both 1-random, then $Y \leqslant_\text{LR} X$ iff $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$. Notice that if $X$ is not 1-random, then $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$ for all $Y$, so vL-reducibility is interesting only on 1-random sets.

Another related reducibility is *extended monotone reducibility*, defined by writing $X \leqslant_{Km^\oplus} Y$ if $X \oplus Z \leqslant_{Km} Y \oplus Z$ for all $Z$. Since all 1-random sets have the same $Km$-degree, by Theorem 6.3.10, $\leqslant_{Km^\oplus}$ implies $\leqslant_\text{vL}$, but $\leqslant_{Km^\oplus}$ also makes sense on non-1-random sets.

The vL-degrees turn out to be a weak measure of degree of randomness. We will see in Corollaries 10.3.10 and 10.3.17 that both $\leqslant_K$ and $\leqslant_C$ refine $\leqslant_\text{vL}$. This is the key fact that allowed Miller and Yu to transfer facts about the vL-degrees to the K- and C-degrees. This method is useful because many basic properties of the vL-degrees are easy to prove from known results.

#### 10.3.1 Basic Properties of the van Lambalgen Degrees

In establishing the basic properties of the van Lambalgen degrees, we use van Lambalgen's Theorem, Kučera's Theorem 8.5.1 that every degree that computes $\emptyset'$ is 1-random, and Corollary 11.2.7, due to Kučera and Terwijn, that for every $X$ there is a $Y >_\text{T} X$ such that every set that is 1-random relative to $X$ is also 1-random relative to $Y$. Although we prove Corollary 11.2.7 only in Chapter 11, since the relevant techniques are more properly presented there, we use it here as a black box.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.3 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

(i) The least vL-degree is $\mathbf{0}_\text{vL} = \lbrace X : X \text{ is not 1-random} \rbrace$.

(ii) If $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$ and $X$ is $n$-random, then $Y$ is $n$-random.

(iii) If $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random, then $X$ and $Y$ have no upper bound in the vL-degrees, so there is no join in the vL-degrees.

(iv) If $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X$ and $Y$ is 1-random, then $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$.

(v) There are 1-random sets $X \equiv_\text{vL} Y$ such that $X <_\text{T} Y$.

(vi) If $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random, then $X \oplus Y <_\text{vL} X, Y$ and $X \mid_\text{vL} Y$.

(vii) There is no maximal vL-degree.

(viii) There is no minimal 1-random vL-degree.

(ix) The $\Sigma_1^0$ theory of $(R, \leqslant_\text{vL})$ is decidable, where $R$ denotes the collection of vL-degrees of 1-random sets.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) Immediate from the definition, since if $X$ is not 1-random then $X \oplus Z$ is not 1-random for any $Z$.

(ii) The $n = 1$ case follows by (i), so let $n > 1$. By Kučera's Theorem, there is a 1-random $Z \equiv_\text{T} \emptyset^{(n-1)}$. Then $X$ is 1-random relative to $Z$, so $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random. Thus, $Y \oplus Z$ is 1-random, so $Y$ is 1-random relative to $Z$, and hence $Y$ is $n$-random.

(iii) Assume for a contradiction that $X, Y \leqslant_\text{vL} Z$. Because $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Z$ and $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random, $Z \oplus Y$ is also 1-random. Therefore, $Y \oplus Z$ is 1-random. But $Y \leqslant_\text{vL} Z$, so $Z \oplus Z$ must also be 1-random, which is a contradiction.

(iv) Let $Z$ be such that $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random. Then $Z$ is 1-random relative to $X$, and hence $Z$ is 1-random relative to $Y$, which implies that $Y \oplus Z$ is 1-random. Thus $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$.

(v) Let $X = \Omega$. By Corollary 11.2.7, there is a $W >_\text{T} X$ such that every set that is 1-random relative to $X$ is also 1-random relative to $W$. By Kučera's Theorem, there is a 1-random $Y \equiv_\text{T} W$. Then $X <_\text{T} Y$. Furthermore, $Z \oplus X$ is 1-random iff $Z$ is 1-random relative to $X$ iff $Z$ is 1-random relative to $W$ iff $Z \oplus Y$ is 1-random. Thus $X \equiv_\text{vL} Y$.

(vi) By (iii), $X \mid_\text{vL} Y$. By (iv), $X \oplus Y \leqslant_\text{vL} X, Y$. Therefore, $X \oplus Y <_\text{vL} X, Y$.

(vii) If $X = X_1 \oplus X_2$ is 1-random, then $X <_\text{vL} X_1, X_2$ by (vi).

(viii) Let $X$ be 1-random and $Y$ be 1-random relative to $X$. Then $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random, so by (i) and (vi), $\emptyset <_\text{vL} X \oplus Y <_\text{vL} X$.

(ix) As in Lerman, it suffices to prove that every finite poset can be embedded into $(R, \leqslant_\text{vL})$. Suppose $\mathbb{P} = (P, \leqslant)$ and $P = \lbrace p_i \rbrace_{i < n}$. Pick a 1-random $X = \bigoplus_{i < n} X_i$. For any $k < n$, define $F(k) = \lbrace i : p_k \leqslant p_i \rbrace$ and let $Y_k = \bigoplus_{i \in F(k)} X_i$. Let $g : P \to R$ be defined by $g(p_k) = Y_k$. It suffices to prove that $p_j \leqslant p_k \Leftrightarrow Y_j \leqslant_\text{vL} Y_k$. If $p_j \leqslant p_k$, then $F(k) \subseteq F(j)$, so $Y_j \leqslant_\text{vL} Y_k$. If $p_j \not\leqslant p_k$, then $k \notin F(j)$, so $X_k \oplus Y_j$ is 1-random. But $X_k \oplus Y_k$ is not 1-random, since $k \in F(k)$. So $Y_j \not\leqslant_\text{vL} Y_k$.

The following corollary improves an earlier result of Yu, Ding, and Downey that $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}} \not\leqslant_K \Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ for all $n > m$. It follows immediately from part (iii) of Theorem 10.3.3, since if $m \neq n$ then $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}} \oplus \Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ is 1-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.4 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $m \neq n$, then $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}}$ and $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ have no upper bound in the vL-degrees.

</div>

It is also clear that $\Omega$ is the least $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random set in the vL-degrees, and that no 2-random set can be vL-above a $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random set.

Notice that parts (ii) and (iv) of Theorem 10.3.3 immediately imply Theorem 8.5.3, which states that if $X$ is $n$-random and $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X$ is 1-random, then $Y$ is $n$-random.

Kučera's Theorem 8.5.1 says that every degree above $\mathbf{0}'$ is 1-random, but no such degree can be $n$-random for any $n > 1$. Thus we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.5)</span></p>

For every $n$-random degree **a** there is a 1-random degree $\mathbf{b} > \mathbf{a}$ that is not 2-random.

</div>

#### 10.3.2 Relativized Randomness and Turing Reducibility

In this subsection we prove the following strong extension of Theorem 8.5.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.6 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Let $X \leqslant_\text{T} Y$ and let $X$ be 1-random. For every $Z$, if $Y$ is 1-random relative to $Z$, then $X$ is also 1-random relative to $Z$.

</div>

To prove this result, we use the following lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.7 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $X$ is 1-random then

$$\forall e \exists c \forall n \, [\mu(\lbrace A : \Phi_e^A \upharpoonright n = X \upharpoonright n \rbrace) \leqslant 2^{-n+c}].$$

</div>

*Proof.* Let $C_\sigma = \lbrace A : \Phi_e^A \upharpoonright n = \sigma \rbrace$. Note that if $\sigma \mid \tau$ then $C_\sigma \cap C_\tau = \emptyset$. Now let $F_i = \lbrace \sigma : \mu(C_\sigma) > 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + i} \rbrace$ and let $Q_i = \llbracket F_i \rrbracket$. The $Q_i$ form a sequence of uniformly $\Sigma_1^0$ classes. We claim that $\mu(Q_i) \leqslant 2^{-i}$. Suppose not. Then there is some prefix-free $D \subseteq F_i$ with $\mu(\llbracket D \rrbracket) > 2^{-i}$. We now have

$$\mu(\lbrace A : \Phi_e^A \upharpoonright \lvert\sigma\rvert = \sigma \text{ for some } \sigma \in D \rbrace) = \sum_{\sigma \in D} \mu(C_\sigma) > 2^i \sum_{\sigma \in D} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} = 2^i \mu(\llbracket D \rrbracket) > 2^i 2^{-i} = 1,$$

which is a contradiction. So the $Q_i$ form a Martin-Löf test. Since $X$ is 1-random, $X \notin Q_c$ for some $c$, and the result follows.

*Proof of Theorem 10.3.6.* Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be as in the theorem. Let $\Phi$ be such that $\Phi^Y = X$, and let $c$ be the constant for this $\Phi$ and $X$ given by Lemma 10.3.7. For each $\sigma$, enumerate a set $F_\sigma$ as follows. If $\Phi^\tau \upharpoonright \lvert\sigma\rvert$ has use exactly $\tau$, then add $\tau$ to $F_\sigma$ provided that doing so preserves the condition $\sum_{\rho \in F_\sigma} 2^{-\lvert\rho\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + c}$. Since each $F_\sigma$ is prefix-free, we have $\mu(\llbracket F_\sigma \rrbracket) = \sum_{\tau \in F_\sigma} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} \leqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + c}$. Moreover, $\llbracket F_{X \upharpoonright n} \rrbracket = \lbrace A : \Phi^A \upharpoonright n = X \upharpoonright n \rbrace$ for all $n$, since the choice of $c$ means that we are not prevented from adding any strings to $F_{X \upharpoonright n}$.

Now suppose that $X$ is not 1-random relative to $Z$. For each $i$, define the $\Sigma_1^{0,Z}$ class $Q_i = \bigcup_{K^Z(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - c - i} \llbracket F_\sigma \rrbracket$. Then

$$\mu(Q_i) \leqslant \sum_{K^Z(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert - c - i} \mu(\llbracket F_\sigma \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_\sigma 2^{-K^Z(\sigma) - i} \leqslant 2^{-i}.$$

Hence the $Q_i$ form a Martin-Löf test relative to $Z$. Since $X$ is not 1-random relative to $Z$, for each $i$ there is an $n$ with $K^Z(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n - c - i$, which implies that $Y \in \lbrace A : \Phi^A \upharpoonright n = X \upharpoonright n \rbrace \subseteq Q_i$, contradicting the assumption that $Y$ is 1-random relative to $Z$.

#### 10.3.3 vL-reducibility, $K$-reducibility, and Joins

The key ingredient in connecting $\leqslant_K$ to $\leqslant_\text{vL}$ was the realization by Miller and Yu that the initial segment complexity of $X$ determines, for any $Z$, whether $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random. This idea then allowed them to prove the important result that $X \leqslant_K Y$ implies $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$, which means that the results of the previous section have consequences for the $K$-degrees.

Recall that strings of length $n$ can be thought of as representing the numbers between $2^n - 1$ and $2^{n+1} - 2$. We also need some additional notation for the proofs below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.8 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Given $X = x_0 x_1 \ldots$ and $Z = z_0 z_1 \ldots$, let $X \widehat{\oplus} Z$ be

$$z_0\, x_0\, x_1\, z_1\, x_2\, x_3\, x_4\, x_5\, z_2 \ldots z_n\, x_{2^n}\, x_{2^n - 2} \ldots x_{2^{n+1} - 3}\, z_{n+1} \ldots$$

</div>

Clearly, $X \widehat{\oplus} Z$ is 1-random iff $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random. In fact, $X \widehat{\oplus} Z \equiv_\text{vL} X \oplus Z$. We can also define $\sigma \widehat{\oplus} \tau$ for strings $\sigma, \tau \in 2^{<\omega}$, provided $2^{\lvert\tau\rvert - 1} - 2 \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert \leqslant 2^{\lvert\tau\rvert} - 2$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.9 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

$X \oplus Z$ is 1-random iff

$$K(X \upharpoonright (Z \upharpoonright n)) \geqslant (Z \upharpoonright n) + n - O(1).$$

</div>

*Proof.* First, assume that $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random. Then $X \widehat{\oplus} Z$ is also 1-random. Let $\sigma = Z \upharpoonright n$ and $\sigma' = Z \upharpoonright (n + 1)$. Then $K(X \upharpoonright \sigma) = K((X \upharpoonright \sigma) \widehat{\oplus} \sigma') \pm O(1)$. (We use $\sigma'$ here to make the $\widehat{\oplus}$-join defined.) But $(X \upharpoonright \sigma) \widehat{\oplus} \sigma' = (X \widehat{\oplus} Z) \upharpoonright (\sigma + n + 1)$, so

$$K(X \upharpoonright \sigma') = K((X \widehat{\oplus} Z) \upharpoonright (\sigma + n + 1)) \pm O(1) \geqslant \sigma + n - O(1).$$

For the other direction, define a prefix-free machine $M$ as follows. To compute $M(\tau)$, look for $\tau_1$, $\tau_2$, $\eta_1$, and $\eta_2$ such that $\tau = \tau_1 \tau_2$, for the universal prefix-free machine $\mathcal{U}$ we have $\mathcal{U}(\tau_1) = \eta_1 \widehat{\oplus} \eta_2$, and $\lvert\eta_1 \tau_2\rvert = \eta_2$. Let $n = \lvert\eta_2\rvert$. Then $\lvert\eta_1\rvert \leqslant 2^n - 2$ and $\eta_2 \geqslant 2^{n-1} - 2$, so there is a string $\tau_2$ such that $\eta_1 \tau_2$ has length $\eta_2$. If these are found, define $M(\tau) = \eta_1 \tau_2$.

Assume that $X \oplus Z$ is not 1-random. Then for each $k$, there is an $m$ such that $K((X \widehat{\oplus} Z) \upharpoonright m) \leqslant m - k$. Take strings $\eta_1$ and $\eta_2$ such that $K(\eta_1 \widehat{\oplus} \eta_2) \leqslant m - k$, and let $\tau_1$ be a minimal $\mathcal{U}$-program for $\eta_1 \widehat{\oplus} \eta_2$. Let $n = \lvert\eta_2\rvert$. Then $\lvert\eta_1\rvert \leqslant 2^n - 2$ and $\eta_2 \geqslant 2^{n-1} - 1$, so there is a string $\tau_2$ such that $\eta_1 \tau_2 = X \upharpoonright \eta_2$. Then $M(\tau_1 \tau_2) = X \upharpoonright \eta_2$. Therefore,

$$K(X \upharpoonright (Z \upharpoonright n)) - (Z \upharpoonright n) - n \text{ is not bounded below.}$$

Because $k$ was arbitrary, $K(X \upharpoonright (Z \upharpoonright n)) - (Z \upharpoonright n) - n$ is not bounded below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.10 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $X \leqslant_K Y$ then $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$.

</div>

Combined with Theorem 10.3.3, this corollary has the following important consequence for the structure of the $K$-degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.11 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

(i) If $X \leqslant_K Y$ and $X$ is $n$-random, then $Y$ is $n$-random.

(ii) If $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random, then $X \mid_K Y$, and $X$ and $Y$ have no upper bound in the $K$-degrees. Therefore, there is no join in the $K$-degrees.

(iii) If $n \neq m$ then the $K$-degrees of $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ and $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}}$ have no upper bound.

</div>

The following is another attractive corollary, which follows from Theorem 10.3.9 because $X$ is 2-random iff $X \oplus \Omega$ is 1-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
