  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.12 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

$X$ is 2-random iff $X$ is 1-random and $K(X \upharpoonright (\Omega \upharpoonright n)) \geqslant \Omega \upharpoonright n + n - O(1)$.

</div>

Another important consequence of Corollary 10.3.10 is the following fundamental result, which shows that, while the cardinality of a $K$-lower cone might be big, its measure is always small. It was originally proved by direct and more complex methods.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.13 — Yu, Ding, and Downey)</span></p>

For any $Y$, we have $\mu(\lbrace X : X \leqslant_K Y \rbrace) = 0$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $X$ is 1-random relative to $Y$, then $X \not\leqslant_\text{vL} Y$, so by Corollary 10.3.10, $X \not\leqslant_K Y$. Since there are measure 1 many $X$ that are 1-random relative to $Y$, the result follows.

Similar methods also show that the cardinality of $K$-degrees of 1-random sets is as large as it can be. (Actually, since $\leqslant_K$ is Borel, the following result follows from Theorem 10.3.13 and Silver's Theorem that if a Borel (or even just coanalytic) equivalence relation has uncountably many classes, then it has continuum many classes.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.14 — Yu and Ding)</span></p>

There are $2^{\aleph_0}$ many $K$-degrees of 1-random sets.

</div>

Theorem 10.3.14 has a direct proof, but it follows from another theorem from classical measure theory, as observed by Miller and Yu.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.15 — Mycielski)</span></p>

For any relation $E \subseteq 2^\omega \times 2^\omega$ such that $E$ includes the diagonal and $\mu(E) = 0$, there is a perfect set $P \subseteq 2^\omega$ such that $\forall X, Y \in P\, (X \neq Y \Rightarrow (X, Y) \in E)$.

</div>

We can get Theorem 10.3.14 from Theorem 10.3.15 by considering the relation $E = \lbrace (X, Y) : X \equiv_K Y \vee X \text{ not 1-random} \vee Y \text{ not 1-random} \rbrace$.

#### 10.3.4 vL-reducibility and $C$-reducibility

In this section we prove analogs of the results of the previous section for initial segment plain complexity. Our main result is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.16 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Let $Z$ be 1-random. The following are equivalent.

(i) $X$ is 1-random relative to $Z$.

(ii) $C(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K^Z(n) - O(1)$.

(iii) $C(X \upharpoonright n) + K(Z \upharpoonright n) \geqslant 2n - O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $Z$ is 1-random and $X$ is 1-random relative to $Z$. Then, relativizing Theorem 6.7.2,

$$C(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant C^Z(X \upharpoonright n) - O(1) \geqslant n - K^Z(n) - O(1),$$

so (i) implies (ii).

Now assume (ii). Since $Z$ is 1-random, by the Ample Excess Lemma, Theorem 6.6.1, $K^Z(n) \leqslant K(Z \upharpoonright n) - n + O(1)$, and hence

$$C(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - K^Z(n) - O(1) \geqslant 2n - K(Z \upharpoonright n) - O(1),$$

so (ii) implies (iii).

Finally, assume (iii). It is straightforward to check that (for any $X$) we have $C(X \upharpoonright (X \upharpoonright n)) \leqslant (X \upharpoonright n) - n + O(1)$. Thus

$$K(Z \upharpoonright (X \upharpoonright n)) \geqslant 2(X \upharpoonright n) - C(X \upharpoonright (X \upharpoonright n)) - O(1) \geqslant (X \upharpoonright n) + n - O(1).$$

Hence, $Z \oplus X$ is 1-random by Theorem 10.3.9. Thus, by van Lambalgen's Theorem, $X$ is 1-random relative to $Z$, so (iii) implies (i).

The following corollary is more or less immediate.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.17 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

(i) $X \oplus Z$ is 1-random iff $C(X \upharpoonright n) + K(Z \upharpoonright n) \geqslant 2n - O(1)$.

(ii) $X \leqslant_C Y$ implies $X \leqslant_\text{vL} Y$.

(iii) Let $X \leqslant_C Y$. If $X$ is $n$-random then $Y$ is $n$-random.

(iv) If $X \oplus Y$ is 1-random then the $C$-degrees of $Y$ and $X$ have no upper bound, and hence there is no join in the $C$-degrees.

(v) If $n \neq m$ then the $C$-degrees of $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(n)}}$ and $\Omega^{\emptyset^{(m)}}$ have no upper bound.

</div>

It follows, by the same arguments as in the previous section, that the measure of the class of sets $C$-reducible to a given set is always 0, and there are continuum many $C$-degrees of 1-random sets.

#### 10.3.5 Contrasting vL-reducibility and $K$-reducibility

One might hope that $K$-reducibility and vL-reducibility coincide on the 1-random sets. However, the following result implies that they differ even for $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random sets. A slight modification of its proof shows that $C$-reducibility and vL-reducibility also do not coincide on the 1-random sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.18 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

For any 1-random set $X$, there is a 1-random set $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X \oplus \emptyset'$ such that $X \mid_K Y$.

</div>

As noted above, $\Omega$ is vL-below every $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random set. On the other hand, Theorem 10.3.18 implies that there is a $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random $Y \mid_K \Omega$. Therefore, $\leqslant_\text{vL}$ does not, in general, imply $\leqslant_K$ on the $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random sets.

The difficulty in proving Theorem 10.3.18 is getting the precise degree bound. Easier methods construct a $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X'$ (in fact even with $Y' \leqslant_\text{T} X'$) such that $X \mid_K Y$, by Corollary 10.3.11. This method would suffice to construct an infinite $K$-antichain in the $\Delta_2^0$ degrees starting with a low 1-random set.

*Proof of Theorem 10.3.18.* Let $R = \lbrace Z : \forall n\, (K(Z \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n) \rbrace$ and note that $\mu(R) > 0$. We define two predicates:

$$A(\tau, p) \iff \mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \tau \rbrace \cap R) > p$$

and

$$B(\sigma, s) \iff \exists n < \lvert\sigma\rvert\, (K(\sigma \upharpoonright n) > K(X \upharpoonright n) + s) \wedge \exists m < \lvert\sigma\rvert\, (K(\sigma \upharpoonright m) < K(X \upharpoonright m) - s),$$

where $\sigma, \tau \in 2^{<\omega}$, $p \in \mathbb{Q}$, and $s \in \mathbb{N}$. Clearly, we can compute whether $B(\sigma, s)$ holds given $X \oplus \emptyset'$. To see that we can compute whether $A(\tau, p)$ holds given $\emptyset'$, note that $A(\tau, p)$ is equivalent to

$$\exists s\, (\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \tau \rbrace \cap (\exists n \leqslant s)\, K_s(Z \upharpoonright n) < n) > p).$$

We construct $Y = \bigcup_s \sigma_s$ by finite initial segments $\sigma_s \in 2^{<\omega}$ such that $B(\sigma_{s+1}, s)$ holds, which guarantees that $X \mid_K Y$. We also require the inductive assumption that $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \sigma_s \rbrace \cap R) > 0$, which ensures that $Y \in R$, because $R$ is closed, and hence that $Y$ is 1-random. Finally, the construction is done relative to the oracle $X \oplus \emptyset'$, so $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X \oplus \emptyset'$.

*Stage $s = 0$:* Let $\sigma_0 = \lambda$. Note that $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \sigma_0 \rbrace \cap R) = \mu(R) > 0$, so the inductive assumption holds for the base case.

*Stage $s + 1$:* We have defined $\sigma_s$ so that $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \sigma_s \rbrace \cap R) > 0$. Using the oracle $X \oplus \emptyset'$, search for $\tau \succ \sigma_s$ and $p < 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}$ such that $B(\tau, s)$ and $\neg A(\tau, p)$. If these are found, then let $\sigma_{s+1} = \tau$ and note that it satisfies our requirements. In particular, $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \sigma_{s+1} \rbrace \cap R) \geqslant 2^{-\lvert\sigma_{s+1}\rvert} - p > 0$.

All that remains is to verify that the search succeeds. We know by Corollary 10.3.11 (ii) that $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \mid_K X \rbrace \cap R) = 1$. Therefore, $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \sigma_s \rbrace \cap R) > 0$. So there is a $\tau \succ \sigma_s$ such that $B(\tau, s)$ and $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \succ \tau \rbrace \cap R) > 0$, which implies that there is a $p \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $p < 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}$ and $\neg A(\tau, p)$.

A similar proof gives the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.3.19 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

For any finite collection $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ of 1-random sets, there is a 1-random $Y \leqslant_\text{T} X_0 \oplus \cdots \oplus X_n \oplus \emptyset'$ such that, for every $i \leqslant n$, the sets $Y$ and $X_i$ have no upper bound in the $K$-degrees.

</div>

### 10.4 Upward Oscillations and $\leqslant_K$-comparable 1-random Sets

The main result of this section is a characterization by Miller and Yu of the functions $g$ for which $K(X \upharpoonright n)$ infinitely often exceeds $n + g(n)$ for every 1-random $X$. As we will see, these are exactly the functions such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)}$ diverges. We will then use this result to show that there are comparable $K$-degrees of 1-random sets. One direction of the Miller-Yu characterization follows from the Ample Excess Lemma (Theorem 6.6.1). For the harder direction, we prove the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.1 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, then there is a 1-random $X$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$. Furthermore, we can ensure that $X \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset'$.

</div>

The proof of this result is broken up into two parts. The first part is essentially technical. We would like to be able to code $f$ into a 1-random set in a compact way, but this may not be possible. Instead, we construct a function $g$ such that $g(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$ and $g$ can be coded compactly, meaning that we can use Gács coding to produce a 1-random $X$ such that $g(n)$ is computable from the first $n$ bits of $X$, for all $n$. Furthermore, we ensure that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$. The second part of the proof is verifying that $X$ is the desired 1-random set, which is the content of the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.2 — Bounding Lemma, Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$ and $g \leqslant_\text{T} X$ with use the identity function, then $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Because $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$, using the KC Theorem, we can ensure that $K^g(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + g(\lvert\sigma\rvert) + O(1)$ for all $\sigma$. Furthermore, it is easy to see from the proof of the KC Theorem that we can arrange things so that, for $\sigma \in 2^n$, the only part of the oracle that is needed is $g \upharpoonright n+1$. Since $g \upharpoonright n+1$ is computable from $X \upharpoonright n$, we have a hope of removing the dependence on the oracle of the short description $\tau$ of $X \upharpoonright n$ given by the KC Theorem. However, it would appear that we need $X \upharpoonright n$ to tell us how to decode $\tau$. As Miller and Yu put it, "it is as if we have encrypted the decryption key along with our message. We would know how to read the message if only we knew what the message said. The heart of the proof is resolving this circularity."

By the KC Theorem, there is a prefix-free oracle machine $M$ and $\tau_0, \tau_1, \ldots$ such that $\lvert\tau_n\rvert \leqslant g(n) + O(1)$ and $M^X(\tau_n) = X \upharpoonright n$ for all $n$. We may assume that $M^X(\tau_n)$ has use exactly $n$ and $M^X(\tau_n)$ reads exactly $\tau_n$ before halting.

We think of $M$ as having one-way, read-only input and oracle tapes, such that reading a bit moves the corresponding head one position (so that we cannot look at the same position of either tape twice). In addition, we think of $M$ as having work tapes, which it can use to store the bits of the input and oracle that it reads, which is why our assumptions do not limit the power of $M$.

The key step of the proof is to transform $M$ into a machine $M^\circ$ with no oracle. We do this by routing any reading requests that $M$ makes to either its input or oracle tape to the input tape of $M^\circ$. We identify $M^\circ$ with the partial computable function that converges on $\tau$ with output $\rho$ iff $M^\circ$ halts after reading exactly $\tau$ on its input tape and writing $\rho$ on its output tape. In other words, we treat $M^\circ$ as a self-delimiting machine, which ensures that $M^\circ$ has prefix-free domain.

Now suppose that $M^X(\tau) \downarrow = \rho$ with use exactly $n$, and that $M$ reads exactly all of the bits of $\tau$ from the input tape. At certain stages of the computation, $M$ asks for the next bit of the input or the next bit of the oracle, and by our assumptions, it cannot ask for the same bit of either tape more than once. Now merge the bits of $\tau$ and $X \upharpoonright n$ together in exactly the order that they are requested by $M$, and call the resulting string $\sigma$. Then $M^\circ(\sigma) \downarrow = \rho$, because the computation of $M^\circ$ on $\sigma$ is indistinguishable from the computation of $M^X$ on $\tau$. Therefore, $K_{M^\circ}(\rho) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert = n + \lvert\tau\rvert$. Applying this observation to the $\tau_n$ shows that

$$K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_{M^\circ}(X \upharpoonright n) + O(1) \leqslant n + \lvert\tau_n\rvert + O(1) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1).$$

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

*Proof of Theorem 10.4.1.* Let $f$ be such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$. We want to construct a function $g$ such that $g(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$ and $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$, and furthermore $g$ can be coded in a compact way. In particular, we require that

1. $g(n) \leqslant f(n)$ for all $n$,
2. $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$,
3. $g(0) = 0$,
4. if $n \not\equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then $g(n) = g(n+1)$, and
5. $\lvert g(n+1) - g(n) \rvert \leqslant 1$ for all $n$.

We want to define $g$ to be (point-wise) maximal among the functions satisfying 1–5. That is, $g$ satisfies 1–5 and if $g'$ satisfies 1–5 then $g(n) \geqslant g'(n)$ for all $n$.

Any function $g$ satisfying 3, 4, and 5 will have $g(n) \leqslant \lfloor \frac{n}{4} \rfloor$. Furthermore, because $g$ is forced to change at a slow rate, the value of $f(n)$ not only bounds the value of $g(n)$, but also places bounds on all values of $g$. For example, if $f(10) = 3$, then $g$ is at most 3 on $[8, 11]$, at most 4 on $[4, 7]$ and $[12, 15]$, and so on. Let $b(i, n)$ be the upper bound placed on $g(n)$ by the value of $f(i)$. Putting together all of the restrictions on $g(n)$, we define

$$g(n) = \min\Big\lbrace\Big\lfloor \tfrac{n}{4}\Big\rfloor, \min_i b(i, n)\Big\rbrace. \tag{10.1}$$

Clearly $g$ satisfies 1, 3, 4, and 5, and is point-wise maximal among functions that do so. To verify that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$, note that for any $i$,

$$\sum_n 2^{-b(i,n)} \leqslant 4 \cdot 2^{-f(i)} + 8 \sum_{n > 0} 2^{-f(i) - n} = 12 \cdot 2^{-f(i)}.$$

Therefore,

$$\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} \leqslant \sum_n \Big(2^{-\lfloor \frac{n}{4}\rfloor} + \sum_i 2^{-b(i,n)}\Big) = \sum_n 2^{-\lfloor \frac{n}{4}\rfloor} + \sum_i \sum_n 2^{-b(i,n)} \leqslant 8 + \sum_i 12 \cdot 2^{-f(i)} < \infty.$$

Next we prove that $g \leqslant_\text{T} f$. Clearly, $b \leqslant_\text{T} f$. Although (10.1) expresses $g(n)$ as the minimum of an infinite $f$-computable sequence, it is not hard to see that we can ignore all but finitely many terms. In particular, if $i \geqslant 2n$, then $b(i, n) \geqslant \lfloor \frac{n}{4} \rfloor$, so

$$g(n) = \min\Big\lbrace\Big\lfloor \tfrac{n}{4}\Big\rfloor, \min_{i < 2n} b(i, n)\Big\rbrace.$$

Thus $g \leqslant_\text{T} f$.

The restrictions placed on $g$ allow us to code it compactly into a set $C$. It is of course necessary only to record the value of $g(n+1) - g(n)$ for all $n \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. Two bits are sufficient to code $g(n+1) - g(n)$ because there are only three possible values. Thus we use the first two bits of $C$ to record $g(4) - g(3)$, the next two for $g(8) - g(7)$, and so on. Note that $g(n)$ can be computed from $C \upharpoonright \lfloor \frac{n}{2} \rfloor$ (or more precisely, $C \upharpoonright (2\lfloor \frac{n}{4} \rfloor)$). Of course, $C \leqslant_\text{T} g \leqslant_\text{T} f$.

By the Kučera-Gács Theorem 8.3.2, there is a 1-random $X$ such that $C \leqslant_\text{T} X$ and $X \leqslant_\text{T} C \oplus \emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset'$. (The upper bound on the complexity of $X$ is clear from the construction in the proof of Theorem 8.3.2, and made more explicit in Theorem 8.5.1.) As was mentioned following the proof of Theorem 8.3.2, the Gács version of that result produces an $X$ that computes $C$ with use $2n$, meaning that only the first $2n$ bits of $X$ are used to compute $C \upharpoonright n$, for all $n$. Therefore, $g(n)$ is computable from $X \upharpoonright n$, for all $n$. Hence the Bounding Lemma 10.4.2 implies that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

The following corollary improves an earlier result by Bienvenu and Downey (Corollary 8.4.3). Their result was the same, but assuming that the function $h$ tends to infinity rather than just being unbounded.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.3 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $h$ is unbounded, then there is a 1-random $X$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) < n + h(n)$ for infinitely many $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Choose a sequence of distinct natural numbers $\lbrace n_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ such that $h(n_i) \geqslant 2i$. Let

$$f(n) = \begin{cases} i & \text{if } n = n_i \\ n & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

We can apply Theorem 10.4.1 because $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-i} + \sum_n 2^{-n} = 4$, so there is a 1-random $X$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$. Therefore,

$$K(X \upharpoonright n_i) \leqslant n_i + f(n_i) + O(1) = n_i + i + O(1) \leqslant n_i + h(n_i) - i + O(1).$$

When $i$ is sufficiently large, $K(X \upharpoonright n_i) \leqslant n_i + h(n_i)$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

To construct comparable 1-random $K$-degrees, we need a stronger form of Theorem 10.4.1. It is clear that we can modify the proof of that result to require that $g(n) = g(n+1)$ whenever $n \not\equiv 7 \pmod{8}$. Then $g$ can be coded into $C$ so that $g \leqslant_\text{T} C$ with use $\lfloor \frac{n}{4} \rfloor$. By Gács coding, there is a 1-random $X \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset'$ such that $g$ is computable from $X$ with use $\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \rfloor$. So for any $Z$, we have $g \leqslant_\text{T} X \oplus Z$ with use $n$. Applying the Bounding Lemma 10.4.2 gives the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.4 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

If $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$ then there is a 1-random $X \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset'$ such that $K((X \oplus Z) \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$ for every $Z$.

</div>

We can now give the desired classification of functions coding the upward oscillations of initial segment complexities of 1-random sets. First we need a relatively straightforward lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.5 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

Assume that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < \infty$.

(i) There is a function $f \leqslant_\text{T} g$ with $f(n) \leqslant g(n)$ for all $n$ and $\limsup_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$.

(ii) There is a function $f \leqslant_\text{T} g'$ such that $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$ and $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) For each $m$, let $n_m = \min\lbrace n : g(n) \geqslant 2m \rbrace$ and let $f(n_m) = \lfloor g(n_m)/2 \rfloor$. Let $f(n) = g(n)$ for all other values of $n$. Then

$$\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} \leqslant \sum_n 2^{-g(n)} + \sum_m 2^{-m} < \infty.$$

Clearly, $f(n) \leqslant g(n)$ for all $n$. Furthermore, $f \leqslant_\text{T} g$ and $\limsup_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$.

(ii) Let $c \geqslant \sum_n 2^{-g(n)}$. Then $g'$ computes an increasing sequence $\lbrace n_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ such that $\sum_{n \geqslant n_i} 2^{-g(n)} \leqslant \frac{c}{2^i}$ for all $i$. We may assume that $n_0 = 0$. Let $f(n) = g(n) - \lfloor \log \lvert\lbrace i : c_i \leqslant n \rbrace\rvert \rfloor$ (or $f(n) = 0$ if this value is negative). Then

$$\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} \leqslant \sum_n \lvert\lbrace i : n_i \leqslant n \rbrace\rvert \cdot 2^{-g(n)} = \sum_i \sum_{n \geqslant n_i} 2^{-g(n)} \leqslant \sum_i \frac{c}{2^i} = 2c < \infty.$$

Furthermore, $f \leqslant_\text{T} g'$ and $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.6 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} = \infty$.

(ii) For every 1-random $X$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) > n + g(n)$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii) We prove the contrapositive. Assume that $X$ is 1-random and there is an $m$ such that $\forall n \geqslant m\, (K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n))$. Then $\sum_n 2^{n - K(X \upharpoonright n)} \geqslant \sum_{n \geqslant m} 2^{-g(n)}$. The first sum is finite by the Ample Excess Lemma, Theorem 6.6.1, so $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)}$ converges.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (i) Again we prove the contrapositive. Assume that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)}$ converges. By Lemma 10.4.5 (ii), there is a function $f$ such that $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$ and $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$. Hence, by Theorem 10.4.1, there is a 1-random $X$ such that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$. This inequality, together with the fact that $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$, implies that $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n)$ for almost all $n$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

We are now ready to prove that there are comparable 1-random $K$-degrees. It seems surprising that this result relies on such difficult methods, whereas obtaining incomparable 1-random $K$-degrees (as in Corollary 10.3.11) is relatively straightforward.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.7 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

For every 1-random $A$, there is a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset'$ such that $B <_K A$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $g(n) = K(A \upharpoonright n) - n$ and note that $g \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset'$. By Lemma 10.4.5 (i), there is a function $f$ majorized by $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$ and $\limsup_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$. Furthermore, $f \leqslant_\text{T} g \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset'$. Applying Theorem 10.4.1 to $f$ produces a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} A \oplus \emptyset'$ such that $K(B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$. But then

$$K(B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1) = K(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1),$$

so $B \leqslant_K A$. Furthermore,

$$\limsup_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - K(B \upharpoonright n) \geqslant \limsup_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - n - f(n) - O(1)$$

$$\geqslant \limsup_n g(n) - f(n) - O(1) = \infty,$$

so $A \nleqslant_K B$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.8 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

For every $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random $A$, there is a $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random $B$ such that $B <_K A$.

</div>

Miller and Yu observed that by weakening the complexity restriction on $B$ in Theorem 10.4.7, we can replace $<_K$ with what appears to be a stronger relation.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.9 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

We say that $B$ is **strongly $K$-below** $A$, and write $B \ll_K A$, if $\lim_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - K(B \upharpoonright n) = \infty$.

</div>

Clearly $B \ll_K A$ implies $B <_K A$, but the converse is open.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.10 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

For every 1-random $A$, there is a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} A'$ such that $B \ll_K A$.

</div>

*Proof.* First, we wish to find a function $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)}$ converges and $g(n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n$ for all $n$. To control the complexity of $B$, we also require $g$ to be low relative to $A$ (otherwise, we could simply let $g(n) = K(A \upharpoonright n) - n$). By the Ample Excess Lemma, Theorem 6.6.1, there is a $c$ such that $\sum_n 2^{n - K(A \upharpoonright n)} \leqslant c$. Define a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class $S \subseteq \omega^\omega$ by

$$S = \Big\lbrace g \in \omega^\omega : \sum_n 2^{-g(n)} \leqslant c \text{ and } \forall n\, (g(n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n) \Big\rbrace.$$

Note that $S$ is computably bounded (meaning that there is a computable function that majorizes every element of $S$), because $K(A \upharpoonright n) - n \leqslant K(n) + O(1) \leqslant 2 \log n + O(1)$. Therefore, by the low basis theorem relativized to $A$, there is a function $g \in S$ such that $g' \leqslant_\text{T} A'$.

By Lemma 10.4.5 (ii), there is an $f$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$ and $\lim_n g(n) - f(n) = \infty$, and furthermore, $f \leqslant_\text{T} g' \leqslant_\text{T} A'$. Apply Theorem 10.4.1 to $f$ to produce a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} f \oplus \emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} A'$ such that $K(B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$. Then

$$\lim_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - K(B \upharpoonright n) \geqslant \lim_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - n - f(n) - O(1)$$

$$\geqslant \lim_n g(n) - f(n) - O(1) = \infty.$$

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Taking $A$ in the previous corollary to be low, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.11 — Miller and Yu)</span></p>

There are $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random sets $A$ and $B$ such that $B \ll_K A$.

</div>

We finish this section with an application of the Bounding Lemma (and other results of Miller and Yu) to extend work of Solovay described earlier in this chapter. Recall Theorem 8.4.3, which states that there is no function $g$ that tends to infinity such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + g(n) - O(1)$ for every 1-random $A$. As we have seen in Corollary 10.3.17, Miller and Yu showed that if $Z$ is 1-random, then $X$ and $Z$ are relatively 1-random iff $K(X \upharpoonright n) + C(Z \upharpoonright n) \geqslant 2n - O(1)$. Taking $Z = \Omega$, we see that a 1-random set $X$ is 2-random iff $K(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + f(n) + O(1)$, where $f(n) = n - C(\Omega \upharpoonright n)$, which tends to infinity because $\Omega$ is not infinitely often $C$-random (see Theorem 6.11.3). Thus we have a "single gap characterization" of 2-randomness (which can be extended to $n$-randomness for $n > 2$ by replacing $\Omega$ by a 1-random set Turing equivalent to $\emptyset^{(n-1)}$), and hence Theorem 8.4.3 does not hold for 2-randomness. Miller [unpublished] has shown that there is a characterization of 2-randomness via a single gap given by a function $f$ that is not just unbounded, but also nondecreasing (i.e., an order). In fact, $f$ can be taken to be Solovay's function $s(n) = -\log \sum_{j \geqslant n} 2^{-K(j)}$ defined in Section 10.2.2.

It will be convenient to use a different characterization of $s$. We take $\Omega = \sum_m 2^{-K(m)}$ (which is a 1-random left-c.e. real and hence a version of $\Omega$) and $\Omega_n = \sum_{m < n} 2^{-K_n(m)}$. Let $\gamma(n) = -\log(\Omega - \Omega_n)$. (It is not hard to show that $\gamma$ is independent up to an additive constant of the choice of version of $\Omega$ and corresponding approximation.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.12 — Miller, unpublished)</span></p>

$\gamma(n) = s(n) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We have $\Omega - \Omega_n \geqslant \sum_{m \geqslant n} 2^{-K(m)}$, so $\gamma(n) \leqslant s(n) + O(1)$. For the other direction, let $f(n) = -\log(\Omega_{n+1} - \Omega_n)$. Then $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} = \Omega < \infty$. Since $f$ is computable, we have $K(m) \leqslant f(m) + O(1)$, so

$$s(n) = -\log \sum_{m \geqslant n} 2^{-K(m)} \leqslant -\log \sum_{m \geqslant n} 2^{-f(m)} + O(1)$$

$$= -\log \sum_{m \geqslant n} (\Omega_{m+1} - \Omega_m) + O(1) = -\log(\Omega - \Omega_n) + O(1) = \gamma(n) + O(1).$$

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Let $\widehat{\gamma}(n) = \max\lbrace k : \Omega_n \upharpoonright k = \Omega \upharpoonright k \rbrace$. Since $\Omega - \Omega_n \leqslant 2^{-\widehat{\gamma}(n)}$, we have $\widehat{\gamma}(n) \leqslant \gamma(n) + O(1)$. (Miller asked whether $\widehat{\gamma}$ is independent up to a constant of the choice of $\Omega$ and $\lbrace \Omega_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, and whether in fact $\widehat{\gamma}(n) = \gamma(n) \pm O(1)$.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.4.13 — Miller, unpublished)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is 2-random.

(ii) $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + \gamma(n) - O(1)$.

(iii) $\lim_n K(A \upharpoonright n) - n - \gamma(n) = \infty$.

The same equivalences hold with $\gamma$ replaced by $\widehat{\gamma}$.

</div>

*Proof.* It is clear that (iii) implies (ii). Since $\widehat{\gamma}(n) \leqslant \gamma(n) + O(1)$, each of (ii) and (iii) for $\gamma$ implies the respective condition for $\widehat{\gamma}$. So it is enough to show that (iii) for $\gamma$ implies (i), and (ii) for $\widehat{\gamma}$ implies (i). We assume that $A$ is 1-random, since otherwise none of the conditions hold.

We first assume that (iii) does not hold and conclude that $A$ is not 2-random. By Corollary 6.6.4, $K^A(n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright n) - n + O(1)$, so there is a $c$ such that $S = \lbrace n : K^A(n) \leqslant \gamma(n) + c \rbrace$ is infinite. Define a prefix-free machine $M^A$ as follows. If $\mathcal{U}^A(\sigma) \downarrow = n$, then let $M^A(\sigma 0) = \Omega_n \upharpoonright (\lvert\sigma\rvert - c)$ and $M^A(\sigma 1) = (\Omega_n + 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert + c}) \upharpoonright (\lvert\sigma\rvert - c)$. Fix $n \in S$ and let $\sigma$ be a minimal length $\mathcal{U}^A$-program for $n$. Then $\lvert\sigma\rvert - c \leqslant \gamma(n)$, so $\Omega - \Omega_n \leqslant 2^{-\widehat{\gamma}(n)} \leqslant 2^{-(\lvert\sigma\rvert - c)}$. Thus one of $M^A(\sigma 0)$ and $M^A(\sigma 1)$ is equal to $\Omega \upharpoonright (\lvert\sigma\rvert - c)$. Therefore, $K^A(\Omega \upharpoonright (\lvert\sigma\rvert - c)) \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + O(1)$ for infinitely many $\sigma$. But then $\Omega$ is not 1-random relative to $A$, so by van Lambalgen's Theorem, $A$ is not 1-random relative to $\Omega$. In other words, $A$ is not 2-random.

We now assume that $A$ is not 2-random and conclude that (ii) does not hold for $\widehat{\gamma}$. Define a function $g$ by recursion on $n$ as follows. To define $g(n)$, look for the length-lexicographically least $\sigma$ such that $U_{n}^{A \upharpoonright n}(\sigma) \prec \Omega_n$. If there is no such $\sigma$ then let $g(n) = n$. Otherwise, let $g(n) = \lvert\sigma\rvert$ and mark $\sigma$ as used. Note that $g(n)$ can be computed from $A \upharpoonright n$, and that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in \text{dom}(\mathcal{U}^A)} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} + \sum_n 2^{-n} < \infty$. Thus, by the Bounding Lemma, Theorem 10.4.2, $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1)$.

By van Lambalgen's Theorem, $\Omega$ is not 1-random relative to $A$. Fix $c$ and let $m$ be such that $K^A(\Omega \upharpoonright m) \leqslant m - c$. Let $\sigma$ be the length-lexicographically least $\mathcal{U}^A$-program for $\Omega \upharpoonright m$. For all sufficiently large $n$, we have $U_n^{A \upharpoonright n}(\sigma) \downarrow$ and $\Omega_n \upharpoonright m = \Omega \upharpoonright m$. Therefore, $\sigma$ is eventually used in the definition of $g$ for some $n$, and we have $g(n) = \lvert\sigma\rvert \leqslant m - c$. But $\Omega_n \upharpoonright m = \Omega \upharpoonright m$, so $\widehat{\gamma}(n) \geqslant m$. Thus

$$K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant n + g(n) + O(1) \leqslant n + m - c + O(1) \leqslant n + \widehat{\gamma}(n) - c + O(1).$$

Since $c$ is arbitrary, (ii) does not hold for $\widehat{\gamma}$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Miller [unpublished] also showed that there is no $A$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n + \alpha(n) - O(1)$.

### 10.5 LR-reducibility

On the 1-random sets, vL- and LR-reducibility coincide, but LR-reducibility is also quite interesting and important on non-1-random sets. Many questions in randomness can be construed as ones about the structure of the LR-degrees. The following basic result provides a tool for investigating LR-reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.1 — Kjos-Hanssen)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$.

(ii) For every $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class $T^A$ of measure less than 1, there is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class $V^B$ such that $\mu(V^B) < 1$ and $T^A \subseteq V^B$.

(iii) For some member $U^A$ of a universal Martin-Löf test relative to $A$, there is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class $V^B$ such that $\mu(V^B) < 1$ and $U^A \subseteq V^B$.

Consequently, $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$ iff every $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class of positive measure has a $\Pi_1^{0,B}$ subclass of positive measure.

</div>

We will give a proof due to Barmpalias, Lewis, and Soskova, which is conceptually simpler than Kjos-Hanssen's original proof, but along similar lines. We begin with three lemmas.

The first concerns oracle Martin-Löf tests. We can think of such a test as a uniformly c.e. sequence $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of sets of axioms of the form $(\tau, \sigma)$, letting $U_n^\beta = \llbracket \lbrace \sigma : \exists \tau \preccurlyeq \beta\, ((\tau, \sigma) \in U_n) \rbrace \rrbracket$, where $\beta \in 2^\omega$ or $\beta \in 2^{<\omega}$. It is easy to see that any oracle Martin-Löf test can be represented in this way, and that the following holds.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.2 — Barmpalias, Lewis, and Soskova)</span></p>

There is a universal oracle Martin-Löf test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ with the following properties.

(i) From a c.e. index for an oracle Martin-Löf test $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, we can compute a $k$ such that for all $A \in 2^\omega$ and all $n$, we have $V_{n+k}^A \subseteq U_n^A$.

(ii) If $(\tau_1, \sigma_1), (\tau_2, \sigma_2) \in U_n$ and $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$, then $\sigma_1 \mid \sigma_2$.

(iii) If $(\tau, \sigma) \in U_n$ then $\lvert\tau\rvert = \lvert\sigma\rvert$ and $(\tau, \sigma) \in U_{n, \lvert\tau\rvert} \setminus U_{n, \lvert\tau\rvert - 1}$.

</div>

For sets of strings $U$ and $V$, let $UV = \lbrace \sigma\tau : \sigma \in U \wedge \tau \in V \rbrace$. Set $U^0 = 2^{<\omega}$ and $U^{n+1} = U^n U$. (So $U^1 = U$ and $U^2 = UU$, for instance.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.3 — Kjos-Hanssen)</span></p>

Let $\llbracket U_0 \rrbracket, \llbracket U_1 \rrbracket$ be open sets and $S$ a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class of measure less than 1 such that $\llbracket U_0 U_1 \rrbracket \subseteq S$. Then there is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class $R$ of measure less than 1 such that $\llbracket U_i \rrbracket \subseteq R$ for at least one $i = 0, 1$.

Hence, by iteration, if $\llbracket U \rrbracket$ is an open set and $S$ is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class of measure less than 1 such that $\llbracket U^n \rrbracket \subseteq S$ for some $n > 0$, then there is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class $R$ of measure less than 1 such that $\llbracket U \rrbracket \subseteq R$.

</div>

*Proof.* It suffices to prove the first part of the lemma, since the second then follows by induction.

First suppose that there is a $\sigma \in U_0$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \setminus S) > 0$. Then we can let $R = \llbracket \lbrace \tau : \llbracket \sigma\tau \rrbracket \subseteq S \rbrace \rrbracket$ and note that $\mu(R) < 1$ and $\llbracket U_1 \rrbracket \subseteq R$.

Otherwise, let $q$ be a rational such that $\mu(S) < 1 - q$ and $(1-q)\mu(R) \leqslant \mu(S)$. Then $\llbracket U_0 \rrbracket \subseteq R$ and $\mu(R) < 1$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.4 — Barmpalias, Lewis, and Soskova)</span></p>

Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a universal oracle Martin-Löf test. If $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$ then there are $\sigma$ and $e > 0$ such that $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \nsubseteq U_1^B$ and $U_e^A \cap \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \subseteq U_1^B \cap \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose otherwise. Let $\sigma_0 = \lambda$. Given $\sigma_n$ such that $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \nsubseteq U_1^B$, let $\sigma_{n+1} \succ \sigma_n$ be such that $\llbracket \sigma_{n+1} \rrbracket \subseteq U_n^A$ and $\llbracket \sigma_{n+1} \rrbracket \nsubseteq U_1^B$, which must exist because $U_n^A \cap \llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \nsubseteq U_1^B$. Let $X = \lim_n \sigma_n$. Then $X \in U_n^A$ for all $n$, so $X$ is not 1-random relative to $A$, but since $X \notin U_1^B$, $X$ is 1-random relative to $B$, which is a contradiction.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

*Proof of Theorem 10.5.1.* First suppose that $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be the test of Lemma 10.5.2. Let $\sigma$ and $e$ be as in Lemma 10.5.4. Let $W^B = \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \cup (U_1^B \cap \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)$. Then $W^B$ is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ class, and $U_e^A \subseteq W^B$, by the choice of $\sigma$ and $e$. But $W^B$ is a $\Pi_1^{0,B}$ class, and every element of $W^B$ is in $\overline{U_1^B}$, and hence is 1-random relative to $B$. Thus $\mu(\overline{W^B}) > 0$, and hence $\mu(W^B) < 1$, so we have (iii).

Now consider a $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class $T^A$ of measure less than 1, thought of as a prefix-free set of strings. Then some subsequence of $\lbrace (T^A)^k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test relative to $A$. By the properties of $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, there is a $k$ such that $(T^A)^k \subseteq U_e^A$. Hence $(T^A)^k \subseteq W^B$ and by Lemma 10.5.3 we have (ii). That is, assume there exists $V^A \subseteq T^B$ with $\mu(V^B) < 1$ and $T^A \subseteq V^B$.

Finally, assume (iii). By the relativized form of Theorem 6.10.2, there are $Y$ and $\sigma$ such that $X = \sigma Y$ and $Y \in \overline{T^B} \subseteq \overline{V^A}$. Then $Y$ is 1-random relative to $A$, and hence so is $X$. Thus $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Space considerations preclude us from giving too many details about the structure of the LR-degrees. Additional results may be found in, for example, the works of Barmpalias et al. We will meet LR-reducibility again in Section 11.9, in the context of studying lowness for weak 2-randomness. Here are some known facts.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.5 — Barmpalias, Lewis, and Soskova)</span></p>

Every non-$GL_2$ set has uncountably many LR-predecessors.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.6 — Barmpalias, Lewis, and Stephan)</span></p>

Let $A$ be noncomputable. Then there are $B \vert_\text{T} A$ and $C >_\text{T} A$ in the LR-degree of $A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.7 — Barmpalias)</span></p>

There is no minimal pair of $\Delta_2^0$ LR-degrees.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.8 — Barmpalias)</span></p>

Every $\Delta_2^0$ LR-degree bounds a nonzero c.e. LR-degree.

</div>

As a representative example, we sketch the proof of Theorem 10.5.8. (The proof of Theorem 10.5.7 is similar.) We select this theorem because its proof involves a technique that is particular to the LR-degrees and relies heavily on Theorem 10.5.1.

*Proof sketch of Theorem 10.5.8.* Let $X \nleqslant_\text{LR} \emptyset$ be $\Delta_2^0$. We build a c.e. set $A$ such that $\emptyset <_\text{LR} A \leqslant_\text{LR} X$. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a universal oracle Martin-Löf test, and write $U$ for $U_4$ (so that $\mu(U^Z) < \frac{1}{8}$ for all $Z$). By Theorem 10.5.1, to ensure that $A \leqslant_\text{LR} X$, it is enough to build a $\Sigma_1^{0,X}$ class $S^X$ such that $\mu(S^X) < 1$ and $U^A \subseteq S^X$.

The construction proceeds by building auxiliary $\Sigma_1^0$ classes $E_e$ and uses the fact that, again by Theorem 10.5.1, for each $i$, either $U_i^X \nsubseteq E_e$ or $\mu(E_e) = 1$, since $X \nleqslant_\text{LR} \emptyset$.

We identify our oracle $\Sigma_1^0$ classes with their sets of generators, so that we can say, for instance, that $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \in U^A[s]$ with a certain use $u$. Whenever we have $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \in (U^A \setminus S^X)[s]$, we let $a(\sigma, s)$ be the use with which $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \in U^A[s]$ and add $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ to $S^X[s+1]$ with use $a(\sigma, s)$. Of course, $X \upharpoonright a(\sigma, s)$ may change several times, requiring us to keep putting $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ back into $S^X$, but since $X$ is $\Delta_2^0$, if $a(\sigma, t) = a(\sigma, s)$ for all $t > s$, then we will eventually have $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \in S^X$ permanently.

This method clearly ensures that $U^A \subseteq S^X$, but since $A$ will not be computable, $A \upharpoonright a(\sigma, s)$ may well change after stage $s$, at a point at which we have already put $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ into $S^X$ with an $X$-correct use. We have then added an interval to $S^X$ that may not be in $U^A$, which is a threat to our requirement that $\mu(S^X) < 1$. Thus we estimate the *cost* $c(n, s)$ of enumerating $n$ into $A$ at stage $s$ to be $\mu(\lbrace Z : Z \in U^A[s] \text{ with use greater than } n \rbrace)$.

The basic module for satisfying $R_e$, using parameters $a_e$ and $b_e$ determined by the construction, proceeds as follows. Let $k_e[s]$ be the least $k$ such that $U_{b_e}^{X[s] \upharpoonright k} \setminus E_e[s] \neq \emptyset$, let $C_e[s] = U_{b_e}^{X[s] \upharpoonright k_e[s]} \setminus E_e[s]$, and let $m_e[s] = \mu(C_e[s])$. The module also has a parameter $r_e$, initially set to 0. At a stage $s_0$ at which we wish to launch an attack on $R_e$, the module proceeds as follows.

1. Let $p_e[s_0] = 2^{-r_e[s_0] - e - 6} m_e[s_0]$. Let $\Delta_e$ be a finite set of strings such that $\mu(\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket) = p_e[s_0]$ and $\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket \cap V_e[s_0] = \emptyset$. Put $\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket$ into $Q^A$ with a fresh large use $n_e$.

2. Wait for a stage $s_1 > s_0$ such that $\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket \subseteq V_e[s_1]$.

   (a) Suppose that, while we are waiting, $X \upharpoonright k_e[s_0]$ changes before $s_1$ is found. Then increase $r_e$ by one, declare $\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket$ is *trash*, and return to step 1.

   (b) Suppose we find $s_1$.

   &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;i. If $c(n_e, s_0) \geqslant a_e p_e[s_0]$, then restrain $A \upharpoonright s_1$ and return to step 1.

   &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;ii. Otherwise, proceed as follows. Put $n_e$ into $A$, thus removing $\llbracket \Delta_e \rrbracket$ from $Q^A$. Put a subset of $C_e[s_0]$ of measure $p_e[s_0]$ into $E_e$ and return to step 1.

The analysis shows that the attack on $R_e$ is satisfied, meaning $Q^A \nsubseteq V_e$. The total measure of our trash is bounded by $2^{-e-5}$. Using the fact that $X$ is $\Delta_2^0$, we can argue that $k_e[s]$ must reach a limit and hence so must $r_e$, so that eventually step 2(a) stops occurring, and $R_e$ can be satisfied as argued above.

The remaining details of the proof involve choosing the parameters of the construction appropriately and applying the finite injury priority method. The formal details are in Barmpalias.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

We finish this section by showing that LR-reducibility, which can be seen as a way to compare the relative derandomization power of sets, can also be characterized in terms of relative compression power. We say that $A$ is **low for $K$ reducible** to $B$, or simply **LK-reducible** to $B$, and write $A \leqslant_\text{LK} B$, if $K^B(\sigma) \leqslant K^A(\sigma) + O(1)$. Clearly, if $A \leqslant_\text{LK} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$. The following result shows that these reducibilities are in fact equivalent.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.9 — Kjos-Hanssen, Miller, and Solomon)</span></p>

If $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{LK} B$.

</div>

To prove this result, we need the following easy lemma from basic analysis.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.10)</span></p>

Let $a_0, a_1, \ldots \in [0, 1)$ be real numbers. Then $\prod_i (1 - a_i) > 0$ iff $\sum_s a_i < \infty$.

</div>

*Proof of Theorem 10.5.9.* Let $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$. Define finite sets $V_0, V_1, \ldots$ as follows. Suppose that $V_t$ has been defined for all $t < s$. Let $n$ and $\tau$ be such that $s = \langle n, \tau \rangle$ (where we identify $\tau$ with a natural number as usual). Let $m$ be the length of the longest string in $\bigcup_{t < s} V_t$ (or $m = 0$ if $s = 0$), and let $V_s = \lbrace \sigma 0^n : \sigma \in 2^m \rbrace$. Note that for any $I \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, we have $\mu(\bigcap_{s \in I} \overline{\llbracket V_s \rrbracket}) = \prod_{\langle n, \tau \rangle \in I} (1 - 2^{-n})$.

Let $I = \lbrace \langle \lvert\sigma\rvert, \tau \rangle : \mathcal{U}^A(\sigma) = \tau \rbrace$. Then $I$ is $A$-c.e., and hence $P = \bigcap_{s \in I} \overline{\llbracket V_s \rrbracket}$ is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class. Furthermore,

$$\sum_{\langle n, \tau \rangle \in I} 2^{-n} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in \text{dom}(\mathcal{U}^A)} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} < 1.$$

Thus, $\langle 0, \tau \rangle \notin I$ for every $\tau$, and hence $\mu(P) = \prod_{\langle n, \tau \rangle \in I} (1 - 2^{-n}) > 0$, by Lemma 10.5.10. Therefore, by Theorem 10.5.1, there is a $\Pi_1^{0,B}$ class $Q \subseteq P$ with $\mu(Q) > 0$.

Let $J = \lbrace \langle n, \tau \rangle : \llbracket V_{\langle n, \tau \rangle} \rrbracket \cap Q = \emptyset \rbrace$. Then $J$ is $B$-c.e., and

$$\prod_{\langle n, \tau \rangle \in J} (1 - 2^{-n}) = \mu\Big(\bigcap_{s \in J} \overline{\llbracket V_s \rrbracket}\Big) \geqslant \mu(Q) > 0.$$

Thus, by Lemma 10.5.10, there is a $c$ such that $\sum_{\langle n, \tau \rangle \in J} 2^{-n} < 2^c$. Let $S = \lbrace (n + c, \tau) : \langle n, \tau \rangle \in J \rbrace$. Then $S$ is a KC set relative to $B$, so if $\langle n, \tau \rangle \in J$ then $K^B(\tau) \leqslant n + O(1)$, and hence $\langle K^A(\tau), \tau \rangle \in J$ for each $\tau$. Thus $K^B(\tau) \leqslant K^A(\tau) + O(1)$, and hence $A \leqslant_\text{LK} B$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

This result has several important consequences. One of them is that if $A$ is 1-random, then the $K$-degree of $A$ is countable. (We will see another one in Theorem 11.2.) Recall that, by Theorem 9.15.1, if $K^X(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K^X(n) + O(1)$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} X'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.11)</span></p>

If $A \equiv_\text{LR} X$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} X'$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $A \equiv_\text{LR} X$ then $A \equiv_\text{LK} X$, so $K^X(A \upharpoonright n) = K^A(A \upharpoonright n) \pm O(1) = K^X(n) \pm O(1)$, so Theorem 9.15.1 applies.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.5.12 — Miller, unpublished)</span></p>

If $X$ is 1-random then the $K$-degree of $X$ is countable.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $A \equiv_K X$. By Corollary 10.3.10, $A \equiv_\text{LR} X$. Since $X$ and $A$ are both 1-random, $A \equiv_\text{LR} X$. By Corollary 10.5.11, $A \leqslant_\text{T} X'$. Thus every element of the $K$-degree of $X$ is computable in $X'$, and hence the $K$-degree of $X$ is countable.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

### 10.6 Almost Everywhere Domination

Motivated by a question in the reverse mathematics of measure theory, Dobrinen and Simpson introduced the following concepts.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.6.1 — Dobrinen and Simpson)</span></p>

(i) A set $A$ is **almost everywhere dominating** if for almost all $X$ (in the sense of measure), for each $g \leqslant_\text{T} X$ there is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ that dominates $g$.

(ii) A set $A$ is **uniformly almost everywhere dominating** if there is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ such that for almost all $X$, the function $f$ dominates all functions $g \leqslant_\text{T} X$.

</div>

As we have seen in Lemma 8.20.6, Kurtz showed that $\emptyset'$ (and hence any set computing $\emptyset'$) is uniformly almost everywhere dominating. Cholak, Greenberg, and Miller showed that there are uniformly almost everywhere dominating sets that do not compute $\emptyset'$. On the other hand, it follows from Martin's Theorem 2.23.7 that every uniformly almost everywhere dominating set is high. It was natural to ask for a characterization of the (uniformly) almost everywhere dominating sets. One has now been found in terms of LR-reducibility. We will show through a sequence of results that the following are equivalent:

(i) $A$ is almost everywhere dominating.

(ii) $A$ is uniformly almost everywhere dominating.

(iii) $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{LR} A$.

In other words, a set $A$ is (uniformly) almost everywhere dominating iff every set that is 1-random relative to $A$ is 2-random. In Section 11.2, we will show that there are incomplete c.e. sets have it, so randomness-theoretic notions are essential in answering this question.

We begin with the following characterizations of (uniform) almost everywhere domination.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.6.2 — Dobrinen and Simpson)</span></p>

(i) A set $A$ is uniformly almost everywhere dominating iff for each $\Pi_2^0$ class $P$ there is a $\Sigma_2^{0,A}$ class $Q \subseteq P$ such that $\mu(Q) = \mu(P)$.

(ii) A set $A$ is almost everywhere dominating iff for each $\Pi_2^0$ class $P$ and each $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class $Q \subseteq P$ such that $\mu(Q) \geqslant \mu(P) - \varepsilon$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) First suppose that $A$ is uniformly almost everywhere dominating, as witnessed by the function $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$. Let $P$ be a $\Pi_2^0$ class. It is easy to define a functional $\Phi$ such that $P = \lbrace X : \Phi^X \text{ total} \rbrace$. Let $g^X(n)$ be the least $s$ such that $\Phi^X(n)[s] \downarrow$. Then $f$ dominates $g$ for almost all $X \in P$, so we can take $Q = \lbrace X : \exists c\, \forall n\, (\Phi^X(n)[f(n) + c] \downarrow) \rbrace$.

Now suppose that the second half of (i) holds. Fix $\varepsilon$ and $e$. Let $P = \lbrace X : \Phi_e^X \text{ total} \rbrace$ and let $Q \subseteq P$ be a $\Sigma_2^{0,A}$ class such that $\mu(Q) = \mu(P)$. Write $Q$ as an effective union of $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ classes $Q_0, Q_1, \ldots$. By compactness, for each $i$, $e$, and $n$, the set $S_{e,i,n} = \lbrace \Phi_e^X(n) : 0^e 1 X \in Q_i \rbrace$ is finite. These sets are uniformly $A$-computable. Let $f(n) = \max \bigcup_{e,i < n} S_{e,i,n}$. Then for each $e$, the function $f$ dominates $\Phi_e^X$ for almost all $X$ for which $\Phi_e^X$ is total, from which it follows that for almost all $X$, the function $f$ dominates all total $\Phi_e^X$.

(ii) is just a nonuniform version of the above. First suppose that $A$ is almost everywhere dominating. Let $P$ be a $\Pi_2^0$ class, and let $g^X$ be as above. For almost all $X \in P$, there is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ that majorizes $g^X$. Since there are only countably many $A$-computable functions, given $\varepsilon > 0$ there are $f_0, \ldots, f_k \leqslant_\text{T} A$ such that for all but measure $\varepsilon$ many $X \in P$, some $f_i$ with $i \leqslant k$ majorizes $g^X$. Let $f(n) = \max_{i \leqslant k} f_i(n)$. Then we can take $Q = \lbrace X : \forall n\, (\Phi^X(n)[f(n)] \downarrow) \rbrace$.

Now suppose that the second half of (ii) holds. Fix $\varepsilon > 0$. Let $P = \lbrace X : \Phi_e^X \text{ total} \rbrace$ and let $Q \subseteq P$ be a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class such that $\mu(Q) \geqslant \mu(P) - \varepsilon$. Arguing by compactness as above, there is an $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ such that $f$ majorizes $\Phi_e^X$ for every $X$ in $Q$. Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary, we see that for almost all $X$, there is an $A$-computable function that majorizes $\Phi_e^X$. Since there are only countably many $e$, it follows that $A$ is almost everywhere dominating.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.6.3 — Binns, Kjos-Hanssen, Lerman, and Solomon)</span></p>

If $A$ is almost everywhere dominating then $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{LR} A$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $U_0, U_1, \ldots$ be a universal Martin-Löf test relative to $\emptyset'$ and let $P = \overline{U_1}$. Then $P$ is a $\Pi_2^0$ class of positive measure all of whose elements are 2-random. By Theorem 10.6.2, there is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class $Q \subseteq P$ of positive measure. Let $X$ be 1-random relative to $A$. By Theorem 6.10.2, there are $Y \in Q$ and $\sigma$ such that $X = \sigma Y$. Then $Y$ is 2-random, and hence so is $X$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

The other direction of our equivalence relies on the following result, which will also be important in Section 11.9.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.6.4 — Kjos-Hanssen, Miller, and Solomon)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A \leqslant_\text{T} B'$ and $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$.

(ii) Every $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class has a $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass of the same measure.

(iii) Every $\Sigma_2^{0,A}$ class has a $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass of the same measure.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii) This part of the proof is similar to the proof of Theorem 10.5.9. Extend the length-lexicographic ordering of strings $\leqslant_\text{L}$ to pairs of strings. We define sets of strings $V_{(\sigma, \tau)}$ inductively. Suppose that we have defined $V_{(\sigma', \tau')}$ for all $(\sigma', \tau') \leqslant_\text{L} (\sigma, \tau)$ and let $k$ be the maximum of the lengths of strings in $\bigcup_{(\sigma', \tau') \leqslant_\text{L} (\sigma, \tau)} V_{(\sigma', \tau')}$. Let $V_{(\sigma, \tau)} = \lbrace \nu 0^{\lvert\tau\rvert} : \nu \in 2^k \rbrace$. It is easy to see that if $I \subseteq 2^{<\omega} \times 2^{<\omega}$, then

$$\mu\Big(\bigcap_{(\sigma, \tau) \in I} \overline{\llbracket V_{(\sigma, \tau)} \rrbracket}\Big) = \prod_{(\sigma, \tau) \in I} (1 - 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}).$$

Let $P = \bigcap_{(\sigma, \tau) \in I} \overline{\llbracket V_{(\sigma, \tau)} \rrbracket}$. Note that $P$ is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class. Recall the fact from Lemma 10.5.10 that if $a_0, a_1, \ldots \in [0, 1]$ then $\prod_i (1 - a_i) > 0$ iff $\sum_i a_i < \infty$, since $W^A$ is prefix-free, so $\mu(P) = \prod_{(\sigma, \tau) \in I} (1 - 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}) > 0$. Thus, by Theorem 10.5.1, there is a $\Pi_1^{0,B}$ class $Q \subseteq P$ with $\mu(Q) > 0$.

Let $J = \lbrace (\sigma, \tau) : \llbracket V_{(\sigma, \tau)} \rrbracket \cap Q = \emptyset \rbrace$. Note that $J$ is $B$-c.e., and $\prod_{(\sigma, \tau) \in J} (1 - 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}) \geqslant \mu(Q) > 0$, so $\sum_{(\sigma, \tau) \in J} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} < \infty$. Since $A \leqslant_\text{T} B'$, there is a $B$-computable approximation $A_0, A_1, \ldots$ to $A$. Let

$$T_s = \lbrace (\sigma, \tau) \in J : \exists t \geqslant s\, (\tau \in W^{A_t}[t] \text{ with use exactly } \sigma) \rbrace$$

and

$$U_s = \lbrace \tau : \exists \sigma\, ((\sigma, \tau) \in T_s) \rbrace.$$

Then $\lbrace T_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ and $\lbrace U_s \rbrace_{s \in \omega}$ are sequences of uniformly $B$-c.e. sets. Let $R = \bigcup_s \overline{\llbracket U_s \rrbracket}$. We claim that $R$ is the desired $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass of $S$.

First notice that $W^A \subseteq U_s$ for all $s$, and hence $R \subseteq S$. Now, for each $(\sigma, \tau) \in T_0 \setminus I$, there is a final stage $t$ such that $\sigma$ is a prefix of $A_t$, as otherwise $(\sigma, \tau) \in I$. Then for any stage $s > t$, we have $(\sigma, \tau) \notin T_s$. Fix $\varepsilon > 0$ and choose $n$ sufficiently large so that if $(\sigma, \tau) \in T_0 \setminus I$ and $\lvert\sigma\rvert, \lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant n$ then $(\sigma, \tau) \notin T_s$. Choose $s$ large enough so that if $(\sigma, \tau) \in T_0 \setminus I$ and $\lvert\sigma\rvert, \lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant n$ then $(\sigma, \tau) \notin T_s$. Then

$$\mu(S \setminus \llbracket U_s \rrbracket) \leqslant \sum_{\tau \in U_s \setminus W^A} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{(\sigma, \tau) \in T_s \setminus I} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert}$$

$$\leqslant \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} : (\sigma, \tau) \in J \wedge \lvert\sigma\rvert, \lvert\tau\rvert > n \rbrace < \varepsilon.$$

Since $\varepsilon > 0$ is arbitrary, $\mu(R) = \mu(S)$.

(ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii) Let $S$ be a $\Sigma_2^{0,A}$ class and let $P_0, P_1, \ldots$ be uniformly $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ classes such that $S = \bigcup_i P_i$. Let $P = \lbrace 0^i 1\alpha : \exists i\, (\alpha \in X_i) \rbrace$. Then $P$ is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class, so by (ii) it has a $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass $Q$ of the same measure. For each $i$, let $Q_i = \lbrace \alpha : 0^i 1\alpha \in Q \rbrace$. Then the $Q_i$ are uniformly $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ classes with $Q_i \subseteq P_i$ for all $i$. If $\mu(Y_i) < \mu(X_i)$ for some $i$, then $\mu(Q) = \sum_i 2^{i+1} \mu(Q_i) < \sum_i 2^{i+1} \mu(P_i) = \mu(P)$, contradicting the choice of $Q$, so $\mu(Q_i) = \mu(X_i) = \mu(Y_i)$ for all $i$. Now let $R = \bigcup_i Q_i$. Then $R$ is a $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass of $P$, and $\mu(P \setminus R) \leqslant \sum_i \mu(P_i - Q_i) = 0$, so $\mu(R) = \mu(P)$.

(iii) $\Rightarrow$ (i) If $P$ is a $\Pi_1^{0,A}$ class of positive measure then by (ii) (which is clearly implied by (iii)), $P$ has a $\Sigma_2^{0,B}$ subclass $S$ of the same measure. Now, $S$ is a countable union of $\Pi_2^0$ classes, so one of these classes has a $\Pi_1^{0,B}$ subclass of positive measure, so by Theorem 10.5.1, $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Next, we show that $A \leqslant_\text{T} B'$. Let $\sigma_n = 0^n 1$ and let $U = \bigcup_{n \in A} \llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket$. Since $U$ is a $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class (and hence a $\Pi_2^{0,A}$ class), by (iii) there is a $\Pi_2^{0,B}$ class $Q$ such that $\mu(U) = \mu(Q) = \sum_{n \in A} 2^{-(n+1)}$. We claim that $n \in A$ iff $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \subseteq Q$. If $n \in A$, then $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \subseteq U \subseteq Q$. On the other hand, if $n \notin A$ and $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \subseteq Q$, then $\mu(Q) \geqslant \sum_{i \in A} 2^{-(i+1)} + 2^{-n} > \mu(U)$, which is a contradiction.

Let $Q_0, Q_1, \ldots$ be uniformly $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ classes such that $Q = \bigcap_k Q_k$. Then $n \in A$ iff $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \subseteq Q_k$ for all $k$. Since $\llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket \subseteq Q_k$ is a $\Sigma_1^{0,B}$ relation, the equivalences show that $A$ is $\Pi_2^{0,B}$. The same argument applied to the $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class $\bigcup_{n \notin A} \llbracket \sigma_n \rrbracket$ shows that $\overline{A}$ is $\Pi_2^{0,B}$ as well, and hence $A \leqslant_\text{T} B'$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(10.6.5 — Kjos-Hanssen, Miller, and Solomon)</span></p>

If $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{LR} A$ then $A$ is uniformly almost everywhere dominating.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{LR} A$. Let $P$ be a $\Pi_2^0$ class. By Theorem 6.8.3, there are uniformly $\Pi_1^{0,\emptyset'}$ classes $R_0, R_1, \ldots \subseteq P$ such that $\mu(P) - \mu(R_n) < 2^{-n}$ for all $n$. Let $R = \bigcup_n R_n$. Then $R$ is a $\Sigma_2^{0,\emptyset'}$ class such that $R \subseteq P$ and $\mu(R) = \mu(P)$. Since $\emptyset' \leqslant_\text{T} A'$, it follows from Theorem 10.6.4 that there is a $\Sigma_2^{0,A}$ class $Q \subseteq R \subseteq P$ such that $\mu(Q) = \mu(R) = \mu(P)$. The theorem now follows from Theorem 10.6.2.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

Note that $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$ does not imply $A \leqslant_\text{T} B'$ because there is a $B$ with uncountably many $A \leqslant_\text{LR} B$ (see Barmpalias, Lewis, and Soskova).

See the work of Kjos-Hanssen, Miller, and Solomon for more on almost everywhere domination and related concepts, including a discussion of the reverse mathematical questions that originally motivated the study of this notion.

## Chapter 11: Randomness-Theoretic Weakness

In this chapter, we introduce an important class of "randomness-theoretically weak" sets, the $K$-trivial sets. As we will see, this class has several natural characterizations, can be used to answer several questions in the theory of algorithmic randomness, and is also of great interest to computability theory.

### 11.1 $K$-triviality

Recall Chaitin's Theorem 3.4.4, which states that $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$ iff $A$ is computable. Chaitin asked whether the analogous result holds for $K$ in place of $C$. He was able to show the following, which is Theorem 9.15.1 for the case $X = S = \emptyset$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.1 — Chaitin)</span></p>

If $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ then $A$ is $\Delta_2^0$.

</div>

Merkle and Stephan noted that essentially the same proof establishes the following result, which we have already encountered in Section 9.15.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.2 — Merkle and Stephan)</span></p>

If $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ for all $n$ in an infinite set $S$ then $A \leqslant_\text{T} S \oplus \emptyset'$.

</div>

The Counting Theorem in fact shows that the width of the tree $T$ in the proof of Theorem 11.1.1 is $O(2^c)$, so we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.3 — Zambella)</span></p>

For each $c$ there are $O(2^c)$ many sets $A$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + c$ for all $n$.

</div>

Of course, if $A$ is computable then $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$, but as we will see in the next subsection, the converse is not true. In other words, even though $A$ may look identical to $\emptyset$ as far as prefix-free initial segment complexity goes, it does not follow that $A$ is computable, even for c.e. sets $A$. Thus we are led to the concept of $K$-triviality.

Recall that a set $A$ is $K$-trivial if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$. Equivalently, $A$ is $K$-trivial if $A \leqslant_K \emptyset$. It makes no difference in the above definition if we restrict our attention to an infinite computable set of $n$'s.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.4)</span></p>

If $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ for all $n$ in an infinite computable set $S$ then $A$ is $K$-trivial.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $h(n)$ be the $n$th element of $S$, in natural order. For any $n$, we have $K(n) = K(h(n)) \pm O(1)$. Furthermore, from a description of $A \upharpoonright h(n)$, we can recover $n$ and thus obtain $A \upharpoonright n$, so $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright h(n)) + O(1)$. Thus $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ for all $n$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

#### 11.1.1 The basic $K$-triviality construction

Solovay was the first to construct a noncomputable $K$-trivial set. His method was adapted by Zambella to construct a noncomputable $K$-trivial c.e. set (see also Calude and Coles). Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan gave a new construction of a $K$-trivial c.e. set, which we present below. (A similar construction had been produced independently by Kummer in unpublished work.) As we will later see, this construction gives a priority-free, and even a requirement-free, solution to Post's Problem.

Before presenting the construction, we make a remark. While the proof below is easy, it is slightly hard to see why it works. So, by way of motivation, suppose that we were asked to "prove" that $\emptyset$ has the same initial segment complexity as $\mathbb{N}$. A complicated way to do so is to build our own prefix-free machine $M$ whose only job is to compute initial segments of $\emptyset$. If $\mathcal{U}(\sigma) \downarrow = 1^n$ then we want $M(\sigma) \downarrow = 0^n$. We can build $M$ *implicitly*, using the KC Theorem, by enumerating the request $(\lvert\sigma\rvert, 0^n)$ every time $\mathcal{U}(\sigma) \downarrow = 1^n$. We are guaranteed that $\sum_{\tau \in \text{dom}\, M} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in \text{dom}\, \mathcal{U}} 2^{-\lvert\sigma\rvert} \leqslant 1$, and hence the KC Theorem applies. Note also that we could, for convenience and as we do in the construction below, use a string of length $\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1$, in which case we would ensure that $\sum_{\tau \in \text{dom}\, M} 2^{-\lvert\tau\rvert} < \frac{1}{2}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.5 — Zambella, after Solovay)</span></p>

There is a noncomputable $K$-trivial c.e. set.

</div>

*Proof.* We define a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ and a KC set $L$. As in the remark above, we follow $\mathcal{U}$ on $n$, in the sense that, when at a stage $s$ we see a shorter $\sigma$ with $\mathcal{U}(\sigma) = n$ than we have previously seen, we enumerate a request $(\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1, A_s \upharpoonright n)$ into $L$. To make $A$ noncomputable, we sometimes need to make $A_{s+1}(n) \neq A_s(n)$. Then for each $j$ with $n < j \leqslant s$, for the currently shortest $\mathcal{U}$-program $\sigma_j$ for $j$, we also enumerate a request $(\lvert\sigma_j\rvert, A_{s+1} \upharpoonright j)$. The construction works by making this extra measure added to the weight of $L$ small.

Let

$$A = \bigg\lbrace n : \exists e\, \exists s \bigg( W_{e,s} \cap A_s = \emptyset \wedge n > 2e \wedge n \in W_{e,s} \wedge \sum_{n < j \leqslant s} 2^{-K_s(j)} \leqslant 2^{-(e+2)} \bigg) \bigg\rbrace,$$

and let $L$ be as described above. Clearly $A$ is c.e. Since $\sum_{j \geqslant m} 2^{-K(j)}$ goes to zero as $m$ increases, if $W_e$ is infinite then $A \cap W_e \neq \emptyset$. Since $\overline{A}$ is also clearly coinfinite, it follows that $A$ is noncomputable. Finally, the extra weight of $L$, beyond one half of the measure of $\text{dom}\, \mathcal{U}$, is bounded by $\sum_e 2^{-(e+2)}$ (corresponding to at most one initial segment change for each $e$), whence the weight of $L$ is bounded by

$$\sum_{\sigma \in \text{dom}\, \mathcal{U}} 2^{-(\lvert\sigma\rvert + 1)} + \sum_e 2^{-(e+2)} \leqslant \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1.$$

So the KC Theorem applies, and hence, since $(K(n), A \upharpoonright n) \in L$ for all $n$, we have $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$.

<span style="float:right;">$\square$</span>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

A useful way to think of this proof is in terms of *cost functions*. The function $c(n, s) = \sum_{n < j \leqslant s} 2^{-K_s(j)}$ represents the cost of enumerating $n$ into $A$ at stage $s$. To ensure that $A$ is $K$-trivial, we need to ensure that $\sum \lbrace c(n, s) : \mu n \in A_{s+1} \setminus A_s \rbrace$ is finite. We will see another example of a cost function construction in the proof of Theorem 11.2.5, and will discuss this idea further below.

The above proof admits several variations. For instance, we can make $A$ promptly simple, or below any nonzero c.e. degree. We cannot control the jump or make $A$ Turing complete, since, as we will see below, all $K$-trivial sets are low.

#### 11.1.2 The requirement-free version

As we will see in Section 11.3, the construction above automatically yields a Turing incomplete c.e. set. It is thus an *injury-free* solution to Post's Problem. It is not, however, *priority-free*, in that the construction depends on an ordering of the simplicity requirements, with stronger requirements allowed to add more to the weight of $L$. We can do methodologically better by giving a priority-free solution to Post's Problem, in the sense that no explicit diagonalization (such as that of $W_e$ above) occurs in the construction of the incomplete c.e. set, and therefore the construction of this set (as opposed to the verification that it is noncomputable) does not depend on an ordering of requirements. We now sketch this method, which is due to Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan, and is rather more like that of Solovay's original proof of the existence of a noncomputable $K$-trivial set.

Let us reconsider the key idea in the proof of Theorem 11.1.5. At certain stages we wish to change an initial segment of $A$ for the sake of diagonalization. Our method is to make sure that the total measure added to the weight of our KC set $L$ (which proves the $K$-triviality of $A$) due to such changes is bounded by 1. Suppose, on the other hand, that we were fortunate enough to have $\mathcal{U}$ itself "cover" the measure needed for these changes. That is, suppose we were at a stage $s$ where we desired to put $n$ into $A_{s+1} \setminus A_s$ and at that very stage $K_s(j)$ changed for all $j \in (n, s]$. Then *in any case* we would need to enumerate new requests describing $A_{s+1} \upharpoonright j$ for all $j \in (n, s]$, whether or not these initial segments change. Thus, at that very stage, we could also change $A_s \upharpoonright j$ for all $j \in (n, s]$ at no extra cost.

The key point is the following. Let $t_0, t_1, \ldots$ be a computable collection of stages. Suppose that we construct a set $A = \bigcup_s A_{t_s}$ so that for $n \leqslant t_s$, if $A_{t_{s+1}} \upharpoonright n \neq A_{t_s} \upharpoonright n$ then $K_{t_s}(j) > K_{t_{s+1}}(j)$ for all $j$ with $n \leqslant j \leqslant t_s$. Then $A$ is $K$-trivial. We are now ready to define such an $A$ in a priority-free way.

Let $t_0, t_1, \ldots$ be a collection of stages such that $t_i$ as a function of $i$ dominates all primitive recursive functions. At each stage $u$, let $\lbrace a_{i,u} : i \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$ list $\overline{A_u}$. Let $A_{t_{u+1}} = A_{t_u} \cup [a_{n,t_u}, t_s]$, where $n$ is the least number less than or equal to $t_s$ such that $K_{t_{s+1}}(j) < K_{t_s}(j)$ for all $j \in (n, t_s]$. (Naturally, if no such $n$ exists, $A_{t_{u+1}} = A_{t_u}$.) Requiring the complexity change for all $j \in (n, t_s]$, rather than just $j \in (a_{n,t_s}, t_s]$, ensures that $A$ is coinfinite, since for each $n$ there are only finitely many $s$ such that $K_{t_{s+1}}(n) < K_{t_s}(n)$.

Note that there is no priority used in the definition of $A$. It is like the Dekker deficiency set or the so-called "dump set" (see Soare).

It remains to prove that $A$ is noncomputable. By the recursion theorem, we can build a prefix-free machine $M$ and know the coding constant $c$ of $M$ in $\mathcal{U}$. That is, if we declare $M(\sigma) = j$ then we will have $\mathcal{U}(\tau) = j$ for some $\tau$ such that $\lvert\tau\rvert \leqslant \lvert\sigma\rvert + c$. Note further that if we put $\sigma$ into the domain of $M$ at stage $t_s$, then $\tau$ will be in the domain of $\mathcal{U}$ by stage $t_{s+1} - 1$.

Now the proof looks like that of Theorem 11.1.5. We devote $2^{-(e+1)}$ of the domain of our machine $M$ to ensuring that $A$ satisfies the $e$th simplicity requirement. When we see $a_{n,t_s}$ occur in $W_{e,t_s}$, where $\sum_{n < j \leqslant t_s} 2^{-K_{t_s}(j)} \leqslant 2^{-(e+c+2)}$, we provide shorter $M_{t_s}$-descriptions of all $j$ with $n < j \leqslant t_s$ so that $K_{t_{s+1}}(j) < K_{t_s}(j)$ for all such $j$. The cost of this change is bounded by $2^{-(e+1)}$, and $a_{n,t_s}$ will enter $A_{t_{s+1}}$, as required.

As observed by Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan, and later formally written out by Nies, many of the usual computability-theoretic variations work for this construction. Thus, for example, there are minimal pairs of degrees containing $K$-trivial sets, and the following holds (though the proof is a bit tricky; see Nies for details).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

If $B$ is a low c.e. set, then there is a $K$-trivial c.e. set $A \nleqslant_\text{T} B$. Indeed, if $B_0, B_1, \ldots$ is a uniformly c.e. sequence of uniformly low sets, then there is a $K$-trivial c.e. set $A$ such that $A \nleqslant_\text{T} B_i$ for all $i$.

</div>

#### 11.1.3 Solovay functions and $K$-triviality

Recall from Definition 3.12.3 that a Solovay function is a computable function such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$ and $f(n) \leqslant K(n) + O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$. In this subsection we point out an attractive connection between $K$-trivials and Solovay functions.

We begin with an analog of Chaitin's Theorem 3.4.4 that all $C$-trivials are computable (where $A$ is $C$-trivial if $C(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant C(n) + O(1)$).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.1.7 — Bienvenu and Downey)</span></p>

Suppose that for each computable function $f$ with $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, there exists a computable function $g$ with $\sum_\sigma 2^{-g(\sigma)} < \infty$ such that $g(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant f(n) + O(1)$. Then $A$ is computable.

</div>
