There is an effective list $\lbrace (B_{e,s})_{s \in \omega}, b_e, Q_e \rbrace$ of $\Delta_2^0$-approximations, constants, and computable sets of stages with the following properties, where $Q_e = \lbrace v_e(0) < v_e(1) < \cdots \rbrace$ (which may be a finite set).

1. For each $e$, the limit $\lim_s B_{e,s}$ exists and is $K$-trivial.
2. Every $K$-trivial set is $\lim_s B_{e,s}$ for some $e$.
3. $B_{e,s}(x) \neq B_{e,s-1}(x) \Rightarrow s = v_e(r)$ for some $r \geqslant 2$.
4. Let $n_s$ be the least number $n$ (if any) such that $B_{e,s}(n) \neq B_{e,s-1}(n)$. If $v_e(s+1)$ is defined, then let $\widehat{c}(n, s) = \sum_{n < x \leqslant v_e(s)} 2^{-K_{v_e(s+1)}(x)}$. Then $\sum_{n_{v_e(s+2)} \downarrow} \widehat{c}(n_{v_e(s+2)}, s) < 2^{b_e}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.10.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

There are uniformly computable approximations $B_{e,s}$ and a computable sequence of constants $d_e$ such that for every $e$, the set $B_e = \lim_s B_{e,s}$ exists and is $K$-trivial via $d_e$; and every $K$-trivial set is $B_e$ for some $e$.

</div>

Using a c.e. version of Lemma 11.10.1, we can obtain a c.e. version of the above result with the same proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.10.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

There are uniformly c.e. sets $A_0, A_1, \ldots$ and a computable sequence of constants $d_e$ such that $A_e$ is $K$-trivial via $d_e$ for each $e$, and every c.e. $K$-trivial set is $A_e$ for some $e$.

</div>

In Theorem 11.4.9, we saw that every $K$-trivial set is low for $K$. However, the constant $c$ in the definition of being low for $K$ was not obtained in a uniform way from the constant of $K$-triviality. The following result shows that this nonuniformity is necessary. We say that $A$ is low for $K$ *via $c$* if $\forall \sigma\, (K(\sigma) \leqslant K^A(\sigma) + c)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.10.4)</span></p>

From an index for a c.e. set $A$ and a $c$ such that $A$ is low for $K$ via $c$, we can effectively compute a lowness index for $A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.10.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

There is no effective way to obtain from a pair $(A, b)$, where $A$ is a c.e. set that is $K$-trivial via $b$, a constant $c$ such that $A$ is low for $K$ via $c$.

</div>

### 11.11 Upper Bounds for the $K$-trivial Sets

We have mentioned in Theorem 11.1.6 that given any low c.e. set $B$ we can construct a $K$-trivial $A \not\leqslant_\text{T} B$. The following result shows that we cannot replace low by $\text{low}_2$. It is a good example of a purely computability-theoretic result that has significant consequences for the study of the interactions between computability and randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.11.1 — Barmpalias and Nies)</span></p>

Let $I$ be a nontrivial $\Sigma_3^0$ ideal in the c.e. Turing degrees. There is a $\text{low}_2$ c.e. set $A$ such that $W \leqslant_\text{T} A$ for all $W \in I$.

</div>

By Corollary 11.5.4 and Theorem 11.6.1, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.11.2 — Barmpalias and Nies)</span></p>

There is a $\text{low}_2$ c.e. set $A$ such that $X \leqslant_\text{T} A$ for all $K$-trivial sets $X$.

</div>

Downey, Hirschfeldt, Nies, and Stephan had earlier shown that there is an incomplete c.e. set $A$ such that $X \leqslant_\text{T} A$ for all $K$-trivial sets $X$. Since the collection of $K$-trivial c.e. sets is $\Sigma_3^0$ and contains the finite sets, by Lemma 5.3.15, there is a c.e. set $A$ such that every $K$-trivial c.e. set is a column of $A$, and every column of $A$ is $K$-trivial. Since the $K$-trivials are closed under join, $A^{|< n|}$ is incomplete for all $n$, so the result follows from the Strong Thickness Lemma, Theorem 2.14.13.

The method of proof of Theorem 11.11.1 is called the $\Delta_3^0$ method, and is slightly more difficult than the $\Pi_2^0$ method we first encountered in the proof of the Minimal Pair Theorem 2.14.1.

The proof builds a $\text{low}_2$ c.e. set $A$ satisfying coding requirements $C_e : U_e \leqslant_\text{T} A$ (where $I$ is given as uniformly c.e. sets $U_0, U_1, U_2, \ldots$ with $U_i \leqslant_\text{T} U_{i+1}$ via a uniform reduction) and lowness requirements $N_i : \lim \sup_s \ell(\tau, s) = \infty \Rightarrow \Phi_i^A$ total. The proof uses a priority tree with $\beta$ nodes (for $C$-requirements), $\tau$ nodes (for $N$-requirements), and $\alpha$ nodes (for subrequirements). The key ideas involve notions of $\tau$-expansionary stages, $\tau$-correctness, and a mechanism to handle conflicts between coding into $A$ and preserving computations.

We have seen that there is a $\text{low}_2$ c.e. degree above all $K$-trivials, but no such low c.e. degree. What happens if the computable enumerability condition is removed? The following is a general theorem of Kučera and Slaman about ideals in the $\Delta_2^0$ degrees, which implies that the $K$-trivials have a low upper bound.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.11.5 — Kučera and Slaman)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for an ideal $I$ in the $\Delta_2^0$ degrees.

(i) $I$ has a low upper bound.

(ii) $I$ is contained in an ideal generated by uniformly $\Delta_2^0$ sets $A_0, A_1, \ldots$, and there is a $\Delta_2^0$ function that dominates every partial function computable by a set in $I$.

</div>

The method of proof is a forcing one akin to the forcing proof of the low basis theorem with coding steps interleaved. A remaining open question is whether the low upper bound for the $K$-trivials can be made 1-random. (Indeed, it is not even known whether there is a 1-random set that computes all $K$-trivials but does not compute $\emptyset'$ (i.e., a difference random set that computes all $K$-trivials); the existence of such a set would answer Question 11.8.2.)

### 11.12 A Gap Phenomenon for $K$-triviality

In Theorem 9.15.8, we saw that there is a minimal pair of $K$-degrees, that is, a pair of non-$K$-trivial sets $A$ and $B$ such that for all $X$, if $X \leqslant_K A, B$ then $X$ is $K$-trivial. In this section, we give the original proof of this result, which is due to Csima and Montalbán and involves a lemma of significant independent interest.

A reasonable strategy for building such a pair of sets would be to ensure that, for some constant $c$ and all $n$,

$$K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + c \quad \text{or} \quad K(B \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + c,$$

while preventing $A$ and $B$ from being $K$-trivial. The problem with such a construction is that, although $\sigma 0^\omega$ is $K$-trivial for any string $\sigma$, the *constant of $K$-triviality depends on $\sigma$*. Thus, while adding 0's to a previously defined initial segment of $A$ might bring the $K$-complexity of longer initial segments down quite a bit, it may never result in an $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + c$ for a given $c$ fixed ahead of time.

The clever solution to this problem, found by Csima and Montalbán, is to prove the following surprising "gap result", which shows that we can replace $c$ in (11.2) by a sufficiently slow-growing function of $n$. Recall that $\text{KT}(c)$ denotes the class of sets that are $K$-trivial via $c$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.12.1 — Csima and Montalbán)</span></p>

There is an order $f$ such that a set $X$ is $K$-trivial iff $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + f(n)$ for almost all $n$.

</div>

The proof builds, for each $e > 0$, an unbounded nondecreasing function $f_e$ such that $f_e(0) = e - 1$ and if $K(X \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K(n) + f_e(n)$ for all $n$, then $X \in \text{KT}(e)$. Given such functions, one takes $f(n) = \min \lbrace f_{2e}(n) - e : e > 0 \rbrace$, which is nondecreasing and unbounded.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(11.12.2 — Csima and Montalbán)</span></p>

There is a minimal pair of $K$-degrees.

</div>

The proof builds $A$ and $B$ by finite extensions. At stage $e + 1$ with $e$ even, one extends $\bar{A}_{e+1} \succ A_e$ so that $K(\bar{A}_{e+1}) > K(\lvert\bar{A}_{e+1}\rvert) + e$, ensuring $A$ is not $K$-trivial. At stage $e + 1$ with $e$ odd, the same is done for $B$. The gap theorem guarantees that $\min(K(A \upharpoonright n), K(B \upharpoonright n)) \leqslant K(n) + f(n)$ for all $n$, which implies that if $X \leqslant_K A, B$ then $X$ is $K$-trivial.

Analysis of the above proofs shows that $f$ can be chosen to be $\Delta_4^0$, and hence so can $A$ and $B$. It is unknown whether there is a minimal pair of $K$-degrees of $\Delta_2^0$ sets. (By Theorem 9.15.8, there is a minimal pair of $K$-degrees of $\Sigma_2^0$ sets.) More interestingly, it is also unknown whether there is a minimal pair of $K$-degrees of left-c.e. reals.

## Chapter 12: Lowness and Triviality for Other Randomness Notions

### 12.1 Schnorr Lowness

We now turn to lowness notions for other notions of randomness. We begin with Schnorr randomness. Since there is no universal Schnorr test, it is not clear that the notions of a set $A$ being *low for Schnorr randomness* (i.e., every Schnorr random set is Schnorr random relative to $A$) and being *low for Schnorr tests* (i.e., every Schnorr test relative to $A$ can be covered by an unrelativized Schnorr test) should be the same. In fact, this was an open question in Ambos-Spies and Kučera. As we will see, it was solved by Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan.

#### 12.1.1 Lowness for Schnorr Tests

A complete characterization of lowness for Schnorr tests was obtained by Terwijn and Zambella, using the notion of computable traceability introduced in Definition 2.23.15. Recall that a degree $\mathbf{a}$ is **computably traceable** if there is a computable function $h$ such that, for each function $f \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{a}$, there is a computable function $g$ satisfying, for all $n$:

(i) $\lvert D_{g(n)} \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ and

(ii) $f(n) \in D_{g(n)}$.

Computable traces are often represented by a single computable set $T = \lbrace \langle y, n \rangle : y \in D_{g(n)} \rbrace$. We will sometimes use this method, and write $T^{[n]}$ for $\lbrace y : \langle y, n \rangle \in T \rbrace$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.1 — Terwijn and Zambella)</span></p>

Let $A$ be computably traceable and let $h$ be a computable order such that $h(0) > 0$. Then $A$ is computably traceable with bound $h$.

</div>

This shows how to arbitrarily slow down the tracing process: if the bound can be made to grow slowly enough, we gain flexibility in constructing Schnorr tests.

The notion of computable traceability was inspired by arguments from set theory, particularly Raisonnier's proof in Shelah's result that the inaccessible cardinal cannot be removed from Solovay's construction of a model of set theory (without the full axiom of choice but with dependent choice) in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable.

As noted in Section 2.23, Terwijn and Zambella observed that the hyperimmune-free degree constructed by Miller and Martin (Theorem 2.17.3) is computably traceable, and that if a degree is computably traceable then it is hyperimmune-free, since computable traceability is actually a uniform version of hyperimmune-freeness. The difference between being hyperimmune-free and being computably traceable for a degree $\mathbf{a}$ is that for the latter there is a single computable bound $h$ that works for all $f \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{a}$, whereas for the former, for each $f \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{a}$ there is a separate computable function bounding $f$. This difference can be turned around into a proof that the concepts are different. We can derive this result most easily using Theorem 12.1.2 below.

Since all computably traceable degrees are hyperimmune-free, no computably traceable degree is $\Delta_2^0$. In particular, if a set is low for 1-randomness then it is not computably traceable. This fact is particularly interesting in view of the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.2 — Terwijn and Zambella)</span></p>

A degree is low for Schnorr tests iff it is computably traceable.

</div>

The proof of the "if" direction uses the computable trace to build an unrelativized Schnorr test covering a given relativized Schnorr test. Given a Schnorr test $\lbrace \llbracket U_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ relative to $A$ with $\mu(\llbracket U_n \rrbracket) = 2^{-n}$, one uses the trace $T$ to enumerate the $V_n$. The key idea is to use $T$ to make sure that the bulk of the $U_n$ is enumerated before we trace it, so the approximation generated by $T$ is good. Using Lemma 12.1.1, one can choose $h$ to be sufficiently slow growing so that $\mu(\llbracket V_n \rrbracket)$ converges computably to $\mu(\llbracket V_n \rrbracket)$.

The other direction is more difficult. Let $A$ be low for Schnorr tests. One uses clopen sets $B_{k,i} = \llbracket \lbrace \tau 1^k : \tau \in 2^i \rbrace \rrbracket$ with $\mu(B_{k,i}) = 2^{-k}$ to code an arbitrary function $g \leqslant_\text{T} A$, building $U_n = \bigcup_{k > n} B_{k,g(k)}$. The test $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Schnorr test relative to $A$, and hence covered by an unrelativized Schnorr test. One of the levels of this unrelativized test, say the fourth one (a $\Sigma_1^0$ class $V$ of measure $\frac{1}{8}$ containing the intersection of the $U_n$), can be used to compute a trace. One defines $T^{[k]} = \lbrace i : \mu(B_{k,i} \setminus V) < 2^{-(i+3)} \rbrace$ and shows $T$ is computable with $\lvert T^{[k]} \rvert < 2^k k$ and $g(k) \in T^{[k]}$ for all $k > n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.3 — Terwijn and Zambella)</span></p>

There are continuum many degrees that are hyperimmune-free but not computably traceable.

</div>

The proof uses the hyperimmune-free basis theorem: there are 1-random (hence Schnorr random) sets of hyperimmune-free degree. Such a set $A$ cannot be low for Schnorr tests (since one can $A$-computably construct a Schnorr test covering $A$ that cannot be covered by an unrelativized Schnorr test), and hence is not computably traceable.

#### 12.1.2 Lowness for Schnorr Randomness

We now turn to the apparently weaker notion of lowness for Schnorr randomness. It is clear that if $A$ is low for Schnorr tests then $A$ is low for Schnorr randomness. Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan showed that the converse also holds, via some intermediate results of independent interest.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.4 — Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

A set $A$ is c.e. traceable iff every Schnorr null set relative to $A$ is Martin-Löf null.

</div>

The proof of the "if" direction follows the proof of Theorem 12.1.2 up to the definition of $V$. Now the measure of $V$ is no longer computable. One can continue to follow the proof of Theorem 12.1.2, with straightforward modifications.

For the other direction, suppose that $A$ is c.e. traceable. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr test relative to $A$ with $\mu(U_n) = 2^{-n}$. We may assume $U_0 \supset U_1 \supset \cdots$ One uses the c.e. trace to build $\widehat{V}_n$, and then the $V_n$ form a Martin-Löf test covering $\bigcap_i U_i$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.5 — Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

If $A$ is of hyperimmune-free degree and c.e. traceable, then $A$ is computably traceable.

</div>

So to show that lowness for Schnorr tests coincides with lowness for Schnorr randomness, it is enough to show that every set that is low for Schnorr randomness has hyperimmune-free degree. This final step was actually the first result proved about lowness for Schnorr randomness, and is due to Bedregal and Nies.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.6 — Bedregal and Nies)</span></p>

If $A$ is either low for computable randomness or low for Schnorr randomness, then $A$ is of hyperimmune-free degree.

</div>

The proof works by assuming $A$ is of hyperimmune degree and building a computably random set $R$ and an $A$-computable martingale $F$ that succeeds on $R$ in the Schnorr sense. One defines strings $\sigma_D$ for certain finite sets $D$ and partial computable martingales $F_D$ that bet evenly except along $\sigma_D$, where they concentrate capital. The key is that $A$'s hyperimmune degree allows it to compute enough about the construction to build $F = \sum_D F_D$ succeeding on $R$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.7 — Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

$A$ is low for Schnorr randomness iff $A$ is computably traceable iff $A$ is low for Schnorr tests.

</div>

#### 12.1.3 Lowness for Computable Measure Machines

Recall the characterization of Schnorr randomness in terms of computable measure machines presented in Section 7.1.3: A prefix-free machine is a **computable measure machine** if the measure of its domain is computable, and Downey and Griffiths showed that $A$ is Schnorr random iff for every computable measure machine $M$ we have $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$. We can relativize this notion, and say that a prefix-free oracle machine with oracle $A$ is an **$A$-computable measure machine** if the measure of its domain is $A$-computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.8 — Downey, Greenberg, Mihailović, and Nies)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **low for computable measure machines** if for each $A$-computable measure machine $M$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $K_N(\sigma) \leqslant K_M(\sigma) + O(1)$.

</div>

In Chapter 11 we saw that lowness for 1-randomness and lowness for $K$ coincide. It is natural to ask whether the same is true for Schnorr randomness. That is, are lowness for Schnorr randomness and lowness for computable measure machines the same?

Downey, Greenberg, Mihailović, and Nies have given a positive answer to this question. By the relativized version of Theorem 7.1.15, a set $X$ is Schnorr random relative to $A$ iff $K_M(X \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for all $A$-computable measure machines $M$. Thus, if a set is low for computable measure machines then it is low for Schnorr randomness. The converse follows from the following result, together with Corollary 12.1.7.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.9 — Downey, Greenberg, Mihailović, and Nies)</span></p>

A set is low for computable measure machines iff it is computably traceable.

</div>

The proof of the "only if" direction follows from the fact that lowness for computable measure machines implies lowness for Schnorr randomness, together with Corollary 12.1.7. The "if" direction constructs, given an $A$-computable measure machine $M$ and a computable trace, a computable measure machine $N$ with $K_N(\sigma) \leqslant K_M(\sigma) + O(1)$, using a KC set argument.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.10 — Downey, Greenberg, Mihailović, and Nies)</span></p>

A set is low for computable measure machines iff it is low for Schnorr randomness.

</div>

Nies has observed that essentially the same proof yields the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.1.11 — Nies)</span></p>

$A$ is c.e. traceable iff for all $A$-computable measure machines $M$ and all $\sigma$, we have $K_M(\sigma) \geqslant K(\sigma) - O(1)$.

</div>

### 12.2 Schnorr Triviality

In Section 9.17, we defined the notion of Schnorr reducibility: $A \leqslant_\text{Sch} B$ if for each computable measure machine $M$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $K_N(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_M(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1)$. Naturally, this notion gives rise to a notion of triviality.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.1 — Downey and Griffiths)</span></p>

$A$ is **Schnorr trivial** if $A \leqslant_\text{Sch} \emptyset$.

</div>

The first construction of a noncomputable Schnorr trivial set was by Downey and Griffiths. As we will see, Schnorr trivial sets behave quite differently from both $K$-trivial sets and sets that are low for Schnorr randomness. However, one implication does hold.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.2 — Franklin)</span></p>

If $A$ is low for Schnorr randomness then $A$ is Schnorr trivial.

</div>

The proof uses the fact that if $A$ is low for Schnorr randomness, then $A$ is low for computable measure machines, by Theorem 12.1.10. Let $M$ be a computable measure machine. Let $L$ be the $A$-computable measure machine defined by letting $L(\sigma) = A \upharpoonright n$ whenever $M(\sigma) = 0^n$. Then there is a computable measure machine $N$ such that $K_N(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant K_L(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1) \leqslant K_N(0^n) + O(1)$.

#### 12.2.1 Degrees of Schnorr Trivial Sets

Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte and Franklin began systematic investigations of the concept of Schnorr triviality. Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte proved the following result, which clearly highlights the differences between Schnorr triviality on the one hand and $K$-triviality or lowness for Schnorr randomness on the other.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.3 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is a Turing complete Schnorr trivial c.e. set.

</div>

We do not give the proof of Theorem 12.2.3 here, as it will follow from Corollary 12.2.16 below.

The following result shows that Schnorr trivials behave rather strangely with respect to Turing reducibility. Later we will explore the question of whether Turing reducibility is the correct notion to study in this context.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.4 — Franklin)</span></p>

Every high degree contains a Schnorr trivial set. In other words, if $\mathbf{a}' \geqslant \mathbf{0}''$ then $\mathbf{a}$ contains a Schnorr trivial set.

</div>

Because both computable and complete Schnorr trivial c.e. sets exist, one might wonder whether every c.e. degree contains a Schnorr trivial set. The following theorem yields a negative answer to this question.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.5 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

There is a c.e. degree $\mathbf{b}$ such that for each $B \in \mathbf{b}$ there is a computable measure machine $N$ with $\forall c\, \exists n\, (K(B \upharpoonright n) > K_N(n) + c)$. Hence $\mathbf{b}$ contains neither Schnorr trivial nor $K$-trivial sets.

</div>

The proof builds a c.e. set $A$ and for each pair of functionals $\Phi$ and $\Psi$ a computable measure machine $N_{\Phi,\Psi}$, defined using the KC Theorem, to satisfy requirements $R_{\Phi,\Psi} : \Psi^{\Phi^A} = A \Rightarrow \forall c\, \exists n\, (K(\Phi^A \upharpoonright n) > K_{N_{\Phi,\Psi}}(n) + c)$. Each such requirement has subrequirements $S_{\Phi,\Psi,i}$, and the strategies use witnesses and a priority tree to manage conflicts.

#### 12.2.2 Schnorr Triviality and Strong Reducibilities

In contrast to Theorem 12.2.3, we have the following result. Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte stated it for left-c.e. reals, but the proof works more generally.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.9 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A$ is $\Delta_2^0$ and Schnorr trivial then it is wtt-incomplete.

</div>

The proof is similar to that of Theorem 11.3.2. Let $A$ be $\Delta_2^0$ and wtt-complete. We construct a c.e. set $B$ that forces the approximation to $A$ to change too often for $A$ to be Schnorr trivial. As in the proof of Theorem 11.3.2, by the recursion theorem, we may assume that a wtt-reduction $\Gamma^A = B$ is given in advance. We also build a computable measure machine $M$ using the KC Theorem to satisfy requirements $R_e : \exists n\, (K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant K_M(n) + e)$.

In Chapter 7, we saw that Schnorr reducibility is naturally related to truth table reducibility. This relationship extends to Schnorr triviality.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.10 — Downey, Griffiths, and LaForte)</span></p>

If $A$ is Schnorr trivial and $B \leqslant_\text{tt} A$, then $B$ is Schnorr trivial.

</div>

#### 12.2.3 Characterizing Schnorr Triviality

Results such as Theorem 12.2.3 show that the beautiful coincidence between $K$-triviality and lowness for 1-randomness, which is one of the arguments for the naturality of the notion of $K$-triviality, does not hold in the case of Schnorr randomness. However, Franklin and Stephan obtained a rather satisfying characterization of Schnorr triviality in terms of relativized truth tables and initial segment complexity, which argues for the naturality of this notion. Their key realization is that to relativize weak notions of randomness like Schnorr or computable randomness, Turing reducibility may not be the correct notion, and should be replaced by truth table reducibility. That is, while we have no hope of showing that Schnorr triviality coincides with lowness for Schnorr randomness, as we did for $K$-triviality, there is some hope for a characterization along such lines if we restrict what we mean by relativization.

We begin by recalling Theorem 7.1.8, which states that the following are equivalent.

(i) $X$ is not Schnorr random.

(ii) There is a computable martingale $d$ and a computable function $f$ such that $\exists^\infty n\, (d(X \upharpoonright f(n)) \geqslant n)$.

(iii) There is a computable martingale $d$ with the savings property and a computable function $f$ such that $\exists^\infty n\, (d(X \upharpoonright f(n)) \geqslant n)$.

Armed with this characterization, Franklin and Stephan suggested the following restricted notion of relativization.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.11 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

A set $X$ is **truth table Schnorr random relative to $A$** if there are no martingale $d \leqslant_\text{tt} A$ and no computable function $g$ such that $\exists^\infty n\, (d(X \upharpoonright g(n)) \geqslant n)$.

</div>

A set $A$ is **truth table low for Schnorr randomness** if every Schnorr random set is tt-Schnorr random relative to $A$.

We can simplify the definition above by considering only computable $g$, rather than all $g \leqslant_\text{tt} A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.12 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

A set $X$ is tt-Schnorr random relative to $A$ iff there are no martingale $d \leqslant_\text{tt} A$ and no computable function $g$ such that $\exists^\infty n\, (d(X \upharpoonright g(n)) \geqslant n)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.13 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

For each $A$, at least one of the following conditions holds.

(i) There is a computably random set that is not tt-Schnorr random relative to $A$.

(ii) There is a computable function $h$ such that, for every computable function $u$, there are uniformly computable finite sets of strings $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ with $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ and $A \upharpoonright u(n) \in T_n$ for all $n$.

</div>

The following is the main characterization result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.14 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) $A$ is Schnorr trivial.

(ii) There is a computable function $h$ such that, for every computable function $u$, there are uniformly computable finite sets of strings $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ with $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ and $A \upharpoonright u(n) \in T_n$ for all $n$.

(iii) The tt-degree of $A$ is computably traceable. That is, there is a computable function $g$ such that for each $f \leqslant_\text{tt} A$, there are uniformly computable finite sets $S_0, S_1, \ldots$ with $\lvert S_n \rvert \leqslant g(n)$ and $f(n) \in S_n$ for all $n$.

(iv) For each computable order $g$ with $g(0) > 0$ and each $f \leqslant_\text{tt} A$, there are uniformly computable finite sets $S_0, S_1, \ldots$ with $\lvert S_n \rvert \leqslant g(n)$ and $f(n) \in S_n$ for all $n$.

(v) $A$ is tt-low for Schnorr randomness.

</div>

The above characterization can be used to show that the Schnorr trivials form an ideal in the tt-degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.15 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

Let $A$ and $B$ be Schnorr trivial. Then $A \oplus B$ is Schnorr trivial. Thus the Schnorr trivial sets form an ideal in the tt-degrees.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.16 — Franklin and Stephan)</span></p>

If a c.e. set $A$ is dense simple and $B \supseteq A$, then $B$ is Schnorr trivial.

</div>

A c.e. set is **dense simple** if the principal function of its complement is dominant (i.e., dominates all computable functions).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.17 — Martin)</span></p>

Every high c.e. degree contains a dense simple c.e. set.

</div>

We can combine this result with the theorem due to Martin to conclude that every high c.e. degree contains a Schnorr trivial c.e. set.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.18 — Franklin)</span></p>

Every high c.e. degree contains a Schnorr trivial c.e. set.

</div>

We can apply the same argument to the $\Pi_1^0$ class of separating sets of a Sacks splitting of a maximal set (which is dense simple) to show the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.2.19 — Franklin)</span></p>

There is a perfect $\Pi_1^0$ class of Schnorr trivial sets.

</div>

Franklin and Stephan observed that their characterization cannot be extended to weak truth table reducibility. They also provided a number of relatively natural examples of Schnorr trivial sets, and considered possible analogs to the fact that a set is $K$-trivial iff it is a base for 1-randomness. Franklin and Stephan observed that the obvious analog (that $A$ is Schnorr trivial iff there is a $B \geqslant_\text{tt} A$ that is Schnorr random relative to $A$) fails, but showed that one direction does hold: if $A \leqslant_\text{tt} B$ and $B$ is Schnorr random relative to $A$, then $A$ is Schnorr trivial. They also considered extensions to stronger reducibilities.

### 12.3 Tracing Weak Truth Table Degrees

#### 12.3.1 Basics

In Theorem 12.2.14 we saw that Schnorr triviality corresponds to a very natural tracing notion when we view the world through strong reducibilities. Specifically, we saw that $A$ is Schnorr trivial iff the truth table degree of $A$ is computably traceable. In this section we will look at the situation for weak truth table reducibility and establish a deep connection between the complexity of a set and tracing properties of its weak truth table degree.

Recall that a set $A$ is **complex** if there is a computable order $h$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright h(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$. As we saw in Theorem 8.16.3, Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan showed that $A$ is complex iff there is a function $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$ such that $C(f(n)) \geqslant n$ for all $n$. Motivated by this result, we make the following definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.1 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **anti-complex** if for every computable order $f$, we have $C(A \upharpoonright f(n)) \leqslant n$ for almost all $n$.

</div>

The intuition here is that anti-complex sets are highly compressible.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.2 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a set $A$.

(i) $A$ is anti-complex.

(ii) The wtt-degree of $A$ is c.e. traceable.

(iii) $A$ is wtt-reducible to a Schnorr trivial set.

</div>

Here, of course, a weak truth table degree $\mathbf{a}$ is **c.e. traceable** if there is a computable order $h$ such that for every function $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} \mathbf{a}$, there is a computable collection $\lbrace W_{g(x)} : x \in \omega \rbrace$ with $\lvert W_{g(x)} \rvert \leqslant h(x)$ and $f(x) \in W_{g(x)}$ for all $x$. The argument of Lemma 12.1.1 shows that we can replace $h$ by any computable order.

An interesting consequence of Theorems 12.2.14 and 12.3.2 is that a weak truth table degree $\mathbf{a}$ is c.e. traceable iff there is a weak truth table degree $\mathbf{b} \geqslant \mathbf{a}$ that contains a set whose truth table degree is computably traceable.

#### 12.3.2 Reducibilities with Tiny Uses

To prove Theorem 12.3.2, Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu introduced yet another variation on weak truth table reducibility.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.3 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

Let $A, B \in 2^\omega$.

(i) We say that $A$ is **uniformly reducible to $B$ with tiny use**, and write $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$, if there is a Turing reduction $\Phi^B = A$ whose use function is dominated by every computable order.

(ii) We say that $A$ is **reducible to $B$ with tiny use**, and write $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$, if for every computable order $h$, there is a Turing reduction of $A$ to $B$ whose use function is bounded by $h$.

</div>

Some of the basic properties of these reducibilities are easy to obtain. For instance, if $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$ then $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$, and if $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ then $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} B$. It is also easy to see that if $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} C \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ then $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$, and if $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} C \leqslant_\text{wtt} B$ then $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$. Thus, the relation $\leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}}$ is invariant on weak truth table degrees (and is preserved by increasing the degree on the range or decreasing the degree on the domain). The same holds for $\leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}}$. Moreover, for a fixed $B$, the classes $\lbrace A : A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B \rbrace$ and $\lbrace A : A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B \rbrace$ are wtt-ideals.

Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu gave another formulation of these notions using not the use functions but their discrete inverses. If $\Phi^B = A$ is a Turing reduction, then let $\Phi(B \upharpoonright n)$ be the longest initial segment of $A$ that is computed by $\Phi^B$ querying the oracle $B$ only on numbers smaller than $n$. Recall that a function is **dominant** if it dominates every computable function.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.4 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

Let $A, B \in 2^\omega$.

(i) $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ iff, for every computable order $g$, there is a Turing reduction $\Phi^B = A$ such that the map $n \mapsto \lvert \Phi(B \upharpoonright n) \rvert$ bounds $g$.

(ii) $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$ iff there is a Turing reduction $\Phi^B = A$ such that the map $n \mapsto \lvert \Phi(B \upharpoonright n) \rvert$ is dominant.

</div>

We begin our analysis of these reducibilities with some further easy observations.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.5)</span></p>

(i) If $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} A$ then $A$ is computable.

(ii) If $A$ is computable then $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$ for all $B$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f(n) = n + 1$. If $\Phi^A = A$ and for all $n$ we have $\Phi(A \upharpoonright n) \succcurlyeq A \upharpoonright n$, which we can ensure since the map bounds $f$, then we can recursively compute $A(n)$ by applying $\Phi$ to $A \upharpoonright n$, which we previously computed. For the second part, use a reduction $\Phi^B = A$ whose use function is the constant function 0. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.6)</span></p>

If $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ and $A$ is noncomputable, then $A <_\text{wtt} B$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $B \leqslant_\text{wtt} A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ then $B$ is computable, and hence so is $A$. $\square$

Thus, for instance, if $\deg_\text{wtt}(B)$ is minimal, then every $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$ is computable. In fact, something stronger is true.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.7 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

If there is a noncomputable $A$ such that $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$, then $B$ is high.

</div>

*Proof.* For any Turing reduction $\Phi$, if $\Phi^B$ is total, then the map $n \mapsto \lvert \Phi(B \upharpoonright n) \rvert$ is computable in $B$ (indeed, weak truth table reducible to $B$). For a $\Phi$ that witnesses that $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} B$, this map dominates every computable function. By Martin's Theorem 2.23.7, $B$ is high. $\square$

#### 12.3.3 Anti-Complex Sets and Tiny Uses

The following straightforward result shows that anti-complexity is invariant for wtt-degrees.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.8 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

A set $A$ is anti-complex iff $C(f(n)) \leqslant n + O(1)$ for every $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$.

</div>

We now come to our first characterization of anti-complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.9 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

A set is anti-complex iff its wtt-degree is c.e. traceable.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is anti-complex, and let $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$. By Lemma 12.3.8, there is a $c$ such that $C(f(n)) \leqslant n + c$ for all $n$. Let $T_n = \lbrace x : C(x) \leqslant n + c \rbrace$. Then $\lbrace T_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a c.e. trace for $f$ bounded by the order $h(n) = n$. We can think of $T^{[n]}$ as the set of strings of length at most $n+1$. We can think of $T_n$ as the $n+1$-th row; and $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{n+c+1}$ for all $n$. By altering finitely many $T_n$, we can get a c.e. trace for $f$ with bound $2^{2n}$. Thus $\deg_\text{wtt}(A)$ is c.e. traceable.

For the other direction, suppose that $\deg_\text{wtt}(A)$ is c.e. traceable, and let $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$. By Lemma 12.1.1, let $\lbrace T_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a c.e. trace for $f$ bounded by the order $h(n) = n$. Let $U$ be our universal machine. We can construct a machine $M$ that on input $\sigma$, first computes $U(\sigma)$, then, if this computation converges, interprets the result as a pair $(n, m)$, and if $m < n$, outputs the $m$th element enumerated into $T_n$. If $f(n)$ is the $m$th element enumerated into $T_n$, then $C(f(n)) \leqslant C_M(f(N)) + O(1) \leqslant C(n,m) + O(1) \leqslant 2C(n) + O(1) \leqslant 2 \log n + O(1)$, so the condition of Lemma 12.3.8 holds. $\square$

For the next characterization, we need a kind of monotone approximation to the function $n \mapsto C(A \upharpoonright n)$. Let $g_A(k)$ be the least $n$ such that $C(A \upharpoonright m) > k$ for all $m \geqslant n$. Clearly $g_A \leqslant_\text{T} A'$. Let $\mathcal{V}$ be our fixed universal (plain) machine. Recall that $\sigma_C^*$ is the first $\tau$ of length $C(\sigma)$ for which we see that $\mathcal{V}(\tau) = \sigma$. Let

$$A^* = \left\lbrace \left(A \upharpoonright g^A(k)\right)_C^* : k \in \mathbb{N} \right\rbrace.$$

Clearly $A^* \leqslant_\text{T} A'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.10 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

For every $A$, the map $k \mapsto (A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*$ is bounded by some computable function, where we identify a string with its position in the length-lexicographic ordering of $2^{<\omega}$.

</div>

*Proof.* Clearly there is a constant $c$ such that $C(\tau 0), C(\tau 1) \leqslant C(\tau) + c$ for all $\tau$. For any $k$, let $\tau_k$ be a binary string and $i < 2$ be such that $A \upharpoonright g_a(k) = \tau_k i$. By definition of $g_A(k)$, we have $C(\tau_k) \leqslant k$, and so $C(A \upharpoonright g_A(k)) \leqslant k + c$. Hence $(A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^* < 2^{k+c+1}$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.11 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) There is some set $B$ such that $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$.

(ii) $A$ is anti-complex.

(iii) $g_A$ is dominant.

(iv) $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} A^*$.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) implies (ii): Assume that $A \leqslant_{\text{T(tu)}} B$. Suppose that $f \leqslant_\text{wtt} A$, so there is a functional $\Gamma$ such that $\Gamma^A = f$ and the use of this computation is bounded by a computable function $g$. We can find a $\Phi$ such that $\Phi^B = A$ and $\Phi(B \upharpoonright n)$ is longer than $A \upharpoonright g(n)$ for all $n$. Then $C(f(n)) \leqslant C(\Phi(B \upharpoonright n)) + O(1) \leqslant C(B \upharpoonright n) + O(1) \leqslant n + O(1)$. By Lemma 12.3.8, $A$ is anti-complex.

(ii) implies (iii): Suppose that $A$ is anti-complex. Let $f$ be an increasing computable function. By definition, $C(A \upharpoonright f(n)) \leqslant n$ for almost all $n$. Hence, $g_A(n) > f(n)$ for almost all $n$. It follows that $g_A$ dominates every computable function.

(iii) implies (iv): For every $A$ we have $A \leqslant_\text{T} A^*$, because for our universal machine $\mathcal{V}$, we have $A = \bigcup \lbrace \mathcal{V}(\sigma) : \sigma \in A^* \rbrace$ (in other words, $A(x) = \mathcal{V}(\sigma)(x)$ for any $\sigma \in A^*$ such that $x < \lvert \mathcal{V}(\sigma) \rvert$), and for every $x$ there is indeed some $\sigma \in A^*$ such that $\lvert \mathcal{V}(\sigma) \rvert > x$. But if $g_A$ is dominant then this reduction witnesses that $A \leqslant_{\text{uT(tu)}} A^*$. To see this, let $\Phi$ be this reduction, and let $f$ be an increasing computable function. We need to show that $n \mapsto \lvert \Phi(A^* \upharpoonright n) \rvert$ dominates $f$.

Let $g$ be a computable function that dominates $k \mapsto (A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*$, which exists by Lemma 12.3.10. Since $g_A$ is dominant, $g_A(k) \geqslant f(g(k+1))$ for almost all $k$. Suppose that $k$ is large enough and that $(A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^* < n \leqslant (A \upharpoonright g_A(k+1))_C^*$. Then $n \leqslant g(k+1)$ and so $g_A(k) \geqslant f(n)$. But $\lvert \Phi(A^* \upharpoonright n) \rvert \geqslant g_A(k)$, since $(A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^* < n$, and from $(A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*$ we can compute $A \upharpoonright g_A(k)$. Thus $\lvert \Phi(A^* \upharpoonright n) \rvert \geqslant f(n)$ as required. $\square$

#### 12.3.4 Anti-Complex Sets and Schnorr Triviality

We have seen that Schnorr triviality is not invariant in the weak truth table degrees. However, Theorem 12.3.2 concerns the downward closure of the wtt-degrees containing Schnorr trivial sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.12 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

Every Schnorr trivial set is anti-complex.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be Schnorr trivial. Fix a computable order $h$. Let $\Phi$ be a wtt-functional with computable bound $g$ on its use function. The map $n \mapsto A \upharpoonright g(n)$ is tt-reducible to $A$, so by Theorem 12.2.14, there is a computable trace $\lbrace T_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ for this map that is bounded by $h$. If $f = \Phi^A$ is total then we can enumerate a trace $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ for $f$ (with bound $h$) by putting $\Phi^\sigma(n)$ into $S_n$ for those $\sigma \in T_n$ for which $\Phi^\sigma$ converges with domain greater than $n$. Hence $\deg_\text{wtt}(A)$ is c.e. traceable. By Lemma 12.3.9, $A$ is anti-complex. $\square$

Combining this result with Lemma 12.3.8, which shows that the class of anti-complex sets is closed downwards under wtt-reducibility, we see that if $A$ is wtt-reducible to a Schnorr trivial set, then it is anti-complex. Combined with Lemma 12.3.11, the following result establishes the converse, and hence completes the proof of Theorem 12.3.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.3.13 — Franklin, Greenberg, Stephan, and Wu)</span></p>

If $g_A$ is dominant then $A$ is wtt-reducible to some Schnorr trivial set.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f_0, f_1, \ldots$ be a sequence of total computable functions such that

1. each $f_i$ is strictly increasing;
2. the range of $f_{i+1}$ is contained in the range of $f_i$; and
3. every computable function is bounded by some $f_i$.

By Lemma 12.3.10, let $g$ be a computable function that bounds the function $k \mapsto (A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*$. For each $k > 0$, let $q_k = \langle (A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*, f_i(k) \rangle$, where $i$ is greatest such that $\langle g(k), f_i(k) \rangle \leqslant g_A(k-1)$. Note that $q_k \leqslant g_A(k-1)$ for all $k > 0$. Let $B = \lbrace q_k : k > 0 \rbrace$. We claim that $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} B$ and $B$ is Schnorr trivial.

For a fixed $n$, let $k$ be greatest such that $g_A(k) \leqslant n$. Then $q_{k+1} \leqslant g_A(k) \leqslant n$ and $A \upharpoonright g_A(k+1)$ can be effectively obtained from $q_{k+1}$. This procedure allows us to generate $A \upharpoonright n$ effectively from $B \upharpoonright n + 1$, so $A \leqslant_\text{wtt} B$.

To see that $B$ is Schnorr trivial, we appeal to Theorem 12.2.14. Here is where we use the fact that $g_A$ is dominant. The point is that, for every $i$, all but finitely many elements of $B$ are pairs whose second coordinates are contained in the range of $f_i$, which follows from the fact that the map $k \mapsto \langle g(k), f_{i}(k) \rangle$ is computable and so dominated by $g_A$: for all but finitely many $k$, we have $q_k = \langle (A \upharpoonright g_A(k))_C^*, f_{i'}(k) \rangle$ for some $i' \geqslant i$, and the range of $f_{i'}$ is contained in the range of $f_i$.

So let $\Psi$ be a truth table functional. There is some $i$ such that $f_i$ bounds the use function of $\Psi$. After specifying a fixed initial segment of $B$ (specifying those $q_{k'}$ whose second coordinates are not in the range of $f_i$), there are at most $2^{kg(k)}$ many possibilities for $B \upharpoonright f_i(k)$, because apart from finitely many fixed numbers, there are only $kg(k)$ many numbers below $f_i(k)$ that can be elements of $B$, as they all have the form $\langle p, f_i(m) \rangle$ for some $p < g(k)$ and $m < k$. After applying $\Psi$, we get a computable trace for $\Psi^B$ whose $k$th element has size at most $2^{kg(k)}$. So $\deg_\text{tt}(B)$ is computably traceable, and hence, by Theorem 12.2.14, $B$ is Schnorr trivial. $\square$

### 12.4 Lowness for Weak Genericity and Randomness

As in other cases, when considering weak 1-randomness, there are two reasonable notions: lowness for weak 1-randomness and the possibly stronger notion of lowness for Kurtz null tests. We begin with a result that sandwiches both notions of lowness between the hyperimmune-free sets and the computably traceable ones.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.1 — Downey, Griffiths, and Reid)</span></p>

(i) If a set is computably traceable then it is low for Kurtz null tests.

(ii) If a set is low for weak 1-randomness then it has hyperimmune-free degree.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) Let $A$ be computably traceable. Let $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Kurtz null test relative to $A$. Then there is an $A$-computable function $f : \omega \to (2^{<\omega})^{<\omega}$ such that $V_n = \llbracket f(n) \rrbracket$. Let $T$ be a computable trace for $f$ with bound $n \mapsto n + 1$. We can think of $T^{[n]}$ as a set of finite sets of strings. Let $g(n) = \bigcup \lbrace D \in T^{[n+1]} : \mu(\llbracket D \rrbracket) \leqslant 2^{-n} \rbrace$ and $V_n = \llbracket g(n) \rrbracket$. Then $\mu(V_n) \leqslant (n+2)2^{-(n+1)} \leqslant 2^{-n}$, so the $V_n$ form a Kurtz null test, and clearly $\bigcap_n V_n \supseteq \bigcap_n U_n$.

(ii) In Corollary 8.11.8, we proved that every hyperimmune degree is weakly 1-random, and clearly no weakly 1-random degree can be low for weak 1-randomness. $\square$

We will now work toward showing that lowness for weak 1-randomness and lowness for Kurtz null tests coincide, and that the sets that are low for weak 1-randomness can be completely characterized as those whose degrees are hyperimmune-free and not DNC. As we will see, these are also the sets that are **low for weak 1-genericity**, i.e., the sets $A$ such that if a set meets every dense $\Sigma_1^0$ class, then it meets every dense $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class.

Recall that a degree $\mathbf{a}$ is DNC if there is a function $f \leqslant_\text{T} \mathbf{a}$ such that $f(e) \neq \Phi_e(e)$ for all $e$. Recall also from Theorem 8.16.8 that Kjos-Hanssen, Merkle, and Stephan showed that if the degree of $B$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC, then for each $g \leqslant_\text{T} B$, there are computable $h$ and $\hat{h}$ such that $\forall n \, \exists m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)] \, (h(m) = g(m))$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.2 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

Suppose the degree of $A$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC.

(i) Every $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class of measure 1 has a $\Sigma_1^0$ subclass of measure 1.

(ii) Every dense $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class has a dense $\Sigma_1^0$ subclass.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $S$ be a $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class of measure 1. Note that $S$ is necessarily dense. We build a $\Sigma_1^0$ subclass of $S$ that has measure 1 and is dense. This construction establishes (i), and (ii) follows by the same proof with all measure considerations removed.

Define a function $f \leqslant_\text{T} A$ as follows. Given $n$, search for an $m > n$ such that every $\sigma \in 2^n$ is extended by some $\tau \in 2^m$ with $\llbracket \tau \rrbracket \in S$. (Note that every $m' > m$ will also have this property.) Then search for a $k \geqslant m$ such that $\mu(\bigcup \lbrace \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket : \sigma \in 2^k \wedge \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \subseteq S \rbrace) \geqslant 1 - 2^{-n}$. Let $f(n) = k$. Since $X$ has hyperimmune-free degree, there is a computable function $p$ such that $p(n) \geqslant f(n)$ for all $n$. Let $q(0) = 0$ and $q(n+1) = p(q(n))$.

Let $g : \mathbb{N} \to (2^{<\omega})^{<\omega}$ be an $A$-computable function such that for all $n$,

1. $g(n) \subseteq 2^{q(n+1)}$,
2. $\llbracket g(n) \rrbracket \subseteq S$,
3. for each $\sigma \in 2^{q(n)}$ there is a $\tau \in g(n)$ extending $\sigma$, and
4. $\mu(\llbracket g(n) \rrbracket) \geqslant 1 - 2^{-n}$.

By Theorem 8.16.8, there are computable functions $h : \mathbb{N} \to (2^{<\omega})^{<\omega}$ and $\hat{h} : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n$,

1. $h(n) \subseteq 2^{q(n+1)}$,
2. for each $\sigma \in 2^{q(n)}$ there is a $\tau \in h(n)$ extending $\sigma$,
3. $\mu(\llbracket h(n) \rrbracket) \geqslant 1 - 2^{-n}$, and
4. there is an $m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)]$ such that $h(m) = g(m)$.

Let

$$T = \lbrace X : \exists n \, \forall m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)] \, (X \upharpoonright q(m+1) \in h(m)) \rbrace.$$

Then $T$ is a $\Sigma_1^0$ class. Furthermore, for each $X \in T$, there is an $n$ and $m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)]$ such that $g(m) = h(m)$. Then $X \in \llbracket h(m) \rrbracket = \llbracket g(m) \rrbracket$, and therefore $T \subseteq S$.

The measure of $T$ is 1 because for all $n$,

$$\mu(\lbrace X : \forall m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)] \, (X \upharpoonright m \in h(m)) \rbrace)$$

$$= 1 - \mu\left(\bigcup_{m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)]} \overline{\llbracket h(m) \rrbracket}\right) \geqslant 1 - \sum_{m \in [n, \hat{h}(n)]} 2^{-m} > 1 - 2^{-n+1}.$$

Finally, $T$ is dense because given $\sigma \in 2^n$, there is an extension $\sigma'$ of $\sigma$ in $2^{q(n)}$. But then there is an extension $\tau_0$ of $\sigma'$ in $h(n)$. Since $h(n) \subseteq 2^{q(n+1)}$, there is an extension $\tau_1$ of $\tau_0$ in $h(n+1)$. Continuing in this way, we get a sequence $\sigma \prec \sigma' \prec \tau_0 \prec \tau_1 \prec \cdots$ such that $\bigcup_i \tau_i$ is an element of $T$ extending $\sigma$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.3 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

If a degree is hyperimmune-free and not DNC then it is low for Kurtz null tests and low for weak 1-genericity.

</div>

Stephan and Yu also completely characterized the sets that are low for weak 1-genericity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.4 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

(i) Every dense $\Sigma_1^{0,A}$ class has a dense $\Sigma_1^0$ subclass.

(ii) $A$ is low for weak 1-genericity.

(iii) The degree of $A$ is hyperimmune-free and every 1-generic is weakly 1-generic relative to $A$.

(iv) The degree of $A$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC.

</div>

*Proof.* Clearly (i) implies (ii), and (ii) implies (iii) by the $n = 0$ case of Theorem 2.24.14, which implies that every hyperimmune degree contains a weakly 1-generic set. Also, (iv) implies (i) by Theorem 12.4.2. To finish the proof, we show that if every 1-generic set is also weakly 1-generic relative to $A$ then the degree of $A$ is not DNC.

Suppose that $A$ is DNC and each 1-generic set is also weakly 1-generic relative to $A$. By Theorem 8.16.4, $A$ is autocomplex, so there is an $A$-computable function $f$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright m) \geqslant n$ for all $m \geqslant f(n)$. By replacing $f$ with the use function of its computation from $A$, we may assume that $f(n)$ can be computed from $A \upharpoonright f(n)$. Let

$$S = \lbrace \sigma^\frown(A \upharpoonright f(\lvert \sigma \rvert)) : \sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \rbrace.$$

Then $S$ is dense. By Theorem 2.24.2, each noncomputable c.e. set computes a 1-generic, so we can choose $B$ to be low for $K$ and 1-generic. By hypothesis, $B$ is weakly 1-generic relative to $A$, so $B$ meets $S$ infinitely often, and hence there are infinitely many $n$ with $(B \upharpoonright n)^\frown(A \upharpoonright f(n)) \prec B$. For such an $n$, we can compute $A \upharpoonright f(n)$ from $B \upharpoonright n + f(n)$ and $n$. Furthermore, with $B$ as an oracle, we can compute $f(n)$ from $n$. Thus, for such $n$,

$$n \leqslant K(A \upharpoonright f(n)) \leqslant K(B \upharpoonright n + f(n), n) + O(1)$$

$$\leqslant K^B(B \upharpoonright n + f(n), n) + O(1) \leqslant K^B(n) + O(1).$$

So there are infinitely many $n$ with $K^B(n) \geqslant n - O(1)$, which is a contradiction. $\square$

It may not be surprising that lowness for weak 1-randomness and lowness for weak 1-genericity are related, since both weak 1-randoms and weak 1-generics occur in each hyperimmune degree (as shown in Theorems 2.24.19 and 8.11.8). The following result shows that these notions of lowness in fact coincide. The original proof of this result was rather complex and used "svelte trees", but subsequently the simple proof we present was discovered by Miller (in giving a simple proof of Theorem 8.10.2).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.5 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

$A$ is low for weak 1-randomness iff $A$ is low for Kurtz null tests iff $A$ is low for weak 1-genericity. Thus, $A$ is low for weak 1-randomness iff $A$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC.

</div>

*Proof.* If the degree of $A$ is hyperimmune-free and not DNC, then, by Theorem 12.4.3, $A$ is both low for Kurtz null tests and low for weak 1-randomness.

By Theorems 12.4.1 and 12.4.4, if $A$ has hyperimmune degree then it is neither low for weak 1-randomness nor low weak 1-genericity.

If $A$ has DNC degree, then, by Theorem 12.4.4, $A$ is not low weak 1-genericity, while by Theorem 8.10.2, there is a 1-random set that is not weakly 1-random relative to $A$, so $A$ is also not low for weak 1-randomness. $\square$

One consequence of this characterization (which already follows from Corollary 12.4.3) is that lowness for weak 1-randomness and lowness for Schnorr randomness are different.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.6 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

There is a perfect $\Pi_1^0$ class of sets that are neither of DNC degree nor c.e. traceable.

</div>

Applying the hyperimmune-free basis theorem to this result (and keeping in mind that a c.e. traceable set of hyperimmune-free degree is computably traceable), we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.7 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

There are sets that are not computably traceable but are low for Kurtz null tests.

</div>

*Proof of Theorem 12.4.6.* We construct a $\Pi_1^0$ class $[P]$ in stages. For each $e$, we have the following requirements for all $A \in [P]$:

$$R_e : \exists n \, (\Phi_e^A(n) = \Phi_n(n))$$

(which ensure that $A$ does not compute a fixed-point free function, and hence does not have a DNC degree, by Theorem 2.22.1) and

$$Q_e : \Phi_e \text{ total and an order} \Rightarrow A \text{ is not c.e. traceable with order } \Phi_e.$$

For the $R$-requirements, we use the recursion theorem with parameters to find a computable function $g$ such that we can control $\Phi_{g(n)}(g(n))$ for all $n$. We will define $\Phi_{g(n)}(g(n))$ for infinitely many $n$ to ensure that for each $e$ and each $A \in [P]$ with $\Phi_e^A$ total, there is some $n$ such that $\Phi_e^A(g(n)) = \Phi_{g(n)}(g(n))$.

The strategy for $R_e$ is as follows. At a stage $s$ at which $R_e$ begins acting, we have pairwise incompatible strings $\sigma_0^s, \ldots, \sigma_m^s$ with $P_s = \bigcup_{i \leqslant m} \llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$. For each $i \leqslant m$ in parallel, we proceed as follows. Working in $\llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$, we pick a large number $d = g(n)$ for some $n$, and wait until there is some $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma_i^s$ with $\Phi_e^\tau(d) \downarrow$. Should this never happen then $\Phi_e^A$ is not total for all $A \in \llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$. If $\tau$ is found then we let $\Phi_d(d) = \Phi_e^\tau(d)$, and prune $P$ so that $P \cap \llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket \tau \rrbracket$, initializing all weaker priority requirements. Now $R_e$ is met within $P \cap \llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$.

The strategy for $Q_e$ is as follows. We define a function $\Psi$ such that $\Psi^A$ is total for all $A \in [P]$ if $\Phi_e$ is total and an order. We break $Q_e$ into subrequirements $Q_{e,i}$ with order $\Phi_e$ for $A \in [P]$. Each such subrequirement works to ensure that $W_i$ does not trace $\Psi^A$ with order $\Phi_e$. Working within a cone $\llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$ as above, we pick a large number $n$ and wait until $\Phi_e(n) \downarrow$. If this convergence ever happens, we choose a $\tau \succcurlyeq \sigma_i^s$ such that $\llbracket \tau \rrbracket$ is currently a subset of $P$ and prune $P$ by removing all elements of $\llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket \tau \rrbracket$. Let $m > \lvert \tau \rvert$ and let $\tau_0, \ldots, \tau_{2^m - 1}$ be the extensions of $\tau$ of length $m$. We ensure that no weaker priority strategy makes $[P] \cap \llbracket \tau_j \rrbracket = \emptyset$. For each $j < 2^m$, we let $\Psi_e^{\tau_j}(k) = 0$ for all $k < n$ for which $\Psi_e^{\tau_j}(k)$ is not yet defined, and let $\Psi_e^{\tau_j}(n) = j$. We also initialize all weaker priority requirements.

From this point on, as long as $\lvert W_i^{[n]} \rvert \leqslant \Phi_e(n)$, whenever $j < 2^m$ enters $W_i^{[n]}$, we prune $P$ to remove all of $\llbracket \tau_j \rrbracket$ from $[P]$ and initialize all weaker priority requirements. This action never removes all of $\llbracket \tau \rrbracket$ from $[P]$, and ensures that $Q_{e,i}$ is satisfied within $\llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$, since it guarantees that if $W_i$ is a trace with order $\Phi_e$ and $A \in [P] \cap \llbracket \sigma_i^s \rrbracket$, then $A \in \llbracket \tau_j \rrbracket$ for some $j \notin W_i^{[n]}$, whence $\Psi_i^A(n) = j \notin W_i^{[n]}$.

The remainder of the proof is a straightforward application of the finite injury method. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.8 — Stephan and Yu)</span></p>

There is a partial computable 0-1 valued function $\psi$ with coinfinite domain such that every set whose characteristic function extends $\psi$ is neither autocomplex nor c.e. traceable.

</div>

We end this section by noting that there are no noncomputable sets that are low for 1-genericity. (Lowness for 1-genericity and for weak 1-genericity were first investigated by Nitzpon, who showed that if $X$ is low for weak 1-genericity then $X$ has hyperimmune-free degree.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.4.9 — Greenberg and Miller [unpublished], Yu)</span></p>

If $X$ is low for 1-genericity then $X$ is computable.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $X$ is noncomputable and low for 1-genericity. By Theorem 2.24.4, there is a 1-generic $A$ such that $A \oplus X \equiv_\text{T} X'$. Then $A$ is 1-generic relative to $X$, so by the relativized form of Theorem 2.24.3, $X'' \equiv_\text{T} (A \oplus X)' \equiv_\text{T} A \oplus X' \equiv_\text{T} X'$, which is a contradiction. $\square$

### 12.5 Lowness for Computable Randomness

As we saw in Theorems 12.1.6 and 12.4.1, if a set is low for either Schnorr randomness or weak 1-randomness, then it is of hyperimmune-free degree. This fact may not be too surprising, since Schnorr randomness and weak 1-randomness are both related to total computable functions, as reflected in tests with uniformly computable measures. Martin-Löf randomness is concerned with c.e. objects, or partial computable functions, which may help explain why the corresponding lowness notion is more closely related to jump traceability than traceability.

What of computable randomness? Here the graded tests are *somewhat* computable, but the overall measure is not. As it turns out, the situation for computable randomness is dramatically different from either of the above cases. We already saw in Theorem 12.1.6 that sets that are low for computable randomness are of hyperimmune-free degree. In this section, we prove the following remarkable result of Nies, which verifies a conjecture of Downey.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.1 — Nies)</span></p>

If a set is low for computable randomness then it is computable.

</div>

We present a different proof from the one given by Nies. This proof is also due to Nies, and is included here with his permission. We will assume that all martingales in this section are rational-valued, which we have already seen in Proposition 7.1.2 we can do with no loss of generality for computable, or more generally $A$-computable, martingales.

The following lemma is obvious but quite useful. Following Nies, we call it the "nonascending path trick".

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.2)</span></p>

Let $M$ be a computable martingale. For each $\sigma$ and each $n > \lvert \sigma \rvert$, we can compute a string $\tau \succ \sigma$ such that $\lvert \tau \rvert = n$ and $M(\tau \upharpoonright k + 1) \leqslant M(\tau \upharpoonright k)$ for each $k$ with $\lvert \sigma \rvert \leqslant k < n - 1$.

</div>

Recall that we say that a martingale $M$ has the **savings property** if

$$\tau \succ \sigma \Rightarrow M(\tau) \geqslant M(\sigma) - 2.$$

We saw in the comments following Proposition 6.3.8 that we may assume without loss of generality that our martingales have the savings property.

A **martingale operator** is a Turing functional $L$ such that $L^X$ is a martingale for each oracle $X$. To prove Theorem 12.5.1, we will define a martingale operator $L$. Given a set $A$ that is low for computable randomness, we will apply the following purely combinatorial lemma to $N = L^A$ and the (non-effectively listed) family $B_0, B_1, \ldots$ of computable martingales with the savings property.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.3)</span></p>

Let $N$ be a martingale with $N(\lambda) \leqslant 1$. Let $B_0, B_1, \ldots$ be martingales with the savings property such that $S[N] \subseteq \bigcup_i S[B_i]$. Then there are $\sigma, d$, and a martingale $M = \sum_{i \leqslant n} q_i B_i$, with each $q_i \in \mathbb{Q}^{>0}$, such that $M(\sigma) < 2$ and $\forall \tau \succ \sigma \, (N(\tau) \downarrow \geqslant 2^d \Rightarrow M(\tau) \geqslant 2)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Assume for a contradiction that no such $M$ exists. We define a sequence of strings $\sigma_0 \prec \sigma_1 \prec \cdots$ and positive rationals $q_0, q_1, \ldots$ such that for all $n$, we have $N(\sigma_n) \geqslant 2^n - 1$ and $\sum_{i < n} q_i B_i(\sigma_n) < 2$.

Let $\sigma_0 = \lambda$ and $q_0 = \frac{1}{B_0(\lambda)}$. Suppose we are given $\sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_{n-1}$ and $q_0, \ldots, q_{n-1}$ with the required properties. Since $\sum_{i < n} q_i B_i(\sigma_{n-1}) < 2$, we can choose $q_n$ to be sufficiently small so that $\sum_{i \leqslant n} q_i B_i(\sigma_{n-1}) < 2$. Let $M = \sum_{i \leqslant n} q_i B_i$. Since we are assuming that the lemma does not hold, there is a $\tau \succ \sigma$ such that $N(\tau) \geqslant 2^n$ and $M(\tau) < 2$. Let $\sigma_n = \tau$.

Now let $X = \bigcup_n \sigma_n$. Then $N$ succeeds on $X$ (though, interestingly, not necessarily in the effective sense of Schnorr). On the other hand, for each $i$ and $n \geqslant i$, we have $q_i B_i(\sigma_n) < 2$. Since $B_i$ has the savings property, $\limsup_n B_i(X \upharpoonright n) < \infty$, so $B_i$ does not succeed on $X$. Thus $X \in S[N] \setminus \bigcup_i S[B_i]$, contrary to hypothesis. $\square$

We are now ready to prove that every set that is low for computable randomness is computable.

*Proof of Theorem 12.5.1.* Let $A$ be low for computable randomness. Let $B_0, B_1, \ldots$ be the family of all (total) computable martingales with the savings property. (Note that this listing is not effective.) Fix an effective listing $\lbrace \eta_m \rbrace_{m \geqslant 1}$ of all triples $(e, \sigma, d)$ with $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $e, d \in \mathbb{N}$. We think of $e$ as the index of a partial computable martingale $M_e$ and think of $\eta_m$ as a potential witness (with $M = M_e$) to the truth of Lemma 12.5.3 for a martingale $N$ that we will build.

We will build, independently but uniformly, rational valued martingale operators $L_m$ for each $m \geqslant 1$, with $L_m^X(\sigma) = 2^{-m}$ for all $X$ and all $\sigma \in 2^{\leqslant m}$, and let $L^X = \sum_{m \geqslant 1} L_m^X$. For each $\sigma$, the contributions of $L_m^X(\sigma)$ for $m > \lvert \sigma \rvert$ will add up to $2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert}$, so $L$ will be a rational valued martingale operator.

We will then let $N = L^A$. Our goal is to use the fact that $A$ is low for computable randomness (which follows from the fact that $A$ is low for computable randomness) to compute $A$. The computation procedure for $A$ will be based on a witness $\eta_m = (e, \sigma, d)$, where $M = M_e$ is total and $M, \sigma, d$ witness the truth of Lemma 12.5.3 for $N = L^A$.

The idea behind our procedure for computing $A$ is the following. Once $L$ is defined, if $\eta_m = (e, \sigma, d)$ is a witness to the truth of Lemma 12.5.3 for $N = L^A$, then let $M = M_e$ and consider the tree

$$T_m = \lbrace \gamma : \forall \tau \succ \sigma \, (L_m^\gamma(\tau) \downarrow \geqslant 2^d \Rightarrow M(\tau) \geqslant 2) \rbrace.$$

By the choice of $\eta_m$ and the fact that $L^A \geqslant L_m^A$, we see that $A$ is a path of $T_m$. Let $k = 2^{d+m}$, and let $E_k$ be the collection of $k$-element sets of strings of equal length. Let $\alpha$ range over elements of $E_k$. We write $\alpha_r$ for the $r$th element of $\alpha$ in lexicographic order, and identify $\alpha$ with the string $\alpha_0 \alpha_1 \ldots \alpha_{k-1}$. For each $\alpha$, we will ensure that $\alpha \not\subseteq T_m$ in an effective way. That is, given $\alpha$, we will be able to find an $s < k$ such that $\alpha_s \notin T_m$. This fact will allow us to define a computable tree $R \supseteq T_m$ of width less than $k$. Since every path of a tree of finite width is computable and $A$ is a path of $T_m \subseteq R$, this fact will imply that $A$ is computable.

Let $\sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_{k-1}$ be the strings of length $d + m$ in lexicographic order. We describe in more detail the strategy that, given $\alpha$, produces an $s$ such that $\alpha_s \notin T_m$. Suppose that $\rho \succcurlyeq \sigma$ is a string such that $M(\rho) < 2$, and no value $L_m^\gamma(\rho) = p$ has been defined for any $\rho' \preccurlyeq \rho$ (we call $\rho$ an $\alpha$-destroyer). In this case we may define $L_m(\rho) = 2^{-m}$ regardless of the oracle. For each $s < k$, let $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 0$ unless $\nu$ is compatible with $\sigma_s$. In that case, let $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 2^{-m + \lvert \nu \rvert}$ if $\nu \preccurlyeq \sigma_s$, and $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 2^d$ if $\sigma_s \preccurlyeq \nu$. We ensure that $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\rho \sigma_s)$ from $\rho$ on, by betting all our capital along $\rho \sigma_s$. Since $M(\rho) < 2$, by the nonascending path trick (Lemma 12.5.2) we can compute an $s$ such that $M(\rho \sigma_s) < 2$. Then $\rho \sigma_s$ witnesses that $\alpha_s \notin T_m$.

We want to carry out this strategy independently for different $\alpha$, so we assign to each $\alpha$ a string $\nu_\alpha$. Given $\eta_m = (e, \sigma, d)$, let $M = M_e$ and let

$$\widehat{M}(\nu) = \max \lbrace M(\nu') : \sigma \preccurlyeq \nu' \preccurlyeq \nu \rbrace.$$

The assignment function $G_m : E_k \to 2^{<\omega}$ mapping $\alpha$ to $\nu_\alpha$ will satisfy the following conditions.

1. The range of $G_m$ is an antichain of strings $\nu$ such that $\widehat{M}(\nu) < 2$.
2. The functions $G_m$ and $G_m^{-1}$ are uniformly partial computable over all $m$.
3. If $M_e(\sigma) < 2$ and $M$ is total, then $G_m$ is total.

We cannot quite apply the strategy above with $\rho = \nu_\alpha$, since we cannot tell whether $G_m$ is total, but if we find out the value of $\nu_\alpha$ at stage $s$, then we can use the nonascending path trick to find a $\rho \succ \nu_\alpha$ of length $s$ such that $\widehat{M}(\rho) < 2$. This $\rho$ will be our $\alpha$-destroyer.

The choice of $G_m$ is irrelevant as long as the above properties hold, so we defer defining $G_m$.

We define $L_m$ by declaring axioms of the form $L_m^\gamma(\rho) = p$, in such a way that

(a) $\lvert \gamma \rvert \leqslant \lvert \rho \rvert$, and given $\gamma$ and $\rho$ we can computably determine whether we ever declare an axiom $L^\gamma(\rho) = p$ for some $p$; and

(b) whenever distinct axioms $L^\gamma(\rho) = p$ and $L^\delta(\rho) = q$ are declared then $\gamma$ and $\delta$ are incompatible.

If some axiom $L_m^\gamma(\rho) = p$ has been declared for $\gamma \prec X$ (which can be determined computably, by (a)), then we let $L_m^X(\rho) = p$. Otherwise, we let $L_m^X(\rho)$ be the "default value" $2^{-m}$.

Given a string $\rho$, we check whether there is a $\nu \preccurlyeq \rho$ such that $G_m^{-1}(\nu) \downarrow$ in exactly $\lvert \rho \rvert$ many steps. If so, then let $\alpha = G_m^{-1}(\nu)$. (We assume the usual convention that it takes at least $\max(\lvert \nu \rvert, \lvert \alpha \rvert)$ many steps for $G_m^{-1}(\nu)$ to converge.) In this case, we declare axioms as follows (implementing the strategy outlined above): For each $\tau = \rho \nu$ and each $s < k$, let $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 0$ unless $\nu$ is compatible with $\sigma_s$. In that case, let $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 2^{-m + \lvert \nu \rvert}$ if $\nu \preccurlyeq \sigma_s$, and $L_m^{\alpha_s}(\tau) = 2^d$ if $\sigma_s \preccurlyeq \nu$.

We have concluded the construction of $L_m$. Clearly (a) holds. Furthermore, (b) holds because $\nu_\alpha$ form an antichain, we declare axioms $L_m^\gamma(\tau) = p$ with $\nu_\alpha \preccurlyeq \tau$ only if $\gamma \in \alpha$, and the elements of $\alpha$ are pairwise incompatible. Finally, $L^X$ is clearly a martingale for each oracle $X$.

Recall that $B_0, B_1, \ldots$ are the (total) computable martingales with the savings property. If $A$ is low for computable randomness, then $S[L^A] \subseteq \bigcup_i S[B_i]$. The linear combination $M$ obtained in Lemma 12.5.3 is computable.
