So the following lemma suffices to compute $A$, since, as explained above, the existence of the tree $R$ implies that $A$ is computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.4)</span></p>

Suppose $\eta_m = (e, \sigma, d)$, where $M = M_e$ is total and $M, \sigma, d$ witness the truth of Lemma 12.5.3 with $N = L^A$. Then there is a computable tree $R \supseteq T_m$ of width less than $k$.

</div>

*Proof.* We define the levels $R^{(j)}$ of $R$ recursively. Let $R^{(0)} = \lbrace \lambda \rbrace$. Let $j > 0$ and suppose we have determined $R^{(j-1)}$. Carry out the following procedure to determine $R^{(j)}$.

1. Let $F$ be the set of strings of length $j$ that extend strings in $R^{(j-1)}$ (so $\lvert F \rvert = 2 \lvert R^{(j-1)} \rvert$).

2. If $\lvert F \rvert \geqslant k$, then let $\alpha$ consist of the $k$ leftmost strings of $F$.

   (a) Compute $\nu_\alpha = G_m(\alpha)$, and let $s$ be the stage at which $G_m(\alpha) \downarrow$.

   (b) By the nonascending path trick, find $\rho \succ \nu_\alpha$ of length $s$ such that $\widehat{M}(\rho) < 2$.

   (c) Search for $r < k$ such that $\widehat{M}(\rho \sigma_r) < 2$. Remove $\alpha_r$ from $F$ and repeat step 2.

3. Once $\lvert F \rvert < k$, let $R^{(j)} = F$.

Clearly $R$ is computable and has width less than $k$. By the definition of $L_m$, for $r$ as above we have $L_m^{\alpha_r}(\rho \sigma_r) = 2^{-d}$, whence $\alpha_r \notin T_m$. Thus $R \supseteq T_m$. $\square$

To conclude the proof it remains to define $G_m$. Recall that $\widehat{M}(\nu) = \max \lbrace M(\nu') : \sigma \preccurlyeq \nu' \preccurlyeq \nu \rbrace$. We first prove a lemma using the following instance of Kolmogorov's Inequality: If $M(\tau) < b$ then

$$\mu_\tau(\lbrace \nu \succcurlyeq \tau : \widehat{M}(\nu) \geqslant b \rbrace) \leqslant \frac{M(\tau)}{b},$$

where $\mu_\tau(X) = 2^{\lvert \tau \rvert} \mu(X \cap \llbracket \tau \rrbracket)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.5)</span></p>

Given $\eta_m$, suppose that $M(\tau) < b$ for $b \in \mathbb{Q}$. Let $P = \lbrace \nu \succcurlyeq \tau : \widehat{M}(\nu) < b \rbrace$ and let $r$ be such that $2^{-r} \leqslant 1 - \frac{M_e(\tau)}{b}$. Then given $i$ we can compute $\nu^{(i)} \in P$ and the strings $\nu^{(i)}$ form an antichain. If $M$ is partial, then we can compute $\nu^{(i)}$ for each $i$ such that $M$ is defined for strings of length up to $i + r + 1$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose inductively that $\nu^{(q)}$ has been computed for $q < i$. Since $\sum_{q < i} 2^{-\lvert \nu^{(q)} \rvert} = 2^{-r}(1 - 2^{-i})$ and $2^{-r} \leqslant \mu_\tau(P)$, by Kolmogorov's Inequality, we can compute $\nu \in P$ such that $\lvert \nu \rvert = i + r + 1$ and $\nu_q \not\preccurlyeq \nu$ for all $q < i$. Let $\nu_i = \nu$. $\square$

To obtain $G_m$, let $b = 2$, and let $n_\alpha$ be a number greater than the length of each string in $\alpha$, assigned to $\alpha$ in an effective 1-1 way. Let $G_m(\alpha) = \nu^{(n_\alpha)}$, as given by the lemma with $\tau = \sigma$. Clearly the required properties hold. $\square$

Extending these techniques, Nies proved similar results about lowness for partial computable martingales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.5.6 — Nies)</span></p>

If every partial computably random set is computably random relative to $A$, then $A$ is computable.

</div>

Another notion of randomness whose corresponding lowness notion sits somewhere between computability and hyperimmune-freeness is Demuth randomness. Downey and Ng showed that if a set is low for Demuth randomness then it is hyperimmune-free, and they conjecture that such a set must in fact be computable.

### 12.6 Lowness for Pairs of Randomness Notions

Recall from Definition 11.9.2 that for classes $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ given by relativizable definitions, $\text{Low}(\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{D})$ is the class of sets $A$ such that $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{D}^A$. We saw in Theorem 11.9.3 that $\text{Low}(\text{W2R}, \text{MLR})$ is exactly the class of $K$-trivial sets, where MLR is the class of 1-random sets and W2R is the class of weakly 2-random sets. We also saw in Section 10.6 that, writing ML2R for the class of 2-random sets, $\text{Low}(\text{ML2R}, \text{MLR})$ is the class of (uniformly) almost everywhere dominating sets.

There has been considerable work on characterizing lowness for other pairs of randomness notions. In this brief section, we state some results in that direction and summarize them, together with Theorems 11.4.11, 11.9.1, 11.9.3, 12.4.5, and 12.5.1 and Corollary 12.1.7, in Table 12.1. We write CompR, SchR, and W1R for the classes of sets that are computably random, Schnorr random, and weakly 1-random, respectively.

| | weak 2- | 1- | comp. | Schnorr | weak 1- |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| weak 2- | $K$-triv. | | | | ? |
| 1- | X | $K$-triv. | c.e. trac. | | |
| comp. | X | X | comp. | comp. trac. | $\neg$DNC nonhigh |
| Schnorr | X | X | X | comp. trac. | $\neg$DNC nonhigh |
| weak 1- | X | X | X | X | $\neg$DNC hyp.-fr. |

*Table 12.1. Lowness for pairs of randomness notions*

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.6.1 — Nies)</span></p>

A set is in $\text{Low}(\text{MLR}, \text{CompR})$ iff it is $K$-trivial.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.6.2 — Kjos-Hanssen, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

A set is in $\text{Low}(\text{MLR}, \text{SchR})$ iff it is c.e. traceable, and in $\text{Low}(\text{CompR}, \text{SchR})$ iff it is computably traceable.

</div>

Note that the first part of the above result is Theorem 12.1.4 (given Corollary 12.1.7).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.6.3 — Nies)</span></p>

A set is in $\text{Low}(\text{W2R}, \text{CompR})$ iff it is $K$-trivial.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.6.4 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

A set is contained in $\text{Low}(\text{MLR}, \text{W1R})$ iff its degree is not DNC, and in $\text{Low}(\text{CompR}, \text{W1R}) = \text{Low}(\text{SchR}, \text{W1R})$ iff its degree is not DNC and not high.

</div>

Note that the first part of the above result is part of Theorem 8.10.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(12.6.5 — Bienvenu and Miller)</span></p>

A set is in $\text{Low}(\text{W2R}, \text{SchR})$ iff it is c.e. traceable.

</div>

At the time of writing, there is no known exact characterization of $\text{Low}(\text{W2R}, \text{W1R})$.

There has also been work on highness for randomness notions, a concept introduced by Franklin, Stephan, and Yu. A survey of lowness and highness for randomness notions may be found in Franklin.

## 13. Algorithmic Dimension

### 13.1 Classical Hausdorff Dimension

Not all classes of measure 0 are created equal, and the classical theory of dimension provides a method for classifying them. Likewise, some nonrandom sets are more random than others. In this chapter, we look at effectivizations of Hausdorff dimension and other notions of dimension, and explore their relationships with calibrating randomness.

The study of measure as a way of specifying the size of sets began with work of Borel and others in the late 19th century. In his famous thesis, Lebesgue introduced the measure that is now called Lebesgue measure. In 1914, Carathéodory gave a more general construction that included Lebesgue measure as a special case. For $\mathbb{R}$, Carathéodory's definition yields the **$s$-dimensional measure** $\mu^s(A) = \inf \lbrace \sum_i \lvert I_i \rvert^s : A \subseteq \bigcup_i I_i \rbrace$, where each $I_i$ is an interval. In 1919, Hausdorff used $s$-dimensional measure to generalize the notion of dimension to non-integer values.

This idea of changing the way we measure open sets by an additional factor in the exponent is realized in Cantor space as follows. For $0 \leqslant s \leqslant 1$, the **$s$-measure** of a clopen set $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ is $\mu_s(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$. Notice the 1-measure is the same as the uniform measure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.1.1)</span></p>

(i) A set of strings $D$ is an **$n$-cover** of $R \subseteq 2^\omega$ if $R \subseteq \llbracket D \rrbracket$ and $D \subseteq 2^{\geqslant n}$.

(ii) Let $H_n^s(R) = \inf \lbrace \sum_{\sigma \in D} \mu_s(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) : D \text{ is an } n\text{-cover of } R \rbrace$.

(iii) The **$s$-dimensional outer Hausdorff measure** of $R$ is $H^s(R) = \lim_n H_n^s(R)$.

</div>

This notion has been widely studied. The fundamental result is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.1.2)</span></p>

For each $R \subseteq 2^\omega$ there is an $s \in [0, 1]$ such that

(i) $H^t(R) = 0$ for all $t > s$ and

(ii) $H^u(R) = \infty$ for all $0 \leqslant u < s$.

</div>

*Proof.* For all $s, r \in [0, 1]$, and $n \in \mathbb{N}$,

$$H_n^{s+r}(R) = \inf \left\lbrace \sum_{\sigma \in D} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} 2^{-r \lvert \sigma \rvert} : D \text{ is an } n\text{-cover of } R \right\rbrace \leqslant 2^{-rn} H_n^s(R).$$

So if $H^s(R) = 0$ then $H_n^{s+r}(R) = 0$, while if $H_n^{s+r}(R) = \infty$ then $H^s(R) = \infty$.

To complete the proof, notice that if $t > 1$ then $\lim_n H_n^t(R) \leqslant \lim_n \sum_{\sigma \in 2^n} 2^{-t \lvert \sigma \rvert} = \lim_n 2^n 2^{-tn} = 0$. $\square$

Theorem 13.1.2 means that the following definition makes sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.1.3)</span></p>

For $R \subseteq 2^\omega$, the **Hausdorff dimension** of $R$ is $\dim_\text{H}(R) = \inf \lbrace s : H^s(R) = 0 \rbrace$.

</div>

Hausdorff dimension has a number of important basic properties.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.1.4)</span></p>

(i) If $\mu(X) > 0$ then $\dim_\text{H}(X) = 1$.

(ii) *(monotonicity)* If $X \subseteq Y$ then $\dim_\text{H}(X) \leqslant \dim_\text{H}(Y)$.

(iii) *(countable stability)* If $P$ is a countable collection of subsets of $2^\omega$, then $\dim_\text{H}(\bigcup_{X \in P} X) = \sup_{X \in P} \dim_\text{H}(X)$. In particular, $\dim_\text{H}(X \cup Y)$ is $\max \lbrace \dim_\text{H}(X), \dim_\text{H}(Y) \rbrace$.

</div>

*Proof sketch.* This proposition is well known, but its proof is worth sketching. We begin with a lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.1.5)</span></p>

If $X$ is measurable then $H^1(X) = \mu(X)$.

</div>

*Proof.* By definition, $\mu(X)$ is the infimum of $\sum_{\sigma \in U} 2^{-\sigma}$ over all covers $U$ of $X$. For any cover $U$ of $X$, we can replace any $\sigma \in U$ such that $\lvert \sigma \rvert < n$ by all its extensions of length $n$ to obtain an $n$-cover $V$ of $X$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in V} 2^{-\sigma} = \sum_{\sigma \in U} 2^{-\sigma}$, so $H_n^1(X) = \mu(X)$ for all $n$. $\square$

Part (i) of Proposition 13.1.4 follows immediately from the lemma. To see that part (ii) holds, take any $s > \dim_\text{H}(Y)$. Then since any $n$-cover of $Y$ is an $n$-cover of $X$, we have $H_n^s(X) \leqslant H_n^s(Y)$ for all $n$, so $H^s(X) = 0$. Part (iii) is proved by a similar manipulation of covers. $\square$

### 13.2 Hausdorff Dimension via Gales

The first person to effectivize Hausdorff dimension explicitly was Lutz. To do so, he used a generalization of the notion of (super)martingale. This idea was, however, in a sense implicit in the work of Schnorr, who used orders to calibrate the rates of success of martingales, as we have seen in Chapter 7, in much the same way that the $s$ factor calibrates the growth rate of the measure of covers. Recall that, for a (super)martingale $d$ and an order $h$, the $h$-success set of $d$ is $S_h[d] = \lbrace A : \limsup_n \frac{d(A \upharpoonright n)}{h(n)} = \infty \rbrace$. As we will see, the theory of Hausdorff dimension can be developed in terms of orders on martingales, but Lutz originally used the following notions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.2.1 — Lutz)</span></p>

An **$s$-gale** is a function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $d(\sigma) = 2^{-s}(d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1))$ for all $\sigma$.

An **$s$-supergale** is a function $d : 2^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $d(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{-s}(d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1))$ for all $\sigma$.

We define the success set $S[d]$ of an $s$-(super)gale in the same way as for martingales.

</div>

A 1-(super)gale is the same as a (super)martingale. For $s < 1$, we can think of an $s$-gale as capturing the idea of betting in an inflationary environment, in which not betting costs us money. In the case of martingales, if we are not prepared to favor one side or the other in our bet following $\sigma$, we just make $d(\sigma i) = d(\sigma)$ for $i = 0, 1$, and are assured of retaining our current capital. In the case of an $s$-gale with $s < 1$, we are no longer able to do so. If we want to have $d(\sigma 0) = d(\sigma 1)$, then we will have $d(\sigma i) < d(\sigma)$ for $i = 0, 1$, and hence will necessarily lose money.

It is quite easy to go between $s$-gales and rates of success of martingales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.2.2)</span></p>

For each (super)martingale $d$ there is an $s$-(super)gale $\widehat{d}$ such that $S[\widehat{d}] = S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$. Conversely, for each $s$-(super)gale $\widehat{d}$ there is a (super)martingale $d$ such that $S[\widehat{d}] = S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$.

</div>

*Proof.* For a (super)martingale $d$, let $\widehat{d}$ be defined by $\widehat{d}(\sigma) = 2^{(s-1)\lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\sigma)$. It is easy to check that $\widehat{d}$ is an $s$-(super)gale. For each $k$, we have $\frac{d(A \upharpoonright n)}{2^{(1-s)n}} = 2^{(s-1)n} d(A \upharpoonright n) > k$ iff $\widehat{d}(A \upharpoonright n) = 2^{(s-1)n} d(A \upharpoonright n) > k$, so $S[\widehat{d}] = S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$. The converse is symmetric. $\square$

Note that, in the above proof, if $s$ is rational then we can pass effectively from $d$ to $\widehat{d}$ or vice versa, so, for instance, $d$ is c.e. iff $\widehat{d}$ is c.e.

Lutz' key insight was that Hausdorff dimension can be quite naturally characterized using either $s$-gales or growth rates of martingales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.2.3 — Lutz)</span></p>

For a class $X \subseteq 2^\omega$ the following are equivalent.

(i) $\dim_\text{H}(X) = r$.

(ii) $r = \inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : X \subseteq S[d] \text{ for some } s\text{-(super)gale } d \rbrace$.

(iii) $r = \inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : X \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d] \text{ for some (super)martingale } d \rbrace$.

</div>

*Proof.* By Lemma 13.2.2, (ii) and (iii) are equivalent.

We first prove that $\dim_\text{H}(X) \geqslant \inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : X \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$ for some (super)martingale $d \rbrace$. We first consider the supermartingale formulation.

Let $s > \dim_\text{H}(X)$ and let $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ witness that $X$ is $H^s$-null. That is, for all $k$ we have $X \subseteq \llbracket U_k \rrbracket$ and $\sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}$. We may assume that the $U_k$ are prefix-free. For each $\sigma$ and $k$, let $U_k^\sigma = \lbrace \tau \in U_k : \sigma \preccurlyeq \tau \rbrace$. For each $k$, let

$$d_k(\sigma) = 2^{\lvert \sigma \rvert} \sum_{\tau \in U_k^\sigma} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert},$$

and let

$$d(\sigma) = \sum_k d_k(\sigma).$$

If $\sigma \in U_k$, then $U_k^{\sigma i} = \emptyset$ for $i = 0, 1$, so $d_k(\sigma 0) + d_k(\sigma 1) = 0 \leqslant 2 d_k(\sigma)$. If $\sigma \notin U_k$, then $U_k^\sigma = U_k^{\sigma 0} \cup U_k^{\sigma 1}$, so

$$d_k(\sigma 0) + d_k(\sigma 1) = 2^{\lvert \sigma \rvert + 1} \left(\sum_{\tau \in U_k^{\sigma 0}} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} + \sum_{\tau \in U_k^{\sigma 1}} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert}\right) = 2 \cdot 2^{\lvert \sigma \rvert} \left(\sum_{\tau \in U_k^\sigma} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert}\right) = 2 d_k(\sigma).$$

Thus each $d_k$ is a supermartingale. Furthermore, $d_k(\lambda) = \sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}$, so $d$ is a supermartingale.

Now suppose that $A \in \bigcap_k \llbracket U_k \rrbracket$. Then for each $k$ there is an $n_k$ such that $A \upharpoonright n_k \in U_k$. Let $t > s$. Then

$$\frac{d(A \upharpoonright n_k)}{2^{(1-t)n_k}} \geqslant \frac{d_k(A \upharpoonright n_k)}{2^{(1-t)n_k}} = \frac{2^{(1-s)n_k}}{2^{(1-t)n_k}} = 2^{(t-s)n_k},$$

so $\limsup_n \frac{d(A \upharpoonright n)}{2^{(1-t)n}} = \infty$, and hence $A \in S_{2^{(1-t)n}}[d]$. Since $t > s > \dim_\text{H}(X)$ are arbitrary, $\dim_\text{H}(X) \geqslant \inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : X \subseteq S[d]$ for some $s$-(super)gale $d \rbrace$.

To get the same result for martingales, we slightly change the definition of $d_k$ to

$$d_k(\sigma) = \begin{cases} 2^{\lvert \sigma \rvert} \sum_{\tau \in U_k^\sigma} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} & \text{if } U_k^\sigma \neq \emptyset \\ 2^{(1-s)m} & \text{if } \sigma \upharpoonright m \in U_k \text{ for } m < \lvert \sigma \rvert \\ 0 & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

We now prove that $\dim_\text{H}(X) \leqslant \inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : X \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$ for some supermartingale $d \rbrace$. Suppose that $d$ is a supermartingale and $X \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$. We may assume that $d(\lambda) \leqslant 1$. Let

$$V_k = \left\lbrace \sigma : \frac{d(\sigma)}{2^{(1-s)\lvert \sigma \rvert}} \geqslant 2^k \right\rbrace.$$

Let $U_k$ be the set of $\sigma \in V_k$ such that $\tau \notin V_k$ for $\tau \prec \sigma$. Then the $U_k$ are prefix-free and cover $X$. Furthermore,

$$\sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \frac{d(\sigma)}{2^{(1-s)\lvert \sigma \rvert}} 2^{-k} = 2^{-k} \sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\sigma) \leqslant 2^{-k},$$

the last inequality following by Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3). Thus $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ witnesses the fact that $X$ is $H^s$-null. $\square$

Lutz made the following remarks about the above characterization: "Informally speaking, the above theorem says that the dimension of a set is the *most hostile environment* (i.e., the most unfavorable payoff schedule, i.e., the infimum $s$) in which a single betting strategy can *achieve infinite winnings* on every element of the set."

### 13.3 Effective Hausdorff Dimension

Using the martingale characterization of Hausdorff dimension, we can effectivize that notion as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.3.1 — Lutz)</span></p>

For a complexity class $\mathcal{C}$ of real-valued functions, the **$\mathcal{C}$ (Hausdorff) dimension** of $R \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : R \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d] \text{ for some martingale } d \in \mathcal{C} \rbrace.$$

For $A \in 2^\omega$, the $\mathcal{C}$ dimension of $A$ is the $\mathcal{C}$ dimension of $\lbrace A \rbrace$.

</div>

We will be particularly interested in the case $\mathcal{C} = \Sigma_1^0$. The $\Sigma_1^0$ Hausdorff dimension of $R$ is sometimes called the **constructive (Hausdorff) dimension** of $R$, but we will refer to it as the **effective Hausdorff dimension**, or simply the **effective dimension**, of $R$, and denote it by $\dim(R)$.

In Lutz' original paper, he defined $\mathcal{C}$ dimension using gales, but in the journal version, he used supergales. The issue here is one of multiplicative optimality versus universality. Let us consider the $\Sigma_1^0$ case. By Theorem 6.3.5, there is a universal c.e. martingale, but while there is an optimal c.e. supermartingale (Theorem 6.3.7), there is no optimal c.e. *martingale* (Corollary 6.3.12). As noted by Hitchcock, Lutz, and others, it is not known whether there is always a c.e. $r$-gale that is universal for the class of all c.e. $r$-supergales. However, we have the following result, which shows that if $r < t$ then there is a $t$-gale that is optimal for the class of all $r$-supergales. Thus gales suffice for defining effective dimension, and Lutz' original formulation in terms of gales is equivalent to the one in terms of supergales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.3.2 — Hitchcock)</span></p>

Let $0 \leqslant r < t$ be rationals. There is a c.e. $t$-gale $d^*$ such that for all c.e. $r$-supergales $d$, we have $S[d] \subseteq S[d^*]$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $d$ be a multiplicatively optimal c.e. $r$-supergale with $d(\lambda) < 1$ (constructed as in the proof of Theorem 6.3.7). It is enough to build $d^*$ so that $S[d] \subseteq S[d^*]$.

Let $A = \lbrace \sigma : d(\sigma) > 1 \rbrace$ and let $A^{[n]} = A \cap 2^n$. By Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3), $\lvert A^{[n]} \rvert \leqslant 2^{rn}$ for all $n$. Let $d_n^*$ be defined by

$$d_n^*(\sigma) = \begin{cases} 2^{-t(n - \lvert \sigma \rvert)} \lvert \lbrace \tau \in A^{[n]} : \sigma \preccurlyeq \tau \rbrace \rvert & \text{if } \lvert \sigma \rvert \leqslant n \\ 2^{(t-1)(\lvert \sigma \rvert - n)} d_n^*(\sigma \upharpoonright (n-1)) & \text{if } \lvert \sigma \rvert > n. \end{cases}$$

Then it is easy to check that each $d_n^*$ is a $t$-gale and $d_n^*(\sigma) = 1$ for all $\sigma \in A^{[n]}$.

Now let $s \in (r, t)$ be rational and let

$$d^* = \sum_n 2^{(s-r)n} d_n^*.$$

Then $d^*(\lambda) = \sum_n 2^{(s-r)n} 2^{-tn} \lvert A^{[n]} \rvert \leqslant \sum_n 2^{(s-t)n} < \infty$, so $d^*$ is well-defined, and hence is a $t$-gale. Furthermore, it is c.e., since $A$ is c.e.

Let $X \in S[d]$. Then $X \upharpoonright (n-1) \in A$ for infinitely many $A$. For such $n$,

$$d^*(X \upharpoonright (n-1)) \geqslant 2^{(s-r)n} d_n^*(X \upharpoonright (n-1)) = 2^{(s-r)n}.$$

Hence $X \in S[d^*]$. $\square$

Of course, this result translates into the language of martingales. Say that a (super)martingale $d$ **$s$-succeeds** on $A$ if $\limsup_n \frac{d(A \upharpoonright n)}{2^{(1-s)n}} = \infty$. We can build a c.e. martingale that $(s + \varepsilon)$-succeeds on every set $A$ for which there is a c.e. supermartingale that $s$-succeeds on $A$. However, we do not know whether for each such $A$ there necessarily is a martingale that $s$-succeeds on $A$.

In any case, $\dim(R)$ is equal to $\inf \lbrace s \in \mathbb{Q} : R \subseteq S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d] \rbrace$, where $d$ is an optimal c.e. supermartingale. It follows immediately that $\dim(R) = \sup \lbrace \dim(A) : A \in R \rbrace$ and, more generally, the effective dimension of an arbitrary union of classes is the supremum of the effective dimensions of the individual classes. (Notice that for classical Hausdorff dimension, the corresponding fact is true only for countable unions.)

We can also effectivize Definition 13.1.1 directly. For $\Sigma_1^0$ dimension, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.3.3)</span></p>

(i) A set of strings $D$ is an **effective $n$-cover** if it is an $n$-cover and c.e.

(ii) Let $\widehat{H}_n^s(R) = \inf \lbrace \sum_{\sigma \in D} \mu_s(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) : D \text{ is an effective } n\text{-cover of } R \rbrace$.

(iii) The **effective $s$-dimensional outer Hausdorff measure** of $R$ is $\widehat{H}^s(R) = \lim_n \widehat{H}_n^s(R)$.

(iv) The **effective Hausdorff dimension** of $R$ is the unique $s \in [0, 1]$ such that $\widehat{H}^t(R) = 0$ for all $t > s$ and $\widehat{H}^u(R) = \infty$ for all $0 \leqslant u < s$.

</div>

It is straightforward to effectivize our earlier work and show that the effective analog of Theorem 13.2.3 holds. That is, the above definition agrees with our earlier definition of effective Hausdorff dimension. A similar comment holds for other suitable complexity classes.

There is another fundamental characterization of effective Hausdorff dimension, in terms of initial segment complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.3.4 — Mayordomo)</span></p>

For $A \in 2^\omega$, we have

$$\dim(A) = \liminf_n \frac{K(A \upharpoonright n)}{n} = \liminf_n \frac{C(A \upharpoonright n)}{n}.$$

</div>

*Proof.* The second equation is immediate, since $C$ and $K$ agree up to a log factor. We prove the first equation.

First suppose that $\dim(A) < s$. Let $d$ be a universal c.e. supermartingale. Then $\limsup_n \frac{d(A \upharpoonright n)}{2^{(1-s)n}} = \infty$. Now, as noted in Section 6.3.2, $d(A \upharpoonright n) = 2^{n - K_M(A \upharpoonright n) + O(1)}$, so $\limsup_n 2^{sn - K(A \upharpoonright n) + O(\log n)} = \infty$. But by Theorem 3.15.4, $K(A \upharpoonright n) \leqslant KM(A \upharpoonright n) + O(\log n)$, so $\limsup_n 2^{sn - K(A \upharpoonright n) + O(\log n)} = \infty$. Thus there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) < sn + O(\log n)$, and hence $\liminf_n \frac{K(A \upharpoonright n)}{n} \leqslant s$.

Now suppose that $\liminf_n \frac{K(A \upharpoonright n)}{n} < r < s$ for rationals $r$ and $s$. Let $D = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) < r \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace$. Then $S$ is c.e., and by Theorem 3.7.6, $\lvert D \cap 2^n \rvert \leqslant O(2^{rn - K(n)})$. Let

$$d(\sigma) = 2^{(s-r)\lvert \sigma \rvert} \left(\sum_{\sigma \tau \in D} 2^{-r \lvert \tau \rvert} + \sum_{\nu \in D \wedge \nu \prec \sigma} 2^{(r-1)(\lvert \sigma \rvert - \lvert \nu \rvert)} \right).$$

Then $d(\lambda) \leqslant \sum_{\tau \in D} 2^{-r \lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant O(\sum_n 2^{rn - K(n)} 2^{-rn}) = O(\sum_n 2^{-K(n)}) < \infty$, and a straightforward calculation shows that $d$ is an $s$-gale. Since $D$ is c.e., so is $d$. There are infinitely many $n$ such that $A \upharpoonright n \in D$. For any such $n$, we have $d(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant 2^{(s-r)n}$, so $d$ succeeds on $A$, and hence $\dim(A) \leqslant s$. $\square$

One consequence of this characterization is that if $A$ has positive effective Hausdorff dimension, then $A$ is complex, since if $\dim(A) > r > 0$, then $C(A \upharpoonright n) > rn$ for almost all $n$.

The Kolmogorov complexity characterization of effective dimension also allows us to easily produce sets with fractional effective dimension. Let $A$ be 1-random. Then $B = A \oplus 0^\omega$ has effective dimension $\frac{1}{2}$, since $K(B \upharpoonright n) = K(A \upharpoonright \lfloor \frac{n}{2} \rfloor) \pm O(1)$, and hence $\liminf_n \frac{K(B \upharpoonright n)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}$.

Recall the following definition. Given an infinite and coinfinite set $X$, let $x_0 < x_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $X$, let $y_0 < y_1 < \cdots$ be the elements of $\overline{X}$, and define $C \oplus_X D$ to be $\lbrace x_n : n \in C \rbrace \cup \lbrace y_n : n \in D \rbrace$. For a computable real $r \in [0, 1]$, we can find a computable $X$ such that $\lim_n \frac{\lvert X \cap [0, n) \rvert}{n} = r$. Then it is easy to check that $A \oplus_X 0^\omega$ has effective dimension $r$. Thus we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.3.5 — Lutz)</span></p>

For every computable real $s$, there is a set of effective Hausdorff dimension $s$.

</div>

Clearly, every 1-random set has effective dimension 1, but if we take $r = 1$ in the above construction, then we have an example of a set of effective dimension 1 that is not 1-random.

Schnorr considered exponential orders on martingales, and hence was in a sense implicitly studying dimension. Satz 17.1 of Schnorr then says that for any measure 0 set $A$ the following are equivalent.

1. There are a computable martingale $d$ and an $a > 1$ such that $A$ is contained in $\lbrace X : \limsup_n \frac{d(X \upharpoonright n)}{a^n} > 0 \rbrace$.

2. There are a Schnorr test $\lbrace \llbracket U_n \rrbracket \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ and a $b > 0$ such that $A$ is contained in $\lbrace X : \limsup_n m_U(X \upharpoonright n) - bn > 0 \rbrace$, where $m_U$ is a "Niveaufunktion" from strings to numbers defined by $m_U(\sigma) = \min \lbrace n : \sigma \in U_n \rbrace$.

This result is a test characterization saying something about the speed at which a set is covered, and resembles characterizations of effective Hausdorff dimension.

In this chapter we will focus on the $\Sigma_1^0$ notion of effective dimension, but as with notions of algorithmic randomness, there are many possible variations. For instance, we can vary the arithmetic complexity of the gales used in the definition of dimension. In Section 13.15, we will examine the effect of replacing $\Sigma_1^0$ gales by computable ones, to yield a notion of computable dimension. We can also take the test set approach and study variations on that, for instance Schnorr dimension, which turns out to be equivalent to computable dimension, as we will see in Section 13.15.

Some known relationships between notions of randomness and dimension are summarized in the following diagram. Here $\Delta_2^0$ randomness is the relativization of computable randomness to $\emptyset'$ (so not the same as 2-randomness), and similarly for Schnorr $\Delta_2^0$ randomness and $\Delta_2^0$ dimension.

$$\Delta_2^0 \text{ random} \Downarrow$$

$$\text{Schnorr } \Delta_2^0 \text{ random} \implies \Delta_2^0 \text{ dimension } 1$$

$$\Downarrow \qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad \Downarrow$$

$$\text{1-random} \implies \text{effective dimension } 1$$

$$\Downarrow \qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad \Downarrow$$

$$\text{computably random} \qquad\qquad\quad$$

$$\Downarrow$$

$$\text{Schnorr random} \implies \text{computable dimension } 1$$

No other implications hold than the ones indicated.

### 13.4 Shift Complex Sets

Not every set of high effective Hausdorff dimension is a "watered down" version of a 1-random set.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.4.1)</span></p>

Let $0 < d < 1$. A set $A$ is **$d$-shift complex** if for every $m < n$, we have $K(A \upharpoonright [m, n]) \geqslant d(n - m) - O(1)$.

</div>

If $A$ is $d$-shift complex then $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant dn - O(1)$, so the effective Hausdorff dimension of $A$ is at least $d$. On the other hand, a 1-random set cannot be $d$-shift complex for any $d > 0$, since it must contain arbitrarily long sequences of 0's. Thus Theorem 13.4.3 below, which states that $d$-shift complex sets exist for every $d \in (0, 1)$, shows that sets of high effective dimension can be quite different from 1-random sets. (Note that any 1-shift complex set would be 1-random, and hence no such sets exist.)

We say that a set $A$ **avoids** a string $\sigma$ if there are no $m < n$ such that $\sigma = A \upharpoonright [m, n]$. Theorem 13.4.3 also follows from a stronger result due to Rumyantsev and Ushakov, who showed that if $c < 1$ and for each $n$, we have a set $F_n \subset 2^n$ with $\lvert F_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{cn}$, then there is an $A$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$, the set $A$ avoids every element of $F_n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.4.2 — Miller)</span></p>

Let $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ and $c \in (\frac{1}{2}, 1)$ be such that $\sum_{\tau \in S} c^{\lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant 2c - 1$. Then there is an $A$ that avoids every element of $S$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $p = \sum_{\tau \in S} c^{\lvert \tau \rvert}$. Since $p < 1$, we have $\lambda \notin S$. Let

$$w(\sigma) = \sum \lbrace c^{\lvert \rho \rvert} : \exists \tau \in S \, (\lvert \rho \rvert < \lvert \tau \rvert \wedge \sigma \rho \text{ ends in } \tau) \rbrace.$$

We think of $w(\sigma)$ as measuring the threat that an extension of $\sigma$ ends in an element of $S$. Note in particular that if $\sigma$ itself ends in some $\tau \in S$ then $w(\sigma) \geqslant c^{\lvert \lambda \rvert} = 1$, so there is an $i < 2$ such that $w(\sigma i) < 1$, and we can let $A(n) = i$.

We have $w(\lambda) = 0$. Suppose that we have defined $A \upharpoonright n = \sigma$ so that $w(\sigma) < 1$. It is easy to check that $c(w(\sigma 0) + w(\sigma 1)) = w(\sigma) + p < 2c$, so there is an $i < 2$ such that $w(\sigma i) < 1$, and we can let $A(n) = i$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.4.3 — Durand, Levin, and Shen)</span></p>

Let $0 < d < 1$. There is a $d$-shift complex set.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $b = -\log(1-d) + 1$, let $c = 2^{-d}$, and let $S = \lbrace \tau \in 2^{<\omega} : K(\tau) \leqslant d \lvert \tau \rvert - b \rbrace$. We have

$$\sum_{\tau \in S} c^{\lvert \tau \rvert} = \sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-d \lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-K(\tau) - b} \leqslant 2^{-b} \sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-K(\tau)}$$

$$\leqslant 2^{-b} = \frac{1 - d}{2} < 2^{1-d} - 1 = 2c - 1,$$

where the last inequality is easy to show using the fact that $d \in (0, 1)$. Thus, by the lemma, there is an $A$ that avoids every element of $S$. This $A$ is clearly $d$-shift complex. $\square$

### 13.5 Partial Randomness

Results such as Theorem 13.3.4 show that effective dimension can be thought of as a notion of "partial randomness". Indeed, as we saw above, a set such as $A \oplus 0^\omega$ for a 1-random $A$, which one would intuitively think of as "$\frac{1}{2}$-random", does indeed have effective dimension $\frac{1}{2}$. In this section, we look at a different, though closely related, approach to defining a notion of $s$-randomness for $s \in [0, 1]$.

We begin with the measure-theoretic approach. We will see that there are at least two reasonable ways to define the concept of $s$-Martin-Löf randomness. The first is a straightforward generalization of the original definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.1 — Tadaki)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$.

(i) A **test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness** is a sequence of uniformly c.e. sets of strings $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in V_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}$ for all $k$.

(ii) $A$ is **weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random** if $A \notin \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$ for all tests for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$.

</div>

We can also define $A$ to be **weakly $s$-random** if $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant sn - O(1)$. The analog of Schnorr's Theorem that 1-randomness is the same as Martin-Löf randomness is straightforward.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.2 — Tadaki)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$ be a computable real. A set $A$ is weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random iff $A$ is weakly $s$-random.

</div>

*Proof.* This proof directly mimics the one of Theorem 6.2.3. We give it here as an example. For other similar results whose proofs are straightforward modifications of the $s = 1$ case, we simply mention that fact.

Suppose that $A$ is weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random. Let $V_k = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) \leqslant s \lvert \sigma \rvert - k \rbrace$. Then $\sum_{\sigma \in V_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in V_k} 2^{-K(\sigma) - k} \leqslant 2^{-k}$, so the $V_k$ form a test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness, and hence $A \notin \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$. Thus there is a $k$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant sn - k$.

Conversely, suppose that $A$ is not weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random. Let $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ be a test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness such that $A \in \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$. Then it is easy to check that $\lbrace (s \lvert \sigma \rvert - k, \sigma) : \sigma \in V_{k^2}, \, k > 2 \rbrace$ is a KC set, so for each $c$ there is an $n$ such that $K(A \upharpoonright n) < sn - c$. $\square$

Of course, weak $s$-randomness is closely related to effective dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.3)</span></p>

For all $A$, we have $\dim(A) = \sup \lbrace s : A \text{ is weakly } s\text{-random} \rbrace$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $A$ is weakly $s$-random then $\liminf_n \frac{K(A \upharpoonright n)}{n} \geqslant s$, so $\dim(A) \geqslant s$. Conversely, if $A$ is not weakly $s$-random and $r < s$, then $\liminf_n \frac{K(A \upharpoonright n)}{n} \leqslant s < r$. Since $r < s$ is arbitrary, $\dim(A) \leqslant s$. $\square$

Whether $A$ is $\dim(A)$-random depends on $A$, of course. We have seen that there are sets of effective dimension 1 that are not 1-random, and it is easy to construct similar examples for $s < 1$. On the other hand, the examples we gave in connection with Theorem 13.3.5 have effective dimension $s$ and are weakly $s$-random, so we have the following extension of that result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.4 — Lutz)</span></p>

For every computable real $s \in [0, 1]$, there is a set of effective Hausdorff dimension $s$ that is weakly $s$-random.

</div>

The discussion above tells us that the concept of $s$-randomness does give us some information beyond what we can get from effective dimension alone.

Using Theorem 13.5.2, we can emulate the proof that $\Omega$ is 1-random to show the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.5 — Tadaki)</span></p>

Let $s \in (0, 1]$ and let

$$\Omega^s = \sum_{U(\sigma) \downarrow} 2^{-\frac{\lvert \sigma \rvert}{s}}.$$

Then $\Omega^s$ is weakly $s$-random.

</div>

Similarly, we can construct a universal test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness and develop other tools of the theory of 1-randomness. For example, it is useful to have an analog of Solovay tests for $s$-randomness.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.6)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$. A **Solovay $s$-test** is a c.e. set $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} < \infty$. A set $A$ is **covered** by this test if $A \in \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ for infinitely many $\sigma \in S$.

</div>

The following result can be proved in much the same way as the $s = 1$ case. Although one direction is slightly weaker than in the $s = 1$ case, this result is enough to establish that for all $A$, we have $\dim(A) = \inf \lbrace s : A \text{ is covered by some Solovay } s\text{-test} \rbrace$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.7 — Reimann)</span></p>

If $A$ is weakly $s$-random then $A$ is not covered by any Solovay $s$-test. If $A$ is covered by some Solovay $s$-test then $A$ is not weakly $t$-random for any $t > s$.

</div>

The following variant will be useful in the proof of Theorem 13.8.1 below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.8)</span></p>

An **interval Solovay $s$-test** is a c.e. set $T$ of intervals in $[0, 1]$ with dyadic rational endpoints such that $\sum_{I \in T} \lvert I \rvert^s < \infty$ (where $\lvert I \rvert$ is the length of $I$).

</div>

It is easy to transform an interval Solovay $s$-test $T$ into a Solovay $s$-test $S$ as follows. For each $[q, q + r] \in T$, first let $n$ be largest such that $2^{-n} < 2r$. Let $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_1$ be such that $0.\sigma_0 = q$ and $0.\sigma_1 = q + 2^{-n}$. Let $\tau_i = (\sigma_i 0^\omega) \upharpoonright n$. Put $\tau_0$ and $\tau_1$ into $S$. It is easy to check that if $A \in I$ for infinitely many $I \in T$ then $A \in \llbracket \tau \rrbracket$ for infinitely many $\tau \in S$, and $\sum_{\tau \in S} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant 2 \sum_{I \in T} (2 \lvert I \rvert)^s < \infty$. Thus, we can use interval Solovay $s$-tests in place of Solovay $s$-tests in bounding the dimension of a real.

Turning now to the martingale approach to randomness, we run into some trouble. The following definitions are natural.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.9)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$.

(i) $A$ is **martingale $s$-random** if for all c.e. martingales $d$, we have $A \notin S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$.

(ii) $A$ is **supermartingale $s$-random** if for all c.e. supermartingales $d$, we have $A \notin S_{2^{(1-s)n}}[d]$.

</div>

It is currently unknown whether these notions are the same, for the kind of reasons we mentioned when discussing Theorem 13.3.2.

One direction of the equivalence does not work. Consider Schnorr's proof that if a set is Martin-Löf random then no c.e. (super)martingale can succeed on it. We are given a c.e. (super)martingale $d$, and when we see $d(\sigma) > 2^k$, we put $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ into a test set $U_k$. Now imagine we follow the same proof for the $s < 1$ case. Here our test sets $V_k$ are sets of strings. We might see that $d_s(\sigma 0) > 2^k$ and put $\sigma 0$ into $V_k$. Since $d$ is only c.e., at some later stage $t > s$ we might see that $d_t(\sigma) > 2^k$. We would like to have $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$, but of course putting $\sigma$ into $V_k$ is a waste, since $\sigma 0$ is already there, and indeed the measure calculation in Schnorr's proof is unlikely to work if $V_k$ is not prefix-free. If we were to follow Schnorr's proof, we would simply put $\sigma 1$ into $V_k$. Unfortunately, $2(2^{-s(\lvert \sigma \rvert + 1)}) > 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$, so the simple measure calculation that allowed Schnorr's proof to succeed is no longer available.

This problem was overcome by the following definition of Calude, Staiger, and Terwijn.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.10 — Calude, Staiger, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$.

(i) A **test for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness** is a sequence of uniformly c.e. sets of strings $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ such that for every $k$ and every prefix-free $\widehat{V}_k \subseteq V_k$, we have $\sum_{\sigma \in \widehat{V}_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}$.

(ii) $A$ is **strongly $s$-Martin-Löf random** if $A \notin \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$ for all tests for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$.

</div>

Now the following result can be proved by a straightforward modification of Schnorr's proof mentioned above. The point is that when we notice that $d(\sigma) > 2^k$, we can put $\sigma$ into $V_k$ even if, say, $\sigma 0$ is already there, because any prefix-free subset of $V_k$ will contain at most one of $\sigma$ and $\sigma 0$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.11 — Calude, Staiger, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Let $s \in (0, 1]$ be a computable real. A set $A$ is strongly $s$-Martin-Löf random iff it is supermartingale $s$-random.

</div>

Reimann and Stephan showed that weak and strong $s$-randomness are indeed distinct. The proof is technical and basically involves careful KC calculations, and we omit it due to space considerations.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.12 — Reimann and Stephan)</span></p>

For all computable $s \in (0, 1)$, there is a set that is weakly but not strongly Martin-Löf $s$-random.

</div>

On the other hand, we do have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.13 — Reimann)</span></p>

If $t < s$ and $A$ is weakly $s$-random, then $A$ is strongly Martin-Löf $t$-random.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $A$ is not strongly Martin-Löf $t$-random, so there is a test for strong $t$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. For each $n$,

$$\sum_{\sigma \in U_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} = \sum_k \sum_{\sigma \in U_n \cap 2^k} 2^{-sk}$$

$$= \sum_k 2^{(t-s)k} \sum_{\sigma \in U_n \cap 2^k} 2^{-tk} \leqslant \sum_k 2^{(t-s)k} 2^{-n},$$

where the last inequality holds because each $U_n \cap 2^k$ is prefix-free. Now, $\sum_k 2^{(t-s)k} < \infty$, so if we let $m$ be such that $\sum_k 2^{(t-s)k - m} \leqslant 1$, then $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \geqslant m}$ is a test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness witnessing the fact that $A$ is not weakly $s$-random. $\square$

As pointed out by Calude, Staiger, and Terwijn, Theorem 13.5.11 implies that strong $s$-randomness can be characterized in terms of $KM$ since, as noted in Section 6.3.2, $KM(\sigma) = -\log d(\sigma) + \lvert \sigma \rvert \pm O(1)$ for an optimal c.e. supermartingale $d$. But we would still want to have a machine-based characterization of strong $s$-randomness. The following definition provides one.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.14 — Downey and Reid)</span></p>

Let $f : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ be a partial computable function computed by a Turing machine $M$, and let $Q \subseteq \text{rng} \, f$. The **$M$-pullback** of $Q$ is the unique subset $Q^*$ of $\text{dom} \, f$ satisfying the following conditions.

(i) $f(Q^*) = Q$.

(ii) If $\sigma \in Q^*$ and $f(\tau) = f(\sigma)$, then $\lvert \tau \rvert \geqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert$.

(iii) If $\sigma \in Q^*$ and $f(\tau) = f(\sigma)$ with $\lvert \tau \rvert = \lvert \sigma \rvert$ then $\sigma$ is enumerated into $\text{dom} \, M$ before $\tau$ is. (We assume that elements enter $\text{dom} \, M$ one at a time.)

A machine $M$ is **$s$-measurable** if for all $Q \subseteq \text{rng} \, M$, we have $\sum_{\sigma \in Q^*} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 1$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.15 — Downey, Reid, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Let $s \in (0, 1]$ be a computable real. A set $A$ is strongly $s$-Martin-Löf random iff $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for all $s$-measurable machines $M$.

</div>

We know of no characterization of strong $s$-randomness in terms of initial segment prefix-free or plain complexity.

Before we prove Theorem 13.5.15, we need a lemma, which takes the role of the KC Theorem in this setting.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.5.16 — Downey, Reid, and Terwijn)</span></p>

Let $s \in (0, 1]$. Let $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ be a test for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness. There is an $s$-measurable machine $M$ such that for each $k$ and $\sigma \in U_{2k+2}$, there is a $\tau$ of length $\lvert \sigma \rvert - (k + 1)$ with $M(\tau) = \sigma$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\sigma \in U_{2k+2}$ then $2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-(2k+2)}$, so $\lvert \sigma \rvert \geqslant 2k + 2$, whence $\lvert \sigma \rvert - (k + 1) > 0$. So for a single such $\sigma$, requiring that there be a $\tau$ of length $\lvert \sigma \rvert - (k + 1)$ with $M(\tau) = \sigma$ does not cause a problem.

We define $M$ in the most obvious way: Whenever we see $\sigma$ enter $U_{2k+2}$, we find the leftmost $\tau$ of length $\lvert \sigma \rvert - (k + 1)$ and let $M(\tau) = \sigma$. We must show that there is always such a $\tau$ available, that is, that for each $n$ there are at most $2^n$ many strings of length $n$ needed for the definition of $M$.

As argued above, if $\sigma \in U_{2k+2}$ then $\lvert \sigma \rvert \geqslant 2k + 2$, so if $k > n - 1$ then $\lvert \sigma \rvert - (k+1) > n$. Thus we need to worry about only those $U_{2k+2}$ with $k \leqslant n - 1$. Note that strings $\sigma \in U_{2k+2}$ that require a domain element $\tau$ of length $n$ must have length $n + k + 1$.

For a set $S$ of strings, let $\#(S, n)$ denote the number of strings of length $n$ in $S$. As the set of all strings in $U_{2k+2}$ of length $n + k + 1$ is prefix-free,

$$\sum \lbrace 2^{-s(n+k+1)} : \sigma \in U_{2k+2} \wedge \lvert \sigma \rvert = n + k + 1 \rbrace \leqslant 2^{-(2k+2)}.$$

Thus

$$\#(U_{2k+2}, n + k + 1) 2^{-s(n+k+1)} \leqslant 2^{-(2k+2)},$$

so

$$\#(U_{2k+2}, n + k + 1) \leqslant 2^{s(n+k+1) - (2k+2)} \leqslant 2^{(n+k+1) - (2k+2)} \leqslant 2^{n-k-1}.$$

Therefore,

$$\sum_{k < n} \#(U_{2k+2}, n + k + 1) = \sum_{k < n} 2^{n - k - 1} \leqslant 2^{n-1} \sum_{k < n} 2^{-k} < 2^n.$$

Finally, we need to show that $M$ is an $s$-measurable machine. Let $Q \subseteq \text{rng} \, M$ be prefix-free. Then $Q \subseteq \bigcup_k U_{2k+2}$, so let $Q_k = U_{2k+2} \cap Q$, which is prefix-free. Then

$$\sum_{\sigma \in Q} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} 2^{s(k+1)} \sum_{\sigma \in Q_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{s(k+1)} 2^{-(2k+2)} \leqslant 2^{-k-1}.$$

Since $Q_k$ is prefix-free and $s \leqslant 1$, it follows that

$$\sum_{\sigma \in Q^*} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_k \sum_{\sigma \in Q_k^*} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_k 2^{-k-1} = 1,$$

where $Q^*$ is as in Definition 13.5.14. $\square$

*Proof of Theorem 13.5.15.* First suppose we have an $s$-measurable machine $M$ such that for each $c$ there is an $n$ with $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) < n - c$. Let $U_k = \lbrace \sigma : K_M(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert - \frac{k}{s} \rbrace$. The $U_k$ are uniformly c.e., and $A \in \llbracket U_k \rrbracket$ for all $k$. Now we need a calculation to show that the $U_k$ form a test for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness. Let $Q \subseteq U_k$ be prefix-free. Then

$$\sum_{\sigma \in Q} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in Q} 2^{-s(K_M(\sigma) + \frac{k}{s})}$$

$$\leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in Q} 2^{-k} 2^{-sK_M(\sigma)} \leqslant 2^{-k} \sum_{\tau \in Q^*} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k},$$

where $Q^*$ is as in Definition 13.5.14.

Now suppose that $A$ is not strongly $s$-random. Then there is a test for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness with $A \in \bigcap_k \llbracket U_k \rrbracket$. Let $M$ be as in Lemma 13.5.16. Then for each $c$ there is an $n$ such that $K_M(A \upharpoonright n) < n - c$. $\square$

### 13.6 A Correspondence Principle for Effective Dimension

Hausdorff dimension and its effective counterpart are of course quite different in general. However, there are situations in which they coincide. We have seen that many important subsets of Cantor space are $\Pi_1^0$ classes.

Hitchcock showed that for such classes, and in fact even for arbitrary countable unions of $\Pi_1^0$ classes, the classical Hausdorff dimension of a class is equal to its effective Hausdorff dimension, and hence to the supremum of the Hausdorff dimensions of its elements. Thus, for such classes, we can think of classical Hausdorff dimension, which at a first look seems clearly to be a global property of a class, as a pointwise phenomenon.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.6.1 — Hitchcock)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a countable union of $\Pi_1^0$ classes. Then $\dim_\text{H} P = \dim P$.

</div>

*Proof.* It is enough to prove the result for the case in which $P$ is a single $\Pi_1^0$ class, since the Hausdorff dimension of a countable union of classes is the supremum of the Hausdorff dimension of the individual classes, and similarly for effective dimension.

It is enough to show that $\dim P \leqslant \dim_\text{H} P$, so let $s > \dim_\text{H} P$. Then for each $n$ there is a set of strings $U_n$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in U_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-n}$ and $P \subseteq \llbracket U_n \rrbracket$. Because $P$ is closed and $2^\omega$ is compact, $P$ is compact, so the $U_n$ can be taken to be finite.

Given $n$, we can search for a finite set of strings $V_n$ and a stage $s$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in V_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-n}$ and $P_s \subseteq \llbracket V_n \rrbracket$, where $P_s$ is as usual the stage $s$ approximation to $P$. We have seen that such a $V_n$ must exist, and we can recognize when we have found one. Then the $V_n$ form a test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness, and $P \subseteq \bigcap_n \llbracket V_n \rrbracket$, so no element of $P$ is weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random, and hence $\dim(A) < s$ for all $A \in P$.

Thus $\dim P < s$. Since $s > \dim_\text{H} P$ is arbitrary, $\dim P \leqslant \dim_\text{H} P$. $\square$

Hitchcock also showed that if $P$ is a $\Sigma_2^0$ class (i.e., a union of uniformly $\Pi_1^0$ classes), then the Hausdorff dimension of $P$ is equal to its computable Hausdorff dimension (that is, the dimension defined using computable, rather than c.e., martingales, which is equivalent to the concept of Schnorr Hausdorff dimension defined below).

Note that, as observed by Hitchcock, Theorem 13.6.1 cannot be extended even to a single $\Pi_2^0$ class, since $\lbrace \Omega \rbrace$ is a $\Pi_2^0$ class.

### 13.7 Hausdorff Dimension and Complexity Extraction

There have been several results about the dimensions of classes of sets defined in computability-theoretic terms. One important theme is the following: given a set of positive effective dimension, to what extent is it possible to extract a greater level of complexity/randomness from that set? As we will see, there are several ways to formalize this question. (Note that we have already seen in Theorem 8.16.6 that there are sets of reasonably high initial segment complexity that do not compute 1-random sets.)

We begin by pointing out a fundamental difference between the degrees of 1-random sets and those of sets of high effective Hausdorff dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.1 — Folklore)</span></p>

Let $A \leqslant_\text{m} B$. There is a $C \equiv_\text{m} B$ such that $A$ and $C$ have the same effective Hausdorff dimension. The same result holds for any weaker reducibility, such as Turing reducibility.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f$ be a fast-growing computable function, such as Ackermann's function (though a much slower-growing function than that will do for this argument). For each $n$, replace $A(f(n))$ by $B(n)$. Call the resulting set $C$. Then $C \equiv_\text{m} B$, and $A$ and $C$ have the same effective Hausdorff dimension because $K(A \upharpoonright m)$ and $K(C \upharpoonright m)$ are very close for all $m$. The same procedure works for any weaker reducibility. $\square$

As we saw in Corollary 8.8.6, Kučera proved that the Turing degrees of 1-random sets are not closed upwards. This fact is true even in the $\Delta_2^0$ degrees, for instance by Theorem 8.8.4. Hence, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.2 — Folklore)</span></p>

There are $\Delta_2^0$ degrees of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 containing no 1-random sets.

</div>

*Proof.* Choose $\mathbf{a} < \mathbf{b} < \mathbf{0'}$ such that $\mathbf{a}$ is 1-random but $\mathbf{b}$ is not. By Lemma 13.7.1, $\mathbf{b}$ contains a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1. $\square$

Since $\Omega \leqslant_\text{tt} \emptyset'$, the method above also gives the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.3 — Reimann)</span></p>

The tt-degree of $\emptyset'$ has effective Hausdorff dimension 1.

</div>

The only way we have seen to make a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 that is not 1-random is to take a 1-random set and "mess it up" by a process such as inserting 0's at a sparse set of locations. It is natural to ask whether this is in some sense the only way we can produce such a set. One way to formalize this question is by asking whether it is always possible to extract randomness from a source of effective dimension 1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Question 13.7.4 — Reimann)</span></p>

Let $A$ have effective Hausdorff dimension 1. Is there necessarily a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$?

</div>

We will give a negative answer to this question due to Greenberg and Miller in Section 13.9, where we will present their construction of a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 and minimal Turing degree. We saw in Corollary 6.9.5 that no minimal degree is 1-random.

Similarly, one of the only methods we have seen for making a set of fractional effective Hausdorff dimension is to take a set of higher effective Hausdorff dimension and "thin it out" (the other method being the construction of shift complex sets in Section 13.4). Again we are led to ask whether, given a set $A$ of fractional effective Hausdorff dimension, it is always possible to find a set $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ of higher effective Hausdorff dimension, or even of effective Hausdorff dimension 1, or even a 1-random $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$. By Lemma 13.7.1, these questions remain the same if we replace $\leqslant_\text{T}$ by $\equiv_\text{T}$, so we can think of them as questions about the relationship between the effective Hausdorff dimension of $A$ and that of its degree. Thus we can ask the following question.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Remark</span><span class="math-callout__name">(Question 13.7.5 — Reimann, Terwijn)</span></p>

Is there a Turing degree $\mathbf{a}$ of effective Hausdorff dimension strictly between 0 and 1? If so, can $\mathbf{a}$ be $\Delta_2^0$?

</div>

Question 13.7.5 is known as the **broken dimension question**. In Section 13.8, we will give a positive answer to it due to Miller. Before doing so, we mention some earlier attempts to solve this question that are interesting and insightful in their own right.

We have seen in Theorem 8.8.1 that 1-random sets can always compute DNC functions. Terwijn proved that this is also the case for sets of nonzero effective Hausdorff dimension. We can easily extract this result from our results on autocomplex sets and DNC functions: As mentioned following Theorem 13.3.4, if $A$ has positive effective Hausdorff dimension, then $A$ is complex, so by Theorem 8.16.4, $A$ computes a DNC function. However, Terwijn's original proof is instructive and short, so we give it below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.6 — Terwijn)</span></p>

If $\dim(A) > 0$ then $A$ can compute a DNC function.

</div>

*Proof.* By Theorem 2.22.1, it is enough to show that $A$ can compute a fixed-point free function.

Let $s$ be such that $\dim(A) > s > 0$ and $\frac{1}{s} \in \mathbb{N}$. For each $e$, let $e_0$ and $e_1$ be indices chosen in some canonical way so that $W_e = W_{e_0} \oplus W_{e_1}$. For a string $\sigma$, let $\widehat{\sigma}$ be the string of length $\lvert \sigma \rvert$ such that $\widehat{\sigma}(n) = 1 - \sigma(n)$. Let

$$B_{e,n} = \left\lbrace \sigma \in 2^{\frac{n}{s}} : \exists t \, (W_{e_0, t} \upharpoonright \tfrac{n}{s} = \sigma \wedge W_{e_1, t} \upharpoonright \tfrac{n}{s} = \widehat{\sigma}) \right\rbrace.$$

If there is a $\sigma$ such that $W_{e_0} \upharpoonright \frac{n}{s} = \sigma$ and $W_{e_1, t} \upharpoonright \frac{n}{s} = \widehat{\sigma}$, then $\sigma \in B_{e,n}$, but no other string is in $B_{e,n}$. Otherwise, $B_{e,n}$ is empty. In either case, $\lvert B_{e,n} \rvert \leqslant 1$, so $\sum_{\sigma \in B_{e,n}} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-s \cdot \frac{n}{s}} = 2^{-n}$, and hence the $B_{e,n}$ form a test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness for each $e$. We can effectively find indices $g(e)$ for each such test. (That is, $W_{\Phi_{g(e)}(n)}$ enumerates $B_{e,n}$ for each $e$ and $n$.)

The usual construction of a universal test applied to tests for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness produces a universal test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that for each index $i$, for the $i$th test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace V_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$, we have $V_{n+i+1} \in U_n$ for all $n$. Since $\dim(A) > s$, there is an $n$ such that $A \notin \llbracket U_n \rrbracket$, so $A \notin \llbracket B_{e, n+g(e)+1} \rrbracket$ for all $e$.

To construct the fixed-point free function $h \leqslant_\text{T} A$, given $e$, let $h(e)$ be an index so that $W_{h(e)_0} \upharpoonright f(n + g(e) + 1) = A \upharpoonright f(n + g(e) + 1)$ and $W_{h(e)_1} \upharpoonright f(n + g(e) + 1) = \overline{A} \upharpoonright f(n + g(e) + 1)$. If $W_{h(e)} = W_e$, then $A \upharpoonright f(n + g(e) + 1) \in B_{e, n+g(e)+1}$, which is not the case, so $W_{h(e)} \neq W_e$ for all $e$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.7 — Terwijn)</span></p>

If $A <_\text{T} \emptyset'$ is c.e. then $\lbrace B : B \leqslant_\text{T} A \rbrace$ has effective Hausdorff dimension 0.

</div>

*Proof.* If not, then $A$ computes a set of positive effective Hausdorff dimension, and hence computes a fixed-point free function. But then $A$ is Turing complete, by Arslanov's Completeness Criterion, Theorem 2.22.3. $\square$

Note that the proofs above give weak truth table reductions, so we also obtain the following corollary for free.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.8)</span></p>

If $A <_\text{wtt} \emptyset'$ is c.e. then $\lbrace B : B \leqslant_\text{wtt} A \rbrace$ has effective Hausdorff dimension 0.

</div>

One consequence of Corollary 13.7.7 is that if $A$ has positive effective Hausdorff dimension, then, by Theorem 8.10.2, $A$ computes an infinite subset of a 1-random set. Thus, if such an $A$ cannot compute a 1-random set then something fairly subtle must be going on.

Reimann and Terwijn (see Reimann) showed that Question 13.7.5 has a positive answer for m-reducibility, even for $\Delta_2^0$ sets, by showing that there is a $\Delta_2^0$ set $A$ such that $\lbrace B : B \leqslant_\text{m} A \rbrace$ has fractional effective Hausdorff dimension. (An example is the set $A$ defined by starting with a $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random set $X$ and letting $A(n) = X(n) \cdot X(n+1)$, where $\cdot$ denotes multiplication.) Before Miller's solution to the broken dimension problem, the best partial result was the following one by Nies and Reimann. We will not prove it here, as it is a consequence of Miller's result presented in the next section. It is worth noting, though, that the method of proof in Nies and Reimann is rather different than that used for Miller's result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.7.9 — Nies and Reimann)</span></p>

For any computable real $\alpha$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$ there is a $\Delta_2^0$ set $A$ such that the effective Hausdorff dimension of $\lbrace B : B \leqslant_\text{wtt} A \rbrace$ is $\alpha$.

</div>

### 13.8 A Turing Degree of Nonintegral Hausdorff Dimension

In this section, we present Miller's proof of the following result, whose significance was discussed in the previous section.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.1 — Miller)</span></p>

Let $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ be a left-c.e. real. There is a $\Delta_2^0$ set $A$ of effective Hausdorff dimension $\alpha$ such that if $B \leqslant_\text{T} A$ then the effective Hausdorff dimension of $B$ is at most $\alpha$.

</div>

*Proof.* To simplify the presentation, we take $\alpha = \frac{1}{2}$. It should be clear how to modify the proof to work for any rational $\alpha$, and not too difficult to see how to handle the general case.

We will build $A$ as the intersection of a sequence of nested $\Pi_1^0$ classes of positive measure. We will begin with a class all of whose members have effective dimension at least $\frac{1}{2}$, thus ensuring that $\dim(A) \geqslant \frac{1}{2}$. At each stage we will satisfy a requirement of the form $\Psi_e^A \text{ total} \Rightarrow \exists k > n \, (K(\Psi_e^A \upharpoonright k) \leqslant (\frac{1}{2} + 2^{-n})k)$. To do so, we will want to wait until a large set of oracles that appear to be in our current $\Pi_1^0$ class $P$ compute the same string via $\Psi_e$, and then compress that string. A key to showing that we can do this will be to use the dimension of the measure of $P$. Thus we will work with $\Pi_1^0$ classes such that $\dim(\mu(P)) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$.

We begin by introducing some concepts that will be needed in the proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.2)</span></p>

Let $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$. The **direct weight** of $S$ is $\text{DW}(S) = \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-\frac{\lvert \sigma \rvert}{2}}$. The **weight** of $S$ is $W(S) = \inf \lbrace \text{DW}(V) : \llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket V \rrbracket \rbrace$.

</div>

Note that $W(S) \leqslant 1$ because $\llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket \lbrace \lambda \rbrace \rrbracket$ and $\text{DW}(\lbrace \lambda \rbrace) = 1$. The weight of $S$ is essentially the minimum cost of compressing some initial segment of each set in $\llbracket S \rrbracket$ by a factor of 2 (although programs cannot have fractional length, so the cost of compressing a string of length 5 by a factor of 2 is actually $2^{-2}$, not $2^{-2.5}$). Of course, there is no reason to think that this compression can be realized effectively. In other words, even if $S$ is c.e., there may not be a prefix-free machine $M$ compressing some initial segment of each set in $\llbracket S \rrbracket$ by a factor of 2 so that the measure of the domain of $M$ is close to $W(S)$.

Suppose that $S$ is finite and let $V$ be such that $\llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket V \rrbracket$. It is suboptimal for $V$ to contain any string incomparable with every $\sigma \in S$ or to contain a proper extension of some $\sigma \in S$. In other words, it is always possible to find a $\widehat{V}$ with $\llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket \widehat{V} \rrbracket$ and $\text{DW}(\widehat{V}) \leqslant \text{DW}(V)$, so that every string in $V$ has an extension in $S$. Therefore, there are only finitely many $V$ that need to be considered in computing the infimum in the definition of $W(S)$. Hence this infimum is achieved, justifying the following definition when $S$ is finite.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.3)</span></p>

An **optimal cover** of $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ is a set $S^{oc} \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ and $\text{DW}(S^{oc}) = W(S)$. For the sake of uniqueness, we also require $\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ to have the minimum measure among all possible contenders.

</div>

It is not hard to see that optimal covers, if they exist, are unique and prefix-free. The analysis above also shows that when $S$ is finite, we can compute both the optimal cover of $S$ and $W(S)$.

Now consider an infinite $S$. Let $\lbrace S_t \rbrace_{t \in \omega}$ be an enumeration of $S$, i.e., a sequence of finite sets $S_0 \subset S_1 \subset \cdots$ such that $S = \bigcup_t S_t$. If $\sigma \in S_t^{oc}$, then the only way for $\sigma$ to not be in $S_{t+1}^{oc}$ is for some $\tau \prec \sigma$ to be in $S_{t+1}^{oc}$. This fact has some nice consequences. First, it implies a **nesting property**: $\llbracket S_t^{oc} \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket S_{t+1}^{oc} \rrbracket$ for all $t$. Second, it proves that the $S_t^{oc}$ have a pointwise limit $V$. It is not hard to see that $V = S^{oc}$, demonstrating that the definition above is valid for any $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$.

If $S$ is c.e., the above construction applied to an effective enumeration shows that the optimal cover $S^{oc}$ is $\Delta_2^0$. More importantly, the nesting property implies that $\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ is a $\Sigma_1^0$ class. There will not generally be a c.e. set $V \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\llbracket V \rrbracket = \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ and $\text{DW}(V) = W(S)$, or even such that the direct weight of any prefix-free subset is bounded by $W(S)$. However, we can find such a $V$ for which the direct weight of any prefix-free subset is bounded by $W(S)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.4)</span></p>

For any c.e. set $S \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, we can effectively find a c.e. $V \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ such that $\llbracket V \rrbracket = \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ and if $P \subseteq V$ is prefix-free, then $\text{DW}(P) \leqslant W(S)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace S_t \rbrace_{t \in \omega}$ be an effective enumeration of $S$. Let $V = \bigcup_t S_t^{oc}$. Then $V$ is c.e. and $\llbracket V \rrbracket = \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$. If there is an infinite prefix-free $P \subseteq V$ such that $\text{DW}(P) > W(S)$, then there is a finite $P' \subset P$ with the same property. So assume that $P \subseteq V$ is finite and prefix-free. We will prove that if $\tau \in V$, then $\text{DW}(P \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}) \leqslant \text{DW}(\lbrace \tau \rbrace)$, by induction on the distance $k$ from $\tau$ to its longest extension in $P$ (the claim being trivial if $P \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}$ is empty). The case $k = 0$ is immediate. Now take $\tau \in V \setminus P$. There is a unique $t$ such that $\tau \in S_{t+1}^{oc} \setminus S_t^{oc}$. Then $\text{DW}(S_t^{oc} \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}) \leqslant \text{DW}(\lbrace \tau \rbrace)$, since every element of $P \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}$ must have entered $V$ by stage $t$. Hence, applying the inductive hypothesis to the elements of $S_t^{oc} \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}$, we have $\text{DW}(P \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}) \leqslant \text{DW}(S_t^{oc} \cap \tau 2^{<\omega}) \leqslant \text{DW}(\lbrace \tau \rbrace)$. Of course, $\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ covers $\llbracket P \rrbracket$, so $\text{DW}(P) \leqslant \text{DW}(S^{oc}) = W(S)$. $\square$

We will build a set $A$ by approximations. As usual — or in terminology borrowed from set theory, the type of *forcing condition* — determines the nature of the requirements that we can satisfy during the construction. Our conditions will be pairs $(\sigma, S)$ with $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $S \subseteq \sigma 2^{<\omega}$ c.e. and such that $\sigma \notin S^{oc}$. A condition describes a restriction on the set $A$ that is being constructed. Specifically, the class of all sets consistent with a condition $(\sigma, S)$ is the $\Pi_1^0$ class $P_{(\sigma, S)} = \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$. Our definition of condition guarantees that $P_{(\sigma, S)}$ is nonempty. We say that a condition $(\tau, T) \preccurlyeq (\sigma, S)$ if $P_{(\tau, T)} \subseteq P_{(\sigma, S)}$. Extending a condition corresponds, of course, to further restricting the possibilities for $A$.

We now establish some basic lemmas about these forcing conditions. The first shows that there is an $S$ such that every element of $P_{(\lambda, S)}$ has effective dimension at least $\frac{1}{2}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.5)</span></p>

Let $S = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) \leqslant \frac{\lvert \sigma \rvert}{2} \rbrace$. Then $(\lambda, S)$ is a forcing condition.

</div>

*Proof.* All that needs to be shown is that $\lambda \notin S^{oc}$. If $\lambda \in S^{oc}$, then $\text{DW}(S) \geqslant W(S) = 1$. But $\text{DW}(S) = \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-\frac{\lvert \sigma \rvert}{2}} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-K(\sigma)} \leqslant \Omega < 1$. $\square$

The next two lemmas show that each $\Pi_1^0$ class corresponding to a condition has positive measure, and that the effective Hausdorff dimension of its measure is at most $\frac{1}{2}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.6)</span></p>

Let $S \subseteq \sigma 2^{<\omega}$. If $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ is nonempty, then it has positive measure.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $n = \lvert \sigma \rvert$. The fact that $\llbracket S \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ implies that $W(S) \leqslant 2^{-\frac{n}{2}}$. Because $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ is nonempty, we know that $\sigma \notin S^{oc}$. Hence $\tau \in S^{oc}$ implies $\lvert \tau \rvert > n$. By these observations,

$$\mu(\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket) = \sum_{\tau \in S^{oc}} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert} < \sum_{\tau \in S^{oc}} 2^{-\frac{\lvert \tau \rvert + n}{2}} = 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} \sum_{\tau \in S^{oc}} 2^{-\frac{\lvert \tau \rvert}{2}}$$

$$= 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} \text{DW}(S^{oc}) = 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} W(S) \leqslant 2^{-n}.$$

Therefore, $\mu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket) > 0$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.7)</span></p>

Let $(\sigma, S)$ be a condition. Then $\dim(\mu(P_{(\sigma, S)})) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$.

</div>

*Proof.* We prove that $\dim(\mu(\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket)) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$, which is sufficient because $\mu(P_{(\sigma, S)}) = 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} - \mu(\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket)$. We may assume, without loss of generality, that $S^{oc}$ is infinite, since otherwise $\mu(\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket)$ is rational. Let $w = W(S)$. Let $V \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ be the c.e. set guaranteed by Lemma 13.8.4. That is, $\llbracket V \rrbracket = \llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket$ and $\text{DW}(P) \leqslant w$ whenever $P \subseteq V$ is prefix-free. Note that $V$ must be infinite. Let $\lbrace V_t \rbrace_{t \in \omega}$ be an effective enumeration of $V$ such that $V_0 = \emptyset$.

Let $s > 1/2$. We produce an interval Solovay $s$-test $T$ covering $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)$. (See Definition 13.5.8.) It consists of two parts, $T_0$ and $T_1$.
