1. If $\tau \in V_{t+1} \setminus V_t$, then put $[\mu(\llbracket V_{t+1} \rrbracket), \mu(\llbracket V_{t+1} \rrbracket) + 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert}]$ into $T_0$.

2. If $\mu(\llbracket V_t \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket) \leqslant k 2^{-n}$ and $\mu(\llbracket V_{t+1} \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket) > k 2^{-n}$ for some $n$ and $k$, then put $[\mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket), \mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket) + 2^{-n}]$ into $T_1$.

Let $T = T_0 \cup T_1$. Notice that $T$ does not actually depend on $s$. Using the fact that $V \cap 2^n$ is prefix-free, we have

$$\sum_{I \in T_0} \lvert I \rvert^s = \sum_{\tau \in V} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} = \sum_n 2^{-sn} \lvert V \cap 2^n \rvert$$

$$= \sum_n 2^{(\frac{1}{2} - s)n} 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} \lvert V \cap 2^n \rvert \leqslant \sum_n 2^{(\frac{1}{2} - s)n} w = \frac{w}{1 - 2^{\frac{1}{2} - s}} < \infty.$$

Now fix $n$ and let $k$ be the number of intervals of length $2^{-n}$ added to $T_1$. By construction, $2^{-n} k < \mu(\llbracket V \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket)$. Let $P \subseteq V \cap 2^{>n}$ be a prefix-free set such that $\llbracket P \rrbracket = \llbracket V \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket$. Then by the same argument as in the previous lemma, $\mu(\llbracket P \rrbracket) < 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} \text{DW}(P)$. Putting these facts together we have $2^{-n} k < 2^{-\frac{n}{2}} w$, so $k < 2^{\frac{n}{2}} w$. Thus

$$\sum_{I \in T_1} \lvert I \rvert^s < \sum_n 2^{\frac{n}{2}} w (2^{-n})^s = \sum_n 2^{(\frac{1}{2} - s)n} w < \infty,$$

which proves that $T$ is an interval Solovay $s$-test.

Next we prove that $T$ covers $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)$. Call $\tau \in V_{t+1} \setminus V_t$ **timely** if $V_{t+1} \cap 2^{\leqslant n} = V \cap 2^{\leqslant n}$, in other words, if only strings longer than $\tau$ enter $V$ after $\tau$. Because $V$ is infinite, there are infinitely many timely $\tau \in V$. Fix one. Let $t + 1$ be the stage at which $\tau$ enters $V$ and let $n = \lvert \tau \rvert$. We claim that there is an interval of length $2^{-n}$ in $T$ that contains $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)$. Note that if $u > t$, then $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket) \leqslant \mu(\llbracket V \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket V_u \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket)$. In response to $\tau$ entering $V$, we put the interval $[\mu(\llbracket V_{t+1} \rrbracket), \mu(\llbracket V_{t+1} \rrbracket) + 2^{-n}]$ into $T_0 \subseteq T$ at stage $t + 1$. Let $I = [\mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket), \mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket) + 2^{-n}]$ be the last interval of length $2^{-n}$ added to $T$. If $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket) \notin I$, then $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket) > \mu(\llbracket V_u \rrbracket) + 2^{-n}$. Since $u > t$, we have $\mu(\llbracket V \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket) > \mu(\llbracket V_u \cap 2^{>n} \rrbracket) + 2^{-n}$, so another interval of length $2^{-n}$ is added to $T_1$ after stage $u$, which is a contradiction. Thus $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket) \in I$.

We have proved that for any $n$ that is the length of a timely element of $V$, there is an interval of length $2^{-n}$ in $T$ that contains $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)$. Since there are infinitely many timely strings, $\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)$ is covered by $T$.

Since $T$ is an interval Solovay $s$-test for every $s > \frac{1}{2}$, it follows that $\dim(\mu(\llbracket S^{oc} \rrbracket)) = \dim(\mu(\llbracket V \rrbracket)) \leqslant \frac{1}{2}$. $\square$

Our final lemma gives a simple hypothesis on a collection of conditions that guarantees that they have a common extension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.8.8)</span></p>

Let $(\sigma, S)$ be a condition. If $(\sigma, S)$ extends the condition from Lemma 13.8.5, then $\dim(\mu(P_{(\sigma, S)})) \geqslant \frac{1}{2}$. Hence, Lemma 13.8.7 is tight.

</div>

Miller noted that, by applying the method of the main part of this proof (presented after the next lemma) to the identity functional, we can show that if $(\sigma, S)$ extends the condition from Lemma 13.8.5, then $\dim(\mu(P_{(\sigma, S)})) \geqslant \frac{1}{2}$. Hence, Lemma 13.8.7 is tight.

### 13.9 DNC Functions and Effective Hausdorff Dimension

This section presents work of Greenberg and Miller connecting the growth rates of DNC functions with effective Hausdorff dimension. In particular, it describes their construction of a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 and minimal Turing degree. (By Corollary 6.9.5, such a set cannot compute a 1-random set, and hence this result answers Question 13.7.4.) This theorem extends earlier work of Downey and Greenberg, who showed that there is a set of minimal Turing degree and effective packing dimension 1 (a concept defined below in Section 13.11.3, as described in Section 13.13).

Greenberg and Miller generalized effective Hausdorff dimension to a class of spaces similar to Cantor space. Let $h : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ be computable. Let $h^\omega$ be the product space $\prod_n \lbrace 0, 1, \ldots, h(n) - 1 \rbrace$. That is, the elements of $h^\omega$ are infinite sequences of natural numbers such that the $n$th element of the sequence is less than $h(n)$. Let $h^n = \prod_{m < n} \lbrace 0, 1, \ldots, h(m) - 1 \rbrace$ and $h^{<\omega} = \bigcup_n h^n$. We give $h^\omega$ a topology similar to that of Cantor space, with the basic open sets being $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket = \lbrace \alpha \in h^\omega : \sigma \prec \alpha \rbrace$ for $\sigma \in h^{<\omega}$. As for Cantor space, for $A \subseteq h^{<\omega}$, we write $\llbracket A \rrbracket$ for $\bigcup_{\sigma \in A} \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$.

The space $h^\omega$ is a compact Polish space. An analogue of the uniform measure for $h^\omega$ is obtained by dividing mass equitably: for $\sigma \in h^{<\omega}$, let

$$\mu^h(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \frac{1}{\lvert h^{\lvert \sigma \rvert} \rvert} = \frac{1}{h(0) \cdots h(\lvert \sigma \rvert - 1)}.$$

Then $\mu^h$ can be extended to a measure on all of $h^\omega$. When $h$ is fixed, we write simply $\mu$ for $\mu^h$.

For a computable function $h : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$, let $\text{DNC}_h$ be the set of all DNC functions in $h^\omega$. Let $\text{DNC}_n$ be the set of all DNC functions in $n^\omega$. Greenberg and Miller's proof uses work of Kumabe and Lewis, who built a DNC function of minimal degree. We will not include the fairly long proof of this result here. An analysis of the construction, which can be found in [170], shows that the following holds.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.1 — Kumabe and Lewis)</span></p>

For any computable order $h : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$, there is an $f \in \text{DNC}_h$ whose Turing degree is minimal.

</div>

Thus, to show that there is a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 and minimal degree, it will be enough to show that there is a computable order $h : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ such that every $f \in \text{DNC}_h$ computes a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1. We do so in the rest of this section.

#### 13.9.1 Dimension in $h$-spaces

Greenberg and Miller generalized many notions we have seen in this chapter to $h$-spaces. The following is the natural generalization of the notions of weak and strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness to $h^\omega$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.2)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$.

1. A *test for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness* is a uniformly c.e. sequence $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ of subsets of $h^{<\omega}$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in V_k} \mu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)^s \leqslant 2^{-k}$ for all $k$.
2. A *test for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness* is a uniformly c.e. sequence $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ of subsets of $h^{<\omega}$ such that for every $k$ and every prefix-free $\widehat{V}_k \subseteq V_k$, we have $\sum_{\sigma \in \widehat{V}_k} \mu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)^s \leqslant 2^{-k}$.
3. $A \in h^\omega$ is *weakly $s$-Martin-Löf random* if $A \notin \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$ for all tests for weak $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$.
4. $A \in h^\omega$ is *strongly $s$-Martin-Löf random* if $A \notin \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$ for all tests for strong $s$-Martin-Löf randomness $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$.

</div>

Many results proved in Section 13.5 still hold for $h^\omega$, such as Theorem 13.5.13, which says that if $t < s$ and $A$ is weakly $s$-random, then $A$ is strongly $t$-random. Thus, for $A \in h^\omega$, the supremum of all $s$ for which $A$ is weakly $s$-random equals the supremum of all $s$ for which $A$ is strongly $s$-random, and, by analogy with the $2^\omega$ case, can be called the *effective (Hausdorff) dimension* of $A$, denoted by $\dim^h(A)$.

As in the $2^\omega$ case, $A \in h^\omega$ is strongly $s$-Martin-Löf random iff there is no c.e. supermartingale that $s$-succeeds on $A$. The proofs are as before, using in particular the fact that Kolmogorov's Inequality holds in $h^\omega$: For a supermartingale $d$ and a prefix-free set $C \subset h^{<\omega}$, we have $\sum_{\sigma \in C} d(\sigma) \mu(\sigma) \leqslant d(\lambda)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.3)</span></p>

Let $s \in [0, 1]$. A *Solovay $s$-test* is a c.e. set $S \subseteq h^{<\omega}$ such that $\sum_{\sigma \in S} \mu(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket)^s < \infty$. A set $A$ is *covered* by this test if $A \in \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket$ for infinitely many $\sigma \in S$.

</div>

Theorem 13.5.7 still holds: If $A$ is weakly $s$-random then $A$ is not covered by any Solovay $s$-test. If $A$ is covered by some Solovay $s$-test then $A$ is not weakly $t$-random for any $t > s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.4)</span></p>

A *supermartingale* (for $h$) is a function $d : h^{<\omega} \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that for all $\sigma \in h^{<\omega}$,

$$\sum_{i < h(\lvert \sigma \rvert)} d(\sigma i) \leqslant h(\lvert \sigma \rvert) d(\sigma).$$

</div>

If $s \in [0, 1]$, we say that a supermartingale $d$ *$s$-succeeds* on $A \in h^\omega$ if $\limsup_n d(A \restriction n) \mu(\llbracket A \restriction n \rrbracket)^{1-s} = \infty$.

Our goal is to use $h$-spaces to construct an element of $2^\omega$ of minimal degree with effective dimension 1. To do so, we will need to be able to translate between $h^\omega$ and $2^\omega$ in a dimension-preserving way. If $h$ is very well-behaved then there is no problem. For example, if $X \in 4^\omega$ and we let $Y(2n) = \lfloor \frac{X(n)}{2} \rfloor$ and $Y(2n+1) = X(n) \bmod 2$, then $Y \equiv_T X$, and it is easy to check that $\dim(Y) = \dim^h(X)$. To handle more complicated $h$-spaces, it is convenient to work through Euclidean space rather than going directly from $h^\omega$ to $2^\omega$.

There is a natural measure-preserving surjection of $h^\omega$ onto the Euclidean interval $[0, 1]$. First map strings to closed intervals: Let $\pi^h(\lambda) = [0, 1]$. Once $\pi^h(\sigma) = I$ is defined, divide $I$ into $h(\lvert \sigma \rvert)$ many intervals $I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_{h(\lvert \sigma \rvert)-1}$ of equal length and let $\pi^h(\sigma k) = I_k$ for $k < h(\lvert \sigma \rvert)$. Note that indeed $\mu^h(\sigma) = \mu(\pi^h(\sigma))$ for all $\sigma$. Now extend $\pi^h$ continuously to $h^\omega$ by letting $\pi^h(X)$ be the unique element of $\bigcap_n \pi^h(X \restriction n)$. (The fact that $h(n) \geqslant 2$ for all $n$ ensures that this intersection is indeed a singleton.) The mapping $\pi^h$ is not quite 1-1, but it is 1-1 if we ignore the (countably many) sequences that are eventually constant. Note that for all $X \in h^\omega$, we have $X \equiv_T \pi^h(X)$.

The theory of effective dimension can also be developed in $[0, 1]$. We do not have martingales, but we can still, for example, define Solovay tests, as we saw in Definition 13.5.8. Let $\dim^{[0,1]}(X)$ be the infimum of all $s$ such that there is an interval Solovay $s$-test that covers $X$ (that is, such that $X$ is in infinitely many elements of the test).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.5 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

For all $h$ and $X \in h^\omega$, we have $\dim^{[0,1]}(\pi^h(X)) \leqslant \dim^h(X)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $s > \dim^h(X)$ and let $S$ be a Solovay $s$-test for $h$ that covers $X$. Since $\pi^h$ is measure-preserving, the image of $S$ under $\pi^h$ is an interval Solovay test covering $\pi^h(X)$. $\square$

Equality of these dimensions does not hold in general, but we do get equality if $h$ does not grow too quickly or irregularly. Suppose that $S$ is an interval Solovay $s$-test covering some $\pi^h(X)$. We would like to cover $X$ by something like $(\pi^h)^{-1}(S)$. The problem, of course, is that the basic sets (closed intervals with rational endpoints) in $[0, 1]$ are finer than the basic sets in $h^\omega$; not every closed interval is in the range of $\pi^h$. We thus need to refine $S$ by replacing every $I \in S$ by finitely many intervals in the range of $\pi^h$. While we can control the Lebesgue measure of such a collection, if the exponent $s$ is smaller than 1 then the process of replacing large intervals by a collection of smaller ones may increase the $s$-weighted sum of the lengths of the intervals significantly. We show that if $h$ does not grow too irregularly and we increase the exponent $s$ slightly, then this sum remains finite.

Let $\mathcal{I}_n = \pi^h(h^n)$ and let $\mathcal{I} = \bigcup_n \mathcal{I}_n = \pi^h(h^{<\omega})$. Let $\gamma_n = \frac{1}{\lvert h^n \rvert}$. Then $\mathcal{I}_n$ consists of $\lvert h^n \rvert$ many closed intervals, each of length $\gamma_n$.

We express the regularity of $h$ via the following condition for $h$ and $t > s \geqslant 0$:

$$\sum_n \frac{h(n)^{1-s}}{(h(0) \cdots h(n))^{t-s}} < \infty. \tag{13.1}$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.6 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

Suppose that $(13.1)$ holds for $t$, $s$, and $h$, and that $S$ is a set of closed intervals in $[0, 1]$ such that $\sum_{I \in S} \lvert I \rvert^s$ is finite. Then $\sum_{I \in \widehat{S}} \lvert I \rvert^t$ is also finite.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $I$ be any closed interval in $[0, 1]$. Let $n_I$ be the unique $n$ such that $\gamma_n \geqslant \lvert I \rvert > \gamma_{n+1}$. Since $\frac{\lvert I \rvert}{\gamma_{n+1}} > k$ and $k \leqslant h(n)$,

$$\lvert I \rvert^t = (k+1) \gamma_{n+1}^t \leqslant 2k \gamma_{n+1}^{t-s} \gamma_{n+1}^s = 2k \frac{\gamma_{n+1}^s}{\lvert I \rvert^s} \gamma_{n+1}^{t-s} \lvert I \rvert^s$$

$$< 2k^{1-s} \gamma_{n+1}^{t-s} \lvert I \rvert^s \leqslant \frac{2h(n)^{1-s}}{(h(0) \cdots h(n))^{t-s}} \lvert I \rvert^s.$$

Thus for all $X \in h^\omega$, if (13.1) holds for $t$, $s$, and $h$, and $\dim^{[0,1]}(\pi^h(X)) < s$, then $\dim^h(X) \leqslant t$. So if (13.1) holds for all $t > s \geqslant 0$, then $\dim^{[0,1]}(\pi^h(X)) = \dim^h(X)$ for all $X \in h^\omega$.

For example, (13.1) holds for all $t > s \geqslant 0$ for the constant function $h(n) = 2$. Thus, as discussed above, dimension in $[0, 1]$ is the same as dimension in $2^\omega$. However, this condition holds for some unbounded functions $h$ as well (for example $h(n) = 2^n$).

The following is a sufficient condition for (13.1) to hold for all $t > s \geqslant 0$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.7 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If

$$\lim_n \frac{\log h(n)}{\sum_{m \leqslant n} \log h(m)} = 0$$

then $(13.1)$ holds for all $t > s \geqslant 0$, and so $\dim^h(X) = \dim^{[0,1]}(\pi^h(X))$ for all $X \in h^\omega$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f(n) = \log h(n)$. Let $t > s \geqslant 0$ and let $\varepsilon < \frac{t-s}{1-s}$. For sufficiently large $n$, we have $f(n) < \varepsilon \sum_{m \leqslant n} f(m)$. Let $g(n) = h(0) \cdots h(n)$ and let $\delta = \varepsilon(1-s) - (t-s) < 0$. For sufficiently large $n$, we have $h(n) < g(n)^\varepsilon$, and hence $\frac{h(n)^{1-s}}{g(n)^{1-s}} < g(n)^\delta$. Thus there is an $N$ such that

$$\sum_{n \geqslant N} \frac{h(n)^{1-s}}{(h(0) \cdots h(n))^{t-s}} < \sum_n g(n)^\delta = \sum_n (2^\delta)^{\log g(n)},$$

which is finite because $2^\delta < 1$ and $\log g(n) \geqslant n$ (since $h(n) \geqslant 2$). $\square$

The regularity condition of Lemma 13.9.7 is not, strictly speaking, a slowness condition, because, for example, $h(n) = 2^{n^2}$ satisfies this condition, yet there is a monotone function that is dominated by $h$ but does not satisfy the condition. However, the condition does hold for all sufficiently slow monotone functions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.8 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If $h$ is nondecreasing and dominated by $2^{kn}$ for some $k$, then

$$\lim_n \frac{\log h(n)}{\sum_{m \leqslant n} \log h(m)} = 0,$$

and so $\dim^h(X) = \dim^{[0,1]}(\pi^h(X))$ for all $X \in h^\omega$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $h$ is bounded then it is eventually constant, and the condition is easily verified, so assume that $h$ is unbounded. Fix $c > 0$. There is an $N_c$ such that $\log h(n) > c$ for all $n \geqslant N_c$. For $n > N_c$,

$$\frac{\log h(n)}{\sum_{m \leqslant n} \log h(m)} \leqslant \frac{kn}{\sum_{N_c}^n \log h(m)} \leqslant \frac{kn}{c(n - N_c)}.$$

Since $\lim_n \frac{kn}{c(n - N_c)} = \frac{k}{c}$, and $c$ is arbitrary, this limit is 0. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.9 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If $h$ is nondecreasing and dominated by $2^{kn}$ for some $k$, then every $X \in h^\omega$ computes a $Y \in 2^\omega$ such that $\dim^h(X) = \dim(Y)$.

</div>

#### 13.9.2 Slow-growing DNC functions and sets of high effective dimension

For natural numbers $a > b > 0$, let $Q_a^b$ be the collection of functions $f$ such that for all $n$,

1. $f(n) \subseteq \lbrace 0, \ldots, a - 1 \rbrace$,
2. $\lvert f(n) \rvert = b$, and
3. if $\Phi_n(n) \downarrow$ then $\Phi_n(n) \notin f(n)$.

By standard coding, $Q_a^b$ can be seen as a computably bounded $\Pi_1^0$ subclass of $\omega^\omega$. Note that $Q_a^1$ is essentially the same as $\text{DNC}_a$.

To connect these sets for different values of $a$ and $b$, we will use the concept of strong reducibility of mass problems from Definition 8.9.1. Recall that $P \subseteq \omega^\omega$ is strongly reducible to $R \subseteq \omega^\omega$ if there is a Turing functional $\Psi$ such that $\Psi^f \in P$ for all $f \in R$. If $P$ is a $\Pi_1^0$ class, then we may assume that the $\Psi$ in the above definition is total, and hence the reduction is a truth table reduction, since we can always define $\Psi^X(n) = 0$ if there is an $s$ such that $X \notin P[s]$ and $\Psi^X(n)[s] \uparrow$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.10 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If $a > b > 0$ then $Q_{a}^{b+1} \leqslant_s Q_a^b$, uniformly in $a$ and $b$. (Uniformity here means that an index for the reduction functional $\Psi$ can be obtained effectively from $a$ and $b$.)

</div>

*Proof.* From $n$ and $y < a$ we can compute an $m_{n,y}$ such that

1. for all $x < a$ such that $x \neq y$, we have $\Phi_{m_{n,y}}(m_{n,y}) \downarrow = x$ iff $\Phi_n(n) \downarrow = x$; and
2. $\Phi_{m_{n,y}}(m_{n,y}) \downarrow = y$ iff either $\Phi_n(n) \downarrow = y$ or $\Phi_n(n) \downarrow = a$.

Let $f \in Q_a^b$. Let $\widehat{f}(n) = \bigcup_{y < a} f(m_{n,y})$. Fix $n$. For all $y < a$, if $\Phi_n(n) \downarrow$ then $\Phi_n(n) \notin f(m_{n,y})$, so $\Phi_n(n) \notin \widehat{f}(n)$. Furthermore, if there is some $y < a$ such that $y \in f(m_{n,y})$, then $\Phi_n(n) \neq a$, so $\Phi_n(n) \notin \widehat{f}(n) \cup \lbrace a \rbrace$. Finally, if $\lvert \widehat{f}(n) \rvert = b$ then $f(m_{n,y})$ is in fact constant for all $y < a$, so that $y \in f(m_{n,y})$ for all $y \in \widehat{f}(n)$. Thus we can define $\Psi$ as follows:

$$\Psi^f(n) = \begin{cases} \text{some subset of } \widehat{f}(n) \text{ of size } b + 1 & \text{if } \lvert \widehat{f}(n) \rvert > b \\ \widehat{f}(n) \cup \lbrace a \rbrace & \text{if } \lvert \widehat{f}(n) \rvert = b. \end{cases}$$

$\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.11 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If $a \geqslant 2$ then $Q_{a+b}^{b+1} \leqslant_s \text{DNC}_a$, uniformly in $a$ and $b$.

</div>

For $a \geqslant 2$ and $c > 0$, let $P_a^c$ be the collection of functions $f \in a^\omega$ such that for all $n$ and all $x < c$, if $\Phi_{\langle n, x \rangle}(\langle n, x \rangle) \downarrow$ then $\Phi_{\langle n, x \rangle}(\langle n, x \rangle) \neq f(n)$. Note that $P_a^1 \equiv_s \text{DNC}_a$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.12 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

For all $a > b > 0$ and $c > 0$, if $c(a - b) < a$ then $P_a^c \leqslant_s Q_a^b$, uniformly in $a$, $b$, and $c$.

</div>

*Proof.* Fix $f \in Q_a^b$ and $n$. For all $x < c$, if $\Phi_{\langle n, x \rangle}(\langle n, x \rangle) \downarrow$ then

$$\Phi_{\langle n, x \rangle}(\langle n, x \rangle) \in [0, a) \setminus \bigcup_{y < c} f(\langle n, y \rangle).$$

The set on the right has size at most $c(a - b)$, so if $c(a - b) < a$ then we can choose some $x < a$ not in that set and define $\Psi^f(n) = x$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.13 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

If $a \geqslant 2$ and $c > 0$ then $P_{ca}^c \leqslant_s \text{DNC}_a$, uniformly in $a$ and $c$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $b = c(a-1) - a + 1$. Then $Q_{a+b}^{b+1} \leqslant_s \text{DNC}_a$ and

$$c((a+b) - (b+1)) = c(a-1) < a + b,$$

so $P_{c(a-1)+1}^c \subseteq P_{ca+b}^c$, and hence $P_{ca}^c \leqslant_s P_{c(a-1)+1}^c$. All these reductions are uniform. $\square$

Greenberg and Miller used the classes $P_{ca}^c$ to construct sequences of positive effective dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.14 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

Let $a \geqslant 2$ and $\varepsilon > 0$. Every $f \in \text{DNC}_a$ computes a set of effective Hausdorff dimension greater than $1 - \varepsilon$, via a reduction that is uniform in $a$ and $\varepsilon$.

</div>

*Proof.* Fix $c > 1$. We work in the space $(ca)^\omega$. Let $d$ be the universal c.e. supermartingale for this space. By scaling we may assume that $d(\lambda) < 1$.

For $\sigma \in (ca)^{<\omega}$, let $S_\sigma$ be the set of $k < c$ such that $d(\sigma k) \geqslant a^{\lvert \sigma \rvert + 1}$. Note that these sets are uniformly c.e. From $\sigma$, we can compute an $m_\sigma$ such that for each $x < c$, we have $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \downarrow = k$ if $k$ is the $x$th element to be enumerated into $S_\sigma$, and $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \uparrow$ if $\lvert S_\sigma \rvert < x$.

The idea here is that if $d(\sigma) \leqslant a^{\lvert \sigma \rvert}$ then $\lvert S_\sigma \rvert \leqslant c$, by the supermartingale condition, so all elements of $S_\sigma$ are "captured" by the map $e \mapsto \Phi_e(e)$. We can thus use a function $g \in P_{ca}^c$ to avoid all such extensions: given such $g$, inductively define $X \in (ca)^{<\omega}$ by letting the $(n+1)$st digit of $X$ be $g(m_{X \restriction n})$. Then, by induction, $d(X \restriction n) \leqslant a^n$ for all $n$.

Now, $\mu^{ca}(X \restriction n) = \frac{a^{-n}}{n!}$, so for $s \geqslant 0$,

$$d(X \restriction n) \mu^{ca}(X \restriction n)^{1-s} \leqslant \frac{a^{sn}}{(n!)^{1-s}}.$$

Let $s < 1$. Let $k$ be such that $k^{s-1} a^{2s-1} < 1$. For almost all $n$ we have $n! > (ka)^n$, so for almost all $n$ we have

$$\frac{a^{sn}}{(n!)^{1-s}} \leqslant \frac{a^{sn}}{(ka)^{n(1-s)}} = (k^{s-1} a^{2s-1})^n < 1,$$

and hence $d$ cannot $s$-succeed on $X$. Thus $\dim^h(X) = 1$, so by Corollary 13.9.9, $X$ computes a $Y \in 2^\omega$ of effective dimension $> 1 - \varepsilon$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.15 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

Let $a \geqslant 2$. Every $f \in \text{DNC}_a$ computes a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1, via a reduction that is uniform in $a$.

</div>

*Proof.* We combine the constructions of sets of effective dimensions closer and closer to 1 into one construction. Let $h(n) = (n+1)a$. Let $d$ be the universal c.e. supermartingale for $h^\omega$. Given $f \in \text{DNC}_a$, obtain $g_n \in P_{na}^n$ for all $n > 0$ uniformly from $f$.

For $\sigma \in h^n$, let $S_\sigma$ be the set of $k \leqslant n$ such that $d(\sigma k) \geqslant a^{\lvert \sigma \rvert + 1}$ and compute an $m_\sigma$ such that for each $x \leqslant n$, we have $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \downarrow = k$ if $k$ is the $x$th element to be enumerated into $S_\sigma$, and $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \uparrow$ if $\lvert S_\sigma \rvert < x$.

As in the previous proof, we can define $X \in h^\omega$ and inductively prove that $d(X \restriction n) \leqslant a^n$ for all $n$.

Now, $\mu^h(X \restriction n) = \frac{a^{-n}}{n!}$, so for $s \geqslant 0$,

$$d(X \restriction n) \mu^h(X \restriction n)^{1-s} \leqslant \frac{a^{sn}}{(n!)^{1-s}}.$$

Let $s < 1$. Let $k$ be such that $k^{s-1} a^{2s-1} < 1$. For almost all $n$ we have $n! > (ka)^n$, so for almost all $n$ we have

$$\frac{a^{sn}}{(n!)^{1-s}} \leqslant \frac{a^{sn}}{(ka)^{n(1-s)}} = (k^{s-1} a^{2s-1})^n < 1,$$

and hence $d$ cannot $s$-succeed on $X$. Thus $\dim^h(X) = 1$, so by Corollary 13.9.9, $X$ computes a $Y \in 2^\omega$ of effective dimension 1. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.16 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

There is a computable order $\widetilde{h} : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ such that every $f \in \text{DNC}_{\widetilde{h}}$ computes a set of effective Hausdorff dimension 1.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $h(n) = (n+1)2^n$, and let $d$ be the universal c.e. supermartingale for $h^\omega$. For $\sigma \in h^n$, let $S_\sigma$ be the set of $k \leqslant n$ such that $d(\sigma k) \geqslant (n+1)!$ and compute an $m_\sigma$ such that for each $x < 2^n$, we have $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \downarrow = k$ if $k$ is the $x$th element to be enumerated into $S_\sigma$, and $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \uparrow$ if $\lvert S_\sigma \rvert < x$.

For $n > 0$, we have $P_{h(n)}^{2^n} \leqslant \text{DNC}_{n+1}$ uniformly in $n$, so there is an effective list of truth table functionals $\Psi_n$ such that $\Psi_n^f \in P_{h(n)}^{2^n}$ for all $f \in \text{DNC}_{n+1}$. Let $\psi_n$ be a computable bound on the use function of $\Psi_n$. Let

$$m_n^* = 1 + \sup \lbrace \langle m_\sigma, x \rangle : \sigma \in h^n \wedge x < 2^n \rbrace$$

and let $u_n = \psi_n(m_n^*)$. Let $u_0 = 0$.

For all $n > 0$, if $\rho$ is a sequence of length $u_n$ that is a $\text{DNC}_{n+1}$-string (that is, $\rho \in (n+1)^{u_n}$ and for all $y < u_n$ such that $\Phi_y(y) \downarrow$, we have $\Phi_y(y) \neq \rho(y)$; or equivalently, $\rho$ is an initial segment of a sequence in $\text{DNC}_{n+1}$) then $\Psi_n(\rho)$ is a $P_{h(n)}^{2^n}$-string (an initial segment of a sequence in $P_{h(n)}^{2^n}$) of length at least $m_n^*$. By increasing $\psi_n$ we may assume that $u_n < u_{n+1}$ for all $n > 0$. Let $\widetilde{h}(k) = n + 1$ for $k \in [u_{n-1}, u_n)$.

If $f \in \text{DNC}_{\widetilde{h}}$ then $f \restriction u_n$ is a $\text{DNC}_{n+1}$-string for all $n > 0$, so combining the reductions $\Psi_n$, we can define a $g \leqslant_T f$ such that for all $n$ and all $\sigma \in h^n$,

1. $g(m_\sigma) < h(\lvert \sigma \rvert)$ and
2. for all $x < 2^n$, if $\Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle) \downarrow$ then $g(m_\sigma) \neq \Phi_{\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle}(\langle m_\sigma, x \rangle)$.

We can now use $g$ to define $X \in h^\omega$ as in the last two constructions, by letting $X(n) = g(m_{X \restriction n})$. By induction on $n$, we can show that $d(X \restriction n) \leqslant n!$. As before, we can do so because if $\sigma \in h^n$ and $d(\sigma) \leqslant n!$, there are at most $2^n$ many immediate successors $\tau$ of $\sigma$ such that $d(\tau) \geqslant (n+1)!$, and so they are all captured by the function $e \mapsto \Phi_e(e)$ and avoided by $g$.

Finally, we show that $\dim^h(X) = 1$, which by Corollary 13.9.9 implies that $X$ computes a $Y \in 2^\omega$ of effective dimension 1.

Let $s < 1$. For any $\sigma \in h^n$,

$$\mu^h(\sigma) = \frac{1}{2^0 2^1 \cdots 2^{(n-1)} n!} = \frac{1}{2^{\binom{n}{2}} n!}.$$

Thus for all $n$,

$$d(X \restriction n) \mu^h(X \restriction n)^{1-s} \leqslant \frac{(n!)^s}{2^{(1-s)\binom{n}{2}}},$$

which is bounded (and indeed tends to 0). Thus $d$ does not $s$-succeed on $X$. $\square$

Thus, by Theorem 13.9.1, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.9.17 — Greenberg and Miller)</span></p>

There is a minimal degree of effective Hausdorff dimension 1.

</div>

### 13.10 $C$-independence and Zimand's Theorem

By Miller's Theorem 13.8.1, we cannot always extract a sequence of arbitrarily high effective Hausdorff dimension from one of nonzero effective Hausdorff dimension. In this section we prove Zimand's theorem that such extraction *is* possible from *two* sufficiently independent sources of positive effective Hausdorff dimension.

We begin by briefly discussing the notion of independence of sources. This notion dates back at least to Chaitin, who suggested that objects $x$ and $y$ should be independent in terms of their information if $I(x) - I(x \mid y)$ and $I(y) - I(y \mid x)$ are small, where $I$ is some kind of information content measure in the wide sense, such as $C$ or $K$. For 1-random sets $A$ and $B$, we could regard $A$ and $B$ as being independent iff they are mutually 1-random.

But what about nonrandom sets? Suppose that $A$ and $B$ have positive effective Hausdorff dimension but are not 1-random. What would it mean to say that $A$ and $B$ are independent? The theory here is largely undeveloped, but the following notions of independence are useful for our purposes. In this section, we will regard log factors as being small.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.1 — Zimand, Calude and Zimand)</span></p>

1. $X$ and $Y$ are *$C$-independent* if

   $$C((X \restriction m)^\frown(Y \restriction n)) \geqslant C(X \restriction m) + C(Y \restriction n) - O(\log m + \log n).$$

2. $X$ and $Y$ are *globally independent* if

   $$C^X(Y \restriction n) \geqslant C(Y \restriction n) - O(\log n)$$

   and

   $$C^Y(X \restriction n) \geqslant C(X \restriction n) - O(\log n).$$

</div>

Since $C$ and $K$ are within an $O(\log)$ factor of each other, both definitions remain the same if we replace $C$ by $K$. Note also that $C((X \restriction m)^\frown(Y \restriction n))$ and $C((Y \restriction n)^\frown(X \restriction m))$ are the same up to an $O(\log m + \log n)$ factor, so the first definition does not depend on the order of $X$ and $Y$.

Clearly, if $X$ and $Y$ are mutually 1-random, then they are globally independent, so global independence can be seen as an extension of the notion of mutual randomness. If $X$ is $K$-trivial, then $X$ and $Y$ are globally independent for all $Y$, since $K(X \restriction n) \leqslant O(\log n)$ and $X$ is low for $K$, so $K^X(Y \restriction n) = K(Y \restriction n) \pm O(1)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.2 — Zimand, Calude and Zimand)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $X$ and $Y$ are $C$-independent.
2. $C(X \restriction m \mid Y \restriction n) \geqslant C(X \restriction m) - O(\log m + \log n)$.
3. $C((X \restriction n)^\frown(Y \restriction n)) \geqslant C(X \restriction n) + C(Y \restriction n) - O(\log n)$.
4. $C(X \restriction n \mid Y \restriction n) \geqslant C(X \restriction n) - O(\log n)$.

The same holds for $K$ in place of $C$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.3 — Calude and Zimand)</span></p>

If $X$ and $Y$ are globally independent, then $X$ and $Y$ are $C$-independent.

</div>

*Proof.* If we have $Y$ as an oracle, we can describe $X \restriction n$ by giving $n$ and a description of $X \restriction n$ from $Y \restriction n$, so $C^Y(X \restriction n) \leqslant C(X \restriction n \mid Y \restriction n) + O(\log n)$. So if $X$ and $Y$ are globally independent, then $C(X \restriction n \mid Y \restriction n) \geqslant C^Y(X \restriction n) - O(\log n) \geqslant C(X \restriction n) - O(\log n)$, and hence item (iv) of Lemma 13.10.2 holds. $\square$

Stephan showed that the converse fails: there are $C$-independent $X$ and $Y$ that are not globally independent. He also showed the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.4 — Stephan)</span></p>

If $X$ and $Y$ are left-c.e. reals of positive effective Hausdorff dimension, then $X$ and $Y$ are not $C$-independent.

</div>

*Proof.* Without loss of generality, there are infinitely many $n$ such that, for the least $s$ for which $Y_s \restriction n = Y \restriction n$, we also have $X_s \restriction n = X \restriction n$. For all such $n$, we have $C(X \restriction n \mid Y \restriction n) = O(1)$. But then, since $\dim(X) > 0$, item (iv) of Lemma 13.10.2 cannot hold. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.5 — Calude and Zimand)</span></p>

If $Y$ is 1-random relative to $X$, then $X$ and $Y$ are $C$-independent.

</div>

*Proof.* By the same argument as in the proof of Theorem 13.10.3, $K^X(Y \restriction n) \leqslant K(Y \restriction n \mid X \restriction n) + 2 \log n + O(1)$. If $X$ and $Y$ are not $C$-independent then there are infinitely many $n$ with $K(Y \restriction n \mid X \restriction n) < K(Y \restriction n) - 5 \log n < n - 3 \log n$. For such $n$, we have $K^X(Y \restriction n) \leqslant n - \log n + O(1)$, contradicting the fact that $Y$ is 1-random relative to $X$. $\square$

We now turn to the task of amplifying the complexity of two $C$-independent sources.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.6 — Zimand)</span></p>

For any rational $q > 0$, there is a truth table reduction $\Psi$ such that if $X$ and $Y$ are $C$-independent and have effective Hausdorff dimension greater than $q$, then $\dim(\Psi^{X \oplus Y}) = 1$. Moreover, an index for $\Psi$ can be determined computably from $q$.

</div>

The idea of the proof will be to chop $X$ and $Y$ into suitable bits and reassemble them. A technical combinatorial lemma (Lemma 13.10.14) will be at the heart of the proof. It will use a well-known tool from probability theory, Chernoff bounds, which can be found in any standard probability textbook, such as Feller.

We give a brief introduction to the main ideas of the proof, following Zimand. Suppose that we have two independent strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$ of length $n$ with $C(\sigma)$ and $C(\tau)$ both equal to $qn$ for a positive rational $q$. (The independence here means that $C(\sigma\tau)$ is approximately $C(\sigma) + C(\tau) = 2qn$.) Suppose further that we can construct a function $E : 2^n \times 2^n \to 2^m$ for some suitable $m$, such that $E$ is *regular*, in the sense that for each sufficiently large rectangle $B_1 \times B_2 \subseteq 2^n \times 2^n$, the function $E$ maps about the same number of pairs in $B_1 \times B_2$ to each $\tau \in 2^m$. Then for a sufficiently large $B \times B \subseteq 2^{qn} \times 2^{qn}$, any $\tau \in 2^m$ has about $\frac{\lvert B \times B \rvert}{2^m}$ many preimages, and any $A \subseteq 2^m$ has about $\frac{\lvert B \times B \rvert}{2^m} \lvert A \rvert$ many preimages.

We claim that if $\rho = E(\sigma, \tau)$ then the $C$-complexity of $\rho$ must be large. Assume for a contradiction that $C(\rho) < (1 - \varepsilon)m$ for a fairly large $\varepsilon$. We have the following facts.

1. The set $B = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : C(\sigma) = qn \rbrace$ has size approximately $2^{qn}$.
2. The set $A = \lbrace \tau \in 2^m : C(\tau) < (1 - \varepsilon)m \rbrace$ has size less than $2^{(1-\varepsilon)m}$.
3. $(\sigma, \tau) \in E^{-1}(A) \cap B \times B$.

Thus $\lvert E^{-1}(A) \cap B \times B \rvert \leqslant \frac{(2^{qn})^2}{2^m}$, approximately. If we effectively enumerate $E^{-1}(A) \cap B \times B$, then each pair of strings in that set can be described by its position in that enumeration, so $C(\sigma, \tau) \leqslant 2qn - \varepsilon m$, approximately, which violates the independence hypothesis on $\sigma$ and $\tau$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.12)</span></p>

A function $E : 2^n \times 2^n \to 2^m$ is *$r$-regular* if for every $B_1, B_2 \subseteq 2^n$ with $\lvert B_i \rvert \geqslant rn$ for $i = 1, 2$ and every $\sigma \in 2^m$,

$$\lvert E^{-1}(\sigma) \cap (B_1 \times B_2) \rvert \leqslant 2^{-m+1} \lvert B_1 \times B_2 \rvert.$$

</div>

The function $E$ is *weakly $r$-regular* if it obeys the above definition when we consider only $B_i$ with $\lvert B_1 \rvert = \lvert B_2 \rvert = \lceil rn \rceil$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.13)</span></p>

If $E : 2^n \times 2^n \to 2^m$ is weakly $r$-regular then $E$ is regular.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.14)</span></p>

For each $r > 0$, if $2^m < r 2^{.99n}$ then with positive probability, a randomly chosen function $E : 2^n \times 2^n \to 2^m$ will be $r$-regular. In particular, an $r$-regular $E : 2^n \times 2^n \to 2^m$ exists (and hence can be found effectively by searching).

</div>

The proof uses Chernoff bounds and a counting argument showing that the probability that any large rectangle violates regularity is small.

The proof of Theorem 13.10.6 proceeds by splitting $X$ and $Y$ into blocks $X_1 X_2 \ldots$ and $Y_1 Y_2 \ldots$, respectively. Each $X_i$ is chosen based on the conditional complexity the previous blocks, and similarly for the $Y_i$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.7)</span></p>

Let $r$ be a rational such that $0 < r < q$. For any sufficiently large $n_0$, we can compute an $n_1 > n_0$ such that

$$C(X \restriction (n_0, n_1) \mid X \restriction n_0) > r(n_1 - n_0).$$

</div>

*Proof.* Let $0 < r' < q - r$ and let $n_1 = \lceil \frac{1-r}{r'} \rceil n_0$. Suppose that $C(X \restriction (n_0, n_1) \mid X \restriction n_0) \leqslant r(n_1 - n_0)$. The string $X \restriction n_1$ can be described by giving a description of $X \restriction n_0$ and a description of $X \restriction (n_0, n_1)$ given $X \restriction n_0$. Thus, for sufficiently large $n_0$, we have

$$C(X \restriction n_1) \leqslant n_0 + O(\log n_0) + r(n_1 - n_0) \leqslant rn_1 + (1-r)n_0 + O(\log n_0) \leqslant rn_1 + r'n_1 + O(\log n_0) < qn_1.$$

$\square$

We use Lemma 13.10.7 to split $X$ and $Y$ into blocks $X_1 X_2 \ldots$ and $Y_1 Y_2 \ldots$, respectively, and then apply $r$-regular functions $E_i$ to produce $Z_i = E_i(X_i, Y_i)$ with $Z = Z_1 Z_2 \ldots$. The key technical lemmas establish that the conditional complexities of the $X_i$ and $Y_i$ blocks are well-behaved, and that $C(Z \restriction k)$ is sufficiently close to $k$ for all $k$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.8)</span></p>

The following hold for all $i$.

1. $C(X_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1}) > r \lvert X_i \rvert$.
2. $\log \lvert X_i \rvert \sim i$ and $\log \lvert \vec{X}_i \rvert \sim i$.

The same holds for the $Y_i$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.9)</span></p>

For all $\sigma$ and $\tau$,

1. $C(\sigma \tau) \leqslant C(\sigma) + C(\tau) + O(\log C(\sigma) + \log C(\tau))$,
2. $C(\tau \sigma) \geqslant C(\sigma) + C(\tau \mid \sigma) - O(\log C(\sigma) + \log C(\tau))$, and
3. $\lvert C(\sigma \mid \tau) - (C(\sigma\tau) - C(\tau)) \rvert < O(\log \lvert \sigma \rvert + \log \lvert \tau \rvert)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.10)</span></p>

For all $i$ and $j$,

1. $\lvert C(\vec{Y}_i \vec{X}_j) - (C(\vec{Y}_i) + C(\vec{X}_j)) \rvert \leqslant O(i + j)$,
2. $\lvert C(\vec{X}_i \vec{Y}_j) - (C(\vec{X}_i) + C(\vec{Y}_j)) \rvert \leqslant O(i + j)$,
3. $\lvert C(X_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_j) - C(X_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1}) \rvert \leqslant O(i + j)$, and
4. $\lvert C(Y_i \mid \vec{X}_j \vec{Y}_{i-1}) - C(Y_i \mid \vec{Y}_{i-1}) \rvert \leqslant O(i + j)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.11)</span></p>

For all $i$,

$$C(X_i Y_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_{i-1}) \geqslant C(X_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_{i-1}) + C(Y_i \mid \vec{X}_i \vec{Y}_{i-1}) - O(i).$$

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.15)</span></p>

For all $\varepsilon > 0$ and all sufficiently large $i$, we have $C(Z_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_{i-1}) \geqslant (1 - \varepsilon) m_i$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.10.16)</span></p>

For any $\delta > 0$ and $n$, we have $C(Z \restriction n) \geqslant (1 - \delta) n - O(1)$. Hence $Z$ has effective Hausdorff dimension 1.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\varepsilon = \frac{\delta}{4}$. By Lemma 13.10.15, $C(Z_i \mid \vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_{i-1}) \geqslant (1 - \varepsilon) m_i$ for almost all $i$. Thus $(Z_i \mid \vec{Z}_{i-1}) \geqslant (1 - \varepsilon) m_i - O(1)$, since we can compute $\vec{Z}_{i-1}$ from $\vec{X}_{i-1} \vec{Y}_{i-1}$. A straightforward induction shows that $C(\vec{Z}_i) \geqslant (1 - 3\varepsilon)(m_1 + \cdots + m_i)$ for sufficiently large $i$.

Now take $\sigma = Z \restriction n$ between $\vec{Z}_{i-1}$ and $\vec{Z}_i$, and assume for a contradiction that $C(\sigma) \leqslant (1 - \delta) \lvert \sigma \rvert$. Then we can describe $\vec{Z}_{i-1}$ by giving a description of $\sigma$, the string $\tau$ such that $\sigma = \vec{Z}_{i-1} \tau$, which takes a further $\lvert \sigma \rvert - \lvert \vec{Z}_{i-1} \rvert \leqslant m_i$ many bits, and $O(\log m_i)$ many further bits to separate the descriptions of $\sigma$ and $\tau$. Therefore, for sufficiently large $i$,

$$C(\vec{Z}_{i-1}) \leqslant (1 - 4\varepsilon)(m_1 + \cdots + m_{i-1}) + (2 - 4\varepsilon) m_i + O(\log m_i)$$

$$< (1 - 3\varepsilon)(m_1 + \cdots + m_{i-1}),$$

the last inequality holding because $\lim_i \frac{m_i}{m_1 + \cdots + m_{i-1}} = 0$. Thus we have a contradiction, and hence $C(Z \restriction n) > (1 - \delta) n$ for almost all $n$. $\square$

This lemma completes the proof of Theorem 13.10.6.

Note that the above result implies that Miller's degree of effective Hausdorff dimension $\frac{1}{2}$ cannot compute two $C$-independent sources of positive effective Hausdorff dimension. This fact likely has interesting consequences for the computational power of such degrees.

### 13.11 Other Notions of Dimension

Hausdorff's extension of Carathéodory's $s$-dimensional measure is certainly not the only generalization of the notion of dimension to non-integer values in geometric measure theory and fractal geometry, and it is also not the only one to have been effectivized. In this section, we will briefly look at a couple of further dimension concepts and their effectivizations. Further details about the classical notions may be found in Federer.

#### 13.11.1 Box counting dimension

For $C \subseteq 2^\omega$, let $C \restriction n = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : \exists \alpha \in C \, (\sigma \prec \alpha) \rbrace$ and define the *upper* and *lower box counting dimensions* of $C$ as

$$\overline{\dim}_\text{B}(C) = \limsup_n \frac{\log(\lvert C \restriction n \rvert)}{n} \quad \text{and} \quad \underline{\dim}_\text{B}(C) = \liminf_n \frac{\log(\lvert C \restriction n \rvert)}{n},$$

respectively. If $\overline{\dim}_\text{B}$ and $\underline{\dim}_\text{B}$ coincide, then this value is called the *box counting dimension*, or *Minkowski dimension*, of $C$. The name "box counting" comes from the fact that, for each level $n$, one simply counts the number of boxes of size $2^{-n}$ needed to cover $C$. The following is clear.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.1)</span></p>

For any $C \subseteq 2^\omega$, we have $\dim_\text{H}(C) \leqslant \underline{\dim}_\text{B}(C) \leqslant \overline{\dim}_\text{B}(C)$.

</div>

(Lower) box counting dimension gives an easy upper bound on Hausdorff dimension, although this estimate may not be very precise. For instance, let $C$ be the class of all sequences that are 0 from some point on. Then we have $\dim_\text{H}(C) = 0$ but $\underline{\dim}_\text{B}(C) = 1$. (In fact, $\overline{\dim}_\text{B}(D) = 1$ for any dense $D \subseteq 2^\omega$.) This observation shows that, in general, box counting dimension is not a stable concept of dimension, since a countable union of classes of box counting dimension 0 such as $C$ can have box counting dimension 1. Staiger has investigated some conditions under which Hausdorff and box counting dimension coincide. Probably the most famous example of such a set is the Mandelbrot set.

One can modify box counting dimension to obtain a countably stable notion, yielding the concept of *modified box counting dimension*, defined as follows. The *lower modified box counting dimension* of $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\dim_\text{MB}(C) = \inf \left\lbrace \sup_i \underline{\dim}_\text{B}(X_i) : C \subseteq \bigcup_i X_i \right\rbrace.$$

The *upper modified box counting dimension* of $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\overline{\dim}_\text{MB}(C) = \inf \left\lbrace \sup_i \overline{\dim}_\text{B}(X_i) : C \subseteq \bigcup_i X_i \right\rbrace.$$

If these values coincide, then they define the *modified box counting dimension* $\dim_\text{MB}(C)$ of $C$. (That is, we split up a set into countably many parts and look at the dimension of the "most complicated" part. Then we optimize this value by looking for the partition with the lowest such dimension.)

The modified box counting dimensions behave more stably than their original counterparts; in particular, all countable classes have modified box counting dimension 0. However, these dimensions are usually hard to calculate, due to the extra inf/sup process involved in their definitions.

#### 13.11.2 Effective box counting dimension

The effectivization of box counting dimension is due to Reimann. An equivalent formulation of effective upper box counting dimension was also given by Athreya, Hitchcock, Lutz, and Mayordomo. This concept turns out to coincide with effective packing dimension, which we discuss below.

We will be concerned only with the dimension of individual sets, and hence will not look at the modified versions of box counting dimension needed for countable stability.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.2 — Reimann)</span></p>

A c.e. set $C \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$ is an *effective box cover* of $B \in 2^\omega$ if $B \restriction n \in C$ for almost all $n$.

</div>

Effective box counting dimension measures how efficiently the initial segments of a set can be "enwrapped" in a c.e. set of strings. For $D \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, let $D^{[n]} = D \cap 2^n$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.3 — Reimann)</span></p>

Let $B \in 2^\omega$. The *effective lower box counting dimension* of $B$ is

$$\underline{\dim}^1_\text{B}(B) = \inf \left\lbrace \liminf_n \frac{\log \lvert C^{[n]} \rvert}{n} : C \text{ is an effective box cover of } B \right\rbrace.$$

The *effective upper box counting dimension* of $B$ is

$$\overline{\dim}^1_\text{B}(B) = \inf \left\lbrace \limsup_n \frac{\log \lvert C^{[n]} \rvert}{n} : C \text{ is an effective box cover of } B \right\rbrace.$$

</div>

Effective upper box counting dimension is the same as a concept analyzed by Athreya, Hitchcock, Lutz, and Mayordomo, and Hitchcock, building on work of Staiger.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.4 — Staiger, Athreya, Hitchcock, Lutz, and Mayordomo)</span></p>

For $A \subseteq 2^{<\omega}$, the *entropy rate* of $A$ is

$$H_A = \limsup_n \frac{\log \lvert A^{[n]} \rvert}{n}.$$

For $A \subseteq \mathbb{N} \times 2^{<\omega}$, let $A^{\text{i.o.}} = \lbrace \beta \in 2^\omega : \exists^\infty n \, (\beta \restriction n \in A) \rbrace$ and $A^{\text{a.e.}} = \lbrace \beta \in 2^\omega : \forall^\infty n \, (\beta \restriction n \in A) \rbrace$. For $C \subseteq 2^\omega$, let

$$\mathcal{H}_1(C) = \inf \lbrace H_A : C \subseteq A^{\text{i.o.}} \wedge A \in \Sigma_1^0 \rbrace$$

and

$$\mathcal{H}_1^{\text{str}}(C) = \inf \lbrace H_A : C \subseteq A^{\text{a.e.}} \wedge A \in \Sigma_1^0 \rbrace.$$

</div>

For $B \in 2^\omega$, let $\mathcal{H}_1(B) = \mathcal{H}_1(\lbrace B \rbrace)$ and $\mathcal{H}_1^{\text{str}}(B) = \mathcal{H}_1^{\text{str}}(\lbrace B \rbrace)$.

It is not hard to see that for any $B \in 2^\omega$, we have $\overline{\dim}^1_\text{B}(B) = \mathcal{H}_1^{\text{str}}(B)$ and $\dim(B) = \mathcal{H}_1(B)$.

It is a trivial observation that, as in the classical case, effective box counting dimension always bounds effective Hausdorff dimension from above; in particular, $\dim(B) \leqslant \underline{\dim}^1_\text{B}(B)$ for any $B \in 2^\omega$.

There is clearly a connection between effective box counting and Kolmogorov complexity, as indicated by the following observation of Kolmogorov, which we met in Theorem 3.2.2 (ii).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.5 — Kolmogorov)</span></p>

Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{N} \times 2^{<\omega}$ be computably enumerable and such that $A_m = \lbrace x : (m, x) \in A \rbrace$ is finite for all $m$. Then $C(x \mid m) \leqslant \log \lvert A_m \rvert + O(1)$ for all $m$ and $x \in A_m$.

</div>

#### 13.11.3 Packing dimension

The classical notion of packing dimension, introduced by Tricot, is defined using *packings* — collections of disjoint open balls centered at points of a set. While Hausdorff dimension uses coverings (from above), packing dimension uses packings (from below), and is hence a dual notion.

For $C \subseteq 2^\omega$, define the *$s$-dimensional packing premeasure* of $C$ as

$$\bar{P}^s(C) = \lim_{\delta \to 0} \sup \left\lbrace \sum_i 2^{-s \lvert \sigma_i \rvert} : \lbrace \llbracket \sigma_i \rrbracket \rbrace \text{ is a packing of } C \text{ with } \lvert \sigma_i \rvert \geqslant \frac{1}{\delta} \right\rbrace.$$

The *$s$-dimensional packing measure* is then

$$P^s(C) = \inf \left\lbrace \sum_i \bar{P}^s(C_i) : C \subseteq \bigcup_i C_i \right\rbrace,$$

and the *packing dimension* of $C$ is

$$\dim_\text{P}(C) = \inf \lbrace s : P^s(C) = 0 \rbrace = \sup \lbrace s : P^s(C) = \infty \rbrace.$$

An important classical result connects packing dimension with modified box counting dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.6 — Tricot)</span></p>

For any $C \subseteq 2^\omega$, $\dim_\text{P}(C) = \overline{\dim}_\text{MB}(C)$.

</div>

We also have the following useful characterization, which parallels the characterization of Hausdorff dimension via $s$-supergales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.7)</span></p>

$\dim_\text{P}(C) = \sup \lbrace s : \exists$ an $s$-supergale $d$ that succeeds strongly on all $A \in C \rbrace$.

</div>

The effective packing dimension of a class $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is defined by restricting to c.e. supergales:

$$\text{Dim}(C) = \inf \lbrace s : \text{there is a c.e. } s\text{-supergale that } s\text{-succeeds strongly on all } A \in C \rbrace.$$

For an individual set $A \in 2^\omega$, the effective packing dimension $\text{Dim}(A)$ is the effective packing dimension of $\lbrace A \rbrace$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.8)</span></p>

For any $B \in 2^\omega$, $\text{Dim}(B) = \overline{\dim}^1_\text{B}(B)$.

</div>

The Kolmogorov complexity characterization of effective packing dimension parallels that of effective Hausdorff dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.11.12)</span></p>

For any $A \in 2^\omega$, $\text{Dim}(A) = \limsup_n \frac{K(A \restriction n)}{n}$.

</div>

### 13.12 Packing Dimension and Complexity Extraction

Many of the questions we have investigated for Hausdorff dimension can also be examined for packing dimension. Some of the results for effective Hausdorff dimension immediately imply the same results for effective packing dimension, such as Greenberg and Miller's construction of a minimal Turing degree of effective Hausdorff dimension 1 (Theorem 13.9.17), which implies the theorem of Downey and Greenberg that there is a minimal degree of effective packing dimension 1 (Theorem 13.13.1). In other ways, however, the two notions of effective dimension are quite different. For instance, we will see in Section 13.14 that there is no correspondence principle for effective packing dimension analogous to Theorem 13.6.1. Another example is Miller's Theorem 13.8.1 that there is a Turing degree of effective Hausdorff dimension $\frac{1}{2}$, which has no analog for packing dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.12.1 — Fortnow, Hitchcock, Pavan, Vinochandran, and Wang)</span></p>

Let $\varepsilon > 0$. If $\text{Dim}(A) > 0$ then there is a $B \equiv_{\text{wtt}} A$ such that $\text{Dim}(B) > 1 - \varepsilon$.

</div>

Thus we conclude that there is a 0-1 law for packing dimension for weak truth table, and hence Turing, degrees. Too recently for a proof to be included in this book, Conidis has shown that there is a set of positive effective packing dimension that does not compute any sets of effective packing dimension 1, and hence there is a Turing degree of effective packing dimension 1 with no element of effective packing dimension 1.

We give a new proof of Theorem 13.12.1 due to Laurent Bienvenu. It is rather simple and relies on the relationship between complexities at endpoints of intervals and their oscillations within these intervals.

*Proof of Theorem 13.12.1.* Let $A$ be such that $\text{Dim}(A) > 0$. Let $t > 0$ be a rational such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant tn$ for infinitely many $n$. Let $m$ be a fixed large integer.

We claim that there exists a rational $t' > 0$ such that $C(\alpha \restriction m^k) \geqslant t' m^k$ for infinitely many $k$. To see this, choose any $n$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant tn$. Let $k$ be such that $n \in [m^{k-1}, m^k)$. Then

$$C(\alpha \restriction m^k) \geqslant C(\alpha \restriction n) - O(\log n) \geqslant tn - O(\log n) \geqslant \frac{t}{m} m^k - O(\log m^k).$$

Thus, for any $t' < \frac{t}{m}$, we have $C(\alpha \restriction m^k) \geqslant t' m^k$ for infinitely many $k$.

Now let $\sigma_k = \alpha \restriction m^k$ and let $s = \limsup_k \frac{C(\sigma_k)}{\lvert \sigma_k \rvert}$. By the above claim we know that $s > 0$. Let $s_1$ and $s_2$ be rationals such that $0 < s_1 < s < s_2$. By the definition of $s$, we have $C(\sigma_k) \leqslant s_2 \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$ for almost all $k$ and for any $d$, we have $C(\sigma_k) \geqslant s_1 \lvert \sigma_k \rvert + d$ for infinitely many $k$.

Let $\mathcal{V}$ be our universal machine. Given $A$ as an oracle, we can compute $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ and, for each $k$, effectively find a $\tau_k$ such that $\mathcal{V}(\tau_k) = \sigma_k$ and $\lvert \tau_k \rvert \leqslant s_2 \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$. Let $D$ be the sequence given by $\tau_0 \tau_1 \tau_2 \ldots$. It is easy to see that $D \leqslant_{\text{wtt}} A$. If we replace a sparse set of bits of $D$ by the bits of $A$ (for instance, replacing each $D(2^n)$ by $A(n)$) to get $B$, we clearly have $B \equiv_{\text{wtt}} A$ and $\text{Dim}(B) = \text{Dim}(D)$.

So let us evaluate $\text{Dim}(D)$. For all $k$, we have $C(\tau_k) \geqslant C(\sigma_k) - O(1)$, as $\sigma_k$ can be computed from $\tau_k$. Thus, for infinitely many $k$, we have $C(\tau_k) \geqslant s_1 \lvert \sigma_k \rvert + d$ for infinitely many $k$. Now,

$$\text{Dim}(D) \geqslant \limsup_k \frac{C(\tau_0 \ldots \tau_k)}{\lvert \tau_0 \ldots \tau_k \rvert}.$$

On the one hand, we have $C(\tau_0 \ldots \tau_k) \geqslant C(\tau_k) - O(\log k)$, and thus $C(\tau_0 \ldots \tau_k) \geqslant s_1 m^k - O(\log k)$ for infinitely many $k$. On the other hand, we have $\lvert \tau_k \rvert \leqslant s_2 m^k$ for all $k$. Therefore,

$$\limsup_k \frac{C(\tau_0 \ldots \tau_k)}{\lvert \tau_0 \ldots \tau_k \rvert} \geqslant \limsup_k \frac{s_1 m^k - O(\log k)}{s_2 (1 + m + \ldots + m^k)} \geqslant \frac{s_1}{s_2} \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right).$$

Thus $\text{Dim}(B) = \text{Dim}(D) \geqslant \frac{s_1}{s_2} (1 - \frac{1}{m})$. Since $s_1$ and $s_2$ can be taken arbitrarily close to each other and $m$ arbitrarily large, the theorem is proved. $\square$

On the other hand, packing dimension does have an effect on the complexity extraction question for Hausdorff dimension. The following result implies that if $A$ is a set of positive effective Hausdorff dimension that does not compute any set of higher effective Hausdorff dimension, then $A$ must have effective packing dimension 1.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.12.2 — Bienvenu, Doty, and Stephan)</span></p>

If $\text{Dim}(A) > 0$ then for each $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a $B \leqslant_T A$ such that $\dim(B) \geqslant \frac{\dim(A)}{\text{Dim}(A)} - \varepsilon$.

</div>

*Proof.* We may assume that $\dim(A) > 0$. Let $s_1$, $s_2$, $s_3$, and $t$ be rationals such that $0 < s_1 < \dim(A) < s_2 < s_3$ and $\text{Dim}(A) < t$, and $\frac{s_1}{t + s_3 - s_1} \geqslant \frac{\dim(A)}{\text{Dim}(A)} - \varepsilon$. Let $\sigma_n \in 2^n$ be such that $A = \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \ldots$. The idea of the proof is to split $A$ effectively into blocks $\nu = \sigma_n \ldots \sigma_{n+k}$ such that $K(\nu)$ is relatively small and use these to find sufficiently incompressible short prefix-free descriptions $\tau_i$ for the $\sigma_i$. We then let $B = \tau_1 \tau_2 \ldots$.

We will make several uses of the fact that

$$K(\sigma\tau) = K(\sigma) + K(\sigma \mid \tau) \pm O(\log \lvert \sigma \rvert + \log \lvert \tau \rvert),$$

and more generally

$$K(\rho_0 \ldots \rho_n) = \sum_{j \leqslant n} K(\rho_j \mid \rho_0 \ldots \rho_{j-1}) \pm O\!\left(\sum_{j \leqslant n} \log \lvert \rho_j \rvert\right).$$

Suppose that we have already defined $\eta = \tau_1 \ldots \tau_{n-1}$ for $\rho = \sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_{n-1}$ so that $\lvert \eta \rvert \leqslant s_3 \lvert \rho \rvert$. By the choice of $s_2$, there are infinitely many $k$ such that for $\nu = \sigma_n \ldots \sigma_{n+k}$, we have $K(\nu \mid \rho) \leqslant s_2 \lvert \nu \rvert$. Fix such a $k$.

If $k$ is large enough (so that quantities such as $(s_3 - s_2) \lvert \nu \rvert$ overwhelm the relevant log terms), then, by symmetry of information,

$$\sum_{i \leqslant k} K(\sigma_{n+i} \mid \rho \nu_{i-1}) \leqslant s_3 \lvert \nu \rvert,$$

and for all $j \leqslant k$, by symmetry of information,

$$K(\nu_j \mid \rho) \leqslant K(\rho \nu_j) - K(\rho) + O(\log \lvert \rho \rvert + \log \lvert \nu_j \rvert) \leqslant t(\lvert \rho \rvert + \lvert \nu_j \rvert) - s_1 \lvert \rho \rvert,$$

so that, again by symmetry of information,

$$\sum_{i \leqslant j} K(\sigma_{n+i} \mid \rho \nu_{i-1}) \leqslant (t - s_1) \lvert \rho \rvert + t \lvert \nu_j \rvert.$$

Given the existence of such $k$, we can effectively find $k$ and $\tau_n, \ldots, \tau_{n+k}$ such that, letting $\nu$ and $\nu_j$ be as above, each $\tau_{n+i}$ is a $\mathcal{U}$-description of $\sigma_{n+i}$ given $\rho \nu_{i-1}$, and we have $\sum_{i \leqslant k} \lvert \tau_{n+i} \rvert \leqslant s_3 \lvert \nu \rvert$ and $\sum_{i \leqslant j} \lvert \tau_{n+i} \rvert \leqslant (t - s_1) \lvert \rho \rvert + t \lvert \nu_j \rvert$ for all $j \leqslant k$, so the recursive definition can continue.

Let $B = \tau_1 \tau_2 \ldots$. Clearly $B \leqslant_T A$, so we are left with showing that $\dim(B) \geqslant \frac{\dim(A)}{\text{Dim}(A)} - \varepsilon$.

Fix $m$. Let $n$, $k$, etc. be as above so that $n \leqslant m \leqslant n + k$. Let $j = m - n$. The choice of the $\tau_i$ as appropriate $\mathcal{U}$-descriptions ensures that $K(\sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_m) \leqslant K(\tau_1 \ldots \tau_m) + O(1)$, so $K(\tau_1 \ldots \tau_m) \geqslant s_1 \lvert \sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_m \rvert - O(1)$. Furthermore,

$$\lvert \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m \rvert \leqslant \lvert \eta \rvert + \lvert \tau_n \ldots \tau_{n+j} \rvert \leqslant s_3 \lvert \rho \rvert + (t - s_1) \lvert \rho \rvert + t \lvert \nu_j \rvert \leqslant (t + s_3 - s_1) \lvert \sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_m \rvert.$$

Thus

$$\liminf_m \frac{K(\tau_1 \ldots \tau_m)}{\lvert \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m \rvert} \geqslant \frac{s_1}{t + s_3 - s_1} \geqslant \frac{\dim(A)}{\text{Dim}(A)} - \varepsilon.$$

Given $l$, let $m$ be least such that $B \restriction l \preccurlyeq \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m$. Then $\lvert \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m \rvert - l \leqslant m$, so $K(\tau_1 \ldots \tau_m) \leqslant K(B \restriction l) + O(m)$. Since $m$ is sublinear in $\lvert \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m \rvert$, it follows that

$$\dim(B) = \liminf_l \frac{K(B \restriction l)}{l} \geqslant \liminf_m \frac{K(\tau_1 \ldots \tau_m)}{\lvert \tau_1 \ldots \tau_m \rvert} \geqslant \frac{\dim(A)}{\text{Dim}(A)} - \varepsilon.$$

$\square$

### 13.13 Clumpy Trees and Minimal Degrees

In this section we introduce the useful notion of *clumpy trees*, using them to show that there is a minimal degree of effective packing dimension 1. This result follows from Theorem 13.9.17, of course, but can be proved using an easier method.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.13.1 — Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

There is a set of minimal Turing degree and effective packing dimension 1.

</div>

*Proof.* We give a notion of forcing $\mathbb{P}$ such that a sufficiently generic filter $G \subset \mathbb{P}$ yields a set $X_G \in 2^\omega$ with effective packing dimension 1 and minimal Turing degree. This notion is a modification of the standard notion of forcing with computable perfect trees due to Sacks. We need to restrict the kind of perfect trees we use so that we can always choose strings that are sufficiently complicated (i.e., not easily compressed), to be initial segments of the set we build. The problem, of course, is that we cannot determine effectively which strings are sufficiently incompressible, but our conditions, the trees, have to be computable. The solution to this problem relies on the following lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.13.2)</span></p>

For each $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $\varepsilon \in \mathbb{Q}^{>0}$ we can computably find an $n$ such that $\frac{K(\sigma\tau)}{\lvert \sigma\tau \rvert} \geqslant 1 - \varepsilon$ for some $\tau \in 2^n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $d = \lvert \sigma \rvert + 1$ and let $S = \lbrace \nu : K(\nu) \leqslant \lvert \nu \rvert - d \rbrace$. Since $\mu(S) \leqslant 2^{-d}$, we have $\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \not\subseteq S$. So letting $m > \frac{d}{\varepsilon}$, we see that there is some $\nu \succ \sigma$ of length $m$ such that $K(\nu) \geqslant m - d$. Then $\frac{K(\nu)}{m} \geqslant 1 - \frac{d}{m} > 1 - \varepsilon$, so we can let $n = m - \lvert \sigma \rvert$. $\square$

We denote the $n$ corresponding to $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$ in the above lemma by $n_\varepsilon(\sigma)$.

A *perfect function tree* is a map $T : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ that preserves extension and incompatibility. We write $[T]$ for the class of all $B$ for which there is an $A$ with $T(A \restriction n) \prec B$ for all $n$. Let $T$ be a perfect function tree, $\sigma \in \text{rng}\, T$, and $\varepsilon \in \mathbb{Q}^{>0}$. Let $\rho = T^{-1}(\sigma)$. We say that $T$ contains an *$\varepsilon$-clump above $\sigma$* if $\sigma\tau \preccurlyeq T(\rho\tau)$ for all $\tau \in 2^{<n_\varepsilon(\sigma)}$. (Note that this condition implies that $T(\rho\tau) = \sigma\tau$ for all $\tau \in 2^{<n_\varepsilon(\sigma)}$.) The idea is that, by the above lemma, such a clump allows us to find extensions of $\sigma$ on $T$ that are fairly incompressible.

Given a perfect function tree $T$ and a positive rational $\varepsilon$, we recursively define a labeling of part of the image of $T$ as follows.

1. Label $T(\lambda)$ by $\varepsilon$.
2. If $\sigma$ is labeled by a rational number $\delta$, and $T$ contains a $\delta$-clump above $\sigma$, then for all binary strings $\tau$ of length $n_\delta(\sigma)$, label $T(\rho\tau)$ by $\frac{\delta}{2}$, where $\rho = T^{-1}(\sigma)$.
3. If $T$ does not contain such a clump, stop the labeling process.

The tree $T$ is called *$\varepsilon$-clumpy* if this labeling process never halts; that is, the $\delta$-clumps required at step 2 are always present.

Let $\mathbb{P}$ be the collection of pairs $(T, \varepsilon)$ where $T$ is an $\varepsilon$-clumpy, computable tree. Note that $\mathbb{P} \neq \emptyset$, since the identity function is an $\varepsilon$-clumpy tree for all $\varepsilon$.

A perfect function tree $S$ *extends* a perfect function tree $T$ if there is some perfect function tree $R$ such that $S = T \circ R$ (where $\circ$ denotes composition). If $(T, \varepsilon), (S, \delta) \in \mathbb{P}$ then we say that $(S, \delta)$ *extends* $(T, \varepsilon)$ if $S$ extends $T$ and $\delta$ is the label of $S(\lambda)$ on $(T, \varepsilon)$ (in particular, we require that $S(\lambda)$ be labeled on $(T, \varepsilon)$).

For $G \subset \mathbb{P}$, if $\bigcap_{(T, \varepsilon) \in G} [T]$ is a singleton, then let $X_G$ be its unique element.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.13.3)</span></p>

If $G \subset \mathbb{P}$ is a sufficiently generic filter, then $X_G$ is well-defined and $\text{Dim}(X_G) = 1$. (In particular, $X_G$ is not computable.)

</div>

*Proof.* Since $G$ is a filter, $\bigcap_{(T, \varepsilon) \in G} [T]$ is nonempty. By considering full subtrees, we see that for each $n$, the set $\lbrace (T, \varepsilon) \in G : \lvert T(\lambda) \rvert > n \rbrace$ is dense in $\mathbb{P}$, so $\bigcap_{(T, \varepsilon) \in G} [T]$ is a singleton, and hence $X_G$ is well-defined.

Let $q < 1$ and let $(T, \varepsilon) \in \mathbb{P}$. There is some $\rho$ such that on $(T, \varepsilon)$, the string $\sigma = T(\rho)$ is labeled by a $\delta$ with $1 - \delta > q$. Then $T(\rho\tau)$ is labeled by $\frac{\delta}{2}$ on $(T, \varepsilon)$ and $(\text{Ext}_\sigma(T), \frac{\delta}{2}) \in \mathbb{P}$ extends $(T, \varepsilon)$. If that condition is in $G$ then $\sigma\tau \preccurlyeq T(\rho\tau) \prec X_G$. Thus the set of conditions that force there to be a $\nu \prec X_G$ such that $\frac{K(\nu)}{\lvert \nu \rvert} > q$ is dense in $\mathbb{P}$. $\square$

We finish by showing that if $G$ is sufficiently generic then $X_G$ has minimal Turing degree. As usual in Sacks forcing, let $\Phi$ be a Turing functional, let

$$\text{Div}_\Phi = \lbrace (T, \varepsilon) \in \mathbb{P} : \exists x \, \forall \sigma \in \text{rng}\, T \, (\Phi^\sigma(x) \uparrow) \rbrace$$

and

$$\text{Tot}_\Phi = \lbrace (T, \varepsilon) \in \mathbb{P} : \forall x \, \forall \sigma \in \text{rng}\, T \, \exists \sigma' \in \text{rng}\, T \, (\sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma \wedge \Phi^{\sigma'}(x) \downarrow) \rbrace.$$

Let $\text{Comp}_\Phi$ be the collection of conditions $(T, \varepsilon) \in \text{Tot}_\Phi$ such that for all $\sigma, \sigma' \in \text{rng}\, T$, the strings $\Phi^\sigma$ and $\Phi^{\sigma'}$ are compatible. If $(T, \varepsilon) \in \text{Comp}_\Phi$ and $(T, \varepsilon) \in G$ then $\Phi^{X_G}$ is computable, since to determine $\Phi^{X_G}(x)$ we need only look for $\sigma \in \text{rng}\, T$ such that $\Phi^\sigma(x) \downarrow$, which must exist and have $\Phi^\sigma(x) = \Phi^{X_G}(x)$.

Let $\text{Sp}_\Phi$ be the collection of conditions $(T, \varepsilon) \in \text{Tot}_\Phi$ such that for all incompatible labeled $\sigma, \sigma' \in \text{rng}\, T$, the strings $\Phi^\sigma$ and $\Phi^{\sigma'}$ are incompatible. Let $(T, \varepsilon) \in G \cap \text{Sp}_\Phi$. We claim that $X_G \leqslant_T \Phi^{X_G}$. Suppose that we have determined an initial segment $\sigma$ of $X_G$. It is easy to check that the labeled nodes are dense in $\text{rng}\, T$, so we can find a labeled $\sigma' \succ \sigma$ in $\text{rng}\, T$ such that $\Phi^{\sigma'} \prec \Phi^{X_G}$. Then we must have $\sigma' \prec X_G$.

Thus if we can show that $\text{Tot}_\Phi \cup \text{Div}_\Phi$ is dense in $\mathbb{P}$, it is enough to show that $\text{Sp}_\Phi \cup \text{Comp}_\Phi$ is *dense below* $\text{Tot}_\Phi$ in $\mathbb{P}$. That is, every condition in $\text{Tot}_\Phi$ is extended by one in $\text{Sp}_\Phi \cup \text{Comp}_\Phi$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.13.4)</span></p>

$\text{Sp}_\Phi \cup \text{Comp}_\Phi$ is dense below $\text{Tot}_\Phi$ in $\mathbb{P}$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that $(T, \varepsilon) \in \text{Tot}_\Phi$ has no extension in $\text{Comp}_\Phi$. Then for all $\sigma \in \text{rng}\, T$, there are $\sigma_0, \sigma_1 \in \text{rng}\, T$ extending $\sigma$ such that $\Phi^{\sigma_0}$ and $\Phi^{\sigma_1}$ are incompatible.

By recursion, we define an extension $(S, \varepsilon)$ of $(T, \varepsilon)$ in $\text{Sp}_\Phi$. We start by letting $S(\lambda) = T(\lambda)$ and labeling this string by $\varepsilon$ on $(S, \varepsilon)$. Suppose that we have defined $\sigma = S(\rho') = T(\rho)$ where $\sigma$ is labeled by $\delta$ on $(T, \varepsilon)$ and by $\gamma$ on $(S, \varepsilon)$. Assume that we have carried out the construction so far so that $n_\gamma(\sigma) \leqslant n_\delta(\sigma)$. For every $\tau$ of length strictly shorter than $n_\gamma(\sigma)$, we can let $S(\rho'\tau) = T(\rho\tau) = \sigma\tau$ (and leave it unlabeled on $(S, \varepsilon)$).

Let $\tau_0, \ldots, \tau_{2^n - 1}$ be the strings of length $n = n_\gamma(\sigma)$. Enumerate the pairs of numbers $i < j < 2^n$ as $(i_0, j_0), \ldots, (i_m, j_m)$. For each $i \leqslant 2^n$ and $k \leqslant m$, define strings $\nu_k^i$ such that $\sigma\tau_i \prec \nu_0^i \prec \cdots \prec \nu_m^i$ and such that every $\nu_k^i$ is in the image of $T$. At step $k$, define $\nu_k^{i_k}$ and $\nu_k^{j_k}$ by finding extensions $\nu_k^{i_k} \succ \nu_{k-1}^{i_k}$ and $\nu_k^{j_k} \succ \nu_{k-1}^{j_k}$ such that $\Phi^{\nu_k^{i_k}}$ and $\Phi^{\nu_k^{j_k}}$ are incompatible, which is possible because $(T, \varepsilon)$ has no extension in $\text{Comp}_\Phi$. Define $S(\rho'\tau_i)$ to be some string $\eta_i \in \text{rng}\, T$ extending $\nu_m^i$ that is labeled by some $\delta_i$ such that $n_{\delta_i}(\eta_i) \geqslant n_{\frac{\gamma}{2}}(\eta_i)$. We can then label $\eta_i$ by $\frac{\gamma}{2}$ on $(S, \varepsilon)$. Note that the inductive hypothesis assumed above is preserved, and so the construction can continue.

This lemma completes the proof of Theorem 13.13.1. $\square$

### 13.14 Building Sets of High Packing Dimension

One way in which effective packing dimension differs from effective Hausdorff dimension is in the absence of a correspondence principle analogous to Theorem 13.6.1. Conidis built a countable $\Pi_1^0$ class $P$ of effective packing dimension 1. (In fact, he built $P$ to have a unique element of rank 1, so that all other elements of $P$ are computable. See Section 2.19.1 for the definition of the rank of an element of a $\Pi_1^0$ class.) Since $P$ is countable, its classical packing dimension is 0. Note also that, by Theorem 13.6.1, the effective Hausdorff dimension of $P$ is 0.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.1 — Conidis)</span></p>

There is a countable $\Pi_1^0$ class of effective packing dimension 1.

</div>

*Proof.* By Lemma 13.13.2, there is a computable sequence $0 = n_0 < n_1 < \cdots$ such that for every $\sigma \in 2^{n_k}$, there is a $\tau \in 2^{n_{k+1}}$ with $\sigma \prec \tau$ and $K(\tau) \geqslant (1 - 2^{-k}) n_{k+1}$. We build finite trees $T_s \subseteq 2^{\leqslant n_s}$ such that $T_0 \subset T_1 \subset \cdots$ and let our class be $P = [\bigcup_s T_s]$. We will have strings $\sigma_k$ whose values will change throughout the construction, but will have limit values such that $\sigma_0 \prec \sigma_1 \prec \cdots$ and $K(\sigma_k) \geqslant (1 - 2^{-k}) \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$ for all $k$.

**Construction.**
*Stage 0.* Let $\sigma_0 = \lambda$ and $T_0 = \lbrace \lambda \rbrace$.

*Stage $s + 1$.* If there is a least $k \leqslant s$ such that $K_s(\sigma_k) < (1 - 2^{-k}) \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$ then redefine $\sigma_k$ to be the leftmost extension $\tau \in 2^{\lvert \sigma_k \rvert}$ of $\sigma_{k-1}$ on $T_s$ such that $K_s(\tau) \geqslant (1 - 2^{-k}) \lvert \tau \rvert$ (which we will argue below must exist). In this case, undefine all $\sigma_j$ for $j > k$.

Let $k$ be largest such that $\sigma_k$ is still defined and let $\tau$ be the leftmost leaf of $T_s$ that extends $\sigma_k$ (so that $\lvert \tau \rvert = n_s$). Define $T_{s+1}$ as follows. Begin by adding all extensions of $\tau$ of length $n_{s+1}$. For every leaf $\rho \neq \tau$ of $T_s$, add $\rho 0^{n_{s+1} - n_s}$ to $T_{s+1}$. Finally, close downwards to obtain a subtree of $2^{\leqslant n_{s+1}}$. Let $\sigma_{k+1} = \tau 0^{n_{s+1} - n_s}$.
**End of Construction.**
