Every time $\sigma_k$ is defined at a stage $s$ to extend some $\tau \in 2^{n_s}$, every extension of $\tau$ of the same length as $\sigma_k$ is added to $T_{s+1}$. Thus, by the choice of the $n_s$, if we ever find that $K_s(\sigma_k) < (1 - 2^{-k}) \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$ then we can redefine $\sigma_k$ as in the construction. Thus the construction never gets stuck, and it follows by induction that every $\sigma_k$ has a final value, which we will refer to simply as $\sigma_k$, such that $\sigma_0 \prec \sigma_1 \prec \cdots$ and $K(\sigma_k) \geqslant (1 - 2^{-k}) \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$ for all $k$.

Let $P = [\bigcup_s T_s]$ and $X = \bigcup_k \sigma_k$. Then $X \in P$ and $\text{Dim}(X) = 1$, so $\text{Dim}(P) = 1$. It is easy to see from the construction that if $\rho \neq \sigma_k$ and $\lvert \rho \rvert = \lvert \sigma_k \rvert$, then every extension of $\rho$ on $P$ is eventually 0. Thus every element of $P$ other than $X$ is eventually 0, and hence $P$ is countable. $\square$

Note that, in the above proof, the $\sigma_k$ never move to the left, so the unique noncomputable element $X$ of $P$ is a left-c.e. real.

This result suggests that it is easier (from a computability-theoretic perspective) to build sets of high effective packing dimension than ones of high effective Hausdorff dimension. (Theorem 13.12.1 can also be seen as evidence for this claim.) Downey and Greenberg have extended Theorem 13.14.1 as follows.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.2 — Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

Every set $A$ of array noncomputable degree computes a set $B$ of effective packing dimension 1. If $A$ is c.e. then $B$ can be taken to be a left-c.e. real that is the unique rank 1 element of a $\Pi_1^0$ class.

</div>

Before proving this result, we make a few comments. The following result shows that c.e. traceable sets have packing dimension 0.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.3 — Essentially Kummer, see Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

If $A$ is c.e. traceable, then for every computable order $h$, we have $C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + h(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be c.e. traceable and fix a computable order $h$. (We may assume that $h(0) > 0$.) By Proposition 2.23.12, $A$ is c.e. traceable with bound $h$. Let $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ be a c.e. trace with bound $h$ for the function $n \mapsto A \restriction n$. We can describe $A \restriction n$ by describing $n$ and the position of $A \restriction n$ in the enumeration of $T_n$, so $C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + h(n) + O(1)$. $\square$

Since we can choose $h$ to be slow growing, and $\log n$ is bounded away from $n$, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.4)</span></p>

If $A$ is c.e. traceable then $\text{Dim}(A) = 0$.

</div>

Combining this result with Theorem 13.14.2 and the fact that a c.e. degree is array computable iff it is c.e. traceable (Theorem 2.23.13), we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.5 — Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a c.e. set $A$.

1. $A$ computes a set of positive effective packing dimension.
2. $A$ computes a set of effective packing dimension 1.
3. The degree of $A$ is array noncomputable.

</div>

Corollary 13.14.4, together with the existence of minimal degrees of packing dimension 1 (Theorem 13.9.17), implies Shore's unpublished result that there is a minimal degree that is not c.e. traceable.

Downey and Ng have further sorted out the connections between high effective packing dimension and notions such as array computability.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.14.6 — Downey and Ng)</span></p>

1. There is a degree that is not c.e. traceable but has effective packing dimension 0.
2. There is a degree that is array computable but has effective packing dimension 1.

</div>

Part (ii) follows by taking a hyperimmune-free 1-random degree and applying Proposition 2.23.10. The degrees in the above result can also be $\Delta_2^0$, and hence there seems to be no way to relate the degrees of packing dimension 1 with any known lowness class.

### 13.15 Computable Dimension and Schnorr Dimension

#### 13.15.1 Basics

It is natural to consider notions of dimension obtained by replacing c.e. gales by computable ones. Recall that a martingale $d$ *$s$-succeeds* on $A$ if $\limsup_n \frac{d(A \restriction n)}{2^{(1-s)n}} = \infty$ and *$s$-succeeds strongly* on $A$ if $\lim_n \frac{d(A \restriction n)}{2^{(1-s)n}} = \infty$. As discussed in Section 13.3, the following definitions were in a sense implicit in the work of Schnorr.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.1 — Lutz, Athreya, Hitchcock, Lutz, and Mayordomo)</span></p>

The *computable Hausdorff dimension* of $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\dim_\text{comp}(C) = \inf \lbrace s : \text{there is a computable martingale that } s\text{-succeeds on all } A \in C \rbrace.$$

The *computable packing dimension* of $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\text{Dim}_\text{comp}(C) = \inf \lbrace s : \text{there is a computable martingale that } s\text{-succeeds strongly on all } A \in C \rbrace.$$

</div>

For $A \in 2^\omega$, we write $\dim_\text{comp}(A)$ for $\dim_\text{comp}(\lbrace A \rbrace)$ and refer to it as the computable Hausdorff dimension of $A$, and similarly for packing dimension.

A different approach to passing from $\Sigma_1^0$ objects to computable ones in the definition of algorithmic dimension is to consider test sets that are computable in the sense of Schnorr.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.2)</span></p>

Let $s \geqslant 0$ be rational. A *Schnorr $s$-test* is a uniformly c.e. sequence $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ of sets of strings such that $\sum_{\sigma \in S_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-n}$ for all $n$ and the reals $\sum_{\sigma \in S_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ are uniformly computable.

A class $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is *Schnorr $s$-null* if there exists a Schnorr $s$-test $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ such that $C \in \bigcap_n \llbracket S_n \rrbracket$.

</div>

The Schnorr 1-null sets are just the Schnorr null sets. As with Schnorr tests, in the above definition we could require that $\sum_{\sigma \in S_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} = 2^{-n}$ and have the same notion of Schnorr $s$-null sets. The sets $S_n$ in a Schnorr $s$-test are actually uniformly computable, since to determine whether $\sigma \in S_n$ it suffices to enumerate $S_n$ until the accumulated sum given by $\sum_{\tau \in S_n} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert}$ exceeds $2^{-n} - 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ (assuming the measure of the $n$th level of the test is in fact $2^{-n}$). If $\sigma$ has not been enumerated so far, it cannot be in $S_n$. But of course the converse does not hold: there are computable sets of strings generating open sets with noncomputable measures. Indeed, we have mentioned before that every $\Sigma_1^0$ class can be generated by a computable set of strings.

We can adapt Definition 13.5.6 to get an $s$-analog of the concept of total Solovay test introduced in Definition 7.1.9. A Solovay $s$-test $D$ is *total* if $\sum_{\sigma \in D} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ is computable.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.3 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

For any rational $s \geqslant 0$, a class $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is Schnorr $s$-null if and only if there is a total Solovay $s$-test that covers every element of $C$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\lbrace S_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr $s$-test. Let $S = \bigcup_n S_n$. Clearly, $S$ is a Solovay $s$-test that covers all of $\bigcap_n S_n$, so it is enough to show that $\sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ is computable. It is easy to see that to compute this sum to within $2^{-n}$, it is enough to compute $\sum_{\sigma \in S_i} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ to within $2^{-2n-3}$ for each $i \leqslant n + 1$.

For the converse, let $S$ be a total Solovay $s$-test. Given $n$, compute $c = \sum_{\sigma \in S} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ to within $2^{-n-2}$. Effectively find a finite $F \subset S$ such that

$$c - 2^{-n-1} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in F} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant c - 2^{-n-2}.$$

Let $S_n = S \setminus F$. Then $S_n$ covers every set that $S$ covers, and $\sum_{\sigma \in S_n} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} < 2^{-n}$. Furthermore, this sum is uniformly computable over all $n$. Thus the $S_n$ form a Schnorr $s$-test whose intersection contains all sets covered by $S$. $\square$

We have the following effective version of Theorem 13.1.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.4 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

For any rationals $t \geqslant s \geqslant 0$, if $C$ is Schnorr $s$-null then it is also Schnorr $t$-null.

</div>

Thus the following definition makes sense.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.5 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

The *Schnorr Hausdorff dimension* of $C \subseteq 2^\omega$ is

$$\dim_\text{S}(C) = \inf \lbrace s \geqslant 0 : C \text{ is Schnorr } s\text{-null} \rbrace.$$

</div>

For $A \in 2^\omega$, we write $\dim_\text{S}(A)$ for $\dim_\text{S}(\lbrace A \rbrace)$ and refer to it as the Schnorr Hausdorff dimension of $A$.

Note that the Schnorr Hausdorff dimension of any class is at most 1, since for any $\varepsilon > 0$ we can choose a computable sequence $k_0, k_1, \ldots$ such that the sets $2^{k_n}$ form a Schnorr $1 + \varepsilon$ test covering all of $2^\omega$.

We saw in Section 8.11.2 that the concepts of computable randomness and Schnorr randomness do not coincide. That is, there are Schnorr random sets on which some computable martingale succeeds. However, the differences vanish when it comes to Hausdorff dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.6 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

For any $B \in 2^\omega$, $\dim_\text{S}(B) = \dim_\text{comp}(B)$.

</div>

*Proof.* ($\leqslant$) Suppose that a computable martingale $d$ $s$-succeeds on $B$. By Theorem 7.1.2, we may assume that $d$ is rational-valued. We may also assume that $s < 1$, since the case $s = 1$ is trivial. It suffices to show that for any $t \in (s, 1)$, we can find a Schnorr $t$-test that covers $B$. Fix such a $t$. Let

$$U_k = \lbrace \sigma : 2^{-(1-t) \lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\sigma) \geqslant 2^k \rbrace.$$

It is easy to see that the $\lbrace U_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ are uniformly computable (since $d$ is rational-valued and computable) and cover $B$, so we are left with showing that the reals $\sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ are uniformly computable.

To approximate $\sum_{\sigma \in U_k} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ to within $2^{-r}$, we first effectively find an $n$ such that $2^{(1-t)n} \geqslant 2^r d(\lambda)$. Let $V = U_k \cap 2^{\leqslant n}$. If $\tau \in U_k \setminus V$ then $d(\tau) \geqslant 2^{(1-t)n} 2^k \geqslant 2^{r+k} d(\lambda)$. So by Kolmogorov's Inequality (Theorem 6.3.3), $\mu(U_k) - \mu(V) \leqslant 2^{-(r+k)}$.

($\geqslant$) Suppose $\dim_\text{S}(B) < s < 1$. We show that there is a computable martingale $d$ that $s$-succeeds on $B$. Let $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr $s$-test that covers $B$. Let

$$d_k(\sigma) = \begin{cases} 2^{(1-s) \lvert \tau \rvert} & \text{if } \tau \preccurlyeq \sigma \text{ for some } \tau \in V_k \\ \sum_{\sigma\tau \in V_k} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert + (1-s)(\lvert \sigma \rvert + \lvert \tau \rvert)} & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

We verify that $d_k$ is a martingale. Given $\sigma$, if there is a $\tau \in V_k$ such that $\tau \preccurlyeq \sigma$, then clearly $d_k(\sigma 0) + d_k(\sigma 1) = 2 d_k(\sigma)$. Otherwise,

$$d_k(\sigma 0) + d_k(\sigma 1) = \sum_{\sigma 0 \tau \in V_k} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert + (1-s)(\lvert \sigma \rvert + \lvert \tau \rvert + 1)} + \sum_{\sigma 1 \tau \in V_k} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert + (1-s)(\lvert \sigma \rvert + \lvert \tau \rvert + 1)} = \sum_{\sigma\rho \in V_k} 2^{-\lvert \rho \rvert + 1 + (1-s)(\lvert \sigma \rvert + \lvert \rho \rvert)} = 2 d_k(\sigma).$$

Furthermore, $d_k(\lambda) = \sum_{\tau \in V_k} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert + (1-s) \lvert \tau \rvert} = \sum_{\tau \in V_k} 2^{-s \lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k}$, so $d = \sum_k d_k$ is also a martingale. It is straightforward to use the fact that $\lbrace V_k \rbrace_{k \in \omega}$ is a Schnorr $s$-test to show that the $d_k$ are uniformly computable, and hence $d$ is computable.

Finally, note that, for $\sigma \in V_k$, we have $d(\sigma) \geqslant d_k(\sigma) = 2^{(1-s) \lvert \sigma \rvert}$, so if $B \in \bigcap_k \llbracket V_k \rrbracket$, then $d(B \restriction n) \geqslant 2^{(1-s)n}$ infinitely often, which means that $d$ $s$-succeeds on $B$. $\square$

Thus, in contrast to randomness, the approaches to dimension via Schnorr tests and via computable martingales yield the same concept. Because of the potential confusion between the terms "effective dimension" (which we have used to mean our primary notion of $\Sigma_1^0$ dimension) and "computable dimension", we will use the term "Schnorr Hausdorff dimension" and the notation $\dim_\text{S}$. For uniformity, we will also refer to computable packing dimension as *Schnorr packing dimension* and use the notation $\text{Dim}_\text{S}$ in place of $\text{Dim}_\text{comp}$.

It follows from the definitions that $\dim_\text{S}(A) \leqslant \text{Dim}_\text{S}(A)$ for any $A$. We call sets for which Schnorr Hausdorff and Schnorr packing dimension coincide *Schnorr regular*, following [391] and [15]. It is easy to construct a non-Schnorr regular sequence; in Section 13.15.4, we will see that such sequences occur even among the c.e. sets.

#### 13.15.2 Examples of Schnorr dimension

The easiest way to construct examples of sequences of non-integral Schnorr Hausdorff dimension is by inserting zeroes into a sequence of Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 1. Note that it easily follows from the definitions that every Schnorr random set has Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 1. On the other hand, it is not hard to show that not every set of Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 1 is Schnorr random.

A second class of examples is based on the fact that Schnorr random sets satisfy the law of large numbers, not only with respect to the uniform measure, but also with respect to other computable Bernoulli distributions. For a sequence $\vec{p} = (p_0, p_1, \ldots)$ of elements of $(0, 1)$, the *Bernoulli measure* $\mu_{\vec{p}}$ is defined by $\mu_{\vec{p}}(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \prod_{\sigma(i)=1} p_i \prod_{\sigma(i)=0} (1 - p_i)$. It is straightforward to modify the definition of Schnorr test to obtain Schnorr randomness notions for arbitrary computable measures, as we did for Martin-Löf randomness in Section 6.12.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.7 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

1. Let $S$ be Schnorr random and let $Z$ be a computable, infinite, coinfinite set such that $\delta_Z = \lim_n \frac{\lvert \lbrace 0, \ldots, n-1 \rbrace \cap Z \rvert}{n}$ exists. Let $S_Z = S \oplus_Z \emptyset$, where $\oplus_Z$ is as defined in Section 6.9. Then $\dim_\text{S}(S_Z) = \delta_Z$.

2. Let $\vec{p} = (p_0, p_1, \ldots)$ be a sequence of uniformly computable reals in $(0, 1)$ such that $p = \lim_n p_n$ exists. Then for any Schnorr $\mu_{\vec{p}}$-random set $B$, we have $\dim_\text{S}(B) = -(p \log p + (1 - p) \log(1 - p))$.

</div>

Part 1 of the theorem is straightforward (using for instance the martingale characterization of Schnorr Hausdorff dimension); part 2 is an easy adaption of the corresponding theorem for effective dimension (as for example in Section 13.5). Lutz proved part 2 for Martin-Löf $\mu_{\vec{p}}$-randomness and effective Hausdorff dimension.

It is not hard to see that for the examples given in Theorem 13.15.7, the Schnorr Hausdorff dimension and Schnorr packing dimension coincide, so these examples describe Schnorr regular sets.

#### 13.15.3 A machine characterization of Schnorr dimension

One of the fundamental aspects of the theory of 1-randomness is the characterization of that notion in terms of initial segment Kolmogorov complexity. There is an equally important correspondence between effective Hausdorff and packing dimensions and Kolmogorov complexity, as we saw in Theorem 13.3.4 and Corollary 13.11.12. A comparably elegant initial segment complexity characterization is not possible for Schnorr randomness, because such a characterization should be relativizable, and would therefore imply that lowness for $K$ implies lowness for Schnorr randomness, which we saw was not the case in Section 12.1.

As we saw in Section 7.1.3, however, it is possible to obtain a machine characterization of Schnorr randomness by restricting ourselves to computable measure machines, that is, prefix-free machines whose domains have computable measures. We now see that we can use such machines to characterize Schnorr dimension as well.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.8 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

The Schnorr Hausdorff dimension of $A$ is the infimum over all computable measure machines $M$ of $\liminf_n \frac{K_M(A \restriction n)}{n}$.

</div>

*Proof.* ($\geqslant$) Let $s > \dim_\text{S}(A)$ be rational. We build a computable measure machine $M$ such that $\liminf_n \frac{K_M(A \restriction n)}{n} < s$.

Let $\lbrace U_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ be a Schnorr $s$-test covering $A$. The KC Theorem is applicable to the set of requests $(\lfloor s \lvert \sigma \rvert \rfloor, \sigma)$ for $\sigma \in \bigcup_{i > 1} U_i$, so there is a prefix-free machine $M$ such that $K_M(\sigma) \leqslant \lfloor s \lvert \sigma \rvert \rfloor$ for all such $\sigma$. Furthermore, $M$ is a computable measure machine because $\sum_{i > 1} \sum_{\sigma \in U_i} 2^{-\lfloor s \lvert \sigma \rvert \rfloor}$ is computable.

We know that for each $i$ there is an $n_i$ such that $A \restriction n_i \in U_i$, and clearly these $n_i$ go to infinity. So there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K_M(A \restriction n) \leqslant \lfloor sn \rfloor \leqslant sn$, which implies that $\liminf_n \frac{K_M(A \restriction n)}{n} \leqslant s$.

($\leqslant$) Suppose there is a computable measure machine $M$ such that $\liminf_n \frac{K_M(A \restriction n)}{n} < s$, where $s$ is rational. Let $S_M = \lbrace \sigma : K_M(\sigma) < s \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace$. We claim that $S_M$ is a total Solovay $s$-test covering $A$. There are infinitely many initial segments of $A$ in $S_M$, so it remains to show that $\sum_{\sigma \in S_M} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ is finite and computable. Finiteness follows from the fact that

$$\sum_{\sigma \in S_M} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} < \sum_{\sigma \in S_M} 2^{-K_M(\sigma)} \leqslant 1.$$

To show computability, given $\varepsilon$, compute an $s$ such that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}(M) \setminus \text{dom}(M_s) \rrbracket) < \varepsilon$, and let $S_{M_s} = \lbrace \sigma : K_{M_s}(\sigma) < s \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace$. Then

$$\sum_{\sigma \in S_M} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{\sigma \in S_{M_s}} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} + \varepsilon. \quad \square$$

An analogous argument, using the correspondence between martingales and tests shown in Theorem 13.15.6, allows us to obtain a machine characterization of Schnorr packing dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.9 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

The Schnorr packing dimension of $A$ is the infimum over all computable measure machines $M$ of $\limsup_n \frac{K_M(A \restriction n)}{n}$.

</div>

#### 13.15.4 Schnorr dimension and computable enumerability

For left-c.e. reals, having high effective dimension has similar computability-theoretic consequences to being 1-random. For Schnorr dimension, a straightforward generalization of Corollary 8.11.4 shows that if $A$ is left-c.e. and $\dim_\text{S}(A) > 0$, then $A$ is high.

Computably enumerable sets are usually of marginal interest in the context of algorithmic randomness, one reason being that they cannot be random relative to most notions of randomness. For instance, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.10 — Folklore)</span></p>

No c.e. set is Schnorr random.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be c.e. We may assume that $A$ is infinite, and hence contains an infinite computable subset $\lbrace b_0 < b_1 < \cdots \rbrace$. Let $G_n = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^{b_n} : \forall i < n \, (\sigma(b_i) = 1) \rbrace$ and let $U_n = \llbracket G_n \rrbracket$. Then $\mu(U_n) = 2^{-n}$, so the $U_n$ form a Schnorr test, and $A \in \bigcap_n U_n$. $\square$

This proof does not immediately extend to showing that a c.e. set $A$ must have Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 0. Defining coverings from the enumeration of $A$ directly might not work either, since the dimension factor leads longer strings to be weighted more, so, depending on the enumeration, the $s$-covering might not be a Schnorr $s$-covering. However, we can exploit the somewhat predictable nature of $A$ to define for each $s > 0$ a computable martingale that $s$-succeeds on $A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.11 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

Every c.e. set has Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 0.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $s \in \mathbb{Q}^{>0}$. Let $\gamma > 2^{1-s} - 1$ be rational. Partition $\mathbb{N}$ into consecutive disjoint intervals $I_0, I_1, \ldots$ so that there is an $\varepsilon > 0$ for which, letting $i_n = \lvert I_n \rvert$ and $j_n = i_0 + i_1 + \cdots + i_n$, we have

$$\lim_n \frac{(1 + \gamma)^{i_n} \left(\frac{1 - \gamma}{1 + \gamma}\right)^{\varepsilon i_n}}{2^{(1-s) j_n}} = \infty.$$

To have this hold, it is enough to have $I_n$ much larger than $I_{n-1}$, for example by letting $\lvert I_n \rvert = 2^{\lvert I_0 \rvert + \cdots + \lvert I_{n-1} \rvert}$. Let $i_n = \lvert I_n \rvert$.

Let $\delta = \limsup_n \frac{\lvert A \cap I_n \rvert}{i_n}$. By replacing $A$ with its complement if needed, we may assume that $\delta > 0$. Let $q < r \in \mathbb{Q}$ be such that $\delta \in (q, r)$ and $r - q < \varepsilon$. There is a computable sequence $n_0 < n_1 < \cdots$ such that $q i_{n_k} < \lvert A \cap I_{n_k} \rvert < r i_{n_k}$ for all $k$.

Let $d$ be defined as follows. Let $d(\lambda) = 1$. If $\lvert \sigma \rvert \notin I_{n_k}$ for all $k$, then let $d(\sigma 0) = d(\sigma 1) = d(\sigma)$. If $\lvert \sigma \rvert \in I_k$, then wait for an $s$ such that $\lvert A_s \cap I_{n_k} \rvert > q i_{n_k}$. If $\lvert \sigma \rvert \in A_s$, then let $d(\sigma 0) = 0$ and $d(\sigma 1) = 2 d(\sigma)$. Otherwise, let $d(\sigma 0) = (1 + \gamma) d(\sigma)$ and $d(\sigma 1) = (1 - \gamma) d(\sigma)$.

When betting along $A$, the number of times we are in the last case of the definition of $d$ and yet $\lvert \sigma \rvert \in A$ is less than $(r - q) i_{n_k} < \varepsilon i_{n_k}$, so

$$d(A \restriction j_{n_k}) > d(A \restriction j_{n_{k-1}})(1 + \gamma)^{i_{n_k}} \left(\frac{1 - \gamma}{1 + \gamma}\right)^{\varepsilon i_{n_k}}.$$

By our choice of $\varepsilon$, we see that $d$ $s$-succeeds on $A$. $\square$

We have seen that effective Hausdorff dimension is stable; that is, the effective Hausdorff dimension of a class is the supremum of the effective Hausdorff dimensions of its elements. It is not hard to see that for every Schnorr 1-test there is a c.e. (and even a computable) set that is not covered by it. Thus the class of all c.e. sets has Schnorr Hausdorff dimension 1, and hence Schnorr Hausdorff dimension is not stable, even for countable classes.

Perhaps surprisingly, there do exist c.e. sets with Schnorr packing dimension 1. This result contrasts with the case of effective packing dimension, since as we will see in Theorem 16.1.1, if $A$ is c.e. then $C(A \restriction n) \leqslant 2 \log n + O(1)$. It can be generalized to show that every hyperimmune degree contains a set of Schnorr packing dimension 1. Downey, Merkle, and Reimann remarked that a straightforward forcing construction shows the existence of degrees that do not contain any set of high Schnorr packing dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.15.12 — Downey, Merkle, and Reimann)</span></p>

If $B$ has hyperimmune degree then there is an $A \equiv_T B$ with Schnorr packing dimension 1. If $B$ is c.e. then $A$ can be chosen to be c.e.

</div>

*Proof.* We begin with the non-c.e. case. It is enough to build $C \leqslant_T B$ with Schnorr packing dimension 1, since we can then let $A = B \oplus_X C$ for a sufficiently sparse computable set $X$ (where $\oplus_X$ is as defined in Section 6.9), say $X = \lbrace 2^n : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$.

Let $g \leqslant_T B$ be such that for any computable function $f$, we have $f(n) < g(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. Effectively partition $\mathbb{N}$ into disjoint consecutive intervals $I_0, I_1, \ldots$ such that $\lvert I_{n+1} \rvert$ is much greater than $\lvert I_0 \rvert + \cdots + \lvert I_n \rvert$. For instance, we can choose the $I_n$ so that $\lvert I_{n+1} \rvert = 2^{\lvert I_0 \rvert + \cdots + \lvert I_n \rvert}$. Let $i_n = \lvert I_n \rvert$.

Let $M_0, M_1, \ldots$ be an effective enumeration of all prefix-free machines. If $\sum_{M_e[g(n)](\sigma) \downarrow} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 1 - 2^{-i_{(e,n)}}$ then let $C \cap I_{(e,n)} = \emptyset$. Otherwise, let $\sigma \in 2^{i_{(e,n)}}$ be such that $K_{M_e[g(n)]}(\sigma) \geqslant i_{(e,n)}$. (If such a $\sigma$ does not exist, then the domain of $M_e$ consists exactly of the finitely many strings of length $i_{(e,n)}$, so we do not have to worry about $M_e$; in this case, let $C \cap I_{(e,n)} = \emptyset$.) Note that $K_{M_e}(\sigma) \geqslant i_{(e,n)}$. Let $C \restriction I_{(e,n)} = \sigma$. We have $C \leqslant_T g \leqslant_T B$.

Assume for a contradiction that $\text{Dim}_\text{S}(C) < 1$. Then there exists a computable measure machine $M$ and an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $K_M(C \restriction n) \leqslant (1 - \varepsilon) n$ for almost all $n$. By Proposition 7.1.18, we may assume that $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}(M) \rrbracket) = 1$. We define a machine $\widetilde{M}$ with the same domain as $M$. If $M(x) \downarrow$ then check whether $\lvert M(x) \rvert = i_0 + i_1 + \cdots + i_k$ for some $k$. If so, then let $\widetilde{M}(x)$ be the last $i_k$ many bits of $M(x)$. Otherwise, let $\widetilde{M}(x) = 0$. Let $e$ be such that $M_e = \widetilde{M}$.

Let $f(n)$ be the least stage $s$ such that $\sum_{M_e[s](\sigma) \downarrow} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} > 1 - 2^{-i_{(e,n)}}$. Since $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}(M_e) \rrbracket) = 1$, the function $f$ is total and computable, so there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f(n) < g(n)$. For each such $n$, we have $K_{M_e}(C \restriction I_{(e,n)}) \geqslant i_{(e,n)}$. On the other hand, for almost all $n$,

$$K_{M_e}(C \restriction I_{(e,n)}) \leqslant K_M(C \restriction I_{(e,0)} \cup \ldots \cup I_{(e,n)}) \leqslant (1 - \varepsilon) \left(\sum_{j \leqslant n} i_{(e,j)}\right) \leqslant \left(1 - \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\right) i_{(e,n)},$$

so we have a contradiction. $\square$

As mentioned in Section 13.15.2, this result, combined with Theorem 13.14.5 (or Theorem 16.1.1 below, which implies that all c.e. sets have effective packing dimension 0), shows that, as opposed to the case of Hausdorff dimension, there are nonhigh sets with Schnorr packing dimension 1 but effective packing dimension 0.

### 13.16 Kolmogorov Complexity and the Dimensions of Individual Strings

In this section, we look at work of Lutz on assigning dimensions to individual *strings*, and a new characterization of prefix-free complexity using such dimensions.

We have seen that the effective dimension of a set can be characterized in terms of initial segment complexity and in terms of c.e. (super)gales. To discretize the latter characterization, Lutz replaced supergales by *termgales*, which can deal with the fact that strings terminate; this change is made by first defining $s$-termgales, and then defining termgales, which are uniform families of $s$-termgales. Next, he replaced "tending to infinity" by "exceeding a finite threshold". Finally, he replaced an optimal $s$-supergale by an optimal termgale.

The basic idea is to introduce a new termination symbol $\square$ to mark the end of a string. A *terminated binary string* is $\sigma \square$ with $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$. Let $T$ be the collection of terminated binary strings.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.1 — Lutz)</span></p>

For $s \geqslant 0$, an *$s$-termgale* is a function $d : 2^{<\omega} \cup T \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ such that $d(\lambda) \leqslant 1$ and for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$,

$$d(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{-s}(d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1) + d(\sigma \square)).$$

</div>

For $s = 1$, the $s$-termgale condition is akin to the usual supermartingale condition, but as noted by Lutz, if each of $0, 1, \square$ is equally likely, independently of previous bits, then the conditional expected capital after betting on the bit to follow $\sigma$ is $\frac{d(\sigma 0) + d(\sigma 1) + d(\sigma \square)}{3} = \frac{2}{3} d(\sigma)$. However, the definition of $s$-termgales is based on the assumption that the termination symbol $\square$ should actually be regarded as having vanishingly small probability. In other words, the 1-termgale payoff condition is the same as the corresponding supermartingale condition, except that the 1-termgale must divert some of its capital to the possibility of $\square$ occurring, and this diversion is without compensation, but $\square$ can occur at most once, and we can make the impact of this diversion of capital small.

Lutz provided the following example. Let $d(\lambda) = 1$. Given $d(\sigma)$, let $d(\sigma 0) = \frac{3}{2} d(\sigma)$ and $d(\sigma 1) = d(\sigma \square) = \frac{1}{4} d(\sigma)$. Then $d$ is a 1-termgale and if $\sigma$ is a string with $n_0$ many 0's and $n_1$ many 1's, then

$$d(\sigma \square) = \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{n_0} \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{n_1 + 1} = 2^{n_0(1 + \log 3) - 2(n_1 + 1)}.$$

Thus if $n_0 \gg \frac{2}{1 + \log 3}(n_0 + n_1 + 1)$, then $d(\sigma \square)$ is significantly greater than $d(\lambda)$ even though $d$ loses three quarters of its capital when $\square$ occurs.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.2)</span></p>

Let $d, d' : 2^{<\omega} \cup T \to \mathbb{R}^{\geqslant 0}$ be such that $2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\sigma) = 2^{-s' \lvert \sigma \rvert} d'(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \cup T$. Then $d$ is an $s$-termgale iff $d'$ is an $s'$-termgale.

In particular, if $d$ is a 0-termgale and $d'(\sigma) = 2^{s \lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \cup T$, then $d'$ is an $s$-termgale, and each $s$-termgale can be obtained this way from a 0-termgale.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.3 — Lutz)</span></p>

Let $d$ be an $s$-termgale and $\tau \in 2^{<\omega}$. Then $\sum_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}} 2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} d(\tau\sigma \square) \leqslant 2^s d(\tau)$.

</div>

We are now ready to define (optimal) termgales.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.4 — Lutz)</span></p>

1. A *termgale* is a family $d = \lbrace d^s : s \geqslant 0 \rbrace$ of $s$-termgales such that $2^{-s \lvert \sigma \rvert} d^s(\sigma) = 2^{-s' \lvert \sigma \rvert} d^{s'}(\sigma)$ for all $s$, $s'$, and $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \cup T$.
2. A termgale is $\Sigma_1^0$, or *constructive*, if $d^0$ is a $\Sigma_1^0$ function.
3. A $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale $\bar{d}$ is *optimal* if for each $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale $d$ there is a $c > 0$ such that for all $s$ and $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, we have $\bar{d}^s(\sigma \square) \geqslant c d^s(\sigma \square)$.

</div>

In the same way that we connected continuous semimeasures with martingales, we can define the *termgale induced* by a c.e. discrete semimeasure $m$ via $d[m]^s(\tau) = 2^{s \lvert \sigma \rvert} \sum_{\tau \preccurlyeq \sigma \square} m(\tau)$. (See Section 3.9 for more on discrete semimeasures.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.5 — Lutz)</span></p>

If $m$ is a maximal c.e. discrete semimeasure then $d[m]$ is an optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale. Thus there is an optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale.

</div>

*Proof.* It is easy to check that $d$ is a termgale. Let $d = \lbrace d^s : s \geqslant 0 \rbrace$ be a termgale. Let $\widehat{m}(\sigma) = d^0(\sigma \square)$. By Lemma 13.16.3, $\widehat{m}$ is a c.e. discrete semimeasure. Since $m$ is multiplicatively optimal, for some $c$ we have $m(\sigma) \geqslant c \widehat{m}(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$. Hence

$$d[m]^s(\sigma \square) = 2^{s \lvert \sigma \square \rvert} m(\sigma) \geqslant 2^{s \lvert \sigma \square \rvert} c \widehat{m}(\sigma) = c d^s(\sigma \square).$$

$\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.6 — Lutz)</span></p>

If $d$ is a termgale, $n > 0$, and $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, then the *dimension of $\sigma$ relative to $d$ at significance level $n$* is

$$\dim_d^n(\sigma) = \inf \lbrace s \geqslant 0 : d^s(\sigma \square) > n \rbrace.$$

</div>

We can characterize this dimension in terms of an optimal termgale.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.7 — Lutz)</span></p>

Let $\bar{d}$ be an optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale, and let $d$ be a $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale. Then for each $n > 0$ there is a $c > 0$ such that, for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$,

$$\dim_{\bar{d}}^n(\sigma) \leqslant \dim_d^1(\sigma) + \frac{c}{1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert}.$$

</div>

Hence, if $\bar{d}_1$ and $\bar{d}_2$ are both optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgales and $n_1, n_2 > 0$, then there is a $c > 0$ such that for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$,

$$\dim_{\bar{d}_1}^{n_1}(\sigma) - \dim_{\bar{d}_2}^{n_2}(\sigma) \leqslant \frac{c}{1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert}.$$

Theorem 13.16.7 is the key to Lutz' definition of the dimension of a string, since it says that if we base our definition on a particular optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale $\bar{d}$ and significance level $n$, then this choice will have little effect on the dimension of a given string, particularly for longer strings. Therefore we fix an optimal $\Sigma_1^0$ termgale $d_\square$ and make the following definition.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.8 — Lutz)</span></p>

$\dim(\sigma) = \dim_{d_\square}^1(\sigma)$ for every $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$.

</div>

In this context, we lose some of the finer points of Hausdorff dimension. For example, it is no longer true that the effective dimension is bounded above by 1. But we do have the following analog.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.9 — Lutz)</span></p>

There is a $b > 0$ such that $\dim(\sigma) \leqslant b$ for all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$.

</div>

*Proof.* For each $\sigma$ and $s$, let $d^s(\sigma) = 2^{(s-2) \lvert \sigma \rvert}$ and $d^s(\sigma \square) = 2^{(s-2) \lvert \sigma \rvert + 1}$. Then it is easy to check that $d = \lbrace d^s : s \geqslant 0 \rbrace$ is a termgale, and $d^2(\sigma \square) = 2$ for all $\sigma$. Thus, by Theorem 13.16.7, there is a $c$ such that $\dim(\sigma) \leqslant 2 + \frac{c}{1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2 + c$, so we can take $b = 2 + c$. $\square$

Finally, we have an analog of the Kolmogorov complexity characterization of effective Hausdorff dimension.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.10 — Lutz)</span></p>

$K(\sigma) = \lvert \sigma \rvert \dim(\sigma) \pm O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $m$ be a maximal c.e. semimeasure, as given in Theorem 3.9.2, and let $d[m]$ be the termgale induced by $m$. For all $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$ and $s > 0$, we have

$$d[m]^s(\sigma \square) > 1 \text{ iff } 2^{s \lvert \sigma \square \rvert} m(\sigma) > 1 \text{ iff } s > \frac{-\log m(\sigma)}{1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert},$$

so $(1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert) \dim_{d[m]}^1(\sigma) = -\log m(\sigma)$.

Thus, by Theorem 13.16.7, $(1 + \lvert \sigma \rvert) \dim(\sigma) = -\log m(\sigma) \pm O(1)$, so by Theorem 13.16.9, $\lvert \sigma \rvert \dim(\sigma) = -\log m(\sigma) \pm O(1)$. But by the Coding Theorem 3.9.4, $K(\sigma) = -\log m(\sigma) \pm O(1)$, so $\lvert \sigma \rvert \dim(\sigma) = K(\sigma) \pm O(1)$. $\square$

Theorem 13.16.10 implies that for any $A \in 2^\omega$, we have $\lim_n \lvert \frac{K(A \restriction n)}{n} - \dim(A \restriction n) \rvert = 0$. Thus the Kolmogorov complexity characterizations of effective Hausdorff and packing dimensions imply the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(13.16.11 — Lutz)</span></p>

For $A \in 2^\omega$, we have $\dim(A) = \liminf_n \dim(A \restriction n)$ and $\text{Dim}(A) = \limsup_n \dim(A \restriction n)$.

</div>

In [254], Lutz also proved several other facts about the dimensions of finite strings. Space considerations preclude us from including this material.

## Chapter 14: Strong Jump Traceability

As we have seen, the $K$-trivial sets form a class of "extremely low" sets, properly contained within the class of superlow sets and closed under join. The study of $K$-triviality has led to an increased interest in notions of computability-theoretic weakness. In particular, various forms of traceability have played important roles. In this chapter we study the notion of *strong jump traceability* introduced by Figueira, Nies, and Stephan, and later studied by Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg, among others, and explore its connections with $K$-triviality and other randomness-theoretic notions.

In the computably enumerable case, we will show that the strongly jump traceable c.e. sets form a proper subideal of the $K$-trivial c.e. sets, and can be characterized as those c.e. sets that are computable from every $\omega$-c.e. (or every superlow) 1-random set. We will also discuss the general case in the last section. In particular, we will show that *every* s.j.t. set is $K$-trivial, a result of Downey and Greenberg.

### 14.1 Basics

Recall that we denote $\Phi_h^A(n)$ by $J^A(n)$, and that a set $A$ is $h$-jump traceable for a computable order $h$ if there are uniformly c.e. sets $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ such that $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ and $J^A(n) \downarrow \Rightarrow J^A(n) \in T_n$ for all $n$. (That is, there is a c.e. trace for $J^A$ with bound $h$.) In this chapter we assume that all orders $h$ have $h(0) > 0$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.1 — Strongly Jump Traceable)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **strongly jump traceable** (s.j.t.) if it is $h$-jump traceable for every computable order $h$.

</div>

It follows easily from the fact that there is a computable function $f$ such that $J^A(f(e, n)) = \Phi_e^A(n)$ for all $e$ and $n$ that if $A$ is s.j.t. then for each computable order $h$ and each partial $A$-computable function $\psi$, there is a c.e. trace for $\psi$ with bound $h$. Indeed, we have the following useful fact.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.2 — Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg; Nies)</span></p>

Let $h_0, h_1, \ldots$ be uniformly computable orders such that $h_n(0) \geqslant n$ for all $n$. From indices for the sequence $h_0, h_1, \ldots$ and for a sequence of uniformly computable functionals $\Psi_0, \Psi_1, \ldots$, we can effectively determine a computable order $g$ such that for all $A$, if $J^A$ has a c.e. trace $\lbrace T_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$ with bound $g$, then each $\Psi_n^A$ has a c.e. trace with bound $h_n$, and these can be obtained effectively from an index for $\lbrace T_i \rbrace_{i \in \omega}$.

</div>

It is not obvious that noncomputable s.j.t. sets exist, but the following result shows that they in fact do.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.3 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

There is a promptly simple s.j.t. c.e. set.

</div>

*Proof sketch.* Suppose that we wish to construct a noncomputable c.e. set $A$ that is $h$-jump traceable for a single given computable order $h$. Let $P_e$ be the $e$th noncomputability requirement, $\overline{A} \neq W_e$, and let $N_e$ be the requirement stating that if $J^A(e) \downarrow$ then $J^A(e) \in T_e$, for a c.e. trace $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ with bound $h$ that we build.

We order the requirements in the following way:

$$\underbrace{N_0 \, N_1 \, N_2 \ldots N_e \ldots}_{h(e)=1} \, P_0 \ldots \underbrace{N_e \ldots}_{h(e)=2} \, P_1 \ldots \underbrace{N_e \ldots}_{h(e)=3} \, P_2 \ldots$$

The construction is straightforward: Each $P_e$ is appointed a follower $x$. If $x$ enters $W_e$ at a stage $s$ at which $P_e$ is not yet satisfied, then we put $x$ into $A$, satisfying $P_e$. If a new computation $J^A(e)$ appears at a stage $s$, then $N_e$ puts its value into $T_e$ and initializes all weaker $P$-requirements, which are then appointed new, large followers. The arrangement of strategies ensures that $\lvert T_e \rvert \leqslant h(e)$.

The key to the success of this construction is that each requirement $P_e$ acts at most once, and does not need to act again even if it is later initialized. To make $A$ be $h$-jump traceable for all computable orders $h$, a further dynamic element needs to be introduced to the priority ordering. The property of a partial computable function being an order is $\Pi_2^0$, and we approximate it: say that a stage is $e$-*expansionary* if at this stage we have further evidence that the $e$th partial computable function $\Phi_e$ is an order. If $s$ is $e$-expansionary then the positive requirements are pushed down the ordering so that for every $n$ such that $\Phi_e(n) \vert s \vert \downarrow$, there are at most $\Phi_e(n)$ many positive requirements stronger than $N_{e,n}$, the requirement that traces $J^A(e)$ with at most $\Phi_e(n)$ many values. $\square$

This proof admits the usual sorts of variations. For instance, Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg showed that for every low c.e. set $B$, there is an s.j.t. c.e. set $A \nleqslant_T B$.

Since $A \leqslant_T B$ implies that $J^A$ is uniformly coded into $J^B$, we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.4 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

The class of strongly jump traceable sets is closed downward under Turing reducibility.

</div>

Figueira, Nies, and Stephan also studied the relationship between strong jump traceability and other kinds of approximations to the jump.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.5 — Well-Approximable)</span></p>

A set $D$ is **well-approximable** if for each computable order $h$ there is a computable $g$ such that for all $n$, we have $\lvert \lbrace s : g(n, s+1) \neq g(n, s) \rbrace \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ and $D(n) = \lim_s g(n, s)$.

</div>

Figueira, Nies, and Stephan studied sets $A$ such that $A'$ is well-approximable. As with strong jump traceability, it is easy to see that the class of such *jump well-approximable* well sets is closed downward under Turing reducibility. We can also study the $J^A$ version of this definition: Downey and Greenberg defined $A$ to be **strongly jump well-approximable** if $J^A$ is well-approximable. Downey and Greenberg conjectured that jump well-approximability, strong jump well-approximability, and strong jump traceability are equivalent, but this is not known to be the case in general. In the c.e. case, it is not hard to show that these notions are indeed the same.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.6 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a c.e. set $A$.

1. $A$ is strongly jump traceable.
2. $A$ is jump well-approximable.
3. $A$ is strongly jump well-approximable.

</div>

Relatively weak computability-theoretic lowness properties do not imply randomness-theoretic lowness. For instance, there are 1-random sets that are low, or even superlow. However, the same is not true of very strong computability-theoretic lowness notions. For instance, we have the following plain complexity characterization of strong jump traceability.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.7 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

A set $A$ is s.j.t. iff for every computable order $h$, we have $C(\sigma) \leqslant C^A(\sigma) + h(C^A(\sigma))$ for almost all $\sigma$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be s.j.t. and let $h$ be a computable order. Let $g(n) = \log h(n)$. Since $\mathcal{U}^A$ is a partial $A$-computable function, there is a c.e. trace $\lbrace T_\tau \rbrace_{\tau \in 2^{<\omega}}$ for $\mathcal{U}^A$ with bound $g(\lvert \tau \rvert)$. Let $\tau$ be a minimal-length $\mathcal{U}^A$-description of $\sigma$. Since $\sigma \in T_\tau$, we can describe $\sigma$ by giving $\tau$ and a number less than or equal to $g(\lvert \sigma \rvert)$ denoting $\sigma$'s position in the enumeration of $T_\tau$. Thus

$$C(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert \tau \rvert + g(\lvert \tau \rvert) + O(1) = C^A(\sigma) + g(C^A(\sigma)) + O(1).$$

Conversely, suppose that for every computable order $h$, we have $C(\sigma) \leqslant C^A(\sigma) + h(C^A(\sigma))$ for almost all $\sigma$. If $J^A(e) \downarrow$ and $\log n = e$ then $C^A(n, J^A(e)) \leqslant C^A(n) + O(1) \leqslant e + O(1)$. Let $c$ be this final $O(1)$ constant. Let $h$ be a computable order and let $g$ be a computable order such that $3^{g(e+c)} \leqslant h(e)$ for all $e$. If $J^A(e) \downarrow$ and $\log n = e$ then $C(n, J^A(e)) \leqslant C^A(n, J^A(e)) + g(C^A(n, J^A(e))) \leqslant e + g(e+c) + c$. Let

$$T_e = \lbrace m : \forall n \, (\log n = e \Rightarrow C(n, m) \leqslant e + g(e+c) + c) \rbrace.$$

Then for almost all $e$, if $J^A(e) \downarrow$, then $J^A(e) \in T^{(e)}$, since for any $n$ with $\log n = e$ we have $C(n, J^A(e)) \leqslant e + g(e+c) + c$. So it is enough to show that $\lvert T_e \rvert \leqslant h(e)$ for almost all $e$. Then for all $m \in T_e$, we have $C(n, m) \leqslant e + g(e+c) + c \leqslant C(n) + g(e+c) + c$, so by Theorem 3.4.6, for almost all $e$, we have $\lvert T_e \rvert \leqslant 3^{g(e+c)} \leqslant h(e)$. $\square$

One consequence of the above result is that jump well-approximability implies strong jump traceability in general.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.1.8 — Figueira, Nies, and Stephan)</span></p>

If $A'$ is well-approximable, then for every computable order $h$, we have $C(\sigma) \leqslant C^A(\sigma) + h(C^A(\sigma))$ for almost all $\sigma$. Thus every jump well-approximable set is s.j.t.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $h$ be a computable order. Let $n_\sigma = C(\sigma \mid \sigma_A^*)$. (Recall that $\sigma_A^*$ is a minimal length $\mathcal{U}^A$-description of $\sigma$.) We have $C(\sigma) \leqslant C^A(\sigma) + 2n_\sigma$ for almost all $\sigma$, so it is enough to show that $n_\sigma \leqslant \frac{h(C^A(\sigma))}{2}$ for almost all $\sigma$. In fact, we will show that $n_\sigma \leqslant 2 \log h(C^A(\sigma)) + O(1)$.

Define a partial $A$-computable function $\Psi(m, n, \rho)$ as follows. Wait until $\sigma = \mathcal{U}^A(\rho)$, if ever. Then search for a $\tau \in 2^n$ such that $\mathcal{U}^\rho(\tau) = \sigma$. If such a $\tau$ is found, $m < n$, and the $m$th bit of $\tau$ is 1, then let $\Psi(m, n, \rho) \downarrow$. The important thing about this definition is that there is some $\tau_\sigma \in 2^{n_\sigma}$ such that $\mathcal{U}^{\sigma_A^*}(\tau_\sigma) = \sigma$ and for $m < n_\sigma$, we have $\Psi(m, n_\sigma, \sigma_A^*) \downarrow$ iff $\tau_\sigma(m) = 1$.

Let $f$ be a computable function such that $A'(f(m, n, \rho)) = 1$ iff $\Psi(m, n, \rho) \downarrow$. Let $b$ be a computable order such that $b(f(m, n, \rho)) \leqslant mnh(\lvert \rho \rvert)$ for all $m$, $n$, and $\rho$. By definition, there is a way to approximate $A'$ so that $A'(k)$ is approximated with at most $b(k)$ many mind changes. Thus, since $\tau_\sigma(m) = 1$ iff $A'(f(m, n_\sigma, \sigma_A^*)) = 1$, we can describe $\tau_\sigma$ from $\sigma_A^*$ by giving $n_\sigma$ and the exact number of times our approximation to $A'(f(n_\sigma - 1, n_\sigma, \sigma_A^*)), \ldots, A'(f(0, n_\sigma, \sigma_A^*))$ changes, which is bounded by $n_\sigma^2 h(\lvert \sigma_A^* \rvert) = n_\sigma^2 h(C^A(\sigma))$. But we can also compute $\sigma$ from $\tau_\sigma$ and $\sigma_A^*$, so

$$n_\sigma = C(\sigma \mid \sigma_A^*) \leqslant C(\tau_\sigma \mid \sigma_A^*) + O(1)$$

$$\leqslant 2 \log n_\sigma + \log(n_\sigma^2 h(C^A(\sigma))) + O(1)$$

$$\leqslant 4 \log n_\sigma + \log h(C^A(\sigma)) + O(1) \leqslant \frac{n_\sigma}{2} + \log h(C^A(\sigma)) + O(1),$$

so $n_\sigma \leqslant 2 \log h(C^A(\sigma)) + O(1)$. $\square$

Ng has shown that the index set of c.e. s.j.t. sets is $\Pi_1^0$-complete. His proof builds on the method of separating $K$-triviality and strong jump traceability from Theorem 14.3.7 below.

Ng has also studied relativized versions of strong jump traceability and related notions. For example, a set $A$ is *ultra jump traceable* if $A$ is strongly jump traceable relative to all c.e. sets $X$. That is, for every c.e. set $X$ and every $X$-computable order $h$, there is an $X$-c.e. trace for $J^A$ with bound $h$. Note that this definition is a "partial" relativization, in that we trace only $J^A$, not $J^{X \oplus A}$. Ng has shown that noncomputable ultra jump traceable sets exist, but no noncomputable set is jump traceable relative to all $\Delta_2^0$ sets in this partial sense. He has also shown that the ultra jump traceable sets form a proper subclass of the s.j.t. sets, and that they cannot be promptly simple. They thus form the first known class defined by a cost function construction that has noncomputable elements but no promptly simple ones. This class is not yet well understood.

### 14.2 The ideal of strongly jump traceable c.e. sets

By Theorem 14.1.4, the class of strongly jump traceable sets is closed downward under Turing reducibility. In this section we show that the join of two s.j.t. c.e. sets is also s.j.t., and so the c.e. s.j.t. sets form an ideal in the c.e. degrees. In fact, we show that for every computable order $h$ there is another computable order $g$ such that if $A_0$ and $A_1$ are c.e. and $g$-jump traceable, then $A \oplus B$ is $h$-jump traceable. This result should be contrasted with Theorem 11.6.4 that there exist superlow c.e. sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A \oplus B \equiv_T \emptyset'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.2.1 — Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

Let $g$ be a computable order. There is a computable order $h$ such that if $A$ and $B$ are $h$-jump traceable c.e. sets, then $A \oplus B$ is $g$-jump traceable. In particular, if $A$ and $B$ are s.j.t. c.e. sets then so is $A \oplus B$.

</div>

*Proof.* This proof is a relatively simple example of the **box promotion** (or **box amplification**) method, so we wish to describe the motivation behind it.

Suppose that we wanted to prove the theorem wrong, that is, to construct s.j.t. c.e. sets $A_0$ and $A_1$ such that $A_0 \oplus A_1$ is not s.j.t. We could attempt to combine the construction of a promptly simple s.j.t. c.e. set in the proof of Theorem 14.1.3 to build $A_0$ and $A_1$ with a strategy to diagonalize against possible traces for $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}$ by changing values of this computation sufficiently often.

The key idea is the following. We are given strongly jump traceable c.e. sets $A_0$ and $A_1$, and a computable order $g$, and wish to trace $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}$ with bound $g$. We define functionals $\Psi_0$ and $\Psi_1$, and want to receive some confirmation that a value of $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}(e)[s]$ is genuine. Say that the computation has use $\sigma_0 \oplus \sigma_1$, where $\sigma_i \prec A_i[s]$. We define $\Psi_i^{\sigma_i}(x) = \sigma_i$ for some $x$. If indeed $\sigma_i \prec A_i$ then $\sigma_i$ must appear in a trace $T_x^i$ for $\Psi_i^{A_i}$. Thus we can wait until both strings $\sigma_i$ appear in the relevant "boxes" $T_x^i$, and only then believe the computation $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}(e)[s]$.

If the bound on the size of $T_x^i$ (which we prescribe in advance, but has to eventually increase with $x$) is $k$, then we originally think of $T_x^i$ as a "$k$-box", a box that may contain up to $k$ many values. After $\sigma_i$ appears in $T_x^i$ and is shown to be wrong, we can think of the promoted box as a $(k-1)$-box. Eventually, if $T_x^i$ is promoted $k - 1$ many times, then it becomes a 1-box. If a string $\sigma_i$ appears in a 1-box then we know it must be a true initial segment of $A_i$. In this way we can limit the number of false $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}(e)$ computations that we trace. Since all requirements act independently, this strategy allows us to trace $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}$ to any computable degree of precision we wish.

The above is the main idea of all "box promotion" constructions. Each such construction has particular combinatorial aspects designed to counter difficulties that arise during the construction. The main idea (which again appears in all box promotion constructions) is to use clusters of boxes (or "meta-boxes") rather than individual boxes. Instead of testing $\sigma_i$ on a single $T_x^i$, we bunch together a finite collection $M_i$ of inputs $x$, and define $\Psi_i^{\sigma_i}(x) = \sigma_i$ for all $x \in M_i$. We believe the computation $J^{A_0 \oplus A_1}(e)[s]$ only if $\sigma_i$ has appeared in $T_x^i$ for all $x \in M_i$. If this computation is believed and later discovered to be false, then all the boxes corresponding to elements of $M_i$ are promoted. We can then break $M_i$ up into smaller meta-boxes and use each separately. Thus we magnify the promotion, to compensate for losses that may occur.

The technical lemmas in the proof (Lemmas 14.2.2–14.2.5) establish properties of the box sizes, priorities, and counting that ensure the construction never gets stuck and the number of values traced is appropriately bounded. $\square$

It is not known at the time of writing whether the join of two (not necessarily c.e.) s.j.t. sets must be s.j.t. (and hence whether the s.j.t. sets form an ideal), though this would follow from Conjecture 14.5.1 below.

### 14.3 Strong jump traceability and $K$-triviality: the c.e. case

In this section, we show that the s.j.t. c.e. sets form a proper subideal of the $K$-trivial c.e. sets. We begin with the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.1 — Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

There is a computable order $h$ such that if $A$ is c.e. and $h$-jump traceable then $A$ is low for $K$, and hence $K$-trivial.

</div>

In Theorem 14.5.2, we will see that in fact *every* s.j.t. set is $K$-trivial. (We will also obtain Theorem 14.3.1 as a corollary to Theorem 14.4.2.) However, we include the following proof of Theorem 14.3.1 here, as it is of technical interest (and proves that every s.j.t. c.e. set is low for $K$ directly, without appealing to the equivalence between lowness for $K$ and $K$-triviality).

*Proof of Theorem 14.3.1.* To show that $A$ is low for $K$ we use the KC Theorem. That is, we build a KC set $L$ such that if $\mathcal{U}^A(\sigma) = \tau$ then $(\lvert \sigma \rvert, \tau) \in L$, so that $K(\tau) \leqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert + O(1)$. We enumerate $A$ and thus approximate $\mathcal{U}^A$. When a string $\sigma$ enters the domain of $\mathcal{U}^A$ at a stage $s$, we need to decide whether to believe the $A_s$-computation that put $\sigma$ in $\text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^{A_s}$. We do so by testing the use $\rho \prec A_s$ of this computation. A first approximation to our strategy for doing so is to build a functional $\Psi$, and look at a trace $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ for $\Psi^A$. Given $\rho$, we choose an $n$ and define $\Psi^\rho(n) = \rho$. We then wait to see whether $\rho$ ever enters $T_n$. If so, then we believe that $\rho \prec A$, and hence that $\sigma \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^A$, so we enumerate $(\lvert \sigma \rvert, \mathcal{U}^A(\sigma))$ into $L$.

The problem, of course, is ensuring that the weight of $L$ is bounded. If we believed only correct computations (that is, if we had $\lvert T_n \rvert = 1$ for all $n$), then the weight of $L$ would be $\mu(\llbracket \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^A \rrbracket) < 1$. But, of course, the $T_n$ grow with $n$, so we will believe some incorrect uses $\rho$. We need to make sure that the $T_n$ grow slowly enough that these mistakes do not prevent $L$ from being a KC set.

To help us do so, rather than treat each string $\sigma$ individually, we deal with strings in batches. When we have a set of strings $C \subseteq \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^{A_s}$ of weight $2^{-k}$ (i.e., such that $\sum_{\sigma \in C} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} = 2^{-k}$), we will verify $A$ up to a use that puts them all in $\text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^{A_s}$. The greater $2^{-k}$ is, the more stringent the test will be (ideally, in the sense that the size of the corresponding $T_n$ is smaller). We will put a bound $m_k$ on the number of times that a set of strings of weight $2^{-k}$ can be believed and yet be incorrect. The argument will succeed as long as $\sum_k m_k 2^{-k}$ is finite.

The combinatorial strategy to keep the $m_k$ small has two ingredients. First, note that two sets of strings of weight $2^{-k}$ can be combined into a single set of weight $2^{-(k-1)}$. So if we are testing one such set, and another one with compatible use appears, then we can let the testing machinery for $2^{-(k-1)}$ take over. The solution is to make every mistake count in our favor in all future tests: a mistake on *some* set means one less possible mistake on *every* other set.

We now proceed with the formal details of the construction. For $c > 1$, we partition $\mathbb{N}$ into consecutive intervals $M_0^c, M_1^c, \ldots$ such that $\lvert M_k^c \rvert = 2^{k(k+c)}$. For $n \in M_k^c$, let $h_c(n) = k + c - 1$. By Lemma 14.1.2, we can determine a computable order $\bar{h}$ such that, from a trace for $J^A$ with bound $\bar{h}$, we can compute, uniformly in $c$, a trace for $\Phi_c^A$ with bound $h_c$.

We will define a functional $\Psi$. By the recursion theorem, we know some $c$ such that $\Psi^X = \Phi_c^X$ for all $X \in 2^\omega$. Let $M_k[0] = M_k^c$ and let $\lbrace T_n \rbrace_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a trace for $\Psi^A$ with $h = h_c$. The definitions of $\Psi$ that we make will all be of the form $\Psi^\rho(n) = \rho$. We then wait to see whether $\rho$ ever enters $T_n$. If so, then we believe that $\rho \prec A$, and hence that $\sigma \in \text{dom}\,\mathcal{U}^A$.

We can think of $M_k[0]$ as a $(k+c)$-dimensional cube with sides of length $2^k$. At each stage $s$, for each $k$ we will have a number $d_k[s] < k + c$ and a function $g_k[s] : d_k[s] \to R_k^+$, which will determine the current value of $M_k$ via the definition $M_k[s] = \lbrace x_f \in M_k[0] : g_k[s] \subset f \rbrace$. We can think of $M_k[s]$ as a $(k + c - d_k)$-dimensional cube.

For $q \in R_k^+$, let $N_k(q)[s] = \lbrace x_f \in M_k[s] : f(d_k[s]) = q \rbrace$, the $q 2^k$th hyperplane of $M_k[s]$.

Let $\rho \preccurlyeq A[s] \restriction s$. We say that $\rho$ is *semiconfirmed* at some point during stage $s$ if for all $x$ such that $\Psi^\rho(x) \downarrow = \rho$ at stage $s$, we have $\rho \in T_x$ at that given point. We say that $\rho$ is *confirmed* if every $\rho' \preccurlyeq \rho$ is semiconfirmed. Note that $\lambda$ is confirmed at every stage, because $\Omega^\lambda = 0$, so we never have $\varrho^{A[s] \vert s}(q) = \lambda$ for any $s$ and $q > 0$, and hence never define $\Psi^\lambda(x)$ for any $x$.

**Construction.** At stage $s$, speed up the enumeration of $A$ and of the $T_n$ (to get $A[s+1]$ and $T_n[s+1]$) so that for all $\rho \preccurlyeq A[s] \restriction s$, either $\rho$ is confirmed or $\rho \nprec A[s+1]$. For each $k \leqslant s$, look for some $q \in R_k^+$ such that $q \leqslant \Omega^{A[s] \vert s}$ and $\varrho^{A[s] \vert s}(q)$ was confirmed at the beginning of the stage but $\varrho^{A[s] \vert s}(q) \nprec A[s+1]$. If there is such a $q$ then extend $g_k$ by letting $g_k(d_k) = q$, so that $d_k[s+1] = d_k[s] + 1$ and $M_k[s+1] = N_k(q)[s]$. **End of Construction.**

**Justification.** Two issues arise: why we can always increase $d_k$ when asked to (since $d_k$ must remain below $k+c$), and why we can always return to the standard configuration.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.2)</span></p>

For each $s$ and $x \in M_k[s+1]$, there are at least $d_k[s]$ many strings in $T_x$, so $d_k[s] \leqslant \lvert T_x \rvert \leqslant h(x) = k + c - 1$.

</div>

*Proof.* Suppose that at stage $s$, we increase $d_k$ by one. This action is witnessed by some $q \in R_k^+$ such that $\rho = \varrho^{A[s] \vert s}(q)$ was confirmed at the beginning of the stage, and we let $M_k[s+1] = N_k(q)[s]$. The confirmation of $\rho$ implies that $\rho \in T_x$ for all $x \in N_k(q)[s]$. But we also have that $\rho \prec A[s]$ and $\rho \nprec A[s+1]$. As $A$ is c.e., $A[s+1]$ is to the right of $\rho$. If we increase $d_k$ at stages $s_0 < s_1$ (witnessed by strings $\rho_0$ and $\rho_1$) then $\rho_0$ is to the left of $A[s_1 + 1]$, while $\rho_1 \prec A[s_1]$ (which is not to the left of $A[s_1 + 1]$). Thus $\rho_0$ is to the left of $\rho_1$, and, in particular, they are distinct. $\square$

The key fact is the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.3)</span></p>

Let $1 \leqslant k \leqslant s$. Then $\Omega^{\nu_k[s]} - \Omega^{\nu_{k-1}[s]} \leqslant 2^{-k+1}$.

</div>

We now show that we can define sets $L_{k,t}$ of relatively small weight such that if $(\sigma, \tau) \in L \setminus \mathcal{U}^A$, then we can "charge" the mistake of adding $(\sigma, \tau)$ to $L$ against some $L_{k,t}$. Formally, we define sets $L_{k,t}$ with the following properties.

1. For each $k$ and $t$, $\sum_{(\sigma,\tau) \in L_{k,t}} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant 2^{-k+1}$.
2. $L \setminus \mathcal{U}^A \subseteq \bigcup_{k,t} L_{k,t}$.
3. For each $k$, there are at most $k+c$ many $t$ such that $L_{k,t}$ is nonempty.

Given these properties, we have

$$\sum_{(\sigma,\tau) \in L \setminus \mathcal{U}^A} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_{k,t} \sum_{(\sigma,\tau) \in L_{k,t}} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} \leqslant \sum_k (k+c) 2^{-k+1} < \infty,$$

as required.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.4)</span></p>

$L \setminus \mathcal{U}^A \subseteq \bigcup_{k,t} L_{k,t}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.5)</span></p>

If $L_{k,t} \neq \emptyset$ then $M_k[t+1] \neq M_k[t+2]$.

</div>

Thus properties 1–3 above hold, and $L$ is a KC set witnessing the fact that $A$ is low for $K$. $\square$

Using more intricate combinatorics it is also possible to establish the following result, where Martin-Löf cuppability is as defined in Definition 11.8.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.6 — Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

There is a computable order $h$ such that if $A$ is c.e. and $h$-jump traceable then $A$ is not Martin-Löf cuppable.

</div>

The relationship between strong jump traceability and $K$-triviality in the computably enumerable case was clarified by Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg, who showed that the s.j.t. c.e. sets form a **proper** subclass of the $K$-trivial c.e. sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.3.7 — Cholak, Downey, and Greenberg)</span></p>

There are a $K$-trivial c.e. set $A$ and a computable order $h$ such that $A$ is not $h$-jump traceable.

</div>

*Proof.* The construction of a $K$-trivial set that is not strongly jump traceable came out of a construction of a $K$-trivial set that is not $n$-c.e. for any $n$. The existence of such sets can be shown using the fact that the class of $K$-trivial sets is closed downwards under Turing reducibility, but let us consider a direct cost function construction of such a set.

The basic construction of a $K$-trivial promptly simple c.e. set can be described as follows. The $e$th requirement wishes to show that the set $A$ we construct is not equal to $\overline{W_e}$. The requirement is given a capital of $2^{-e}$. It appoints a follower $x_0$, and waits for its realization, that is, for $x_0$ to enter $W_e$. If, upon realization, the cost of changing $A(x_0)$ is greater than $2^{-e}$, the follower is abandoned, a new one $x_1$ is picked, and the process repeats itself.

To make $A$ not strongly jump traceable, rather than not $n$-c.e., we need to change $J^A(x)$ on some $x$ more than $h(x)$ many times, where $h$ is some computable order we specify in advance (and $x$ is a number for which we can control $J^A(x)$). To change $J^A(x)$ we change $A$ below the use of the computation that gives the current value of $J^A(x)$. Each time we do so, we can use a different element of $A$, so $A$ can be made c.e. However, the main feature of the argument outlined above, that the same $x$ receives attention several times, remains, so a similar strategy to the one we discussed will work here.

We will define a computable order $h$ shortly. We may assume that $\lvert W_e^{[n]} \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ for all $n$ (by stopping elements from entering $W_e$ if their enumeration would cause this condition to be violated). We enumerate a set $A$ and define a partial function $p = \Phi^A$. The requirement $R_e$ is that $\lbrace W_e^{[n]} \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is not a trace for $p$. The requirements act independently of each other. Let us discuss the action of a single requirement $R_e$.

Let $T_e$ be the set of all sequences $(k_0, k_1, \ldots, k_i)$ with $i < e + 2$ and $k_j < 2^{(e+2)2^j}$ for each $j \leqslant i$. We think of $T_e$ as a tree. An element $\sigma \in T_e$ is a *leaf* of $T_e$ if it has length $e + 2$. Let $\varepsilon_\sigma = 2^{-(e+2)2^{\lvert \sigma \rvert}}$ for each $\sigma \in T_e$. Each leaf $\sigma \in T_e$ will correspond to an attempt at meeting requirement $R_e$. If $i < e + 2$ then $\varepsilon_{\sigma \vert i}$ is the amount that we are willing to spend on the $(e + 2 - i)$th attack with the follower corresponding to $\sigma$, in the sense of the standard ($K$-triviality) cost function.

The tree $T_e$ and the rationals $\varepsilon_\sigma$ were chosen so that $\varepsilon_\lambda = 2^{-(e+2)}$; if $\sigma \in T_e$ is not a leaf, then it has exactly $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_\sigma}$ many immediate successors on $T_e$; and if $\sigma \in T_e$ is not a leaf, then $\varepsilon_\sigma$ is the sum of $\varepsilon_\tau$ over the immediate successors $\tau$ of $\sigma$ on $T_e$.

Using these facts, we can show by reverse induction on $\lvert \sigma \rvert$ that for each $\sigma \in T_e$ that is not a leaf, the sum of $\varepsilon_\tau$ as $\tau$ ranges over all extensions of $\sigma$ on $T_e$ that are not leaves is $(e + 2 - \lvert \sigma \rvert) \varepsilon_\sigma$. Thus the sum of $\varepsilon_\tau$ as $\tau$ ranges over all elements of $T_e$ that are not leaves is $(e+2)2^{-(e+2)}$. This quantity is the total amount we will allow $R_e$ to spend, so the construction will obey the standard cost function, as $\sum_e (e+2) 2^{-(e+2)}$ is finite.

We can now define $h$. Partition $\mathbb{N}$ into intervals $I_e$ so that $\max I_e + 1 = \min I_{e+1}$, letting the size of $I_e$ be the number of leaves of $T_e$. Index the elements of $I_e$ as $x_\sigma$ for leaves $\sigma$ of $T_e$. Let $h(n) = e + 1$ for all $n \in I_e$.

If not yet satisfied at a stage $s$, the requirement $R_e$ will have a pointer $\sigma$ pointing at some leaf of $T_e$, and will be conducting an attack via $x_{\sigma_e}$. This attack will have a level $i < e + 2$, which decreases with time, until the attack either is abandoned or fully succeeds when we get to the root. $\square$

These results suggest a question: Can $K$-triviality be characterized via $h$-jump traceability for some class of orders $h$? In investigating this question, it is most natural to take the alternate definition that a set $A$ is $h$-jump traceable if every partial $A$-computable function has a c.e. trace with bound $h$. One suggestion was to consider the class of computable orders $h$ such that $\sum_n \frac{1}{h(n)} < \infty$. Using the golden run method of Nies discussed in Chapter 11, it is not difficult to show that if a c.e. set $A$ is $K$-trivial then $A$ is $h$-jump traceable for all such $h$. However, work of Ng and Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg has shown that there are c.e. sets that are $h$-jump traceable for all such $h$ but are not $K$-trivial. It remains open whether some other reasonably natural class of orders may capture $K$-triviality exactly. One possibility would be the class of all computable orders $h$ such that $\sum 2^{-h(n)} < \infty$.

Hölzl, Kräling, and Merkle have shown that $A$ is $K$-trivial iff $A$ is $O(g(n) - K(n))$-jump traceable for all Solovay functions $g$, and that, moreover, there is a single Solovay function $f$ such that $A$ is $K$-trivial iff $A$ is $O(f(n) - K(n))$-jump traceable. However, the presence of $K$ in this result might strike some as unsatisfying.

### 14.4 Strong jump traceability and diamond classes

Recall that for a class $S \subseteq 2^\omega$, we define $S^\diamond$ to be the collection of c.e. sets computable from every 1-random element of $S$. In this section, we prove theorems of Greenberg and Nies and Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies that show that the class of strongly jump traceable c.e. sets coincides with both $(\omega\text{-c.e.})^\diamond$ and $\text{superlow}^\diamond$. This result lends evidence to the claim that strong jump traceability is a natural notion to study in the context of algorithmic randomness, and further demonstrates the usefulness of the notion of diamond classes. As we will see, it also provides a useful tool for the study of superlowness.

It is worth noting that, by Theorem 2.19.10 applied to a $\Pi_1^0$ class of 1-random sets, for every c.e. set $A$, there is a low 1-random set that does not compute $A$, so $\text{low}^\diamond$ (and hence $(\Delta_2^0)^\diamond$) contains only the computable sets.

We will use a characterization of strong jump traceability in terms of certain special cost functions. Recall the general notion of a cost function from Definition 11.5.1. Theorems 11.5.3 and 11.10.2 say that $K$-trivial sets have special approximations that change only a few times, and hence have low cost. Greenberg and Nies began with these results as a starting point and studied the cost functions associated with s.j.t. sets. The following definition isolates an important property of the standard cost function $c(n, s) = \sum_{n < i \leqslant s} 2^{-K_s(i)}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.1 — Benign Cost Function)</span></p>

A monotone cost function $c$ is **benign** if there is a computable function $g : \mathbb{Q}^{>0} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for each positive rational $q$, if $I$ is a set of pairwise disjoint intervals of natural numbers such that $c(n, s) \geqslant q$ for all $[n, s) \in I$, then $\lvert I \rvert \leqslant g(q)$.

</div>

Note that benign cost functions always satisfy the limit condition. For example, the standard cost function $c$ is benign: If $I$ is as in the above definition, then $\lvert I \rvert \leqslant \frac{1}{q}$, since

$$q \lvert I \rvert \leqslant \sum_{[n,s) \in I} c(n, s) \leqslant \sum_{[n,s) \in I} \sum_{n < i \leqslant s} 2^{-K(i)} \leqslant \sum_i 2^{-K(i)} \leqslant 1.$$

Thus the following result implies Theorem 14.3.1 that every s.j.t. c.e. set is $K$-trivial.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.2 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

A c.e. set is strongly jump traceable iff it obeys all benign cost functions.

</div>

One direction of this theorem follows from the following lemma, since if a c.e. set obeys all benign cost functions then it is $K$-trivial, and hence jump traceable, by Theorem 11.4.8.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.3 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

Let $A$ be a jump traceable c.e. set and let $h$ be a computable order with $h(0) > 0$. There is a benign cost function $c$ such that if $A$ obeys $c$, then $J^A$ has a c.e. trace bounded by $h$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\widehat{h}(n) = h(n) - 1$ if $h(n) > 1$, and let $\widehat{h}(n) = 1$ otherwise.

Let $\psi(n)$ be the least stage at which $J^A(n)$ converges with an $A$-correct computation, if there is such a stage. Since $A$ is c.e., tracing $J^A(n)$ is equivalent to tracing the partial function $\psi$. Since $A$ is jump traceable, there is a c.e. trace $S_0, S_1, \ldots$ for $\psi$ bounded by some computable order $g$.

If $J^A(n)[s] \downarrow$ with use $k$, then we say that this computation is *certified* if there is some $t < s$ such that $A_s \restriction k = A_t \restriction k$ and $t \in S_n[s]$. We want to ensure that the cost of all $x < k$ at stage $s$ is at least $\frac{1}{\widehat{h}(n)}$, so let $c(x, s)$ be the maximum of all $\frac{1}{\widehat{h}(n)}$ such that there is some $u \leqslant s$ at which a computation $J^A(n)[u]$ with use greater than $x$ is certified (where this maximum is $0$ if there is no such $n$). Note that $c$ is indeed a monotone cost function.

We argue that $c$ is benign. Let $q > 0$ be a rational and let $I$ be a set of pairwise disjoint intervals of natural numbers such that $c(x, s) \geqslant q$ for all $[x, s) \in I$. Let $n^*$ be such that $\widehat{h}(n^*) > \frac{1}{q}$. Let $[x, s) \in I$ with $n > k$, there is a $t \in (x, s]$ such that $J^A(n)[u]$ with use $k > x$ is certified. Let $t$ witness this certification.
