Then $t \in (x, s)$, since $x < k < t < u \leqslant s$, and so, since the intervals in $I$ are pairwise disjoint and $t \in S_n$, we have $\lvert I \rvert \leqslant \sum_{n < n^*} \lvert S_n \rvert \leqslant \sum_{n < n^*} g(n)$. Since $n^*$ is obtained effectively from $q$, and $g$ is computable, this bound on $\lvert I \rvert$ is computable.

Now suppose $A$ obeys $c$. It is easy to see that there is a computable enumeration $\widehat{A}_0, \widehat{A}_1, \ldots$ of $A$ such that, letting $n_s$ be the least number in $\widehat{A}_{s+1} \setminus \widehat{A}_s$, we have $\sum_s c(n_s, s) < 1$. Enumerate a trace $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ for $J^A$ as follows. Enumerate $J^{\widehat{A}_s}(n)$ into $T_n$ if there is some $u < s$ such that $\widehat{A}_u \restriction k = \widehat{A}_s \restriction k$, where $k$ is the use of the computation $J^{\widehat{A}_s}(n)$, and this computation is certified at stage $u$.

Fix $n$ and let $s_0 < s_1 < \cdots < s_{\lvert T_n \rvert - 1}$ be the stages at which we enumerate numbers into $T_n$. Say that the computation $J^{\widehat{A}_{s_i}}(n)$ gets certified at stage $u_i < s_i$. Let $k_i$ be the use of that computation. For each $i < \lvert T_n \rvert - 1$, there is some stage $v_i \in [u_i, u_{i+1})$ such that $\widehat{A}_{v_i} \restriction k_i \neq \widehat{A}_{v_i + 1} \restriction k_i$, since by stage $s_{i+1}$, the computation $J^{\widehat{A}_{s_i}}(n)$ is injured by some number below $k_i$ entering $A$. Since $c(k_i - 1, v_i) \geqslant c(k_i - 1, s_i) \geqslant \frac{1}{\widehat{h}(n)}$, we have $\lvert T_n \rvert - 1 \leqslant \widehat{h}(n)$. Thus, by altering finitely many $T_n$ if necessary, we have $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant h(n)$ for all $n$. $\square$

The more difficult direction of Theorem 14.4.2 is the following lemma, whose proof we only sketch, since the methodology is quite similar to that of the proof of Theorem 14.3.1 that every s.j.t. c.e. set is $K$-trivial.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.4 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

For each benign cost function $c$, there is a computable order $h$ with $h(0) > 0$ such that for any c.e. set $A$, if $J^A$ has a c.e. trace bounded by $h$ then $A$ obeys $c$.

</div>

*Proof sketch.* We define an $A$-partial computable function $\Psi^A$. Using the recursion theorem (or universal traces, as in the original paper), we may assume we have a trace $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ with a bound $h$ that is sufficiently slow growing to make the following box promotion construction work, such that $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ traces $\Psi^A$ for all but finitely many values.

As we have seen, the idea of a box promotion construction is to attempt to certify certain approximations to initial segments of $A$. Here we use certification to obtain an enumeration of $A$ that obeys $c$. The part of the construction dealing with $x$'s for which $c(x, s) \geqslant 2^{-k}$, call it requirement $R_k$, may ignore those $x$'s for which $c(x, s) \geqslant 2^{-(k-1)}$, as these need to be certified by even stronger "boxes" $T_n$. All of these certification processes must work in concert. The fact that $c$ is benign, witnessed by a computable bound function $g$, allows us to set in advance the size of the necessary meta-boxes, thus making $h$ computable. A meta-box for $R_k$ can be broken up at most $k$ many times, so the necessary size for an original $R_k$ meta-box is $g(2^{-k})^{k+1}$.

The details of the construction are quite similar to those in the proof of Theorem 14.3.1, and may be found in the original paper. $\square$

Establishing a relationship between diamond classes and s.j.t. c.e. sets begins with the following basic result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.5 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

Let $Y$ be a $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random set. Then there is a monotone cost function $c$ satisfying the limit condition such that every c.e. set that obeys $c$ is computable from $Y$. If $Y$ is $\omega$-c.e., then $c$ can be chosen to be benign.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $c(n, 0) = 2^{-n}$ for all $n$. For $s > 0$, let $k_s$ be the least $k$ such that $Y_s \restriction k \neq Y_{s-1} \restriction k$ (which we may assume always exists). Let $c(n, s) = \max(c(n, s-1), 2^{-k_s})$ for all $n < s$ and let $c(n, s) = c(n, s-1)$ for all $n \geqslant s$. It is easy to check that $c$ is a monotone cost function satisfying the limit condition. If there is a computable function $g$ such that $\lvert \lbrace s : Y_s(m) \neq Y_{s-1}(m) \rbrace \rvert < g(m)$ for all $k$, and $I$ is a set of pairwise disjoint intervals of natural numbers such that $c(n, s) \geqslant 2^{-k}$ for all $[n, s) \in I$, then for each $[n, s) \in I$ with $n > k$, there is a $t \in (n, s]$ such that $Y_t \restriction k \neq Y_{t-1} \restriction k$, and hence $\lvert I \rvert \leqslant (k+1) + \sum_{m < k} g(m)$. Thus, if our approximation to $Y$ is $\omega$-c.e. then $c$ is benign.

Suppose that the c.e. set $A$ obeys $c$, as witnessed by the approximation $A_0, A_1, \ldots$. For each $n$ and $t \geqslant n$, let $s_n(t)$ be the least $s \leqslant t$ such that $Y_r \restriction n = Y_t \restriction n$ for all $r \in [s, t]$. Without loss of generality, we may assume that $s_u(t) \geqslant n$ for all $t \geqslant n$. At each stage $t$, if $n$ is least such that $A_t \restriction s_n(t) \neq A_{t-1} \restriction s_n(t)$, then enumerate $\llbracket Y_t \restriction n - 1 \rrbracket$ into a Solovay test $G$. It is easy to check that the fact that $A_0, A_1, \ldots$ obeys $c$ implies that $\sum_{\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \in G} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} < \infty$.

Since $Y$ is 1-random, only finitely many initial segments of $Y$ are enumerated into $G$. We claim that if $t > v, n$ and $Y \restriction n - 1 = Y_t \restriction n - 1$, then $A \restriction n = A_t \restriction n$, which implies that $A \leqslant_T Y$. Assume otherwise, and let $u > t$ be such that $A_u \restriction n \neq A_{u-1} \restriction n$. Then $A_u \restriction s_m(u) \neq A_{u-1} \restriction s_m(u)$. Let $m$ be the least such that $A_u \restriction s_m(u) \neq A_{u-1} \restriction s_m(u)$. Then $m \leqslant n$, and we enumerate $\llbracket Y_u \restriction m - 1 \rrbracket$ into $G$. Since $u > v$, $Y_t \restriction m - 1 = Y \restriction m - 1 \neq Y_u \restriction m - 1$. But then $s_u(m-1) > t > n$, so $A_u \restriction s_{m-1}(u) \neq A_{u-1} \restriction s_{m-1}(u)$, contradicting the minimality of $m$. $\square$

Combining this result with Theorem 14.4.2, we have the following result. (Note that $(\omega\text{-c.e.})^\diamond \subseteq \text{superlow}^\diamond$, since every superlow set is $\omega$-c.e.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.6 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

Every strongly jump traceable c.e. set is in $(\omega\text{-c.e.})^\diamond$, and hence in $\text{superlow}^\diamond$.

</div>

This result has some powerful computability-theoretic consequences. It can be used to prove results of Ng and Diamondstone whose direct proofs are rather intricate. (These results answer natural questions about superlowness stemming from well-known results on lowness.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.7 — Greenberg and Nies)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class. For each $B$ there is a $Y \in P$ such that $(Y \oplus B)' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} B'$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.8 — Ng)</span></p>

There is a c.e. set $A$ such that for every superlow c.e. set $B$, the join $A \oplus B$ is also superlow.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be an s.j.t. c.e. set. If $B$ is superlow, then applying Lemma 14.4.7 to a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class of 1-random sets, we see that there is a 1-random set $Y$ such that $Y' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} B' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} \emptyset'$. Then $Y$ is superlow, so by Corollary 14.4.6, $A \leqslant_T Y$, and hence $(A \oplus B)' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} (Y \oplus B)' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} \emptyset'$. $\square$

Note that the above proof in fact proves the stronger result that if $A$ is any s.j.t. c.e. set and $B$ is any (not necessarily c.e.) superlow set, then $A \oplus B$ is also superlow. Taking $A$ to be a promptly simple s.j.t. c.e. set (as given by Theorem 14.1.3), we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.9 — Diamondstone)</span></p>

There is a promptly simple c.e. set $A$ such that for every superlow c.e. set $B$, we have $A \oplus B <_T \emptyset'$.

</div>

As it turns out, Corollary 14.4.6 has a strong converse, which does not require the hypothesis of computable enumerability. (It remains open whether Corollary 14.4.6 holds beyond the c.e. sets.)

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.10 — Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

If a set is computable in every superlow 1-random set, then it is strongly jump traceable.

</div>

Note that it is not difficult to prove that if a set is computable in every superlow 1-random set then it is $K$-trivial: Let $X$ be a superlow 1-random set. By the superlow basis theorem relative to $X$, there is a superlow set $Y$ that is 1-random relative to $X$. By van Lambalgen's Theorem, $X$ and $Y$ are relatively 1-random, so by Corollary 11.7.3, $A$ is $K$-trivial.

We will prove Theorem 14.4.10 below. Since $(\omega\text{-c.e.})^\diamond \subseteq \text{superlow}^\diamond$, combining Corollary 14.4.6 and Theorem 14.4.10, we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.11)</span></p>

The following are equivalent for a c.e. set $A$.

1. $A$ is strongly jump traceable.
2. $A \in (\omega\text{-c.e.})^\diamond$.
3. $A \in \text{superlow}^\diamond$.

</div>

This result implies Theorem 14.2.1, that the s.j.t. c.e. sets are closed under joins, because every diamond class is obviously closed under joins.

Recall that a set $X$ is superhigh if $\emptyset'' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} X'$. Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies showed that strong jump traceability of c.e. sets is also equivalent to being in $\text{superhigh}^\diamond$.

The proof of Theorem 14.4.10 does not make special use of 1-randomness, and in fact establishes the following stronger result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.12 — Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class, and let $A$ be a jump traceable set computable from every superlow member of $P$. Then $A$ is strongly jump traceable.

</div>

By Corollary 2.19.9, every nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class has a superlow member, so if $A$ satisfies the hypothesis of Theorem 14.4.12 then it is superlow. Thus, by Theorem 11.4.7, the extra hypothesis that $A$ be jump traceable is not needed if $A$ is c.e.

Theorem 14.4.10 follows from Theorem 14.4.12 as follows. Let $A \in \text{superlow}^\diamond$. Let $X \oplus Y$ be superlow and 1-random. Then both $X$ and $Y$ are superlow and 1-random, so $A \leqslant_T X, Y$. But $Y$ is 1-random relative to $X$, and hence relative to $A$, so $A$ is a base for 1-randomness, and hence $K$-trivial. By Theorem 11.4.8, $A$ is jump traceable, so applying Theorem 14.4.12 to a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class $P$ all of whose elements are 1-random shows that $A$ is s.j.t.

Another corollary to Theorem 14.4.12 comes from the Scott Basis Theorem 2.21.2, which implies that every completion of PA computes a 1-random set. The reduction in the proof of that theorem is clearly a wtt-reduction, so it is also the case that every $\omega$-c.e. completion of PA computes an $\omega$-c.e. 1-random set. Thus we have the following result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.13 — Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

A c.e. set $A$ is strongly jump traceable iff it is computable from every superlow (or equivalently, every $\omega$-c.e.) completion of PA.

</div>

To prove Theorem 14.4.12, we will not use the jump traceability of $A$ directly, but instead will use the existence of certain nice approximations to $A$-partial computable functions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.14 — Restrained Approximation)</span></p>

Let $(A_s)_{s \in \omega}$ be a computable approximation to a $\Delta_2^0$ set $A$, and let $(\Psi_s)_{s \in \omega}$ be an enumeration of a functional $\Psi$. We say that the pair $((A_s)_{s \in \omega}, (\Psi_s)_{s \in \omega})$ is a **restrained $A$-approximation** of $\Psi^A$ if there is a computable function $g$ such that for each $n$, the number of stages $s$ such that $\Psi_s^{A_s}(n) \downarrow$ and $A_{s+1} \restriction \psi_s^{A_s}(n) \neq A_s \restriction \psi_s^{A_s}(n)$ is bounded by $g(n)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.15 — Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is superlow and jump traceable.
2. Each $A$-partial computable function has a restrained $A$-approximation.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.17)</span></p>

If $B$ is a jump traceable c.e. set, then every $B$-partial computable function has a restrained $B$-approximation.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $\theta = \Phi^B$ be a $B$-partial computable function. Let $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ be a c.e. trace for $n \mapsto B \restriction \varphi^B(n)$ with a computable bound $h$. Define an enumeration of a functional $\Psi$ by letting $\Psi_s^\tau(n) = \Phi_s^\tau(n)$ if $\tau \prec B_s$ and $\tau \in T_n[s]$. It is easy to verify that $\Psi^B = \theta$, and there are at most $h(n)$ many stages $s$ such that $\Psi_s^{B_s}(y) \downarrow$ and $B_{s+1} \restriction \psi_s^{B_s}(y) \neq B_s \restriction \psi_s^{B_s}(y)$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.18)</span></p>

If $A$ is computable from every superlow 1-random set, then every $A$-partial computable function has a restrained $A$-approximation.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A$ be computable from every superlow 1-random set. As noted following Theorem 14.4.10, $A$ is $K$-trivial. By Theorem 11.5.4, $A$ is computable from some c.e. $K$-trivial set $B$. By Theorem 11.4.8, $B$ is jump traceable, so by Lemma 14.4.17, every $B$-partial computable function has a restrained $B$-approximation, from which it follows easily that every $A$-partial computable function has a restrained $A$-approximation. $\square$

Thus Theorem 14.4.10 follows from Theorem 14.4.16, which we now prove.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.16 — Greenberg, Hirschfeldt, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $P$ be a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class, and let $A$ be a set such that every $A$-partial computable function has a restrained $A$-approximation. If $A$ is computable from every superlow element of $P$ then $A$ is strongly jump traceable.

</div>

*Proof of Theorem 14.4.16.* Let $P$ be a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class and let $A$ be computable from every superlow element of $P$ and such that every $A$-partial computable function has a restrained $A$-approximation. Fix a computable order $h$ and an $A$-partial computable function $\theta$. We show that $\theta$ has a c.e. trace bounded by $n \mapsto 2^{h(n)}$, which is enough to establish strong jump traceability, since $h$ is arbitrary. (Of course, it is enough to do this for the single case $\theta = J^A$.) Fix a restrained $A$-approximation $((A_s)_{s \in \omega}, (\Psi_s)_{s \in \omega})$ to $\theta$.

The key concept in this proof is that of a *golden pair*. Recall that in the proof of the (super)low basis theorem, we build a sequence of $\Pi_1^0$ classes $Q_0 \supseteq Q_1 \supseteq \cdots$ by letting $Q_{n+1} = Q_n$ if $n \in X'$ for all $X \in Q_n$ and otherwise letting $Q_{n+1} = \lbrace X \in Q_n : n \notin X' \rbrace$. Then $\bigcap_n Q_n$ contains a single element $Z$, which is superlow, because to compute $Z'(n)$, we need only computably approximate which case of the definition of $Q_{n+1}$ obtains, which we can do recursively, going through at most $2^k$ many versions of each $Q_k$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.19 — Golden Pair)</span></p>

A pair $(Q, \Phi)$, consisting of a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class $Q$ and a functional $\Phi$, is a **golden pair** (for $\Psi$ and $h$) if for almost all $n$ such that $\Psi^A(n) \downarrow$ and all $X \in Q_{h(n)}$, we have $\Phi^X \succcurlyeq A \restriction \psi^A(n)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.4.20)</span></p>

If there is a golden pair then $\theta$ has a c.e. trace bounded by $n \mapsto 2^{h(n)}$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $Q, \Phi$ be a golden pair. We enumerate $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ as follows. If at stage $s$ there is a $\sigma$ such that $\Psi_s^\sigma(n) \downarrow = k$ and $\sigma \preccurlyeq \Phi^X$ for every $X \in Q_{h(n),s}[s]$, then put $k$ into $T_n$. The $T_n$ are uniformly c.e. For each different guess $Q_{h(n),s}$, at most one number $k$ gets enumerated into $T_n$, so $\lvert T_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{h(n)}$. By the definition of golden pair, for almost all $n \in \text{dom}\,\theta$, we have $A \restriction \psi^A(n) \preccurlyeq \Phi^X$ for all $X \in Q_{h(n)}$, which implies that if $s$ is sufficiently large, then $\theta = \Psi^{A \restriction \psi^A(n)}(n) \in T_n$. Thus we can make finitely many changes to the $T_n$ to obtain a c.e. trace for $\theta$ with bound $n \mapsto 2^{h(n)}$. $\square$

So we are left with showing that a golden pair exists. We can think of a golden pair as arising from a failed attempt at building a superlow set $Z \in P$ that does not compute $A$. Such an attempt can be made, starting with any $Q \subseteq P$, by interspersing the superlowness classes $Q_n$ with ones attempting to diagonalize against computations yielding $A$, that is, classes of the form $\lbrace X \in Q_m : \Phi_e^X \not\succcurlyeq \tau \rbrace$ for some $\tau \prec A$. For each $e$ and $n$ we have a strategy $S_n^e$ that monitors whether this class is empty for $\tau = A \restriction \psi^A(n)$ and $m = h(n)$. As long as the class looks nonempty, so that it appears that $S_n^e$ has succeeded in ensuring that $\Phi_e^Z \neq A$, we start a new superlow basis construction with this class, but at level $e + 1$ now.

The construction is a nonuniform argument in the spirit of the golden run method, but with a procedure calling structure of unbounded depth, as in box promotion arguments. The key lemmas (14.4.21–14.4.24) establish that a golden run of some $R^e$ must exist, which yields the golden pair. $\square$

### 14.5 Strong jump traceability and $K$-triviality: the general case

In general, s.j.t. sets are not as well understood as the special case of c.e. s.j.t. sets. It would be quite helpful if the following analog to Corollary 11.5.4 were to be established.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--remark" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Conjecture</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.1 — Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

Every s.j.t. set is computable from some c.e. s.j.t. set.

</div>

Downey and Greenberg showed that all s.j.t. sets are $\Delta_2^0$, and have recently improved this result considerably by showing that all s.j.t. sets are $K$-trivial. This result extends Theorem 14.3.1, but its proof is perhaps simpler.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.2 — Downey and Greenberg)</span></p>

There is a computable order $h$ such that every $h$-jump traceable set is $K$-trivial.

</div>

Before proving this result, we note that not all orders $h$ suffice.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.3 — Nies)</span></p>

There are continuum many $2^{2^{n+1}}$-jump traceable sets.

</div>

*Proof.* We build a trace $T_0, T_1, \ldots$ and a sequence of uniformly computable functions $f_s : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ such that $f = \lim_s f_s$ exists. Let $f_0(\sigma) = \sigma$ for all $\sigma$. At stage $s + 1$, look for the length-lexicographically least $\nu$ such that $J^{f_s(\tau)}(\lvert \nu \rvert) \downarrow \notin T_{e,s}$ for some $\tau \succcurlyeq \nu$ with $\tau \in 2^{\leqslant s+1}$. If no such $\nu$ exists then let $f_{s+1}(\sigma) = f_s(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$ and proceed to the next stage. Otherwise, for every $\rho$, let $f_{s+1}(\nu \rho) = \tau \rho$. For every $\sigma \not\succcurlyeq \nu$, let $f_{s+1}(\sigma) = f_s(\sigma)$. Enumerate $J^{f_s(\tau)}(\lvert \nu \rvert)$ into $T_e$.

It is easy to see that for every $\sigma \in 2^e$, the value of $f_s(\sigma)$ changes at most $2^{e+1} - 1$ many times, and causes at most that many elements to be put into $T_e$, whence $\lvert T_e \rvert < 2^{2^{e+1}}$. Moreover, the image of $2^\omega$ under $f$ has size continuum, and for every member $A$ of this image, we have $J^A(e) \downarrow \Rightarrow J^A(e) \in T_e$ for all $e$. $\square$

It is not hard to check that, as shown by Nies, the above proof can be used to define a perfect $\Pi_1^0$ class of $2^{2^{n+1}}$-jump traceable sets. It would be interesting to improve the bounds on the levels of jump traceability that hold of continuum many sets, given by this result on the one hand, and the proof of Theorem 14.5.2 below on the other. This question is of course connected with the one mentioned at the end of Section 14.3, as well as with the question of what level of jump traceability is needed to ensure $\Delta_2^0$-ness.

*Proof of Theorem 14.5.2.* We build a computable trace $\bar{h}$ such that if $J^A$ is $\bar{h}$-traceable then $A$ is $K$-trivial. By Theorem 11.1.8, there is a computable function $g$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-g(n)} < 1$ and if $K(X \restriction n) \leqslant g(n) + O(1)$, then $X$ is $K$-trivial. We wish to build a KC set to ensure that $K(A \restriction n) \leqslant g(n) + O(1)$. We have no direct access to $A$, so for each $n$, we will need to include $(g(n), \sigma)$ in our KC set for several $\sigma \in 2^n$. To keep the weight of our set finite, we will need to limit the number of such $\sigma$. As in previous constructions in this chapter, we use a functional $\Psi$ as a tester. By not believing that a given $\sigma$ is a possible initial segment of $A$ until it has been sufficiently tested, we will be able to limit the number of $\sigma$'s we consider.

Partition $\mathbb{N}$ into consecutive intervals $I_1, I_2, \ldots$ such that $\lvert I_k \rvert = 2^{2^{2k}}$. For $e > 1$ and $n \in I_k$, let $h_e(n) = k + e$. By Lemma 14.1.2, we can determine a computable order $\bar{h}$ such that, for any $A$, from a trace for $J^A$ with bound $\bar{h}$, we can compute, uniformly in $e$, a trace for $\Phi_e^A$ with bound $h_e$. Let $J^A$ be $\bar{h}$-traceable. We show that $A$ is $K$-trivial.

We will define a functional $\Psi$. By the recursion theorem, we know some $e$ such that $\Psi^X = \Phi_e^X$ for all $X$. Let $\lbrace T_n \rbrace_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a trace for $\Psi^A$ with bound $h = h_e$, computed from $e$ as described above.

Let $b$ be such that $\lvert I_k \rvert \geqslant 2^{(k+e)2^k} + 2^k$ for all $k \geqslant b$. Our testing procedures will happen at levels $k \geqslant b$. For each $k \geqslant b$, let $t_0^k, \ldots, t_{2^k - 1}^k$ be the first $2^k$ many numbers in $I_k$, and let $M^k$ be the next $2^{(k+e)2^k}$ many numbers in $I_k$. The set $M^k$ is split into $2^{k+e}$ many subsets, which are further split into $2^{k+e}$ many subsets, and so on for $2^k$ many levels. We index these subsets as follows. Let $M^k(\lambda) = M^k$. If $\nu$ is a sequence of subsets of $[0, k+e)$ of length less than or equal to $2^k$ and $M^k(\nu)$ is defined, then split $M^k(\nu)$ evenly into $2^{k+e}$ many subsets $M^k(\nu B)$ for $B \subseteq [0, k+e)$. We will use both the $t_m^k$ and the elements of $M^k$ as test inputs.

The construction uses a recursive procedure on $k \geqslant b$, and $m < 2^k$ within each level $k$, to determine which strings of length $n_m^k$ are $(k, m)$-*approved*, and how our testing at level $m$ of $M^k$ is conducted. A string $\sigma \in 2^{n_m^k}$ is $(k, m)$-preapproved if for every $l \in [b, k]$ and $j < 2^l$ such that either $j 2^{-l} < m 2^{-k}$ or both $l < k$ and $j 2^{-l} = m 2^{-k}$, the string $\sigma \restriction 2^{n_j^l}$ is $(l, j)$-approved.

We say that $\sigma_i(k, m)$ is $(k, m)$-approved if for every $n$ for which we define $\Psi^{\sigma_i(k,m)}(n) = \sigma_i(k, m)$, we have $\sigma_i(k, m) \in T_n$.

We now define our KC set $G$. For $k \geqslant b$, let $S_k$ be the set of all $(k, m)$-approved strings, over all $m < 2^k$. For $k > b$ and $\sigma \in S_k$, let $\sigma_k^-$ be the longest initial segment of $\sigma$ of length $n_l^{k-1}$ for some $l$ such that $n_l^{k-1}$ is defined. For $\sigma \in S_b$, let $\sigma_b^- = \lambda$. For $k \geqslant b$ and $\sigma \in S_k$, let

$$G_k(\sigma) = \lbrace (g(\lvert \tau \rvert), \tau) : \sigma_k^- \prec \tau \preccurlyeq \sigma \rbrace.$$

Let $G_k = \bigcup_{\sigma \in S_k} G_k(\sigma)$ and $G = \bigcup_{k \geqslant b} G_k$. Then $G$ is c.e. We show that the weight of $G$ is bounded.

To do so, we redefine $G$ in terms of certain subsets of $G_k$. For $k \geqslant b$, let $L_k$ be the set of maximal elements of $S_k$ (that is, elements of $S_k$ with no proper extensions in $S_k$). Let $A_b = S_b$. For $k > b$, let $A_k = L_k \setminus S_{k-1}$. The following lemmas show that the $A_k$ are small, and hence can be used in bounding the weight of $G$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.4)</span></p>

$\lvert A_k \rvert \leqslant k + e$ for all $k \geqslant b$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.5)</span></p>

Let $k > b$ and let $\sigma \in S_k \setminus L_k$. Then there are a $j \in [b, k)$ and a $\rho \in S_j$ such that $G_k(\sigma) \subseteq G_j(\rho)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.6)</span></p>

Let $k \geqslant b$ and $\sigma \in S_k$. Then there are a $j \in [b, k]$ and a $\rho \in A_j$ such that $G_k(\sigma) \subseteq G_j(\rho)$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.7)</span></p>

If $\sigma \in A_k$ then $2^{-g(\lvert \sigma \rvert)} < 2^{-(k-1)}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(14.5.8)</span></p>

Let $k > b$ and $\sigma \in A_k$. Then the weight of $G_k(\sigma)$ is bounded by $2^{-(k-2)}$.

</div>

Thus weight of $G = H$ is bounded by the weight of $G_b$ plus

$$\sum_{k > b} \lvert A_k \rvert 2^{-(k-2)} \leqslant \sum_{k > b} (k+e) 2^{-(k-2)} < \infty.$$

So $G$ is a KC set, and hence, if $\sigma_k^- \prec \tau \preccurlyeq \sigma$ for $\sigma$ in some $S_k$, then $K(\tau) \leqslant g(\lvert \tau \rvert) + O(1)$.

Finally, for $k \geqslant b$, let $m_k$ be the greatest $m < 2^k$ such that $m 2^{-k} < \sum_l 2^{-g(l)}$. Let $\sigma(b-1) = 0$. For $k \geqslant b$, let $\sigma(k) = A \restriction n_{m_k}^k$. It is easy to see that for each $k \geqslant b$, we have $\sigma(k) \in S_k$ and $\sigma(k)_k^- = \sigma(k-1)$. Thus $K(A \restriction n) \leqslant g(n) + O(1)$, and hence $A$ is $K$-trivial. $\square$

## Chapter 15: $\Omega$ as an Operator

### 15.1 Introduction

We have already seen that Chaitin's $\Omega$ is a natural example of a 1-random real. We have also seen that, in algorithmic randomness, prefix-free machines are the analogs of partial computable functions, and the measures of the domains of prefix-free machines, that is, left-computably enumerable reals, take the role of the computably enumerable sets. In more detail, we have the following:

1. The domains of partial computable functions are exactly the c.e. sets, while the measures of the domains of prefix-free machines are exactly the left-c.e. reals.
2. The canonical example of a noncomputable set is the halting problem $\emptyset'$, i.e., the domain of a universal partial computable function. The canonical example of a 1-random real is $\Omega$, the halting probability of a universal prefix-free machine.
3. $\emptyset'$ is well-defined up to computable permutation, while $\Omega$ is well-defined up to Solovay equivalence.

So far in this book we have dodged the "machine-dependence bullet" by choosing a fixed standard universal machine when looking at relativized halting probabilities and studying properties that do not depend on that choice. In this chapter we will look at results of Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies that grapple with versions of $\Omega$ as operators. Most of the results and proofs in this chapter are taken from that paper.

Relativizing the definition of $\emptyset'$ gives the jump operator. If $A \in 2^\omega$, then $A'$ is the domain of a universal machine relative to $A$. Myhill's Theorem 2.4.12 relativizes, so $A'$ is well-defined up to computable permutation. Furthermore, if $A \equiv_T B$, then $A'$ and $B'$ differ by a computable permutation. In particular, the jump is well-defined on the Turing degrees.

What happens when the definition of $\Omega$ is relativized, by defining it relative to a particular universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$? We will see that the Kučera-Slaman Theorem 9.2.3 and Theorem 9.2.2 of Calude, Hertling, Khoussainov, and Wang both relativize (with some care, as we see below). However, if we wish to look at $\Omega$ as an analog of the jump, then we might hope that $\Omega_U^A$ is well-defined, not just up to Solovay equivalence relative to $A$, but even up to Turing degree. Similarly, we might hope for $\Omega_U$ to be a degree invariant operator; that is, if $A \equiv_T B$ then $\Omega_U^A \equiv_T \Omega_U^B$.

Were this the case, $\Omega_U$ would provide a counterexample to a long-standing conjecture of Martin: it would induce an operator on the Turing degrees that is neither increasing nor constant on any cone. But as we show in Theorem 15.7.7, there are oracles $A =^* B$ (i.e., $A$ and $B$ agree except on a finite set) such that $\Omega_U^A$ and $\Omega_U^B$ are not only Turing incomparable, but even relatively random. In particular, by choosing these $A$ and $B$ appropriately, we can ensure that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real while making $\Omega_U^B$ as random as we like. It follows easily that the Turing degree of $\Omega_U^A$ generally depends on the choice of $U$, and in fact, that the degree of randomness of $\Omega_U^A$ can vary drastically with this choice.

If $U$ is a universal prefix-free oracle machine, then we call the map taking $A$ to $\Omega_U^A$ an **Omega operator**. In spite of their failure to be Turing degree invariant, it turns out that Omega operators are rather interesting, and provide the first natural example of interesting c.e. operators that are not CEA. For example, in Section 15.4, we show that the range of an Omega operator has positive measure, and that every 2-random real is in the range of some Omega operator. (This fact is not true of every 1-random real.) In Section 15.5, we prove that $A$ is mapped to a left-c.e. real by some Omega operator iff $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $A$. (Such $A$'s are called *low for $\Omega$*.)

In the final section of this chapter, we consider the analytic behavior of Omega operators. We prove that Omega operators are lower semicontinuous but not continuous, and moreover, that they are continuous exactly at the 1-generic reals. We also produce an Omega operator that does not have a closed range. On the other hand, we prove that every non-2-random that is in the closure of the range of an Omega operator is actually in that range. As a consequence, for each $U$ there is an $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A = \sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$.

### 15.2 Omega operators

Let us begin by recalling that a prefix-free oracle machine $U$ is universal if for every prefix-free oracle machine $M$ there is a prefix $\rho_M \in 2^{<\omega}$, called a coding string of $M$ in $U$, such that

$$\forall A \in 2^\omega \, \forall \sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \, (U^A(\rho_M \sigma) = M^A(\sigma)).$$

Note that this condition is stronger than the requirement that $U^A$ be a universal $A$-computable prefix-free machine for all $A$. For a prefix-free oracle machine $M$, let $\Omega_M^A$ be the halting probability of $M^A$. Formally, $\Omega_M^A = \sum_{M^A(\sigma) \downarrow} 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert}$. This definition gives rise to an operator $\Omega_M : 2^\omega \to [0, 1]$. As mentioned above, if $U$ is a universal prefix-free oracle machine, then we call $\Omega_U$ an **Omega operator**.

It is of course a straightforward relativization of the fact that $\Omega$ is 1-random that if $\Omega_U$ is an Omega operator then $\Omega_U^A$ is 1-random relative to $A$. The following is a strengthening of this fact.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.2.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $\Omega_U$ be an Omega operator. There is a $b$ such that, for each $A$, the real $\Omega_U^A$ is 1-random relative to $A$ with constant $b$; that is, $\forall n \, (K^A(\Omega_U^A \restriction n) \geqslant n - b)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We define a prefix-free oracle machine $M$ as follows. We identify a string $\tau$ with the rational $0.\tau$. For any $A \in 2^\omega$ and $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$, first calculate $\tau = U^A(\sigma)$. Then wait for a stage $s$ such that $\Omega_U^A[s] \geqslant \tau - 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert}$. If such an $s$ is found, then let $M^A(\sigma) = s + 1$. Because $M^A(\sigma) > s$, the convergence of $M^A(\sigma)$ cannot already be taken into account in the calculation of $\Omega_U^A[s]$ (by the usual assumption on the stage by stage approximation to $U$), so, letting $\rho$ be a coding string of $M$ in $U$, in this case $\Omega_U^A[s] \geqslant \Omega_U^A[s] + 2^{-\lvert \rho \sigma \rvert}$. Thus, either $\Omega_U^A < \tau - 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert}$ or $\Omega_U^A \geqslant \Omega_U^A[s] + 2^{-\lvert \rho \sigma \rvert} > \tau - 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert} + 2^{-\lvert \rho \sigma \rvert}$.

Assume for a contradiction that there is an $A$ and an $n$ such that $K^A(\Omega_U^A \restriction n) < n - \lvert \rho \rvert - 1$. Letting $\sigma$ be a minimal length program for $\Omega_U^A \restriction n$, we have shown that either $\Omega_U^A - (\Omega_U^A \restriction n) < -2^{-n}$ or $\Omega_U^A - (\Omega_U^A \restriction n) \geqslant -2^{-n} + 2^{-\lvert \rho \sigma \rvert} > -2^{-n} + 2^{-n+1} = 2^{-n}$.

Neither of these possibilities can happen, so we have a contradiction. Hence, for every $A$ we have $\forall n \, (K^A(\Omega_U^A \restriction n) \geqslant n - \lvert \rho \rvert - 1)$, so we can take $b = \lvert \rho \rvert + 1$. $\square$

Let $U$ and $b$ be as in the theorem. It is clear that if we define $K^A$ using $U$, then there is a $c$ such that $\forall A \in 2^\omega \, \forall \sigma \in 2^{<\omega} \, (K(\sigma) \geqslant K^A(\sigma) - c)$, so all reals in the range of $\Omega_U$ are 1-random with constant $b + c$. In other words, the range of $\Omega_U$ is contained in the closed set $\lbrace X : \forall n \, (K(X \restriction n) \geqslant n - b - c) \rbrace$. In particular, every real in the closure of the range of $\Omega_U$ is 1-random. We will discuss the range of $\Omega_U$ and its closure in more depth in Section 15.10.

Of course, $\Omega_U^A$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real, and every $A$-left-c.e. real is computable from $A'$, hence $\Omega_U^A \leqslant_T A'$. It is not usually the case that $\Omega_U^A \equiv_T A'$. Indeed, by Theorem 11.7.2, this cannot be the case unless $A$ is $K$-trivial. On the other hand, the fact that $\Omega \equiv_T \emptyset'$ has a natural relativization in the following simple result.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.2.2)</span></p>

$\Omega_U^A \oplus A \equiv_T A'$.

</div>

*Proof.* It is clear that $\Omega_U^A \oplus A \leqslant_T A'$. For the other direction, define a prefix-free oracle machine $M$ such that $M^A(0^n 1) \downarrow$ iff $n \in A'$, for all $A \in 2^\omega$ and $n \in \omega$. Assume that $U$ simulates $M$ by the prefix $\tau \in 2^{<\omega}$. To determine whether $n \in A'$, search for a stage $s$ such that $\Omega_U^A - \Omega_U^A[s] < 2^{-(\lvert \tau \rvert + n + 1)}$. This search can be done computably in $\Omega_U^A \oplus A$. Note that $U^A$ cannot converge on a string of length $\lvert \tau \rvert + n + 1$ after stage $s$, so $n \in A'$ iff $M^A(0^n 1) \downarrow$ iff $U^A(\tau 0^n 1)[s] \downarrow$. Therefore, $A' \leqslant_T \Omega_U^A \oplus A$. $\square$

Recall that $B \in 2^\omega$ is generalized low ($\text{GL}_1$) if $B' \leqslant_T B \oplus \emptyset'$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.2.3 — Nies and Stephan)</span></p>

If a $\Delta_s^0$ set $A$ is 1-random relative to $B$, then $B$ is $\text{GL}_1$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $f(n) = \mu s \geqslant s \, (A_t \restriction n = A_s \restriction n)$, and note that $f \leqslant_T \emptyset'$. If $\Phi_e^B(e) \downarrow$ then let $V_e = \llbracket A_s \restriction e + 1 \rrbracket$ for the least $s$ such that $\Phi_e^B(e)[s] \downarrow$. Otherwise, let $V_e = \emptyset$. Let $U_n = \bigcup_{e \geqslant n} V_e$. It is easy to see that $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test relative to $B$, so $A$ is in only finitely many $V_e$'s. So for almost all $e$ such that $\Phi_e^B(e) \downarrow$, we must have $f(e) \geqslant (\mu s) \, \Phi_e^B(e)[s] \downarrow$. Hence $B' \leqslant_T B \oplus \emptyset'$. $\square$

Theorem 15.2.3 implies that the class of low 1-random reals is closed under the action of any Omega operator.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.2.4)</span></p>

For each $\Delta_2^0$ 1-random $A$, the real $\Omega_U^A$ is $\text{GL}_1$. If $A$ is also low, then $\Omega_U^A$ is low.

</div>

### 15.3 $A$-1-random $A$-left-c.e. reals

We can relativize Solovay reducibility as follows. For $A, X, Y \in 2^\omega$, we write $Y \leqslant_s^A X$ to mean that there are a $c$ and a partial $A$-computable $\varphi : 2^{<\omega} \to 2^{<\omega}$ such that if $q < X$, then $\varphi(q) \downarrow < Y$ and $Y - \varphi(q) < c(X - q)$. We say that an $A$-left-c.e. real $X$ is *$A$-Solovay complete* if $Y \leqslant_s^A X$ for every $A$-left-c.e. real $Y$.

Some basic facts about Solovay reducibility relativize easily, with essentially the same proofs as before. For example:

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.3.1)</span></p>

If $Y$ is 1-random relative to $A$ and $Y \leqslant_s^A X$, then $X$ is also 1-random relative to $A$.

</div>

The relativization of the Kučera-Slaman Theorem is equally straightforward.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.3.2)</span></p>

If an $A$-left-c.e. real $X$ is 1-random relative to $A$ then $X$ is $A$-Solovay complete.

</div>

On the other hand, a satisfactory relativization of Theorem 9.2.2, which states that each 1-random left-c.e. real is a halting probability, presents some difficulty. The direct relativization states that if $X$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real and is $A$-Solovay complete, then there is an oracle prefix-free machine $M$ such that $M^A$ is universal for $A$-computable prefix-free machines and $X = \Omega_M^A$. It is by no means clear, though, that we should be able to relativize Theorem 9.2.2 to build a machine that is universal in our strong sense. Nevertheless, the relativized theorem is true.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.3.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $X$ be an $A$-left-c.e. real that is $A$-Solovay complete. Then there is a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$ such that $X = \Omega_U^A$.

</div>

Combining Theorems 15.2.1, 15.3.1, 15.3.2, and 15.3.3, we have the following corollary.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.3.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

For $A, X \in 2^\omega$, the following are equivalent.

1. $X$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real and 1-random relative to $A$.
2. $X$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real and $A$-Solovay complete.
3. $X = \Omega_U^A$ for some universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$.

</div>

### 15.4 Reals in the range of some Omega operator

We proved in the last section that $X$ is in the range of some Omega operator iff there is an $A$ such that $X$ is both 1-random relative to $A$ and an $A$-left-c.e. real. What restrictions does this place on $X$?

The impression we have is that $\Omega$ is a very special 1-random real, and results such as Stephan's Theorem 8.8.4 that most 1-random reals are computationally feeble suggest that most 1-random reals do not resemble $\Omega$ at all. However, we will see that this impression is not true in relativized form. In this section, we show that every 2-random real is 1-random relative to $A$ and an $A$-left-c.e. real for some $A$, but that not every 1-random real has this property. Furthermore, we prove that the range of every Omega operator has positive measure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.4.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

If $X$ is 2-random, then there is an $A$ such that $X$ is 1-random relative to $A$ and an $A$-left-c.e. real.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $A = \frac{(1-X+\Omega)}{2}$. Then $X = 1 - 2A + \Omega$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real. Because $X$ is 2-random, $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $X$. But then $A$ is 1-random relative to $X$ (because clearly, $\Omega \equiv_s^X \frac{(1-X+\Omega)}{2}$). Therefore, applying van Lambalgen's theorem again, $X$ is 1-random relative to $A$. $\square$

As was mentioned above, the previous theorem cannot be proved if $X$ is assumed only to be 1-random.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.4.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$1 - \Omega$ is not in the range of any Omega operator.

</div>

*Proof.* The 1-random real $X = 1 - \Omega$ is a right-c.e. real, i.e., the limit of a decreasing computable sequence of rationals. Assume that $X$ is an $A$-left-c.e. real for some $A$. Then $A$ computes sequences limiting to $X$ from both sides, and hence $X \leqslant_T A$. Therefore, $X$ is not 1-random relative to $A$. $\square$

It would not be difficult to prove that $1 - \Omega$ cannot even be in the closure of the range of an Omega operator, but a direct proof is unnecessary because this fact follows from Theorem 15.10.4 below.

We now consider a fixed Omega operator. We will use the theorem of Lusin that analytic sets (i.e., projections of Borel sets) are measurable. We will also use the fact that the image of an analytic set under any Borel operator — for example, $\Omega_U$ — is also analytic. For a class $S \subseteq 2^\omega$, we write $\Omega_U[S]$ for the image of $S$ under the operator $\Omega_U$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.4.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

The range of $\Omega_U$ has positive measure. In fact, if $S \subseteq 2^\omega$ is any analytic set whose downward closure under $\leqslant_T$ is all of $2^\omega$, then $\mu(\Omega_U[S]) > 0$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $R = \Omega_U[S]$. Note that $R$ is an analytic subset of $2^\omega$ and hence is measurable. Assume for a contradiction that $\mu(R) = 0$. In particular, the outer measure is zero, which means that there is a nested sequence $U_0 \supseteq U_1 \supseteq U_2 \supseteq \cdots$ of open subsets of $2^\omega$ such that for each $n$ we have $R \subseteq U_n$ and $\mu(U_n) \leqslant 2^{-n}$. Take a set $B \in S$ that codes $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ in some effective way. Then $\lbrace U_n \rbrace_{n \in \omega}$ is a Martin-Löf test relative to $B$, which implies that $\Omega_U^B \notin \bigcap_n U_n$. But $R \subseteq \bigcap_n U_n$, so $\Omega_U^B \notin R = \Omega_U[S]$, which is a contradiction. Thus $\mu(R) > 0$. $\square$

This theorem implies that many null classes have $\Omega_U$-images with positive measure, for example $\lbrace A : \forall n \, (2n \notin A) \rbrace$.

We finish this section with a simple consequence of Theorem 15.4.3.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.4.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

For almost every $X$, there is an $A$ such that $X =^* \Omega_U^A$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $S = \lbrace X : \exists A \, (X =^* \Omega_U^A) \rbrace$. Then $S$ is a $\Sigma_1^1$ class, and hence measurable by Lusin's theorem mentioned above, and is closed under $=^*$. But $\mu(S) \geqslant \mu(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U) > 0$. It follows from Kolmogorov's 0-1 Law that $\mu(S) = 1$. $\square$

### 15.5 Lowness for $\Omega$

We begin this section with the following question: For which oracles $A$ is there a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$ such that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real? We will see that this property holds for almost every $A$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.5.1 — Low for $\Omega$)</span></p>

A set $A$ is **low for $\Omega$** if $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $A$.

</div>

It clearly does not matter in this definition which version of $\Omega$ we take, since all versions are Solovay equivalent.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.5.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$A \in 2^\omega$ is low for $\Omega$ iff there is a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$ such that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real.

</div>

*Proof.* First assume that there is a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$ such that $X = \Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real. Every 1-random left-c.e. real is Solovay equivalent to $\Omega$, which means that $X \leqslant_s^A \Omega$. Both $X$ and $\Omega$ are $A$-left-c.e. reals. Applying Theorem 15.3.1, we see that since $X$ is 1-random relative to $A$, so is $\Omega$.

For the other direction, assume that $A$ is low for $\Omega$. Then $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $A$ and an $A$-left-c.e. real, so by Corollary 15.3.4, $\Omega = \Omega_U^A$ for some universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$. $\square$

It follows from the above proof and Proposition 15.2.2 that if $A$ is low for $\Omega$, then $\Omega \oplus A \equiv_T A'$. Therefore, $A' \equiv_T \emptyset' \oplus A$; that is, $A$ is $\text{GL}_1$, a fact which also follows from Theorem 15.2.3.

We now show that almost every set is low for $\Omega$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.5.3 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn)</span></p>

A 1-random set $A$ is low for $\Omega$ iff $A$ is 2-random.

</div>

*Proof.* Assume that $A$ is 1-random. Since $\Omega \equiv_T \emptyset'$, we have that $A$ is 2-random iff $A$ is 1-random relative to $\Omega$ iff $\Omega$ is 1-random relative to $A$, where the last equivalence follows from van Lambalgen's Theorem. $\square$

More evidence for the ubiquity of low for $\Omega$ sets is the following low for $\Omega$ basis theorem, which we already met as Theorem 8.7.2, and is an immediate corollary of Theorem 15.5.2 and Theorem 15.7.1 below.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.5.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies; Reimann and Slaman)</span></p>

Every nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class contains a $\emptyset'$-left-c.e. real that is low for $\Omega$.

</div>

Every $K$-trivial set is low for 1-randomness, and hence low for $\Omega$. However, by the previous result applied to the $\Pi_1^0$ class of completions of Peano Arithmetic, there are also sets that are low for $\Omega$ but neither 1-random nor $K$-trivial. (Suppose that $A$ is low for $\Omega$ and has PA degree. By Theorem 8.8.4 and the fact that no 2-random set can compute $\emptyset'$, as argued for instance in the paragraph above Theorem 8.5.3, $A$ cannot be 2-random, and hence, by Theorem 15.5.3, $A$ cannot be 1-random. As mentioned above Theorem 8.8.4, $A$ computes a 1-random set, so by Corollary 11.4.10, $A$ cannot be $K$-trivial.)

### 15.6 Weak lowness for $K$

In this section we introduce the concept of weak lowness for $K$, and find surprising connections with the previous sections and with 2-randomness, culminating in a proof of two results of Miller: that having infinitely often maximal $K$-complexity and being 2-random coincide, and that 2-random sets are infinitely often quite trivial as oracles with respect to prefix-free complexity.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.6.1 — Weakly Low for $K$)</span></p>

We say that $A$ is **weakly low for $K$** if there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(n) \leqslant K^A(n) + O(1)$.

</div>

#### 15.6.1 Weak lowness for $K$ and lowness for $\Omega$

Like their stronger analogs, lowness for $K$ and lowness for 1-randomness, weak lowness for $K$ and lowness for $\Omega$ coincide.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.6.2 — Miller)</span></p>

$A$ is weakly low for $K$ iff $A$ is low for $\Omega$.

</div>

*Proof.* We first prove that if $A$ is weakly low for $K$ then $A$ is low for $\Omega$. We prove the contrapositive. First, we define families of uniformly c.e. sets $\lbrace W_\sigma \rbrace_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}}$ and $\lbrace D_\sigma \rbrace_{\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}}$. Fix $\sigma \in 2^{<\omega}$. Search for the least stage $s$ such that $\sigma \prec \Omega_s$. If no such stage is found, then let $W_\sigma$ and $D_\sigma$ be empty. Otherwise, for any $\tau$ such that $U(\tau)$ converges for the first time after stage $s$, enumerate $(\lvert \tau \rvert, U(\tau))$ into $D_\sigma$. Also enumerate $(\lvert \tau \rvert, U(\tau))$ into $W_\sigma$ as long as such enumeration preserves the condition that $\sum_{(d,n) \in W_\sigma} 2^{-d} \leqslant 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert}$.

We claim that if $\sigma \prec \Omega$, then $W_\sigma = D_\sigma$. It follows from our definition that $\sum_{(d,n) \in D_\sigma} 2^{-d} \leqslant \Omega - \Omega_s$. But if $\sigma \prec \Omega$ then $\Omega - \Omega_s \leqslant 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert}$, so in this case $\sum_{(d,n) \in W_\sigma} 2^{-d} \leqslant 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert}$, and hence $W_\sigma = D_\sigma$. The idea is that we have used an approximation of $\Omega$ to efficiently approximate all but finitely many values of $K(n)$.

Next, consider the $A$-c.e. set $W = \lbrace (d + \lvert \tau \rvert - \lvert \sigma \rvert, n) : U^A(\tau) = \sigma \text{ and } (d, n) \in W_\sigma \rbrace$. By the construction of $\lbrace W_\sigma \rbrace$, $\sum_{(e,n) \in W} 2^{-e} \leqslant \sum_{U^A(\tau) \downarrow} 2^{-\lvert \tau \rvert} \leqslant 1$. Thus $W$ is a KC set relative to $A$. Then for any $c$, there are $\tau$ and $\sigma$ such that $U^A(\tau) = \sigma$, with $\lvert \sigma \rvert - \lvert \tau \rvert \geqslant c$ and $\sigma \prec \Omega$. So for almost all $e$ such that $\Phi_e^B(e) \downarrow$, we must have $f(e) \geqslant (\mu s) \Phi_e^B(e)[s] \downarrow$.

Now, assume that $\Omega$ is not 1-random relative to $A$. Then for any $c$, there are $\tau$ and $\sigma$ such that $U^A(\tau) = \sigma$, with $\lvert \sigma \rvert - \lvert \tau \rvert \geqslant c$ and $\sigma \prec \Omega$. But this means that $K^A(n) \leqslant K(n) + \lvert \tau \rvert - \lvert \sigma \rvert + O(1) \leqslant K(n) - c + O(1)$, for all but finitely many $n$. But $c$ is arbitrary, so $A$ is not weakly low for $K$.

The other half of Miller's proof is much more difficult, but Bienvenu (personal communication to Downey) observed that it can be replaced with an argument based on the results of Bienvenu and Downey on Solovay functions.

Recall that a Solovay function is a computable function $f$ such that $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)} < \infty$, whence $K(n) \leqslant f(n) + O(1)$, and $\liminf_n f(n) - K(n) < \infty$. Recall also the Bienvenu-Downey characterization of Solovay functions, Theorem 9.4.1: A computable function $f$ is a Solovay function iff $\sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$ is 1-random. We apply this result. Suppose that $A$ is not weakly low for $K$. Then $\lim_n K(n) - K^A(n) = \infty$. Let $f$ be computable and such that $\Omega = \sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$. Then $f(n) \geqslant K(n) - O(1)$, and hence $\lim_n f(n) - K^A(n) = \infty$. Therefore, $f$ is not an $A$-Solovay function, and so Theorem 9.4.1 relativized to $A$ implies that $\Omega = \sum_n 2^{-f(n)}$ is not 1-random relative to $A$. $\square$

As a consequence, we have the following weakly low for $K$ basis theorem.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.6.3 — Miller)</span></p>

Every nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class has a member that is weakly low for $K$.

</div>

*Proof.* Given the above theorem, this result follows from Theorem 15.5.4. $\square$

The following corollary improves an earlier unpublished result of Miller that 3-random sets are weakly low for $K$. It follows from Theorems 15.5.3 and 15.6.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.6.4 — Nies, Stephan, and Terwijn; Miller)</span></p>

A 1-random set is weakly low for $K$ iff it is 2-random.

</div>

This is one of several results we have encountered that exhibit various ways in which highly random sets are relatively computationally powerless. These results point to an interesting phenomenon: in many aspects, highly random sets resemble highly nonrandom ones. One way to look at this phenomenon is that while highly random sets have high information, in the sense of initial segment complexity, this is "useless information". It would be of great potential interest to develop precise ways to define usefulness of information in the context of algorithmic randomness.

#### 15.6.2 Infinitely often strongly $K$-random sets

We recall from Section 6.11 that $A$ is infinitely often strongly $K$-random if $K(A \restriction n) \geqslant n + K(n) - O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$, and infinitely often $C$-random if $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant n - O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$. In that section, we saw that every 3-random set is infinitely often strongly $K$-random, that every infinitely often strongly $K$-random set is infinitely often $C$-random, and that being infinitely often $C$-random is the same as being 2-random. Thus it was a fundamental question whether being infinitely often $C$-random and being infinitely often strongly $K$-random are equivalent. Miller answered this question in the affirmative, using results discussed above.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.6.5 — Miller)</span></p>

If $A$ is 2-random then it is infinitely often strongly $K$-random.

</div>

*Proof.* Assume that $A$ is 2-random. By Theorems 15.5.3 and 15.6.2, $A$ is weakly low for $K$. By Corollary 6.6.4, $K^A(n) \leqslant K(A \restriction n) - n + O(1)$, so $K(A \restriction n) \geqslant n + K^A(n) - O(1)$. Because $A$ is weakly low for $K$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $K(n) \leqslant K^A(n) + O(1)$. For such $n$, we have $K(A \restriction n) \geqslant n + K(n) - O(1)$, so $A$ is infinitely often strongly $K$-random. $\square$

### 15.7 When $\Omega^A$ is a left-c.e. real

In this section, we consider sets $A$ for which $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real. Far from being a rare property, we will show that $\mu \lbrace A : \Omega_U^A \text{ is a left-c.e. real} \rbrace > 0$ for any fixed universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$. On the other hand, we will see that only a left-c.e. real can have an $\Omega_U$-preimage with positive measure. So left-c.e. reals clearly play an important role in understanding $\Omega_U$. Their main application here is in our proof that no Omega operator is degree invariant.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $M$ be a prefix-free oracle machine. If $P \subseteq 2^\omega$ is a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class, then there is a $\emptyset'$-left-c.e. real $A \in P$ such that $\Omega_M^A = \inf \lbrace \Omega_M^X : X \in P \rbrace$, which is a left-c.e. real.

</div>

We now consider sets $X$ such that $\Omega_U^{-1}(X)$ has positive measure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $M$ be a prefix-free oracle machine. If $\mu \lbrace A : \Omega_M^A = X \rbrace > 0$ then $X$ is a left-c.e. real.

</div>

For $X \in 2^\omega$, let $m_U(X) = \mu(\lbrace A : \Omega_U^A = X \rbrace)$. Define the **spectrum** of $\Omega_U$ to be $\text{Spec}(\Omega_U) = \lbrace X : m_U(X) > 0 \rbrace$. By the lemma, the spectrum is a set of 1-random left-c.e. reals. We prove that it is nonempty. We use the fact that every 2-random set is weakly 2-random, and hence cannot be contained in a null $\Pi_2^0$ class.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$m_U(X) > 0$ iff there is a 1-random $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A = X$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$\text{Spec}(\Omega_U)$ is nonempty.

</div>

*Proof.* Apply Theorem 15.7.1 to a nonempty $\Pi_1^0$ class containing only 1-random sets to obtain a 1-random $A$ such that $X = \Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real. By Lemma 15.7.3, $X \in \text{Spec}(\Omega_U)$. $\square$

We have proved that $\Omega_U$ maps a set of positive measure to the left-c.e. reals. We now prove that this is not the case for every set, can be mapped to a left-c.e. real by *some* Omega operator). We prove that each of these outcomes occurs with positive measure, in Theorems 15.7.4 and 15.7.5, respectively. Theorem 15.7.5 has no obvious connection to left-c.e. reals, but in fact, Theorem 15.7.4 — applied to a modification of the universal machine $U$ — is used to prove it.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

There is an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $\mu(\lbrace B : \Omega_U^B \text{ is 1-random relative to } Z \rbrace) \geqslant \varepsilon$ for all $Z$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$0 < \mu(\lbrace A : \Omega_U^A \text{ is a left-c.e. real} \rbrace) < 1$.

</div>

The most important consequence of the work in this section is the following resoundingly negative answer to the question of whether $\Omega_U$ is degree invariant.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.7 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

For all $Z$, there are $A =^* B$ such that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real and $\Omega_U^B$ is 1-random relative to each other.

(ii) There are $A =^* B$ such that $\Omega_U^A \vert_T \Omega_U^B$ (and, in fact, $\Omega_U^A$ and $\Omega_U^B$ are 1-random relative to each other).

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.7.8 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

There is a properly $\Sigma_2^0$ set $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real.

</div>

### 15.8 $\Omega^A$ for $K$-trivial $A$

In previous sections, we considered sets that can be mapped to left-c.e. reals by *some* Omega operator. We now look at sets $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real for *every* universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$. We will see that these are exactly the $K$-trivials.

Recall Lemma 11.5.5, which states that if $A$ is $K$-trivial and $M$ is a prefix-free oracle machine, then there is a computable sequence of stages $v(0) < v(1) < \cdots$ such that

$$\sum \lbrace \widehat{c}(x, r) : x \text{ is minimal s.t. } A_{v(r+1)}(x) \neq A_{v(r+2)}(x) \rbrace < \infty,$$

where $\widehat{c}(x, r)$ measures the maximum amount that $\Omega_M^A[v(r+1)]$ can decrease because of an $A(x)$ change after stage $v(r+1)$, provided we count only the $M^A(\sigma)$ computations with use $\leqslant v(r)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.8.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Let $U$ be a universal prefix-free oracle machine. The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is $K$-trivial.
2. $A$ is $\Delta_2^0$ and $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real.
3. $A \leqslant_T \Omega_U^A$.
4. $A' \equiv_T \Omega_U^A$.

</div>

*Proof.* (ii) $\Rightarrow$ (iii) follows from the fact that each 1-random left-c.e. real is Turing complete. (iii) $\Rightarrow$ (i) follows from Theorem 11.7.2, since (iii) implies that $A$ is a base for 1-randomness. (iii) $\Leftrightarrow$ (iv) by Proposition 15.2.2. So we are left with showing that (i) $\Rightarrow$ (ii).

Let $A$ be $K$-trivial. By Theorem 11.1.1, $A$ is $\Delta_2^0$. We show that there is an $r_0 \in \mathbb{N}$ and an effective sequence $\lbrace q_r \rbrace_{r \in \omega}$ of rationals such that $\Omega_U^A = \sup_{r \geqslant r_0} q_r$, and hence $\Omega_U^A$ is a left-c.e. real.

Applying Lemma 11.5.5 to $U$, we obtain a computable sequence of stages $v(0) < v(1) < \cdots$ such that (15.2) holds. The desired sequence of rationals is defined by letting

$$q_r = \sum \lbrace 2^{-\lvert \sigma \rvert} : U^A(\sigma)[v(r+1)] \downarrow \wedge \text{use}(U^A(\sigma)[v(r+1)]) \leqslant v(r) \rbrace.$$

Thus $q_r$ measures the computations existing at stage $v(r+1)$ whose use is at most $v(r)$. We define $r_0$ below; first we verify that $\Omega_U^A \leqslant \sup_{r \geqslant r_0} q_r$ for any $r_0$. Given $\sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_m \in \text{dom}(U^A)$, choose $r_1$ so that each computation $U^A(\sigma_i)$ has settled by stage $v(r_1)$, with use $\leqslant v(r_1)$. If $r \geqslant r_1$ then $q_r \geqslant \sum_{i \leqslant m} 2^{-\lvert \sigma_i \rvert}$. Therefore, $\Omega_U^A \leqslant \lim \sup_r q_r \leqslant \sup_{r \geqslant r_0} q_r$.

Now define a Solovay test $\lbrace I_r \rbrace_{r \in \omega}$ as follows: if $x$ is minimal such that $A_{v(r+1)}(x) \neq A_{v(r+2)}(x)$, then let $I_r = [q_r - \widehat{c}(x, r), q_r]$. Then $\sum_r \lvert I_r \rvert$ is finite by (15.2), so $\lbrace I_r \rbrace_{r \in \omega}$ is indeed a Solovay test. Also note that, by the comment after the lemma, $\min I_r \leqslant \max I_{r+1}$ for each $r$.

Since $\Omega_U^A$ is 1-random, there is an $r_0$ such that $\Omega_U^A \notin I_r$ for all $r \geqslant r_0$. We show that $q_r < \Omega_U^A$ for each $r \geqslant r_0$. Fix $r \geqslant r_0$. Let $t \geqslant r$ be the first non-deficiency stage for the enumeration $t \mapsto A_{v(t+1)}$. That is, if $x$ is minimal such that $A_{v(t+1)}(x) \neq A_{v(t+2)}(x)$, then $\forall t' \geqslant t \, \forall y < x \, (A_{v(t'+1)}(y) = A_{v(t+1)}(y))$.

The quantity $q_t - \widehat{c}(x, t)$ measures the computations $U^A(\sigma)[v(t+1)]$ with use $\leqslant v(t)$. Now $\Omega_U^A \notin I_r$ for $u = t - 1, \ldots, u = r$, we see that $\Omega_U^A \geqslant \max I_r = q_r$. Therefore, $\Omega_U^A \geqslant \sup_{r \geqslant r_0} q_r$. $\square$

One consequence of this theorem is that all Omega operators are degree invariant on the $K$-trivials. The next example shows that they need not be degree invariant anywhere else.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.8.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

There is an Omega operator that is degree invariant only on $K$-trivials.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $M$ be a prefix-free oracle machine such that $\Omega_M^A = \begin{cases} A & \text{if } A(0) = 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } A(0) = 1. \end{cases}$

For any $A$, define $\widehat{A}$ by $\widehat{A}(n) = A(n)$ iff $n \neq 0$. Define a universal prefix-free oracle oracle machine $V$ by $V^A(00\sigma) = U^A(\sigma)$, $V^A(01\sigma) = M^A(\sigma)$ for all $\sigma$. Then $\lvert \Omega_V^A - \Omega_V^{\widehat{A}} \rvert = \frac{1}{2}$ for all $A$. Assume that $\Omega_V^A \leqslant_T \Omega_V^{\widehat{A}}$. Then $A \leqslant_T \Omega_V^A$ is a base for 1-randomness and hence $K$-trivial by Theorem 11.7.2. If $\Omega_V^A \vert_T \Omega_V^{\widehat{A}}$, then $A$ is not $K$-trivial. Therefore, if $A$ is not $K$-trivial, then $\Omega_V^A \vert_T \Omega_V^{\widehat{A}}$. $\square$

The following corollary summarizes Theorem 15.8.1 and Example 15.8.2.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.8.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is $K$-trivial.
2. Every Omega operator takes $A$ to a left-c.e. real.
3. Every Omega operator is degree invariant on the Turing degree of $A$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.8.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

Every $K$-trivial is a left-d.c.e. real.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $V$ be the machine from the example. Assume that $A$ is $K$-trivial and let $\widehat{A}$ be as in the example. Then $\Omega_V^A$ and $\Omega_V^{\widehat{A}}$ are both left-c.e. reals by Theorem 15.8.1. Therefore, $A = 2 \lvert \Omega_V^A - \Omega_V^{\widehat{A}} \rvert$ is a left-d.c.e. real. $\square$

By Theorem 5.4.7, the left-d.c.e. reals form a real closed field. The corollary gives us a nontrivial real closed subfield: the $K$-trivial reals. To see this, recall that in Chapter 11 we have seen that the $K$-trivials form an ideal in the Turing degrees. Because a zero of an odd degree polynomial can be computed relative to its coefficients, the $K$-trivial reals also form a real closed field.

### 15.9 $K$-triviality and left-d.c.e. reals

Having mentioned left-d.c.e. reals in the previous section, we now show that they can be used to give a rather surprising characterization of $K$-triviality, which does not mention randomness or initial segment complexity in any obvious way.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.9.1)</span></p>

$A$ is **low for left-d.c.e. reals** if the class of reals that are left-d.c.e. relative to $A$ coincides with the class of left-d.c.e. reals.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.9.2 — Miller)</span></p>

$A$ is $K$-trivial iff $A$ is low for left-d.c.e. reals.

</div>

*Proof.* First suppose that $A$ is low for left-d.c.e. reals. Then $\Omega^A$ is left-d.c.e. and 1-random, so by Rettinger's Theorem 9.2.4, either $\Omega^A$ or $1 - \Omega^A$ is a left-c.e. real. But $1 - \Omega^A$ cannot be a left-c.e. real, since that would imply $\Omega^A$ is $A$-computable. So $\Omega^A$ is a left-c.e. real, and hence $\Omega^A \equiv_T \emptyset'$. But $A$ must be a left-d.c.e. real relative to itself, so $A$ is $\Delta_2^0$, and hence $A \leqslant_T \Omega^A$. By Theorem 15.8.1, $A$ is $K$-trivial.

Now suppose that $A$ is $K$-trivial. Let $\alpha$ be a left-c.e. real relative to $A$. Then $\Omega^A + \alpha$ is a left-c.e. real relative to $A$ and is 1-random, so it is a version of $\Omega$ relative to $A$. Thus, by Theorem 15.8.1, $\Omega^A + \alpha$ is an unrelativized version of $\Omega$, and so is a left-c.e. real. Now let $\delta = \alpha - \beta$ be a left-d.c.e. real relative to $A$. Then $\delta = (\Omega^A + \alpha) - (\Omega^A + \beta)$, and hence $\delta$ is a left-d.c.e. real. $\square$

### 15.10 Analytic behavior of Omega operators

In this section, we examine Omega operators from the perspective of analysis. Given a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$, we consider two questions:

1. To what extent is $\Omega_U$ continuous?
2. How complex is the range of $\Omega_U$?

To answer the first question, we show that $\Omega_U$ is lower semicontinuous but not continuous. Furthermore, we prove that it is continuous exactly at the 1-generic reals. Together with the semicontinuity, this fact implies that $\Omega_U$ can achieve its supremum only at a 1-generic. But must $\Omega_U$ actually achieve its supremum? This question relates to the second question above. We write $S^c$ for the closure of $S$. Theorem 15.10.4 states that any real in $\text{rng}(\Omega_U)^c \setminus \text{rng}(\Omega_U)$ must be 2-random. Because $X = \sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$ is a left-c.e. real, and hence not 2-random, there is an $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A = X$. It is natural to ask whether $\text{rng}(\Omega_U)$ is closed. In other words, is Theorem 15.10.4 vacuous? Example 15.10.6 demonstrates that for *some* choice of $U$, the range of $\Omega_U$ is not closed, and indeed, $\mu(\text{rng}(\Omega_U)) < \mu(\text{rng}(\Omega_U)^c)$. Whether this is the case for *all* universal prefix-free oracle machines is open. Furthermore, we know of no nontrivial upper bound on the complexity of $\text{rng}(\Omega_U)$, but we do show below that $\text{rng}(\Omega_U)^c$ is a $\Pi_3^0$ class.

A function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is *lower semicontinuous* if $\lbrace x : f(x) > a \rbrace$ is open for every $a \in \mathbb{R}$. We show that for any prefix-free oracle machine $M$, the function $\Omega_M$ is lower semicontinuous. Note that for any $A$,

$$\forall \delta > 0 \, \exists m \, (\Omega_M^A - \Omega_M^{A \restriction m} \leqslant \delta), \tag{15.3}$$

and hence $\forall X \succ A \restriction m \, (\Omega_M^X - \Omega_M^A \leqslant \delta)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.1 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$\Omega_M$ is lower semicontinuous for every prefix-free oracle machine $M$.

</div>

We next prove that Omega operators are not continuous, and characterize their points of continuity. An open set $S \subseteq 2^\omega$ is *dense along $A$* if each initial segment of $A$ has an extension in $S$. It is easy to see that $A$ is 1-generic iff $A$ is in every $\Sigma_1^0$ class that is dense along $A$. We prove that $\Omega_U$ is continuous exactly on the 1-generics, for any universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.2 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

The following are equivalent.

1. $A$ is 1-generic.
2. If $M$ is a prefix-free oracle machine, then $\Omega_M$ is continuous at $A$.
3. There is a universal prefix-free oracle machine $U$ such that $\Omega_U$ is continuous at $A$.

</div>

Let $U$ be a universal prefix-free oracle machine.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.3 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

If $\Omega_U^A = \sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$, then $A$ is 1-generic.

</div>

The above corollary does not guarantee that the supremum is achieved. Surprisingly, it is. In fact, we can prove quite a bit more. One way to view the proof of the following theorem is that we are trying to prevent any real that is not 2-random from being in the closure of the range of $\Omega_U$. If we fail for some $X$, then it will turn out that $X \in \text{rng}(\Omega_U)$. Note that this fact is a consequence of universality; it is easy to construct a prefix-free oracle machine $M$ such that $\Omega_M$ does not achieve its supremum.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.4 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

If $X \in \text{rng}(\Omega_U)^c \setminus \text{rng}(\Omega_U)$, then $X$ is 2-random.

</div>

If $X \in \text{rng}(\Omega_U)$ is not 2-random, then an examination of the above construction gives an upper bound on the complexity of $\Omega_U^{-1}[X]$. The $\Pi_1^0$ classes $C_p$ can be computed uniformly. The $B_q$ are also $\Pi_1^0$ classes and can be found uniformly in $X \oplus \emptyset'$. Therefore, $\Omega_U^{-1}[X] = \bigcap_{q < X} B_q \cap \bigcap_{p > X} C_p$ is a nonempty $\Pi_1^{0, X \oplus \emptyset'}$ class.

The following corollary gives an interesting special case of Theorem 15.10.4. It is not hard to prove that there is an $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A = \inf(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$ (see Theorem 15.7.1). It is much less obvious that $\Omega_U$ achieves its supremum.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.5 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

There is an $A$ such that $\Omega_U^A = \sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Since $\sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$ is a left-c.e. real, it is not 2-random, so the corollary is immediate from Theorem 15.10.4. $\square$

By Proposition 8.11.9, no 1-generic is 1-random, so $\mu(\lbrace A : \Omega_U^A = \sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U) \rbrace) = 0$. Therefore, $\sup(\text{rng}\,\Omega_U)$ is an example of a left-c.e. real that is not in $\text{Spec}(\Omega_U)$.
