One might ask whether Theorem 15.10.4 is vacuous. In other words, is the range of $\Omega_U$ actually closed? We can construct a specific universal prefix-free oracle machine such that it is not. The construction is somewhat similar to the proof of Theorem 15.4.3. In that case, we avoided a measure zero *closed* set by using a natural number to code a finite open cover with sufficiently small measure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--question" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Example</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.6 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

There is a universal prefix-free oracle machine $V$ such that $\mu(\text{rng}(\Omega_V)) < \mu(\text{rng}(\Omega_V)^c)$.

</div>

Note that the proof above shows that if $U$ is a universal prefix-free oracle machine and $S = \lbrace \Omega_U^{0^n 1 0^\omega} : n \in \mathbb{N} \rbrace$, then $S^c$ has positive measure and $S^c \setminus S$ contains only 2-randoms.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Proposition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(15.10.7 — Downey, Hirschfeldt, Miller, and Nies)</span></p>

$\text{rng}(\Omega_U)^c$ is a $\Pi_3^0$ class.

</div>

## Chapter 16: Complexity of Computably Enumerable Sets

### 16.1 Barzdins' Lemma and Kummer Complex Sets

In this section, we look at the initial segment complexity of c.e. sets, including a fascinating gap phenomenon uncovered by Kummer. We begin with an old result of Barzdins.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.1 — Barzdins' Lemma)</span></p>

If $A$ is a c.e. set then $C(A \restriction n \mid n) \leqslant \log n + O(1)$ and $C(A \restriction n) \leqslant 2 \log n + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* To describe $A \restriction n$ given $n$, it suffices to supply the number $k_n$ of elements in $A \restriction n$ and an $e$ such that $A = W_e$, since we can recover $A \restriction n$ from this information by running the enumeration of $W_e$ until $k_n$ many elements appear in $W_e \restriction n$. Such a description can be given in $\log n + O(1)$ many bits.

For the second part of the lemma, we can encode $k_n$ and $n$ as two strings $\sigma$ and $\tau$, respectively, each of length $\log n$. We can recover $\sigma$ and $\tau$ from $\sigma\tau$ because we know the length of each of these two strings is exactly half the length of $\sigma\tau$. Thus we can describe $A \restriction n$ in $2 \log n + O(1)$ many bits. $\square$

Barzdins also constructed an example of a c.e. set $A$ with $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant \log n$ for all $n$. If $C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + O(1)$ for all $n$ then, by the proof of Theorem 3.4.4, $A$ is computable. A long-standing open question was whether the $2 \log n$ in the second part of Theorem 16.1.1 is optimal. The best we could hope for is a c.e. set $A$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant 2 \log n - O(1)$ infinitely often, since the following is known.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.2 — Solovay [unpublished])</span></p>

There is no c.e. set $A$ such that $C(A \restriction n \mid n) \geqslant \log n - O(1)$ for all $n$. Similarly, there is no c.e. set $A$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant 2 \log n - O(1)$ for all $n$.

</div>

More recently, a simple proof of a result stronger than Solovay's was discovered.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.3 — Hölzl, Kräling, and Merkle)</span></p>

For any c.e. set $A$ there exist infinitely many $m$ such that $C(A \restriction m \mid m) = O(1)$ and $C(A \restriction m) \leqslant C(m) + O(1)$.

</div>

*Proof.* We may assume that $A$ is infinite. Let $m$ be such that $m$ enters $A$ at stage $s$ and no number less than $m$ enters $A$ after stage $s$. There must be infinitely many such $m$, and the theorem clearly holds for any of them. $\square$

Solovay explicitly asked whether there is a c.e. set $A$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant 2 \log n - O(1)$ infinitely often. As we will see the answer is yes, and there is a precise characterization of the degrees that contain such sets.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.4 — Kummer Complex)</span></p>

A c.e. set $A$ is **Kummer complex** if for each $d$ there are infinitely many $n$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant 2 \log n - d$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.5 — Kummer)</span></p>

There is a Kummer complex c.e. set.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $t_0 = 0$ and $t_{k+1} = 2^{t_k}$. Let $I_k = (t_k, t_{k+1}]$ and

$$f(k) = \sum_{i=t_k+1}^{t_{k+1}} (i - t_k + 1).$$

Note that $f(k)$ asymptotically approaches $\frac{t_{k+1}^2}{2}$, and hence $\log f(k) > 2 \log t_{k+1} - 2$ for sufficiently large $k$. So it is enough to build a c.e. set $A$ such that for each $k$ there is an $n \in I_k$ with $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant \log f(k)$.

Enumerate $A$ as follows. At stage $s + 1$, for each $k \leqslant s$, if $C_s(A_s \restriction n) < \log f(k)$ for all $n \in I_k$ and $I_k \nsubseteq A_s$, then put the smallest element of $\overline{A_s} \cap I_k$ into $A_{s+1}$.

Now suppose that $C(A \restriction n) < \log f(k)$ for all $n \in I_k$. Then there must be a stage $s$ such that $A_s \restriction n = A \restriction n$ and $C_s(A_s \restriction n) < \log f(k)$. We must have $I_k \nsubseteq A_s$, since otherwise the smallest element of $\overline{A_s} \cap I_k$ would enter $A$, contradicting the assumption that $A_s \restriction n = A \restriction n$. Thus, all of $I_k$ is eventually put into $A$. So for each $n \in I_k$ there are stages $s_0 < \cdots < s_{n-t_k}$ such that $A_{s_{i+1}} \restriction n \neq A_{s_i} \restriction n$ and $C_{s_i}(A_{s_i}) < \log f(k)$, and hence there are at least $n - t_k + 1$ many strings $\sigma$ with $\lvert \sigma \rvert = n$ and $C(\sigma) < \log f(k)$. Thus, there are at least $f(k)$ many strings $\sigma$ such that $C(\sigma) < \log f(k)$, which is a contradiction. $\square$

Kummer also gave an exact characterization of the degrees containing Kummer complex c.e. sets, using the notion of array noncomputability discussed in Section 2.23.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.1.6 — Kummer's Gap Theorem)</span></p>

(i) A c.e. degree contains a Kummer complex set iff it is array noncomputable.

(ii) In addition, if $A$ is c.e. and of array computable degree, then for every computable order $f$,

$$C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + f(n) + O(1).$$

(iii) Hence the c.e. degrees exhibit the following gap phenomenon: for each c.e. degree $\mathbf{a}$, either

- (a) there is a c.e. set $A \in \mathbf{a}$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant 2 \log n - O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$, or
- (b) there is no c.e. set $A \in \mathbf{a}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $C(A \restriction n) \geqslant (1 + \varepsilon) \log n - O(1)$ for infinitely many $n$.

</div>

*Proof.* Part (iii) follows immediately from parts (i) and (ii), so we prove the latter.

*Part (i):* To make $A$ Kummer complex, all we need is to have the construction from Theorem 16.1.5 work for infinitely many intervals. Let $I_k$ and $f(k)$ be as in the proof of that theorem, and let $\mathcal{I}$ be the very strong array $\lbrace I_k \rbrace_{k \in \mathbb{N}}$. Let $A$ be an $\mathcal{I}$-a.n.c. c.e. set.

Define a c.e. set $W$ as follows. At stage $s + 1$, for each $k \leqslant s$, if $C_s(A_s \restriction n) < \log f(k)$ for all $n \in I_k$ and $I_k \nsubseteq A_s$, then put the smallest element of $\overline{A_s} \cap I_k$ into $W$.

Since $A$ is $\mathcal{I}$-a.n.c., there are infinitely many $k$ such that $A \cap I_k = W \cap I_k$. A similar argument to that in the proof of Theorem 16.1.5 now shows that, for any such $k$, if $C(A \restriction n) < \log f(k)$ for all $n \in I_k$ then $I_k \subset A$, and hence that for each $n \in I_k$, there are at least $n - t_k + 1$ many strings $\sigma$ with $\lvert \sigma \rvert = n$ and $C(\sigma) < \log f(k)$, which leads to the same contradiction as before. Thus $A$ is Kummer complex.

By Theorem 2.23.4, each array noncomputable c.e. degree contains an $\mathcal{I}$-a.n.c. set, so each such degree contains a Kummer complex set.

*Part (ii):* Let $A$ be c.e. and of array computable degree, and let $f$ be a computable order. Let $m(n) = \max\lbrace i : f(i) < n \rbrace$. Note that $m$ is a computable order, and is defined for almost all $n$. Let $g(n) = A \restriction m(n)$. (If $m(n)$ is undefined then let $g(n) = 0$.) Since $g$ is computable in $A$, Proposition 2.23.6 implies that there is a total computable approximation $\lbrace g_s \rbrace_{s \in \mathbb{N}}$ such that $\lim_s g_s(n) = g(n)$ and $\lvert \lbrace s : g_s(n) \neq g_{s+1}(n) \rbrace \rvert \leqslant n$ for all $n$. (Recall that this cardinality is known as the number of mind changes of $g$ at $n$.)

Suppose that we are given $n$ and, for $k_n = \min\lbrace i : m(i) \geqslant n \rbrace$, we are also given the exact number $p_n$ of mind changes of $g$ at $k_n$. Then we can compute $g(k_n) = A \restriction m(k_n)$, and hence also compute $A \restriction n$, since $m(k_n) \geqslant n$. In other words, we can describe $A \restriction n$ given $n$ and $p_n$, so

$$C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + 2 \log p_n + O(1).$$

By the definition of $k_n$, we have $m(k_n - 1) < n$, so by the definition of $m$, we have $k_n - 1 \leqslant f(n)$. Furthermore, $p_n \leqslant k_n$, so

$$C(A \restriction n) \leqslant \log n + 2 \log f(n) + O(1) \leqslant \log n + f(n) + O(1),$$

as desired. $\square$

Kummer's Gap Theorem should be compared with results such as Downey and Greenberg's Theorem 13.14.2 on packing dimension and the Barmpalias, Downey, and Greenberg result on cl-degrees, Theorem 9.14.4, which also concern array computability.

It is natural to ask whether there is a classification of, say, all jump classes in terms of initial segment complexity.

### 16.2 The Entropy of Computably Enumerable Sets

In this section we consider Chaitin's notion of the *entropy* of a computably enumerable set, in the spirit of the Coding Theorem 3.9.4, and its relationship with the following notion. By a **c.e. operator** we mean a function $W$ taking sets $X$ to $W_e^X$ for some $e$. We denote the value of $W$ on $X$ by $W^A$. For a c.e. operator $W$, the **enumeration probability** $P_W(A)$ of a set $A$ relative to $W$ is the measure of the class of all $X$ such that $W^X = A$. This probability depends on $W$, of course, but there are **optimal** c.e. operators $V$ such that for each c.e. operator $W$ there is an $\varepsilon > 0$ for which $P_V(A) \geqslant \varepsilon P_W(A)$ for all $A$. We fix such a $V$ and write $P(A)$ for $P_V(A)$. More generally, for a class $\mathcal{C} \subseteq 2^\omega$, we let $P(\mathcal{C}) = \mu(\lbrace X : V^X \in \mathcal{C} \rbrace)$.

If $A$ is c.e., then clearly $P(A) > 0$. Recall that Theorem 8.12.1 says that the converse is also true: if $P(A) > 0$ then $A$ is c.e. The proof of Theorem 8.12.1 is the classic use of the "majority vote" technique, which uses the Lebesgue Density Theorem to find some string $\sigma$ such that $n \in A$ iff a majority (in the sense of measure) of the strings extending $\sigma$ believe that $n \in A$. Thus a c.e. index for $A$ is determined by a nonuniform argument based on the Lebesgue Density Theorem. The question we address in this section is: What effect does the enumeration probability of a c.e. set have on the difficulty of describing a c.e. index for it?

<div class="math-callout math-callout--definition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Definition</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.1 — Chaitin)</span></p>

For a c.e. set $A$, let $H(A) = \lceil -\log P(A) \rceil$ and $I(A) = \min\lbrace K(j) : A = W_j \rbrace$.

</div>

Chaitin established some of the basic properties of these and related notions, while Solovay showed that there is indeed a deep relationship between them.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.2 — Solovay)</span></p>

$I(A) \leqslant 3H(A) + 2 \log H(A) + O(1)$.

</div>

It is not known to what extent Solovay's bound may be improved, but Vereshchagin showed that a tighter bound can be extracted from Solovay's proof when $A$ is finite.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.3 — Vereshchagin)</span></p>

If $A$ is finite then $I(A) \leqslant 2H(A) + 2 \log H(A) + O(1)$.

</div>

We will prove both of these results in this section. Our account of Solovay's proof will be along the lines of the one given by Vereshchagin. The proof relies on a combinatorial result of Martin (see Solovay), so we begin by presenting that result.

**Martin's game** is defined as follows. The game has parameters $K$ and $N$. A **configuration** of the game is an $N$-tuple $(S_0, \ldots, S_{N-1})$ of clopen subsets of $2^\omega$. The initial configuration is $(2^\omega, \ldots, 2^\omega)$. There are two players. On its turn, player I plays a clopen set $Y \subset 2^\omega$ with $\mu(Y) > \frac{1}{K+1}$. If $Y$ is disjoint from $S_0, \ldots, S_{N-1}$ then player I wins. If not, then player II's move is to pick an $i < N$ with $S_i \cap Y \neq \emptyset$ and replace $S_i$ by $Y$. Player II wins if it can prevent player I from ever winning.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.4 — Martin, see Solovay)</span></p>

In the game above, player II has a computable winning strategy if $N = \frac{K(K+1)}{2}$.

</div>

*Proof.* This proof follows Solovay. It uses a property of configurations called *property M*. This property is computably checkable, the initial configuration has it, and if a configuration has property M, then for any move by player I, player II has a move that results in a configuration that also has property M. Given such a property, the computable strategy for player II is simply to pick the least $i$ so that $S_i \cap Y \neq \emptyset$ and the resulting configuration has property M.

Suppose that $N = \frac{K(K+1)}{2}$. Let $T = \lbrace (j, i) : i \leqslant j < K \rbrace$. Then $\lvert T \rvert = N$. Given a bijection $h : T \to N$ and a configuration $(Y_0, \ldots, Y_{N-1})$, we write $Z_{j,i}$ for $Y_{h(j,i)}$. We think of this bijection as arranging the $Y_k$ into a triangular array

$$Z_{0,0}$$

$$Z_{1,0} \qquad Z_{1,1}$$

$$\vdots$$

$$Z_{K-1,0} \qquad Z_{K-1,1} \qquad \cdots \qquad Z_{K-1,K-1}.$$

We say that $(Y_0, \ldots, Y_{N-1})$ has **property M** if for some bijection $h : T \to N$, the resulting triangular array has the following property: For all $j < K$, if $i_0 \leqslant i_1 \leqslant \cdots \leqslant i_{d-1} \leqslant j$, then $\mu\!\left(\bigcup_{k < d} Z_{j, i_k}\right) > \frac{d}{K+1}$.

Clearly, the initial configuration has property M, and we can effectively check whether a given configuration has property M. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.5)</span></p>

Let $(Y_0, \ldots, Y_{N-1})$ be a configuration with property M. Let $Y$ be a clopen subset of $2^\omega$ with $\mu(Y) > \frac{1}{K+1}$. Then there is an $i < N$ such that the configuration obtained by replacing $Y_i$ by $Y$ still has property M.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $h : T \to N$ be a bijection witnessing that $(Y_0, \ldots, Y_{N-1})$ has property M, and let $Z_{j,i}$ for $(j, i) \in T$ be the corresponding triangular array. Then $\mu\!\left(\bigcup_{i < N} Z_{K-1,i}\right) > \frac{K}{K+1}$, so some $Z_{K-1,i}$ has nonempty intersection with $Y$. Let $j$ be least such that $Z_{j,i} \cap Y \neq \emptyset$ for some $i$. Permuting the $j$th row if necessary, we may assume that $Z_{j,j} \cap Y \neq \emptyset$. We prove that if we replace $Y_{h(j,j)}$ by $Y$, the resulting configuration has property M.

We build a new array by replacing $Z_{j,j}$ by $Y$ and then swapping the rest of the $j$th row with the $(j-1)$st row. That is, let $Z'_{j,j} = Y$, let $Z'_{j-1,i} = Z_{j,i}$ and $Z'_{j,i} = Z_{j-1,i}$ for $i \leqslant j - 1$, and for all other $k$ and $i$, let $Z'_{k,i} = Z_{k,i}$. We claim that this new array witnesses that $(Y_0, \ldots, Y_{h(j,j)-1}, Y, Y_{h(j,j)+1}, \ldots, Y_{N-1})$ has property M.

Since property M works on a row-by-row basis, we do not need to worry about any rows other than the $(j-1)$st and the $j$th. The $(j-1)$st row is the same as the $j$th row of the original array, so we do not need to worry about it either. So we need to show only that if $i_0 \leqslant \cdots \leqslant i_{d-1} \leqslant j$ then $\mu\!\left(\bigcup_{k < d} Z'_{j,i_k}\right) > \frac{d}{K+1}$. If $i_{d-1} < j$ then all elements involved in the union are taken from the $(j-1)$st row of the original array, so we are done. Thus, we may assume that $i_{d-1} = j$, so that $Z'_{j,i_{d-1}} = Y$. By our choice of $j$, we know that $Y \cap Z_{j-1,i} = \emptyset$ for all $i \leqslant j - 1$. Thus $\mu\!\left(\bigcup_{k < d} Z'_{j,i_k}\right) = \mu\!\left(\bigcup_{k < d-1} Z_{j-1,i_k}\right) + \mu(Y) > \frac{d-1}{K+1} + \frac{1}{K+1} = \frac{d}{K+1}$, as required. $\square$

As explained above, this lemma completes the proof of the theorem.

We are now ready to prove Theorem 16.2.2.

*Proof of Theorem 16.2.2.* We will show that, for each $k$, we can enumerate $O(2^{2k})$ many c.e. sets such that every set $A$ with $P(A) \geqslant 2^{-k}$ is among them. If this enumeration were uniform in $k$, then, taking $k = H(A)$, we could describe an index for $A$ simply by giving $k$ and the position of $A$ in our enumeration, which would require fewer than $2H(A) + 2 \log H(A)$ many bits. The enumeration will not be uniform in $k$, however, but there will be nonuniformly given strings $\rho_k \in 2^k$ such that the enumeration for $k$ can be done on input $\rho_{k+1}$. The extra bits needed to describe $\rho_{k+1}$ are what will drive our bound to $3H(A) + 2 \log H(A) + O(1)$.

For a string $\sigma$, let $F_\sigma$ be the finite set determined by $\sigma$, that is, $F_\sigma = \lbrace n < \lvert \sigma \rvert : \sigma(n) = 1 \rbrace$. Let $S_t(\sigma) = \lbrace X : V^X[t] \in \llbracket \sigma \rrbracket \rbrace$. The following lemma is immediate.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.6)</span></p>

$P(A) = \lim_n P(\llbracket A \restriction n \rrbracket)$.

</div>

We will also need the following lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.7)</span></p>

$P(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \lim_t \mu(S_t(\sigma))$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $S_t^1$ be the class of all $X$ such that $V^X[t] \supseteq F_\sigma$ and $S_t^2$ be the class of all $X \in S_t^1$ such that $V^X[t] \restriction \lvert \sigma \rvert \supsetneq F_\sigma$. Let $S^1 = \bigcup_t S_t^1$ and $S^2 = \bigcup_t S_t^2$. Then $P(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \mu(S^1 \setminus S^2) = \mu(S^1) - \mu(S^2) = \lim_t(\mu(S_t^1) - \mu(S_t^2)) = \lim_t \mu(S_t(\sigma))$. $\square$

It thus makes sense to define $P_t(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) = \mu(S_t(\sigma))$.

An **approximating sequence** for a set $A$ is a sequence $\sigma_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ such that $F_{\sigma_0} \subseteq F_{\sigma_1} \subseteq \cdots$ and $\bigcup_i F_{\sigma_i} = A$.

We say that the pair $(t, n)$ is **good** for $\sigma$ if $\lvert \sigma \rvert = n$ and $P_t(\llbracket \sigma \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-k-1}$. We say that a sequence of pairs $(t_0, n_0), (t_1, n_1), \ldots$ is **good** for $A$ if there is an approximating sequence $\tau_0, \sigma_1, \ldots$ for $A$ and $j_0 < j_1 < \cdots$ such that $(t_{j_i}, n_{j_i})$ is good for $\sigma_i$ for each $i$.

The proof now boils down to the following two lemmas.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.8)</span></p>

There are strings $\rho_k \in 2^k$ and an algorithm that, on input $\rho_{k+1}$, computes a sequence of pairs $(t_0, n_0), (t_1, n_1), \ldots$ that is good for all $A$ such that $P(A) \geqslant 2^{-k}$.

</div>

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.9)</span></p>

There is an algorithm that, when given a sequence of pairs as in Lemma 16.2.8 as an oracle, enumerates $O(2^{2k})$ many sets $C_0, \ldots, C_{N-1}$ such that every set $A$ for which the sequence is good coincides with some $C_i$.

</div>

Given these lemmas, the theorem follows easily: Let $k = H(A)$, so that $P(A) \geqslant 2^{-k}$. Let $\rho_{k+1}$ be as in Lemma 16.2.8, and let $C_0, \ldots, C_{N-1}$ be as in Lemma 16.2.9 when the oracle is the sequence computed from $\rho_{k+1}$. Then there is an $i < N$ such that $A = C_i$. Given $\rho_{k+1}$ and $i$, we can determine an index $j$ such that $A = W_j$. We can determine $k$ from $\rho_{k+1}$, so

$$I(A) \leqslant K(\rho_{k+1}) + K(i \mid k) \leqslant k + 2 \log k + 2k + O(1) = 3H(A) + 2 \log H(A) + O(1).$$

The proof of Lemma 16.2.9 uses Martin's game. Let $K = 2^{k+1}$ and $N = \frac{K(K+1)}{2}$. We are given a sequence of pairs $(t_0, n_0), (t_1, n_1), \ldots$ that is good for all $A$ such that $P(A) \geqslant 2^{-k}$. We will enumerate sets $C_0, \ldots, C_{N-1}$ using Martin's game. We begin by letting $(X_0[0], \ldots, X_{N-1}[0])$ be the initial configuration and $C_0[0], \ldots, C_{N-1}[0]$ all be empty.

At the end of stage $j$ of the algorithm, we have a configuration $(X_0[j], \ldots, X_{N-1}[j])$ and finite sets $C_0[j], \ldots, C_{N-1}[j]$ with the following properties.

(i) $C_i[j] \subseteq [0, n_j)$.

(ii) For every $\sigma$ for which $(t_j, n_j)$ is good, $C_i[j] = F_\sigma$ for some $i < N$.

(iii) $C_i[j] \subseteq V^X[t_j]$ for all $X \in X_i[j]$.

At stage $j + 1$, we proceed as follows. Let $\sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_l$ be the strings for which $(t_{j+1}, n_{j+1})$ is good. Play $Y = S_t(\sigma_0)$ for player I. Since $\mu(S_t(\sigma_0)) = P_t(\llbracket \sigma_0 \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-k-1} > \frac{1}{K+1}$, this move is legal. Let $X_i[j]$ be the set that the computable winning strategy for player II replaces by $Y$, and let $X_i[j + 1] = Y$.

Since $X_i[j] \cap Y \neq \emptyset$, there is some $X \in X_i[j]$ with $V^X[t_{j+1}] \in \llbracket \sigma_0 \rrbracket$. By condition (iii), $C_i[j] \subseteq V^X[t_j] \subseteq V^X[t_{j+1}]$, so by condition (i), $C_i[j] \subseteq V^X[t_{j+1}] \cap [0, n_{j+1}) = F_{\sigma_0}$. So we can let $C_i[j + 1] = F_{\sigma_0}$. Note that now $C_i[j + 1] \subseteq V^X[t_{j+1}]$ for all $X \in X_i[j + 1]$, and hence (iii) remains true for this $i$.

Now repeat this procedure for $\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_l$ in turn. By definition, the sets $S_t(\sigma_0), \ldots, S_t(\sigma_l)$ are pairwise disjoint, so the strategy for player II always picks a new $X_i[j]$ to replace. For all $i$ such that $X_i[j]$ is not picked, let $C_i[j + 1] = C_i[j]$ and $X_i[j + 1] = X_i[j]$. Note that (iii) is true for all such $i$, since $V^X[t_{j+1}] \supseteq V^X[t_j]$ for all $X$.

Let $C_i = \bigcup_j C_i[j]$. To see that the algorithm works, and hence complete the proof of Lemma 16.2.9, assume that there is an approximating sequence $\tau_0, \tau_1, \ldots$ for $A$ and $j_0 < j_1 < \cdots$ such that $(t_{j_l}, n_{j_l})$ is good for $\tau_l$. Then for each $l$ there is an $i < N$ such that $C_i[j_l] = F_{\tau_l}$. So for some $i < N$, there are infinitely many $l$ such that $C_i[j_l] = F_{\tau_l}$. Then $A = C_i$. $\square$

Finally, we prove Vereshchagin's Theorem 16.2.3.

*Proof of Theorem 16.2.3.* For a finite set $B$, let $S_t(B) = \lbrace X : V^X[t] = B \rbrace$ and $P_t(B) = \mu(S_t(B))$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.2.10)</span></p>

For all finite sets $B$, we have $P(B) = \lim_t P_t(B)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $S_t^1$ be the class of all $X$ such that $V^X[t] \supseteq B$ and $S_t^2$ be the class of all $X \in S_t^1$ such that $V^X[t] \neq B$. Now the proof is as in Lemma 16.2.7. $\square$

Given $k$, we will enumerate $O(2^{2k})$ many finite sets $C_0, \ldots, C_{N-1}$ so that at the end of step $t$ of the enumeration, every finite set $B$ with $P_t(B) \geqslant 2^{-k-1}$ coincides with $C_i[t]$ for some $i < N$. Suppose we can do so. If $B$ is finite and $P(B) \geqslant 2^{-k}$, then by Lemma 16.2.10, $P_t(B) \geqslant 2^{-k-1}$ for almost all $t$, so for some $i$ and infinitely many $t$, we have $C_i[t] = B$. Then $B = C_i$. So to describe $B$ we need to provide only $k$ and $i$, which requires fewer than $2H(A) + 2 \log H(A)$ many bits.

We perform our enumeration using Martin's game with $K = 2^k$ so that at the end of each step $t$, the following two conditions hold, where $(X_0[t], \ldots, X_{N-1}[t])$ is the configuration of the game at the end of step $t$.

(i) If $P_t(B) \geqslant 2^{-k-1}$ then $C_i[t] = B$ for some $i < N$.

(ii) For all $i < N$, we have $C_i[t] \subseteq V^X[t]$ for all $X \in X_i[t]$.

As before, we begin with $C_i[0] = \emptyset$ and $X_i[0] = 2^\omega$. At step $t + 1$ we let $B_0, \ldots, B_s$ be all sets $B$ such that $P_{t+1}(B) \geqslant 2^{-k-1}$ and play $Y = S_{t+1}(B_0)$ for player I in Martin's game. Let $X_i[t]$ be the set that the computable winning strategy for player II replaces by $Y$. Let $X_i[t + 1] = Y$. Since $X_i[t] \cap Y \neq \emptyset$, there is an $X \in X_i[t]$ with $B_0 = V^X[t + 1]$. By condition (ii), we have $C_i[t] \subseteq V^X[t] \subseteq V^X[t + 1] = B_0$, so we can let $C_i[t + 1] = B_0$. As before, we repeat this procedure for each $B_1, \ldots, B_s$ in turn, and at the end, for any $i$ such that $X_i[t]$ is not replaced, let $X_i[t + 1] = X_i[t]$ and $C_i[t + 1] = C_i[t]$. This algorithm clearly has the necessary properties. $\square$

### 16.3 The Collection of Nonrandom Strings

#### 16.3.1 The Plain and Conditional Cases

Probably the most natural computably enumerable set associated with randomness is

$$\overline{R}_C = \lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace,$$

the collection of strings that are nonrandom relative to plain complexity. This set has been extensively studied by Kummer, Muchnik, Positselsky, Allender, and others. We have already seen in Chapter 3 that $\overline{R}_C$ is a simple c.e. set, and hence is not m-complete. On the other hand, it is easily seen to be Turing complete. (This is Exercise 2.63 in Li and Vitányi.)

In this section we will prove the remarkable fact, due to Kummer, that $\overline{R}_C$ is in fact truth table complete, which is by no means obvious. In fact, the reduction built in Kummer's proof is a *conjunctive* tt-reduction. (A **conjunctive tt-reduction** from $A$ to $B$ is a computable function $f$ such that for all $n$, we have $n \in A$ iff $D_{f(n)} \in B$.)

The problem with proving this result, roughly speaking, is as follows. Suppose that we are attempting to define a reduction from the halting problem $\emptyset'$ to $\overline{R}_C$ to show that $\emptyset' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} \overline{R}_C$. The most natural idea is to have a collection of strings $F_n$ for each $n$ and attempt to ensure that $F_n \subset \overline{R}_C$ iff $n \in \emptyset'$. If we choose $F_n$ appropriately, if $n$ enters $\emptyset'$ then we can lower the complexity of all elements of $F_n$ and force them into $\overline{R}_C$. However, it can also happen that these complexities decrease without us doing anything, forcing all of $F_n$ into $\overline{R}_C$ even though $n \notin \emptyset'$. This possibility is particularly hard to overcome because we need to specify the sets $F_n$ ahead of time, since we are trying to build a tt-reduction. Furthermore, we cannot make the $F_n$ too large. As we will see, Kummer used nonuniformity and a clever counting method to overcome this difficulty. His idea is the basis for other arguments such as Muchnik's Theorem 16.3.1 below.

We consider the **overgraph**

$$O_Q = \lbrace (\sigma, \tau, n) : Q(\sigma \mid \tau) < n \rbrace$$

for $Q$ equal to $K$, $Km$, or $C$ (or other similar complexity measures).

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.1 — Muchnik)</span></p>

For any choice of $Q$, the overgraph $O_Q$ is m-complete.

</div>

*Proof.* We fix $Q$ as $C$, but the proof works with easy modifications for other complexities. Let $O = O_C$, and let $O_s = \lbrace (\sigma, \tau, n) : C_s(\sigma \mid \tau) < n \rbrace$.

In the course of our construction, we will build a partial computable function $f$. By the recursion theorem, we assume we know a constant $d$ such that if $f(\tau) = \sigma$ then $C(\sigma \mid \tau) < d$.

We will build partial functions $g_i : \mathbb{N} \to 2^{<\omega} \times 2^{<\omega} \times \mathbb{N}$ for $i < 2^d$. For the largest $i$ such that $g_i(n)$ is defined for infinitely many $n$, we will be able to argue that, for all sufficiently large $n$, we can find a stage $s$ such that either $n \in \emptyset'_s$, or $n \in \emptyset'$ iff $g_i(n) \in O$.

Let $\sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_{2^d-1}$ be pairwise distinct strings. The values of $g_i$ will be of the form $(\sigma_i, \tau, d)$. The basic idea is that we will be able to ensure for $i_0$ that, from some stage on, and for almost all $n$, the following occurs. First, we can find a stage $s$ such that either $n \in \emptyset'_s$ or $g_{i_0}(n)$ is permanently defined at stage $s$ as $(\sigma_{i_0}, \tau, d)$ for some $\tau$. Next, if the latter case occurs and $n$ enters $\emptyset'$ later on, then $f(\tau) = \sigma_{i_0}$, so $g_{i_0}(n) \in O$ by the choice of $d$. To ensure this last condition, we design the construction so that, if $g_{i_0}(n)$ enters $O$ without $n$ entering $\emptyset'$, then we define $g_j(m) = (\sigma_j, \tau, d)$ for some $j > i_0$, which cannot happen more than finitely often by the choice of $i_0$.

**Construction.** Initially, all strings are active. At stage $s$, proceed as follows. For each active $\tau$ with $\lvert \tau \rvert \leqslant s$, find the least $i < 2^d$ such that $(\sigma_i, \tau, d) \notin O_s$, which must exist since $\lbrace \sigma : (\sigma, \tau, d) \in O \rbrace < 2^d$. For the least $n \notin \emptyset'_s$ such that $g_i$ is not defined, let $g_i(n) = (\sigma_i, \tau, d)$. Undefine $g_j(m)$ for all $j < i$ and all $m$ such that $g_j(m) = (\sigma_j, \tau, d)$. We say that $i$ *acts* at stage $s$.

If $n$ enters $\emptyset'$ at stage $s$, then for the largest $i$ such that $g_i(n)$ is defined (if any), let $\tau$ be such that $g_i(n) = (\sigma_i, \tau, d)$, declare $\tau$ to be inactive, and let $f(\tau) = \sigma_i$.

**End of Construction.**

Let $i < 2^d$ be largest such that $i$ acts at infinitely many stages. Let $s_0$ be a stage after which no $j > i$ ever acts. This describes an m-reduction from $\emptyset'$ to $O$.

Let $n$ be large enough so that $g_i(n)$ is not defined at the end of stage $s_0$ and $g_j(n)$ is never defined for $j > i$. Search for a stage $s > s_0$ such that either $n \in \emptyset'_s$ or $g_i(n)$ becomes defined at stage $s$. It is clear from the construction that one of these events must occur. If it is the former, then we know $n \in \emptyset'$. Otherwise, we claim that $g_i(n) \in O$ iff $n \in \emptyset'$.

If $n \in \emptyset'$, then $n$ enters $\emptyset'$ at some stage $t > s$. At that stage, $i$ is largest such that $g_i(n)$ is defined, so $f(\tau) = \sigma_i$, and hence $C(\sigma_i \mid \tau) < d$, which means that $g_i(n) = (\sigma_i, \tau, d) \in O$.

Conversely, suppose that $g_i(n) = (\sigma_i, \tau, d) \in O$. Assume for a contradiction that $n \notin \emptyset'$. At stage $s$, we cancel all $g_j(m)$ such that $j < i$ and $g_j(m) = (\sigma_j, \tau, d)$, and no $g_j(m)$ ever gets defined as $(\sigma_j, \tau, d)$ after that stage, so $\tau$ is permanently active. Let $t > s_0$ be such that $(\sigma_i, \tau, d) \in O_t$. We must have $(\sigma_j, \tau, d) \in O$ for $j < i$, since otherwise $g_i(n)$ would not have been defined as $(\sigma_i, \tau, d)$. But by the choice of $s_0$ and $t$, there cannot be a $j > i$ such that $(\sigma_j, \tau, d) \notin O_t$, since then the least such $j$ would be active at $t$. So in fact $(\sigma_j, \tau, d) \in O$ for all $j < 2^d$, which is not possible. $\square$

We now prove Kummer's Theorem. The construction has a finite set of parameters (the $i < 2^{d+1}$ below). We will build our tt-reduction based on the largest parameter selected infinitely often during the construction. The necessity of knowing this parameter to define the reduction is the source of the nonuniformity in the proof.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.2 — Kummer)</span></p>

$\overline{R}_C$ is truth table complete.

</div>

*Proof.* We will define a plain machine $\Phi$ with constant $d$ given by the recursion theorem. To do so, we will build uniformly c.e. sets of strings $E_0, E_1, \ldots$ such that for each $n$ we have $E_n \subseteq 2^n$ and $\lvert E_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{n-d-1}$. Then for each $n$, if $\sigma \in E_n$ we can ensure that $\Phi(\tau) = \sigma$ for some $\tau \in 2^{n-d-1}$. By doing so, we will ensure that for each $n$ and $\sigma \in E_n$, we have $C(\sigma) \leqslant C_\Phi(\sigma) + d < n$, or in other words, that $\bigcup_n E_n \in \overline{R}_C$.

To obtain our tt-reduction, for each $i < 2^{d-1}$, we will define a possibly infinite sequence of finite sets of strings $S_{i,0}, S_{i,1}, \ldots$ At the end of the proof we will argue that there is a largest $i$ such that the corresponding sequence is infinite, and that for almost all $x$, we have $x \in \emptyset'$ iff $S_{i_0,x} \subseteq \overline{R}_C$. Hence, we will have a tt-reduction (indeed, a conjunctive tt-reduction) from $\emptyset'$ to $\overline{R}_C$.

The definition of the $S_{i,x}$ has stronger priority than that of the $S_{j,y}$ for $j < i$. If $S_{j,y}$ is defined then all of its elements will have the same length, denoted by $m_{j,y}$. At a later stage, some stronger priority $S_{i,x}$ may assert control and occupy this length, in which case we will undefine $m_{j,y}$. The basic idea here is that we work with the largest $i$ such that all $S_{i,x}$ are defined. We set up our conditions so that if $S_{i,x} \subseteq \overline{R}_C$, then this increase in the number of nonrandom strings of a particular length allows some $j > i$ to define a new $S_{j,y}$. So if this situation happens infinitely often, then we contradict the maximality of $i$.

We now proceed with the formal construction. Let $O_{n,s} = \lbrace \sigma \in 2^n : C_s(\sigma) < n \rbrace$.

**Construction.** Fix a parameter $d$.

*Stage 0.* Let $\ell(i) = 0$ for all $i$.

*Stage $s + 1$.* Check whether there is an $i < 2^{d+1}$ and an $n \leqslant s$ such that

(i) $n$ is unused and $n \geqslant d + 1$,

(ii) $n \neq m_{j,x}$ for all $j \geqslant i$ and all $x$, and

(iii) $i 2^{n-d-1} \leqslant \lvert O_{n,s} \rvert$.

If so, then choose the largest such $i$, and for this $i$ the least such $n$, and then perform the following actions.

1. Declare $m_{j,x}$ to be undefined for all $j$ and $x$ such that $m_{j,x} = n$.
2. Let $S_{i,\ell(i)}$ be the set of the least $k$ elements in $2^n \setminus O_{n,s}$, where $k = \min(2^n - \lvert O_{n,s} \rvert, 2^{n-d-1})$.
3. Let $m_{i,\ell(i)} = n$.
4. Increment $\ell(i)$ by 1.

For all $j$ and $x$ such that $x \in \emptyset'_{s+1}$ and $m_{j,x}$ is defined and unused, let $E_{m_{j,x}} = S_{j,x}$ and declare that $m_{j,x}$ is *used*.

**End of Construction.**

Clearly, the $E_n$ are uniformly c.e. Furthermore, at any stage $s + 1$, if $m_{j,x}$ is defined then $\lvert S_{j,x} \rvert \leqslant 2^{m_{j,x}-d-1}$, so $\lvert E_n \rvert \leqslant 2^{n-d-1}$ for all $n$. Thus we can define a plain machine $\Phi$ such that, for each $n$ and $\sigma \in E_n$ then $\Phi(\tau) = \sigma$ for some $\tau \in 2^{n-d-1}$. By the recursion theorem, we may assume that $d$ is a coding constant of $\Phi$, so that for each $n$ and $\sigma \in E_n$, $C(\sigma) \leqslant C_\Phi(\sigma) + d < n$. In other words, $\bigcup_n E_n \in \overline{R}_C$.

Conditions (i)–(iii) in the construction always hold for $i = 0$ and $n = s$ (once $s \geqslant d + 1$). So at every large enough stage some $i$ is chosen. Since there are only $2^{d+1}$ many relevant $i$, there is a largest $i$ such that $i$ is chosen infinitely often. Let $i_0$ be this $i$. Let $s_0$ be such that no $j > i$ is ever chosen at a stage after $s_0$. Notice that $S_{i_0, x}$ is defined for all $x$, and if $x \neq y$ then the strings in $S_{i_0, x}$ and $S_{i_0, y}$ are of different lengths. Furthermore, once $m_{i_0, x}$ is defined at a stage after $s_0$, which must happen, then $m_{i_0, x}$ is never later undefined. So there is an $x_0$ such that for all $x \geqslant x_0$, once $m_{i_0, x}$ is defined, it is never later undefined.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.3)</span></p>

Let $x \geqslant x_0$. If $x \in \emptyset'$, then $E_{m_{i_0, x}} = S_{i_0, x}$, while if $x \notin \emptyset'$, then $E_{m_{i_0, x}} = \emptyset$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $s$ be the stage at which $m_{i_0, x}$ first becomes defined as a value $n$. Then $n$ is unused at the beginning of that stage, whence $E_n = \emptyset$ at the beginning of stage $s$. Since $m_{i_0, x} = n$ at all later stages, $E_n$ remains empty at all later stages unless $x$ enters $\emptyset'$, in which case it is defined to be $S_{i_0, x}$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.4)</span></p>

For every $x \geqslant x_0$, we have $x \in \emptyset'$ iff $S_{i_0, x} \subseteq \overline{R}_C$.

</div>

*Proof.* As argued above, $\bigcup_n E_n \in \overline{R}_C$, so by the lemma, if $x \geqslant x_0$ and $x \in \emptyset'$, then $S_{i_0, x} \subseteq \overline{R}_C$.

For the other direction, assume for a contradiction that there is an $x \geqslant x_0$ such that $x \notin \emptyset'$ but $S_{i_0, x} \subseteq \overline{R}_C$. Let $s + 1$ be the stage at which $m_{i_0, x}$ becomes defined. By construction, we have $\lvert O_{n,s} \rvert \geqslant i_0 2^{n-d-1}$ and $S_{i_0, x} \cap O_{n,s} = \emptyset$. By hypothesis, there is a stage $t > \max(s_0, s)$ such that $S_{i_0, x} \subseteq O_{n,t}$. Now, $\lvert S_{i_0, x} \rvert$ is either $2^n - \lvert O_{n,s} \rvert$ or $2^{n-d-1}$. It cannot be the former, since then we would have $O_{n,t} = 2^n$, which is clearly impossible. So $\lvert S_{i_0, x} \rvert = 2^{n-d-1}$, and hence $\lvert O_{n,t} \rvert \geqslant (i_0 + 1) 2^{n-d-1}$. Since $\lvert O_{n,t} \rvert < 2^n$, we have $i_0 + 1 < 2^{d+1}$. Thus conditions (i)–(iii) in the construction are satisfied at stage $t + 1$ for $i = i_0 + 1$ and $n = t + 1$, and hence the $i$ chosen at stage $t + 1$ is greater than $i_0$, contradicting the fact that $t > s_0$. $\square$

It follows immediately from the corollary that $\emptyset' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} \overline{R}_C$. $\square$

With a relatively straightforward modification of the previous proof (using suitable sets of strings $L_\sigma$ in place of $2^n$), Kummer proved the following result, which applies to sets like $\lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma) \leqslant \log \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.5 — Kummer)</span></p>

Let $f$ be computable, with $f(\sigma) \leqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert$ and $\lim_{\lvert \sigma \rvert} f(\sigma) = \infty$. Then the set $\lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma) < f(\sigma) \rbrace$ is conjunctive tt-complete.

</div>

There has been some exciting work on efficient reductions to $\overline{R}_C$, such as that of Allender, Buhrman, Koucký, van Melkebeek, and Ronneburger, among others. These authors observed that the Kummer reduction, while having computable use, has exponential growth in the number of queries used. They asked what sets can be, say, polynomial time reduced to $\overline{R}_C$, as well as other time- and space-bounded variants of this question. Amazingly, these questions seem to have bearing on basic separation problems between complexity classes. For instance, $\text{PSPACE} \subseteq \text{P}^{\overline{R}_C}$. The methods used in this work are highly nontrivial, and beyond the scope of this book.

#### 16.3.2 The Prefix-Free Case

As in defining a notion of randomness for strings using prefix-free complexity (see Section 3.8), we have two reasonable analogs of $\overline{R}_C$ in the prefix-free case. Let

$$\overline{R}_K^{\text{tr}} = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert + K(\lvert \sigma \rvert) \rbrace$$

(the set of non-strongly $K$-random strings) and

$$\overline{R}_K = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert \rbrace$$

(the set of non-weakly $K$-random strings).

Clearly $\overline{R}_K$ is c.e., but it had been a long-standing open question, going back to Solovay, whether $\overline{R}_K^{\text{tr}}$ is c.e. This question was answered by Miller.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.6 — Miller)</span></p>

Fix $c \geqslant 0$ and let $B = \lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert + K(\lvert \sigma \rvert) - c \rbrace$. Let $A \supseteq B$ be such that $\lvert A \cap 2^n \rvert \leqslant 2^{n-1}$ for all $n$. Then $A$ is not c.e.

</div>

Before proving this theorem, we point out two consequences. The first one, which follows immediately from the theorem, answers Solovay's question.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.7)</span></p>

For all $c \geqslant 0$, the set $\lbrace \sigma : K(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert + K(\lvert \sigma \rvert) - c \rbrace$ is not c.e.

</div>

Miller pointed out that Theorem 16.3.6 also gives a weak form of Solovay's theorem that there are strings that are $C$-random but not strongly $K$-random. While the following result is weaker than Solovay's, its proof is much easier.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.8)</span></p>

Let $c \geqslant 0$ and $d > 0$. There is a string that is $C$-random with constant $d$ but not strongly $K$-random with constant $c$.

</div>

*Proof.* If this is not the case, then the set $B$ in Theorem 16.3.6 is contained in $A = \lbrace \sigma : C(\sigma) < \lvert \sigma \rvert - d \rbrace$. But $A$ is c.e. and $\lvert A \cap 2^n \rvert < 2^{n-d}$ for all $n$, contradicting the theorem. $\square$

*Proof of Theorem 16.3.6.* Assume for a contradiction that $A$ is c.e. We build a KC set $L$ to define a prefix-free machine $M$, with coding constant $k$ given by the recursion theorem. For any $s$ and $n$ such that $K_s(n) < K_{s-1}(n)$, find $2^{n-k-c-2}$ many strings of length $n$ that are not in $A_s$, which exist by hypothesis, and for each such string $\tau$, enumerate $(n + K_s(n) - k - c - 1, \tau)$ into $L$. It is easy to check that $L$ is indeed a KC set.

If $K_{s-1}(n) \neq K(n)$, then our definition of $M$ ensures that at least $2^{n-k-c-2}$ many strings of length $n$ that are not in $A_s$ are each compressed by at least $c + 1$ many bits. These strings must then eventually be added to $B$, and hence to $A$, so $\lvert A \cap 2^n \rvert \geqslant \lvert A_s \cap 2^n \rvert + 2^{n-k-c-2}$.

Let $b$ be greatest such that $\lvert A \cap 2^n \rvert \geqslant b 2^{n-k-c-2}$ for infinitely many $n$. Define a partial computable function $f$ as follows. If $\lvert A_s \cap 2^n \rvert \geqslant b 2^{n-k-c-2}$, then let $f(n) = K_s(n)$. By the argument above and the choice of $b$, for almost all $n$, if $f(n)$ is defined then $f(n) = K(n)$. Furthermore, the choice of $b$ guarantees that $f$ has infinite domain. Thus, by changing finitely many values of $f$, we have a partial computable function with infinite domain that is equal to $K(n)$ wherever it is defined, contradicting Proposition 3.5.5. $\square$

Let us now turn to $\overline{R}_K$. This set is c.e., and it is also clearly Turing complete. But is it tt-complete? An. A. Muchnik showed that the answer may be negative, depending on the choice of universal machine. (We will see later that the answer may also be positive for other choices of universal machine.) Thus we will consider the set $\overline{R}_K^U$ for a particular universal prefix-free machine $U$, which is $\overline{R}_K$ when $K$ is defined using $U$. Muchnik also considered the overgraph of $K$, that is, the set

$$O_K^U = \lbrace (\sigma, n) : K(\sigma) < n \rbrace,$$

where $K$ is defined using $U$. Of course, Kummer's Theorem 16.3.2 implies that the overgraph of $C$, that is, the set $O_C = \lbrace (\sigma, n) : C(\sigma) < n \rbrace$, is tt-complete independent of the choice of universal machine, but Muchnik showed that the situation is different for $K$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.9 — Muchnik)</span></p>

There exist universal prefix-free machines $V$ and $U$ such that

(i) $O_K^V$ is tt-complete but

(ii) $O_K^U$ (and hence $\overline{R}_K^U$) is not tt-complete.

</div>

*Proof.* (i) We define $V$ using our fixed universal prefix-free machine $\mathcal{U}$. Let $g(k) = k$ if $k$ is even and $g(k) = k + 1$ is $k$ is odd. Let

$$L = \lbrace (g(k) + 3, \sigma) : K_\mathcal{U}(\sigma) \leqslant k \rbrace \cup \lbrace (g(k) + 2, 0^n) : K_\mathcal{U}(0^n) \leqslant k \wedge n \in \emptyset' \rbrace.$$

Then $L$ is clearly a KC set. Let $V$ be the prefix-free machine corresponding to $L$. Then $V$ is universal, and $n \in \emptyset'$ iff $K_V(0^n)$ is even. If $c$ is such that $K_V(0^n) \leqslant \log n + c$ for all $n$, then $K_V(0^n)$ can be determined by querying $O_K^V$ on $(k, 0^n)$ for $k \leqslant \log n + c$, and hence $\emptyset' \leqslant_{\text{tt}} O_K^V$.

(ii) This argument is much more difficult. We will follow Muchnik's proof, which uses strategies for finite games. It may well be possible to remove this feature of the proof, but it is an interesting technique in its own right.

The kind of game we will consider here is determined by

1. a finite set of positions (vertices),
2. a set of allowable moves for each of the two players,
3. two complementary subsets of the positions, $S_1$ and $S_2$, and
4. an initial position $d_0$.

The set of moves will allow players to stay in the same position, but will not allow a position to be returned to once it is left. The game begins at position $d_0$, and the two players play in turns, making allowable moves from one position to another. Since positions cannot be returned to once left, the game must stabilize at some stage, and if that stable position is in $S_i$, then player $i$ wins.

Clearly this game is determined, and we can effectively find a winning strategy for one of the players.

We will construct a $\Sigma_1^0$ function $F$ and define $H(\sigma) = \min\lbrace K(\sigma) + 2, F(\sigma) \rbrace$. We will show that $\sum_\sigma 2^{-H(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$, and hence we can use the KC Theorem to build a corresponding prefix-free machine $U$, which will clearly be universal. We will also construct a computably enumerable set $W$ such that $W \nleqslant_{\text{tt}} O_K^U$. Let $\Gamma_n$ denote the $n$th partial truth table reduction. We will construct the diagonalizing set $W$ as a set of triples $\langle n, i, j \rangle$, using those with first coordinate $n$ to diagonalize against $\Gamma_n$.

In the construction, we will have numbers $i_n$ and $j_n$ and a finite game $G_n$ associated with $n$. Each time $n$ is considered, we can either let one of the players play the current game $G_n$, or we can change both $G_n$ and the numbers $i_n$ and $j_n$. The positions of a game $G_n$ will be determined by a finite set of strings $A_n$, a set of functions $A_n \to \mathbb{N}$, and $q$ and $p$ are pairs of rational numbers. The idea is that each position, and hence $h$, can be used to provide a guess as to the values of $O_K^U$ queried by $\Gamma_n$ on input $\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle$, by assuming that $H$ equals $h$ on $A_n$. Player 1's moves will determine the value of $F$ (or, more precisely, the approximation $F_s$ to $F$) on $A_n$, and hence will have to be carefully bounded to ensure that $\sum_\sigma 2^{-H(\sigma)} \leqslant 1$. Player 2's moves will be determined by the value of $K_s$ on strings in $A_n$. The rational $q$ will code how much has been spent on changing $F$, and the rational $p$ will code how much of the measure of the standard universal prefix-free machine has been spent by player 2 in changing $K_s$ at strings in $A_n$. We place a bound on $p$ and $q$. If stronger priority strategies (those working for $\Gamma_m$ with $m < n$) do not intervene, then either player 1 will be able to complete its strategy, or the approximation to the values of $K$ on strings in $A_n$ will change so much that player 2 will want to violate the rules of the game (by moving to an $(h, q, p)$ where $p$ is above our bound). In that case we cancel the game and start a new one. This situation will occur only finitely often, since each time it does, it causes a large change in the values of $K$ on string in $A_n$. $\square$

#### 16.3.3 The Overgraphs of Universal Monotone Machines

In this section, we present results of Day on the overgraph relation for monotone and process complexity. The first result answers a question posed by Muchnik and Positselsky. For a monotone machine $M$, let $Km_M = \min\lbrace \lvert \tau \rvert : \sigma \preccurlyeq M(\tau) \downarrow \rbrace$ and define the overgraph $O_{Km}^M$ to be $\lbrace (\sigma, k) : Km_M(\sigma) < k \rbrace$. Recall from Theorem 3.16.2 that for a universal monotone machine $U$, the function $M_U$ defined by $M_U(\sigma) = \mu(\llbracket \lbrace \tau : \exists \sigma' \succcurlyeq \sigma \, ((\tau, \sigma') \in U) \rbrace \rrbracket)$ is an optimal c.e. continuous semimeasure.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.14 — Day)</span></p>

For any universal monotone machine $U$, the overgraph $O_{Km}^U$ is tt-complete.

</div>

*Proof.* We will construct a monotone machine $M$ whose index $d$ in $U$ is known by the recursion theorem. To give this proof the widest possible applicability, we will make $M$ a strict process machine (which recall means that if $\tau \in \text{dom}(M)$ and $\tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$, then $\tau' \in \text{dom}(M)$ and $M(\tau') \preccurlyeq M(\tau)$). In addition to $M$, we need to build a truth table reduction $\Gamma$. For this proof, we will omit the $Km$ subscript and write $O^M$ for $O_{Km}^M$. This notation allows us to use the subscript position as follows: For a monotone machine $M$, let $O_k^M = \lbrace \sigma : (\sigma, k) \in O^M \rbrace$.

Our truth table reduction will work as follows. For each $x$, a set of strings $S_x$ will be specified. The reduction will determine which strings are in $O_{d+x}^U$ and make a decision as to whether or not $x \in \emptyset'$ based on this information.

The simplest thing to do would be to try to encode the fact that $x$ enters $\emptyset'$ by adding all of $S_x$ to $O_{d+x}^U$. However, we run against the same problem as in proving Kummer's Theorem 16.3.2, that the opponent could wait until the truth table used by $\Gamma(x)$ is defined, and then adopt a winning strategy to ensure either that $\Gamma^{O^U}(x) = 0$ or that $\Gamma^{O^U}(x) = 1$. By adding $x$ to $\emptyset'$ in the first case and keeping it out in the second case, the opponent could ensure $\Gamma^{O^U}(x) \neq \emptyset'(x)$.

We overcome this problem as in the proof of Theorem 16.3.2 by making the reduction nonuniform. In this case, we will allow it to be wrong on some initial segment of $\emptyset'$. The reduction will be constructed in such a way that the cost to the opponent of making the reduction incorrect for any $x$ is so significant that the reduction can be incorrect only a finite number of times.

The strict process machine $M$ we use for adding pairs to the overgraph will be constructed as follows. For each $\tau \in 2^x$ we will choose some $\sigma_\tau$, so that $\lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in 2^x \rbrace$ is a prefix-free set. The pairs $(\tau, \sigma_\tau)$ will be candidates for addition to our machine. We will make sure that if $\tau' \prec \tau$, then either $\sigma_{\tau'} \preccurlyeq \sigma_\tau$ or $(\tau', \sigma_{\tau'})$ is never added to $M$. If we decide to add $(\tau, \sigma_\tau)$ to $M$, then $Km(\sigma_\tau) \leqslant \lvert \tau \rvert + d = x + d$, and hence $\sigma_\tau \in O_{d+x}^U$.

Now, the opponent has the ability to add $\sigma_\tau$ to $O_{d+x}^U$ as well. If the opponent does this, then it must have increased the measure on $\sigma_\tau$, i.e., set $M_U(\llbracket \sigma_x \rrbracket)$ to at least $2^{-d-x}$. Provided we have not described any extension of $\sigma_\tau$ with $M$, we can now bypass this measure spent by the universal machine. Bypassing the measure allows us to ensure the reduction works on almost all inputs. We wait until an appropriate stage $s$ when we have some lower bound on the measure that we can bypass. When we define $S_s$, we ensure that $\sigma_v$ does not extend $\sigma_\tau$ for all $v \in 2^x$. Instead we will let such $\sigma_v$ extend some $\rho_\tau$ incompatible with $\sigma_\tau$.

However, the opponent still has one last trick up its sleeve. Before it adds some string $\sigma_\tau$ to $O_{d+\lvert \tau \rvert}^U$, it can try to force us to enumerate some $(\tau', \sigma_{\tau'})$ into $M$ with $\sigma_\tau \preccurlyeq \sigma_{\tau'}$. Doing so would prevent us from bypassing the measure the opponent spends on $\sigma_\tau$, because if $\tau' \prec v$, then we must have $\sigma_\tau \prec \sigma_{\tau'} \prec \sigma_v$. The following reduction $\Gamma$ is designed to minimize the problem this situation causes.

The reduction $\Gamma$ will be defined as follows. First $\Gamma(0) = 0$. If $x \neq 0$, then at stage $x$ in the construction, a set $S_x$ will be defined. This set will have $2^x$ many elements and will be indexed by $2^x$ so that for each $\tau \in 2^x$ there is a unique string $\sigma_\tau \in S_x$. Let $\overline{\tau}$ be the string of length $\lvert \tau \rvert$ defined by $\overline{\tau}(k) = 1 - \tau(k)$. To determine whether $x \in \emptyset'$, the reduction $\Gamma$ runs the construction until $S_x$ is defined and then determines which elements of the set $S_x$ are in $O_{d+x}^U$. If $S_x \subseteq O_{d+x}^U$, then $\Gamma(x) = 0$. Otherwise $\Gamma$ finds the leftmost $\tau \in 2^x$ such that either

1. exactly one of $\sigma_\tau$ and $\sigma_{\overline{\tau}}$ are in $O_{d+x}^U$, in which case $\Gamma(x) = 1$; or

2. neither $\sigma_\tau$ nor $\sigma_{\overline{\tau}}$ are in $O_{d+x}^U$, in which case $\Gamma(x) = 0$.

This reduction can be thought of as checking pairs of strings in a certain order. For example, consider $S_3$. First the reduction checks whether $\sigma_{000}$ and $\sigma_{111}$ are in $O_{d+3}^U$. If they are not both in then the reduction can give an answer immediately. If they are both in, then the reduction checks whether $\sigma_{001}$ and $\sigma_{110}$ are in, and so on. This process can be described simply by looking at the indices of the $\sigma$'s involved, e.g., first 000 and 111, then 001 and 110, then 010 and 101, and finally 011 and 100.

Note that for a given $x$, the value of the reduction $\Gamma(x)$ can be changed by adding a single element of $S_x$ to $O_{d+x}^U$ (provided $S_x \nsubseteq O_{d+x}^U$). If at some stage $s$ of the construction $\Gamma^{O^{U_s}}(x) = 0$, then there are two possible choices of string for changing the reduction to 1, while if $\Gamma^{O^{U_s}}(x) = 1$ then there is only one possible string that can be enumerated into the overgraph to change $\Gamma(x)$ to 0. Also note that if $(\tau, \sigma_\tau)$ is enumerated into $M$, then the only reason to enumerate $(\overline{\tau}, \sigma_{\overline{\tau}})$ into $M$ is to keep $M$ a strict process machine.

Now, if we get to a stage $s$ where for some $x$, we have $S_x \subseteq O_{d+x}^{U_s}$ and $x \in \emptyset'_s$, then we no longer have any ability to change the reduction. At this point we give up making the reduction work on $x$, and in fact we go further and give up trying to make the reduction work on any value below $s + 2$. We will have a marker that points to a value after which the reduction works. We move the marker to point to $s + 1$ and call $s + 1$ a **marker move stage**. The reason that the marker cannot be moved infinitely often is that now when we define $S_{s+1}$, we can do it in such a way as to avoid extending some strings $\sigma_\tau$ that have been added to $O_{d+\lvert \tau \rvert}^U$.

In looking for strings that have measure we can bypass, we do not consider just those strings in $S_x$. We consider all strings $\sigma_\tau$, where $\tau$ can have any length, such that for any $\rho$ that occurs no later than $\tau$ in the search order of $\Gamma$, we have $\sigma_\rho \in O_{d+\lvert \rho \rvert}^{U_s}$. Given this set of strings $S$, we let $T$ be the set of indices describing the strings in $S$. We use $T$ instead of $S$ because it is easier to deal with. As $S_x \subseteq O_{d+\lvert x \rvert}^{U_s}$, it follows that $\mu(\llbracket T \rrbracket) = 1$. Let $\widehat{T}$ be the prefix-free set formed by taking those strings in $T$ that are maximal with respect to the $\preccurlyeq$ ordering. The way we choose our $\sigma_\tau$ during the construction will allow us to find a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket \widehat{T} \rrbracket)$.

Let $B$ be the set of strings in $\widehat{T}$ that index strings enumerated into the overgraph by the opponent and whose corresponding measures we can bypass. We will be able to show that we can find a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket B \rrbracket)$ because nearly half the strings in $\widehat{T}$ must be in $B$. The reason for this fact is a little complicated and will be detailed in the proof.

For the verification of the proof, it is useful to formalize the order that the reduction uses the strings in $S_x$, which is done by defining a relation on $2^k$ as follows: $\tau_1 \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau_2$ if $\min(\tau_1, \overline{\tau_1}) \leqslant_{\text{L}} \min(\tau_2, \overline{\tau_2})$ where the minimum is with respect to the lexicographic ordering. While $\preccurlyeq_\Gamma$ is reflexive and transitive, it is not an ordering, as antisymmetry fails. However, if $\tau \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \rho$ and $\rho \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau$, then either $\rho = \tau$ or $\rho = \overline{\tau}$. The relation $\preccurlyeq_\Gamma$ is total in the sense that for all $\tau, \rho \in 2^k$, either $\tau \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \rho$ or $\rho \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau$. Note that this fact implies that if $\tau \not\preccurlyeq_\Gamma \rho$ then $\rho \prec_\Gamma \tau$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.15)</span></p>

Let $\tau_1, \tau_2, v_1, v_2$ with $\lvert \tau_1 \rvert = \lvert \tau_2 \rvert < \lvert v_1 \rvert = \lvert v_2 \rvert$ be such that $\tau_1 \prec v_1$ and $\tau_2 \prec v_2$. Then $\tau_1 \prec_\Gamma \tau_2$ implies $v_1 \prec_\Gamma v_2$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\tau_1 \prec_\Gamma \tau_2$, then $\tau_1 \neq \overline{\tau_1}$ so $\tau_1 \neq \overline{\tau_1}$. Assume that $\tau_1 <_{\text{L}} \overline{\tau_1}$, the other case being symmetric. If $\tau_1 \prec_\Gamma \tau_2$, then $\tau_1 <_{\text{L}} \tau_2$ and $\tau_1 <_{\text{L}} \overline{\tau_2}$. Now, as $\tau_1 \prec v_1$ and $\tau_2 \prec v_2$, it follows that $v_1 <_{\text{L}} v_2$ and $v_1 <_{\text{L}} \overline{v_2}$. Hence, $\min(v_1, \overline{v_1}) <_{\text{L}} \min(v_2, \overline{v_2})$, and so $v_1 \prec_\Gamma v_2$. $\square$

In the construction and verification that follow, we will assume that we have sped up our stages sufficiently so that when we enumerate descriptions into our machine $M$, corresponding descriptions appear immediately in $U$. This assumption simplifies notation, as it implies that $O_k^{M_s} \subseteq O_{d+k}^{U_s}$ and $\lbrace (\sigma, n) : (\sigma, n) \in O^{M_s} \rbrace \subseteq O^{U_s}$.

**Construction.**

*Stage 0.* Let $\sigma_\lambda = 0$ and $S_0 = \lbrace \sigma_\lambda \rbrace$. The set $S_0$ is used only to start the construction and will not be used by $\Gamma$. Let $M_0 = \lbrace (\lambda, \sigma_\lambda) \rbrace$. Let $R_0 = \lbrace 0 \rbrace$. The set $R_s$ is used to determine the position of the marker at stage $s$.

*Stage $s + 1$.* We assume $M_s$ is a strict process machine. Let the marker $c_s$ be the largest element in $R_s$. First we need to define $S_{s+1}$.

If $s + 1 \neq c_s$, then $s + 1$ is not a marker move stage. In this case, for all $\tau \in 2^s$, choose four extensions $\sigma_{\tau 0}, \sigma_{\tau 1}, \rho_{\tau 0}, \rho_{\tau 1}$ of $\sigma_\tau$ that are pairwise incompatible and not in $O_{d+s+1}^{U_s}$, which is possible because $O_{d+s+1}^{U_s}$ is finite. Let $S_{s+1} = \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in 2^{s+1} \rbrace$.

If $c_s = s + 1$ then $s + 1$ is a marker move stage. The construction of $S_{s+1}$ will be done in such a way as to avoid extending some strings $\sigma_\tau$ that have been added to $O_{d+\lvert \tau \rvert}^U$. The procedure for finding the $\sigma_\tau$ to avoid is as follows.

For all $k$ with $1 \leqslant k \leqslant s$, let

$$T_k^{s+1} = \lbrace \tau \in 2^k : \forall \tau' \in 2^k \, (\tau' \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau \Rightarrow \sigma_{\tau'} \in O_{d+k}^{U_s}) \rbrace.$$

The set $T_k^{s+1}$ is defined this way because this is the order in which the reduction $\Gamma$ examines the strings in $S_k$. Let $T^{s+1} = \bigcup_{1 \leqslant k \leqslant s} T_k^{s+1}$.

We want to work with a prefix-free set, so let $\widehat{T}^{s+1} = \lbrace \tau \in T^{s+1} : \forall \tau' \succ \tau \, (\tau' \notin T^{s+1}) \rbrace$. Note that this set consists of the maximal elements of $T^{s+1}$, while prefix-free sets are usually constructed using minimal elements.

To gather together those strings whose descriptions we can bypass, let $B^{s+1} = \lbrace \tau \in \widehat{T}^{s+1} : \forall \tau' \succcurlyeq \tau \, (\tau' \notin \text{dom}(M_{s-1})) \rbrace$. For all $v \in B^{s+1}$, let $\lbrace v_0, \ldots, v_n \rbrace$ be the set of extensions of $v$ of length $s + 1$. Choose $\sigma_{v_0}, \rho_{v_0}, \ldots, \sigma_{v_n}, \rho_{v_n}$ that are pairwise incompatible, not in $O_{d+s+1}^{U_s}$, and extend $\rho_v$. For all $\tau \in 2^{s+1}$ that do not extend some $v \in B^{s+1}$, choose a $\sigma_\tau$ and a $\rho_\tau$ that are incompatible, not in $O_{d+s+1}^{U_s}$, and extend $\sigma_{\tau'}$, where $\tau' = \tau \restriction (\lvert \tau \rvert - 1)$. Again let $S_{s+1} = \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in 2^{s+1} \rbrace$.

Second, we need to determine which descriptions to commit to our machine $M$. Let $X_{s+1} = \lbrace x : c_s < x < s \wedge \Gamma^{O^{U_s}}(x) \neq \emptyset'_s(x) \rbrace$. If $X_{s+1} = \emptyset$, then let $M_{s+1} = M_s$ and $R_{s+1} = R_s$ and proceed to the next stage. Otherwise, if for any $x \in X_s$ we have $S_x \subseteq O_{d+x}^{U_s}$, then let $M_{s+1} = M_s$ and $R_{s+1} = R_s \cup \lbrace s + 2 \rbrace$, which will cause the marker to be moved at the next stage. In this case, proceed to the next stage.

Otherwise, let $x_s$ be the least element of $X_s$, and choose $\tau \in 2^{x_s}$ such that $\sigma_\tau \notin O_{d+x_s}^{U_s}$ and $\sigma_{\tau'} \in O_{d+x_s}^{U_s}$ for all $\tau' \prec_\Gamma \tau$. We are going to add $(\tau, \sigma_\tau)$ to $M_{s+1}$. However, we want to make $M$ a strict process machine, so we need to ensure that $\text{dom}(M_{s+1})$ is closed under substrings.

Let $v$ be the longest initial segment of $\tau$ such that $v \in \text{dom}(M_s)$. Consider any $\tau' \prec \tau$. If $\tau' \preccurlyeq v$, then $\tau' \in \text{dom}(M_s)$ by our assumption that $M_s$ is a strict process machine. Now suppose that $v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$. If $\lvert \tau' \rvert \leqslant c_s$, then we want to have $M_{s+1}(\tau') = M_s(v)$. Otherwise, we want to have

$$M_{s+1} = M_s \cup \lbrace (\tau', M_s(v)) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert \leqslant c_s \rbrace$$

$$\cup \lbrace (\tau', \sigma_{\tau'}) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert > c_s \rbrace.$$

Finally, let $R_{s+1} = R_s$.

**End of Construction.**

First we will show that $M$ is a strict process machine. To do so, we need the following lemma.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.16)</span></p>

If $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$ and $\sigma_{\tau_1} \not\prec \sigma_{\tau_2}$, then $M(\tau_1) \neq \sigma_{\tau_1}$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$ and $\sigma_{\tau_1} \not\prec \sigma_{\tau_2}$, then there must be some marker move stage $s$ with $\lvert \tau_1 \rvert < s \leqslant \lvert \tau_2 \rvert$ and $\rho_{\tau_0} \prec \sigma_{\tau_2}$ for some $\tau_0 \preccurlyeq \tau_1$. Thus, $\tau_1 \notin \text{dom}(M_s)$, by the definition of $B^s$. However, then for all stages $t > s$, we have $M_t(\tau_1) \neq \sigma_{\tau_1}$, because once the marker has moved, if $\tau_1$ is added to the domain of $M_t$, then $M_t(\tau_1) = \sigma_v$ for some $v \prec \tau_1$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.17)</span></p>

$M$ is a strict process machine.

</div>

*Proof.* We proceed by induction on the stages of the construction. Clearly $M_0$ is a strict process machine. If $M_s$ is a strict process machine then the construction ensures that $M_{s+1}$ is at least a function whose domain is closed under initial segments, because if $M_{s+1} \neq M_s$, then $M_{s+1}$ is formed by taking some $\tau \notin \text{dom}(M_s)$ and finding the longest $v \prec \tau$ such that $v \in \text{dom}(M_s)$. The strings that we add to the domain of $M_{s+1}$ are exactly those strings $\tau'$ such that $v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$.

We also need to show that $M_{s+1}$ is a process. In the construction, $M_{s+1}$ is defined to be $M_s \cup \lbrace (\tau', M_s(v)) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert \leqslant c_s \rbrace \cup \lbrace (\tau', \sigma_{\tau'}) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert > c_s \rbrace$.

First we show that $\widehat{M}_{s+1} = M_s \cup \lbrace (\tau', M_s(v)) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert \leqslant c_s \rbrace$ is a process. Take any $\tau_1, \tau_2 \in \text{dom}(\widehat{M}_{s+1})$ with $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$. If $\tau_2 \in M_s$ then $\tau_1 \in M_s$, so $\widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_1) = M_s(\tau_1) \preccurlyeq M_s(\tau_2) = \widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_2)$. If $\tau_2 \notin M_s$, then $\tau_1 \preccurlyeq v$, so $\widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_1) \preccurlyeq \widehat{M}_{s+1}(v) = \widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_2)$. Finally, if neither $\tau_1$ nor $\tau_2$ are in $M_s$ then $\widehat{M}_{s+1}(v) = \widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_1) = \widehat{M}_{s+1}(\tau_2)$.

The set $\lbrace (\tau', \sigma_{\tau'}) : v \prec \tau' \preccurlyeq \tau \wedge \lvert \tau' \rvert > c_s \rbrace$ is also a process: For any $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$ in this set, $\sigma_{\tau_1} \prec \sigma_{\tau_2}$ because the marker is not moved between the definitions of $\sigma_{\tau_1}$ and $\sigma_{\tau_2}$.

To show that the union of these two processes is a process, consider $M_{s+1}(v)$. By construction it must be that $M_{s+1}(v) = M_s(v) \preccurlyeq \sigma_\tau$, and so $M_{s+1}(v) \preccurlyeq M_{s+1}(\tau)$.

Now for any $\tau_1 \prec \tau_2$ with $\tau_1$ in the domain of the first process and $\tau_2$ in the domain of the second process, as $M_{s+1}(v) \preccurlyeq M_{s+1}(v)$ and $M_{s+1}(\tau_2) \preccurlyeq M_{s+1}(\tau)$, it follows that $M_{s+1}(\tau_1) \preccurlyeq M_{s+1}(\tau_2)$. Therefore $M_{s+1}$ is a strict process machine. It follows that $M = \bigcup_s M_s$ is also a strict process machine. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.18)</span></p>

If there are only a finite number of marker move stages, then $\Gamma^{O^U}(x) = \emptyset'(x)$ for almost all $x$.

</div>

*Proof.* Let $s_0$ be the last marker move stage and assume for a contradiction that there is a least $x_0 > s_0$ such that $\Gamma^{O^U}(x_0) \neq \emptyset'(x_0)$. Let $s_1 > x_0$ be a stage such that $\emptyset'_{s_1} \restriction (x_0 + 1) = \emptyset' \restriction (x_0 + 1)$ and $S_x \cap O_{d+x}^{U_{s_1}} = S_x \cap O_{d+x}^{U_{s_1}}$ for all $x \leqslant x_0$. The latter condition implies that $\Gamma^{O^{U_{s_1}}}(x) = \Gamma^{O^U}(x)$ for all $x \leqslant x_0$.

If $x_0 \in X_{s_1}$, then it must be the least element of $X_{s_1}$. Because there are no more marker move stages, it must be that $S_{x_0} \nsubseteq O_{d+x_0}^{U_{s_1}}$. But there is a $(\tau, \sigma_\tau)$ with $\tau \in 2^{x_0}$ and $\sigma_\tau \notin O_{d+x_0}^{U_{s_1}}$ added to $M_{s_1+1}$. This enumeration adds $\sigma_\tau$ to $O_{d+x_0}^U$, which is not possible because $S_{x_0} \cap O_{d+x_0}^{U_{s_1}} = S_{x_0} \cap O_{d+x_0}^U$. Hence $x_0 \notin X_{s_1}$, and so $\Gamma^{O^U}(x_0) = \Gamma^{O^{U_{s_1}}}(x_0) = \emptyset'_{s_1}(x_0) = \emptyset'(x_0)$, a contradiction. $\square$

Now we need to show that there are only a finite number of marker move stages. We will be able to do so because each time the marker is moved, a portion of the measure that the universal machine has spent is bypassed by the construction, and can no longer be used to affect $\Gamma$. By showing that there is a lower bound on the amount of measure that is bypassed each time the marker is moved, we will show that the marker can be moved only a finite number of times, since the domain of the universal machine eventually runs out of measure. For any $x$ there is a direct relation between the indices of the strings in $S_x$ and the measure required to be allocated to these strings to add them to $O_{d+x}^U$. Hence, to determine a lower bound on the amount of measure bypassed, it is useful to find a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket B^s \rrbracket)$. The first step toward achieving this bound will be to find a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket \widehat{T}^s \rrbracket)$.

For the rest of the verification, fix $s$ to be a particular marker move stage. As $s$ is fixed, $T_k$ will be used for $T_k^s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.19)</span></p>

Let $\tau, \rho \in 2^k$ with $1 \leqslant k < s$. If $\tau \in T_k$ and $\rho \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau$, then $\rho \in T_k$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\rho \notin T_k$ then for some $v \in 2^k$ such that $v \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \rho$, we have $\sigma_v \notin O_{d+k}^{U_s}$. However, by the transitivity of the $\preccurlyeq_\Gamma$ relation, $v \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau$, and so $\tau \notin T_k$. $\square$

Note that this lemma implies that if $\tau \in T_k$, then $\overline{\tau} \in T_k$ as well.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.20)</span></p>

If $1 \leqslant k < j < s$ then either $\llbracket T_k \rrbracket \subset \llbracket T_j \rrbracket$ or $\llbracket T_j \rrbracket \subset \llbracket T_k \rrbracket$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\llbracket T_j \rrbracket \not\subset \llbracket T_k \rrbracket$, then there is some $v \in T_j$ such that if $\tau = v \restriction k$, then $\tau' \prec \tau$ (because the relation $\prec_\Gamma$ is total and if $\tau \preccurlyeq_\Gamma \tau'$ then by definition $\tau' \notin T_k$). Now let $v'$ be any extension of $\tau'$ such that $\lvert v' \rvert = j$. By Lemma 16.3.15, $v' \prec_\Gamma v$, and hence $v' \in T_j$ by Lemma 16.3.19. Thus $\llbracket T_k \rrbracket \subset \llbracket T_j \rrbracket$. $\square$

Let $x$ be greatest such that $S_x \subseteq O_{d+x}^{U_s}$. Since $s$ is a marker move stage, $x$ is greater than any previous marker move stage, so this assumption will be true after at most 3 marker move stages. We may assume that $x \geqslant 4$ because $x$ is greater than any previous marker move stage.

As $j(0) = x$ and $S_x \subseteq O_{d+x}^{U_s}$, we have $\mu(\llbracket T_{j(0)} \rrbracket) = 1$. Additionally, $x$ is greater than any previous marker move stage. The previous lemma shows there are $j(0) < j(1) < \cdots < j(n)$ with $j(n) = s - 1$, such that $\llbracket T_{j(0)} \rrbracket \supseteq \cdots \supseteq \llbracket T_{j(n)} \rrbracket$ and $\llbracket T_l \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket$ whenever $j(i) < l \leqslant j(i + 1)$.

For $i < n$, let $\widehat{T}_j(i) = \lbrace \tau \in T_{j(i)} : \forall \tau' \in T_{j(i+1)} \, (\tau \not\prec \tau') \rbrace$. Let $\widehat{T}_{j(n)} = T_{j(n)}$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.21)</span></p>

For all $i < n$, we have $\mu(\llbracket \widehat{T}_{j(i)} \rrbracket) \geqslant \mu(\llbracket T_{j(i)} \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket) - 2^{-j(i)+1}$.

</div>

*Proof.* We have $\llbracket T_{j(i)} \rrbracket \supseteq \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket$, so let $\tau$ be an element of $T_{j(i)}$ for which there is some $v \prec \tau$ such that if $\tau = v \restriction j(i + 1)$ and $v \notin T_{j(i+1)}$, but for all $v'$ with $\lvert v' \rvert = j(i + 1)$, we have $v' \in T_{j(i+1)}$. Now take any $\tau' \in T_{j(i)}$. If $\tau' \prec_\Gamma \tau$ then $\llbracket \tau' \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket$. If $\tau \prec_\Gamma \tau'$ then $\llbracket \tau' \rrbracket$ must be one of $\tau$ or $\overline{\tau}$. The lemma follows, as $\mu(\llbracket \lbrace \tau, \overline{\tau} \rbrace \rrbracket) = 2^{-j(i)+1}$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.22)</span></p>

$\widehat{T} = \bigcup_{i \leqslant n} \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\tau \in \widehat{T}$, then by definition, $\tau \not\prec \tau'$ for all $\tau' \in T$. Hence $\tau \in T_{j(i)}$ for some $i$ and $\tau \notin T_{j(i+1)}$ for all $\tau' \in T_{j(i+1)}$ for all $\tau \prec \tau'$. Thus $\tau \in \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$. Thus $\widehat{T} \subseteq \bigcup_{i \leqslant n} \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$.

For the other direction, first note that $\widehat{T}_{j(n)} = T_{s-1} \subseteq \widehat{T}$, because any maximal-length element under $\prec$ must be a maximal element under $\preccurlyeq$. If $\tau \in \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$ for some $i < n$, then $\tau \not\prec \tau'$ for all $\tau' \in T_{j(i+1)}$. Now, $\llbracket T_l \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket$ for all $l > j(i)$, so $\tau \not\prec \tau'$ for all $\tau' \in T_l$, and hence $\tau \in \widehat{T}$. Thus $\bigcup_{i \leqslant n} \widehat{T}_{j(i)} \subseteq \widehat{T}$. $\square$

Using these lemmas:

$$\mu(\llbracket \widehat{T} \rrbracket) = \sum_{i \leqslant n} \mu(\llbracket \widehat{T}_{j(i)} \rrbracket) \geqslant \sum_{i < n} \left( \mu(\llbracket T_{j(i)} \rrbracket \setminus \llbracket T_{j(i+1)} \rrbracket) - 2 \cdot 2^{-j(i)+1} \right) + \mu(\llbracket T_{j(n)} \rrbracket) = \mu(\llbracket T_{j(0)} \rrbracket) - \sum_{i < n} 2^{-j(i)+1} > \frac{3}{4}.$$

Now we have achieved the first step discussed above by finding a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket \widehat{T} \rrbracket)$. The next step is to find a lower bound on $\mu(\llbracket B^s \rrbracket)$. Recall that $B^s$ was defined in the construction to be $\lbrace \tau \in \widehat{T} : \forall \tau' \succcurlyeq \tau \, (\tau' \notin \text{dom}(M_{s-1})) \rbrace$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.23)</span></p>

If $\tau \in \widehat{T}$ then $\overline{\tau} \in \widehat{T}$.

</div>

*Proof.* If $\tau \in \widehat{T}$, then $\tau \in \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$ for some $i$. So $\tau \in T_{j(i)}$, and thus $\overline{\tau} \in T_{j(i)}$. If $\overline{\tau} \prec \overline{v}$ and $\lvert \overline{v} \rvert = j(i + 1)$, then $\tau \prec v$, and hence $v \notin T_{j(i+1)}$ (as $\tau \in \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$). Thus $\overline{v} \notin T_{j(i+1)}$, and so $\overline{\tau} \in \widehat{T}_{j(i)}$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.24)</span></p>
