If $\tau, \overline{\tau} \notin B^s$, then there exists $v \in \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$ such that either $\tau \prec v$ or $\overline{\tau} \prec v$.

</div>

*Proof.* This lemma follows from the fact that we add descriptions to $M$ for only two reasons. The first is to change the reduction and the second is to ensure that the domain is closed under substrings. Assume that there is no $v \in \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$ such that either $\tau \prec v$ or $\overline{\tau} \prec v$. In this case there is no need to add $\tau$ or $\overline{\tau}$ to the domain of $M_{s-1}$ to close it under substrings. So if $\tau \notin B^s$, then it must be that $\tau \in \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$. Furthermore, we must have added $\tau$ to the domain of $M_{s-1}$ to change the reduction $\Gamma$. In this case there is no reason why we should add $\overline{\tau}$ to change the reduction as well. Hence $\overline{\tau} \notin \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$, so $\overline{\tau} \in B^s$. $\square$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.25)</span></p>

If $\tau_1, \tau_2 \in \widehat{T}$ are such that $\lvert \tau_1 \rvert = \lvert \tau_2 \rvert$ and $\tau_1 \prec_\Gamma \tau_2$, then at least one of $\tau_2$ and $\overline{\tau_2}$ is in $B^s$.

</div>

*Proof.* First we know by Lemma 16.3.23 that $\overline{\tau_1}, \overline{\tau_2} \in \widehat{T}$. Assume for a contradiction that $\tau_2, \overline{\tau_2} \notin B^s$.

By the previous lemma, if $\tau_2, \overline{\tau_2} \notin B^s$, then there must be some $v_2 \in \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$ such that either $\tau_2 \prec v_2$ or $\overline{\tau_2} \prec v_2$. We will assume without loss of generality that $\tau_2 \prec v_2$.

Let $k = \lvert v_2 \rvert$. Note that $k < s$. As $v_2 \in \text{dom}(M_{s-1})$, by the way the construction chooses pairs to add to $M$, hence $v \in T_k$. Take any $v_1$ extending $\tau_1$ with $\lvert v_1 \rvert = k$. As $\tau_1 \prec_\Gamma \tau_2$, by Lemma 16.3.15, $v_1 \prec_\Gamma v_2$, and thus $v_1 \in T_k$ by Lemma 16.3.19. Thus $\llbracket \tau_1 \rrbracket \subseteq \llbracket T_k \rrbracket$ and so $\tau_1 \notin \widehat{T}$, which contradicts our initial assumption. $\square$

We can use this last lemma to put a lower bound on the measure of $B^s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.26)</span></p>

If $s$ is a marker move stage then $\mu(\llbracket B^s \rrbracket) \geqslant \frac{1}{4}$.

</div>

*Proof.* The previous lemma tells us that for any given length $l$, there is at most a single $\tau \in \widehat{T}$ of length $l$ such that neither $\tau$ nor $\overline{\tau}$ is in $B^s$. For any string $v$ of length $l$ such that $v \neq \tau$ and $v \neq \overline{\tau}$, either $v$ or $\overline{v}$ is in $B^s$. Thus

$$\mu(\llbracket B^s \rrbracket) \geqslant \frac{1}{2} \left( \mu(\llbracket \widehat{T}^s \rrbracket) - \sum_{i \leqslant m} 2 \cdot 2^{-j(i)} \right) > \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} \right) = \frac{1}{4}.$$

$\square$

Now for all $\tau \in B^s$, we have $\sigma_\tau \in O_{d+\lvert \tau \rvert}^U$ and so $Km(\sigma_\tau) \leqslant d + \lvert \tau \rvert$. Additionally, by construction, as $B^s$ is a prefix-free set, so is $\lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in B^s \rbrace$. Hence it follows that

$$M_U(\llbracket \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in B^s \rbrace \rrbracket) = \sum_{\tau \in B^s} M_U(\llbracket \sigma_\tau \rrbracket) \geqslant \sum_{\tau \in B} 2^{-d-\lvert \tau \rvert} = 2^{-d} \sum_{\tau \in B} \mu(\llbracket \tau \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-d} \mu(\llbracket B^s \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-d-2}.$$

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.27)</span></p>

If $s_1$ and $s_2$ are both marker move stages and $s_1 \neq s_2$, then the sets $\llbracket \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in B^{s_1} \rbrace \rrbracket$ and $\llbracket \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in B^{s_2} \rbrace \rrbracket$ are disjoint.

</div>

*Proof.* Take any $\tau \in B^{s_1}$ and $v \in B^{s_2}$. By construction, $\lvert \tau \rvert < s_1$, and the length of $v$ is larger than any previous marker move stage, so in particular $\lvert v \rvert > s_1 > \lvert \tau \rvert$. Now, if $\tau \not\prec v$, then the construction ensures that $\sigma_v$ and $\sigma_\tau$ are incompatible. If $\tau \prec v$, then again by construction $\rho_\tau \prec \sigma_v$ and hence $\sigma_v$ and $\sigma_\tau$ are incompatible. $\square$

We can now finish the proof of the theorem. By Lemma 16.3.17, $M$ is a monotone machine. By Lemma 16.3.18, if there are a finite number of marker moves then $\Gamma^{O^U}(x) = \emptyset'(x)$ for all but finitely many $x$. But there can be only a finite number of marker move stages because if $R$ is the set of all marker move stages, then by the previous lemma

$$M_U(\llbracket \lambda \rrbracket) \geqslant \sum_{s \in R} M_U(\llbracket \lbrace \sigma_\tau : \tau \in B^s \rbrace \rrbracket) \geqslant \lvert R \rvert 2^{-d-2}.$$

Hence $\lvert R \rvert \leqslant 2^{d+2}$, and in particular $R$ is finite. $\square$

The proof above still works if $O_{Km}^U$ is replaced by $O_{KM}^U = \lbrace (\sigma, k) : -\log(M_U(\sigma)) < k \rbrace$: During the verification of the construction, it is only the measure that the universal machine places on elements of $S_x$ that is considered, and not the length of their shortest descriptions. Additionally, if the construction enumerates some pair $(\sigma, n)$ into $M$, this enumeration adds $(\sigma, n + d)$ to $O_{KM}^U$ as well as $O_{Km}^U$, because $O_{Km}^U \subseteq O_{KM}^U$. Thus we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.28 — Day)</span></p>

For any universal monotone machine $U$ the overgraph $O_{KM}^U$ is truth table complete.

</div>

Also, a universal process machine is a monotone machine. Hence the limitations of a universal monotone machine exploited in the proof also apply to a universal process machine. Furthermore, the machine $M$ constructed in the proof is a strict process machine, so we have the following.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--proposition" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Corollary</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.29 — Day)</span></p>

For any universal process machine $U$, or any universal strict process machine $V$, the overgraphs $O_{Km_D}^U$ and $O_{Km_S}^U$ are truth table complete.

</div>

An interesting point about the above construction is that $\emptyset'$ can determine the correct tt-reduction, because $R$ is finite and enumerated during the construction, and hence $\emptyset'$ can determine its size. The construction used in the proof of Kummer's Theorem 16.3.2 is different. It uses a finite set of sequences $S_1, \ldots, S_n$, and the key to unraveling the construction is determining the maximum $i$ such that $S_i$ is infinite, which cannot be done using a $\emptyset'$ oracle.

#### 16.3.4 The Strict Process Complexity Case

This section presents Day's proof that there are universal strict process machines whose set of nonrandom strings is not tt-complete. It is open whether this result holds for the case where "strict" is removed. In this section, we write $\overline{R}^M$ for $\overline{R}_{Km_S}^M$.

First we show that it is possible to construct a universal strict process machine whose set of nonrandom strings is closed under extensions.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.30 — Day)</span></p>

There exists a universal strict process machine $V$ such that $\overline{R}^V$ is closed under extensions; i.e., if $\sigma \in \overline{R}^V$ and $\sigma' \succ \sigma$, then $\sigma' \in \overline{R}^V$.

</div>

*Proof.* We build $V$ from a standard universal strict process machine $U$. Let $V(\lambda) = \lambda$ and let $V(0\tau) = U(\tau)$ for all $\tau$. This definition ensures the universality of $V$. We use strings in the domain of $V$ starting with 1 to get the desired closure property. We can effectively enumerate the set $S$ of strings $\tau$ such that $U(\tau) \downarrow = \sigma$ for some $\sigma$ such that $\lvert \tau \rvert \leqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert - 2$. If $\tau \in S$, we know that $U(\rho) \downarrow$ for all $\rho \prec \tau$, so we can also effectively enumerate the prefix-free set $T$ of strings $\tau \in S$ such that $\rho \notin S$ for all $\rho \prec \tau$. For each $\tau \in T$, let $V(1\tau\nu) = U(\tau)\nu$ for all $\nu$ and let $V(1\rho) = \lambda$ for all $\rho \prec \tau$.

This definition clearly makes $V$ a universal strict process machine. We now verify that $\overline{R}^V$ is closed under extensions. Let $\sigma \in \overline{R}^V$, and let $\nu$ witness that fact. That is, $V(\nu) = \sigma$ and $\lvert \nu \rvert < \lvert \sigma \rvert$. If $\nu$ is of the form $0\tau$, then $U(\tau) = \sigma$ and $\lvert \tau \rvert \leqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert - 2$, so $\tau \in S$. Let $\tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$ be in $T$ and let $\sigma' = U(\tau')$. Note that $\sigma' \preccurlyeq \sigma$. If $\nu$ is of the form $1\tau$, then, since $\sigma \neq \lambda$, there must be a $\tau' \in T$ such that $\tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$. Let $\sigma' = V(1\tau')$, and again note that $\sigma' \preccurlyeq \sigma$.

In either case, for every $\rho$, we have $V(1\tau'\rho) = \sigma'\rho$, and $\lvert 1\tau'\rho \rvert < \lvert \sigma'\rho \rvert$, so $\sigma'\rho \in \overline{R}^V$. In particular, every extension of $\sigma$ is in $\overline{R}^V$. $\square$

The argument used in the previous proof does not generalize to process machines. Day gave the following example. Let $U$ be a universal process machine. Suppose that we see that $U(00) = 0000$ and $U(10) = 0001$, and so far $U(\lambda)$ has not converged. If we try to follow the above construction, we set $V(100) = 0000$ and $V(110) = 0001$. If now $U(\lambda)$ converges to 00, then we would like to set $V(1) = 00$, and somehow use extensions of 1 to make all extensions of 00 nonrandom. However, consider 001. It is not possible to set $V(10) = 001$ or $V(11) = 001$ and keep $V$ a process machine.

The definition of $V$ in the proof of Theorem 16.3.30 can be carried out in a stage-by-stage manner. That is, whenever $U_s(\tau) \downarrow = \sigma$ and $\lvert \tau \rvert \leqslant \lvert \sigma \rvert - 2$, we can determine the unique $\tau' \preccurlyeq \tau$ such that $\tau' \in T$, and immediately define $V(1\tau'\nu) = U(\tau)\nu$ for all $\nu$ and $V(1\rho) = \lambda$ for all $\rho \prec \tau'$. This action ensures that $\overline{R}^{V_s}$ is closed under extensions for all $s$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Theorem</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.31 — Day)</span></p>

There exists a universal strict process machine $V$ such that $\overline{R}^V$ is not tt-complete.

</div>

*Proof.* To prove this theorem we build a universal strict process machine $V$ and a c.e. set $A \nleqslant_{\text{tt}} \overline{R}^V$. As discussed above, we assume that the arbitrator acts behind the scenes and that $\overline{R}^{V_s}$ is closed under extensions for all $s$. Let $\Gamma_0, \Gamma_1, \ldots$ be an enumeration of all truth table reductions. Let $D_s = \lbrace \tau : U_s(\tau) \downarrow \wedge \lvert \tau \rvert < \lvert U_s(\tau) \rvert - 3 \rbrace$. The construction will ensure that if $\tau \in D_s$, then $U_s(\tau) \in \overline{R}^V$. We will use $D_s$ to determine how much the opponent spends in the games.

We will show that if the opponent plays a move in a game between stages $s_0$ and $s_1$, then we can determine a lower bound for $\mu(\llbracket D_{s_1} \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket D_{s_0} \rrbracket)$.

We want to satisfy the following requirements for all $n$.

$$R_n : \exists i, j \left( A(\langle n, i, j \rangle) \neq \Gamma_n^{\overline{R}^V}(\langle n, i, j \rangle) \right).$$

The triples $\langle n, i, j \rangle$ will be used as follows. The number $n$ represents the reduction to be diagonalized against. The number $i$ is incremented every time the requirement is injured by a stronger priority requirement. It also provides an upper bound on the measure that can be used by the players in the game. The number $j$ provides us with a series of games for each diagonalization. It will be incremented if our opponent ever "breaks the rules" of the game by using too much measure.

**Construction.**

*Stage 0.* Let $V(\lambda) = V(0) = V(00) = \lambda$. Let $A_0 = \emptyset$.

*Stage $2s + 1$.* For all $n < s$, if $R_n$ does not have a witness, assign $\langle n, 0, 0 \rangle$ to $R_n$. For $n < s$, let $\langle n_p, i_p, j_p \rangle$ be the current witness for $R_n$. If $R_n$ does not have a game assigned, run $\Gamma_n(\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle)$ for $s$ many steps to see whether it returns a truth table. If it does, let $X_n = \lbrace \sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_k \rbrace$ be the set of strings used as variables by this truth table. For the purpose of this game, we will assume that stronger priority requirements have stopped acting, i.e., that the associated games are finished. So let $Y_n = \lbrace \sigma \in X_n : \forall \tau \in \bigcup_{i < n} X_i \, (\sigma \not\preccurlyeq \tau \vee \tau \in \overline{R}^{V_{2s}}) \rbrace$. Notice that $\sigma \in X_n \setminus Y_n$ iff adding $\sigma$ to $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+1}}$ would change some variable used by a higher priority game (as $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+1}}$ is closed under extensions).

The game $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$ is defined as follows. We will assume that the strings in $X_n \setminus Y_n$ do not change. The vertices in the game correspond to possible truth assignments to the variables in $Y_n$. The vertices are labeled with the value of the corresponding line in the truth table (assuming those variables in $X_n \setminus Y_n$ retain their current values). An edge exists from vertex $v_1$ to vertex $v_2$ if it possible to go from the row associated with $v_1$ to the row associated with $v_2$ by changing some of the truth table variables from 0 to 1. If going from vertex $v_1$ to vertex $v_2$ requires changing exactly the variables in $\Sigma \subseteq Y_n$, then the cost associated with the edge is $\mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$. The amount of measure each player has to expend on the game is $2^{-n-i_n-6}$. The game $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$, though defined, is said to be uninitialized.

We allow the opponent to move by letting $V(000\tau) = \sigma$ for all $\tau$ and $\sigma$ such that $U_s(\tau) = \sigma$.

*Stage $2s + 2$.* We determine whether there is any game that the champion needs to attend to. We find all games assigned to requirements that are uninitialized or where the opponent has made a move. The opponent is considered to have made a move if some new strings used by the truth table reduction have been enumerated into $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+1}} \setminus \overline{R}^{V_{2s}}$. If such games exist, let $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$ be the strongest priority game (i.e., the game with the smallest $n$) that needs attention. First we reset all weaker priority games: For all $p$ such that $n < p \leqslant s$, let $\langle n_p, i_p, j_p \rangle$ be the current witness assigned to $R_p$. Remove the witness and the associated game and let $\langle n_p, i_p + 1, 0 \rangle$ be the new witness.

If $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$ is uninitialized, then we set the start position for the game to be the vertex that corresponds to assigning $\sigma_i$ a truth value of 1 iff $\sigma_i \in \overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$. The champion decides whether to take a winning strategy to ensure that $\Gamma_n(\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle) = 0$ or one to ensure that $\Gamma_n(\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle) = 1$. Of course, one of these must exist. In the first case we add $\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle$ to $A_s$; in the second case we leave it out. Let $\Sigma$ be the set of strings that the champion needs to enumerate into $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$ for the first step of this strategy.

If $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$ is a game in which the opponent has made a move, then let $2s_0$ be the stage at which this game was initialized. If $\mu(\llbracket D_s \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket D_{s_0} \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^{-n-i_n-6}$, then the opponent has exceeded the allocated measure for the game $G_{\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle}$. In this case, remove $\langle n, i_n, j_n \rangle$ as a witness for $R_n$ and also remove the game. Let $\langle n, i_n, j_n + 1 \rangle$ be the new witness. Let $\Sigma = \emptyset$. If the opponent has not exceeded the allocated measure, then let $\Sigma$ be the set of strings that the champion needs to enumerate into $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$ for the next move in the predetermined winning strategy.

Now we need to add $\Sigma$ to $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$ in order to make the champion's next move. We know that the arbitrator will ensure that $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$ is closed under extensions. So if we take $\widehat{\Sigma} = \lbrace \sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_k \rbrace$ to be a prefix-free set formed by taking the $\preccurlyeq$-minimal elements of $\Sigma$, then the champion needs only to enumerate $\widehat{\Sigma}$ into $\overline{R}^{V_{2s+2}}$. We will use the KC Theorem to request a string $\tau_i$ of length $\lvert \sigma_i \rvert - 3$ for each $i \leqslant k$. For each such $i$, let $V(01\tau_i) = \sigma_i$ and $V(01\tau) = \lambda$ for all $\tau \prec \tau_i$.

Note that the champion decreases the measure available for future requests by $2^3 \mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$. However, by scaling, we can regard the champion as having $\frac{1}{8}$ much measure to spend, and this move as costing it $\mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$.

**End of Construction.**

The first step in verifying this construction is to show that if the opponent makes a move then it must pay the cost of that move.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.32)</span></p>

If the opponent enumerates a set of strings $\Sigma$ into $\overline{R}^{V_{2s_1}} \setminus \overline{R}^{V_{2s_0}}$, then $\mu(\llbracket D_{s_1} \rrbracket) - \mu(\llbracket D_{s_0} \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^4 \mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$.

</div>

*Proof.* Form a prefix-free set $\widehat{\Sigma} = \lbrace \sigma_0, \ldots, \sigma_k \rbrace$ from the set of minimal elements of $\Sigma$ under the $\preccurlyeq$ relation. For all $i \leqslant k$, there exists some $\tau_i$ with $U_{s_1}(\tau_i) \preccurlyeq \sigma_i$ such that $\lvert \tau_i \rvert \leqslant \lvert U_{s_1}(\tau_i) \rvert - 4$ and $\tau_i \notin \text{dom}(U_{s_0})$. Let $\widehat{C}$ be a minimal subset of $C$ such that $\llbracket \widehat{C} \rrbracket \supseteq \llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket$. Then $\mu(\llbracket \widehat{C} \rrbracket) \geqslant \mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$. For all $v \in \widehat{C}$, choose an $i$ such that $U(\tau_i) = v$ and let $I$ be the set of all such $i$. The set $\lbrace \tau_i : i \in I \rbrace$ is prefix-free, because its image under $U$ is prefix-free and $U$ is a strict process machine. It follows that

$$\mu(\llbracket \lbrace \tau_i : i \in I \rbrace \rrbracket) = \sum_{i \in I} 2^{-\lvert \tau_i \rvert} \geqslant \sum_{i \in I} 2^{4 - \lvert U_{s_1}(\tau_i) \rvert} = 2^4 \mu(\llbracket \widehat{C} \rrbracket) \geqslant 2^4 \mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket).$$

Finally, take any $i \leqslant k$. If $\tau_i \prec \tau$, then $\tau \notin \text{dom}(U_{s_0})$. If $\tau \prec \tau_i$ and $\tau \in D_{s_0}$, then $U_{s_0}(\tau) \preccurlyeq U_{s_1}(\tau_i) \preccurlyeq \sigma_i$, it follows that $\sigma_i \in \overline{R}^{V_{s_0}}$. But we assumed that $\sigma_i \notin \overline{R}^{V_{s_0}}$, and hence $\llbracket \lbrace \tau_i : i \leqslant k \rbrace \rrbracket \cap \llbracket D_{s_0} \rrbracket = \emptyset$. The result follows, since $D_{s_0} \subseteq D_{s_1}$. $\square$

Again by scaling, we can regard the opponent as having $\frac{1}{16}$ much measure to spend, and the cost of the move as being $\mu(\llbracket \Sigma \rrbracket)$.

<div class="math-callout math-callout--theorem" markdown="1">
  <p class="math-callout__title"><span class="math-callout__label">Lemma</span><span class="math-callout__name">(16.3.33)</span></p>

$V$ is a strict process machine.

</div>

*Proof.* $U$ is by assumption a strict process machine, so to check that $V$ is a strict process machine, we need to check only the strings enumerated into $V$ by the champion. As the champion is effectively a prefix-free machine, we need to show only that the champion does not run out of measure.

We divide the games into two sorts, those games $G_{\langle n, i, j \rangle}$ with $j = 0$, and all other games. We know the champion always keeps within the rules of the game. Let $C$ be the cost to the champion of playing those games with $j = 0$. Then $C$ is less than the sum of the measures allocated to each game. Hence, $C \leqslant \sum_n \sum_i 2^{-n-i-6} = \sum_n 2^{-n-5} = \frac{1}{16}$.

Now, $j$ is incremented only if the opponent exceeds the amount of measure allocated to a game. Hence the measure the champion spends on these games is always less than the measure the opponent spends overall. As the opponent has only $\frac{1}{16}$ to spend, it follows that the champion spends less than $\frac{1}{16} + \frac{1}{16} = \frac{1}{8}$, which is exactly the measure available to the champion. $\square$
